#### ↳ Re: QSAM

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vkgc7$8nn$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <7vjucf$ibk$1@plonk.apk.net>,
  "DENVER \"SHUCKINS\" GEORGE" <shuckins@ptcom.net> wrote:
> This is a multi-part message in MIME format.

Please don't post in MIME, just pure ASCII, it's easier for all of us...

>
> ------=_NextPart_000_006C_01BF2433.B9C216A0
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> method but for what ?????

What does what mean? Are you asking about QSAM? If yes, the answer is
quite simple: QSAM is about an IBM synonym for 'sequential file'. You
just code FD and SELECT, you OPEN, READ, WRITE and CLOSE. Don't forget
to code either a 'AT END' clause or ask the FILE-STATUS after every
access to the file.

QSAM does not HAVE an access-method, it IS an access-method (abbrev. for
'Queued Sequential Access Method').

>
> ------=_NextPart_000_006C_01BF2433.B9C216A0
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> it must be a access method but for what
?????</FONT></DIV></BODY></HTML>
>

>
>
```

#### ↳ Re: QSAM

- **From:** joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net (John McKown)
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<slrn81r1vs.5s9.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net>`
- **References:** `<7vjucf$ibk$1@plonk.apk.net>`

```
On Mon, 1 Nov 1999 06:38:36 -0500, DENVER \"SHUCKINS\" GEORGE 
<shuckins@ptcom.net> wrote:
>
>What does this mean ?  Is it like VSAM   I know it must be a access =
>method but for what ?????

QSAM is used for reading simple sequential files. Starting at the 
beginning, reading one record at a time and invoking the AT END clause
when there are no more records to read. 

Hope that helps,
John
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: QSAM

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381E6843.D70C2CC3@att.net>`
- **References:** `<7vjucf$ibk$1@plonk.apk.net> <slrn81r1vs.5s9.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net>`

```
John McKown wrote:
> 
(snip)
> >
> >What does this mean ?  Is it like VSAM   I know it must be a access =
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> when there are no more records to read.

"QSAM" = "Queued Sequential Access Method" (as opposed to "BSAM", the
"Basic" SAM), i.e., GET & PUT macros, with implied WAITs (BSAM uses
READ/WRITE/CHECK macros (CHECK = wait)). QSAM handles deblocking, BSAM
doesn't, etc.

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: QSAM

- **From:** "Erik Hahn" <erik_m_hahn@email.msn.com>

- **References:** `<7vjucf$ibk$1@plonk.apk.net>`

```
Queued Sequential Access Method (aka flat files).  On mainframes, access to
this is completely standard.  You must read each record, in order, from the
beginning.

method but for what ?????
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: QSAM

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00

```
>QSAM - Queued Sequential Access Method
>BSAM - Basic Sequential Access Method

I have pretty much ignored the acronyms and either used what I call
"flat files" or ESDS VSAM files.  I suppose my flat files are either
QSAM or BSAM as I work in an MVS environment.  I'm guessing that old
computers used BSAM, and new ones used QSAM as IBM marketers came up
with a new term to indicate a slight upgrade in how they accessed the
file.

Is there a difference in the two types of files that users/programmers
can see?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: QSAM

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38222180.76560227@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<381ecb48.5628553@news2.ibm.net> <19991101190439.24295.00000947@ng-fb1.aol.com> <381EFAB3.F7C80B21@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Tue, 02 Nov 1999 07:52:35 -0700, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:

>>QSAM - Queued Sequential Access Method
>>BSAM - Basic Sequential Access Method
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>can see?

Yes, there is, but only in assembler.  (At least I don't know how to use BSAM in COBOL)
QSAM is a strictly synchronous access method, and after issuing the GET macro instruction
your program just stops and waits until the IO is complete.  Also, QSAM beforms the
deblocking for you.

(B)SAM, on the other hand, allows for some asynchronous access, which means that after the
READ request your program continues, and go do other things, but you will have to issue a
CHECK instruction to test (and possibly wait) for the completion of the I/O


     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         Lesser known machine instructions: BUE BRANCH ON UNDETECTED ERROR
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: QSAM

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381F877D.B9D1AD8@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<381ecb48.5628553@news2.ibm.net> <19991101190439.24295.00000947@ng-fb1.aol.com> <381EFAB3.F7C80B21@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
QSAM and BSAM (along with BDAM) are members of the SAM -
Sequential Access Method family.  BDAM - Basic Direct Access
Method - has been replaced by VSAM, as has BISAM and QISAM
(Indexed Sequential Access Method family.)

At the access method level, BSAM is READ and WRITE of physical
blocks.  QSAM is GET and PUT of logical records.  Calls to QSAM
wait until a logical record is available before returning.  BSAM
returns immediately and requires that a CHECK or WAIT be done
before assuming the I/O is complete (i.e. looking at a just read
block, or reusing a just written buffer.)  BSAM also allows
positioning in the file by POINTing to the next block you want to
read or write.

The performance for both is quite good.  If you do several READS
at the start of the program, all buffers will be filled in one
I/O.  If you can process the data fast enough and get a buffer
returned in time, you can keep the I/O going.  QSAM will try hard
to keep buffers filled so that each GET you do will always be able
to return a record without waiting for actual I/O.

In the old days (like before MVS), you could make a big impact
doing I/O by increasing the number of buffers, and using BSAM to
keep buffers scheduled for I/O.  When SAM/E appeared, QSAM took a
big performance gain, catching up to BSAM.  To get better
performance then either access method, you have to write your own
channel programs and manage real storage yourself (EXCP/VR).

VSAM uses a different interface to the raw I/O system (called
media manager) and can out perform either.  Compare a striped data
set of 1 stripe (which is managed by media manager) with a regular
SAM data set.  1 stripe will win almost all the time, and tie when
it doesn't win.

Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> >QSAM - Queued Sequential Access Method
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> can see?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: QSAM

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38204B59.72A48187@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<381ecb48.5628553@news2.ibm.net> <19991101190439.24295.00000947@ng-fb1.aol.com> <381EFAB3.F7C80B21@NOSPAMhome.com> <381F877D.B9D1AD8@techie.com>`

```
Jared Thomas wrote:
> 
> QSAM and BSAM (along with BDAM) are members of the SAM -
> Sequential Access Method family.  BDAM - Basic Direct Access
> Method - has been replaced by VSAM, as has BISAM and QISAM
> (Indexed Sequential Access Method family.)

I believe IBM intended EVERYTHING to be replaced by VSAM.  In fact, to
keep current functionality, I converted all of the files from our Univac
9030 system to VSAM.  VSAM was built in three flavors - ESDS (replacing
various SAM), RRDS ((I think), replacing relative (we used those a
lot)), and KSDS (keyed).   It has been years since I have seen any ESDS
or RRDS datasets (dataset is IBM for file, partitioned dataset is IBM
for a particular type of file which looks like a library!! 8^) ).

The term BDAM sounds kind of like relative files - is it?   

Nowadays, most people use VSAM interchangeably with ISAM.  We don't care
what's behind it, and use IDCAMS utilities to create the file (why not
use that word?).  Databases have taken over, and we are quite happy with
databases, flat files, and some keyed files.  We can use IBM terminology
or use the terminology everybody else uses.

It is nice learning the history of various xSAM and xDAM file formats
though.  Funny, I was programming IBM COBOL 30 years ago and didn't care
about QSAM & BSAM etc.  They were all just flat files to me!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: QSAM

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3822ce2c.171681865@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<381ecb48.5628553@news2.ibm.net> <19991101190439.24295.00000947@ng-fb1.aol.com> <381EFAB3.F7C80B21@NOSPAMhome.com> <381F877D.B9D1AD8@techie.com> <38204B59.72A48187@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Wed, 03 Nov 1999 07:48:57 -0700, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:

>I believe IBM intended EVERYTHING to be replaced by VSAM. 

Yes, that was their intention.  But they only succeeded in getting rid of ISAM, all the
other access methods are still around, and still going strong
>
>The term BDAM sounds kind of like relative files - is it?   
>
Basic Direct Access Method, which allows to access data by relative block number. And yes,
that is similar to RRDS

>Nowadays, most people use VSAM interchangeably with ISAM.  We don't care
>what's behind it, and use IDCAMS utilities to create the file (why not
>use that word?).

Because files and datasets are a different thing.  A file is a handle to a dataset, file =
DDNAME, data set = DSNAME

>It is nice learning the history of various xSAM and xDAM file formats
>though.  Funny, I was programming IBM COBOL 30 years ago and didn't care
>about QSAM & BSAM etc.  They were all just flat files to me!

They are the same files.  It's just inassembler that you decide if you want to use BSAM
(using READ and WRITE macros) or QSAM (using GET and OUT)  or something else alltogether
(like EXCP = EXecute Channel Program).  Actually, I wouldn't know what COBOL is doing....

         2 + 2 = 5 for extremely large values of 2.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: QSAM

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vs9br$spp$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<381ecb48.5628553@news2.ibm.net> <19991101190439.24295.00000947@ng-fb1.aol.com> <381EFAB3.F7C80B21@NOSPAMhome.com> <381F877D.B9D1AD8@techie.com> <38204B59.72A48187@NOSPAMhome.com> <382122A8.378C018@att.net>`

```
snips
> >
> > The term BDAM sounds kind of like relative files - is it?
>
> Basic Direct Access Method
> >
Here's some info from my vague memory many beers (oops - years) ago.
This is what we did - at the time I remember thinking it was always
necessary. Perhaps there were other options.
With BDAM, a programmer declared the number of blocks he was going to
use for the file. Then he wrote an assembler randomizing routine, based
on some key in the records. Using the key, the rr calculated the
precise block the record should be in. I can't remember the math
anymore, but it seems we used some sort of modulo arithmetic using a
prime number. I.e., divide the key by 13.
You couldn't simply divide by the number of blocks, because then (if
you assigned keys sequentially (e.g., Purchase Order Number), the
records would all be in the 'early' blocks - you needed to spread them
around (see below).
When you read/wrote a record you called the randomizing routine to
access the block. Somehow the precise record (of all those in the
block) we wanted came back to us - I don't remember the tech details on
that.
 The theory was to write a randomizing routine that would spread the
records around the blocks. I.e., if there were many records for Chicago
and few for Hick City, you wanted to avoid a 'lump' of records on the
disk in the blocks calculated for Chicago. So the rr would try to
spread Chicago's records all over the place. Similarly, key 6788 should
go to a block far from key 6789. There was a utility that showed the
demographics of the population of the blocks, as I recall.
Whenever you needed more blocks you had to modify the calculation of
the randomizing routine.

Sorry for the wishy-washy on details - it was too long ago.
John


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: QSAM

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m7tv1s8in1ln34s67n9amcs5f6ffk7o5dn@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<381ecb48.5628553@news2.ibm.net> <19991101190439.24295.00000947@ng-fb1.aol.com> <381EFAB3.F7C80B21@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Tue, 02 Nov 1999 07:52:35 -0700 Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
wrote:

:>>QSAM - Queued Sequential Access Method
:>>BSAM - Basic Sequential Access Method

:>I have pretty much ignored the acronyms and either used what I call
:>"flat files" or ESDS VSAM files.  I suppose my flat files are either
:>QSAM or BSAM as I work in an MVS environment.  I'm guessing that old
:>computers used BSAM, and new ones used QSAM as IBM marketers came up
:>with a new term to indicate a slight upgrade in how they accessed the
:>file.

:>Is there a difference in the two types of files that users/programmers
:>can see?

BSAM and QSAM are access methods, not file types. EXCP is also a valid access
method for flat files. The difference between these three access methods is
the amount of control and complexity of the code.

EXCP will allow complete control over the file. You can read specific blocks,
specific bytes within blocks, in any order, at any time. The tradeoff is that
you must write the channel programs, i.e., the orders issued to the device to
perform the functions. Most control, most complexity.

BSAM will allow repositioning to a specific block, ability to read/write keys,
process multiple PDS members and the ability to perform the I/O asynchronously
at your convenience. The trade off is that you will have to block/unblock the
records and provide buffer management. A lot of control, somewhat complex.

QSAM will allow you to easily read/write sequentially thru the file. Records
will be blocked/unblocked and buffers will be handled automatically. The trade
off is that you cannot reposition the file or choose when to schedule the I/O.
Limited control, relatively simple.

In general QSAM will be used as it is the easiest access method to code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: QSAM

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vrtpg$lal$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7vjucf$ibk$1@plonk.apk.net> <381D9A37.427B7684@zip.com.au> <381ecb48.5628553@news2.ibm.net>`

```
Everything said below is true BUT in the way of additional information, it is
POSSIBLE to use VSAM ESDS (not KSDS) files instead of QSAM files in almost
all IBM mainframe COBOL programs.  There is a MINOR change to the
Select/Assign statement - but other than that, you program should work the
same regardless of which you use.  I am *not* recommending this (as I believe
it can have serious performance problems) but only mentioning it in
relationship to the question of QSAM vs VSAM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: QSAM

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38221E86.A2FA90BF@att.net>`
- **References:** `<7vjucf$ibk$1@plonk.apk.net> <381D9A37.427B7684@zip.com.au> <381ecb48.5628553@news2.ibm.net> <7vrtpg$lal$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> Everything said below is true BUT in the way of additional information, it is
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> relationship to the question of QSAM vs VSAM.

I'm by no means a VSAM guru, but AFAIK we still don't have the
flexibility deleting & defining ESDS's that we've always had with flat
files, not to mention GDG's. What I'm thinking of here is the ability to
allocate a flat file with JCL only (which I *think* is possible now, but
who's going to go back and update all their production JCL?)

Bill Lynch
```

- **Message-ID:** `<VD2%3.51280$Hk.468916@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<7vjucf$ibk$1@plonk.apk.net> <381D9A37.427B7684@zip.com.au> <381ecb48.5628553@news2.ibm.net> <7vrtpg$lal$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <3823ceb8.171821906@news2.ibm.net> <3821E95D.62A8E39E@NOSPAMhome.com> <3822E262.6762C6EF@zip.com.au> <38271641.7156590@news2.ibm.net> <3824281A.BAAA0A33@zip.com.au> <382bf6b1.41519201@news2.attglobal.net>`

```
Yes, I'm working os OS-390 version 2.6 (about the latest) and NO
you can NOT Browse as VSAM KEYED file.

BUT VSAM does NOT mean INDEXED, it means Virtual Storage Access Method
(as in Multiple Virtual Systems) and there are 4 type osVSAM files:

Entry Sequenced Data Sets (ESDS) otherwise known as Sequential BUT they
don't run
under QSAM and were infact created VIA IDCAMS DEFINE

Linear (???) mainly used by DB2

Keyed - what almost everyone means when they say VSAM

Relative Record Data Set (RRDS) AKA a 'relative file'

So if the file is a RRDS maybe?, an ESDS quite likely, a linear DS ?

Aloha



Volker Bandke <vbandke@ibm.net> wrote in message
news:382bf6b1.41519201@news2.attglobal.net...
> On Sun, 07 Nov 1999 00:07:38 +1100, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:
>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>          What does CICS mean? - Changing It Can't Succeed
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: QSAM

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383CEBA8.7C5F1B16@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<7vjucf$ibk$1@plonk.apk.net> <381D9A37.427B7684@zip.com.au> <381ecb48.5628553@news2.ibm.net> <7vrtpg$lal$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <3823ceb8.171821906@news2.ibm.net> <3821E95D.62A8E39E@NOSPAMhome.com> <3822E262.6762C6EF@zip.com.au> <38271641.7156590@news2.ibm.net> <3824281A.BAAA0A33@zip.com.au> <382bf6b1.41519201@news2.attglobal.net> <VD2%3.51280$Hk.468916@news1.mia>`

```


James King wrote:
> 
> Yes, I'm working os OS-390 version 2.6 (about the latest) and NO
> you can NOT Browse as VSAM KEYED file.
...
There is, however, a very reasonably priced ISPF/VSAM Utility available
from MacKinney Systems (http://www.mackinney.com/ ) that supports
edit/browse of VSAM files, and will also allow edit/browse of sequential
files with attributes that might not be supported by ISPF Editor.  If
you have things configured appropriately, users requesting "browse" from
a 3.4 ds list might not even be aware they are not using native ISPF
browse on a VSAM file.  We have several add-ons to ISPF, and sometimes
you forget what is native ISPF and what is not. 
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: y2k code expansion

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mgiT3.248$Wn.9607@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<B9UZOLkv5rU0ug9vlnhaChWtezmm@4ax.com> <VB6T3.1927$iS.87878@viper>`

```
Gumbo wrote in message ...
> Time and money spent [now] will always be better spent than
>debugging time and overtime money spent later.
>


While I agree with this as a 'default' and 'highly desireable' option, there
are times when a manager has to make a decision which favors short-term over
long-term gains.

When I worked as an executive in The Real World, I always found that if you
share the rationale for such an 'obviously stupid' decision, you build
support for it. It also helps all employees understand a bit better that as
much as the boss may want to focus on a very narrow front or exclusively on
the long term, often he/she cannot.

MCM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: y2k code expansion

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Su9U3.559$GH5.20838@viper>`
- **References:** `<B9UZOLkv5rU0ug9vlnhaChWtezmm@4ax.com> <VB6T3.1927$iS.87878@viper> <mgiT3.248$Wn.9607@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <4xiT3.31893$oa2.33593@iad-read.news.verio.net> <mVrT3.854$Wn.37234@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Making decisions on what can be done in a prticular time as a career choice
does not and never will make for a better or even good product.

Allowing for known mistakes now which WILL HAVE TO be fixed later, will
always cause more problems and more money. Pay rates grow. Inflation grows.
Any manger will tell you this. Yet most of them forget that meeting the ALL
MIGHTY deadline will cost much more when the problem is eventually SOLVED
AND PREVENTED from happening again.

I never needed a business degree to understand why these decisions are done.
The problem with most programmers is they are not directly responsible for
meeting deadlines.

The problem with management is they are responsible for deadlines. In their
effort to extend deadlines for us programmers to do the job right, they
receive a great amount of friction. This friction is higher with the level
of management. Thus over time they become more and more reluctant to change
dealines, because after all "WE MET THE DEADLINE, EVEN THOUGH WE DIDN'T FIX
IT RIGHT". Thus over time every project becomes more and more infected with
incomplete solutions, commonly known as patches.

Eventually the time spent maintenancing the package will grow to such a
point, MANAGEMENT will have to purchase another package to replace the
infected pile of puss their management decisions have created.

All this would have been prevented by simply fixing it right the first time
and being a little late. Just because their is the word DEAD in DEADLINE
does not mean you will DIE if you miss it.
```

#### ↳ Re: y2k code expansion

- **From:** "Walker" <jwalker@novia.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9vmW3.181$7%6.20676@newscene.newscene.com>`
- **References:** `<B9UZOLkv5rU0ug9vlnhaChWtezmm@4ax.com>`

```
expand the mmddyy in vsamkey to ccyymmdd format using a passer.
the passer can read the file swap the fields and set the century by however
logic you use.
then all files have the be done this way, and any flat files that are
offloaded to
all programs referencing any of these changed vsam files and the offloaded
flat files will need remediating.
```

---

<a id="t-4ce79e7754"></a>
## Thread: Would You SIgn This Contract? — 34 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>`

```
I was recently offered a job with the following paragraph in the
contract. I have obfuscated the name of the job shop.  You probably
have never heard of XYZ.  You probably have heard of XYZ's client.

>Salary and Terms:  The Employee will be paid  semi-monthly on a W-2 
>basis as per the details in the attachment to this Agreement.  XYZ 
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>XYZ.

This is not for a telecommuting contract, this is for me to go cross
country and work in their office, under their supervision, on their
applications, which I have never seen before.  What do you think of
this contract?  Would you accept a performance based contract where
the complete definition of performance is "determined by XYZ's
Client's sole discretion"?

The headhunter from XYZ tells me "The contract is solid and fair for
all parties concerned."
```

#### ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** "Phil Meeks" <pmeeks~@~fmtc.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vlf4o$a7$1@news3.infoave.net>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>`

```
The fact that W-2 was specified makes you an employee of the client. And no
employee that I know of would be subject to an employment agreement that
stipulated that they are to be paid only if the work is satisfactory.
Employees are simply terminated for unsatisfactory work. But they must be
paid for hours worked up to termination.

No, I would not sign this, and I would like to know if you have found anyone
that would work under this agreement. My advice is to strike the offending
paragraph from the agreement and stand firm.

As far as the headhunter's comments, remember, a headhunter gets paid for a
placement. The headhunter only cares that you accept the job, and will say
anything it takes to get you to do so, even if it is untrue. The headhunter
has to make a living and eat -- he's just trying to survive.
```

#### ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** "T.Totaler" <t-total@noboose4u.org>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381DC909.5755@noboose4u.org>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>`

```
I would not sign such a contract and suggest you don't. You work on-site
and an accepted practice for that arrangement is that the client signs
your timesheet every week which is all you need in order to get paid. As
far as what the headhunter from XYZ tells you, you don't need to listen
to that. It's their job to fuck you up when necessary.

At the same time, you will never get a truly 100% guarantee of being
paid--even with the timesheet: if the cleint doesn't sign it, you stand
to lose a week' (at the most) worth of pay. So, be good <g> and keep'em
happy (the client, that is.)

If you sign it like it is, when you're done, and say, you don't get paid
for the last half a month (plus whatever the "money-aging" period is,
can be quite a bit of money)--how are you gonna know why? They'll tell
you, well the client didn't like your shit... You're gonna fly back
cross the country to hunt it all down? Better safe than sorry, guy <g>.
Have a signed timesheet and make sure it is what your payment is based
on rather than some bs. It's a document you can take to court should,
god forbid, the need arise.
-------------------
Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> I was recently offered a job with the following paragraph in the
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> l   |/ Yes, I'm back.
```

- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>`

```
I'm with the others: don't sign this contract.  Strike the
clause, and go from there.  I suggest something to the 
effect that you get paid for every hour you work, and you
will invoice your agency for hours worked (if the agency
wants a timesheet signed so be it).

The headhunter from XYZ is making a pretty penny off you.
Let them earn some of their dough and assume some of the
risk of contracting.

Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> I was recently offered a job with the following paragraph in the
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> all parties concerned."
```

#### ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<61296DDE13F7C015.611FBFC188624049.0CC6B03F6055B1AF@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 01 Nov 1999 16:00:16 -0800, Randall Bart
<Barticus@usa.spam.net> enlightened us:

>I was recently offered a job with the following paragraph in the
>contract. I have obfuscated the name of the job shop.  You probably
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>all parties concerned."

That clause is horrible and, as others have said, don't sign it.  Some
have said that it wouldn't hold up because of the W-2 stipulation, but
as I remember from a labor law course I once took, the fact that you
signed a contract that contained that language would override W-2
labor laws.

If they insist on keeping that clause in, ask them to add a clause
that says if you are unhappy or not satisfied with your office
environment, they have to pay you double :)

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Whenever I find myself in a difficult situation, I ask myself "What
Would Jesus Do?"  The mental image of my opposition being cast into
pits of hellfire for all eternity *is* comforting, but probably not
what the inventors of the phrase had in mind.


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

```
Randall..

  I totally agree with what the others have said. This is a disaster

  Second, headhunter from XYZ does not know a good contract from a hole
```

#### ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** olczyk@interaccess.com (Thaddeus L. Olczyk)
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381ea5e0.18079109@nntp.interaccess.com>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>`

```
First off : I hope you realise that the clause says that if the
company determines your work is unsatisfactory at any point
they can force you to return all of the money you've been paid.
Let's play a little wiht this job. Suppose you work for nine months,
then suddenly the Client says that only the first month was preformed
satisfactory you have to pay them back ( at least thats what the
clause says ) for the last eight months salary. This can even be used
as blackmail. Say you've been there for nine months, and you hit a
snag: as an example something that happened to a friend of mine, he
did VB work with some C dlls. They wanted him to redo the C in VB.
He kept insisting that in VB that part would be too slow. Eventually
they parted ways on this issue, if not friendly then at least
amicably. If this clause was part of that contract they could say that
since he was leaving  with the project unfinished, his work during
that period was unsatisfactory. Thus he would be forced to pay back
the money ( probably not legally, but they can make you sweat it).
Thus they can blackmail you into doing things against your ethics.

Another thing that can happen is that they fall very far behind, or
start to reneg on things in the contract (such as vactaion pay ). They
get so bad about this that you walk out. When you try to sue them for
back pay, they make the same claim-- work unfinished implies job
preformance unsatisfactory. Since they haven't paid you it will be
even harder to get their money.

No matter what the agency says, I would insist on something in writing
from the client concerning what is and is not satisfactory. If the
client is seriously worried, then I would suggest that you make some
kind of procedure for drawing up a timetable. Don't plan for more then
a month ahead ( and leave plenty of wiggle room ). Easiest is to just
have a meeting once a month where you have a schedual for the next
month and you both sign off on it. Remember also that you have to
allow for modification of the schedual for emergencies. Example
in my last job, I would often get a call about a bug. Sometimes it
turned out to be a bug of users of my stuff ( COM objects that used
files to communicate with embedded sopftware. I wrote the COM objects
), the embedded side often created corrupt files, which I had to
analyse, or my own. In any case they would often shoot a one/two day
hole in my schedual. I don't mind as long as management makes
allowances for these holes.

Finally. In your reply to the agent I would say that you spoke to a
lawyer ( you might actually want to do this ), and his reaction was
that it was not solid and fair for all parties. Furthermore even if it
was, it would not be acceptable to you which is all that counts.
Oh yes, and insist that he and the client both submit to you in
writing what they expect your rate, and the rate the client pays to
be. If he complains point out it is in the contract workers bill of
rights.
```

- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol

```
> This is not for a telecommuting contract, this is for me to go cross
> country and work in their office, under their supervision, on their
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

And how much is the contractor getting for filling this position?
I would sign it if 
1: I was crazy, Certifiably
2: I was independently wealthy.
3: I had nothing better to do, no responsibilities, no cares or
financial needs.
4: Had a gun to my head.
5: Had a gun to my families head.
6: I was suicidal and wanted to die slowly and painfully.
7: All of the above.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vn10d$2ol$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com> <381EE0AA.7B61FF97@ford.com>`

```
In article <381EE0AA.7B61FF97@ford.com>,
  Random Chance <nospam@ford.com> wrote:
> Randall Bart wrote:
> Contract SNIP
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No way should you sign this. Even if you do a tremendous job
technically, there's always the situation where you run up against
office politics. A common scenario is where the local inbreds don't
appreciate the hot-shot high-paid contractors coming in cross-country
to save the day. I've often said that it's not the technical side of
the job that's the toughest, it's the personnel side. Especially as a
contractor, who is expected to get things done, notwithstanding the
local's reluctance to participate in such an effort. If you have to
shove them out of the way to provide the desired level of 'satisfactory
results', they'll sabotage you somewhere along the next project.
John


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** Jerry Stuckle <jstucklex@attglobal.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381EFB52.49F9@attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>`

```
Randall,

There's another part of this others have come close to, but missed.

You will be an EMPLOYEE of XYZ.  Federal law steps in here, and you must
be paid for your work.  As others have indicated, XYZ can terminate
you.  But they must still pay you at the agreed upon rate for the hours
worked (or minimum wage, whichever is greater).

Now - if you were on a 1099 and being paid for deliverables, an
unsatisfactory deliverable is reason for not being paid.  You have the
choice of making it right or accepting the loss.  But as an employee
paid by the hour, the rules are different.

See your attorney ASAP!

Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> I was recently offered a job with the following paragraph in the
…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
> l   |/ Yes, I'm back.
```

#### ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<03tt1s8ln6lvth43hnqol9e6omlg9mi7tu@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 01 Nov 1999 16:00:16 -0800 Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net> wrote:

:>I was recently offered a job with the following paragraph in the
:>contract. I have obfuscated the name of the job shop.  You probably
:>have never heard of XYZ.  You probably have heard of XYZ's client.

:>>Salary and Terms:  The Employee will be paid  semi-monthly on a W-2 
:>>basis as per the details in the attachment to this Agreement.  XYZ 
:>>shall not be obliged to pay Employee for any services performed by the 
:>>Employee pursuant to the enclosed job order, which are not performed 
:>>to XYZ's Client's satisfaction. XYZ's Client's satisfaction is 
:>>determined by XYZ's Client's sole discretion. In the event, XYZ has 
:>>rendered payment to the Employee for such unsatisfactory services, the 
:>>XYZ may at its sole option and discretion, either (i) receive a credit 
:>>from Employee to be applied against any future business with XYZ or 
:>>(ii) receive a refund from Employee for the full amount of money's 
:>>paid for such unsatisfactory service within 15 days of such demand by 
:>>XYZ.

:>This is not for a telecommuting contract, this is for me to go cross
:>country and work in their office, under their supervision, on their
:>applications, which I have never seen before.  What do you think of
:>this contract?  Would you accept a performance based contract where
:>the complete definition of performance is "determined by XYZ's
:>Client's sole discretion"?

It doesn't matter if you are telecommuting or going on site.

This is a clause that any employer would like you to sign but one no sane
contractor would sign.

You have zero leverage and the client can keep on changing the specifications
and tell you, if you do not make the change we will not be satisfied.

I had an offer like that several years ago for a six month project with a
similar "initial" clause. I absolutely refused and told the client we can
negotiate what the program will do (fixed fee) and include that in the
contract but there is no way I would allow the client a blank check.

Client didn't agree and then a month later wanted to talk again. I demanded
(and received) an advance fee just to talk about the issue but the clients
employees could not agree among themselves what the specifications would be.

They also had a clause that the program (for a S/390) would properly support
year 2100. I had to strike that since there is no hardware clock value for
past 2041 or so. They did agree to that. 

:>The headhunter from XYZ tells me "The contract is solid and fair for
:>all parties concerned."

How can you tell when a headhunter is lieing?

His lips are moving.

If the headhunter is so hot and heavy see if they will agree to place $X/hour
in escrow which you can collect if XYZ's client won't pay?

Didn't think so.
```

#### ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** "Paul M. Cook" <pmBERMUDASHORTScook@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<svET3.22$ye7.549@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>`

```
Sounds to me like they have built in a convenient way of ripping you off for
a 2 weeks pay.   I wouldn't sign a contract like that if you held a gun to
my head.  Hopefully it won't come down to that for you.  It is truly
criminal the things that agencies get away with.  You could fight them I
suppose but in the end it would cost you more to get your money from them
than the amount itself and this I am sure they know all too well.

Line through the section you posted, initialize and date it and send it back
to them with the understanding that you'll sign if they agree to the
revision.  If they don't, I'd find another agency.

Paul
```

##### ↳ ↳ Exactly. Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** "T.Totaler" <t-total@noboose4u.org>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381EA967.364D@noboose4u.org>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com> <svET3.22$ye7.549@paloalto-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Paul M. Cook wrote:
> Sounds to me like they have built in a convenient way of ripping you off for
> a 2 weeks pay.
Now, remember, unless invoices are due on receipt (which I doubt is the
case here), two weeks may easily become 1.5 months. 

Or the agent finds someone cheaper than the guy A, tells the guy A he's
dona a bad job and is let go, tells the client the guy A has run off,
puts the new, cheaper guy B (or shall we say, H-1B <g>) in, and keeps
the outstanding money due to the guy A. The client doesnt' know any
better except furthering his belief that all programmers are
irresponsible assholes, the agent is getting a bigger cut now, the new B
guy's happy he's not in Bombay anymore, and the guy A can't do much
about any of it either coz "the client didn't like his work."
```

#### ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** Chip Calvert <cbciv@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hyEfOMb4Lk+28fdjeY=x50SapyLf@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>`

```
On Mon, 01 Nov 1999 16:00:16 -0800, Randall Bart
<Barticus@usa.spam.net> wrote:

[snip ridiculous contract clause saying that he will work W2 and only
be paid if work is "satisfactory" to client]

>What do you think of
>this contract?  Would you accept a performance based contract where
>the complete definition of performance is "determined by XYZ's
>Client's sole discretion"?

Do not sign this contract as long as it contains this clause.  First,
as Jerry pointed out, it is likely in violation of federal labor laws.
Second, it's a perfect way for them to screw you any time they feel
like it.  As others have pointed out, they could turn around and
demand all the money they've paid you back, saying that your work is
"unsatisfactory."

If you were contacting on a 1099 basis, I would still say no.  The
correct way to do it would be to have milestones or deliverables at
(or for) which the client would sign off on the work thus far and pay
you the amount specified in the contract.

>The headhunter from XYZ tells me "The contract is solid and fair for
>all parties concerned."

Bullshit.  Not only is it completely unfair, it's probably not legal.
Stop dealing with this headhunter immediately.  They've just shown
their colors.
```

#### ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381F251E.70A342B6@home.com>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>`

```


Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> I was recently offered a job with the following paragraph in the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> >Salary and Terms:  etc...

Come on Randall - put that legalistic mind to work ! You obviously smell
a rat, otherwise you wouldn't have posted. Go back at them with a draft
of conditions under which you would be prepared to work, with the
annotation it is a draft and subject to finalization between both
parties. If they reject it out of hand - forget 'em.

If they reject you - you could always offer Jose Capucho's name if they
don't mind the programs being written in Portuguese. Just think of the
frequent flyer points Jose could accrue.<G>

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** Tony@ask.me (Tony)
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.1288e699632dc7a3989e87@netnews.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>`

```
In article <oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>, Barticus@usa.spam.net 
says...
> I was recently offered a job with the following paragraph in the
> contract. I have obfuscated the name of the job shop.  You probably
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> all parties concerned."

It's an employment agreement and not an independent consulting contract, 
first off.  If you want to be an employee, get employed by a company that 
assumes all risks (that's one of the benefits of employment!) rather than 
you.  The headhunter is a scumbag, lose him.

Tony
```

```
Georgi
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol

```
specification beyond "the user likes it when done".

Mind you, I have done that ...

Perhaps if the rates are in the $500 per hour range, and you are confident
... on the other hand, if that is what they want, then they should have the
balls to sub-contract the entire job. Sounds like a bunch of flakes to me.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991105093907.02525.00001273@ng-cr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7vmvt1$h10$3@news.igs.net>`

```
>Perhaps if the rates are in the $500 per hour range, and you are confident
>... on the other hand, if that is what they want, then they should have the
>balls to sub-contract the entire job.

If there is a risk of losing one's money when the rates are, say $100 an hour,
then the risk isn't less at a rate of $500 an hour.  In fact, the exposure to
risk is even higher!  You would stand to lose even more of your labor
investment in the latter case, and the employer would have an even greater
incentive to reneg.  You rightly advise Mr. Bart not to agree to the absurd
terms he was offerred,  but the reasoning behind your aside about rates is
flawed.

RoadRaat
```

```
Randall,
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** "T.Totaler" <t-total@nobooze4u.org>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381F6FD3.6875@nobooze4u.org>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com> <7voh3h$6m0$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
WOB Consulting wrote:
> 
> RUN don't WALK from this one. It's a recipe for disaster and an
> outrageous clause to agree with.
I don't disagree, but I'm a bit surprised by the extreme degree of
catastrophizing in this (and many other) messages in this thread. Yes,
it is not a good condition to sign, probably even an unacceptable one.
But a "recipe for a disaster"? You've gotta negotiate sometimes, that's
all. No need to get all riled up. That's a couple of lines in their
favour, you don't like it, so ask for a modification or walk away--w/o
turing red, gasping for air, going postal or having a heart attack out
of indignation <g>. If you want a quiet life, get an ft job and
relax--there's no shame in that. If you want to go around on your own,
you *will* run into stressful things, so take a course in stress
management. Money's good, and (imo) it's worth it.

> It has almost come to the point that contractors need to pass contracts
> on to his/her attorney (at $500.00 USD a pop) for scrutiny and
> "revision".
No shit! Yeah, it's about time you start doing just that <g>. Btw, I've
never had to pay "$500 a pop" for a run-of-the-mill contract review.
Normally it's about 100 to 150 and then you can re-use what you're got
or the new re-reviews will be even shorter--and thus cheaper. Compared
to the money you stand to earn (or lose <g>) it's peanuts.

> This only reinforces my opinion of pimps. They are THE lowest form of
> life (even lower than car salesman).
So what? I never bought a car on a "no-haggle" lot <g>. It's no fun and
they rip you off worse than on a normal, "full-bullshit" place. I like
to fuck with them and give them heart attacks myself. You have more say
than you think you do, my friend. In fact, most of "independents" gotta
kiss the "pimps'" ass. 99% of "independent consultants" that you can see
around wouldn't be "consultants" at all if the "pimps" didn't constantly
create this market. They are the ones who keep most of the blow-hard
code-head morons in business. Otherwise, all these geniuses would be
been sitting out their 30-year careers two heads per cubicle for the
glorious pay of $47K a year and a gold watch upon retirement.

My point is, don't raise unnecessary stink, just learn the system and
how to get around. The "pimps" may be car salesmen, but there are plenty
accomplished imbeciles among the "technical people" as well. If you
don't like the system, change it if you can, otherwise live and let
live.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991105101521.02525.00001277@ng-cr1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<MPG.128a04bdf0205f5e9896a8@news.earthlink.net>`

```
>  The reason I think this is a "recipe for a disaster" is because I do 
>not see any definition for the term "satisfactory" in the contract. 

There is not merely consensus about this contract, but in fact unanimity. 
Still, the above reply (and several others) to T. Totaler fails to acknowledge
a legitimate point he was trying to make.

T. Totaler didn't say that the clause was not absurd.  He said that receiving
such an offer wasn't yet a disaster.  That's an important distinction.

There is no "recipe for disaster" if you have no intention of signing such an
onerous contract.  The only person who faces possible disaster here is an
impotent schlub who thinks he has to please everybody and who never imagines
that safe-guarding his self-interest is his own responsibility, not someone
else's.  THAT is a TRUE "recipe for disaster".

If you're sensible, you have a dependable labor lawyer and you have your
contracts reviewed.  In that case, there is no need to cultivate a mentality of
"impending disaster".  Rather, one can concentrate on the tasks of negotiating
favorable agreements and performing honest labor.  
T. Totaler made a good point about not becoming enraged in the negotiating
process.  He didn't trivialize the undesirability of the offer Mr. Bart
received.  He merely suggested that the appropriate response to an unacceptable
term is a simple, "no."  This leaves open the possibility of salvaging
something from an undesirable situation.  It is not in a person's own interest
to deprive himself of the potential benefits of negotiation.

I rather doubt that anything could have been salvaged in Mr. Bart's case. 
However, a cool head and well-reasoned counter-proposals might, in some
situations, transform an unacceptable deal into a favorable one.  Although the
rage in this thread is righteous, I think anyone could benefit by giving T.
Totaler's response serious consideration.

RoadRaat
```

```
First it is a recipe for disaster because you can be tricked into
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** olczyk@interaccess.com (Thaddeus L. Olczyk)
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382464df.4535984@nntp.interaccess.com>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com> <7voh3h$6m0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <381F6FD3.6875@nobooze4u.org>`

```
On Wed, 03 Nov 1999 04:12:19 +0500, "T.Totaler"
<t-total@nobooze4u.org> wrote:

> You've gotta negotiate sometimes, that's
>all. No need to get all riled up. That's a couple of lines in their
>favour, you don't like it, so ask for a modification or walk away--w/o
>turing red, gasping for air, going postal or having a heart attack out
>of indignation <g>. 
Sorry. Premature posting. To continue...
There's negotation, and then there trying to rip someone off.
Either this was written by someone naive, or it was written
by someone who was trying to rip the guy off. In either case
you have to wonder what else is in the contract. I for one don't want
to be an expert in employment law, I want to code. I don't get paid
for knowing the law, I get paid for coding. Now I will agree that you
have to have some knowledge of the law, but I don't think you should
have to be an expert.
As for getting angry, getting angry can be good. If you get angry
after the incident it's good because you will be encouraged to walk
away when you should. Getting angry in front of the guy is even
better, if done correctly. You get to see his reaction to your anger.
For example, if he quickly says "OK.OK. No need to go ballistic."
and then strikes it without a thought, then it's an indication that
he was trying to pull one over on you. Either walk away, or run as
fast as you can to your lawyer.
```

```
maybe.  Looks like they got a bad contractor in the past, and all
subsequent contractors get to pay for it.  

It could also be to scare off those who really "can't" do the job.  If

From the other point of view - you could do a great job, fix all their
code, and NOT GET PAID.  That's why I would not sign it.  The road of
trust is a TWO way street.
```

#### ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** stephen_j_spiro@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80777c$dlg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>`

```
As others have said, if you are a W-2 employee, it is illegal.

As for subcontractors, SURPRISE, SURPRISE, it is a FAIRLY ROUTINE
element in contracts, at least in the New York area.  And while I have
been ripped off by both agencies (well, one) and clients, it is more
likely to be the client.  The agency has the clause because they would
rather lose your business than the client's, in case of a dispute.
They will ALWAYS side with the client (in the nicest, most sympathetic
possible way to you!) in a dispute.  Twice, I have come back from being
out sick to find that I have not only beeen terminated but am not being
paid for some period at the end.  My absence made it possible for
someone to scapegoat ME for problems in the project!
The simple solution is to specify that a timesheet signed by a
representative of the client is complete proof of  satisfactory work.
In that connection, some contracts specify that the contractor does not
get paid until the agency gets paid by the client.  Put a limit on
it... no more than 30 days, for instance.  That way, the last check
cannot be withheld in a dipute.
And if you are sick, send your timesheet to the client for signature,
WITH A SASE!

Stephen J Spiro
President, Wizard Systems


In article <oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com>,
  Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net> wrote:
> I was recently offered a job with the following paragraph in the

…[4 more quoted lines elided]…

the
> >Employee pursuant to the enclosed job order, which are not performed
> >to XYZ's Client's satisfaction. XYZ's Client's satisfaction is

the

credit

> >(ii) receive a refund from Employee for the full amount of money's
> >paid for such unsatisfactory service within 15 days of such demand
by
> >XYZ.
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
```

```
Using MF COBOL 3.1.56, shared runtime, in DOS box under Windows 98.

The call to sound the bell X"E5" does not seem to be consistent.  On one
system I get window's sounds, on other nothing.

Is there is setting that I am missing (SET COBSW=+C+C1-N+G+M+P1)
  or
Is there a difference between release levels of Windows98

Charlie Goodman
```

- **References:** `<iTrT3.497$Zu4.10993@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com>`

```
under Windows, is mapped to an error sound scheme, much like the colours on
the screen.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: CALL X"E5" win98 MF

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vmdh5$rd5$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
donald tees wrote in message <7vlk32$msp$1@news.igs.net>...
>I use Fujitsu, not MF, but see similar behaviour.  I believe that "BELL"
>under Windows, is mapped to an error sound scheme, much like the colours on
>the screen.
>


That is correct for MF COBOL too. The Win32 'Beep' API is used and on
Windows 95 it plays the system beep if you do not have a sound card, and the
default sound if you do.
```

#### ↳ Re: CALL X"E5" win98 MF

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vmd6l$hbp$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<iTrT3.497$Zu4.10993@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com>`

```
    The "normal" X"E5"  does seem to redirect on Win98.  Pick a subtle sound
(I use WCCLICK.WAV).  One possible reason for "no sound" is the nature of
the xe5 beep.  On an OLD pc, it produced a beep somewhat like the dos beep.
But as pc's got faster, the sound slide into higher and higher pitches,
becoming a click.  By now, even the click is gone, unless dogs and bats
still hear something.

    I was forced to create a routine that turned ansi.sys support on,
displayed a ^G, and then turned ansi.sys support back off.  This gets me a
real beep on non-win98 pc's.  Pity it doesn't work elsewhere.  I do not know
how to turn redirection off.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CALL X"E5" win98 MF

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vqebq$qn$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<iTrT3.497$Zu4.10993@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com> <7vmd6l$hbp$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <tCzT3.522$Zu4.11768@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com> <rTzT3.523$Zu4.11505@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com>`

```

Charles Goodman wrote in message ...
>Further investigation, beep works when restarted in "SAFE" mode.
>Can anyone guess what is interfering?
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>


I use the "Beep" method under class Alarm.
Although when invoking this method only once
from a PC by todays standards you get a poxy "bip"
in which you need awesome hearing to hear it, so
to get round this I just simply set a loop for about 10 normally.
Thus invoking the method 10 times creating a louder  beep
sound. ---note this is only when the end-uers don't have sound cards.


Simon R Hart
Eaton, Ottery St. Mary, UK.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CALL X"E5" win98 MF

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)

- **References:** `<iTrT3.497$Zu4.10993@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com> <7vmd6l$hbp$2@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <tCzT3.522$Zu4.11768@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com> <rTzT3.523$Zu4.11505@news1.rdc1.mb.home.com> <7vqebq$qn$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
"Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com> schrieb:

>I use the "Beep" method under class Alarm.
>Although when invoking this method only once
>from a PC by todays standards you get a poxy "bip"
>in which you need awesome hearing to hear it, so
>to get round this I just simply set a loop for about 10 normally.

The behaviour of this command is controlled by runtime-switch P1. MF
switched the default for this setting from 3.1 to 3.2 and I had quite
a lot of fun with machines that beeeep-beeeep-beeeeped instead of
buzzing.

Best regards
```

```
Bear in mind that Sven's suggestion applies only to 16-bit DOS applications.
As I said in an earlier post, the 32-bit version of the product uses the
Win32 Beep API so the P1 switch will have no effect if you are using a
32-bit MF product which, if I remember correctly, I think you are.
```

<a id="t-c68a2f8d53"></a>
## Thread: Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page? — 22 this month / 33 total

_Thread started [1999-10](1999-10.md#t-c68a2f8d53)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381DAE50.17FC79FD@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7vh5or$mj4$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <7vi1dl$g9u$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> Ib Tanding <ib@tanding.dk> wrote:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> and abandoned.

Interesting.  On the mainframes I work, I never see blank pages before
my output, and I always start with a print after advancing page.  I
expect the system knows that the first print is on the first page.   But
what we don't have (that I know of), is the ability to pick front or
back side of a sheet.  I don't think the system KNOWS we're printing on
two sided paper.  Probably because I don't print to paper, I print to a
queue from which the paper and printer pull the data to be printed.  It
can go to green bar or to laser at print time.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vknm5$lnk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7vh5or$mj4$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <7vi1dl$g9u$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381DAE50.17FC79FD@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@nospamhome.com> wrote:

: Interesting.  On the mainframes I work, I never see blank pages before
: my output, and I always start with a print after advancing page.  I
: expect the system knows that the first print is on the first page.   But

Or perhaps it is set to suppress completely blank pages.

: what we don't have (that I know of), is the ability to pick front or
: back side of a sheet.  I don't think the system KNOWS we're printing on
: two sided paper.  Probably because I don't print to paper, I print to a
: queue from which the paper and printer pull the data to be printed.  It
: can go to green bar or to laser at print time.

Most machines that I use also go via a queue because this allows
several users to print to the same printer at the same time and
then have them dequeued.  However, I usually have the queues set
to print nothing except what is sent to it.  This way I can control
exactly how the printers are to behave.

It is also important to have all software working the same.  Most
Wordprocessors will have no page throw at start and will have a
page throw at end.  Other software the same.

Also, like wordprocessors et al, I have initialisation of printers
built into my programs (via common routines) so when the user 
chooses which printer to use (or the program chooses it for him)
it can be initialised as portrait or landscape.

It is important that this initialisation is done as part of the
report - before the first heading - if it is done as a separate
process it can be printed from the queue at a different time.
ie someone else's initialisation and report can get between
them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3822CC78.CE8A76EC@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7vh5or$mj4$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <7vi1dl$g9u$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381DAE50.17FC79FD@NOSPAMhome.com> <7vknm5$lnk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee <brazee@nospamhome.com> wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Or perhaps it is set to suppress completely blank pages.

On IMB mainframes, VS/VSE/MVS/ESA and all htat follows/preceeded, I remember
that we indeed always started with "WRITE OUTLINE FROM HEADING-1 AFTER ADVANCING
PAGE". This was due to and IF in the print-sub-routine that tested IF
LINECOUNTER > PAGEMAX which was forced true the first time with VALUE 99 for the
counter.

The output however was never preceeded with a blank page. After the separator
page, containing userid/jobid a.s.o, always immediately the first output page
followed. I almost know for sure the print-queue manager (aka the spolling
subsystem) recognized that if the very first character in the print file was the
"SKIP PAGE"  character that was ignored to prevent a blank page.

But if one wrote "WRITE blankline AFTER PAGE" twice as the first two writes, you
surely got a blank page.

A report never was ended with a page skip, because that was unnecessary. The
aforementioned spooling system did always insert a page skip and a separator
page between reports.

Good old days.

Simultaniously Peripheral Operations On-Line - SPOOL. Who remembers?

Old Frog
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vv8jv$qee$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7vh5or$mj4$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <7vi1dl$g9u$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381DAE50.17FC79FD@NOSPAMhome.com> <7vknm5$lnk$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3822CC78.CE8A76EC@IMN.nl>`

```
The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@imn.nl> wrote:
B
: subsystem) recognized that if the very first character in the print file was the
: "SKIP PAGE"  character that was ignored to prevent a blank page.

: A report never was ended with a page skip, because that was unnecessary. The
: aforementioned spooling system did always insert a page skip and a separator

If this was done on PC systems with no spooling, or minimal
spooling then the reports would be separated, but it would
have to wait for the 2nd report to throw the page so the
report could be removed, or have manual intervention.

I recall changing my pagination logic when going from mainframes
to micros in the late 70's.  We certainly didn't have spoolers
on those 8bit machines.  It was also necessary to check status
on opening the printer because it may be in use (it was
multiuser) and ensure the printer was closed as soon as possible.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page?

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dHBU3.30636$23.1618125@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```

The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl> wrote in message
> The output however was never preceeded with a blank page. After the
separator
> page, containing userid/jobid a.s.o, always immediately the first output
page
> followed. I almost know for sure the print-queue manager (aka the spolling
> subsystem) recognized that if the very first character in the print file
was the
> "SKIP PAGE"  character that was ignored to prevent a blank page.
>
> But if one wrote "WRITE blankline AFTER PAGE" twice as the first two
writes, you
> surely got a blank page.
>
> A report never was ended with a page skip, because that was unnecessary.
The
> aforementioned spooling system did always insert a page skip and a
separator
> page between reports.

We could have used a spooler at a previous job.  As a personal standard I
wrote my first header after 0 lines and ended any printout with a blank line
AFTER PAGE.  Not all programs were written that way and printouts were
ruined more than once when the previous job didn't finish with a final form
feed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers and column headings on each page?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Z4aU3.579$GH5.21121@viper>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7vh5or$mj4$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <7vi1dl$g9u$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <381DAE50.17FC79FD@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
>what we don't have (that I know of), is the ability to pick front or
>back side of a sheet.  I don't think the system KNOWS we're printing on
>two sided paper.  Probably because I don't print to paper, I print to a


Any duplex printing I have ever done assumes you are starting from the front
of a page. Thus to print starting on the back of a page would be done by
using the appropriate printer settings, typically the FORMS command from
mainframes. Further, printing a blank page as the first page to get to the
back page to start the printing.

I have done this type of thing on both mainframe and PC printers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vkosr$lto$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net> <%76T3.1911$iS.87782@viper> <381DB160.293ADB06@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
: Gumbo wrote:
:> 
:> My preferred method would be to use a conditional:
:> 
:> 05 LINE-COUNT PIC X(003) VALUE ZERO USAGE BINARY.
:>     88 IN-BODY-OF-PAGE VALUES 1 THROUGH 40.
:> *************************************************** VALUE 40 TO ALLOW FOR
:> BUFFER AT BOTTOM OF PAGE
:>     88 PAGE-BREAK VALUES 41 THROUGH 999.

I dislike condition names (88 levels).  Though they appear to
make the code more readable when the code is written they make
it difficult to manage large projects later.

Given a typical system with dozens to hundreds of source files
it is usual (for me) to locate programs that use particular data
items by greping for them.  In this particular case I may do a
'grep Line-Count *.CBL *.PD >linec.grp'  then pick up the
output file in the editor.  This contains the file names so
I can jump from there to each file.

When 88s are used I also have to grep for each of these to
find all places where the data item is used.

I find it preferable to have a variable such as 'line-limit'
which is set to the default.  These items are in a copybook
'printer.ws' along with other items related to report printing.

In particular cases the 'line-limit' can be reset by the program
dynamically which an 88 level cannot.  This gives more
flexability in the common print routines.

For example I can set line-limit to zero to suppress page throws
(setting heading-count to zero suppresses headings).  It may
aslo be that if a landscape printer is selected I need to
change line-limit to around 52, while it can be 64 for the
same device in portrait mode.

I can also grep for where line-limit is changed with
grep " to line-limit" *.pd *.cbl.

(thinks: hmmm what if I did a compute: 'grep "t[eo] line-limit" ..')
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Useful regular expressions (was Code to print page numbers...)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YcmT3.17288$23.953051@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net> <%76T3.1911$iS.87782@viper> <381DB160.293ADB06@NOSPAMhome.com> <7vkosr$lto$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message
news:7vkosr$lto$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...

> I can also grep for where line-limit is changed with
> grep " to line-limit" *.pd *.cbl.
>
> (thinks: hmmm what if I did a compute: 'grep "t[eo] line-limit" ..')

When checking to see what can modify a specific data name, I took these
reserved words and looked for the last two characters of them:

COMPUTE
FROM
TO
INTO
GIVING
ACCEPT

The regular expression when I am in "vi" looks like
/[TONP][EOMGT]  *LINE-LIMIT/        There are two spaces in front of the "*"
to request at least one space before the item I'm searching for.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Useful regular expressions (was Code to print page numbers...)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ezEeOIDdfbxMX9LSH3CVaV30jzSQ@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net> <%76T3.1911$iS.87782@viper> <381DB160.293ADB06@NOSPAMhome.com> <7vkosr$lto$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <YcmT3.17288$23.953051@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
The venerable "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com> bestowed
upon comp.lang.cobol on Mon, 1 Nov 1999 13:53:24 -0600 these words:

>When checking to see what can modify a specific data name, I took these
>reserved words and looked for the last two characters of them:
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>to request at least one space before the item I'm searching for.

This is useful but incomplete.  What about:

MULTIPLY A BY B
DIVIDE A BY B
SET A UP BY 1
CALL "SUBROUTINE" USING A

Also, the updated field can come before the keyword (WRITE or
RELEASE), and more than one token after the keyword:

ADD 1 TO A
         B
         C.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<IjET3.21280$23.1108017@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net> illuminated comp.lang.cobol thusly:
> >When checking to see what can modify a specific data name, I took these
> >reserved words and looked for the last two characters of them:
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> >/[TONP][EOMGT]  *LINE-LIMIT/        There are two spaces in front of the
"*"
> >to request at least one space before the item I'm searching for.
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>          C.

It's hardly the only tool I use, but it's a useful first step.  Any tool
used to scan source code can fail when it meets esoteric or obfuscated code,
but as long as the user is aware of these limitations the tool can still be
useful.  The exceptions test the rule.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vod48$b3i@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net> <%76T3.1911$iS.87782@viper> <381DB160.293ADB06@NOSPAMhome.com> <7vkosr$lto$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
I prefer:

PRINT-ENTRY.
    IF LINE-COUNT < MAX-LINES
       CONTINUE
    ELSE
        PERFORM PAGE-HEADING
    END-IF.

Jeff



---------- you posted:
PRINT-ENTRY.
    IF (LINE-COUNT NOT < MAX-LINES)
        PERFORM PAGE-HEADING
    END-IF.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<619U3.533$GH5.20097@viper>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net> <%76T3.1911$iS.87782@viper> <381DB160.293ADB06@NOSPAMhome.com> <7vkosr$lto$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
All good points. And I agree with them.

I just never had a need for changing orientation of a report or modifying it
in the ways you mentioned. So I geuuss I pickked up a little bad habit.

With larger more complex reporting programs, and possibly multiple reports,
it would definitely be better to set all of the margins, header, body and
trailer as well as normal page headers, into fields which can be easily
modified. Especially in the development phase.

However I rarely, not in several years, have had to write a report in COBOL.
I have found it much easier, for simpler reports, to do them with SyncSort
and even Dyl280 and EazyTrieve.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3821975E.A547DF91@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net> <%76T3.1911$iS.87782@viper> <381DB160.293ADB06@NOSPAMhome.com> <7vkosr$lto$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <619U3.533$GH5.20097@viper>`

```
Gumbo wrote:
> 
> However I rarely, not in several years, have had to write a report in COBOL.

> I have found it much easier, for simpler reports, to do them with SyncSort
> and even Dyl280 and EazyTrieve.

It is.  But Cobol is pretty easy too for experienced programmers.

For medium complexity, SyncSort drops out, and occasionally even
EasyTrieve is not sufficient (I am not familiar with Dy1280).  I will
also eschew EasyTrieve if the complexity is beyond what most maintenance
programmers understand.

When you get complex enough, the tool of choice changes.  


BTW, I still think most report languages need to be replaced or changed
to produce a variation of a HTML protocol, which allows for run-time
switching of media, page formatting, font size, and even headers
controlled by the user.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WX8U3.529$GH5.20166@viper>`

```
>I like that.  All except the period bit.
I didn't like it at first either. But experience with other programming
styles which use them have showed me the wisdom of not using them.

It forces the use of scope terminators. In my opinion, never a bad thing.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382195CD.FD1D8572@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net> <%76T3.1911$iS.87782@viper> <381DB160.293ADB06@NOSPAMhome.com> <WX8U3.529$GH5.20166@viper>`

```
Gumbo wrote:
> 
> >I like that.  All except the period bit.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> It forces the use of scope terminators. In my opinion, never a bad thing.

Our standards won't allow dangling periods.  I don't care for that
standard, but that's OK.  Unfortunately, we don't yet have a standard
forcing scope terminators.  Maybe they will consider that now that all
ANSI 68 & 72 is being converted.

The IBM converter software messes up when we have COPY code without
periods.

We also have a pre-compiler which puts in its own non-terminated IF
logic.  The only safe way of handling this is to finish it with a
period.

To me, a standard requiring some scope terminators - at least all IF
statements, is a good idea in a shop which enforces standards (ours
does).  Trouble is, a lot of our code is purchased and can't be changed
if we want the supplied fixes, so we use their standards as well.

My personal opinion is that END-IF should be mandatory where possible,
and periods should be encouraged where it makes the full-stop more
clear.
```
