###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3821D163.6E75CD7A@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net> <%76T3.1911$iS.87782@viper> <381DB160.293ADB06@NOSPAMhome.com> <WX8U3.529$GH5.20166@viper> <382195CD.FD1D8572@NOSPAMhome.com> <7vsj1e$t5$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote in message
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>     wmklein <at> ix dot netcom dot com

* WHEN THE CONVERTER ENCOUNTERS A COPY IDMS RECORD-NAME STATEMENT
  WITHOUT A PERIOD AT THE END, IT INSERTS THE PERIOD.  UNFORTUNATELY
  IT ALSO DELETES THE FOLLOWING LINE, WHICH THEN MUST BE PUT BACK
  MANUALLY.  FOR EXAMPLE:
  IT CHANGED
   COPY IDMS RECORD IADWRACT.
   COPY IDMS RECORD IADMR651          <== NOTE NO PERIOD
   COPY IDMS RECORD IADWRPLN.
   COPY IDMS IADRCCR.
  TO
   COPY IDMS RECORD IADWRACT.
   COPY IDMS RECORD IADMR651.         <== NOTE ADDED A PERIOD
                                      <== NOTE DELETED LINE
   COPY IDMS IADRCCR.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ CCCA, IDMS, and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vskt4$jj3$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net> <%76T3.1911$iS.87782@viper> <381DB160.293ADB06@NOSPAMhome.com> <WX8U3.529$GH5.20166@viper> <382195CD.FD1D8572@NOSPAMhome.com> <7vsj1e$t5$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <3821D163.6E75CD7A@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Was that COPY statement (without the period) previously handled by a
preprocessor?  or did it "get to" the OS/VS COBOL compiler for resolution?
If so, I think you must have had some "modification" to your OS/VS COBOL
compiler for that ever to compile "cleanly".

The CCCA does provide "source code" for its conversion routines.  I would
think that you could modify the one that deletes the line following the
missing period.  (Check the documentation on "modifying LCPs") Better yet,
what does IBM support say about this?  The CCCA is no a "program product"
(not just program offering) and I think their support should be able to help
you.

*IF* the IDMS product actually handled the COPY statement *before* it ever
made it to the IBM compiler (via a preprocessor), then I can see IBM not
providing support for this "non-Standard" feature.  However, if you ask, they
may still consider it - as IDMS is not that unusual in COBOL conversions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: CCCA, IDMS, and COPY statements

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3821E6CB.ABF0422B@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net> <%76T3.1911$iS.87782@viper> <381DB160.293ADB06@NOSPAMhome.com> <WX8U3.529$GH5.20166@viper> <382195CD.FD1D8572@NOSPAMhome.com> <7vsj1e$t5$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <3821D163.6E75CD7A@NOSPAMhome.com> <7vskt4$jj3$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
It was handled by a preprocessor.  I hadn't noticed that - I cut and
pasted this from a document of things to watch out for which have
occurred in our shop.

But you're right - IDMS isn't that unusual.   We don't mind when the
processor tells us to handle ' ON ' clauses ourself, we certainly don't
want the converter to convert them when they are really IDMS
commands!!!  (obtain this-record on db-any-error perform error-routine).

William M. Klein wrote:
> 
> Was that COPY statement (without the period) previously handled by a
…[55 more quoted lines elided]…
> >    COPY IDMS IADRCCR.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381DB0D7.E4475817@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7vh2le$vg0$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <p3XS3.7094$DM3.67389@news4.mia> <78XS3.1717$UG4.14861@newsfeed.slurp.net> <381C6159.52E54F35@NOSPAMhome.com> <OzZS3.7490$9H3.77432@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> Astonishment. :-)

I guess I am so used to this, that I don't see it being astonishing. 
The condition I am testing for is "should I print the header", not
"should I skip printing the header".  I am seeing our methods:
mine
Increment line counter.
If line counter > max, print header, set line counter.
Print detail

vs yours
If line counter => max, print header, set line counter.
Print detail
Increment counter.

Not much difference, I'm used to the former, but I'll agree the latter
looks a bit cleaner.

One advantage to your method though is that people often start off with:
     01  LINE-COUNTER   pic 99  value 99.
If they do that (bad practice), my code will increment it to 00, yours
won't.  Yours will work, mine won't.
```

---

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize statement on MVS Cobol

- **From:** "Barvin" <barvin@mwis.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QmsU3.1441$yF6.10058@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
I use initialize pretty extensively and its been my experience (acucobol)
that initialize does work on FILLER if it has a VALUE assigned.
i.e.  FILLER    PIC X(100) VALUE SPACES.
it will initialize to the value stated, otherwise, yes, it does skip FILLER

David
barvin@mwis.net
The Barvin Web Site
http://barvin.mwis.net

news:W9ZS3.7447$9H3.75550@news3.mia...
> The FILLER will not be initialized.  If the other fields are not being
> initialized, there is a problem with your initialize statement (I don't
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize statement on MVS Cobol

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vv98t$qee$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<381C5BC3.5508B118@sympatico.ca> <W9ZS3.7447$9H3.75550@news3.mia> <QmsU3.1441$yF6.10058@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Barvin <barvin@mwis.net> wrote:
: I use initialize pretty extensively and its been my experience (acucobol)
: that initialize does work on FILLER if it has a VALUE assigned.
: i.e.  FILLER    PIC X(100) VALUE SPACES.
: it will initialize to the value stated, otherwise, yes, it does skip FILLER

Aren't you confusing initial value on loading with the effects
of the INITIALISE statement.

The VALUE cllause will ensure that this is set to spaces when
the program is loaded.  If the program does an INITIALISE
before any other statements have changed that area then it
certainly will be spaces.

However, this does not mean that the INITIALISE will set
that item _to_ spaces.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize statement on MVS Cobol

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vuaek$k02$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<381C5BC3.5508B118@sympatico.ca> <W9ZS3.7447$9H3.75550@news3.mia> <QmsU3.1441$yF6.10058@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
If you have an item defined as:

  05  Group1.
       10 Filler Pic X(10) value "1234567890".
        10 Othr Pic X.

and do
    Move all "X" to Group1
    INITIALIZE of GROUP1.

You better NOT get the first 10 bytes changed from X's to numbers (or
spaces). If you do, your compiler has a BUG.  (There will be a way in the
next Standard to "initialize to value" which will "reset" the 1st 10 bytes to
their initial values.)
```

#### ↳ Re: Initialize statement on MVS Cobol

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vj9ku$csh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<381C5BC3.5508B118@sympatico.ca>`

```
In article <381C5BC3.5508B118@sympatico.ca>,
  =?iso-8859-1?Q?Jean=2DRen=E9?= Duval <jean-ren.duval@sympatico.ca>
wrote:
> Hi all.
>
>   Last week I had a problem with an intialize statement . It looked
like
> this.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>    Before doing any read in my program I'm doing an intialize
statement
> instead of the usual perform statement with spaces for a alphanumeric
an
> zeroes for a numeric to save time.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>  I noticed after some testing, that the array was not intialize at
all.
> Leaving me with no choice then using the old fashion way of
> initializing  variables.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>

What's so "old fashioned" with moving SPACES to the group level and
moving ZERO to the Packed-Decimal field ? This is MUCH more efficient
that an INITIALIZE statement, which will generate several more
instructions. The old fashioned method will ALSO initialize the FILLER.
The INITIALIZE verb has its place, but should be used with caution as
it can potentially generate an extrodinary number of instructions in an
IBM Big-Iron (MVS/VSE) environment.

Cheers,

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Initialize statement on MVS Cobol

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381DB522.AF25B948@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<381C5BC3.5508B118@sympatico.ca> <7vj9ku$csh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
WOB Consulting wrote:

> What's so "old fashioned" with moving SPACES to the group level and
> moving ZERO to the Packed-Decimal field ? This is MUCH more efficient
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> IBM Big-Iron (MVS/VSE) environment.

And even more importantly - the INITIALIZE command is a work in
progress, and obviously is not well understood by enough programmers.  I
will pay for extra instructions for ease of maintenance - but it's not
there yet.
```

=========================text=============================
Subject: Re: API calls from MF OO COBOL
Date: Thu, 9 Sep 1999 07:46:32 -0400
From: "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
Organization: MindSpring Enterprises
Newsgroups: comp.lang.cobol

   01  wsProcessControlWorkareas.
           05  wsWaitResult            dword value 0.
           05  wsStartupInfo.
               10  wsStartupInfoLength word value 66.
               10  filler              pointer value NULL.
               10  filler              pointer value NULL.
               10  filler              pointer value NULL.
               10  filler              dword value 0.
               10  filler              dword value 0.
               10  filler              dword value 0.
               10  filler              dword value 0.
               10  filler              dword value 0.
               10  filler              dword value 0.
               10  filler              dword value 0.
               10  filler              dword value 0.
               10  filler              word value 0.
               10  filler              word value 0.
               10  filler              pointer value NULL.
               10  filler              handle value 0.
               10  filler              handle value 0.
               10  filler              handle value 0.
           05  wsNotePadProcessInfo.
               10  hNotePad           handle value 0.
               10  hNotePadThread     handle value 0.
               10  wsNotePadProcessID dword value 0.
               10  wsNotepadThreadID  dword value 0.

       Call WINAPI 'CreateProcessA'
             using by value 0
                   by reference z'Notepad.EXE C:\Readme.txt'
                   by value 0
                   by value 0
                   by value 1
                   by value 0
                   by value 0
                   by value 0
                   by reference wsStartupInfo
                   by reference wsNotePadProcessInfo
             returning winBoolResponse.

           Call WINAPI 'WaitForInputIdle'
             using by value hNotePad
                      by value -1
             returning inBoolResponse.
=========================end==============================
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Hey Grumpy Old Men

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yghT3.16218$23.909596@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<7vhad4$70d$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <325778775638E8A3.E033AFADDFCAFB30.E1006852BE6EA718@lp.airnews.net>`

```

SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote in message
news:325778775638E8A3.E033AFADDFCAFB30.E1006852BE6EA718@lp.airnews.net...
> On Sun, 31 Oct 1999 12:50:32 +0100, "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
> enlightened us:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> grumpiness intended.

Amen to that.  One assumes the poster knows something about their problem
coming in.  If a student needs help knowing how a process works and someone
suggests an algorithm to handle it, the student should still be responsible
for coding it.  Posting an entire working program to do the job is, as you
say, a great disservice to the student.
```

#### ↳ Re: Hey Grumpy Old Men

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iS6T3.31709$oa2.28992@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<7vhad4$70d$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
In article <7vhad4$70d$1@news.inet.tele.dk>, Ib Tanding <ib@tanding.dk> wrote:


[snippage]

>Though it hurts me that students often are
>treated like dirt and that is the reason for the title.

It does not hurt me when someone asks me to do their homework for them...
but I guess I am made of Sterner Stuff.

>
>If you think about what you do, when you have a problem. You will consult
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>so I think that it is important to let them in.

The 'price of admission' might be more than a mere question... it might
include a showing of What Has Been Done.  (I've noticed that when *that*
happens the response is a little different).

>
>It is important because we owe it to the language. We owe it to the next
>generation. What is expierience if nobody can benefit from it?

I question the benefit of showing that someone else will do your homework
for you if you only ask... but I guess I am from the Olde Schoole.

>
>I think we need a 'greenhouse'-newsgroup if we cannot treat students
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Think about it and let us see your opinion.

As my Drill Sergeant told us, long ago, about opinions... no, never mind
that.  From what I have seen... when a student shows a bit of effort an
answer is usually provided faster than I can code one out.  When a student
shows effort such as 'How do I make a timer-object... and I cannot show
code because someone will steal it' then I say 'do your own homework.
please'.

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Hey Grumpy Old Men

- **From:** "xyz" <Suze.V@online.be>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vjppc$4fr$1@trex.antw.online.be>`
- **References:** `<7vhad4$70d$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <iS6T3.31709$oa2.28992@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
I'm a young IT-student. I like to program and find cobol rather a fun
language, even if in my eyes, it looks rather compact in comparison with
other languages.  A week ago I, asked a question in this newsgroup (the one
about the timer), I was rather na�f and didn't know that the prhasing of my
sentence collided with the opinions of several cobol-programmers.  In
contrast to what many of you think, I'm not a student (and I think many of
us are, but not all is good, there are student who aren't), we want to learn
and we do not intend that you should provide us with a source file, but with
a hint or a simular synthax involving to help us on the way.
For those who I have offended by asking a rather basis, without background,
question; "I apologize.", I wasn't my intention to come over to you as
rather rude, but it was the lack of experience. But every experience is an
experience that you are richer.

In this week that I've been reading, I've very well learned a lot about
everything, not only cobol. If found most of the entries to be interesting
and educational.  I'll keep on reading them...

p.s. to docdwarf
And as reply on "the other students will then steal my project..."; for the
moment I've got a very unic source file, which no other studens in my school
have.  This is a project that I've been working on for weeks, I'm quite
(rather a lot) proud of it, and I hate to find out that, if I posted it, an
other student copied it and my project (where all my time and hard spent
work went into) wouldn't be 'my OWN' project.  That's why I was being this
protective of it ... hope you'll understand.  And what you always refer to
'do your own homework'; if it's for cobol there aren't many students who can
say they've spent the same amount of time in class, behind my books, looking
in the help-file of my compiler, looking in cobol-books in the library and
looking on the internet ... also on this newsgroup ...
```

Well, that certainly clears things up... I think.  But onwards to the

[snippolinio]

>p.s. to docdwarf

…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>protective of it ... hope you'll understand.

One of the things I understand is 'modularised code'; if you did not want
to post your entire project then you might have, as someone else did, post
only the paragraphs which related to the timer-sequence.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hey Grumpy Old Men

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381D9D02.6F076056@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7vhad4$70d$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <iS6T3.31709$oa2.28992@iad-read.news.verio.net> <7vjppc$4fr$1@trex.antw.online.be>`

```
xyz wrote:
> p.s. to docdwarf
> And as reply on "the other students will then steal my project...";
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> out that, if I posted it, an other student copied it and my project 

This is a show me the money question.  Like in University you must
show that you have learned something.  The fact that the result is not
perfect is not the issue just the direction is there.

If you have a problem paraphrasing the problem without code often will
not give sufficient detail to work out what the problem really is.
Post a code snippet (10 or 20 lines will not hurt it must fit into the
whole process after all).  It will show us a direction and prove that
you are not blindly asking for your assignments without thought.

The vertically challenged doctor is in but he likes to make a correct
diagnosis,
Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Hey Grumpy Old Men

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uzyqVsLJ$GA.118@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<7vhad4$70d$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
The degree of "grumpiness" in each newsgroup is different, mostly related to
how much people have tried to abuse the services offered there.

Over in the comp.databases.ms-access newsgroup, I've seen the most blatant
newbie questions answered with calm replies, even though some other newbie
asked the same question the day before.

I've seen the same sort of annoyed attitude witnessed here on both the C and
C++ newsgroups.

It really is a matter of accumulated newsgroup experience.

Though I definitely weigh in on the side of politeness.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Hey Grumpy Old Men

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vmvsu$h10$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7vhad4$70d$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <uzyqVsLJ$GA.118@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
I am not sure if telling a student to do their own homework is grumpyness.
Most of the posters in here have children, and a good number have
grandchildren (as do I).  Telling some teenager to go away and do their own
homework, as opposed to doing it for them, is simply good parenting. The
only grumpiness involved may be in making too fast a judgement as to who is
a kid trying for a free ride, and who is a hard worker trying to get info.
After thirty years of programming, it can be hard to tell the young from the
in-experienced.


ChrisOsborne wrote in message ...
>The degree of "grumpiness" in each newsgroup is different, mostly related
to
>how much people have tried to abuse the services offered there.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>I've seen the same sort of annoyed attitude witnessed here on both the C
and
>C++ newsgroups.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Hey Grumpy Old Men

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381F5FEA.FA4212A2@home.com>`

```


xyz wrote:
> 
> To all of the senior programmers, give us 'rookies' a little bit of respect,
> most of us are here to learn, I think only a small factor wants to abuse
> your knowledge, but you can't let that come to harm with the 'good'
> students.

The only true way that we know you are legit. is if you post some source
code to show you have made some effort towards solving the problem.
Doesn't matter if you approach is wrong, but it shows you tried - and be
sure, on that basis you will get help.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

> To all of the senior programmers, give us 'rookies' a little bit of respect,
> most of us are here to learn, I think only a small factor wants to abuse
> your knowledge, but you can't let that come to harm with the 'good'
> students.

I think our grumpyness has more to do with other "old men" not giving
what we feel is the appropriate hint level.  When a student asks for
help, the range of answers can go from "do your homework", to coding the
program, and everything in between.

For the student to learn the best, the answer should be the minimum
amount of clues to enable the student to come up with the solution.  By
the nature of this medium, determining this "minimum" is not obvious
within the time-lines we have to work - but we can make assumptions.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Hey Grumpy Old Men

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pkIT3.3238$B15.34991@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<7vhad4$70d$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <7vmjqi$3po$1@trex.antw.online.be> <381EF6DB.BF219684@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
>For the student to learn the best, the answer should be the minimum
>amount of clues to enable the student to come up with the solution.  By
>the nature of this medium, determining this "minimum" is not obvious
>within the time-lines we have to work - but we can make assumptions.

Howard, you have expressed what appears to be the 'conventional wisdom'
around here about helping newbies. :)  In most areas of learning, I would
agree.  But my own experience is that I learned *greatly* from seeing
how other programmers solved problems.  Programming is a mixture between
science, craft and art.  Do we expect writers, for example, to derive,
by themselves, all the principles of good writing?  No, we inundate them
with good writing examples, and explanations of why writing is good or
bad.  When we learn to program, we are learning much more than just 'how
to write code'.  We should be learning 'how to write great code'.  Unless
we expect every newbie programmer to reinvent all the wheels from
scratch, we should be giving them a lot of input on techniques for doing
things.  Every very good programmer I know has at some point spent a lot
of time examining other's code.  If the student is lazy and unmotivated
enough to use the code and fail to assimilate the techniques, they aren't
going very far anyway.  In the final analysis, a good programmer is going
to be motivated to learn all they can.  I believe giving them a well
thought out solution to a problem they are much interested in can be very
beneficial. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: recursion in cobol

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381d178f.38359751@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<381D0FEA.70605043@attglobal.net>`

```
On Sun, 31 Oct 1999 22:58:34 -0500, "H. D'Mello"
<dmello@attglobal.net> wrote:

>Is there a way to do recursion in cobol as it is done in the c language
>or
>pl/1 or smalltalk?
>Thanks.
>

Yes and No.

The standard PRESENTLY does not allow for it, but the proposed
standard does.  

Many (but not all) COBOL compilers on the market today offer a
recursion method - each is different.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: recursion in cobol

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fFnT3.1119$aS6.11128@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<381D0FEA.70605043@attglobal.net> <_emT3.838$w9.118486@news.flash.net>`

```
Steve Kalemkiewicz wrote:
>what is the benefit of recursion?

Some algorithms are more clearly and simply expressed recursively (e.g.
Quicksort).  Recursion is not strictly *necessary*, but it can be very
helpful.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: recursion in cobol

- **From:** "Bill Wood" <beavis27@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e0oXsvOJ$GA.199@cpmsnbbsa02>`
- **References:** `<381D0FEA.70605043@attglobal.net> <_emT3.838$w9.118486@news.flash.net>`

```
Here's an example of some code that we use.  Our application requires the
user enter a temporary directory.  Let's say they choose "c:\windows\Program
Files\Local Apps\Our App\Temp Directory" as the temporary directory.  I need
to know if this directory exists and if not I need to create it, but the
problem is you can only create one level deep at a time and since this
directory has multiple parts each part must be checked until I get to the
first directory in the path or I find a directory that exists.  So, the
solution is to pass into the routine a directory structure.  The routine
determines if the directory exists.  If so it returns with a good status,
otherwise it calls itself and continues to do so until it gets to the first
directory or finds one that exists.  If it has called itself, when it
returns it creates the directory and exits.  The result is I only write one
piece of code to create the direcotry and one piece to check for the
existence of a directory, and I don't care how many subdirectories the user
enters because the routine just keeps calling itself.  You could manage this
by parsing the original directory and counting the pieces and performing a
loop x number of times, but the recursion is more elegant and more
straightforward.

bill

Steve Kalemkiewicz wrote in message <_emT3.838$w9.118486@news.flash.net>...
>what is the benefit of recursion?
>
>-SPK
>
>
```

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: recursion in cobol

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381fad13.100359717@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<381D0FEA.70605043@attglobal.net> <_emT3.838$w9.118486@news.flash.net>`

```
On Mon, 01 Nov 1999 19:55:38 GMT, "Steve Kalemkiewicz"
<senseisk@hotmail.com> wrote:

>what is the benefit of recursion?
>

I use it with a common data entry routine.  The data entry routine has
a button you can press to see a graphical tree view of the data.  From
there, you can click on a data item and edit it with the same data
entry routine.  I don't want to lose my original place, or distribute
several "copies" of the same program with different names, and I want
the original entry screen displayed and available while the "second"
is up.  Recursion is the only way to go.  

Right now, I am fighting figuring out how to do this with Fujitsu, and
have just decided to wait for conversion until v5 is released
(reportedly WITH recursion for basic COBOL programs).
---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

#### ↳ Re: recursion in cobol

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vnmlj$sqq$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<381D0FEA.70605043@attglobal.net>`

```
H. D'Mello <dmello@attglobal.net> wrote:
: Is there a way to do recursion in cobol as it is done in the c language
: or
: pl/1 or smalltalk?
: Thanks.

The 'Programmer's Guide to Writing Programs' in MicroFocus Object
Cobol for Unix shows how to do recursion - ie it does support
this.  This, however, is supplier dependent.  Different versions
may or may not support recursion.

Anything that can be done recursively can be done iteritively,
usually faster.  The advantage of a recursive routine is that
it 'looks after' the storing of each recursion's local data
in the stack frame.  To convert to iteritive it is necessary
to build and look after your own stack and move up and down
this with each iteration.
```

<a id="t-09d6b9991f"></a>
## Thread: MF COMP-5 (was MF COBOL and IBM data types) — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### MF COMP-5 (was MF COBOL and IBM data types)

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381E6500.1BA5C681@techie.com>`

```
Russell Styles wrote:
> 
>     As I understand it, comp-5 is supposed to be a system dependant format,
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> format.

OK, I'll bite.

Why would a compiler *not* use the native system format for BINARY
or COMP data in the first place, thus requiring a COMP-5 to get
next to the hardware?


Regards,
Jared Thomas
mailto:jared@techie.com
http://www.radiks.net/~jmthomas
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COMP-5 (was MF COBOL and IBM data types)

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vmd6n$hbp$4@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<381E6500.1BA5C681@techie.com>`

```
    Portability is the most likely reason.  Most systems (many?) seem to use
the Motorola layout for binary data.  This allows systems to exchange binary
data, it makes sorting (unsigned binary at least) easier, it allows normal
humans to look at the hex value of a comp (binary) item.

    Whomever thought up the Intel binary format must have been on drugs,
IMHO.

    Of course, this would slow down math somewhat.  Now that 32 bit and even
64 bit math can be done on native data, it might be helpfull to use COMP-5
more often.


Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com> wrote in message
news:381E6500.1BA5C681@techie.com...
> Russell Styles wrote:
> >
> >     As I understand it, comp-5 is supposed to be a system dependant
format,
> > normally used for
> > speed purposes.  Ie, on an Intel box running dos/windows comp-5 would be
in
> > intel endian format,
> > ie backwards.  On the other hand the other binary items are in non-intel
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.radiks.net/~jmthomas
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COMP-5 (was MF COBOL and IBM data types)

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vmd2b$rb8$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<381E6500.1BA5C681@techie.com>`

```

Jared Thomas wrote in message <381E6500.1BA5C681@techie.com>...
>
>Why would a compiler *not* use the native system format for BINARY
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Jared,
        In a word: portability (or compatibility). If you share data it
needs to be in a consistent format. Consider a system where you have Intel
and non-Intel clients sharing data. If the data was stored in native format
there would be serious problems sharing that data.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF COMP-5 (was MF COBOL and IBM data types)

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381F618A.6BE92506@home.com>`
- **References:** `<381E6500.1BA5C681@techie.com> <7vmd2b$rb8$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```


Gael Wilson wrote:
> 
> Jared Thomas wrote in message <381E6500.1BA5C681@techie.com>...
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> there would be serious problems sharing that data.

And about compatibility. When Mr. Gates issued his last-but-one COBOL 
he warned don't use Comp-5 in files. Then when he issued the last (which 
was MicroFocus) - he commented "See...?".

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF COMP-5 (was MF COBOL and IBM data types)

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s55U3.724$eJ4.21447@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<381E6500.1BA5C681@techie.com> <7vmd2b$rb8$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```

Gael Wilson <gael.wilson@merant.com

> Jared,
>         In a word: portability (or compatibility). If you share data
it
> needs to be in a consistent format.

Yes, BUT, if you use COMP-5 internally for indexes, counters,
and the like, AND don't share these internal variables,
why not get the 50% (or 10% or 1%) increase in efficiency?

And, of course, calling system functions may mandate that
you use the hardware-native formats.

So there are uses for COMP-5.
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COMP-5 (was MF COBOL and IBM data types)

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vmvsv$h10$2@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<381E6500.1BA5C681@techie.com>`

```
Because the same Cobol program often runs on multiple platforms.

Jared Thomas wrote in message <381E6500.1BA5C681@techie.com>...
>Russell Styles wrote:
>>
>>     As I understand it, comp-5 is supposed to be a system dependant
format,
>> normally used for
>> speed purposes.  Ie, on an Intel box running dos/windows comp-5 would be
in
>> intel endian format,
>> ie backwards.  On the other hand the other binary items are in non-intel
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>http://www.radiks.net/~jmthomas
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COMP-5 (was MF COBOL and IBM data types)

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<brru1sor1joit17tkfmq9dkmg06s0k9fmv@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<381E6500.1BA5C681@techie.com>`

```
On Mon, 01 Nov 1999 22:13:52 -0600 Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com> wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>Why would a compiler *not* use the native system format for BINARY
:>or COMP data in the first place, thus requiring a COMP-5 to get
:>next to the hardware?

Because data is shipped between computer systems. 

A record with a COMP field had better be identical on ALL implementations of
COBOL.

Perhaps it is worth defining a non-interchangeable data type for efficiency?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF COMP-5 (was MF COBOL and IBM data types)

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CdLT3.3406$B15.39136@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<381E6500.1BA5C681@techie.com> <brru1sor1joit17tkfmq9dkmg06s0k9fmv@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
>Jared Thomas wrote:
>:>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>COBOL.

Au contraire.  The COBOL 85 standard specifically defines usage COMP
to be implementor defined.  I suspect Micro Focus chose to make COMP
a big-endian binary is to be compatible with IBM mainframes, and they
added COMP-5 to permit efficient use of little-endian binary on Intel
PCs when desired.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF COMP-5 (was MF COBOL and IBM data types)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vrspg$tq9$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

<a id="t-df2e435470"></a>
## Thread: Search Table Problem — 7 msgs

### Search Table Problem

- **From:** "Steve Kalemkiewicz" <senseisk@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lonT3.893$w9.123453@news.flash.net>`

```
I haven't used tables in Cobol before so I decided to take an old QBASIC
program and convert it to Cobol.  After reading my manual and then
converting the code, I ran the program and I don't get the correct output.
I went over the code and even showed it to others, but no one can seem to
figure out what is wrong.

The program is to read the PART NO, and the QTY from a sequential file.  For
every record read the program is to search a table for the PART NO and
retrieve the DESCRIPTION.  If there is no DESCRIPTION found then it is to
indicate so on the Detail Line.  Then the program is to search another table
using the PART NO to find the UNIT PRICE.  If the UNIT PRICE is found then
the TOTAL AMOUNT is calculated by UNIT PRICE * QTY, otherwise TOTAL AMOUNT
is to be blank and UNIT PRICE is to be "*NONE".



*****************
Program Code   (shortened form obviously)
*****************
000170 FILE-CONTROL.
000180     SELECT IN-PART-FILE
000190         ASSIGN TO "C:\BAS5B.DAT"
000200         ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL
000210         ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL.
000220
000230     SELECT OT-PRINT-FILE
000240         ASSIGN TO PRINTER.
000250*
000260*
000270 DATA DIVISION.
000280
000290 FILE SECTION.
000300
000310 FD  IN-PART-FILE
000320     RECORD CONTAINS 8 CHARACTERS
000330     LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD
000340     DATA RECORD IS IN-PART-RECORD.
000350 01  IN-PART-RECORD.
000360     05  IN-NO           PIC X(5).
000370     05  IN-QTY          PIC 9(3).
000380
000390 FD  OT-PRINT-FILE
000400     RECORD CONTAINS 80 CHARACTERS
000410     LABEL RECORDS ARE OMITTED
000420     DATA RECORD IS OT-PRINT-LINE.
000430 01  OT-PRINT-LINE       PIC X(80).
000440
000450 WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
000460
000990

001000 01  DL1-DETAIL-LINE.
001010     05  FILLER          PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
001020     05  DL1-NO          PIC X(5).
001030     05  FILLER          PIC X(6)  VALUE SPACES.
001040     05  DL1-DESCRIPTION PIC X(18).
001050     05  FILLER          PIC X(5)  VALUE SPACES.
001060     05  DL1-QTY         PIC ZZ9.
001070     05  FILLER          PIC X(5)  VALUE SPACES.
001080     05  DL1-PRICE       PIC ZZ.99.
001090     05  FILLER          PIC X(5)  VALUE SPACES.
001100     05  DL1-AMOUNT      PIC Z,ZZZ.99.
001110     05  FILLER          PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
001120
001130 01  DL2-DETAIL-LINE.
001140     05  FILLER          PIC X(10) VALUE SPACES.
001150     05  DL2-NO          PIC X(5).
001160     05  FILLER          PIC X(6)  VALUE SPACES.
001170     05  DL2-DESCRIPTION PIC X(18).
001180     05  FILLER          PIC X(5)  VALUE SPACES.
001190     05  DL2-QTY         PIC ZZ9.
001200     05  FILLER          PIC X(5)  VALUE SPACES.
001210     05  FILLER          PIC X(5)  VALUE "*NONE".
001220     05  FILLER          PIC X(23) VALUE SPACES.
001230
001240 01  TL1-TOTAL-LINE.
001250     05  FILLER          PIC X(9)  VALUE SPACES.
001260     05  FILLER          PIC X(17) VALUE "END OF PRICE LIST".
001270     05  FILLER          PIC X(54) VALUE SPACES.
001280
001290 01  DES-DESC-TABLE-DESCRIPTION.
001300     05  DES-DESC-TABLE-DATA.
001310         10  FILLER      PIC X(20) VALUE "17526BOLT           ".
001320         10  FILLER      PIC X(20) VALUE "18171SCREW          ".
001330         10  FILLER      PIC X(20) VALUE "19226NAIL           ".
001340         10  FILLER      PIC X(20) VALUE "25116NUT            ".
001350         10  FILLER      PIC X(20) VALUE "29258MAGNESIUM COVER".
001360     05  DES-DESC-TABLE      REDEFINES DES-DESC-TABLE-DATA.
001370         10 DES-DESC-ENTRIES OCCURS 5 TIMES
001380                             INDEXED BY DES-TABLE-INDEX.
001390             15  DES-NO          PIC X(5).
001400             15  DES-DESCRIPTION PIC X(15).
001410
001420 01  PRC-PRICE-TABLE-DESCRIPTION.
001430     05  PRC-PRICE-TABLE-DATA.
001440         10  FILLER      PIC X(9)  VALUE "175260126".
001450         10  FILLER      PIC X(9)  VALUE "181710059".
001460         10  FILLER      PIC X(9)  VALUE "192260173".
001470         10  FILLER      PIC X(9)  VALUE "292585843".
001480     05  PRC-PRICE-TABLE     REDEFINES PRC-PRICE-TABLE-DATA.
001490         10  PRC-PRICE-ENTRIES OCCURS 4 TIMES
001500                               INDEXED BY PRC-TABLE-INDEX.
001510             15  PRC-NO          PIC X(5).
001520             15  PRC-PRICE       PIC 99V99.
001530
001540 01  WRK-WORK-AREAS.
001550     05  WRK-DESCRIPTION PIC X(18).
001560     05  WRK-AMOUNT      PIC 9(4)V99.
001570*
001580*
001590 PROCEDURE DIVISION.
001600
001610 0000-MAINLINE.
001620     PERFORM 1000-INITIALIZE.
001630     IF SWT-EOF IS EQUAL TO "NO " THEN
001640         PERFORM 2000-PROCESS
001650             UNTIL SWT-EOF IS EQUAL TO "YES"
001660         PERFORM 3000-TOTALS
001670     ELSE
001680         DISPLAY "NO RECORDS IN FILE"
001690     END-IF.
001700     CLOSE IN-PART-FILE
001710         OT-PRINT-FILE.
001720     STOP RUN.
001730
001740 1000-INITIALIZE.

001840
001850 2000-PROCESS.
001860     IF CON-LINES-USED IS GREATER THAN 50
001870         PERFORM 2100-HEADINGS
001880     END-IF.
001890     SEARCH DES-DESC-ENTRIES
001900         AT END
001910             MOVE "**NO DESCRIPTION**" TO WRK-DESCRIPTION
001920         WHEN DES-NO (DES-TABLE-INDEX) = IN-NO
001930             MOVE DES-DESCRIPTION (DES-TABLE-INDEX)
001940                 TO WRK-DESCRIPTION
001950     END-SEARCH.
001960     SEARCH PRC-PRICE-ENTRIES
001970         AT END
001980             MOVE IN-NO TO DL2-NO
001990             MOVE WRK-DESCRIPTION TO DL2-DESCRIPTION
002000             MOVE IN-QTY TO DL2-QTY
002010             MOVE DL2-DETAIL-LINE TO OT-PRINT-LINE
002020         WHEN PRC-NO (PRC-TABLE-INDEX) = IN-NO
002030             MOVE SPACES TO DL1-DETAIL-LINE
002040             MOVE IN-NO TO DL1-NO
002050             MOVE WRK-DESCRIPTION TO DL1-DESCRIPTION
002060             MOVE IN-QTY TO DL1-QTY
002070             MOVE PRC-PRICE (PRC-TABLE-INDEX) TO DL1-PRICE
002080             MULTIPLY IN-QTY BY PRC-PRICE (PRC-TABLE-INDEX)
002090                 GIVING WRK-AMOUNT
002100             MOVE WRK-AMOUNT TO DL1-AMOUNT
002110             MOVE DL1-DETAIL-LINE TO OT-PRINT-LINE
002120     END-SEARCH.
002130     PERFORM 9000-WRITE.
002140     MOVE 1 TO CON-SPACING.
002150     PERFORM 8000-READ.
002160
002170 2100-HEADINGS.

002390
002400 8000-READ.
002410     READ IN-PART-FILE
002420         AT END MOVE "YES" TO SWT-EOF.
002430
002440 9000-WRITE.
002450     WRITE OT-PRINT-LINE
002460         AFTER ADVANCING CON-SPACING.
002470     ADD CON-SPACING TO CON-LINES-USED.

******
FILE
******
25116029
29258185
18071056
17526093
29266720

***********
OUTPUT
**********
PartNo - Description - Qty - Unit Price - Total Amount

25116      NUT                                     29          *NONE
29258      MAGNESIUM COVER     185        *NONE
18071      **NO DESCRIPTION**      56         *NONE
17526      **NO DESCRIPTION**      93         *NONE
29266      **NO DESCRIPTION**     720        *NONE

As the output shows, there is no AMOUNT or UNIT PRICE.

1st is supposed to have -  Part No, Description, Qty, NO UNIT PRICE, BLANK
TOTAL AMOUNT
2nd is supposed to have - Part No, Description, Qty, Unit Price, Amount
3rd is supposed to have - Part No, NO DESCRIPTION, Qty, NO UNIT PRICE, BLANK
TOTAL AMOUNT
4th is supposed to have - Part No, Description, Qty, Unit Price, Amount
5th is supposed to have - Part No, NO DESCRIPTION, Qty, NO UNIT PRICE, BLANK
TOTAL AMOUNT

If you need more info, I can provide it.

Thanks in advance

-SPK
```

#### ↳ Re: Search Table Problem

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vl0v8$sr@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<lonT3.893$w9.123453@news.flash.net>`

```
When using a SEARCH verb, you must set the index of the table before the
search.  Thus, add in
SET DES-TABLE-INDEX TO 1.
before searching that table, and likewise for the 2nd SEARCH.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer

For
>every record read the program is to search a table for the PART NO and

>indicate so on the Detail Line.  Then the program is to search another
table
>using the PART NO to find the UNIT PRICE.  If the UNIT PRICE is found then
>the TOTAL AMOUNT is calculated by UNIT PRICE * QTY, otherwise TOTAL AMOUNT
…[185 more quoted lines elided]…
>3rd is supposed to have - Part No, NO DESCRIPTION, Qty, NO UNIT PRICE,
BLANK
>TOTAL AMOUNT
>4th is supposed to have - Part No, Description, Qty, Unit Price, Amount

```
Are you doing a READ of the file in 1000-INITIALIZE?  If not, you are
entering 2000-PROCESS without having a record in storage.  You should
probably move your record description to WORKING-STORAGE and do a READ
file INTO instead of just READ.

Regards,





On Mon, 01 Nov 1999 21:13:53 GMT, "Steve Kalemkiewicz"
<senseisk@hotmail.com> enlightened us:

>I haven't used tables in Cobol before so I decided to take an old QBASIC
>program and convert it to Cobol.  After reading my manual and then
…[206 more quoted lines elided]…
>

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Whenever I find myself in a difficult situation, I ask myself "What
Would Jesus Do?"  The mental image of my opposition being cast into
pits of hellfire for all eternity *is* comforting, but probably not
what the inventors of the phrase had in mind.


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: Search Table Problem

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TbKT3.20574$l22.180905@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<lonT3.893$w9.123453@news.flash.net>`

```
That's easy.
1) You must SEARCH item-name VARYING an INDEX-NAME FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL
INDEX-NAME > max-number

or
2) Order the table and add
>001360     05  DES-DESC-TABLE      REDEFINES DES-DESC-TABLE-DATA.
>001370         10 DES-DESC-ENTRIES OCCURS 5 TIMES
>001380                             INDEXED BY DES-TABLE-INDEX
                                               ascending (or descending) key
is DES-NO.
>001390             15  DES-NO          PIC X(5).

AND code SEARCH ALL which automatically handles adjusting the index.  But
table MUST be ordered.

table
>using the PART NO to find the UNIT PRICE.  If the UNIT PRICE is found then
>the TOTAL AMOUNT is calculated by UNIT PRICE * QTY, otherwise TOTAL AMOUNT
…[185 more quoted lines elided]…

>4th is supposed to have - Part No, Description, Qty, Unit Price, Amount

<a id="t-c291574efe"></a>
## Thread: End-xxxx Statements — 7 msgs

### End-xxxx Statements

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s81dd4b9.097@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Ok - this may be very rudimentary but I've gone brain dead looking at some particularly nasty code (the other program from h**** we have here) and I think (make that pretty sure I know) that there is major trouble in it.

When there is an in-line perform the statements being performed must be between the PERFORM and the END-PERFORM right?

Seems someone coded a 

PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1
     UNTIL SUB = MAX-ENTRIES
END-PERFORM

MOVE WS-HOLD-REC (SUB) TO WS-REC-DEFINITION
more stuff
period

(all after the END-PERFORM)

which of course is NOT what I think they wanted to do.  However what it is they wanted to do isn't intuitively obvious because of the nasty mix of END statements and periods. (wasn't there a discussion on this a time back?)

Life is not good right now.


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: End-xxxx Statements

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<c0rT3.559$Ht5.25226@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<s81dd4b9.097@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```

Eileen Preston <epreston@lear.com> wrote in message
news:s81dd4b9.097@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM...
> Ok - this may be very rudimentary but I've gone brain dead looking
at some particularly nasty code (the other program from h**** we have
here) and I think (make that pretty sure I know) that there is major
trouble in it.
>
> When there is an in-line perform the statements being performed must
be between the PERFORM and the END-PERFORM right?

## Right

>
> Seems someone coded a
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-PERFORM

## The above does no useful work. Although a variation might:
PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1
        UNTIL WS-HOLD-REC(SUB) = 'END'
     END-PERFORM.
Which will end up with SUB being a counter for the number of
table entries.

>
> MOVE WS-HOLD-REC (SUB) TO WS-REC-DEFINITION
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> which of course is NOT what I think they wanted to do.  However what
it is they wanted to do isn't intuitively obvious because of the nasty
mix of END statements and periods. (wasn't there a discussion on this
a time back?)
>
> Life is not good right now.

## Be of good cheer. Life will get worse.
>
>
>  Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
>  Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: End-xxxx Statements

- **From:** "Bill Wood" <beavis27@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<u$MRaBXJ$GA.350@cpmsnbbsa05>`
- **References:** `<s81dd4b9.097@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```

Eileen Preston wrote in message ...

<SNIP>

>
>PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Bummer.  It looks like someone screwed up big time.  I believe the result
will be SUB = MAX-ENTRIES after exiting this loop and then moving onto the
next piece of code.  That may have been what someone was trying to do (maybe
they eliminated everything inside the PERFORM loop), but I doubt it.  There
is no bigger job than maintaining someone else's bad code!

bill
```

#### ↳ Re: End-xxxx Statements

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381EF527.21B43A27@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<s81dd4b9.097@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
This is pretty much the same thing as saying:
MOVE MAX-ENTRIES TO SUB. 
or SET SUB TO MAX-ENTRIES
MOVE WS-HOLD-REC (SUB) TO WS-REC-DEFINITION

Eileen Preston wrote:
> 
> Ok - this may be very rudimentary but I've gone brain dead looking at some particularly nasty code (the other program from h**** we have here) and I think (make that pretty sure I know) that there is major trouble in it.
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: End-xxxx Statements

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381F2A7B.15F3E7CB@home.com>`
- **References:** `<s81dd4b9.097@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```


Eileen Preston wrote:
> 
> Ok - this may be very rudimentary but I've gone brain dead looking at some particularly nasty code (the other program from h**** we have here) and I think (make that pretty sure I know) that there is major trouble in it.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 
Well Life can get better. If you have Exit Perform in your compiler,
then it makes life easier. My preference is :-

(a) 

Set DontEndPerform to true
PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1
      UNTIL SUB >= MAX-ENTRIES or EndPerform

    if	RecordFits
        MOVE WS-HOLD-REC (SUB) TO WS-REC-DEFINITION
        more stuff

     else set EndPerform to true
 END-PERFORM

(b) 

PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1
      UNTIL SUB >= MAX-ENTRIES

    if	RecordFits
        MOVE WS-HOLD-REC (SUB) TO WS-REC-DEFINITION
        more stuff

     else EXIT PERFORM
 END-PERFORM

Whether you test using = or >=  depends upon whether you are using Test
After or Test Before.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: End-xxxx Statements

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vnsnd$tan$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<s81dd4b9.097@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <381F2A7B.15F3E7CB@home.com>`

```
: Eileen Preston wrote:
:> 
:> PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1
:>      UNTIL SUB = MAX-ENTRIES
:> END-PERFORM
:> 
:> MOVE WS-HOLD-REC (SUB) TO WS-REC-DEFINITION
:> more stuff
:> period
:> 
:> (all after the END-PERFORM)
:> 
:> which of course is NOT what I think they wanted to do.

It may be what is required, just an obscure way of doing it.
If Max-Entries is initialised to zero then this program
will loop until Sub wraps around and then give a bound error.

I suspect that Max-Entries may initially be 1.  If 'more stuff'
includes ADD 1 TO Max-Entries, then the PERFORM is just a time
wasting way of doing MOVE Max-Entries TO Sub.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: End-xxxx Statements

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WeMT3.858$6s6.25147@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<s81dd4b9.097@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <381F2A7B.15F3E7CB@home.com>`

```
EXIT PERFORM - I tried that just today using IBM COBOL for S/390. Got a
syntax error. Checked the doc. Had to rewrite the paragraph.

I thought EXIT PERFORM [CYCLE] was part of ANSI '89 and was included in
current S/390 COBOL.

Bummer.

MCM
PS:  However, I did notice in bookmanager that the word "CYCLE" is "RDF"
(reserved for future development).


James J. Gavan wrote in message <381F2A7B.15F3E7CB@home.com>...
>(b)
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991101143852.15780.00001246@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
Good Afternoon,

The company I work for is interested in purchasing the source code to an
existing inventory control system.  They are interested in a unit inventory
control system designed for the hard goods type of customer.  (i.e. Hardware
store , general merchandise).

We currently use MicroFocus NetExpress and Flexus Sp2.  Does anyone have such a
system for sale ?  Or do you know someone who does ?

Thanks for your time,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: $ for Source Code to Inventory System - COBOL Win9x/NT

- **From:** John Chafin <jchafin@cgwinc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vmpu2$t8t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19991101143852.15780.00001246@ng-fq1.aol.com>`

```
In article <19991101143852.15780.00001246@ng-fq1.aol.com>,
  bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:
> Good Afternoon,
>
> The company I work for is interested in purchasing the source code to
an
> existing inventory control system.  They are interested in a unit
inventory
> control system designed for the hard goods type of customer.  (i.e.
Hardware
> store , general merchandise).
>
> We currently use MicroFocus NetExpress and Flexus Sp2.  Does anyone
have such a
> system for sale ?  Or do you know someone who does ?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Bob,

We have a full blown WMS (soon to be SCE and Ecommerce) system that has
a comprehensive inventory control component and a point of sale
component. It's written in CA-Realia COBOL and uses Flexus SP2. Why
don't you email me (jchafin@cgwinc.com) with more information about
what you're looking for (features and functions) and I can give you
more information about our product.

Look forward to hearing from you.

John Chafin


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Uncompiler Cobol 85

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#PhMQmKJ$GA.265@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<7vjcmb$3tp$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```
Are you looking for a disassembler?  Something that takes object code and
turns it into assembly language?  They exist, but you have to go looking for
them.

On the other hand, if you're looking for something that takes the assembly
code result from a disassembly operation and turns that into COBOL, well,
I'm not sure there is such a product (keep looking, though).


Garwig Ruddy wrote in message <7vjcmb$3tp$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>...
>I want a uncompiler for the Cobol 85.
>
>
```

<a id="t-d04939b499"></a>
## Thread: COBOL and COM — 1 this month / 3 total

_Thread started [1999-10](1999-10.md#t-d04939b499)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and COM

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381d78ad.67928046@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<LY7R3.753$hP2.12538@news4.mia> <7vafmd$rfd$1@news4.svr.pol.co.uk>`

```
"Gerald Moull" <gerald@moull.freeserve.co.uk> wrote:

>
>There are companies that provide ActiveX controls that basically act an an
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Realia though.

Gerald:

No, these products do not work with CA Realia COBOL.  COBOL sp2 does
and it provides the ability to embed and control Active X (COM)
objects directly from the COBOL procedural code.

Sorry for the commercial, but just wanted to make sure that we are
also included on the Active X/COBOL list.



Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

## Thread: C calls from cobol. — 1 this month / 3 total

_Thread started [1999-10](1999-10.md#t-10e1eb60d9)._

#### ↳ Re: C calls from cobol.

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381d79f8.68258615@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<7vc1nn$k90$1@fe2.cs.interbusiness.it>`

```
"ACI Leasing S.p.A." <leasing@lucca.aci.it> wrote:

>I have written a C routine and compiled.
>Can I call it from i-Cobol?
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sure.  COBOL sp2 supports ICOBOL and the sp2.dll screen handler is
written in C.  The sp2 call interface is exactly the same as what you
have written above.

The short answer is, "Yes, you can."

Contact Egan Systems for more information.  Their web site is
www.icobol.com.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

<a id="t-2f2b3b212d"></a>
## Thread: Input File (READ) — 1 this month / 7 total

_Thread started [1999-10](1999-10.md#t-2f2b3b212d)._

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Input File (READ)

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-01T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381D8964.C261B3E0@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<7uqv5q$oma$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <DC8Q3.479$Lm1.28301@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
Jerry P wrote:
> 
> Assumption: Each 'Record' in your file is terminated by
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> record definition with spaces.

Not on *my* platform.

I would suggest that the record is expanded with a count of the number of
occurrences. something like:

01  recordLayout.
    03  field-1-in-fixed-part  pic ... .
    03  field-2-in-fixed-part  pic ... .
    ...
    03  field-n-in-fixed-part  pic ... .
    03  count-of-elements      pic 99.
    03  element                pic ...
                        occurs 0 to ... times
                  depending on count-of-elements.

that way, after a READ, use count-of-elements to get the correct number of
elements. Of course the contents of this file, especially of the variable
count-of-elements, needs to be garuanteed/reliable, but that could be
established by a well screening input-program.

Frog
```

Ack! Never thought I'd actually *hear* that term again. I used to be the MK4
expert when I first started working as an entry level programmer at Broward
Community College in Davie, FL. Last time I checked (back in 1987), they
were still using it for their reporting needs. I understand from a prior
post, that the utility isn't Y2K compliant, huh? Oh well... it was great,
for its time. I mean, these days, there are much better products out on the
market.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: An old language called MARK4

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vo4s1$c91$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<7vmo9t$rha$2@news.ibm.net.il> <7vna70$eb4$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <bcGT3.1767$f16.50937@news2.mia> <eRNe+0NPKbtF@vmsmail.gov.bc.ca>`

```
I am not surprised, but thank you for the information. I saw it for the
first time in september 1981 when I started a new project in CICS/COBOL/DLI.
I will never forget it,  because some of the programmers kept telling me how
easy everything would be if they were allowed to make some of the reports in
MARK IV.

Through the eighties I saw the manuals almost everywhere and the last time
was in 93-94, when we were offloading Cobol-source to a PC network. Some of
the old timers there were not pleased with the fact, that they had to
rewrite their MARK IV programs in Cobol.

70-71 means, that MARK IV came at the same time as CICS but CICS is till
arround. Amazing isn't it.

Regards
Ib
Rodger Whitlock skrev i meddelelsen ...
>"The Programmer" <someone@somewhere.com> wrote:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

Simon R Hart

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>

Attaching new message posts concerning one subject (an online COBOL manual)
to the end of a thread about another subject (the MARK4 computer language)
is usually considered something of a misstep on usenet (unless there is some
other reason I can't see right away; the last post to this thread was back
on the 5th of November, and this new post does not seem to continue the
discussion).

Try posting an original message to this newsgroup.

But before you do, read the COBOL FAQ (Frequently Asked Questions; which can
be located using most search engines; here's one address:
http://pweb.netcom.com/~wmklein/cobolfaq.htm).

Look up FAQ Entry 10.10 for a short answer to your question about COBOL
Unleashed.

For longer answers, go to www.deja.com, do a powersearch on this newsgropu
using the keywords COBOL and Unleashed to get many, many longer answers to
your book question.


Sincerely,
     Chris Osborne

## Thread: Tables ???? — 19 msgs

### Tables ????

- **From:** "Klaas Berend" <kbpoel@concepts.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vmg39$bf7$1@news.concepts.nl>`

```
This is my problem.
I have a file called result1.seq In this file you can find the following
information.
Levnumber        ArtId                        Numbersold
1                            01                            100
1                            02                            200
2                            03                            150
2                            04                            300
3                            05                            100
3                            06                             50

Now I have to count the numbersold by each Levnumber.
So like this Levnumber 1               Totalsold 300
The results are output for a new file.
How can I do this???
```

#### ↳ Re: Tables ????

- **From:** "George E. Zielinski" <georgezielinski@retired.airforce.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37397D20F96C7408.68179DA6CF4B6B8E.4372853533C3AEB5@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<7vmg39$bf7$1@news.concepts.nl>`

```
This looks like a homework assignment, so I will attempt to give you a
pointer in the right direction rather than work it out for you.

First ensure that your file is in order by Levnumber, then read each record.
Accumulate the totals. Only produce an output when the Levnumber changes.
And don't forget to reset your totals to zero after each output.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Tables ????

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38251EE2.F0A82266@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<7vmg39$bf7$1@news.concepts.nl> <19991102093219.27555.00000197@ngol03.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
> 
> >This is my problem.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> >The results are output for a new file.

> This doesn't really look like a 'table' process.  As long as the input
> file is ALWAYS ordered by LevNumber, you should be able to process
> sequentially do your accumulations and on change of LevNumber
> print your results.

Not necessarily; it depends on how one approaches the problem and the nature
of the data. If 'levnumber' is represented by positive integers falling in a
limited range, say, 1-1000, there exists a most straightforward sortless
solution not obvious only to minds locked in the 'control break' logic:

1) Create a table with 1000 buckets and initialize their contents to 0.
2) Read the file and for each record, add 'numbersold' to the bucket whose
subscript is the same as 'levnumber'.
3) Loop over the table. If a bucket is empty, skip it; otherwise, write its
number to a file as 'levnumber' and its contents - as 'totalsold'.

One would be surprised how many real-world problems can be solved like that
without ever resorting to sorting (no pun intended). If the range of the
keys is too wide and hence a table capable of accommodating the entire
universe of key values cannot be allocated, one can organize the table as a
hash table instead of the key-indexed table described above. In this case,
the keys need not be integers, they can be anything, and that will always
work faster than anything else will, if the total number of keys in the file
times key length fits into the memory. With contemporary memories it usually
means millions of distinct keys.

That is, if one thinks in terms of sorting, i.e. rearranging data in such a
way that alike keys are brought together as closely as possible (which is
one of the the hardest and most resource-consuming computer tasks), this
does not look like tables are relevant to the problem; but if one thinks in
terms of distribution, i.e. placing keys as separately from each other as
possible, it looks exactly like a 'table' process.

On the same note, when it comes to searching tables, the common mindset
seems to be locked in SEARCH ALL routine just because COBOL provides it free
of charge. More times than I care to recall, I've heard the nonsense (of
course, pronounced in the most authoritative fashion) about binary search
being the fastest and most efficient among existing lookup algorithms, while
in reality, the same COBOL table organized as a key-indexed or hash table
(depending on the keys' nature) searches an order of magnitude faster, to
say nothing of the fact that, to boot, the direct-addressing approaches
require no sorting or unduplication of any kind.     

Kind regards,
===================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL                    
===================
```

whose

> 3) Loop over the table. If a bucket is empty, skip it; otherwise,
write its

like that

table as a

always

the file
> times key length fits into the memory. With contemporary memories it
usually

> That is, if one thinks in terms of sorting, i.e. rearranging data in
such a
> way that alike keys are brought together as closely as possible
(which is
> one of the the hardest and most resource-consuming computer tasks),
this
> does not look like tables are relevant to the problem; but if one
thinks in
> terms of distribution, i.e. placing keys as separately from each
other as
> possible, it looks exactly like a 'table' process.
>
> On the same note, when it comes to searching tables, the common
mindset
> seems to be locked in SEARCH ALL routine just because COBOL provides
it free
> of charge. More times than I care to recall, I've heard the nonsense
(of
> course, pronounced in the most authoritative fashion) about binary
search
> being the fastest and most efficient among existing lookup
algorithms, while
> in reality, the same COBOL table organized as a key-indexed or hash
table

faster, to
> say nothing of the fact that, to boot, the direct-addressing
approaches
> require no sorting or unduplication of any kind.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
If you know approximately how many different numbers there are, rather
than the range, you can make a table which is much smaller than the
whole range.
If the number an vary considerably from execution to exectution, you
might want to use a variable length table.
Also, on an IBM mainframe, a binary search (SEARCH ALL) is more
efficient only if there are more than approximately 75 entries in the
table.  Less than (approximately) 75 entries, the sequential search is
more efficient.

Stephen J Spiro
President, Wizard Systems
Member, J4 COBOL Committee


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables ????

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38262B8E.F75DEF67@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<7vmg39$bf7$1@news.concepts.nl> <19991102093219.27555.00000197@ngol03.aol.com> <38251EE2.F0A82266@earthlink.net> <8053un$tou$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
While my reading of the COBOL SEARCH ALL code back in the mid to late
1970's was that the code was poor, is it still that bad on COBOL for MVS
and VM and later?  If I get energetic, I may look at the code and see
how bad it is on the setup.  As I recall at the time the break even
point between our assembler serial sub-routine and our binary search
assembler sub-routine was 16 entries.  These routines were originally
written before the SEARCH verb was available.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca

stephen_j_spiro@my-deja.com wrote:

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Tables ????

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** stephen_j_spiro@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80726e$9k8$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7vmg39$bf7$1@news.concepts.nl> <19991102093219.27555.00000197@ngol03.aol.com> <38251EE2.F0A82266@earthlink.net> <8053un$tou$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38262B8E.F75DEF67@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
Clark, 16 table entries is a maximum of 4 lookups in a binary search.
There is just more overhead in the instruction.. 75 was the  number I
heard a long time ago, and I don't imagine they've changed the
algorithm.  I'd be interested in hesaring fromn anyone at IBM who knows
for sure.

Stephen J Spiro
President, Wizard Systems
Member, J4 COBOL Committee

#### ↳ Re: Tables ????

- **From:** "Unbeliever" <popsider@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<802scm$f68$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7vmg39$bf7$1@news.concepts.nl>`

```
Try (Sync)SORTSUM instead of COBOL.

Something like
SORT FIELDS=(1,1,ZD,A)
SUM FIELDS=(4,3,ZD)
OUTREC=(1,1,       Levnumber
                     4,3)      Numbersold

Yepp - I routinely now use INREC & OUTREC.
Last week I had to change a program to increase field sizes in a file.
I also ran synchsort on the existing GDG. I used OUTREC= along
with statements to slice & dice the input record, inserting hex LVs
before each expanded field. I love doing this with MVS utils, because
it's far safer, doesn't require as much coding time as Cobol, runs
faster, and requires no real maintenance, if you want to keep it!
```

```
In article <80c55p$19q$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  john_mindock@my-deja.com wrote:

> For more fun:
> Take a look at the options available for Y2K sorting in SYNCSORT
> (DFSORT has them too). I.e., you identify a field as a Year and it
> will sort '00' after '99', based on a window (which can be floating or
> fixed).

With DFSORT, you can identify either complete date fields (e.g.
C'yymmdd', P'dddyy', etc) or year fields (e.g. C'yy', P'yy', etc) to be
sorted, merged, compared or transformed using a century window.  For
complete details, see the following URL:

http://www.storage.ibm.com/software/sort/srtmy2p.htm

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team
DFSORT is on the Web at URL:
http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-a63b75a29c"></a>
## Thread: general knowledge help for a student — 11 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### general knowledge help for a student

- **From:** lex d <luminous@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381E6666.3A93E888@home.com>`

```
hi everyone,

i'm doing a project on Cobol for my programming class (the topic: an
exploration of the evolution of programming languages with consideration
given to the evolution of computer technology) and while doing preliminary
research (i think my thesis will be something along the lines of "the
natural language structure prevalent in Cobol changed the face of
programming for its bridging of the gap between machine and man language,
more people can understand this language and therefore create programs,
etc, etc".. ive just started reasrching really. 

what i've come across a lot is the referring of COBOL as business
oriented.. i'm not quite sure of the term, and i feel that if i asked my
teacher what it meant, he'd probably flunk me out of his course! :P so can
someone here explain it? what is an alternative to business oriented?

if anyone knows of any good places on the web or books that'll help me
research my topic, or has any general ideas/comments/etc they can share
with me i'd be greatful :) 

thanks,

alexis
```

#### ↳ Re: general knowledge help for a student

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vndob$ppg$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<381E6666.3A93E888@home.com>`

```
Many secrets in this world are not published on internet. I haven't got the
ideal reference, but I have a good memory. Here is a few pieces of
information for you, and if you like, you ar welcome to mail me for further
questions.

The years from fifties through the sixties we had many different ways to put
programs into the computers.
Auto Codes were slightly formalized 'machine language' (or perhaps one pass
assembler)
Neat, which was NCR's Assembler
BAL, Basic assembler language for IBM 360/2x and 360-Assembler for 360/3x
and up
RPG was born in these years, but none of them were designed for a special
purpose.

Computing was a mystery to almost anyone outside the business, by even
within the business we had different categories of purpose for computing.
Technical applications and business applications were the 2 major
categories.

Technical computing was based upon Math, and very typical was that you
entered a very limited amount of data, and you received a small amount of
data, but that old rig was often
processing for days and weeks to give the result.

Business applications were made to produce accounting, invoices, delivery
notes and all that stuff an office appriciate.

When COBOL was born, we knew what 'business oriented' meant - and I think
you know it as well.

Soon after Cobol another language, 'ALGOL' was born. ALGgebraic Oriented
Language was for Math at Office. Insurance and statistics were the places
where that language were widely used.

But, you know,  after Cobol nothing of value ever ocurred (This will make
this thread grow beyond your wildest imagination)

Good luck with your project

Ib
lex d skrev i meddelelsen <381E6666.3A93E888@home.com>...
>hi everyone,
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>luminous@home.com
```

The first machine that I came across with RPG was an ICL/Univac
1004 which had a whole 1000 characters of memory and was 
programmed with a plug board.  The RPG compiler was a plugboard
about 2 feet square.  The program was compiled into memory
and executed to produce the report from tape or card pack data.
```
