#### ↳ Re: general knowledge help for a student

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<CtKT3.247$oq1.15656@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<381E6666.3A93E888@home.com>`

```

lex d <luminous@home.com

Once upon a time the programming world was divided into
"Business" and "Scientific" (some also say "Light" and "Dark").

Business programming was characterized by a high volume
of input and output with very little computation (Read record,
compute PAY = HRS * RATE, put record. Repeat 5000 times).

Scientific programming was characterized by a low volume
of I-O but lots of computation (Read a matrix of values on
ten punched cards, compute the inverse of the matrix in
ten hours of solid computing, print ten lines of output).

These original divisions have today blurred and new ones
have been born (Sons of the Dark).
```

#### ↳ Re: general knowledge help for a student

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<C2CT3.2807$aS6.29572@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<381E6666.3A93E888@home.com>`

```
lex d wrote:
>
>what i've come across a lot is the referring of COBOL as business
>oriented.. i'm not quite sure of the term, and i feel that if i asked my
>teacher what it meant, he'd probably flunk me out of his course! :P so can
>someone here explain it? what is an alternative to business oriented?

Business = commerce, money, production, sales, inventory, financial
reporting, payroll, accounts payable, accounts receivable, etc., etc.
All the things that permit a society to have resources enough to pay
teachers like yours, who don't have a clue where their pay checks come
from.  And chances are, your teacher's paycheck is still printed by a
COBOL program.  If it was in one of the more 'fashionable' languages,
it would likely say 1456.349999999901 instead of $1,456.35. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: general knowledge help for a student

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2BC5F844BE592AF0.ACB2BF605EF45AD1.C7076AA5803A172D@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<381E6666.3A93E888@home.com> <C2CT3.2807$aS6.29572@news4.mia>`

```
On Tue, 2 Nov 1999 07:53:58 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> enlightened us:

>lex d wrote:
>>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>it would likely say 1456.349999999901 instead of $1,456.35. :-)

To add to what Judson has said, COBOL is an acronym for COmmon
Business Oriented Language.  The prime reason it was invented was to
make it easier to write programs for business applications as opposed
to engineering, scientific or systems oriented applications.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Whenever I find myself in a difficult situation, I ask myself "What
Would Jesus Do?"  The mental image of my opposition being cast into
pits of hellfire for all eternity *is* comforting, but probably not
what the inventors of the phrase had in mind.


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: general knowledge help for a student

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vn358$ir7$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<381E6666.3A93E888@home.com>`

```
There are many aspects of "business oriented", and I am sure others will
mention more.  However here are a few starters.

Cobol is designed for easy *reading* rather than fast and dirty writing.
This is the exact opposite of languages like C, where you are dealing (in
theory) with academic code.  Business code evolves, rather than being
written.  Because of that, it is far more important to be able to write a
simple program that can be expanded by the next programmer than it is to be
able to whack out a large program in a hurry. A large proportion of Cobol is
documentary.

Cobol has far more sophisticated data typing than most languages, and is one
of the very few with intregal decimal data types.  Rounding and overflow in
business follows DECIMAL rules, not binary. This is crucial when dealing in
monetary units.

Cobol is, by far, the most platform independent language.  This is important
for both the longevity of programs, and for cost control within data
processing.

Cobol has, within the language, file structures that allow the building of
quite complex record structures.  While record keeping can be done in just
about any language, Cobol was designed for it.  That shows.

Languages for computers are generally designed to solve specific types of
problems.  C was designed as a replacement for assembler, and is ideal for
an OS, for example.  In fact, it is good for just about anything that I
would have written in assembler twenty years back.  Basic was written to
make it easy for post grad arts students to get a computer course.  Pascal
was designed for teaching.  Fortran was designed for formula translation.
Cobol was designed for accounts receivable, payables, and payroll.  The
above paragraphs and things like "PICTURE $$$,$$9.99" demonstrate that.

lex d wrote in message <381E6666.3A93E888@home.com>...
>hi everyone,
>
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
>luminous@home.com
```

<a id="t-c4c7ae9e38"></a>
## Thread: MDI ? — 6 msgs

### MDI ?

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991102084431.11231.00001186@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
Good Morning,

I am responding to an a RFP and one of the requirements is that the software
must be a MDI style application.  I am not aware of what MDI stands for.  Could
someone help me out ?

Thanks,
Bob Hennessey
```

#### ↳ Re: MDI ?

- **From:** phil@sircs.co.uk (Philip Pike)
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381eec93.1233319@news.easynet.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<19991102084431.11231.00001186@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
On 02 Nov 1999 13:44:31 GMT, bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:

>Good Morning,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Bob,

This is some snippets abou MDI forms from the MSDN Library provided
with Visual Studio 6.0, hope this what you mean by MDI


An MDI (multiple-document interface) form is a window that acts as the
background of an application and is the container for forms that have
their MDIChild property set to True.


An application can have only one MDIForm object but manyMDI child
forms. If an MDI child form has menus, the child form's menu bar
automatically replaces the MDIForm object's menu bar when the MDI
child form is active. A minimized MDI child form is displayed as an
icon within the MDIForm.

An MDIForm object can contain only Menu and PictureBox controls and
custom controls that have an Align property. To place other controls
on an MDIForm, you can draw a picture box on the form, and then draw
other controls inside the picture box. You can use the Print method to
display text in a picture box on an MDIForm, but you can't use this
method to display text on the MDIForm itself.

Philip Pike
```

#### ↳ Re: MDI ?

- **From:** "Oscar T. Grouch" <dustbin@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<qrCT3.14954$it.416923@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`
- **References:** `<19991102084431.11231.00001186@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```

Bob7536 <bob7536@aol.com> wrote in message
news:19991102084431.11231.00001186@ng-fd1.aol.com...
> Good Morning,
>
> I am responding to an a RFP and one of the requirements is that the
software
> must be a MDI style application.  I am not aware of what MDI stands for.
Could
> someone help me out ?
>
> Thanks,
> Bob Hennessey
>
(M)ultiple (D)ocument (I)nterface

It refers to applications where multiple forms are maintained within the
same container form. If you take a look at MS Word, or Outlook, or just
about anything that Micro$oft publishes then you're looking at an MDI.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MDI ?

- **From:** bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536)
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991102101535.14574.00000182@ng-fd1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<qrCT3.14954$it.416923@news2.rdc1.on.home.com>`

```
Thanks for the information.

Bob Hennessey
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MDI ?

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3822D707.68B2D60B@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<qrCT3.14954$it.416923@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <19991102101535.14574.00000182@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
Bob7536 wrote:
> 
> Thanks for the information.
> 
> Bob Hennessey

Ms Word 2000 is no longer MDI but SDI (single doc. interface).

MS Word '95, '97:
-----------------
When opening a second word doc, a new child window was opened *within* the main
window. You could cascade or tile these child windows of maximize then *within*
the borders of the main window. (The main win could be non-maximized, but I
rarely saw a Word-user which did not maximize the window.)
In the task bar you see only ONE entry.

MS Word 2000:
-------------
When opening a second word doc, a new main window is opened.
In the task bar you see one entry PER open word document.

Got it? :)

Frog
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MDI ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vv2sg$3aq$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<qrCT3.14954$it.416923@news2.rdc1.on.home.com> <19991102101535.14574.00000182@ng-fd1.aol.com> <3822D707.68B2D60B@IMN.nl>`

```
The COBOL Frog wrote in message <3822D707.68B2D60B@IMN.nl>...
>Ms Word 2000 is no longer MDI but SDI (single doc. interface).

Interesting point really, Microsoft has stated that they are not encouraging
MDI anymore. The focus is changed from application centric to document
centric.

MDI means that any child window is bound to the originating window of the
application, thus, such an application would for instance only have one
instance on the Windows taskbar, while with and SDI application each
individual window will appear on the windows taskbar. Equally, a minimized
mdi child will rest in peace in its master window, while a minimized SDi
child will rest in windows workspace. Just as you say.

(Sorry if I have repeated something already said, I have partly missed this
thread)

Cheesle
```

## Thread: Abandon all hope ye who enter here — 9 msgs

### Abandon all hope ye who enter here

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991102001043.04095.00001145@ng-ff1.aol.com>`

```
Well, I've completed all the training I'm gonna get at the Chubb Institute. 
Now we move on to database applications.  I'm glad the curriculum has been
revised to emphasize more up-to-date tools,

BUT...

I wonder if our training is just sparse enough in everything to get us work
nowhere.  We don't do anything in CICS now, for example.  How do I compete?

AND...

Everybody who is a fan of COBOL waxes about how there's still so much COBOL
code in production, and it sure isn't gonna disappear overnight.

BUT...

The maintenance isn't gonna be handled by us tyros just entering the field. 
The companies who still hire in COBOL don't hire anybody who just learned it,
it seems.

Would someone care to contradict me, and tell me that the past three months
I've spent cracking my skull on JCL and COBOL haven't been a colossal waste of
time?

RoadRaat
```

#### ↳ Re: Abandon all hope ye who enter here

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BB0DD1C910F4D020.51BEDD2C62386537.0E37477F31ADE72B@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<19991102001043.04095.00001145@ng-ff1.aol.com>`

```
On 02 Nov 1999 05:10:43 GMT, roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat) enlightened
us:

>Well, I've completed all the training I'm gonna get at the Chubb Institute. 
>Now we move on to database applications.  I'm glad the curriculum has been
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>RoadRaat

Knowledge gained about anything is never a waste of time.  And believe
it or not, much of the maintenance that is performed on existing Cobol
programs is done by junior programmers.  Shops certainly don't turn
their new development projects over to newbies.  

As for CICS, I didn't learn it in college either.  I took advantage of
a training course the company I was working for offered to learn it.
Many companies will pay you up to 80% of tuition costs if you take
courses that enhance your abilities.  Going off to learn CICS would
qualify.

Learn all you can in school.  Just don't stop there.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Whenever I find myself in a difficult situation, I ask myself "What
Would Jesus Do?"  The mental image of my opposition being cast into
pits of hellfire for all eternity *is* comforting, but probably not
what the inventors of the phrase had in mind.


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

```
Back to work...

RoadRaat
```

```
I graduated college in computer programming over 3 months ago, mainly in COBOL and
CICS.  I can not get a position in this line of work either, and I've been on
several interviews.  Sorry, but companies see entry - level as someone they would
have to stop and teach how to use their systems, and they would rather just trade
the experienced ones around.
```

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3821954f.34031881@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Joey,

I'm not so sure about that.  While someone who HAS moved about a bit
is more likely to be able to pick a new system easily - experienced or
not, some training will be required.   I think if the company had a
serious need they would hire.  They granted the interview, right?  To
me that says you had a chance.  They are looking for a diamond in the
rough.  The best entry level candidate would be flexible and
tenacious.  You have to NOT be rigid in your thinking.  Hope that
helps some.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Abandon all hope ye who enter here

- **From:** "Barvin" <barvin@mwis.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uesU3.1435$yF6.9059@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<19991102001043.04095.00001145@ng-ff1.aol.com> <381FC424.62C15D3C@takethisout.yahoo.com> <3821954f.34031881@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
An entry-level true story.
I sent out 70 resumes.
Got only 2 interviews.
I was feeling discouraged.
One called me back in.
Then called me back in again.
Then they offered me a job.
5 months after that first resume went out.
Turned out none of the old timers had time for the big 'ol Y2K project.
First day on the job as a real life coboler and I find out that I've got to
have
some 600 programs Y2K compliant by a looming deadline.
Now close to 2 years later the Y2K project seems like ancient history and
I've done
quite a few new programs.
The moral here?
Fight the discouragement and keep on knocking!

David
barvin@mwis.net
The Barvin Web Site
http://barvin.mwis.net
```

#### ↳ Re: Abandon all hope ye who enter here

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vn2c1$3o4$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19991102001043.04095.00001145@ng-ff1.aol.com>`

```

> > Would someone care to contradict me, and tell me that the past
three months
> I've spent cracking my skull on JCL and COBOL haven't been a colossal
waste of
> time?

My opinion:
I don't think it matters what language a person learned. If they're
just out of school, they are not going to be whiz-bang at the technical
aspects of the job. They can't out-code the incumbents. (In fact,
declaring themselves as such probably nixes the job prospect.)

So why would any employer hire a newbie for anything?
Maybe because:
A. They could work on some 'simpler' aspects of the systems, which are
now assigned to more-experienced people. The more-experienced people
could be freed up for assignment to more difficult tasks.
B. They are probably less expensive than oldtimers.
C. They don't mind doing the 'mundane'work that the oldbies are tired
of.
D. They aren't burned out (yet) so will apply the extra effort.
E. They are more likely (than some oldbies)to feel they need to perform
well to keep their jobs.
F. They enjoy the challenge of new tasks and the rapid change of this
profession.

Maybe a good interview strategy is to convince the hirer that you are a
guy who likes to get his hands dirty on the code, who enjoys solving
problems, who has an old-fashioned work ethic, and also who gets along
with all kinds of personalities. One real bad thing - whining about
whatever.

John


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Little-Endian and Big-Endian?

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381f9ad4_1@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<E47B36D9946CD311995000A0C9EA3E925F5FD1@voyager.okc.disa.mil> <7vn9j1$rim$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz> wrote in message
news:7vn9j1$rim$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz...
>
> Gulliver's Travels.
> Eggs.


To elaborate: There were these two kinds of people: the big-endians who
maintained that the proper way to eat an egg would be first to seat it
firmly
on its blunt end, and the little-endians who maintained that the proper way
to seat it was on its pointed end.
```

#### ↳ Re: Little-Endian and Big-Endian?

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381F1959.4989@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<E47B36D9946CD311995000A0C9EA3E925F5FD1@voyager.okc.disa.mil>`

```
Thompson, William wrote:
> 
> I think I understand what in general it refers to, but not specifically.
> Can somebody explain where this term came from and what its roots are?

Binary numbers may be stored with the first byte being the highest value byte, or with 
the first byte being the lowest value byte:

The default shown is known as "Little Endian", a  computer architecture in which, within 
a given 16- or 32-bit word, bytes at lower addresses have lower significance (the word 
is stored "little-end-first"). The PDP-11 and VAX families of computers and Intel 
microprocessors and a lot of communications and networking hardware are little-endian. 

Big Endian is a computer architecture in which, within a given multi-byte numeric 
representation, the most significant byte has the lowest address (the word is stored 
"big-end-first"). Most processors, including the IBM 370 family, the PDP-10, the 
Motorola microprocessor families, and most of the various RISC designs, are big-endian. 
```

<a id="t-e6b8c4f69b"></a>
## Thread: (MicroFocus) DISPLAY statements to stdout ? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### (MicroFocus) DISPLAY statements to stdout ?

- **From:** "Jacques Vidal" <syster.soft@wanadoo.fr>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vm9rh$goh$1@wanadoo.fr>`

```
Hi,

Does anybody know how to redirect DISPLAY statements to
standard output with Win32 MicroFocus COBOL? I've tried
DISPLAY ... UPON SYSOUT and other things, but am still
unable to redirect DISPLAYed messages to a file. I guess
DISPLAYs default to display memory. How can I change this
behaviour?

TIA,

Jacques Vidal
```

#### ↳ Re: (MicroFocus) DISPLAY statements to stdout ?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381F3F50.8DAA4FFF@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7vm9rh$goh$1@wanadoo.fr>`

```


Jacques Vidal wrote:
> 
> Hi,
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> behaviour?

Not sure if this helps, but this is the NetExpress syntax :-

Formatï¿½1 (Line at a Time)

DISPLAY identifier-1 identifier-2 ...
        iteral-1     literal-2    
        UPON mnemonic-name  [ WITH NO ADVANCING ]
            function-name 
              [ON EXCEPTION imperative-statement-1]
              [NOT ON EXCEPTION imperative-statement-2]
              [END-DISPLAY]

(Allowing you to specify mnemonic/function in SPECIAL NAMES). The
mnemonic-name must be associated with a function-name in the
SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph in the Environment Division. For a list of valid
function-names, see the general rules of the SPECIAL-NAMES paragraph.
The mnemonic-name can have the following compiler-defined values, which
are specified in detail in the general rules.
	COMMAND-LINE
	ARGUMENT-NAME
	ARGUMENT-NUMBER
	ENVIRONMENT-VALUE
	ENVIRONMENT-NAME
	SYSERR

A bit of a half-baked approach, but if you are wanting to get it to a
file - from Windows, set printer to write to disk, then "DISPLAY UPON
PRINTER" ????

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: (MicroFocus) DISPLAY statements to stdout ?

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vmd6k$hbp$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<7vm9rh$goh$1@wanadoo.fr>`

```
    Assuming plain, ordinary displays without the AT clause (they roll the
screen when they reach the bottom of the screen), set COBSW=+S5.  The color
will change (if you are using ansi.sys, and have set a default color).  Then
all plain displays will redirect.  Note that this includes trace messages.
If you use display AT for normal program use (who would use a plain
display), then the screen will look normal when redirection is active.  Note
that trace output can create long files.



Jacques Vidal <syster.soft@wanadoo.fr> wrote in message
news:7vm9rh$goh$1@wanadoo.fr...
> Hi,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
G Moore wrote:
> 
> On Sat, 30 Oct 1999 04:39:09 -0600, Howard Brazee
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> mmddyy fields might take up access time you don't have.

I have always worked on mainframes.  They all have to have ways of
updating the data structures.  Admittedly some very time critical
systems have to have tricky ways of minimizing down time (Covia uses
IDMS to store a very complicated airline reservation system with
virtually no downtime) - but there has to be a way of doing this.
```

<a id="t-5e05553a22"></a>
## Thread: Final notes on intialize statement — 3 msgs

### Final notes on intialize statement

- **From:** Jean-René Duval <jean-ren.duval@sympatico.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381E5F79.65B049E5@sympatico.ca>`

```
Thank you all for your comment.

I just found out that when you initalize a group that has an occurs
statement on it, it will not intialize that array. Like Howard said
"this command is at work" and has some pervert effect.

I tried this command first because of extremely long number of variables
to be reset before every read statement (254). This shortcut cost me
some headache and half a day for debuging it.

I should've learned that shortcuts leave us with the Y2K bug in the
first place.

We'll never learn.;(

Regards.

jean-ren.duvalxxx@sympatico.ca
```

#### ↳ Re: Final notes on intialize statement

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<r%JT3.20569$l22.181091@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<381E5F79.65B049E5@sympatico.ca>`

```
Well, had you taken the time to READ the notes on initialize, it tells you
that
FILLER,
and
redefines
ARE  N O T   initialized.


    All such elementary receiving fields, including all occurrences of table
items within the group, are affected,  with the following exceptions:

       Index data items

       Object references

       Data items defined with USAGE IS POINTER or USAGE IS
PROCEDURE-POINTER

       Elementary FILLER data items

       Items that are subordinate to identifier-1 and contain a REDEFINES
clause, or any items subordinate to  such an item.  (However, identifier-1
can contain a REDEFINES clause or be subordinate to a redefining  item.)

In other words, since you can't  'MOVE SPACES TO FILLER', initialize ignores
it.
Since you can't move values to an object and to the object that redefines it
as well
AND have them both valid, it only initializes the item being redefined.

01  WS-XX.
   05  ...
   05 ...
   05  TABLEX OCCURS 10 times.
        10  FILLER ..
    05  XX REDEFINES TABLEX.

TABLEX will be inited.


A subscripted item can be specified for identifier-1.  A complete table can
be initialized only by specifying identifier-1 as a group that contains the
complete table.

The description of the data item referenced by identifier-1 or any items
subordinate to identifier-1 cannot
contain the DEPENDING ON phrase of the OCCURS clause.  The description of
the data item described by identifier-1 can contain the DEPENDING phrase of
the OCCURS clause.

A little knowledge is a dangerous thing!


Jean-Ren� Duval wrote in message <381E5F79.65B049E5@sympatico.ca>...
>Thank you all for your comment.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Final notes on intialize statement

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-11-02T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vmd6m$hbp$3@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<381E5F79.65B049E5@sympatico.ca>`

```
    Maybe I missed the comments, but the easy way out would be to insert
another group above the group with an occurs.  Then initialize it.

    Initialize seems to have more holes in it then it deserves, although
this behavior seems reasonable.

Jean-Ren� Duval <jean-ren.duval@sympatico.ca> wrote in message
news:381E5F79.65B049E5@sympatico.ca...
> Thank you all for your comment.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
learning modern COBOL dialects.  Along with replies, I got an e-mail
from a Denver area company which offered a half or full day class in
just that.

class.  We are converting all of our cobol to COBOL for MVS.
```

```
You're correct.  The code generator is "template driven" which means
that anyone can edit the template (it's an ASCII text file) to suit
their needs.

We have customers who have created their own conversion tools and have
a new product coming out in the next few months which generates
HTML/CGI script from the code generator.

</shameless plug>

....my only question...not being as conversant in the OO world as my
friends, Jimmy and Simon....should each screen be its own object or
should each screen *control* be its own object?

....or is it a matter of personal preference?

I guess if each control were its own object, the source program would
grow dramatically in size, which means you now have more code to
maintain.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OO Cobol lesson

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3828127D.42EFB18A@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38207E08.A35E3406@NOSPAMhome.com> <slrn821usp.220.joarmc@linux2.johnmckown.net> <3822CB1D.C09C7538@zip.com.au> <38231f72.14110570@news.enter.net> <382429FC.F55F7D1@zip.com.au> <3824ECDB.B50F5410@home.com> <3825b6c3.3632601@news.enter.net> <804t3m$9cf$1@news.igs.net> <3826c590.903423@news.enter.net>`

```
Bob Wolfe wrote:

Snipped your shameless plug for an excellent product  see
www.flexus.com :-}
> 
> ....my only question...not being as conversant in the OO world as my
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

The simple answer is both.

When you decide to go OO then it really 'should' be an all or nothing
solution.  The screen would derive from a standard screen object and
contain the edit boxes, combo, ???? which and in turn objects derived
from generic solutions.

Surprisingly the code sounds like it is bloated but in reality it is
not.  Most objects just need the simple derivation of the parent and
do not contain their own code.  You might override the on-exit method
to do some validation but the rest is just what the parents give you.

An example of this is an FTP object in Delphi.  I can establish a ftp
client sign on and transmit a few files and it all takes about 10
lines of code.  This is the way it should be done though.

A good OO system has many smaller objects that do reasonably simple
tasks that combine to more functional (but still small) objects which
combine to major functional objects which combine to an application
object.  I have rules of thumb that a method should be 5 lines of code
and there should not be more than 10 methods to a class.  This may be
overkill but it gives you an idea of just how tiered the proper stuff
is.

Thanks
Ken
```

## Thread: PerformVarying - Tables — 8 msgs

### PerformVarying - Tables

- **From:** dc606@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vp65o$lar$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I think the problem is with PERFORM VARYING statement.  Output is not
correct.

1.  First LOCATION VALUE for first record is a blank space.

2.  When values are moved from tables (Title, Location and Education)
to Detail line, the value moved is the value one(1) line before correct
value defined in table.

Any idea why?


 01  TITLE-VALUE.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '010PRESIDENT'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '020VICE PRES'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '030MKT VP'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '040MKT MGR'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '050MKT REP'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '060DP VP'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '070DP MGR'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '080DP PROG'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '090CLERK'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '010ADM ASST'.


       01  TITLE-TABLE REDEFINES TITLE-VALUE.
           05  TITLES OCCURS 10 TIMES.
               10  TITLE-CODE          PIC X(3).
               10  EXP-TITLE           PIC X(15).

           05  REPORT-TITLE            PIC X(10).




       01 LOCATION-VALUE.
          05   PIC X(14)   VALUE 'MIAMIAMI'.
          05   PIC X(14)   VALUE 'CHICHICAGO'.
          05   PIC X(14)   VALUE 'LA LOS ANGELES'.
          05   PIC X(14)   VALUE 'NY NEW YORK'.
          05   PIC X(14)   VALUE 'ATLATLANTA'.

       01 LOCATION-TABLE REDEFINES LOCATION-VALUE.
          05   LOCATIONS OCCURS 5 TIMES.
               10  LOCATION-CODE       PIC X(3).
               10  EXP-LOCATION        PIC X(11).

           05  REPORT-LOCATION         PIC X(14).




       01 EDUCATION-VALUE.
          05  PIC X(15)   VALUE '1GRADE SCHOOL'.
          05  PIC X(15)   VALUE '2HIGH SCHOOL'.
          05  PIC X(15)   VALUE '3ASSOCIATES'.
          05  PIC X(15)   VALUE '4BACHELORS'.
          05  PIC X(15)   VALUE '5MASTERS'.
          05  PIC X(15)   VALUE '6DOCTORATE'.

       01 EDUCATION-TABLE REDEFINES EDUCATION-VALUE.
          05   EDUCATION OCCURS 6 TIMES.
               10  EDUCATION-CODE      PIC 9.
               10  EXP-EDUCATION       PIC X(14).

          05   REPORT-EDUCATION        PIC X(15).


       PROCEDURE DIVISION.


           PERFORM UNTIL WS-END-OF-FILE-SWITCH='YES'
               READ SALARY-FILE
                   AT END
                       MOVE 'YES' TO WS-END-OF-FILE-SWITCH
                   NOT AT END
                       PERFORM 400-CREATE-SALARY-REPORT
                       PERFORM 900-WRITE-DETAIL-LINE
                   END-READ
          END-PERFORM.
          CLOSE SALARY-FILE
                SALARY-REPORT.
          STOP RUN.




          400-CREATE-SALARY-REPORT.
          PERFORM 500-FIND-TITLE
           VARYING WS-TITLE-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL WS-TITLE-SUB > 10
               OR SAL-TITLE-CODE=TITLE-CODE(WS-TITLE-SUB).

          PERFORM 600-FIND-LOCATION
           VARYING WS-LOCATION-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL WS-LOCATION-SUB > 5
                   OR SAL-LOCATION-CODE=LOCATION-CODE(WS-LOCATION-SUB).


           PERFORM 700-FIND-EDUCATION
            VARYING WS-EDUCATION-SUB FROM 1 BY 1
               UNTIL WS-EDUCATION-SUB > 6
                   OR
                   SAL-EDUCATION-CODE=EDUCATION-CODE(WS-EDUCATION-SUB).




           500-FIND-TITLE.
               IF (WS-TITLE-SUB) NOT=10
                   MOVE EXP-TITLE(WS-TITLE-SUB) TO REPORT-TITLE

               END-IF.


           600-FIND-LOCATION.
               IF (WS-LOCATION-SUB) NOT=5
                  MOVE EXP-LOCATION (WS-LOCATION-SUB) TO
                        REPORT-LOCATION
               END-IF.




           700-FIND-EDUCATION.
               IF (WS-EDUCATION-SUB) NOT=6
                  MOVE EXP-EDUCATION (WS-EDUCATION-SUB) TO
                        REPORT-EDUCATION
               END-IF.




           900-WRITE-DETAIL-LINE.
               MOVE SAL-SOC-SEC-NO TO DET-SSN
               MOVE SAL-NAME-AND-INITIALS TO DET-NAME
               MOVE REPORT-TITLE TO DET-TITLE
               MOVE REPORT-LOCATION TO DET-LOCATION
               MOVE REPORT-EDUCATION TO DET-EDUCATION
               MOVE SAL-RATING TO DET-RATING
               MOVE SAL-SALARY TO DET-SALARY
               WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL-LINE


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: PerformVarying - Tables

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4B33E7A1C94A603F.82DE4253412820EF.89F739B9C7CC59F7@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<7vp65o$lar$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Wed, 03 Nov 1999 11:26:48 GMT, dc606@my-deja.com enlightened us:

>I think the problem is with PERFORM VARYING statement.  Output is not
>correct.
…[143 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.

Maybe this will help.  Given the format of:

PERFORM procedure-name VARYING identifier-1 FROM literal-2 BY
literal-3 UNTIL condition-1 OR condition-2

The logic flow if the PERFORM verb with VARYING and UNTIL options is:

A.  First time, identifier-1 is set equal to literal-2;

B.  Check condition-1 if true exit procedure-name

C.  Check condition-2 if true exit procedure-name

D.  Execute statements in procedure-name

E.  identifier-1 is incremented by literal-3

F.  Loop back to B.

Regards,
  
          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Whenever I find myself in a difficult situation, I ask myself "What
Would Jesus Do?"  The mental image of my opposition being cast into
pits of hellfire for all eternity *is* comforting, but probably not
what the inventors of the phrase had in mind.


Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: PerformVarying - Tables

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vpa1n$kcf@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<7vp65o$lar$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The UNTIL clause on the PERFORM is tested BEFORE the paragraph is performed.
So if the correct entry is in position 5 of the table the paragraph is
performed with subscripts of 1,2,3,4 but not 5.  There are a number of
correct solutions to this problem which I'm sure others will be happy to
supply (SEARCH ALL, etc.).  But since your code seems to expect to always
find a match, the easiest way to fix your program without rewriting much of
it is to change the equal compare to greater than as well as eliminate the
IF...END-IF construct inside paragraphs 500, 600, and 700 since they are
essentially worthless.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer

dc606@my-deja.com wrote in message <7vp65o$lar$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I think the problem is with PERFORM VARYING statement.  Output is not
>correct.
…[143 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: PerformVarying - Tables

- **From:** "Anton Rusbach" <a.rusbach@coss.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vpc8j$724$1@news1.xs4all.nl>`
- **References:** `<7vp65o$lar$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7vpa1n$kcf@netnews1.apci.com>`

```
> The UNTIL clause on the PERFORM is tested BEFORE the paragraph is
performed.

correct, unless you PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL (Cobol85)


grtx,
Anton
```

#### ↳ Re: PerformVarying - Tables

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991103093302.14508.00001129@ngol04.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7vp65o$lar$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <7vp65o$lar$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, dc606@my-deja.com writes:

>
>I think the problem is with PERFORM VARYING statement.  Output is not
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any idea why?

     <snip>

>
>          400-CREATE-SALARY-REPORT.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
           <snip>

            Each of these PERFORMs are doing a sequential/linear search
            of your table and moving EVERY entry to the print line EXCEPT
            the one you want.   This is because of the UNTIL condition that 
            is by default tested AFTER the procedures and subscript increment.
            You could just as easily remove the paragraph name from the
           perform and move the If test in the paragraph immediately after
           the perform.
```

```
    05 Report-Education     PIC

               10  EXP-TITLE           PIC X(15).

                10  EXP-LOCATION        PIC X(11).

                10  EDUCATION-CODE      PIC 9.
```

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

```
dc606@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Thanks for your comment.
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…

Neither.. We really are heading into personal opinion here.

I prefer the layouts to be totally separate.  Memory is not a major
issue and a really smart compiler would eliminate the unused storage
(I don't think any would today).  This fulfils the 'least surprise'
rule.

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL and HTML Programming?

- **From:** "van Daal, Peter" <Peter.vanDaal@nl.origin-it.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00

## Thread: pic x(5) to pic 9(5) — 16 this month / 18 total

### pic x(5) to pic 9(5)

- **From:** webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr>`

```
Hi

I have the following variables :

01 var-x           pic x(5).
01 var-9           pic 9(5).

My problem is that var-x contains
numeric date align on the left and with
blank on the right (ie : 15 + 3 blanks
on the right in var-x)

What can I do to obtain 00015 in var-9 ?

thanks

Christophe
```

#### ↳ Re: pic x(5) to pic 9(5)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38208284.5A500A10@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr>`

```
webmaster wrote:
> 
> Hi
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Christophe

several possibilities:
If you know your format, do a redefines to 3 characters.  

If you have a new compiler use NUMVAL().

You can look at each character (redefines & occurs), from right to left
- the first numeric character gets placed in your receiving field, the
next gets multiplied by 10 and added to receiving field, etc.

You can inspect to see how many numeric characters you have, then only
move that many characters using reference modification.  (I suspect this
is what you will want to do)
```

#### ↳ Re: pic x(5) to pic 9(5)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vq2if$s43$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr>`

```
webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr> wrote:

: 01 var-x           pic x(5).
: 01 var-9           pic 9(5).

: blank on the right (ie : 15 + 3 blanks
: on the right in var-x)

: What can I do to obtain 00015 in var-9 ?

      COMPUTE Var-9 = FUNCTION NUMVAL(Var-X)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: pic x(5) to pic 9(5)

- **From:** Alex Flinsch <aflinsch@att.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382194BA.63202D7@att.com>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:

> In article <3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr>, webmaster  <webmaster@eii.fr> wrote:
> >Hi
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> DD

Normally I would agree with DD, but it is a slow day here. So here goes

WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01  CONFUSING-WAY-TO-DO-IT.
    05  VAR-X       PIC X(05) VALUE '15   '.
    05  VAR-9       PIC 9(05) VALUE ZERO.
    05  VAR-9X REDEFINES VAR-9 PIC X(05).
    05  VAR-X-SWAP-IN.
        10  VAR-X-SWP4  PIC X(04).
        10  VAR-X-SWP1  PIC X(01).
    05  VAR-X-SWAP-OUT.
        10  VAR-X-SWP1  PIC X(01).
        10  VAR-X-SWP4  PIC X(04).
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
0000-MAIN.
     MOVE VAR-X TO VAR-X-SWAP-IN
     PERFORM UNTIL VAR-X-SWP1 OF VAR-X-SWAP-IN NOT = SPACE
        MOVE CORRESPONDING VAR-X-SWAP-IN TO VAR-X-SWAP-OUT
        MOVE VAR-X-SWAP-OUT TO VAR-X-SWAP-IN
     END-PERFORM
     MOVE VAR-X-SWAP-IN TO VAR-X
     INSPECT VAR-X REPLACING LEADING SPACES BY '0'
     MOVE VAR-X TO VAR-9X
     DISPLAY VAR-9
     GOBACK..
```

#### ↳ Re: pic x(5) to pic 9(5)

- **From:** "Robert Heady" <r.heady@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lFoU3.2$rC1.1791@client>`
- **References:** `<3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr>`

```
webmaster wrote in message <3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr>...
>Hi
>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The easiest way I know is:

       01  var-x                   pic x(5) value '15   '.
       01  var-red redefines var-x pic z(5).
       01  var-9                   pic 9(5).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       A.
           Move var-red to var-9.
           Stop run.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: pic x(5) to pic 9(5)

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vt69u$o25$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr> <lFoU3.2$rC1.1791@client>`

```
If you have a field defined as  PIC Z(5) and that data item includes "15   "
(3 trailing spaces), then you have INCOMPATIBLE DATA - and all "bets are
off".  The data would have to be "   15" (3 leading spaces) for it to be
defined.

As stated much earlier in this thread, the NUMVAL intrinsic function would do
what was requested - but certainly NOT just redefining it as PIC Z(5)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: pic x(5) to pic 9(5)

- **From:** "Rob Heady" <r.heady.nospam@flash.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zAPU3.2044$OQ.262394@news.flash.net>`
- **References:** `<3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr> <lFoU3.2$rC1.1791@client> <7vt69u$o25$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
The original post did not specify which COBOL vendor he was using.  The
solution I provided works with RM/COBOL and Acucobol.  It is true that MF
COBOL will return:  Error 163 Illegal character in Numeric Field when the
contents of the PIC Z(5) have trailing spaces.   The standard does not
specify the action to take when the data item value does not conform to the
PICTURE character-string.  So neither RM/COBOL nor MF COBOL are wrong in
what they do, at least in regards what Standard COBOL specifies.  I should
have qualified my response with: Depending on how strict the enforcement of
the PICTURE character-string of the COBOL you are using ....
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: pic x(5) to pic 9(5)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<801at8$tm6$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr> <lFoU3.2$rC1.1791@client> <7vt69u$o25$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <zAPU3.2044$OQ.262394@news.flash.net>`

```
The thing to remember about incompatible data is that what your compiler does
may vary from release to release - not just from compiler to compiler.  (Ask
any IBM mainframe COBOL programmer about S0C7s in OS/VS COBOL vs VS COBOL
II).  You are correct that the Standard does NOT say what needs to happen
with incompatible data l- but  what any vendor does with it today is NOT
something you should rely on.

FYI,
  The draft of the next Standard *does* explicitly provide for an "exception"
to be raised by this situation.  What you (the programmer) do with it, is
still up to you, but I sure would not develop new code relying on specific
compiler behavior in this case.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: pic x(5) to pic 9(5)

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E6rU3.819$_Y3.28503@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr> <lFoU3.2$rC1.1791@client>`

```
I can't stand it anymore!

If you don't have/ aren't permitted to use FUNCTION NUMVAL,

I have the following variables :
>>
>>01 var-x           pic x(5).
>>01 var-9           pic 9(5).

ADD: 10 VAR-X-9  REDEFINES VAR-X PIC 9(05).
>>

MOVE ZERO TO SPACE-COUNTER
INSPECT VAR-X TALLYING SPACE-COUNTER FOR ALL SPACE
INSPECT VAR-X  REPLACING ALL SPACE BY '0'
PERFORM SPACE-COUNTER TIMES
  DIVIDE 10 INTO VAR-X-9 END-DIVIDE
END-PERFORM
MOVE VAR-X-9  TO VAR-9.

(Obviously, assumes VAR-X is left justified) (So add you own edits for
'numeric')

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: pic x(5) to pic 9(5)

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vsa90$74j$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr>`

```
webmaster wrote in message <3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr>...
>Hi
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>What can I do to obtain 00015 in var-9 ?

This is interesting, do you know for sure that you are getting 2 digit
numbers, or will the number always be left justified and may be of variable
size? If the latter is true, how do you distinguish 1 and 10?

    10000
    10000

Just asking.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: pic x(5) to pic 9(5)

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ud9U3.547$GH5.20425@viper>`
- **References:** `<3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr>`

```
Your problem seems to be that your originating field is JUSTIFIED LEFT and
the way you want it to look would be JUSTIFIED RIGHT.

Look up the JUSTIFIED RIGHT clause. If you define the PIC X field with a
JUSTIFIED RIGHT CLAUSE when the values are originally filled in to the PIC X
field will be filled in properly. Thus a MOVE from the PIC X to the PIC 9
field will properly convert the field if leading ZZZs are used. ex: PIC
ZZZZ9.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: pic x(5) to pic 9(5)

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vrahl$8bi$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<3820885E.52EDB6C6@eii.fr> <ud9U3.547$GH5.20425@viper>`

```
Gumbo <a@a.com> wrote:
: Your problem seems to be that your originating field is JUSTIFIED LEFT and
: the way you want it to look would be JUSTIFIED RIGHT.

: Look up the JUSTIFIED RIGHT clause. If you define the PIC X field with a
: JUSTIFIED RIGHT CLAUSE when the values are originally filled in to the PIC X
: field will be filled in properly. 

I think that you misunderstand the JUSTIFIED clause.  It will
not drop spaces in the right hand end of a field.  This may
depend on how you think the field is 'originally filled', but
for the purpose you describe it probably won't work.

That is to say if the value ws 'originally filled' by
     MOVE "15"       TO Var-X

then JUST RIGHT will work, but so will "   15" and this is
unlikely to be realistic.

: Thus a MOVE from the PIC X to the PIC 9
: field will properly convert the field if leading ZZZs are used. ex: PIC
: ZZZZ9.

It is Var-X that needs to be PIC ZZZZ9 for this to work, not the
Var-9 fied which should still be PIC 9(5).  ANSI-85 can do
'de-editing' as long as the digits are aligned correctly and
are valid for the picture.

MicroFocus can de-edit in a move from an edited numeric to a
numeric regardless of alignment (with compiler flag DE-EDIT"1")
so that:

         01  Var-X.
             03  Var-E            PIC ZZZZ9.
         01  Var-9                PIC 9(5).

             MOVE "15"       TO Var-X
             MOVE Var-E      TO Var-9

results in the correct value (15) being in the Var-9 field.
This should be available with the next standard (as I
understand it).
```

---

<a id="t-a0830db459"></a>
## Thread: Timer that counts down — 5 msgs

### Timer that counts down

- **From:** Coolie <scheperNOscSPAM@wanadoo.be.invalid>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<004aa0e3.5a755228@usw-ex0101-008.remarq.com>`

```
Hye,

I'm a Belgian student and I'm currently writing a cobol- program in
CaRealia-Workbench.


I would like to have a timer that counts down (vsually) and which can
be interrupted by a keystroke.


Ex: When the user does something wrong, there appears a warningbox
which contains a timer that counts down, if the counter reaches 0, the
warningbox disappears. But the user can exit the warningbox by pressing
a key.


Does anybody know the programcode for this function.

Thanks to anybody who can help me.

Greetings, Wim Verschueren





* Sent from RemarQ http://www.remarq.com The Internet's Discussion Network *
The fastest and easiest way to search and participate in Usenet - Free!
```

#### ↳ Re: Timer that counts down

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vsq5s$q0o$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```

Coolie wrote in message <004aa0e3.5a755228@usw-ex0101-008.remarq.com>...
>Hye,
>
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Greetings, Wim Verschueren


Show us your code attempt as it stands(if any).

As far as program code their isn't any function which will do this: hint:
you simply have to accept the time then test the second's variable for
change, if so then update the static text if GUI is used.

You will have to register this method as idle, in order for the rest of the
application to work while the counter is counting down.
If you dont understand what I mean by idle then if I were you (being a
newbie) ignore the timer.
I often use this method for status bars, updating the system status or time,
and I think it's the best method for using Cobol.


Simon R Hart
Eaton, Ottery, St. Mary, UK.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Timer that counts down

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00

## Thread: Fujitsu COBOL debugger — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu COBOL debugger

- **From:** Shawn Motard <924511@ican.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<381FCA60.DC9CC403@ican.net>`

```
Hello all,

I was just wondering if anyone knew of a place on the net where I could
get the debugging feature for the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.  I got a
version in the book I bought for school, but since it is an educational
version, the debugger is not included.  If anyone can help me out (I
guess my classmates too), I would GREATLY appreciate it.

Thank in advance to all who reply,

Shawn  :)
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu COBOL debugger

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3821977e.34591029@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<381FCA60.DC9CC403@ican.net>`

```
On Wed, 03 Nov 1999 00:38:40 -0500, Shawn Motard <924511@ican.net>
wrote:

>Hello all,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thank in advance to all who reply,

Shawn, what book are you using?  I was unaware of any version shipped
with a book without a debugger.  If you have Sams Teach Yourself COBOL
in 24 Hours (by moi) you should have the debugger.  Watch the screen
cam movie from Hour 11 to see how to setup a compile to active it and
run it.

If you do NOT have my book, which book are you using?  I'm curious!

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

```
Well I learn something new every day.  That text used to use the
CA-Realia compiler I think.  So they are using Fujitsu now.  Are you
SURE the debugger is not included?  Look for: WINSVD.EXE.  You have to
compile with the TEST directive, and Link with the debug option.
```

#### ↳ Re: ascii codes HELP

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Al_T3.5052$B15.58032@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<E9XT3.2535$7O3.60091@sodalite.nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
Serge Mallais wrote:
>How can I get the cents sign " � " to display on a report?

If you're talking about a DOS program, move hex literal X"9B" to a
PIC X field on print line, and it should work, or use a '$' format
character and do an INSPECT replacing "$" with X"9B".  If it's for
Windows, I don't have a clue. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ascii codes HELP

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38220C0F.DA694CBF@home.com>`
- **References:** `<E9XT3.2535$7O3.60091@sodalite.nbnet.nb.ca> <Al_T3.5052$B15.58032@news3.mia>`

```


Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Serge Mallais wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> character and do an INSPECT replacing "$" with X"9B".

>  If it's for  Windows, I don't have a clue. :-)

And nor do I ! Having an option to show Imperial/Metric I wanted my GUI
displays to show, for temperatures, the degree symbol followed by one
character for (F)arenheit or (C)elsius. It wont let you 'cheat' by
putting in x"??" for the degree symbol. Very unsatisfctory, but you
probably have to select a different font - just for one character !!!!.

Dear Mr. Gates, howz about...........

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: ascii codes HELP

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vtt8g$hck$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<E9XT3.2535$7O3.60091@sodalite.nbnet.nb.ca> <Al_T3.5052$B15.58032@news3.mia> <38220C0F.DA694CBF@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <38220C0F.DA694CBF@home.com>...
>probably have to select a different font - just for one character !!!!.

Not so, I have tried to get the person who raised the question to tell me
where he has the problem, without success, for you on the other side. This
is no big deal.

While MSDOS was locked to its code pages and the limitation of 127/256,
windows Ansi is way larger and provides you with the cent sign as well.
Without the need to alter font. Font changes will generally not be necessary
as long as you are inside your own language group. Although language group
may be a little to extensive terminology here, it is the best I can come up
with right now.

Do a document in word or what ever, get your cent sign in, save as a text
file, load the file in Cobol and use a redefines and you will get the value.
The cent sign should show up in ansi as value 162.
�

Enjoy.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any one have [menuing system source] in cobol ?

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38220086.4ABA0A58@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3820A768.9DB3AC@home.com> <19991103182446.02794.00000786@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```


Sff5ky wrote:
> 
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> consistent regardless of the compiler.

I'm not quite clear what ws.min is after, but his two messages to me :-
...........
hi, 
is menuing system with GUI in dos.
...........
hi,
sorry .. 
some thing to add, 
compiler is either fujitsu or coboli(wrong spelling?)
Menuing system in dos base with gui.
...............

Thane or other Fujitsu users - can you help ? (Is he saying he wants to
run a DOS window in Win98 ?)

ff5ky - regarding your query (and I'm sure the authors wont mind me
quoting them) :-

(a) Simple DOS ANSI DISPLAY :-

Arranga/Coyle - OO COBOL  :-

Move spaces to user-selections
initialize message-information

Display "	Library system	"
Display "			"
Display "	1. Check-out	"
Display	"	2. Check-in	"
Display "			"
Display "	Please select 1, 2 or 3 "
 with no advancing
Accept user-selection

Evaluate user-selection
	when 1....
	when 2...
	when 3...
	when etc...
End-evaluate


(b) Screen Section :-

Thane Hubbell - TYC24Hours :-

000263 Screen Section.
000264 01  Main-Screen
000265     Blank Screen, Auto, Required,
000266     Foreground-Color Is 7,
000267     Background-Color Is 1.
000268     03  Line 1 Column 29 Value "Program Selection Menu".
000269     03  Line 3 Column 1  Value "F1  Telephone Number Format".
000270     03  Line 5 Column 1  Value "F2  Days Between Dates".
000271     03  Line 7 Column 1  Value "F3  Exit".
000272     03  Line 9 Column 1  Pic X To Dummy-Field Secure.
000285 Procedure Division.
000286 Chapt023a-Start.
000287     Perform Until All-Done
000288       Display Main-Screen
000297       Accept Main-Screen
000298       Evaluate True
000307          When F1-Pressed
000317               Call "Chapt23b"
000318               Cancel "Chapt23b"
000327          When F2-Pressed
000337               Call "Chapt23c"
000338          When F3-Pressed
000339               Set All-Done To True
000347          When Other
000357               Continue
000367       End-Evaluate
000377     End-Perform
000460     Stop Run

As regards the Screen Section, I would think the various compilers are
pretty compatible - with the exception of AcuCobol, which offers the
best of both worlds, a Screen Section which does Text or GUI.

For info - in the M/F DOS version, I had an indexed file containing menu
text lines and a program makemenu.cbl which accessed this file to
create text menus using Screen Section.

(c) GUI - now that's a very different baby. Take NetExpress as an
example :-

(i) 
Their own own Dialog System will create dialogs and menus - I don't use
it. (Their demo example uses RECURSION to produce the menu - but I did
use this demo as the basis for creating (iii) below).

(ii)
Using the same concept as C/C++ they also provide a Micro Focus      
Dialog Editor, (based on M/S Dialog Editor); coupled to this you create
a file mymenu.mnu which is included in your Resource File, compiled to
Resource.dll, and is callable from (1) Structured, (2) calls to Win API
(which Ken Mullins uses) (3) and I can use the same Resource file as Ken
to call from OO.

(iii)
Approach (ii) necessitates - design the menu, generate an h.cpy file for 
M/S linking (#define CUSTOMER_NAME 1001) and a file of 	Level 78s (78
CUSTOMER-NAME value 1001) which is a 'carbon copy' of the h.cpy file, to
be used in your program. I find this a bit tedious, so having drafted
what the menu should look like, using mywindow.cbl I have a table in
Working-Storage and then pass an OO OrderedCollection to makemenu.cbl to
generate the MenuBar and SubMenus. My logic for doing it this way - If I
add a menu item I need only change/recompile two files (a) The
business.cbl to handle the new event and (b) mywindow.cbl to include the
additional item in Working-Storage; makemenu.cbl remains unchanged.

Another feature tied to approach (iii); I currently have 62 items in my
MasterMenu, excluding separators, and five lines which are :-

Edit Files	Data Entry
   Items	 Readings - enter history
		 Readings - with Items (Item data already setup)
		 Readings - without items (Item not yet setup)
		 Readings - enquiries (view records - no editing)

All five access the same program(class); when setting up the menu events
I have the option of using two parameters. For all five I use the first
parameter to access a common method and then use the second parameter to 
pass the option 1 through 5 - and of course, the invoked/called
class/program behaves differently, depending upon the option chosen.

Note the procedures above for Dialog System and Dialog Editor are
specific to the N/E compiler. I don't think it will give you any
assistance with Fujitsu, but if you want to see my source, e-mail me
direct.

Now perhaps some of the other compiler users can illustrate with the
tools they use - Thane - Fujitsu and SP2 ?, Cheesle - Acucobol, (showing
text/GUI) ? and Liant etc ????  Judson - do you want to add anything ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any one have [menuing system source] in cobol ?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00

```
I can't really demonstrate it in a message.  It's graphical.
Basically you make your menu items using the panel editor (although
you can do it dynamically, I rarely do) then assign numbers ot the
menu options.  When the user chooses a menu option, sp2 tells you
which numbered menu option was returned.  Code accordingly.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Any one have [menuing system source] in cobol ?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vv1ev$2ta$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<3820A768.9DB3AC@home.com> <19991103182446.02794.00000786@ng-cm1.aol.com> <38220086.4ABA0A58@home.com> <382282c1.27767318@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote in message <382282c1.27767318@news1.attglobal.net>...
>>tools they use - Thane - Fujitsu and SP2 ?, Cheesle - Acucobol, (showing

Acucobol has a platform independent menu system, proprietary of course, here
is a sample:

Notice that this sample implies a lot of assumptions, it is made in a hurry
to provide a generic understanding of the concept, not to provide a fully
functional demo. For those who want that I would refer to www.acucorp.com
for running samples.

      *Escape key place holder.
       77  KEY-ENTERED                         9(03).

      * IDs for items on the menu
       78  MENU-EXIT                           VALUE 101.
       78  MENU-CHANGE-SIGN                    VALUE 201.
       78  MENU-CLEAR-ENTRY                    VALUE 202.
       78  MENU-CLEAR-ALL                      VALUE 203.

      *Create Menu root.
           CALL    "W$MENU"         USING
                   WMENU-NEW.
      *If the menu creation returns zero, it is either out of memory or
      *something else bad is going on.
           IF      RETURN-CODE      = ZERO
                   GOBACK.

           MOVE    RETURN-CODE      TO MENU-HANDLE.
      *Add top level item "Exit".
           CALL    "W$MENU"         USING
                   WMENU-ADD
                   MENU-HANDLE
                   0
                   0
                   "E&xit"
                   MENU-EXIT.

           CALL    "W$MENU"         USING
                   WMENU-NEW.

           IF      RETURN-CODE      = ZERO
                   GOBACK.

           MOVE    RETURN-CODE      TO SUB-HANDLE-1.
      *Add top level item "Edit"
           CALL    "W$MENU"         USING
                   WMENU-ADD
                   MENU-HANDLE
                   0
                   0
                   "&Edit"
                   0
                   SUB-HANDLE-1.
      *Add sub item "Change sign" to "Edit"
           CALL    "W$MENU"         USING
                   WMENU-ADD
                   SUB-HANDLE-1
                   0
                   0
                   "Change &sign"
                   MENU-CHANGE-SIGN.
      *Make a separator after "Change sign" and before next item
           CALL    "W$MENU"         USING
                   WMENU-ADD
                   SUB-HANDLE-1
                   0
                   W-SEPARATOR.
      *Add sub item Clear entry to "Edit"
           CALL    "W$MENU"         USING
                   WMENU-ADD
                   SUB-HANDLE-1
                   0
                   0
                   "Clear &entry"
                   MENU-CLEAR-ENTRY.
      *Add sub item "Clear all" to "Edit"
           CALL    "W$MENU"         USING
                   WMENU-ADD
                   SUB-HANDLE-1
                   0
                   0
                   "&Clear all"
                   MENU-CLEAR-ALL.

      *Show the menu
           CALL    "W$MENU"         USING
                   WMENU-SHOW
                   MENU-HANDLE.

      *Any accept will provide the menu selection as exception from now on.
      *We may want to disable the menu if we want, and we can hide it.
      *Unless we actually delete it, it will live as long as the runtime
      *executes.

      * Delete a menu

           CALL    "W$MENU"         USING
                   WMENU-DESTROY
                   MENU-HANDLE.

      * Check mark a menu

           CALL    "W$MENU"         USING
                   WMENU-CHECK
                   MENU-HANDLE
                   MENU-CLEAR-ALL.


      *Example of an entry with a menu established in the background;

           PERFORM UNTIL            1 = 2
                   ACCEPT           ONE-CHAR
                                    LINE 1
                                    AUTOTERMINATE
                                    NO-ECHO
                                    ON EXCEPTION
                                       KEY-ENTERED
                                       CONTINUE
                                    END-ACCEPT

      * Handle exception keys here
                   IF               ONE-CHAR = SPACE

                                    EVALUATE KEY-ENTERED
                                      WHEN   MENU-EXIT
                                             STOP RUN
                                      WHEN   MENU-CHANGE-SIGN
      *Do something
                                      WHEN   MENU-CLEAR-ALL
      *Do something
                                    END-EVALUATE

                                    EXIT PERFORM CYCLE

                                    END-IF

      * Handle data keys here
               EVALUATE ONE-CHAR
                   WHEN '0' THRU '9'
      *Do something
                   WHEN DEC-POINT
      *Do something
                   WHEN OTHER
      *Do something
               END-EVALUATE

           END-PERFORM.

The menu provided above will appear like this:

 Exit Edit

Where Edit will have the following submenu:

  Change sign
  -----------
  Clear entry
  Clear all

This menu will appear and the application logic behave equally, no matter
whether the host is gui or character.

Although I am not able to show it here, & notation and underlined characters
are supported.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: Any one have [menuing system source] in cobol ?

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ahWT3.4598$B15.53321@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<382012dd.0@news.tm.net.my>`

```
Monkey wrote:
>
>Any one have [menuing system source/example] in cobol ?
>could u send it 4 me ? . or ... where to download ?

If you mean menus using the Screen Section, then get a copy of Sams
"COBOL Unleashed", ISBN 0-672-31254-9, by Jon Wessler, et al.  Look in
Chapter 25, "Character and Screen-Based Interfaces", and you will find
an example application.  The entire source code is on the CD (plus
lots of other goodies, including Fujitsu COBOL v3).
```

## Thread: problem with DEPENDING ON — 10 msgs

### problem with DEPENDING ON

- **From:** Sylvain DUVAL <sylvain.duval@village.uunet.be>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3820BB89.E78E78A9@village.uunet.be>`

```
01 TABATT.
    02 elt-att occurs 8.
        03 TRefAtt      pic x(5).
        03 TQPartAtt    pic 9.
        03 elt-attente occurs 0 to 9 depending on TQPartAtt.
            04 TDateAtt   pic 9(4).
            04 TNomPreAtt pic x(2).
            04 TAdrAtt    pic x(2).


There's a problem with the "depending on TQPartAtt"
```

#### ↳ Re: problem with DEPENDING ON

- **From:** Alex Flinsch <aflinsch@att.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382195E6.EF1776BD@att.com>`
- **References:** `<3820BB89.E78E78A9@village.uunet.be>`

```


Sylvain DUVAL wrote:

> 01 TABATT.
>     02 elt-att occurs 8.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> There's a problem with the "depending on TQPartAtt"

The problem is that TQPartAtt is indexed and the compilier cannot
determine which TQPartAtt you are referring to.
I have often wished that the above were a legal construct myself, as I
have come across several situations where it could be used effectivvlly
```

#### ↳ Re: problem with DEPENDING ON

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991103194803.11480.00000650@ng-bj1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3820BB89.E78E78A9@village.uunet.be>`

```
>
>There's a problem with the "depending on TQPartAtt"
>


I tried this out in Fujitsu and found that it does not like having the ODO
field within another occurs.

      *
       01 TABATT.
           02 elt-att occurs 8.
               03 TRefAtt      pic x(5).
               03 TQPartAtt    pic 9.
      *  DATA-NAME 'TQPartAtt' of occurs clause must not be 
      *         an item that requires subscript
               03 elt-attente        occurs 0 to 9
                                     depending on TQPartAtt.
                   04 TDateAtt   pic 9(4).
                   04 TNomPreAtt pic x(2).
                   04 TAdrAtt    pic x(2).
      *
```

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382dd06b.172256611@news2.ibm.net>`

```
On 04 Nov 1999 00:48:03 GMT, sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky) wrote:

>>
>>There's a problem with the "depending on TQPartAtt"
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>               03 elt-attente        occurs 0 to 9

Shouldn't that be 1 to 0??


>                                     depending on TQPartAtt.
>                   04 TDateAtt   pic 9(4).
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Also, in my book (IBM COBOL for ttis and that) it says that ODO cannot be used inside an
OCCURS


     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         2 + 2 = 5 for extremely large values of 2.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: problem with DEPENDING ON

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vrtk0$uek$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3820BB89.E78E78A9@village.uunet.be> <19991103194803.11480.00000650@ng-bj1.aol.com>`

```
Having an ODO (Occurs Depending ON) under another OCCURS clause *or* having
an OCCURS clause under an ODO is an "old" IBM extension. (I think it may have
been legal in the '68 Standard - but its rules were contradictory).  It is
explicitly DIS-allowed in the '85 Standard (and the draft of the next
Standard).  Some vendors still support it - IBM calls it a "complex ODO" -
and documents it as an extension.
```

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00

```

Can you or anybody else explain why messages don't get delivered. Often
I'll reply to a message, (and I'm not talking about taking out the
'spam' bit), 'Reply to All' that is the originator and the NewsGroup.
The originator's mail gets rejected, sometimes with messages like, "You
are not authorised to contact IBM......"  $$@##*&& !!!

I've had problems sending to Mr. Klein, when it was "idx.com ?' and also
Ray Leech. I have you both setup in my Address Book, so outside of NG
when I generate "New Message" and select either of you, the message gets
delivered. ???
 
Bill - I've taken 'nospam' out of your address - be curious to see if it
arrives.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Undelivered messages

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vsle7$u1t$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3820BB89.E78E78A9@village.uunet.be> <19991103194803.11480.00000650@ng-bj1.aol.com> <7vrtk0$uek$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <3821D890.51A48E63@home.com>`

```
Yes it was wet (on and off) - seemed a whole lot like the UK in October and
November.

As for undeliverable mail, many pieces of software used for reading and
posting to newsgroups have a place to put "reply to id".  (I know that all
the variations on Outlook do - and I think others do as well).

If you put some "spam" rejecter in this field, you will cut down on
"automatically generated" spam that comes from those developing mailing lists
from NG posts.   I have done this - as have several others.  I also include a
"spelled out" version of my ID that can be used in my signature line.   I
hope that not too many people are bothered by needing to "forward" or CC
notes to me - rather than using REPLY - but it HAS cut down on the spam that
I get.  (My ID in the FAQ still causes me to get more than I like, but that
seems worth it to me)

Another "issue" is that some ISPs are set up so that you can either default
to REJECTING mail from specific other ISPs (e.g. hotmail) - or where you have
to explicitly ALLOW a user to contact you.  Again, this is to prevent SPAM
but is a step that at least I haven't taken yet.
```

```
Jimmy
```

<a id="t-a8fd5a4d67"></a>
## Thread: ANSI Standards Test Suite? — 3 this month / 4 total

_Topics: [`COBOL standards (ANS, ISO, 74/85/2002/2014)`](topics.md#standards)_

_Thread started [1999-10](1999-10.md#t-a8fd5a4d67)._

#### ↳ Re: ANSI Standards Test Suite?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-03T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vqprl$l20$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7vape5$np3$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The '74 Standard was done on a typewriter (as I recall) - it is certainly NOT
available "on-line" (and I sure hope has NOTHING to do with anything that is
being created now).

The '85 Standard was done in NROFF (with Amendments in various word
processing software). I do not know of any where it is generally available
"on-line" - but you could try contacting the J4 chair to see if it is. (I
think there is a CD "floating around" with some variation of it).

There is a test suite from NIST for determining if a compiler is "conforming"
or not.  Again, the J4 chair could (probably) point you to how to contact
them.  They have gone out of the business of validating ANY programming
languages, so I don't know what has happened to the test suite.

The J4 chair (who is out of town this week), can normally be reached at:
   doncobol <at> mediaone.net
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: ANSI Standards Test Suite?

- **From:** "Tom Morrison" <t.morrison@liant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<pAgU3.42$Aq1.1082@client>`
- **References:** `<7vape5$np3$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <7vqprl$l20$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:7vqprl$l20$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net...
> The '74 Standard was done on a typewriter (as I recall) -

Yep.  Peggy Beard's typewriter, in fact.  A quote from the Forward of
X3.23-1974:

"The members of Technical Committee X3J4 wish to note the special
contribution of the secretary, Miss P. A. Beard, whose devotion and hard
work made this revision possible."

Peggy Beard is still hard at work, though not in COBOL standardization.
She came by our offices just a couple of weeks ago while on a business trip
through Austin.  While the late Adm. Grace Hopper gets the publicity, and
justly so, there were several women who were key contributors to the COBOL
standardization effort.  Peggy's name should be on the COBOL Honor Roll.

Tom Morrison
Liant Software Corporation
```

