###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting code

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3827F2F1.AFD23964@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<7vtlff$gp5$1@news.igs.net> <7vu6a0$ppf$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3822DFB1.3F5A96D5@zip.com.au> <7vuuvh$4jl$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3824328A.CC742191@zip.com.au> <3826DF9F.F6816A5F@NOSPAMhome.com> <_0GV3.1002$VO5.11333@news3.mia> <382766CD.1F27337E@worldnet.att.net> <808qcg$ok5$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`

```
Rick Price wrote:
> 
> Yeah but how many versions back do you go?

Endevor supports 100 versions before you have to compress (I believe). 
We've only had it about six years, so that's more than enough so far.
> 
> And how do you decide what was replaced with what?

Endevor also has a "browse history" function, so you can view
added/deleted lines relative to the version you are looking at. 
Sometimes I use superc because I don't want to fire up another TSO app.

> 
> I like well commented programs.  As far as I'm concerned, commented out code
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rick

It's certainly a debatable subject.  Some people find interspersed
comments of any variety offensive.  My boss didn't like commented-out
code because he felt it made the programs harder to understand.  I
tended to agree with him, but he had felt very strongly about it so it
was best to stay on his good side.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting code

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3828153B.E5D82F7E@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<7vtlff$gp5$1@news.igs.net> <7vu6a0$ppf$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3822DFB1.3F5A96D5@zip.com.au> <7vuuvh$4jl$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3824328A.CC742191@zip.com.au> <3826DF9F.F6816A5F@NOSPAMhome.com> <_0GV3.1002$VO5.11333@news3.mia> <382766CD.1F27337E@worldnet.att.net> <808qcg$ok5$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`

```
Rick Price wrote:
> 
> I like well commented programs.  As far as I'm concerned, commented
> out code is comments.  Sure when it becomes redundant it can be
> removed but where's the pain?

I will mostly disown this opinion but it is interesting to consider...

Comments should not exist in code.  If the code requires a comment you
can either rename variables to should the true purpose or separate the
function into a separate called module (perform) this effectively
becomes your comment and eliminates it.

This has the benefit of extracting smaller functions that can be
easily reused in other sections and assists removing of duplicated
code.

....

I have paraphrased a comment from comp.object.  I kind of agree with
it. Comments should be restricted to the why.

I am currently reading refactoring by Martin Fowler.  He says
something similar to the above.  Once I have finished the book I will
attempt to give a summary.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting code

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3828671B.8C57531B@home.com>`
- **References:** `<7vtlff$gp5$1@news.igs.net> <7vu6a0$ppf$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3822DFB1.3F5A96D5@zip.com.au> <7vuuvh$4jl$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3824328A.CC742191@zip.com.au> <3826DF9F.F6816A5F@NOSPAMhome.com> <_0GV3.1002$VO5.11333@news3.mia> <382766CD.1F27337E@worldnet.att.net> <808qcg$ok5$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3828153B.E5D82F7E@zip.com.au>`

```


Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> Rick Price wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> code.

Ken,

This kind of ties-in with your comments about small methods in OO
doesn't it. Generally speaking, I rarely use commenting - but sometimes
it can be useful to have a one or two-liner comment to remind me why I
coded in a particular way, so that I don't fall into the trap of coding
something that I found out previously didn't work.

And like Judson, I don't keep 'junk' to refer to, as to how the program
used to perform - I'm only interested how it works now with its current
function - and it's that I want to change, should the program fail, not
the history.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting code

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<809pq8$it$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<7vtlff$gp5$1@news.igs.net> <7vu6a0$ppf$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3822DFB1.3F5A96D5@zip.com.au> <7vuuvh$4jl$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3824328A.CC742191@zip.com.au> <3826DF9F.F6816A5F@NOSPAMhome.com> <_0GV3.1002$VO5.11333@news3.mia> <382766CD.1F27337E@worldnet.att.net> <808qcg$ok5$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3828153B.E5D82F7E@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au> wrote:

: Comments should not exist in code.  If the code requires a comment you
: can either rename variables to should the true purpose or separate the
: function into a separate called module (perform) this effectively
: becomes your comment and eliminates it.
: ....

I entirely agree.  When maintenance is done the original comments
in a program are often overlooked and not changed to represent
how the program now works.  This may be because, for example, the
comment is at the PERFORM of a piece of code and when the paragraph
itself is changed the programmer does not see them to change them.

Out of date, or simply wrong, comments are worse than no comments
at all.  Clearly written code that concentrates on using
descriptive paragraph names and variable names means the same
to the computer and to the programmer.

Commented code tends to distract the reader and make them think
that the code follows the comment, making the reader less
attentive to what the code _actually_ does.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting code

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80a9ck$gg2$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7vtlff$gp5$1@news.igs.net> <7vu6a0$ppf$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3822DFB1.3F5A96D5@zip.com.au> <7vuuvh$4jl$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3824328A.CC742191@zip.com.au> <3826DF9F.F6816A5F@NOSPAMhome.com> <_0GV3.1002$VO5.11333@news3.mia> <382766CD.1F27337E@worldnet.att.net> <808qcg$ok5$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3828153B.E5D82F7E@zip.com.au> <809pq8$it$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote in message <809pq8
<BIG SNIPPAGE>

>Commented code tends to distract the reader and make them think
>that the code follows the comment, making the reader less
>attentive to what the code _actually_ does.
>
I agree. Recently I have learning OOP stuff, and I have been using the
examples from the Fujitsu 4.0 disk.  I was going nuts trying to understand
what was happening, until I got smart and removed all the comments.  Once I
had taken all the garbage out  (all rather fractured nonsense that said far
less about the code than the code, and made reading the code impossible), I
found the programs *much* easier to understand.  I could see more than a
couple of lines at once, for one thing.

Why anybody would think a list of data names is "documentary" when preceded
by an asterisk, while a record description with picture clauses and level
numbers that shows the inter-relationships and data type is not
"documentary", is beyond me.  I got the impression that the examples were
written by a C coder that did not like Cobol.
```

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Tue, 09 Nov 1999 23:36:11 +1100, Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> particulary tricky code I comment what is being done and why and how.

A rare occasion where I kind of disagree.

If the code is obscure rewrite it to be clearer.  Unless there are
severe penalties on run time (Verified not opinion).  You are often
not optimizing the right thing.

I tend to leave comments in code I could not understand on my
understanding of the code with 'I THINK' in it.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting code

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0YdW3.4952$z26.57947@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<7vtlff$gp5$1@news.igs.net> <7vu6a0$ppf$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3822DFB1.3F5A96D5@zip.com.au> <7vuuvh$4jl$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3824328A.CC742191@zip.com.au> <3826DF9F.F6816A5F@NOSPAMhome.com> <_0GV3.1002$VO5.11333@news3.mia> <382766CD.1F27337E@worldnet.att.net> <808qcg$ok5$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3828153B.E5D82F7E@zip.com.au> <3828ccea.215104609@news1.attglobal.net> <3829411F.52FFAEE4@zip.com.au>`

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
>
>If the code is obscure rewrite it to be clearer.  Unless there are
>severe penalties on run time (Verified not opinion).  You are often
>not optimizing the right thing.

Not always possible. :-)  Sometimes code is based on an algorithm
that is complex or abstract to the point that you could not write
the code in a way as to be clear why you are doing what.  You don't
see this a lot in COBOL programs, but some algorithms can be really
obscure, unless commented.  Calculating the date of Easter, say for
a list of holidays in a payroll program, is not crystal clear. :-)
In such cases, I put a comment block describing the algorithm.  And
also write the code to be as clear as possible. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting code

```
> > Interesting.  Mostly I find myself leaving notes to myself.  "This
> > looks wrong, but it isn't", or "You did this on purpose".   In
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Sometimes the obscurity isn't in the code, it's in the business rules. 
A note to say that this is really what accounting, or purchasing, or the
CIO really wants can be helpful.

The right way, the wrong way, and the Army way...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting code

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38282BE2.7AFB576@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7vtlff$gp5$1@news.igs.net> <7vu6a0$ppf$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3822DFB1.3F5A96D5@zip.com.au> <7vuuvh$4jl$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3824328A.CC742191@zip.com.au> <3826DF9F.F6816A5F@NOSPAMhome.com> <_0GV3.1002$VO5.11333@news3.mia> <382766CD.1F27337E@worldnet.att.net>`

```
Arnold Trembley wrote:

> One of my former bosses hated it as well, and part of our code walkthru
> process was to ensure that no commented-out code was left in the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> anything unusual changed.

That works pretty well if the change you care about is in the very last
migration.  If you have to go though various previous changes, it
doesn't work as well.

I recommend that you have a standard that when code is migrated - all
commented code over, say 3 months old gets deleted UNLESS there is a
***  leave this *** statement.  Let the programmers decide to leave
comments where you have regular changes.

If you have three migrations of different changes within a month, all of
the changes will be easy to see, but more stable code no longer has the
commented code.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting code

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991109114631.11319.00000053@ngol01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<38282BE2.7AFB576@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <38282BE2.7AFB576@NOSPAMhome.com>, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> writes:

>
>I recommend that you have a standard that when code is migrated - all
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I don't usually use a time period  for determining the removal of dead code.
In past exerience, I have used a rule of 3 major maintenance cycles before 
cleaning out dead stuff.  Although there have been exceptions, where a
particular
piece of code handled a unique function/feature that I might want to use as 
a reference at a later date, or where I found code being re-introduced on a 
alternating project cycle.   (delete the code this month, three months of
updates,
then OH BTW! re-instate that code from 4 months ago 'We need it again').

I am not fortunate enough to be working in a shop with any kind of version
control.
Any recovery that I have is done purely thru my own backup/recovery procedures.
Currently involves using IND$FILE to download every member that I have touched
today and PKZIPing the files into an archive that rolls by project or by date 
depending on the PDS that I am capturing from.
```

#### ↳ Re: Interesting code

- **From:** "Don Pace" <donp@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf3385$1137cdc0$4e9e99ce@ns.webzone.net>`
- **References:** `<7vtlff$gp5$1@news.igs.net>`

```
I have been programming in Cobol for quite a number of years.  I see the
concept of putting the period on a line by itself as another one of those
bright ideas of some Professor Popoff that has never spent one day actually
programming in a real environment.  Just another nail on the Cobol coffin.

donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote in article
<7vtlff$gp5$1@news.igs.net>...
I was reading code (by people in my employ) when I ran across this snippet
I
thought interesting. I have not been a proponent of the one period on a
line
style in the past, but I thought this usage rather interesting. The fact
that two lines have been commented out but left in as comments is also
rather interesting, stylistically.  Any thoughts?


           MOVE SELECTOR-TOP TO SP2-WD-TITLE.
           CALL "SP2" USING SP2-OPEN-WINDOW SP2-WINDOW-DEF.
           IF SP2-WD-RET-CODE IS NOT EQUAL TO ZERO
               DISPLAY "SP2-WD-RET-CODE = ", SP2-WD-RET-CODE
      *         STOP RUN
           .
           MOVE LOW-VALUES TO SP2-PD-DATA.
      *     MOVE -1 TO SP2-PD-MENU-ROWS.
           MOVE SELECTOR-TOTAL-WIDTH TO SP2-PD-WIDTH.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Interesting code

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3838948C.EEAEA2CA@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<7vtlff$gp5$1@news.igs.net> <01bf3385$1137cdc0$4e9e99ce@ns.webzone.net> <383a323f.64365048@news.gw.total-web.net>`

```
As someone who has been coding in COBOL since 1963, I vote for the 2
periods per paragraph: 1 after the paragraph name and one at the end of
the paragraph on a line by itself.  It makes wok with the scope
terminators simpler and means that I can move things around in the
paragraph.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca 

Paul Knudsen wrote:
>

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

```

<a id="t-9ec121746d"></a>
## Thread: Fujitsu PowerCOBOL Question — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu PowerCOBOL Question

- **From:** "Anthony Marinaro" <amarinaro@keyb-t.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3826f586.0@news.BVSERVICES>`

```
I am running powercobol on an NT 4 system and cannot link the object code to
an executable. The linker app. returns a fatal error 'LNK1561: entry point
must be defined' and 'U1077: return code 0x68' I was just wondering if
anyone has encountered the same situation or knows how to correct it. Thanks

TM
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu PowerCOBOL Question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<806vqq$2d5$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3826f586.0@news.BVSERVICES>`

```
Either add the "@OPTIONS MAIN" statement or otherwise mark your "main"
program as MAIN.
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu PowerCOBOL Question

- **From:** "Ian Draper" <idraper@dove.net.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38331dff@curlew.mtx.net.au>`
- **References:** `<3826f586.0@news.BVSERVICES>`

```
For the Link error try putting in an END PROGRAM xxxxx. statement as the
last statement of the program. (xxxxx is the program name).

Anthony Marinaro <amarinaro@keyb-t.com> wrote in message
news:3826f586.0@news.BVSERVICES...
> I am running powercobol on an NT 4 system and cannot link the object code
to
> an executable. The linker app. returns a fatal error 'LNK1561: entry point
> must be defined' and 'U1077: return code 0x68' I was just wondering if
> anyone has encountered the same situation or knows how to correct it.
Thanks
>
> TM
>
>
```

<a id="t-1fbc38389f"></a>
## Thread: Calling DD, was: COBOL COMPILED WITH DATA=31: PLUSES & MINUSES? — 42 msgs

### Re: Calling DD, was: COBOL COMPILED WITH DATA=31: PLUSES & MINUSES?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tLlU3.33299$oa2.71287@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<008d01bf24bc$e0f271a0$8df32cc7@cylemon-cyber> <7kepWhCNP4qd-pn2-XWVbfSVO9nYp@localhost> <3821490F.88C3FACD@worldnet.att.net> <3821DF2E.5EFC6BFC@strata-group.Xcom>`

```
In article <3821DF2E.5EFC6BFC@strata-group.Xcom>,
Steve Dover  <swd@strata-group.Xcom> wrote:

[snippimente]

>For performance reasons,
>entire VSAM datasets were loaded into tables in memory.  IIRC, we
>did not run out of addressable memory back then, because the VSAM
>datasets were not that large.

Ah, yes... I've had discussions like

'All we gotta do is make an indexed file with the branch number and name,
that way we can look up the name based on the number'

'Ummmm... how many branches are there?'

'Two hundred and seventy two.'

'Well... with a four-digit branch number and a 31-character name I can
load the file into a table and decrease access time and disk use.'

'Hey, 'consultant', when I want your opinion I'll give it to you.'


>But I can see where it could be a
>problem for some shops.

Weeeeellllll.. when it was we'd do things like define contiguous tables
and refer to elements by 'out of range' subscripts.  Consider:

01  BIG-HURKIN-TBL01.
    05  BHT01-ITEM OCCURS 1000.
        10  BHT01-DEPTNO PIC 9(05).
        10  BHT01-STUFF  PIC X(25).
01  BIG-HURKIN-TBL02.
    05  BHT02-ITEM OCCURS 1000.
        10  BHT02-DEPTNO PIC 9(05).
        10  BHT02-STUFF  PIC X(25).

... and y'load *that* sucker up during 0100-HOUSEKEEPING from your
lookup-table file.  Then when you refer to BHT01-DEPTNO (SUB1) and SUB1 =
1005 you are actually looking at the fifth entry in BIG-HURKIN-TBL02 (or
BHT02-DEPTNO (5).

*Classic* trade-off of resources... decreases EXCPs, increases core use.

DD


(headers expanded to comp.lang.cobol... just to annoy the Grumpy Old
Folks)
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling DD, was: COBOL COMPILED WITH DATA=31: PLUSES & MINUSES?

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vsqii$ecr@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<008d01bf24bc$e0f271a0$8df32cc7@cylemon-cyber> <7kepWhCNP4qd-pn2-XWVbfSVO9nYp@localhost> <3821490F.88C3FACD@worldnet.att.net> <3821DF2E.5EFC6BFC@strata-group.Xcom> <tLlU3.33299$oa2.71287@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message ...
>Weeeeellllll.. when it was we'd do things like define contiguous tables
>and refer to elements by 'out of range' subscripts.  Consider:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Funny you should mention this.  I just had a discussion this morning with
two of our contract COBOL programmers about this same issue.  I was asking
about using COBOL-II vs. COBOL-I.  When they had a situation recently where
a table reached its limits, instead of doing the above (which was one of my
recommendations), they redid the table-load and table-search logic to go to
one table then the other because they didn't know that you could "over
subscript".  They also thought about (finally) converting to COBOL-II, but
there were too many other things which wouldn't have converted (AFTER
POSITIONING, etc.) to make it a clean conversion.  I suppose that's why so
many of our programs are still being compiled under the old compiler.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer
```

- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<382266d2.1351424434@news.bright.net>`
- **References:** `<008d01bf24bc$e0f271a0$8df32cc7@cylemon-cyber> <3821DF2E.5EFC6BFC@strata-group.Xcom> <tLlU3.33299$oa2.71287@iad-read.news.verio.net> <7vsqii$ecr@netnews1.apci.com> <RkmU3.33305$oa2.71736@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

Alan Russell <RUSSELAH@apci.com> wrote:
<<<snip on-topic crap:>>>
->>Funny you should mention this.  I just had a discussion this morning with
->>two of our contract COBOL programmers about this same issue.

docdwarf@clark.net () was, like, :
->What?  I just had my tin-foil-and-egg-salad hat reclaibrated and there are
->*still brainwave-leaks that the Space Aliens can steal?  Maybe I should
->get the pivot-joints checked.

Ah TOL' yuh, DD, ah TOL' yuh! It's the (missing) Special Secret
Ingredient in the Egg Salad; you're lettin' th' Aliens get atcher
brainwaves thru the teevee!

It's not your pivot-joints; your file says everything's fine except
for the missing SSI. You should contact your boss on Secret Channel
2b. Oh, and don't tell anyone the Secret Channel.

<<<snip>>>
->Pfah... yet again proving 'there's always enough time to fix it but never
->enough time to do it right.'

Some months ago, The Maintenance Guy installed an inline filter in the
plant where I work. It's to be cleaned once per shift. He didn't
install *valves* on the thing, so cleaning it meant a 15-20 gallon
MESS.

Weeks later, after much bitching on our part, another Maintenance Man
came to install valves on either side of the filter. The Maintenance
Stuporvisor failed to see any humor in the situation when I brought up
the observation above.

If this sorta thing happens in the IT industry like it happens in
industries where I've worked... I gotta get more groceries!
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Calling DD, was: COBOL COMPILED WITH DATA=31: PLUSES & MINUSES?

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vsunq$cn9$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<008d01bf24bc$e0f271a0$8df32cc7@cylemon-cyber> <7kepWhCNP4qd-pn2-XWVbfSVO9nYp@localhost> <3821490F.88C3FACD@worldnet.att.net> <3821DF2E.5EFC6BFC@strata-group.Xcom> <tLlU3.33299$oa2.71287@iad-read.news.verio.net> <7vsqii$ecr@netnews1.apci.com>`

```
Pretty PLEASE, do NOT assume that two "consecutive" 01-levels in
Working-Storage section will actually be stored in "order" in memory and that
"over-subscripting" will work.

IN FACT, this is a major "migration inhibitor" for some sites going from
OS/VS COBOL to VS COBOL II or IBM COBOL for this-and-that.  The reason that
this becomes a problem is that the "newer" IBM optimizer is able to detect
"unreferenced" working-storage items - and not bother to allocate storage for
them. Therefore, (for example) if you have

  01   Tabl1.
         05 Each-Elem Occurs 32000 times.
             10   Elem  Pic X.
   01 Tabl2.
        05  filler     Pic  X(32000).

If you then ONLY reference "Elem" including the items over 32000 - the newer
IBM compiler will "assume" that you are telling it the truth and you never
use Tabl2.  Hence it gets no storage.  Therefore, any reference to ELEM
(32001) will get WHATEVER happens to appear in storage after Tabl1.

Also (fyi) the use of the EXTERNAL attribute makes WS-items non-consecutive.

For those who think this is an IBM-mainframe only issue, look at the Micro
Focus 01-Shuffle directive (and I suspect several other vendors' options).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Calling DD, was: COBOL COMPILED WITH DATA=31: PLUSES & MINUSES?

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8_rU3.33441$oa2.76740@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<008d01bf24bc$e0f271a0$8df32cc7@cylemon-cyber> <tLlU3.33299$oa2.71287@iad-read.news.verio.net> <7vsqii$ecr@netnews1.apci.com> <7vsunq$cn9$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
In article <7vsunq$cn9$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>,
William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote:
>Pretty PLEASE, do NOT assume that two "consecutive" 01-levels in
>Working-Storage section will actually be stored in "order" in memory and that
>"over-subscripting" will work.

All respect to your experiences, Mr Klein, but I've yet to run across an
example where it didn't work under IKFCBL00.

>
>IN FACT, this is a major "migration inhibitor" for some sites going from
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>(32001) will get WHATEVER happens to appear in storage after Tabl1.

I recall being warned about something like this... which is why in
0100-HOUSEKEEPING there were statements (appropriately commented, of
course) such as:

    PERFORM 0105-INIT-TBLS THRU 0105-IT-EX 
     VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1
      UNTIL SUB1 > MAX-TBL-ENTRIES.

...
0105-INIT-TBLS.

    MOVE BIG-HURKIN-TBL-BLANKLINE TO BHT01-ITEM (SUB1).
    MOVE BIG-HURKIN-TBL-BLANKLINE TO BHT02-ITEM (SUB1)
    MOVE SMALLER-TBL-BLANKLINE    TO SMTB01-ITEM (SUB1).

... etc.

DD

>
>Also (fyi) the use of the EXTERNAL attribute makes WS-items non-consecutive.
…[54 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Calling DD, was: COBOL COMPILED WITH DATA=31: PLUSES & MINUSES?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vu9qq$4f1$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I believe (but won't swear to it) that you are correct that this will always
work with OS/VS COBOL (and was - unfortunately - not that unusual a
technique).  My "warning" is to anyone who is developing (or even
maintaining) code NOT to rely on this in programs intended to work in the
future (or with other compilers)
```

```
Except that tthis is illegal (who cares) and does not work:  If you try this code in COBOL
for this and that, and have the optimizer have its word, it will possibly 

	rearrange the working storage items
	drop the BIG-HURKIN-TBL02 alltogether, as it is not referenced 

Also, it does not allow to use SSRANGE during program development for finding other,
nonplanned index overruns (or underruns, for that matter)
```

```
disasters.  At least that will indicate that the tables aren't
independent.
```

- **References:** `<008d01bf24bc$e0f271a0$8df32cc7@cylemon-cyber> <7kepWhCNP4qd-pn2-XWVbfSVO9nYp@localhost> <3821490F.88C3FACD@worldnet.att.net> <3821DF2E.5EFC6BFC@strata-group.Xcom> <tLlU3.33299$oa2.71287@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38229765.11780291@news1.newscene.com> <7vua15$lvg$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> Sorry - you can't put them under the same 01 level.  The "whole problem" is
> that this (OLD - no longer marketed or supported) compiler (OS/VS COBOL) did
> NOT support 01-levels that were larger than 32K.

When we started converting to COBOL for MVS, I e-mailed people to watch
look for these over subscripted tables (which we have), and convert them
to larger tables.  So far, I haven't heard of any problems due to a
table being missed in the conversion.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Calling DD, was: COBOL COMPILED WITH DATA=31: PLUSES & MINUSES?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Alan Russell" <RUSSELAH@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vv0r4$liu@netnews1.apci.com>`
- **References:** `<008d01bf24bc$e0f271a0$8df32cc7@cylemon-cyber> <7kepWhCNP4qd-pn2-XWVbfSVO9nYp@localhost> <3821490F.88C3FACD@worldnet.att.net> <3821DF2E.5EFC6BFC@strata-group.Xcom> <tLlU3.33299$oa2.71287@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38229765.11780291@news1.newscene.com> <7vua15$lvg$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <3822EC6C.68BAC701@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote in message +ADw-3822EC6C.68BAC701+AEA-NOSPAMhome.com+AD4-...
+AD4-William M. Klein wrote:
+AD4-
+AD4APg- Sorry - you can't put them under the same 01 level.  The +ACI-whole problem+ACI-
is
+AD4APg- that this (OLD - no longer marketed or supported) compiler (OS/VS COBOL)
did
+AD4APg- NOT support 01-levels that were larger than 32K.
+AD4-
+AD4-When we started converting to COBOL for MVS, I e-mailed people to watch
+AD4-look for these over subscripted tables (which we have), and convert them
+AD4-to larger tables.  So far, I haven't heard of any problems due to a
+AD4-table being missed in the conversion.

I totally agree that it's high time that we began the process of getting rid
of the old OS/VS COBOL compiler.  But so far it has been viewed (by
management and techies alike) as a non-value-added project, i.e. as long as
it's not broken, don't fix it.  But, having successfully gotten rid of a
half-dozen old technologies this past year (with Y2K as an incentive),
perhaps I can make some headway this time.

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah+AEA-apci.com
Home - AHRussell+AEA-computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Calling DD, was: COBOL COMPILED WITH DATA=31: PLUSES & MINUSES?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VLGU3.738$UI2.19821@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<008d01bf24bc$e0f271a0$8df32cc7@cylemon-cyber> <7kepWhCNP4qd-pn2-XWVbfSVO9nYp@localhost> <3821490F.88C3FACD@worldnet.att.net> <3821DF2E.5EFC6BFC@strata-group.Xcom> <tLlU3.33299$oa2.71287@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38229765.11780291@news1.newscene.com> <7vua15$lvg$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <3822EC6C.68BAC701@NOSPAMhome.com> <7vv0r4$liu@netnews1.apci.com>`

```
Alan Russell wrote in message <7vv0r4$liu@netnews1.apci.com>...
>I totally agree that it's high time that we began the process of getting
rid
>of the old OS/VS COBOL compiler.  But so far it has been viewed (by
>management and techies alike) as a non-value-added project, i.e. as long as
>it's not broken, don't fix it.  But, having successfully gotten rid of a
>half-dozen old technologies this past year (with Y2K as an incentive),
>perhaps I can make some headway this time.


The incentive I present to my clients to migrate to *at least* VS/COBOL II
is this:

The current generation of COBOL trainees in programming classes are not
learning how to program without scope terminators, EVALUATE, reference
modifiers, etc ... you can see that here in the newsgroup with questions on
"how do I do this without..."

Finding employees or contractors to maintain OS/VS systems will be difficult
and expensive, so migrate now.

(This past week I was doing some of this. Sheesh, I cannot recall the last
time I had seen that many nested "ELSE NEXT SENTENCEs" in one program!).

MCM
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>

```
Alan Russell wrote:

> I totally agree that it's high time that we began the process of getting rid
> of the old OS/VS COBOL compiler.  But so far it has been viewed (by
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> perhaps I can make some headway this time.

Y2K was the big sell here.  We had a mixture of ANSI 68 & ANSI 72, with
a scattering of COBOL II in special applications.  None of the above can
accept a simple date with century the way most vendors COBOL will. 
Going to one compiler is a big sell, although we haven't been able to
convert all of the COBOL II (dealing with sockets and such called by C)
reliably yet.

You may have lost your big opportunity.
```

```
"Dean T. Miller" wrote:
> 
> Hi DD,
…[24 more quoted lines elided]…
> modifying the programs could be very dangerous.

There, Mr Miller, lies the rub; 'shouldn't' is a rather... malleable
evaluation, at times.  I agree whole-heartedly that these constructs are
*not* best left to the tender mercies of archetypical 2-year programmers
performing maintenance; I've also found myself in situations where
Management has demanded the use of such a construct as a system rewrite
Just Wasn't In The Budget.

> 
> Now I can see why Tom B. was saying programmers aren't really
> "professional."

And why might that be... because the coders aren't allocated the
time/money to Do Things Right?

> 
> BTW, prefix the above with:
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> independent.

Rightly or wrongly I remembered something about not making any one item
larger than a certain size, hence the multiple 01s; the comments I
usually include in the code (beginning with *** WARNING !!  TWO-YEAR
PROGRAMMER-CODE STOPS HERE ! ! PROCEED WITH CAUTION ***) are for the
maintenance-coders.

DD
```

#### ↳ Re: Calling DD, was: COBOL COMPILED WITH DATA=31: PLUSES & MINUSES?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38242646.B9AD919A@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<008d01bf24bc$e0f271a0$8df32cc7@cylemon-cyber> <7kepWhCNP4qd-pn2-XWVbfSVO9nYp@localhost> <3821490F.88C3FACD@worldnet.att.net> <3821DF2E.5EFC6BFC@strata-group.Xcom> <tLlU3.33299$oa2.71287@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 
> In article <3821DF2E.5EFC6BFC@strata-group.Xcom>,
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> 


Have you ever heard of Caching.   The simple first level of this is:

IF not this-value equal previous-value
	perform read-it
end-if

This makes a surprising amount of difference.

Level two is where you have a nominate cache and keep a count of the
last look up count and discard the the oldest (Least Recently Used)

Finally:

Look up batch LSR pools.  I had a program that ran for one hour,
included batch LSR's in the major hit file and reduced it to 15
minutes (awful package program though).  Another well written
application saved about 1/3 of the run time.   Zero code changes.

Ken

PS:  Volker beat me to it DD do you use optimise?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Calling DD, was: COBOL COMPILED WITH DATA=31: PLUSES & MINUSES?

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382430BA.42F9B9CD@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<008d01bf24bc$e0f271a0$8df32cc7@cylemon-cyber> <7kepWhCNP4qd-pn2-XWVbfSVO9nYp@localhost> <3821490F.88C3FACD@worldnet.att.net> <3821DF2E.5EFC6BFC@strata-group.Xcom> <tLlU3.33299$oa2.71287@iad-read.news.verio.net> <38242646.B9AD919A@zip.com.au> <38242B5D.D371C2DB@home.com>`

```
The Goobers wrote:
> 
> >
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Batch LSR is a JCL change.  Basically your rename the DD on the VSAM
cluster (xyz to xyz1) and then create a new ddname (xyz) that refers
the the other (xyz1).  From memory is was a SUBSYS command (I always
copy it).

I assume that you are reasonably recent on the operating system even
if you are running OldBol.

Ken
```

```
Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
> The Goobers wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> if you are running OldBol.

Sometimes yes, sometimes no... this one of the difficulties which gets
encountered, one shop to the next.  My concerns were with the structure
of applications and mentioned some of the rather... idiosyncratic and
arfiticial obstacles I've encountered, over the years, when trying to
build a solution to a problem *only from the applications side*. 
('Nope, you can't code a SEARCH, ol' Bobby don't understand those and
he'll never approve it for QA')

To add a solution from the systems side might work, certainly... then
again, it might bring in yet another set of prejudices, preconceptions
and resistances.

I, of course, am willing to stand on my head if it gets the job done so
if you've a reference where I might further research this I'd appreciate
it.

DD
```

<a id="t-9148c65516"></a>
## Thread: Code to print page numbers___another student's question — 28 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Personally I avoid negative IF statments like the plague.  But to sorta answer your question:  Using an 'equal to' condition on something like this can mess you up if you don't have exactly the right number of lines on a particular page - like your double spacing - where you add 2 to the line-counter instead of 1 and go 1 past MAX-LINES.  Unless you know that each page will be EXACTLY alike don't do it.

Instead of this code:
PRINT-ENTRY.
    IF (LINE-COUNT NOT < MAX-LINES)
        PERFORM PAGE-HEADING
    END-IF.

I would code:
PRINT-ENTRY.
  IF LINE-COUNT > MAX-LINES
        PERFORM PAGE-HEADING
  END-IF.

Notice that 1) I use the 'greater than'  and 2) I don't have the parens as I only use parens for compound IF statements

IF (LINE-COUNT > MAX-LINES) AND (CONTROL-BREAK = 'N')
   PERFORM PAGE-HEADING
END-IF.

Lousy code - but an example.

When working with 'sloppy' counters (thinks like line-counters, subscripts) 'equal to' can be dangerous because you risk not hitting the equal condition and going off to who knows where.

Just my thoughts.

Eileen


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<AylU3.7947$aS6.83877@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Eileen Preston wrote:
>Personally I avoid negative IF statments like the plague.

Why?  A programmer should be able to understand any logic statement
that is clearly and simply written, negative or no.  The essence of
computer programming is logic.  My suggestion is, if you have problems
using and understanding simple 'not' logic, then study and practice
your logic rules until you can use and understand them well.  To do
anything less is an acceptance of a real deficiency in your programming
ability.  Would you trust a doctor who said "I avoid using some very
important diagnostic tools, because they are too difficult for me to
understand?"  You would probably respond "Get away from me, and go back
to medical school!"  When it comes to using logic correctly in computer
programming, if you can't stand the heat, get out of the kitchen. :-)

>Instead of this code:
>PRINT-ENTRY.
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>  END-IF.

The above two statements are not equivalent.  LINE-COUNT = MAX-LINES
would be found true on the first, but fall through the second.  Also
you need to consider that other factors are involved.  For example, if
you want to print lines to 50, then page break, it is most logical to
set MAX-LINES = 50.  For (LINE-COUNT > MAX-LINES) to work properly,
then LINE-COUNT must be maintained to be 1 greater than the actual
number of lines printed, or you must set MAX-LINES to 1 less than the
actual limit.  Either of these violates the Rule of Least Astonishment.
However, using the simple 'NOT' violates no proper design rule.  It is
the simplest and most straight forward expression for this particular
purpose. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3821ec9c.179452839@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <AylU3.7947$aS6.83877@news4.mia>`

```
On Thu, 4 Nov 1999 13:56:36 -0600, "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>Eileen Preston wrote:
>>Personally I avoid negative IF statments like the plague.
>
>Why?  A programmer should be able to understand any logic statement
>that is clearly and simply written, negative or no. 

While I generally agree with you I strongly disagree with you on this point.  A program
written by an experienced programmer has two types of audiences, the compiler (that really
knows the relevant laws on AND, OR, NOT etc), and the (sometimes not so) experienced
programmer that has to read some body else's code containing NOT A OR B (or was it NOT (A
OR B))???

I have been giving g classes in Programming for more than 15 years now, and find
(unfortunately), that about 80% of the students now don't have the slightest idea of
Aristotelean Logic, Boolean algebra (which is more or less the same thing)  or De Morgan's
Law (a specific case)

Avoiding NOT in an IF expression just keeps things simpler for the uninitiated <G>
The following just might be confusing:

IF NOT A > B AND C > D

IF A NOT > B AND C > D

IF A NOT > C AND D

IF NOT A > B AND C

IF NOT A > B OR C > D

IF A NOT > B OR C > D

IF A NOT > C OR  D

Coding this without NOT avoids some maintenance hassles

> The essence of
>computer programming is logic.  My suggestion is, if you have problems
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>programming, if you can't stand the heat, get out of the kitchen. :-)


Agreed.

But.... you have to work with the material you get, and the material _I_ get are young
students/trainees that have huge problems seeing any difference in the examples above


     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         Better to understand a little than to misunderstand a lot.
```

```
I would simply use parenthesis to clarify the meaning.  I totally agree that
I do not like to see the sort of thing you are talking about, but I think
the NOT far too useful to get ones shorts in a knot over it. Meanings can
easily be clarified by other methods.

find
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<y%mU3.8268$aS6.85493@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <AylU3.7947$aS6.83877@news4.mia> <3821ec9c.179452839@news2.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>NOT A OR B (or was it NOT (A OR B))???

Don't misunderstand.  I certainly advocate making logic as clear and
simple as possible, and I hate obfuscated code or logic.  I also hate
long, convoluted sentences using ELSE and branches with only NEXT
SENTENCE or CONTINUE, simply to avoid a simple NOT.  A programmer
should learn to use NOT, just like any other logical operator, always
going for the clearest, simplest expression of the logic.

>I have been giving g classes in Programming for more than 15 years
>now, and find (unfortunately), that about 80% of the students now
>don't have the slightest idea of Aristotelean Logic, Boolean algebra
>(which is more or less the same thing) or De Morgan's Law (a specific
>case)

No wonder there is so much crummy software these days! :)  But a
programmer does not necessarily have to know formal boolean algebra,
but they had *better* know how to understand and formulate precise
logical expressions! :-)

Not only would I not hire a programmer who was deficient in logic, if
a client of mine hired one, I would do my best to make it clear to the
client that they were making a Big Mistake.  A person who cannot
understand and articulate logic simply has no place being a programmer,
in my book.  It's like a person wanting to drive, but who cannot read
road signs, or a farmer who can't grow things!  If a new programmer is
simply ignorant of logic, then they need study and practice.  If they
can't learn it, then they need to find a job in another field.  I'm
sorry if this sounds harsh. :-)

>But.... you have to work with the material you get, and the material _I_
>get are young students/trainees that have huge problems seeing any
>difference in the examples above

Then you need to teach them the difference. :-)  As I said, it's no
wonder why we are getting so much crummy software these days.  And to
make matters worse, instead of making our programming tools simpler and
easier to use, they are getting more complex and difficult.  This leaves
the new programmer, particularly the less talented ones, with even less
time and energy to devote toward perfecting their code writing skills. :-(
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3821FDEF.B766B47F@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <AylU3.7947$aS6.83877@news4.mia> <3821ec9c.179452839@news2.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:

> Avoiding NOT in an IF expression just keeps things simpler for the uninitiated <G>
> The following just might be confusing:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Coding this without NOT avoids some maintenance hassles

So when the above code makes sense to use, then I use parenthesis.  A
redundant set of parenthesis can save me a call in the middle of the
night after someone ill maintained my program.

I also tend to have code such as the following:
   IF a = b
   or a = d...

It costs nothing to repeat the "a =", and it is easy to comment out a
condition.
   IF  (a = b or a = c)
   and (y = z or l = m)
     is also a clarifier.

I don't see any need to for parenthesize in 
   IF a = b or c = d, but lots of people always put them here.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mCnU3.8340$aS6.85687@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <AylU3.7947$aS6.83877@news4.mia> <3821ec9c.179452839@news2.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
>
>The following just might be confusing:

The difficulty lies in the failure to use parenthesis,
rather than the use of NOT.  Below is the way I would
code each example.

>IF NOT A > B AND C > D

    IF NOT ( (A > B)
              AND
             (C > D) )

>IF A NOT > B AND C > D

    IF (A NOT > B)
        AND
       (C > D)

>IF A NOT > C AND D

    IF (A NOT > C)
        AND
       (A NOT > D)

>IF NOT A > B AND C

    IF NOT ( (A > B)
              AND
             (A > C) )

>IF NOT A > B OR C > D

    IF NOT ( (A > B)
              OR
             (C > D) )

>IF A NOT > B OR C > D

    IF (A NOT > B)
        OR
       (C > D)

>IF A NOT > C OR  D

    IF (A NOT > C)
        OR
       (A NOT > D)

I use parenthesis, even redundant parenthesis, to clarify.  I don't
think any of them are unclear, expressed in this manner. :-)
```

```
In my opinion this is easier to read/understand
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3822EE33.7D739817@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <AylU3.7947$aS6.83877@news4.mia> <3821ec9c.179452839@news2.ibm.net> <mCnU3.8340$aS6.85687@news4.mia> <38297aa3.215775258@news2.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:

> >>IF NOT A > B AND C > D
> >
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> In my opinion this is easier to read/understand

Sometimes.  It all depends on what business rules you are implementing. 
Follow the logic which is closer to the business rules, so that people
don't have to stop and translate.  (of course, variables will be named
more appropriately)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KaDU3.9637$B15.104636@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <AylU3.7947$aS6.83877@news4.mia> <3821ec9c.179452839@news2.ibm.net> <mCnU3.8340$aS6.85687@news4.mia> <38297aa3.215775258@news2.ibm.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>Volker Bandke wrote:
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Correct, and of course I knew that _you_ would know.

Ah, we're both wrong. :-(  After I posted that message and turned off
my PC, I started thinking that I had written that one incorrectly.
NOT comes before AND and OR in precedence.  The correct syntax is:

    IF (NOT A > B)
        AND
       (C > D)

Just to make *sure*, I wrote a little COBOL program to test the forms,
and this is the result.  Test1 is your original, Test2 is my original,
Test3 is my correction, and Test4 is yours below.  The code is at the
bottom of this post.

 A B  C D  TEST1 TEST2 TEST3 TEST4
 - -  - -  ----- ----- ----- -----
 1 2  1 2  FALSE TRUE  FALSE TRUE
 1 2  2 1  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE  TRUE
 2 1  1 2  FALSE TRUE  FALSE TRUE
 2 1  2 1  FALSE FALSE FALSE FALSE

As you can see, my first form (and yours below, which exactly matches
it) are not equivalent to the original.  But this only illustrates my
point:  the problem is operator precedence, rather than NOT.  It is
just as easy to be confused by multiple AND/OR conditionals without
parenthesis. :-)

>But why not code it as
>
>    IF ( A <= B )  OR  ( C <= D)
>
>In my opinion this is easier to read/understand

This could be true, but it depends on what the 'sense' of the test was.
To me, it is almost as important to formulate the condition so as to
reflect the nature of what the programmer is looking for, as to make it
'simple'.  More is going on in a programmer's mind when examining a
conditional test than in simply understanding the specific logic.  The
programmer is initially scanning the code to understand what the logic
of the program is trying to do.  For this purpose, the code should be
rapidly assimilable, as to intent.  Only after a given conditional is
targeted for close scrutiny is the detailed logic fully analyzed.  I
seek to structure conditionals to most clearly expresses the intuitive
sense of the purpose of the conditional test, as well as to be easy to
read.  For example, if the condition '(A > B) AND (C > D)' was commonly
used in the program to test for a specific condition, and this code was
to test for that condition being false, then I would likely use the
former structure.  To use your form in this case would make the specific
logic simpler, but could mask the real purpose of the conditional. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38230195.7F8B2246@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <AylU3.7947$aS6.83877@news4.mia> <3821ec9c.179452839@news2.ibm.net> <mCnU3.8340$aS6.85687@news4.mia> <38297aa3.215775258@news2.ibm.net> <KaDU3.9637$B15.104636@news3.mia>`

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> Ah, we're both wrong. :-(  After I posted that message and turned off
> my PC, I started thinking that I had written that one incorrectly.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> Just to make *sure*, I wrote a little COBOL program to test the forms,

Which illustrates that we should NEVER slow down a maintenance
programmer by having him try to guess what we REALLY meant and by what
the compiler would do.  Use parenthesis every time there may be
confusion!!!
```

```
>the compiler would do.  Use parenthesis every time there may be
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1sAV3.664$Hk.4406@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <AylU3.7947$aS6.83877@news4.mia> <3821ec9c.179452839@news2.ibm.net> <mCnU3.8340$aS6.85687@news4.mia> <38297aa3.215775258@news2.ibm.net> <KaDU3.9637$B15.104636@news3.mia> <38230195.7F8B2246@NOSPAMhome.com> <DOsV3.175$Av4.6962@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`

```
Gumbo wrote:
>
>ALWAYS USE PARENTHESIS to avoid confused programmers. Do not allow for
>interpretation for what MAY be confusion to you IS confusion to someone
>somewhere.

Absolutely!  I *always* use parenthesis around conditionals, even the
most simple ones.  I find that the consistent format seems easier to
rapidly assimilate than only using parenthesis when necessary, or
on more complex conditionals.  For example:

IF (...)

IF (...)
    AND
   (...)

IF (...)
    AND
   ( (...)
      OR
     (...) )

IF (...)
    OR
   ( (...)
      AND
     (...) )
```

```
I agree.  Simply using lots of parenthesis is a much simpler (and a LOT less
restrictive) method of clarification than a standard that outlaws the word
NOT.  NOT is just too useful.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3822E474.7813D79E@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <AylU3.7947$aS6.83877@news4.mia> <3821ec9c.179452839@news2.ibm.net> <mCnU3.8340$aS6.85687@news4.mia> <38297aa3.215775258@news2.ibm.net>`

```


Volker Bandke wrote:
> Judson McClendon wrote:
> >Volker Bandke wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> In my opinion this is easier to read/understand
But, Volker,

When you teach your students to use a<=b|c<=d i.s.o. not(a>b&c>d), you are
teaching the equivalence of (x)|(y) and (ï¿½x)&(ï¿½y). in other words the De
Morgan's Law. In other words you are initiating them :)

Personally I avoid, and teach others to avoid: combinations of "NOT" with "AND"
and "NOT" with "OR". (I did consider to type the previous sentence without
quotes and caps ;-) but could resist). I do not (teach to) avoid NOT sec.

To avoid the combi's, I have two tools:

IF noNotPlusAndOrOr
   continue
ELSE
   ....
END-IF

or 

IF morganedCondition
   ....
END-IF

Huib
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Logic statements was Re: Code to print page numbers ..

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38224619.99725376@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <AylU3.7947$aS6.83877@news4.mia> <3821ec9c.179452839@news2.ibm.net>`

```
While I use NOT, whenever I am coding anything that might be ambiguous
and require me to remember precedence rules I use parentheses so that it
is clear to me what is going on.  After over 30 years of using COBOL, I
have to admit that I would check the manual (or my copy of the standard
if I couldn't find a manual) on some the IF statements you have coded. 
Part of it is that I couldn't be bothered memorizing the order of
combination for either the arithmetic statements or the logical ones.  I
have taken boolean algebra (over 30 years ago) and while I did well in
the course and used the information, I think I have forgotten virtually
all of it.  I don't recall taking Aristotelian Logic at Rutgers
University and have never heard of De Morgan's Law.  My COBOL programs
work well enough to cause very few problems in production and one of
them is decoding and playing with SMF records including the abysmally
designed type 30 record. That program is also decoding some of the bit
switch bytes on the type 14/15 records.  

In the particular instance, I now code "IF LINE-COUNT >= LINE-MAX etc."
instead of "IF LINE-COUNT NOT < LINE-MAX etc." because I think it is
slightly clearer and it takes less room on the line. 

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca 

Volker Bandke wrote:
   snip
> 
> I have been giving g classes in Programming for more than 15 years now, and find
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** Rodger Whitlock <totototo@mail.pacificcoast.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZkBDu86CpqJF@vmsmail.gov.bc.ca>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <AylU3.7947$aS6.83877@news4.mia> <3821ec9c.179452839@news2.ibm.net>`

```
If I might comment on writing complex boolean expressions:

Usually these do not stand alone, but as part of a larger 
decision tree. One has code along these lines:

if <complex condition #1> then <action #1>
else
  if <complex condition #2> then <action #2>
  else...

In my cobol writing days, I found that an old-fashioned 
decision table was very helpful in setting up such code. Not 
only did it help you verify that you hadn't overlooked some 
odd combination of circumstances (completeness), but also made 
excellent documentation when written into the code as comments.

By modelling the nesting of the if's on the structure of the 
table, you made future maintenance a piece of cake. And these 
complex decision trees often require modification.[1] Heaven 
help us all though, when some rookie modifies the code without 
modifying the documentary table!

I generally preferred nested if's to complex parenthesization.

It really didn't matter if lots of the terminal actions were 
"next sentence".

[1] I suspect I am preaching to the choir.
```

```
Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3828DE69.D50492E7@home.com>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <AylU3.7947$aS6.83877@news4.mia> <3821ec9c.179452839@news2.ibm.net> <ZkBDu86CpqJF@vmsmail.gov.bc.ca> <3828CE27.B8D48C7D@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```


Clark Morris wrote:
> 
> Have you worked with the decision table capabilities of the EVALUATE
> verb.  They look interesting but I found them confusing because I have
> rarely used decision tables.

Clark they are a godsend. A little story - both design and programming. 

As Ken Foskey said in another message, the names of the stupid will not
be changed to hide their identity. Debenhams department store group in
UK. Given three years notice by HM Government about the introduction of
VAT(Value Added Tax), we finally got the bean-counters agreeing to what
we could do about it, some 12 months before D-Day.  

I literally ran out of analysts - so as well as controlling
implementation through committees, I re-designed the General Ledger and
as a side-line a Stock Transfer system. Manually, the latter was like a
bunch of Monopoly money, I owe him, he owes me etc. spread over
different departments and 60 stores. At financial year-end the deputy
chief bean-counter Fred Boone (the Goon), had to reconcile this mess -
which took about 6 months. I insisted it should be computerized, 'though
I knew it was somewhat complex. Freddy boy resisted me all the way, even
when it went live.

Got a committee of five, including Freddy, "OK gentlemen I'm going to do
some 'Decision Tables'". "Huh ?". "Can I transfer from Fashion to
Fashion ?". "Can I transfer from Fashion to Foodhall ?" "Nah!". "Can I
transfer from Foodhall to Expense". "Nah. Well, yes if it is food going
into the management's dining room. Oh yes, and from Fashion to Expense
if it was associated with display or advertising". Conclude meeting.

Next meeting - "Well here's a set of tables based on what you told me
last time. Correct or not ?". Minor alterations. "Now give me the rules
for each valid stock transfer gentlemen".

The final Decision Tables became my program specification handed over to
the programmer.

Boone the Goon ? After first month of live running, with owlish like
eyes he came rushing in, "This will save me six months work at the end
of the year". And what he didn't mention, a large corporation was able
to get its financial statement out to shareholders and the LSE, six
months earlier.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<iEjU3.33275$oa2.68349@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
In article <s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>,
Eileen Preston <epreston@lear.com> wrote:
>Personally I avoid negative IF statments like the plague.

With all due respect, Ms Preston, I must disagree; I have found negative
conditionals to be *exquistely* readable when properly used for, say,
editing data. Consider:

05  WS-TST-MM PIC 99 VALUE ZEROES.
    88  VALID-MONTH VALUES 1 THRU 12.

...

MOVE PARM-MM TO WS-TST-MM.
IF NOT VALID-MONTH
    PERFORM TOUT-CEST-FROMAGE THRU TCF-EX.

DD
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vsjh7$98o$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM> <iEjU3.33275$oa2.68349@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```
<docdwarf@clark.net> wrote in message
news:iEjU3.33275$oa2.68349@iad-read.news.verio.net...
> In article <s82170ef.050@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>,
> Eileen Preston <epreston@lear.com> wrote:
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I agree with you that there are times when negative tests work well, but your
specific example shows a good case where a "positive" test MIGHT be better
for "self-documenting code", i.e.

 Either:
   (This is good if you have a compiler that "optimizes" for most commonly
performed logic paths being coded first)

 05  WS-TST-MM PIC 99 VALUE ZEROES.
     88  VALID-MONTH VALUES 1 THRU 12.

 ...

 MOVE PARM-MM TO WS-TST-MM.
 IF VALID-MONTH
        continue
 Else
     PERFORM TOUT-CEST-FROMAGE THRU TCF-EX.


 Or:
   (This is good if you have a 'negative test' that may get changed over
time, eg. 'good' vs 'bad' department codes)

 05  WS-TST-MM PIC 99 VALUE ZEROES.
     88  VALID-MONTH VALUES 1 THRU 12.
     88 Invalid-Month  Value 0 13 thru 99.
  ...

 MOVE PARM-MM TO WS-TST-MM.
 IF InVALID-MONTH
        PERFORM TOUT-CEST-FROMAGE THRU TCF-EX.

   ****

Again, both of these are "style" issues, but do show how you CAN code around
'negative' tests AND may have reasons for doing so.
```

---

<a id="t-03cf0ccf4e"></a>
## Thread: IBM OS390/MVS CICS COBOL - ISCICS — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### IBM OS390/MVS CICS COBOL - ISCICS

- **From:** Andre de Jager <ajager@mweb.co.za>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3821D99B.823A3071@mweb.co.za>`

```
In the 'C' environment there is a function to check if you are running under CICS or not
    i = iscics()
that will return 1 if you are running under cics and zero if you are not.

Unfortunately calling 'C' from Cobol has become very hazardous under LE370.

Has any one got an easy way to enquire if you are being called from a main program under 'CICS'
environment or under BATCH environment.

Assembler or COBOL or LE utility will do very nicely.

Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM OS390/MVS CICS COBOL - ISCICS

- **From:** TINLC <agent01413@deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7mYiOMtqezl+f8SInZ5sDSvFqvNR@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3821D99B.823A3071@mweb.co.za>`

```
On Thu, 04 Nov 1999 21:08:12 +0200, Andre de Jager <ajager@mweb.co.za>
sacrificed an address to the spam gods to say:

>In the 'C' environment there is a function to check if you are running under CICS or not
>    i = iscics()
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks

I've always issued an ASSIGN APPLID or SYSID query to determine which
CICS region I'm executing in, if any.  These are installation defined
values, so you'll need to experiment during testing to see what comes
back in each case, or ask your domain engineer.  In the case of a CICS
environment, APPLID will contain the 8 character region ID (so what
you'll get back during unit testing will vary from acceptence testing
will vary from production).  I dont remember what came back under
batch, but I remember that it was readily capable of differentiation
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM OS390/MVS CICS COBOL - ISCICS

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382293CB.E7BE3170@att.net>`

```
TINLC wrote:
> 
(snip)
> >Has any one got an easy way to enquire if you are being called from a main program under 'CICS'
> >environment or under BATCH environment.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> batch, but I remember that it was readily capable of differentiation

I wouldn't expect anything to come back if you invoke a CICS program
with an EXEC statement; rather, I'd expect it to blow up (S0C4).

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM OS390/MVS CICS COBOL - ISCICS

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3823e4b3.177440606@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<3821D99B.823A3071@mweb.co.za>`

```
On Thu, 04 Nov 1999 21:08:12 +0200, Andre de Jager <ajager@mweb.co.za> wrote:

>In the 'C' environment there is a function to check if you are running under CICS or not
>    i = iscics()
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Thanks


Code a little assembler (sub)program which does

	DFHAFCD TYPE=LOCATE,CB=CSA,REG=(5)

	
This will return the address of the CSA if you are running under CICS, or it will return
0.  Thus you can set the programs return code accordingly.



     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         Better to understand a little than to misunderstand a lot.
```

#### ↳ Re: IBM OS390/MVS CICS COBOL - ISCICS

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vuqgp$nj1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<3821D99B.823A3071@mweb.co.za>`

```
In article <3821D99B.823A3071@mweb.co.za>,
  Andre de Jager <ajager@mweb.co.za> wrote:
> In the 'C' environment there is a function to check if you are running
under CICS or not
>     i = iscics()
> that will return 1 if you are running under cics and zero if you are
not.
>
> Unfortunately calling 'C' from Cobol has become very hazardous under
LE370.
>

Oh no, you must do anything wrong. I have absolutly no problems with ILC
under LE. Could you specify the kind of 'hazard' you experience?

> Has any one got an easy way to enquire if you are being called from a
main program under 'CICS'
> environment or under BATCH environment.
>
> Assembler or COBOL or LE utility will do very nicely.

In ASSEMBLER, I would use the CSVQUERY SEARCH=JPA macro with INEPNAME
pointed to 'DFHSIP'. I use the following table:

DC CL8'DFSRRC00',F'0'    IMS MPP, BMP, BATCH, BTS
DC CL8'DFHSIP  ',F'8'    CICS
DC CL8'IKJEFT01',F'4'    TSO NORMAL
DC CL8'IKJEFT25',F'4'    TSO ALIAS OF IKJEFT01
DC CL8'TSOSESS ',F'4'    TSO-PLUS
DC CL8'********',F'16'   END OF LIST

CSVQUERY returns 0 in R15 if the named program is loaded in the current
address space.

There is also a COBOL program originally written by Roland Schiradin and
Gilbert Saint-flour. The essential few lines are:

   Linkage Section.
   01 cb1.
       05 ptr1 Pointer Occurs 256.
   Procedure Division.
 *    PSA
       SET Address of cb1 to null
 *    TCB
       SET Address of cb1 to ptr1(136)
 *    EXT2
       SET Address Of cb1 To ptr1(53)
       if ptr1(6) = NULL
           display 'BATCH'
       else
           display 'CICS '
       end-if

Daniel
------------------------------------------------------------
visit us at:
http://www.winterthur.com


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: IBM OS390/MVS CICS COBOL - ISCICS

- **From:** Tim Josling <tej@melbpc.org.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3823FA06.D0F57C5A@melbpc.org.au>`
- **References:** `<3821D99B.823A3071@mweb.co.za> <7vuqgp$nj1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I was researching this a while back. The book says under CICS you
can't call C from COBOL. You have to do EXEC CICS LINK. This is a
catch 22 if you are trying to find out if you are running under
CICS.

As usual on the mainframe, 'write a little assembler routine',

Tim Josling


Daniel Jacot wrote:
> Oh no, you must do anything wrong. I have absolutly no problems with ILC
> under LE. Could you specify the kind of 'hazard' you experience?
```

```
do it either.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM OS390/MVS CICS COBOL - ISCICS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** stephen_j_spiro@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8053e0$tc1$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
If you are writing a subroutine which can be
called from either batch or on-line programs, AND
IT MAKES A DIFFERENCE, you should set a flag  to
indicate the environment.  Yeah, I know you can
get deep enough into the calls not to have this
information, but this is usually handled by
designing the system before coding it.

Stephen J Spiro
President, Wizard Systems
Member, J4 COBOL Committee
stephenjspiro@hotmail.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: IBM OS390/MVS CICS COBOL - ISCICS

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Andre de Jager <ajager@mweb.co.za>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38273C9E.136F2C2A@mweb.co.za>`
- **References:** `<3821D99B.823A3071@mweb.co.za> <7vuqgp$nj1$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3823FA06.D0F57C5A@melbpc.org.au> <3825BD97.2DCEDA78@techie.com>`

```
The Hazards are
In Os390 Release 2.7 a small bug
    Cobol Pgm A Calls C Pgm 1 and returns
    Cobol Pgm A Calls Cobol Pgm B
        Cobol Pgm B Calls C Pgm 2 and returns, Pgm B Returns to A
     Cobol Pgm A Calls C Pgm 1 again.

Unfortunately program 2 has stomped all over program 1's static variables and crunch. Finally got a
fix from IBM.

Under Os390 1.2 watch out for static variables in 'C'.

In fact as soon as you try to implement a black box solution in 'C' you start to get problems from
one release of OS390 to another.

And Yes I have also seen the documentation that says you cannot call 'C' from Cobol although I
believe this does not apply to LeC.

Andre de Jager
Cape Town

Jared Thomas wrote:

> Things changed.  Read up on the latest and greatest stuff and I
> believe it can be done.  Sorry I can't recall the exact release
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.radiks.net/~jmthomas
```

## Thread: Ascii symbols for Windows — 9 msgs

### Ascii symbols for Windows

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38224B65.280B2617@home.com>`

```
Does anybody know if there is a table which you can use to 'translate'
ascii symbols to the Windows GUI equivalent, e.g :-

	characters for cents, degree symbol (temperatures), or
	pick up the British pound sign in N. America, etc...

I know for instance that if I use tab x'09' it will show up as a black
blob, if I don't hide/position it; So assume Windows must be doing some
sort of equivalence. ( x"0d0a' or x'00' function as you would expect
them to). 

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Ascii symbols for Windows

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vttcb$hd1$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<38224B65.280B2617@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote in message <38224B65.280B2617@home.com>...
>Does anybody know if there is a table which you can use to 'translate'
>ascii symbols to the Windows GUI equivalent, e.g :-

Windows has two functions for this;

    OemToAnsi
and
    AnsiToOem

Which both take two null terminated character strings as arguments.

You have twin functions that works on buffers as well.

No magic at all.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: Ascii symbols for Windows

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3822EA2A.23C62C47@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<38224B65.280B2617@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> Does anybody know if there is a table which you can use to 'translate'
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

Windows can change.  I have a couple of web pages of chararacters on my
wall, but those pages are now gone from the web.  Just a quick search
found http://www.epsb.edmonton.ab.ca/schools/ottewell/chart.htm which
gives them in HTML format - in dos editors you can usually do ALT-171
for ï¿½.

I find that I like to spell coï¿½rdinate the way I was taught.  So I went
to a Windows editor, used the character mapping facility to create the
letter I wanted, and saved the word in a file.  When I was writing this
message, I opened that file, copied the word, and pasted it in this
message.

Another thing I do is use a hex editor to create all possible characters
(ASCII or EBCDIC) in a file.  A little experimenting will show me which
characters will print, and how they show in different printers.
```

#### ↳ Re: Ascii symbols for Windows

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38228370.27942514@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<38224B65.280B2617@home.com>`

```
On Fri, 05 Nov 1999 03:05:07 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:

>Does anybody know if there is a table which you can use to 'translate'
>ascii symbols to the Windows GUI equivalent, e.g :-
>

Sp2 can do it. <G>
```
