###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Ascii symbols for Windows

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3827A9EB.66EC56D@home.com>`
- **References:** `<38224B65.280B2617@home.com> <38228370.27942514@news1.attglobal.net> <38249B90.25CE1386@home.com> <804mql$oph$1@news.cerf.net>`

```


Cheesle wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan wrote in message <38249B90.25CE1386@home.com>...
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Eh, Jimmy, I got lost here. What do you mean by saying:

Ok Cheesle - the above was the shorthand version; now the long-hand :-

I'm designing initially, then programming. So I convert data files from
RM/COBOL to NetExpress. Where possible I'm even generating new codes
using Dictionaries. When finished I give user a message box. 'Files
converted, see error-listing-X (if applicable) and ascii-delimited files
a,b,c,d,e, etc..' He presses OK and I STOP RUN. So he has to get to my
files through Explorer.

Then I pause; hey, I could automate this better. I'll generate the files
above but having finished, I'll come up with a dialog which will let him
view the ascii files, error files and also allow him to add new records
to a Description File which will clean-up the error-files when he
re-runs. (This without exiting the transfer process).

Now I go to the on-line help looking for a likely source - bring up an
editor, call an editor, link to another package, use OLE - nah! it wants
me to hard-code to the Registry, (and I want to use Notepad/Word to test
it, and he will be using his favourite editor, the pathname being held
in a default file). I look and look, for a suitable 'English
expression'. I give up and ask Merant through Answerlab - use
'CreateProcess' comes back the answer - who the hell would associate my
'English' need with a funky Windows word like 'CreateProcess'.

I seem to recall that in a past life, one WMK, confessed to Pete
Dashwood as having written manuals. Similarly the on-line help is
written by tech-writers who are too close to COBOL. They should be
written by tech-writers, perhaps on a contractual basis to the compiler
vendors, who haven't a clue what COBOL is about. (I've made this
recommendation to Merant, resulting from a questionnaire they sent out).
There's a big difference between programmers working for compiler
vendors, and us developers at the other end of the stick.

This business of vendor programmer v. developer programmer; following is
my favourite from Class EntryField :--

EntryField Methods : autoswipe
Switch auto swipe on or off depending on the boolean parameter. 
Using Parameters
lnkAutoSwipeState PIC 99 COMP-5 
-------------------------------------
EntryField Method IsAutoswipe
Return auto swipe state. 
Returning Parameter
lnkAutoSwipeState PIC 99 COMP-5 
--------------------------------------

Very informative isn't it ? The source of the above - comment statements
made by the vendor programmer in the source of Class entryfield.cbl. And
as I recall, I didn't find anything in the Microsoft SDK about 'autoing'
or 'swiping'. The vendor programmer knows exactly what it means. Above
is contained in Class Book which shows hierarchies and methods for each
class, but no other reference in on-line help. Now, if I install Dialog
System, which is not default installation, and I don't use it, there is
quite a bit about autoswipe. Basically I could fill an entry field with
a prompt "yyyy/mm/dd". 

Ok - so you are going to say I've got to get to know Windows (or the
SDK). No - if I am going to become an 'expert' let it be in COBOL - I'll
look at the Windows stuff as I find a need for it.

I hope you aren't lost now <G>

Jimmy
```

## Thread: call and cobrun with MF COBOL — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### call and cobrun with MF COBOL

- **From:** webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3821DA7D.C320D84B@eii.fr>`

```
hi

I have a MF COBOL program myprog.int
which has a linkage section with a few
parameters.

Most of the time this program is called
from other programs with a call "myprog"
using myparameters cancel "myprog"
statement.

However, it do also need to directly run
this program from a UNIX shell with a
cobrun myprog command.

When a do this, I can not supply the
parameters of the linkage section which
does not matter since I do not need them
in this case, but myprog does not work
properly.

If I do not supply the linkage
parameters and do cobrun myprog, it does
work, but some variables are
destroyed...

What can I do ?

thnks

Christophe
```

#### ↳ Re: call and cobrun with MF COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38221590.63064C40@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3821DA7D.C320D84B@eii.fr>`

```


webmaster wrote:
> 
> hi
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
> destroyed...

Christophe,

Might I suggest that rather than:-

your Unix Shell - cobrun myprog command

create a dummy/triggger program which either initializes or puts values
into the call parameters then :-

Unix Shell - cobrun mydummy command (which calls myprogram with mydummy
parameters) ?

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: call and cobrun with MF COBOL

- **From:** webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38229096.AF040D02@eii.fr>`
- **References:** `<3821DA7D.C320D84B@eii.fr> <38221590.63064C40@home.com>`

```
Jimmy,

It is true at the moment I can not find any other solution than the one you
said.

But, doing this, I introduce one more step, one more process, in an already
complicated environment :

an apache server calls a perl cgi script which call a Unix shell cgi script
on another apache server which call a dummy COBOL prog which call my real
COBOL prog....

I have a bit worrying about the maintenance of that when someone need to
chance some programs, about the memory used when dozens of users are doing
it at the same time, etc.

If I could have "only" one COBOL prog I would be happier !

thanks

Christophe

"James J. Gavan" wrote:

> webmaster wrote:
> >
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: call and cobrun with MF COBOL

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vuak3$t1j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3821DA7D.C320D84B@eii.fr>`

```
Christophe,

webmaster wrote in message <3821DA7D.C320D84B@eii.fr>...
>hi
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>

One thing you can do is test whether or not the linkage parameters have been
supplied.
To do this, add the following at the start of the code

IF ADDRESS OF <linkage-data-item> = NULL
* linkage item not supplied

You will also need to set the 'command_line_linkage' run-time tuneable as it
controls whether the command line can be received by linkage in the main
program.

I hope this helps.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: call and cobrun with MF COBOL

- **From:** webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3822D5F6.80B1413B@eii.fr>`
- **References:** `<3821DA7D.C320D84B@eii.fr> <7vuak3$t1j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Hi

Here is the beginning of my prog :

       linkage section.
       01 lkqui                        pic x(2).
          88 mtl                                 value "00".
          88 sp                                  value "01" thru "20".

       procedure division using lkqui.
       if address of lkqui=null
          display "no linkage"
       end-if.
       ...

when I run my prog with "cobrun myprog", I do not need any linkage parameters
nor command line parameters.

However my problem is that in this case the program does not works properly
anymore. Strange things append : it
can not open some files anymore, some if .. then .. else structures does not
work anymore...

I think the prog has got memory problem which produce uncontrolable effects when
the linkage is not supply (with
cobrun).

Moreover, the test if address of lkqui=null do not work. When I run my prog with
cobrun, no parameters are
supply and it does not display no linkage...

thanks

Christophe

Gael Wilson wrote:

> Christophe,
>
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> I hope this helps.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: call and cobrun with MF COBOL

- **From:** Kevin Digweed <Kevin.Digweed@MERANT.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38231E86.E8A98D96@MERANT.com>`
- **References:** `<3821DA7D.C320D84B@eii.fr> <7vuak3$t1j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <3822D5F6.80B1413B@eii.fr>`

```
Hi Christophe.

As Gael has already mentioned - unless the command_line_linkage tuneable
is set to FALSE (by default it is set to TRUE), your main COBOL program
will receive exactly one parameter via the linkage section. IIRC, the
format is:

01 command-line.
  03 cmd-line-length pic 9(8) comp.
  03 cmd-line-text pic x(1000).

You then access it something like:

procedure division using command-line.
display cmd-line-text(1:cmd-line-length)

Therefore, if you just run an arbitrary sub-program via cobrun, the
first parameter (in your case, lkqui) does not have a NULL address and
will contain "garbage" values.

Look up the "cobconfig" configuration file in your manual for a
description of the tuneable.

Cheers, Kev.

webmaster wrote:
> 
> Hi
…[68 more quoted lines elided]…
> > I hope this helps.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: call and cobrun with MF COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38269897.18BBE4EB@eii.fr>`
- **References:** `<3821DA7D.C320D84B@eii.fr> <7vuak3$t1j$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <3822D5F6.80B1413B@eii.fr> <38231E86.E8A98D96@MERANT.com>`

```
The problem then is that  I have the following progs :


1/ apache trigger un unix shell
2/ in the unix shell : cobrun prog1.int   (no parameters)
3/ in prog1.int .... call prog2.int using parameters_from_prog1

So can I set command_line_linkage to FALSE dynamically in my unix shell and then put
it back to TRUE ?

With that, will I avoid having that "garbage" values spoiling my prog ?

thanks

Christophe

Kevin Digweed wrote:

> Hi Christophe.

```

## Thread: Screen Section — 25 this month / 66 total

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

_Thread started [1999-10](1999-10.md#t-3db31f7b9c)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screen Section

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vruki$n0f$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<381734D2.6199A8B3@home.com> <7v763r$e4k$1@news.hitter.net> <7vaf2n$pc2$1@plutonium.btinternet.com>`

```
This is still a semi-contentious issue at J4 and WG4 - but as it stands the
"traditional" Screen Section (with ACCEPT/DISPLAY - but for a NON-gui
interface) is being ADDED to the draft of the next Standard as a REQUIRED
feature (but in the Processor Dependent list).

PROs:

1) There are existing X/Open conforming applications that this will make
portable

2) There is *STILL* no GUI "Standard" for Unix or (most) Mainframe
environments.  This will give SOME portable user-interface for such
applications

3) The use of the current ANSI/ISO "DISPLAY" is still used SIGNIFICANTLY as a
"debugging" tool (when you don't want or can't invoke a "modern" debugger).
Adding the enhanced Accept/Display is viewed as a useful "quick and dirty"
debugging tool.


CONs:

1) All the world is becoming Windows "look alike" - so anything that doesn't
work that way will have a serious problem in "real world" use.

2) Those vendors that already support a 'green screen' interface (such as IBM
CICS) probably won't implement this feature anyway - and their users wouldn't
want to use it if they did.

3) What programmers "want" is to code once - use with any compiler on any
platform AND to get the 'user-interface' that is appropriate to that
environment.  This feature definitely will NOT provide that.

 ***

Personally, I believe fairly strongly that it will "nice" to have this in the
Standard - but will certainly not be the proof of the new Standard for
wide-spread use.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screen Section

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
William M. Klein wrote in message <7vruki$n0f$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>...
>3) What programmers "want" is to code once - use with any compiler on any
>platform AND to get the 'user-interface' that is appropriate to that
>environment.  This feature definitely will NOT provide that.

This is what you get with Acucobol.

>>Simon R Hart <hart1@technologist.com> wrote in message
>> Yes, I can see this being a valid statement, *only* because we have many,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>> After all, "everyone" uses GUI displays now no matter what platform.

I have a big problem seeing that Screen sections and GUI is mutually
exclusive. I believe the solution that is chosen in Acucobol is elegant and
efficient. It is GUI all over. Best of all; it is portable.

As I have said previously, Borland Delphi and Visual Basic and certainly a
number of other tools have evolved into using a Screen section equivalent.
It would be really stupid if Cobol which has had it for several years,
should leave it, while others start using it.

Cheesle
```

feature.  Therefore, the Acucorp feature is portable across platforms (where

Interface Management System - I think) that does provide what you are talking
about (for functionality) but which never "caught on" among implementors.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screen Section

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8048hi$t5r$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<381734D2.6199A8B3@home.com> <7v763r$e4k$1@news.hitter.net> <7vaf2n$pc2$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <7vruki$n0f$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <7vsap9$76f$1@news.cerf.net> <7vskgo$7oj$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <7vtlfk$gp5$2@news.igs.net> <7vtu78$hoi$1@news.cerf.net> <7vvpit$qrc$2@news.igs.net> <8024v8$s6j$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
I could use the screen sections from one Cobol in another, or, for example,
use screen sections on one platform, but a GUI on another. The screen
interface would be in a library that exists outside the main code, rather
than imbedded.

As I see current systems, the disk I-O, screen I-O, and the main program
logic should be clearly delineated into three distinct modules.  Only the
calling sequence between the three need be fixed.  OO probably gives us the
cleanest break.

It is not unusual in a modern system to use three different languages for
those three functions (for example, SQL & a DBM, SP2, and pure Cobol).  By
making all three of those OO Cobol modules, then you have the ability to
substitute any given one without changing the other two.


Cheesle wrote in message <8024v8$s6j$1@news.cerf.net>...
>
>donald tees wrote in message <7vvpit$qrc$2@news.igs.net>...
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screen Section

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38264942.BA622CDC@home.com>`
- **References:** `<381734D2.6199A8B3@home.com> <7v763r$e4k$1@news.hitter.net> <7vaf2n$pc2$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <7vruki$n0f$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <7vsap9$76f$1@news.cerf.net> <7vskgo$7oj$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <7vtlfk$gp5$2@news.igs.net> <7vtu78$hoi$1@news.cerf.net> <7vvpit$qrc$2@news.igs.net> <8024v8$s6j$1@news.cerf.net> <8048hi$t5r$1@news.igs.net> <3825CC11.D8B8F2F3@home.com> <804n8g$ovf$1@news.cerf.net>`

```


Cheesle wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan wrote in message <3825CC11.D8B8F2F3@home.com>...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Unfortunately this often ends up as "Three-times-as-slow-systems" :-)

Sorry Cheesle - just not true. 

Logically, you would think having all three lumped together would ensure
best results. Keeping the concept of three-tier, as I think Donald said,
allows you to switch the modules for screen presentation and file
handling, without a major upheaval.

In my own situation, from my business.cbl in two programs(classes) I can
hop back and forth between two full windows (window1.cbl and
window2.cbl) without any show of slowness whatsoever, and on OK update
the business.cbl invokes my file routines (dbi.cbl) and does a
write/rewrite without apparent delay - then displays window1.cbl to
accept next choice or exit.

I've mentioned before - N/E users have found COBOL GUIs more effective
than doing calls to Visual Basic - and to the best of my knowledge, they
weren't even referring to OO. I have no personal experience of VB - so I
just have to take what they have said on face value.

There is a fractional slowness when I first invoke business.cbl because
in the two programs I'm referring to, I am dynamically creating tables.
But I consider this minimal delay acceptable at program start-up.

I see in your separate reply to Donald you mention VB - or ActiveX could
be the answer. Well, I've commented above about VB and as regards
ActiveX - you may recall Pete Dashwood 'swears by it'. Not something
I've yet got into, but taking that three-tier approach I just swap -
window1 and window2.cbl which are GUIs now become window3 and
window4.cbl which are ActiveX(s) - but here we are talking specific to a
platform. Perhaps better still, at some future date they become SP2
to overcome the platform problem.

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screen Section

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3823561e.28108665@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<381734D2.6199A8B3@home.com> <7v763r$e4k$1@news.hitter.net> <7vaf2n$pc2$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <7vruki$n0f$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <7vsap9$76f$1@news.cerf.net> <7vskgo$7oj$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <7vtlfk$gp5$2@news.igs.net> <7vtu78$hoi$1@news.cerf.net> <38232275.14880965@news.enter.net> <7vvdea$8ck$1@news.cerf.net>`

```
"Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no> wrote:

>Bob Wolfe wrote in message <38232275.14880965@news.enter.net>...
>>The last thing I want to do is get in another spirited discussion
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Correct, however, what is happening inside what is called is not Cobol.

...and what is happening inside the Acucobol runtime is not COBOL
either.

I'm completely and totally perplexed why you insist on comparing the
Acucobol-GT syntax with the internals of our sp2.dll.  That is
absolutely comparing apples and oranges.

You *should* be making the comparision as follows:

Compare the actual logic which the COBOL programmer writes to control
the screens in both products....ergo Acu-GT screen syntax with COBOL
sp2 CALL's.

If you want to compare the internals of the sp2 screen handler, then
you should compare that with the internals of the Acucobol runtime.

>You are much too sensitive about this :-)

If I am sensitive about this, it is because the logic which is used by
the COBOL programmer in a COBOL sp2 application to control the GUI
screen is, in fact COBOL.  In fact, it is ANSI standard COBOL.  You
keep telling people it is not, but it *is*!

The logic which is used by the COBOL programmer in an Acucobol
application to control the GUI screen is, in fact an extension to the
COBOL language.

In BOTH cases, the WinAPI is ultimately being invoked by a non-COBOL
program.  In the case of the sp2.dll, the WinAPI is in fact being
invoked by a C language program.  In the case of the Acucobol
WRUN32.EXE, the WinAPI is in fact being invoked by a C language
program.

If I am sensitive, it is because the programming interface in sp2 IS
COBOL.....whatever is "under the covers" is immaterial.

>Sp2 is a good utility, it just isn't something for me.

That's fine.  I'm sure that you've seen my posts before which suggest
that we do NOT believe that sp2 is right for everyone.  We wouldn't be
so pompous or arrogant as to suggest otherwise.

I know that there are companies out there who do indeed attempt to
convince people that they have the ultimate tool.  They make
ridiculous claims that their tool is a "one size fits all" tool.
There is no tool available which is "all things to all people" and my
advice is to turn and run in the other direction when you hear a sales
rep make that claim.


Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screen Section

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38227f5e.26900323@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<381734D2.6199A8B3@home.com> <7v763r$e4k$1@news.hitter.net> <7vaf2n$pc2$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <7vruki$n0f$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <382250AF.876500BE@home.com>`

```
On Fri, 05 Nov 1999 03:27:36 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:

>
>
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>

New GUI objects are added all the time.  There's noting static or
compatible about a GUI across platforms.  Within a very limted set,
yes, but get more "Graphical" than the most rudimentary GUI and you
have things that just aren't portable.

OS/2's containers for example are not supported by win32s.  

I am of the opinion that the UI is going to change faster than any
language standard can accept.  Is GUI defined in any other languages
standard?  It's always platform dependant, OS dependant, and should
remain so.   I don't think the interface (and that means perhaps
excluding the screen section) should be standardized.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screen Section

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3824A1A7.C72E256B@home.com>`
- **References:** `<381734D2.6199A8B3@home.com> <7v763r$e4k$1@news.hitter.net> <7vaf2n$pc2$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <7vruki$n0f$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <382250AF.876500BE@home.com> <38227f5e.26900323@news1.attglobal.net>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Fri, 05 Nov 1999 03:27:36 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> excluding the screen section) should be standardized.

Thane,

You may be right - I just don't know. But specifically I'm thinking of
the 'standard' windows controls which are all in fact 'mini-windows'.
There isn't a limitless set. They appear in M/S DialogEditor and the
M/F Dialog Editor. 

Accepting these controls as the basis I can't see a reason why they
shouldn't be obtainable from an enhanced Screen Section, regardless of
platform.

When you say :-

> I am of the opinion that the UI is going to change faster than any
> language standard can accept.  

Do you have specific areas in mind. Consider your message about Fujitsu
- W. Gates III can do what he likes with Windows 2000, but it is
irrelevant until our COBOL compilers/tools comply.

Now let's try another tack. Simon Hart - you studied Smalltalk at
university - are its GUIs so far removed from Windows that they are
totally incompatible, or are they close to what you are using in
NetExpress.

Anybody dabbling with Linux - again are GUI controls incompatible ?

Remember folks - Xerox PARC Simula/Smalltalk - simulation and GUIs being

On Sat, 06 Nov 1999 21:37:39 GMT, "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
wrote:

>Thane,
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Linux has GUI with KDE or GNOME - X/Windows has been around as long or
longer than Windows - some people where I work run it as an interface
under VMS - which has it's own GUI - the controls are similar, but
different from MS-Windows.  There may or may not be a common subset.
We should not LIMIT COBOL to this set, nor should we limit our
development with COBOL by such a set.  Standardizing something as
fluid as the UI is just not a good idea IMHO.  Keep it external.

>
>> I am of the opinion that the UI is going to change faster than any
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

That's a platform dependant area.  If the platform changes out from
under you, you are at a loss.  The vendors need to keep up - but
SHOULD be able to rely on at least some level of downward
compatibility.  


---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Screen Section

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vubdh$rok$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<381734D2.6199A8B3@home.com> <7v763r$e4k$1@news.hitter.net> <7vaf2n$pc2$1@plutonium.btinternet.com> <7vruki$n0f$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <382250AF.876500BE@home.com>`

```
Playing "devil's advocate" (or something) - as most readers of this NG know,
I do NOT agree with some of J4's/WG4's decisions - but ...

The problems are: (I think)

A) J4 and WG4 do not want to LIMIT COBOL to those environments that have
screens.  (For example, some day your car's carburetor COULD be programmed in
COBOL)

B) As stated elsewhere in this thread there is VERY little in UI's that are
actually available in all (or even most) platforms.  "Assuming" a Windows
look-and-feel, may make "real-world" sense (at least for some people) but
most certainly DOES NOT make sense for "international Standards".  (If there
WERE a standard for UNIX, this might not be as big an issue, but considering
the largest "open system" still doesn't have a Standard GUI is pretty
"discouraging".)

C) I keep mentioning FIMS - but this really WAS an attempt to get something
that was cross-language and cross-compiler for "interfaces".  There was
originally native COBOL syntax - but it was changed to a COBOL "binding".
But there STILL isn't sufficient user-demand for this to get implemented on
many platforms.  Until this (or this type of thing) to get true user-demand,
I don't see it getting into the COBOL Standard.
```

---

### In-line PERFORM with no statements

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vsag6$tpd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
 An in-line perform with no statements, like

     PERFORM
       UNTIL <test>
     END-PERFORM

compiles suceesfully under MVS COBOL/LE370 but gives a compile error
under Fujitsu v3.  The Fujitsu compiler requires you to have at least
one statement in an in-line PERFORM (even if it is only CONTINUE).

Is this difference caused by
- changes in the rules for COBOL
- an implementation option for compilers
- an error in one of the compilers?
```

#### ↳ Re: In-line PERFORM with no statements

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FwiU3.7539$aS6.81040@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<7vsag6$tpd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Ed Milne wrote:
> An in-line perform with no statements, like
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>- an error in one of the compilers?

The difference is because the MVS compiler permits the non-standard
form, and Fujitsu does not.  The COBOL 85 standard Format 3 PERFORM
syntax is as follows (on page VI-109):

    PERFORM [ procedure-name-1 [ {THRU|THROUGH } procedure-name-2 ] ]
    -------                       ---- -------

        [ WITH TEST {BEFORE|AFTER} ]  UNTIL condition-1
               ----  ------ -----     -----

        [ imperative-statement-1  END-PERFORM ]
                                  -----------

In other words, you can use END-PERFORM, if and only if, you include
an imperative statement.  This requirement made is on all the PERFORM
statement formats.  The purpose of END-PERFORM is to delimit the code
included in the inline PERFORM.  If there is no inline code, there is
no need for an END-PERFORM. :-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: In-line PERFORM with no statements

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vurje$ofi$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7vsag6$tpd$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <FwiU3.7539$aS6.81040@news4.mia>`

```
In article <FwiU3.7539$aS6.81040@news4.mia>,
  "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
<snip>
> The difference is because the MVS compiler permits the non-standard
> form, and Fujitsu does not.  The COBOL 85 standard Format 3 PERFORM
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
> --

This agrees with the IBM reference manual but it is not what the
compiler actually does.
```

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>

#### ↳ Re: In-line PERFORM with no statements

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3822E5D9.C34A5C38@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<7vsag6$tpd$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Ed Milne wrote:
8< .... >8
> compiles suceesfully under MVS COBOL/LE370 but gives a compile error
> under Fujitsu v3.  The Fujitsu compiler requires you to have at least
> one statement in an in-line PERFORM (even if it is only CONTINUE).
Always compile with non-standard flagging all. Always. ALWAYS. Even if the code
will never be ported, eventually you yourself will. And learning things
No-Longer to do is much hardier than learning things to do.

My experience is that using standard COBOL will pay, sooner or later.

Of course there are extensions sometimes to be used. (goback, return-code,
call-convention). I try to separate those in a generic sub-module, copymember
etc.

The Frog
```

<a id="t-c4b3c036b8"></a>
## Thread: TestBefore/After Examples Please — 9 msgs

### TestBefore/After Examples Please

- **From:** dc606@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vrnvt$g7p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I am having a hard time understanding the PERFORM VARYING.  I understand
there are two kinds:

1. one that tests before.
2. one that tests after.

And the default is test before.


Can someone please give me an example of how each one
is coded.

Thank you


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: TestBefore/After Examples Please

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vs0ov$h1i$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7vrnvt$g7p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
It is quite simple.  The default is to check before so:

   MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG.
   PERFORM READ-FILE UNTIL END FLAG IS EQUAL TO "Y".
*****does not execute READ-FILE at all*****

   MOVE "Y" TO END-FLAG.
   PERFORM READ-FILE  WITH TEST AFTER
          UNTIL END FLAG IS EQUAL TO "Y".
***** executes READ-FILE once(provided END-FLAG is not reset)*****

The same thing is true of varying. So if the condition is true on entry, the
perform would not happen. IE:-

PERFORM XXX VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1
    UNTIL SUB1 IS EQUAL TO 1.

Would never execute XXX, as the condition would be true on the first
instance.

PERFORM XXX VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1
    UNTIL SUB1 IS EQUAL TO 2.
executes 1 time.

PERFORM XXX VARYING SUB1 FROM 1 BY 1
    UNTIL SUB1 IS GREATER THAN 2.
executes 2 times(and is the common usage).

dc606@my-deja.com wrote in message <7vrnvt$g7p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I am having a hard time understanding the PERFORM VARYING.  I understand
>there are two kinds:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: TestBefore/After Examples Please

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991104063852.13442.00000777@ngol05.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7vrnvt$g7p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <7vrnvt$g7p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, dc606@my-deja.com writes:

>
>I am having a hard time understanding the PERFORM VARYING.  I understand
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Default tests after is equivalent to  DO/UNTIL

     PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1
             UNTIL ((SUB > SUB-LIMIT) OR
                        (EARLY-EXIT))
             some data manipulation
             some test for a condition
                   set EARLY-EXIT TO TRUE
                   end-if
    END-PERFORM

Testing conditions before

     PERFORM VARYING SUB FROM 1 BY 1
             UNTIL ((SUB > SUB-LIMIT) OR
                        (EARLY-EXIT))
             WITH TEST BEFORE
             some data manipulation
             some test for a condition
                   set EARLY-EXIT TO TRUE
                   end-if
    END-PERFORM

This is the basic idea of how it goes together.
I do not have a syntax validated sample as
I have never run into a situation where I have 
used this type of syntax.  Also, my manual
is not readily available for verification of the
placement of 'WITH TEST BEFORE'.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: TestBefore/After Examples Please

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nEpU3.464$dL.20073@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<7vrnvt$g7p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <19991104063852.13442.00000777@ngol05.aol.com>`

```
Pay no attention to the man behind the curtain. WITH TEST BEFORE
is the default. WITH TEST AFTER, if desired, must be coded.
The difference is whether the data manipulation code within
the loop gets executed at the terminal condition, viz:

PERFORM UNTIL (condition)
     [test for exiting condition is done here]
     data manipulation
     END-PERFORM.

PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL (condition)
     data manipulation
     [test for exiting condition done here]
     END-PERFORM.

Specifically,

PERFORM VARYING COUNT FROM 1
     BY 1 UNTIL COUNTER = 3
     ADD COUNT TO SUM
     END-PERFORM
(Result: SUM = 3)

differs from

PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER VARYING COUNT FROM 1
     BY 1 UNTIL COUNTER = 3
     ADD COUNT TO SUM
     END-PERFORM
(Result: SUM = 6)



Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123
> Default tests after is equivalent to  DO/UNTIL
>
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: TestBefore/After Examples Please

- **From:** The COBOL Frog <H.Klink@IMN.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3822E023.86637E56@IMN.nl>`
- **References:** `<7vrnvt$g7p$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <19991104063852.13442.00000777@ngol05.aol.com> <nEpU3.464$dL.20073@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
Jerry P wrote:
> PERFORM UNTIL (condition)
>      [test for exiting condition is done here]
>      data manipulation
>      END-PERFORM.

Personally I prefer
  PERFORM WITH TEST BEFORE UNTIL (condition)
       [test for exiting condition is done here]
       data manipulation
       END-PERFORM.
i.e. explicitly code the BEFORE.
I know it's default, but coding it explicitly I show the future reader that I
did really know what I wanted.


> PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL (condition)
>      data manipulation
>      [test for exiting condition done here]
>      END-PERFORM.

Frog
```

#### ↳ Re: TestBefore/After Examples Please

- **From:** "Unbeliever" <popsider@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<802rpd$slq$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7vrnvt$g7p$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Been allot of funky responses on this, and if I ever interview SOME of these
people,
I will personally kill them.

Don't bother with test before. After a very short time, you'll remember.
Use TEST AFTER if you need to perform at least once.
If you have a choice, use default (test before).
That's all there is to it.

Although some people have responded with syntactically correct code, it
makes
it much longer & time consuming to read & understand. So when I get paged at
3:45am (I normally get up at 5am), 4 days in a row, and have to figure out
what the
intent of the logic is - I want it as simple as possible.

The same applies when I have a daily job gone gaga, and I have 37 minutes
remaining,
in which to figure out the problem, code a fix and rerun it
(I don't even have time to test - don't tell anybody I said that), before
the next daily run.



<dc606@my-deja.com> wrote in message news:7vrnvt$g7p$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> I am having a hard time understanding the PERFORM VARYING.  I understand
> there are two kinds:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

---

<a id="t-478bd43ad8"></a>
## Thread: Hey Grumpy Old Men — 10 msgs

### Hey Grumpy Old Men

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s8216e3b.017@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Checking body parts - nope - not male - grumpy maybe (gotta check with the resident teenager) - but not male.

DD gave a very good answer.  I too would be glad to point someone in the right direction if some indication of effort to solve the problem was exhibited in the asking of the question.  

To directly ask 'How do I......' implies that research wasn't done.  To say 'I've done A, B, C and D and it just doesn't seem to come together' implies that an effort was put forth and another pair of eyes would help.

Many of the questions asked could easily be answered by looking in the table of contents or index of the text book being used as they are basic coding structures that should be covered in your average COBOL 101 class.

Most of us when we were learning our trade didn't have newsgroups to ask questions of, some here may have even learned by the seat of their pants because they didn't have the benifit of formal instruction and were in the right place at the right time.  We learned by trial and error and finally either turned in the assignment wrong or got direction from the instructor while doing the lab work (for those of us who had computer labs to work in) or from others who have completed the assignment or upper classmen who have completed the course.  

I don't do my kids' homework for them.  I get their textbook, look thru it to find the reference to what they are trying to do, go over it with them, walk them thru a problem or 2, and then they are on their own.  They don't dare to ask until they have rattled it around in their brains first because my  reponse to a question is  1) Did you look it up?  2) Have you tried it yet? 3) What was your result?  And they better answer 1) Yes, 2) Yes, and 3) This (show me an example)  Why should I do any different with the students here?

Da (maybe) grumpy (Old??!!) woman.


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Hey Grumpy Old Men

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

- **References:** `<s8216e3b.017@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Eileen Preston wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> Da (maybe) grumpy (Old??!!) woman.

In the spirit of "You don't have to be Jewish to love Levy's" & "you
don't have to be old to be a dirty old man", you don't have to be male
to be a grumpy old man:-)

Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: Hey Grumpy Old Men

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3821D33A.B85CF43@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<s8216e3b.017@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Eileen Preston wrote:

> Most of us when we were learning our trade didn't have newsgroups to ask questions of, some here may have even learned by the seat of their pants because they didn't have the benifit of formal instruction and were in the right place at the right time.  We learned by trial and error and finally either turned in the assignment wrong or got direction from the instructor while doing the lab work (for those of us who had computer labs to work in) or from others who have completed the assignment or upper classmen who have completed the course.

The most important thing to do with early computer courses was to make
sure you didn't have any typos when punching your cards.  When the best
you could hope for was one run per night, you also had better not be
taking a compressed summer course.  So it was check, recheck, and check
again to make sure you didn't waste your one run.
```

- **Message-ID:** `<38236374.AB009905@home.com>`

```


Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Volker Bandke wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> all returned "brummige old men". :-)

Netscape spellchecker - wrote to Nilo(Brazil) referring to St. Anthony
of Padua - spellchecker suggested Papua !

Tell you two things I've learned with spellcheckers/translators :-

(a) Don't use spellcheckers for your geography assignments

(b) If you aren't completely foolish - don't try translating into    
Portuguese - not unless you are prepared to converse in Portuguese,
for ever, and for ever, and for ever, and ........

Jimmy
```

Hi Eileen,

You are right. Sorry about the title, but the dialog in some of the threads
inspired me to refer to the movie-title. And .... I hope that we all can
make our comments with a little smile.

I think the discussion has been very positive. It shows the students what we
expect from them, and if they do their part we will be there to help them.
Or 'Life is a cycling tour. You need to pedal (hard) before free-wheel'.

BTW did you ever see a grumpy woman in any of the threads - until now I
mean? Sorry for that, I just cannot help it. Actually I don't know whether
you are grumpy or old or 'not male' - and i'm not sure that you should let a
resident teenager decide on any of them.

Your vews are very close to mine, and I thank you for expressing them.

Keep smiling (even to the teenager)
Ib

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Report Writer

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vrvbg$ejs$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<19991104070104.01866.00000587@ng-cp1.aol.com>`

```
What compiler are you using?  Many (but not all) compilers support the
ANSI/ISO Report Writer.  There are also vendors who provide "add-ons" for
those compilers that don't support it via their own compile-time features.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Report Writer

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382233E5.6EBEFA44@siber.com>`

No, you don't.

You can read MF data files into Crystal Reports directly
using mfdata2cr product that can be bought from Siber Systems.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Report Writer

- **From:** "Cheesle" <cheesle@online.NoSpamPlease.no>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vt00c$oi7$1@news.cerf.net>`
- **References:** `<7vs9r1$6en$1@news.cerf.net> <19991104114559.11278.00001769@ng-fd1.aol.com>`

```
Bob7536 wrote in message <19991104114559.11278.00001769@ng-fd1.aol.com>...
>Good Morning,
>
>Yes Win9x/NT.

Good morning to you too, although it is way away from morning here :-)

Would you be interested in looking into a brand new form designer?

    If you provide me with an email address, I could send you a document
describing the product.

Generally speaking, it has these advantages;

    The cobol application is in charge throughout the print, from the point
of choosing printer, via
    providing application logic manipulated data to handling error
conditions. If preferred, the
    end user will never know of anything but your Cobol application.

    The end user has a graphical utility to modify the form freely. Without
the need to modify the
    cobol application. This includes, images, fonts, frames, duplex
printing, bin selection, you
    name it.

    Data is provided by the Cobol application the way the Cobol application
want to provide it,
    and not read from any file.

Cheesle
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL Report Writer

- **From:** maxomtech@wiscasset.net
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vtg7n$qjq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19991104070104.01866.00000587@ng-cp1.aol.com>`

```
There are several options, Crystal Reports is what we ship with our ODBC
interface (since is is ODBC, the end-user can actually chose any ODBC
compliant product).  If ODBC is not an option then depending on what
COBOL vendor you use (we use MF, RM, and Acu), then you might look at
Cipher Systems (www.ciphersys.com), they have a report writer actually
written in COBOL.  Also, IQ report writer (don't have any info handy)
and Snow Software (www.snowsoft.com).

Hope it helps.

Chris Loy
Maxom Holdings, Inc.
http://www.maxom.com


In article <19991104070104.01866.00000587@ng-cp1.aol.com>,
  bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:
> Good Morning,
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: COBOL Report Writer

- **From:** "Barvin" <barvin@mwis.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GTrU3.1405$yF6.7946@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<19991104070104.01866.00000587@ng-cp1.aol.com> <7vtg7n$qjq$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
we offer both IQ and Cipher Systems Companion to our users.
most our users are unix but some are dos.
I believe Companion supports all platforms.
And Companion is a cobol program.
These report writers read our index data files (vision files, acucobol)
based on the
dictionary.
I am the one who manages Companion and writes the dictionaries, etc.
So I could tell you more than you need to know about it.

<a id="t-cb966fca9c"></a>
## Thread: Sorting — 2 this month / 17 total

_Thread started [1999-10](1999-10.md#t-cb966fca9c)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mW9U3.570$GH5.20943@viper>`
- **References:** `<7v7l33$ek6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <3817765D.B4527B87@NOSPAMhome.com> <iBiS3.8892$qy6.121888@news2.mia> <D2tS3.1219$jD2.33721@typhoon01.swbell.net> <Jw5T3.1886$iS.87064@viper> <sr7T3.744$td2.30144@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
I know my terminology is off. I haven't had to think about this stuff in a
long time, but here it goes....
>1 ADAMS            4
>2 BROWN          1
>3 CLARK            3
>4 DOE                 2


using your example the sort I am thinking about would be,,,,

Adding the names in some random order would have the table look like:

BROWN
CLARK
DOE
ADAMS

The index array would hold values:
4
1
2
3
Given Arrays NAME and NAME-INDEX, to refer to the first NAME you would:
PERFORM VARYING CTR FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL END-OF-TABLE
    DISPLAY NAME(NAME-INDEX(CTR))
END-PERFORM

Thus if you were to apply a SORT to the NAME table, no values would have to
be moved, just the NAME-INDEX values would be moved around. With small data
fields this would be inappropriate, but if each table entry is quite large
the actual moves would be significantly less than moving all the data in the
table entry.

Obviously I don't know the correct terminology for this type of sort, but it
always seemed like a good way to speed up any table sort.

Oh yeah...the actual sort I was thinking of, a modified BUBBLE SORT, I think
it is actually called a SHELL sort

PERFORM VARYING CTR1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL CTR1 > MAX-TABLE-ENTRY
    PERFORM VARYING CTR2 FROM CTR1+1 BY 1 UNTIL CTR2 > MAX-TABLE-ENTRY
        IF NAME(NAME-INDEX(CTR1)) > NAME(NAME-INDEX(CTR2))
            TEMP = NAME-INDEX(CTR1)
            NAME-INDEX(CTR1) = NAME-INDEX(CTR2)
            NAME-INDEX(CTR2) = TEMP
        END-IF
    END-PERFORM
END-PERFORM
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Sorting

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vrv8k$tm1$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7v7l33$ek6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <qSOR3.712$jn3.32239@typhoon01.swbell.net> <CC3S3.345$iS.19137@viper>`

```
In the draft Standard (and in several existing implementations - including
MERANT), a TABLE SORT is valid, i.e.

01 Whatever.
    05 Sort-Work  occurs 10 times.
          10 Sort-Key Pic XX
           10 OtherStuf  Pic XXX.

   ....

SORT SORT-WORK ON ASCENDING KEY SORT-KEY.

(I can't remember but I think that
    INPUT PROCEDURE IS WAFFLE-IRON
    OUTPUT PROCEDURE IS DIABETES.
would even be allowed).

If you have your table defined with an ASCENDING/DESCENDING key, you don't
even need to tell it what to sort on - unless you want to sort on some other
field (which is allowed).
```

## Thread: Code to print page numbers___another student's question — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### RE: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** "Thompson, William" <wthompson@okc.disa.mil>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E47B36D9946CD311995000A0C9EA3E925F64ED@voyager.okc.disa.mil>`

```
How astute, yes, you are making sense...

No matter that you will ALWAYS know that you are only incrementing by one
and the increment in question will it will NEVER pass the limit without
equaling the limit and you will test for the condition EVERY time....

....testing for "less than or equal to" or "not less than" is just a small
insurance policy against the existence of Murphy's Law.....

-----Original Message-----
From: Pat [mailto:patp@cpinternet.com]

snip

PRINT-ENTRY.
    IF (LINE-COUNT NOT < MAX-LINES)
        PERFORM PAGE-HEADING
    END-IF.

The question I've had since last year - in my first few courses - is this:
do I need to say 'not less than' or can I say 'equal to' or 'less than or
equal to'????  Or could double spacing maybe mess us the 'equal to'?

Hope I'm making sense,
Thanks,
Pat

snip


 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Code to print page numbers___another student's question

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38219928.50611609@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<E47B36D9946CD311995000A0C9EA3E925F64ED@voyager.okc.disa.mil>`

```
> PRINT-ENTRY.
>     IF (LINE-COUNT NOT < MAX-LINES)
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pat

Let's say somewhere down the line someone decides to add a detail
paragraph.  Maybe the detail line gets expanded to two because they
wanted to add a field which just won't fit on the original detail line. 
And since these two lines always have to be together, they don't want to
go to top of page between the two lines.

Or the users just want double spacing...

The change is:
 PRINT-ENTRY.
     IF (LINE-COUNT NOT < MAX-LINES)
         PERFORM PAGE-HEADING
     END-IF.
     PRINT DETAIL-LINE-1   AFTER ADVANCING 2
     PRINT DETAIL-LINE-2   AFTER ADVANCING 1.
     ADD 3     TO LINE-COUNT.

Or worse, DETAIL-LINE-2 only gets printed sometimes.  Maybe your test
works quite nicely because it gets printed an even number of times.  But
in production, you get headings for the first 12 pages, and then
everything else is printed without a heading.
```

## Thread: MF Cobol and Lawson on HP UNIX — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF Cobol and Lawson on HP UNIX

- **From:** "Peter Bodman" <peter.bodman@layzer.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf26d5$5e648a00$027076c1@bodmap.londis>`

```

Does anybody know of any tools to help analyse the performance of
MF Cobol code at run time ?

We have a Hewlett Packard K460 4 way running HP/UX 10.20 with Lawson
Financials version 7.0.8 and Informix V7.24.

We are experiencing some servre performance problems with some specific
Lawson code that is undergoing volumetric testing.

The Lawson code has been written in MF Cobol version V1.101

The code in question (LE129) runs very slowly but system monitoring of both
UNIX and Informix reveal no bottlenecks. It would appear that the
constraints (if any) are within the Lawson/Cobol arena.

Lawson support has been next to useless so we are wondering if anybody has
any
exeperience in this area that could be of use ?

Any suggestions would be welcome !

Peter Bodman
```

## Thread: MF COBOL and IBM data types — 2 this month / 15 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

_Thread started [1999-10](1999-10.md#t-7860b6c4e8)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL and IBM data types

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vrrmh$c0j$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7vbkm9$4dv$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <19991029093200.13133.00000002@ngol06.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky <sff5ky@aol.comxxx123> wrote in message
news:19991029093200.13133.00000002@ngol06.aol.com...
> In article <7vbkm9$4dv$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>, "Rick Price"
> <rick@hpd.co.uk> writes:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Please read the original comment.  COMP *is* Packed-Decimal (or the same as
COMP-3) on OS/400 - however, it is binary on OS/390.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF COBOL and IBM data types

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-04T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vrse0$v2v$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3819000F.8AD518A0@san.rr.com> <7vbkm9$4dv$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <3819A24D.BBDD02BE@san.rr.com>`

```
Just one other additional bit of information on this thread.

*BOTH* Micro Focus (MERANT) and IBM provide support for creating "EBCDIC"
files on the PC.  Both fully support all the BINARY options and datatypes
that are compatible on OS/390 and PC.

HOWEVER, only IBM provides an option for COMP-1 and COMP-2 to be stored in
OS/390 style floating-point format.  MERANT supports COMP-1 and COMP-2 - but
uses IEEE format (and provides a utility WFL for converting between the two
formats).  IBM allows you to 'select' which format you use on the PC.

NOTE WELL: Very few IBM COBOL applications (that I know of) actually use
COMP-1 or COMP-2 - but if you need it, this is an issue in choosing between
the 2 compilers.
```

I have tried using:

Write Print-Line after Advancing 2 Lines and I get following error:

"Unrecognized verb" for this statement.

Any idea why?



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.

#### ↳ Re: After Advancing 2 lines - unrecognized verb

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382367D5.4843@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<7vuv0i$qvg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
dc606@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> I have tried using:
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.

The syntax you posted here looks fine for mainframe LE/370 COBOL.  Maybe
you are in a different compiler that doesn't support it?  Older
mainframe COBOL required "AFTER POSITIONING" instead of "AFTER
ADVANCING".

Pete
```

#### ↳ Re: After Advancing 2 lines - unrecognized verb

- **From:** Cityboy@Concentric.Net [Ron]
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<802mtg$e74@chronicle.concentric.net>`
- **References:** `<7vuv0i$qvg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
>I have tried using:
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

AFTER ADVANCING has worked on every IBM compiler I've ever used going
back to IBM DOS (mainframe) on a 360. A period makes no difference
unless it prematurely terminates the statement.  The syntax is fine
for an IBM compiler. If this is *exactly* the the way you have it
coded in the program AND you are using an older IBM compiler such as
OS Cobol or VS Cobol II, it may not recognize the verb in lower case.
If that's not it, please post more of code and what compiler you are
using so we see.
```

- **References:** `<7vuv0i$qvg$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <802mtg$e74@chronicle.concentric.net>`

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>

What nonsense.  There is a difference between syntax and style.

Gumbo wrote in message <1TsV3.179$Av4.7108@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>...
>>Write Print-Line after Advancing 2 Lines and I get following error:
>>
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: After Advancing 2 lines - unrecognized verb

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xCmW3.1891$Av4.37866@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<7vuv0i$qvg$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <1TsV3.179$Av4.7108@typhoon.columbus.rr.com> <806ov0$dcl$1@news.igs.net>`

```
>What nonsense.  There is a difference between syntax and style.
I said the same thing when I was first told to eliminate periods and use
scope terminators.

Style techiniques can and do eliminate syntax errors. The only way to find
out is to develop a relatively complex program from scratch and check it out
yourself.

It not only eliminates errant period erros but allows for easy insertion of
code into complicated IF and PERFORM blocks.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: After Advancing 2 lines - unrecognized verb

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80ebn4$p01$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<7vuv0i$qvg$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <1TsV3.179$Av4.7108@typhoon.columbus.rr.com> <806ov0$dcl$1@news.igs.net> <xCmW3.1891$Av4.37866@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`

```

Gumbo wrote in message ...
>>What nonsense.  There is a difference between syntax and style.


>I said the same thing when I was first told to eliminate periods and use
>scope terminators.
>
>Style techiniques can and do eliminate syntax errors. The only way to find
>out is to develop a relatively complex program from scratch and check it
out
>yourself.


I have.  Several. maybe even several thousand. It is still nonsense when
someone is told to revise a programs style from scratch to fix a bug that
has nothing to do with style.

>
>It not only eliminates errant period erros but allows for easy insertion of
>code into complicated IF and PERFORM blocks.
>
I do not use complicated if and perform blocks.  I simplify them.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: After Advancing 2 lines - unrecognized verb

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382ACF4F.ABE28B1B@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<7vuv0i$qvg$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <1TsV3.179$Av4.7108@typhoon.columbus.rr.com> <806ov0$dcl$1@news.igs.net> <xCmW3.1891$Av4.37866@typhoon.columbus.rr.com> <80ebn4$p01$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:

> >It not only eliminates errant period erros but allows for easy insertion of
> >code into complicated IF and PERFORM blocks.
> >
> I do not use complicated if and perform blocks.  I simplify them.

Bravo.  That makes much more sense than eliminating periods from code
which already is using scope terminators.

Scope terminators and simplified logic clarify your code.  Eliminating
periods does not.
```

#### ↳ Re: After Advancing 2 lines - unrecognized verb

- **From:** jcj0347@aol.com (JCJ0347)
- **Date:** 1999-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991111162257.29850.00000045@ng-co1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<7vuv0i$qvg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
>I have tried using:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any idea why?

I sometimes beat myself to death trying to figure out why a
simple line of code like this won't work, only to find out I
have misspelled a reserved word.  Remember the thread
a few months ago about ASSIGN and ASSGIN? And
PERFORM and PERFROM?

Advancing can easily become Adanvcing when your fingers
get ahead of your brain.  Try re-typing the line carefully
and I'll bet you it works.

JC Jones.
```

---

<a id="t-c205d7c7e8"></a>
## Thread: could someone get me on the right track?? — 4 this month / 27 total

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### could someone get me on the right track??

- **From:** Sunshyne <sunshynef@uswest.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38238C8F.DF7E9AEA@uswest.net>`

```
Hi guys, great newsgroup.

I just started Cobol a couple weeks ago.  We had to type a
program out of the book and run it, making a couple
modifications to run it on a pc.
I can't get it to work.  I've enclosed the program and the
.dat file and would appreciate some guidance. I tried
emailing my instructor to no avail.

Thank you,
Sunshyne
```

#### ↳ Re: could someone get me on the right track??

- **From:** "Erlend Moen" <erlend@disys.no>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<808ltb$t94$1@troll.powertech.no>`
- **References:** `<38238C8F.DF7E9AEA@uswest.net>`

```
There are some syntax-errors in your code - you have not been placing the
periods correct and misspelled other things (I guess you should be more
accurate when you copy the code from the book :-) ).
Here is a version that runs:

       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
       PROGRAM-ID. SAMPLE.
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.   SELECT EMPLOYEE-DATA
                           ASSIGN TO "EMP.DAT"
                            ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
                       SELECT PAYROLL-LISTING
                           ASSIGN TO PRINTER
                            ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  EMPLOYEE-DATA.
       01  EMPLOYEE-RECORD.
           05  EMPLOYEE-NAME-IN      PIC X(20).
           05  HOURS-WORKED-IN       PIC 9(2).
           05  HOURLY-RATE-IN        PIC 9V99.
       FD  PAYROLL-LISTING.
       01  PRINT-REC.
           05                        PIC X(20).
           05  NAME-OUT                PIC X(20).
           05                          PIC X(10).
           05  HOURS-OUT               PIC 9(2).
           05                          PIC X(8).
           05  RATE-OUT                PIC 9.99.
           05                          PIC X(6).
           05 WEEKLY-WAGES-OUT        PIC 999.99.
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS     PIC XXX VALUE 'YES'.
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       100-MAIN-MODULE.
           OPEN INPUT EMPLOYEE-DATA
                OUTPUT PAYROLL-LISTING
           PERFORM UNTIL ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS = 'NO '
               READ EMPLOYEE-DATA
                    AT END
                         MOVE 'NO ' TO ARE-THERE-MORE-RECORDS
                    NOT AT END
                         PERFORM 200-WAGE-ROUTINE
               END-READ
           END-PERFORM
           CLOSE EMPLOYEE-DATA
                 PAYROLL-LISTING
           STOP RUN.
       200-WAGE-ROUTINE.
           MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-REC
           MOVE EMPLOYEE-NAME-IN TO NAME-OUT
           MOVE HOURS-WORKED-IN TO HOURS-OUT
           MOVE HOURLY-RATE-IN TO RATE-OUT
           MULTIPLY HOURS-WORKED-IN BY HOURLY-RATE-IN
                    GIVING WEEKLY-WAGES-OUT
           WRITE PRINT-REC.

<-emo->
```

#### ↳ Re: could someone get me on the right track??

Sunshyne wrote:
>
>I just started Cobol a couple weeks ago.  We had to type a
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>emailing my instructor to no avail.

I can make a good guess which book you are using, and I think that
approach to teaching is extremely doubtful (to be generous).  My best
advice: buy a copy of "Teach Yourself COBOL in 24 Hours", by Thane
Hubbell.  You get an excellent tutorial, you get a free compiler, and
you get access to the author, who carefully responds to questions.
No doubt Thane will respond to this very post. :-)
```

<a id="t-8451f3e5dd"></a>
## Thread: Will Fujitsu V3 Work with Windows 2000? — 17 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Will Fujitsu V3 Work with Windows 2000?

- **From:** steveei@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vtku1$tmh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I recently downloaded the free Fujitsu v3 from http://www.adtools.com
and used it to compile a 4,500 line IBM COBOL II mainframe program into
a dll.  This program, an actuarial calculation module with no I/O
except through the LINKAGE SECTION, proved a very easy port to Fujitsu
and is working great when called from Visual Basic 6.

In thinking about turning this functionality live in production,
questions have been raised concerning how this fits into our client
operating system plans.  We are being told that the government
agency I work for will require all PC's to be migrated to Windows 2000
as early as (gulp) March 2000.  So:  Has anyone running a Windows 2000
beta tried running Fujitsu v3 dll's and, if so, what have experiences
been?

An obvious answer to this email is to buy a commercial Fijitsu
product.  Well, maybe we will, especially if we find good uses for
Fujitsu COBOL beyond this one actuarial program.  However, it would be
nice to have a reasonable expectation that this will continue to be
optional.

Thanks for all wisdom and opinions.

Steve Eisenberg    Wynnewood, Pennsylvania  U.S.A.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Will Fujitsu V3 Work with Windows 2000?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uWKU3.100$GH6.8540@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<7vtku1$tmh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
FJ 4.0 won't work under Win2000, so I doubt FJ3 will.

Try it and see - Beta releases of Win2000 are easily
available from Microsoft for $20.00

For more info on compatibility issues, see FJ's
website at www.adtools.com.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Will Fujitsu V3 Work with Windows 2000?

- **From:** steveei@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<800d9v$scm$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<7vtku1$tmh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <uWKU3.100$GH6.8540@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
In article <uWKU3.100$GH6.8540@typhoon01.swbell.net>,
  "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com> wrote:
> FJ 4.0 won't work under Win2000, so I doubt FJ3 will.
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Well, according to Microsoft's Windows 2000 software compatibility list
(see
http://msdnisv.microsoft.com/windows/professional/deploy/compatible/sear
ch.asp), Fujitsu COBOL 4.2 is Windows 2000 ready, meaning that it works
under Windows 2000 but does not take advantage of features new to
Windows 2000.  4.2 is the only version of Fujitsu COBOL that
Microsoft's Windows 2000 compatibility page has any information on.  As
for www.adtools.com, the only thing I see there on the subject is that
Fujitsu plans to release a new COBOL version that will take advantage
of the new Windows 2000 features.  But maybe I missed something more on
the www.adtools.com site.

Obviously your suggestion that we get the Windows 2000 beta and try out
our application on it is the right one.  I guess I was a little lazy in
posting here rather that going out and testing for myself.

Steve Eisenberg   Wynnewood, Pennsylvania  U.S.A.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Will Fujitsu V3 Work with Windows 2000?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2t6V3.237$zd3.22907@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```

<steveei@my-deja.com>

> Well, according to Microsoft's Windows 2000 software compatibility
list
> (see
>
http://msdnisv.microsoft.com/windows/professional/deploy/compatible/se
ar
> ch.asp), Fujitsu COBOL 4.2 is Windows 2000 ready, meaning that it
works
> under Windows 2000 but does not take advantage of features new to
> Windows 2000.  4.2 is the only version of Fujitsu COBOL that
> Microsoft's Windows 2000 compatibility page has any information on.
As
> for www.adtools.com, the only thing I see there on the subject is
that
> Fujitsu plans to release a new COBOL version that will take
advantage
> of the new Windows 2000 features.  But maybe I missed something more
on
> the www.adtools.com site.
>

When attempting to work out a bug in 4.2L02, I mentioned I could try
the code on Win3.11, Win95, Win98, WinNT-4, and Win2000 (all of
which we have installed in our shop) if that would help FJ track down
the bug. They responded via e-mail to not bother trying Win2000
inasmuch as that was guaranteed to be unsuccessful. I don't
remember why (wouldn't install, wouldn't compile, wouldn't run, etc.),
just that FJ didn't like Win2000.

But we haven't tried it - took 'em at their word.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Will Fujitsu V3 Work with Windows 2000?

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@worf.netins.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38245BAC.F55343B9@worf.netins.net>`
- **References:** `<7vtku1$tmh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <cREU3.9827$B15.106409@news3.mia> <3823b7e1.77412052@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> Just for the record, I asked Fujitsu support about this - v3 will not
> install properly under Windows 2000 - so no, it will not work.
> Fujitsu's Windows 2000 product will be v5.

Thane:

We are planning to migrate to Win 2000 next summer.  Will
the version of Fujitsu COBOL shipping with your book be
updated to co-exist with Win 2000 or will those of us using
your text need to go hunting for a new version of COBOL [:(]
or a new text (double [:(])?

Thanks for any help.

Floyd
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Will Fujitsu V3 Work with Windows 2000?

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38246c89.123666442@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<7vtku1$tmh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <cREU3.9827$B15.106409@news3.mia> <3823b7e1.77412052@news1.attglobal.net> <38245BAC.F55343B9@worf.netins.net>`

```
On Sat, 06 Nov 1999 11:47:40 -0500, Floyd Johnson
<floydj@worf.netins.net> wrote:

>Thane Hubbell wrote:
>> 
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Will you be chaning workstations or servers or both?  If the
workstations remain Windows 95/98 - then you should be OK... but...

Well, the bad news is that No, there are no plans a PRESENT to update
the v3 that comes with my book to a later version.   I'm as distressed
as anyone but it would require a "second edition" and a new agreement
with Fujitsu - who I am certain is not willing to "give away" a new
release of their compiler.  

All that said, I know WHY v4 doesn't work - but it is ENTIRELY
POSSIBLE (Though unsupported) that v3 WILL work with Windows 2000.
The main difference is actually the INSTALL - and V3 does not require
the same registry entries as v4 and later.  

So... the best thing I can think of right now, is to TRY v3 on Windows
2000 - has anyone attempted it?  Please report here!

Now some opinion.  Microsoft, with Windows 2000 has abandoned backward
compatibility.  This is a SAD thing.  It's not my fault, and it's not
Fujitsu's fault either.  

If it becomes an issue, and I find a definitive answer that Fujitsu
3.0 will NOT work under Windows 2000, I will broach the subject of a
second edition with the publisher (Some minor rewrite of the book will
be necessary).  We had planned the next edition to coincide with
approval of the next standard, and the release of a standards
conforming compiler.  We'll see what comes first - Windows 2000 or the
next COBOL standard!

Bottom line - if I can find that v3 Works under Windows 2000 - we'll
rest easy.  If not, I have to wait until the official release of
Windows 2000 (or at least a non beta vesion) and then either convince
Fujitsu to allow me use of a free version of v5 (or find another
compiler), convince the publisher that a second edition based on just
a compiler change will fly, and them make the modifications and get it
to print.

Simpler, if v3 doesn't work, is to generate examples that work with MF
Personal COBOL (Not hard - everything in the book is pretty generic,
only two things I can think of that need changing, return values form
the screen section, and the method for getting the command line).

I'll be happy to do this if necessary. 
---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Will Fujitsu V3 Work with Windows 2000?

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Floyd Johnson <floydj@worf.netins.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382473FF.29C8F25D@worf.netins.net>`
- **References:** `<7vtku1$tmh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <cREU3.9827$B15.106409@news3.mia> <3823b7e1.77412052@news1.attglobal.net> <38245BAC.F55343B9@worf.netins.net> <38246c89.123666442@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
> >> Just for the record, I asked Fujitsu support about this - v3 will not
> >> install properly under Windows 2000 - so no, it will not work.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> workstations remain Windows 95/98 - then you should be OK... but...

Our servers are already using NT - workstations will be
updated to Win 2000.
 
> Well, the bad news is that No, there are no plans a PRESENT to update
> the v3 that comes with my book to a later version.   

Ouch - but ...

> All that said, I know WHY v4 doesn't work - but it is ENTIRELY
> POSSIBLE (Though unsupported) that v3 WILL work with Windows 2000.
> The main difference is actually the INSTALL - and V3 does not require
> the same registry entries as v4 and later.

According to an earlier post v4.2 will work with Win 2000 -
am I misunderstanding something?
 
> So... the best thing I can think of right now, is to TRY v3 on Windows
> 2000 - has anyone attempted it?  Please report here!

Will do that next summer - if installation goes as planned.

> Now some opinion.  Microsoft, with Windows 2000 has abandoned backward
> compatibility.  This is a SAD thing.  It's not my fault, and it's not
> Fujitsu's fault either.

Not just "SAD" but "BAD" - given the current state of
affairs, it gives everybody a chance to take a new look at
the available options.

> If it becomes an issue, and I find a definitive answer that Fujitsu
> 3.0 will NOT work under Windows 2000, ...

I thought you had already found this definitive answer - see
your earlier post.  Now I am confused.

> I will broach the subject of a
> second edition with the publisher (Some minor rewrite of the book will
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> I'll be happy to do this if necessary.

Thanks - keep us (not only Thane but everybody) informed of
what is discovered.  I will be lucky to get a look at a copy
of Win 2000 before they come to my desk and say, "It's
time."  I will be interested in any future news.
```

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382500d3.161633919@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<7vtku1$tmh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <cREU3.9827$B15.106409@news3.mia> <3823b7e1.77412052@news1.attglobal.net> <38245BAC.F55343B9@worf.netins.net> <38246c89.123666442@news1.attglobal.net> <382473FF.29C8F25D@worf.netins.net>`

```
On Sat, 06 Nov 1999 13:31:27 -0500, Floyd Johnson
<floydj@worf.netins.net> wrote:
>
>
>According to an earlier post v4.2 will work with Win 2000 -
>am I misunderstanding something?
>

My understanding is that even 4.2 won't install under Windows 2000.
Something about a necessary change in the setup procedure.  

>> So... the best thing I can think of right now, is to TRY v3 on Windows
>> 2000 - has anyone attempted it?  Please report here!
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>your earlier post.  Now I am confused.

v3 is no longer officially supported by Fujitsu.  No one at Fujitsu
has tried it, but they know of problems with 4.x and the install.  

Here is what they said:

Thane,

Thanks.  It wouldn't work.  they'd have to upgrade to our latest
version.  We had to change our setup.ins files for doing the setup, so
that it would install to be used on Windows 2000.  It's likely it
would
not work.
---------
That doesn't mean 3.0 won't work.  3.0 was not as registry dependant
as 4.0 and 4.2 is.  *I* think it just might work.  They only way to
tell is to try it.  No one running Windows 2000 has written to me
about compiler install failures --- but then maybe none of my readers
have installed under Windows 2000.  Monday I will ask around at work
to see of we have a machine running Windows 2000 anywhere, and I'll
try the v3 install and compiler validation from my book.  If we don't
have one, I have a friend running it (he works at Dell) and I'll have
him try it out.  

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

Progeni has a new COBOL freeware utility called SIZER.  With SIZER you copy
any COBOL record layout to the clipboard and this application will calculate
"the size in bytes" of the (largest) record found.

How to be one of the top three to earn $50.00
We invite you to download SIZER to your system and use the freeware utility.
Then tell us about your experience in 25 words or less.  Comments can be
good or bad.  We simply want your opinion.  Progeni will pick the best three
comments during the first week in January and immediately posted them on the
website www.aboutlegacycoding.com.  Checks will be mailed the second week in
January.

E-mail your experiences to bje@progeni.com
