<a id="t-85296134b3"></a>
## Thread: Handshakling betwwen Unix & Pc — 2 msgs

### Handshakling betwwen Unix & Pc

- **From:** "low" <prestig@tm.net.my>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38222401.0@news2.tm.net.my>`

```
How do unix server send & receive data from a PC ?

I got an unix server connected to a pc using RS232 cable. In unix i have a C
program read data from a text file and sent to the pc.  C program in unix
will drop into listen mode after it send data. Program in pc will receive
the data & sent acknowledment back to unix.

the problem i face is the unix does not receive acknowledment data as
expected. The unix either does not receive any feedback or receive some last
portion of acknowledment.
Is it posibble the pc send acknowledment too fast & unix can not responce so
fast ?


Can anyone provide some solution to perform data communication between pc &
Unix ?
Thanks
Low
```

#### ↳ Re: Handshakling betwwen Unix & Pc

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<800rml$jjp$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<38222401.0@news2.tm.net.my>`

```
Hi,
I believe, that both units are listeners until something in either end of
that fine RS232 cable provokes the unit to go to send mode.

If you start the transmission with an STX, you can look for that on the
Com-port. The sender should end the transmission with an ETX. In that way
the sender can say to receiver: 'I will wait for your ACK'.

When ACK is received, both units go listening again.

Now you are telling me, that you do not receive the ACK. Did the receiving
unit actually turn to send mode and was the ACK sent?

You can display the dialog on screen in TTY-manner if you pack up received
data in - let's say - 70 bytes blocks. Displaying the dialog could show you
what goes on.

I beleive that one of the units forgets to change from Send to Receive or
reverse.

Hope, that it helps

Ib
low skrev i meddelelsen <38222401.0@news2.tm.net.my>...
>How do unix server send & receive data from a PC ?
>
>I got an unix server connected to a pc using RS232 cable. In unix i have a
C
>program read data from a text file and sent to the pc.  C program in unix
>will drop into listen mode after it send data. Program in pc will receive
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>expected. The unix either does not receive any feedback or receive some
last
>portion of acknowledment.
>Is it posibble the pc send acknowledment too fast & unix can not responce
so
>fast ?
>
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-0aea51ccbc"></a>
## Thread: Would You SIgn This Contract? — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Jobs, careers, recruiting, salary`](topics.md#jobs) · [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yYsiOBIqNO2SYyOK9I1XwWK6IbSj@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com> <OZPEbBjJ$GA.241@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
The venerable "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC"
<MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com> bestowed upon alt.computer.consultants on
Wed, 3 Nov 1999 13:18:38 -0600 these words:

>PLEASE post here who this @$$hole recruiter/broker company is who even
>handed you this ripoff!

Should I?  Do you want me to reveal XYZ?  XYZ's client?  XYZ's
headhunter?

For all concerned, thanx for the advice, but I was really only doing
this as an opinion poll.  I had already rejected the contract.  I
would never post here about a contract still under negotiation though
I might ask a few of you privately.

I was appalled at the clause, and never would think of signing it.
Some of you suggested I cut off negotiation immediately, and I would
have, if it weren't I've already had several jobs fall thru this year,
and I'm in no position to wait until January.  I was hoping to
negotiate a better contract, but after three revisions the contract
still said they had the right to stop paying me, and to present me
with a bill for previous months already paid.  And for those who
asked, neither the contract nor the job order contained any
performance description past XYZ's Client's satisfaction determined at
XYZ's client's sole discretion.

I turned it down first thing Monday, and received a rather snotty
reply to my turndown including "The contract is solid and fair for
all parties concerned."  So I thought I would ask if you all thought
it was solid and fair.

I didn't tally the responses, but I'm pretty sure that zero people
said I should sign this contract.
```

#### ↳ Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** Tim Keating <NotForJunkEmail@mediaone11.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Y=QiOM+ab=2JhjHfuP4ewbWpKLl3@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com> <OZPEbBjJ$GA.241@cpmsnbbsa02> <yYsiOBIqNO2SYyOK9I1XwWK6IbSj@4ax.com>`

```
On Fri, 05 Nov 1999 01:44:28 -0800, Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net> wrote:

>The venerable "Robert M. Pritchett - RMP Consulting Partners LLC"
><MSN.Bus.News@rmpcp.com> bestowed upon alt.computer.consultants on
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
>XYZ's client's sole discretion.

Tell them to remove the clause. Since this is not a FIXED bid contract.

I.E. No warranties on T&M contracts where clients SHARE the risks in exchange 
    for paying for only actual services rendered. 

It would be wise to only offer warranties on FIXED bid contracts,
     where the appropriate risk premiums(2.5 to 5x costs/time) have been added in.

If they want a refund, tell them they'll have to prove it to a judge.
   Which is the normal recourse for un-satistified customers without such clauses written into 
    contracts. . 

I also add my own intellectual property rights clause.   I'll have to look up the exact language.

It goes something like this..

All uncompensated, per stated contractual rates, Intellectual property and product produced 
  for client shall have ownership revert to consultant, should any portion of debt 
  for property or product produced remain outstanding for more than 60 days.

Then, be sure to slap on copyright notices on everything you create, or modify 
   significantly.   Thus, if client tries to screw you, and continues using your uncompensated 
   work, you can nail them of a Federal Copyright violation.  

>I turned it down first thing Monday, and received a rather snotty
>reply to my turndown including "The contract is solid and fair for
>all parties concerned."  So I thought I would ask if you all thought
>it was solid and fair.

You got to be prepared to walk away. It's the only way to get a fair deal.

>
>I didn't tally the responses, but I'm pretty sure that zero people
>said I should sign this contract.

See above.. don't sign. 

Tim Keating
ktcnslt32@mediaone1.net,
 (Note: remove numeric digits from email address before responding.)
```

##### ↳ ↳ 2 Finalize on Agencies (Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** Philemon <philemon@bogus_you.are.ell.org>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38229CD2.3293@bogus_you.are.ell.org>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com> <OZPEbBjJ$GA.241@cpmsnbbsa02> <yYsiOBIqNO2SYyOK9I1XwWK6IbSj@4ax.com> <Y=QiOM+ab=2JhjHfuP4ewbWpKLl3@4ax.com>`

```
> On Fri, 05 Nov 1999 01:44:28 -0800, Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net> wrote:
> >after three revisions the contract
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> >all parties concerned."
So, here's a few rules of thumb I use in order to minimize aggravation
and the loss of time:
1. Only look when in a job, never be out of job unless by your own
volition.
   (when you are, avoid recruiters as a plague.)
2. Never pick up the phone, let them talk to the answering machine first
3. In your greeting indicate clearly that you will be only able to 
   return those out-of-state recruiting calls for which a toll-free
number
   is given.

4. Listen to the message: does the guy sound like a piece of trash out
of
   a trailer part? If you don't like the sound of it (for any reason),
or
   there's no toll-free number (or invitation to call collect) do not
   return the call.

5. Expect the worst, don't waste time on moral disputes. 99% of rec
firms
   will do exactly like the one Randall dealt with this time. Therefore,
   when calling back, make sure that you ask rather soon about what
   specifically they are working on that prompted them to give you a
call.
   If they reply with "what are your top 3 skills" or "how about an
updated
   version of your resume" you're safe to hang up--they're fishing for
   resumes, the recruiter is probably a new hire. If you have you CV
   on-line somewhere, give the the url.

6. If they do share (may be a lie of course) see if that's
   a good fit in your opinion. If so, try to get them to
   connect you with the client on the phone so you can: 
   a) make sure it's not bullshit and there's a client
   b) have a chat with a real consumer so as to remove the
      last possibility of a mismatch
   c) test what relationships this rec firm has with the client,
      if they can talk to a tech party there, or all they know
      is an HR imbecile who doesn't know a shit.

   If they can't do that, that is a very bad sign. I personally
   always excuse myself if a phone interview cannot be arranged
   for.

7. Ask if they have a "standard" contract agreement that they "normally"
   ask "their" consultants to sign. (Ha ha. Yes they do <g>.) ASK FOR
   A SAMPLE COPY before you spend any more time with them. If refused,
   drop the fuckers right there.

8. Take a look at the document. If there's something you'd like
   to change or add some of your own--ask them about it. Some will 
   work with you on that, some won't. If the latter, drop the fuckers.

The idea is not to fight them down or outsmart somehow or change the
world. The idea is to avoid wasting time. Therefore in your discussions
with a rec. firm it is important to maintain a constant discovery,
prodding for crucial information. The earlier you see the red flag (or a
brick wall rather) the less time you'll waste on this dead-end
"opportunity." Of course, one will eventually see it anyway, but you
might first have to go for a couple of interviews <g>, and that's a lot
of time, travel expenses, and extra dry-cleaning.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: 2 Finalize on Agencies (Re: Would You SIgn This Contract?

- **From:** Philemon <philemon@bogus_you.are.ell.org>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.computer.consultants,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38229F43.6730@bogus_you.are.ell.org>`
- **References:** `<oiQeOOu0ogoqrECvbUzMlix7HBJH@4ax.com> <OZPEbBjJ$GA.241@cpmsnbbsa02> <yYsiOBIqNO2SYyOK9I1XwWK6IbSj@4ax.com> <Y=QiOM+ab=2JhjHfuP4ewbWpKLl3@4ax.com> <38229CD2.3293@bogus_you.are.ell.org>`

```
What I forgot to say is that ideally you should only deal with recr.
when you don't need a job. Trivial, I know. A less trivial one: if you
are out of job, try to find one w/o recruiters. In other words, look for
jobs in many places, and only deal with recrs in *certain*
circumstances. Deling with them when having no job while needing one
makes all these "political" discussions irrelevant--your ass is theirs
and they'll ef you with a zucchini <g>.
```

---

<a id="t-a966fc5e35"></a>
## Thread: I need interview questions! — 1 msgs

### Re: I need interview questions!

- **From:** Jared Thomas <jared@techie.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38238C44.23748E43@techie.com>`
- **References:** `<dXJU3.6072$d6.9738@newsr2.maine.rr.com>`

```
1.  Verify with your boss why you are doing the interview. 
Sometimes it is "can you work with the guy?", sometimes it's "Does
to guy know his stuff?" and frequently, both.

2.  Try asking stuff about where your shop's leading edge is.  Has
he coded nested programs?  Can he explain what different compiler
options do?  Tell you what data format is good for indexes, why or
why not 01 levels are contiguous in memory.  How much storage does
an Occurs Depending On really take?  What are the migration issues
to the next version of the compiler (if you aren't there yet).

Can he explain "Block contains 0 records" and how that affects
file size?  Does he know how to access VSAM or Oracle or what ever
data base you may have?  Can he work with the data base program
outside of COBOL (like SQL/PS or Oracle) or DB2 import/export
utilities?

3.  Find out about his coding philosophies.  I've seen fights in
shops over things like packed data vs binary data, paragraphs with
a single period, performing sections instead of paragraph thru
end-paragraph.  If you have a rigid program layout, have him look
at a sample and see if he can live with the standards you
enforce.  This is most important because he is also interviewing
you to see what he will have to put up with.

4. For IBM, does he use Expediter, Abend-Aid, Smart Test, File
Aid, StarTool or what ever tools your shop uses?  How about the
general utilities on your platform?  Sort?  ICETOOL?  

Has he run with LE/370, and can find the failing program in the
LE/370 formatted output?  

How about JCL?  Can he create a file, suggest performance
strategies like more buffers, bigger blocks?  Can he code JCL to
implement these?  How about JCL IF/THEN/ELSE statements?

Are you CICS, DB2, or IMS?  Can he run CICS enough to get his
program linked and loaded to replace the currently running code? 
Can he translate an error message from a CICS or IMS abend back to
a COBOL error (e.g. CICS ASRA back to 0C4 back to "bad index
value"?) 

Can he debug from a dump if he has to?  I keep a couple of old
dumps (easy problems) around and show people.  I see if he can
find the registers, figure out which statement failed from the
load module offset, find the data, etc.  Does he know that the TGT
is?

5.  Since you are in insurance does he understand the vocabulary? 
How about the basic math for calculating reserves or the possible
programing changes needed to meet XXX regulations.  How about
Interest Sensitive Life, and fund based annuities?

Can he learn?  Did he study anything in school or do some LOMA
courses?  (The FLMI and CLU designations are LOMA sponsored.)

6.  Find out generally what else he thinks he is good at besides
COBOL.  For example, could he code an extract program, move data
to Access or EXCEL and massage it there?  If you are IBM, does he
understand FTP and other things of "Unix" origin?  If he is Unix
or PC, does he understand that IBM keeps files as records, not
byte streams?  The idea is to see if he might have something else
that could be useful in the future.  For example, what if you were
going to start posting rates on a Web Site?  Might he be able to
contribute, not that you have anything to do with web sites today.

7.  The one thing I find difficult (other than by watching a
person work) is to figure out how they approach problems.  Do they
spin wheels trying all combinations?  Do they stop and look up
something they don't know?  Can they get reasonable test data
together?  Do they accept errors and changes gracefully, learn
something, fix the problem, and move on?  Or will they fight to
the end to do something their way,  ignore advice from others, and
be possessive of the code?

Hope this gets you started thinking!
```

---

## Thread: Coming November 15, 1999 — 2 msgs

### Re: Coming November 15, 1999

- **From:** "The Programmer" <someone@somewhere.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<HvDU3.9673$B15.105050@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<cIBU3.17$jN1.837@client>`

```
As an incentive to subscribe, they offer a freebie sizer. Unfortunately,
they stupid thing is junk. I fed it the following layout, and it couldn't
count the 85 bytes. Kept returning to me with an invalid syntax error.

01  WS-RECORD.
    05  WS-MAIN.
        10  WS-FLD1  PIC  X.
        10  WS-FLD2  PIC S9999.
        10  WS-FLD3  PIC  X(30).
        10  FILLER   PIC  X(50).
    05  WS-OVERLAY   REDEFINES WS-MAIN.
        10  FILLER   PIC  X(80).
        10  WS-FLD4  PIC  X(05).
```

- **References:** `<cIBU3.17$jN1.837@client> <HvDU3.9673$B15.105050@news3.mia>`

```

sizer

and also came up with 85 bytes after inserting a sequence field.

> they stupid thing is junk. I fed it the following layout, and it couldn't
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
```

<a id="t-a24de0c999"></a>
## Thread: MF COBOL Server Express and Large Files in Unix — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF COBOL Server Express and Large Files in Unix

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vv0ma$5a7$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`

```
As I understand it, server express is the next MF offering on unix, and it
allows for 'native files' > 2gb.  I.e it allows for the 'new' unix large
file system.

I believe this is now available on RS unix but not yet on HP -UX.  Also it
will be limited to HP-UX 11 and will not be available to earlier releases.

Does anybody know if:
. Server express is just a development system or also includes a compiler?
. If it is a development tool will MF Cobol 4.1 give the same file size
capabilities?

The reason I'm asking is that we do not develop in MF Cobol.  We develop on
other platforms and simply use MF COBOL as a compiler.  We don't normally
need anything fancy on the Unix.  So if MF supplied a simple compiler that
allowed for Unix large file systems we would be happy.

The last I heard, MF COBOL 4.1 handled large files by file striping (a
nonsense system if I ever saw one).  However the MF site says that MF Cobol
4.1 on HP-UX 10.20 is not the same as MF Cobol 4.1 on HP-UX 11.  So maybe on
HP-UX 11 it does things the right way!  Can anyone confirm this?

Ta

Rick
```

#### ↳ Re: MF COBOL Server Express and Large Files in Unix

- **From:** "Gazaloo" <gaz@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cNEU3.15816$Ec1.175092@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`
- **References:** `<7vv0ma$5a7$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`

```
support for native large files is SX-only. (why is striping a "nonsense
system"?)

SX is a full blown compiler and dev system (as is the COBOL product).

SX is available on AIX & Solaris. HPUX 11 is slated for delivery within the
next few months.

MERANT has just released a 4.1.30 release of the UNIX COBOL that is
available on multiple platforms (including HPUX 10.20 & 11). right now it's
in manufacturing, but i think you can pick it up from Technical Support.
```

<a id="t-04c765c72a"></a>
## Thread: newbie question:microfocus & fujitsu — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers) · [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### newbie question:microfocus & fujitsu

- **From:** "tk" <tk@clubi.ie>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vuehe$h0t$1@fire.medianet.ie>`

```
We are using microfocus cobol in school and I'm trying to use fujitsu at
home.  Micro focus is not particular about indentation(in school).  How do
you set fujitsu to do this?  Anytime I compile I get errors because my
spacing isn't right!

-thanks!
```

#### ↳ Re: newbie question:microfocus & fujitsu

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-05T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7vug6l$mu9$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<7vuehe$h0t$1@fire.medianet.ie>`

```
Use the

   SRF(VAR)

compiler directive.  (or specify "variable format" in you compiler options)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reuse of old data

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
    When accessing an international asset, such as comp.lang.cobol, we need
to try and ignore local slang whenever possible.

    Since this is probably not possible, be aware that to Americans, the
only insult in the "tool" bit is in being compared to say, a hammer.  A
Muppet is a small furry hand puppet popular with children of all ages.  If
you speak of a "bloody idiot", then he has obviously injured himself and
needs medical aid.  Calling someone a "Bastard" is really quite a mild
insult, at least compared to popular American notions of British slang, and
does not involve his or her parents at all.

    All joking aside, the "Muppets" bit is totally baffling.

Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk...
> In article <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net>,
> docdwarf@clark.net writes
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
Colloquialisms are not the only terms subject to misinterpretation... is a
vest a waistcoat, a 'weskit' or an undergarment?  Is 'tabling' a subject
opening or closing discussion on it?  Is a 'bonnet' worn by a maiden or an
automobile?

DD
```

- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38285639.CE2CA50F@NOSPAMhome.com> <809ser$vdu$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <3828871B.EC21FF0A@NOSPAMhome.com> <3828E2C4.C427BED3@att.net> <38298209.15482A5A@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
The Mary Tyler Moore "joke" about Dick van Dyke was JUST a joke - and not one
that I can figure out how to "rephrase" in a family oriented NG <G>. She told
it on national TV, but her wording was something that she could get away with
and I don't think I could.
```

```
news:80c67s$87a$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net...
> The Mary Tyler Moore "joke" about Dick van Dyke was JUST a joke -
and not one
> that I can figure out how to "rephrase" in a family oriented NG <G>.
She told
> it on national TV, but her wording was something that she could get
away with
> and I don't think I could.
>
Heard her tell Jay Leno one time that she took her dog to the
vet with the complaint that the dog kept trying to hump her
leg. "Well," the vet said, "we can fix that with a simple operation
called 'neutering'." "Gee, doc," Mary said, "I was thinking more
along the lines of a toenail-clip and something for his breath."
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reuse of old jokes

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1C0tOD1dvpOQx04UmY+vPOpYY7hA@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38285639.CE2CA50F@NOSPAMhome.com> <809ser$vdu$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <3828871B.EC21FF0A@NOSPAMhome.com> <3828E2C4.C427BED3@att.net> <38298209.15482A5A@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
The venerable Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> bestowed upon
comp.lang.cobol on Wed, 10 Nov 1999 07:32:41 -0700 these words:

>> Okay, I'll ask: how did Dick van Dyke get his name?
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>using their real names.

Yes it's a joke.  Worse yet, "Dick Van Dyke" is the punch line.  The
fact that Mary Tyler Moore told it, makes it a little funnier, because
she's associated with him, but when she starts to tell the story, you
don't know it's about Dick Van Dyke.  She just tells the story of this
actor she knew, who couldn't get any work because of his offensive
name.  Then he changed his name to Dick Van Dyke and became a success.

I won't tell you the offensive name.  This way when you're not
thinking about Dick Van Dyke, someone can tell you the joke and it
will be funny.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reuse of old data

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```


Russell Styles wrote:
> 
>     When accessing an international asset, such as comp.lang.cobol, we need
> to try and ignore local slang whenever possible.

Surprising what the passing of time does to the language on both sides
of the water and elsewhere. To us on this side, Sheila is a girl's name
- but has a more generic meaning to Ken Foskey and the folks Down Under.

Had a very dear friend here about 5 years ago, (now dead) - he was
tickled pink and rushed to get paper and pencil to jot down, "One brick
short of load, The lights are on and nobody's home, The elevator doesn't
go all the way to the top, He's rowing with only one oar etc...".
'Though from TV I hear they have an English equivalent, "An orchard
short of an apple". 

How about you Mr. Klein - pick up any 'funnies' on your recent visit ?

At long last I've got used to calling a spanner a wrench and
substituting trunk for boot, (I gather over there they have boot sales
as opposed to our garage sales), and I now call a wing on the auto a
panel. I wasn't familiar with 'tool' which in my days would have been
'He's a right p....". Like you I'm absolutely lost about the 'Muppets'
bit.

(Be nice if I could get my favourite Muppet show on a video - Harry
Belafonte singing Day-O ! with the Muppets <G>).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reuse of old data

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VS3W3.719$QY4.27370@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com> <809p90$l5r$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>`

```
Chuck Stevens wrote in message <809p90$l5r$1@mail.pl.unisys.com>...
>Hope he didn't miss the one I grew up with in the Hill Country of Texas:
"A
>few bales shy of a full loft".
>
             Top Ten "Explanations" of "User Error"
             -------------------------------------
10.  A couple of parts were left over after assembly..
 9.  Two logs short of a cord..
 8.  The lights are on, but some of the bulbs are burned out...
 7.  The bus isn't making all the scheduled stops...
 6.  Singing in a different key ...
 5.  Two bricks short of a load..
 4.  Reading from the wrong page...
 3.  Not playing with a full deck...
 2.  The elevator isn't reaching all the floors...
 1.  The lights are on, but no one's home...

           Top Ten "Explanations" of "Programmer Error"
           --------------------------------------------

10. The carry flag is stuck in the 'on' position...
 9. Two clusters short of a sector ....
 8. Must be a couple of un-OPENed files somewhere ....
 7. At least one ELSE short of a complete IF block ...
 6. So that's what you get when you divide by zero...
 5. Part of the buffer was left in the cache...
 4. Some of the high bits are masked off...
 3. One byte short of a fullword...
 2. It all swaps out, but some ain't swapping back in...
 1. The beanie propeller isn't making full rpm's...
```

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<xceW3.5032$z26.58245@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com> <809p90$l5r$1@mail.pl.unisys.com> <VS3W3.719$QY4.27370@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <3828D615.8FBFFD5F@home.com> <3828E3DF.DDB6ECF9@att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
>
>As long as you're at it, how about "Not the sharpest knife in the
>drawer", "Not the sharpest tool in the shed" (from a current pop song, &
>(my fav) "Sharp as a marble".

"Two sandwiches short of a picnic."

Since we're in a humor vein, here are some "Your mama is so fat..."
jokes that my daughter sent me recently.  I hope no one is offended,
I weigh 205 lbs. (93 kg.) myself. :-)

Your mama is so fat ...

    When she dances she makes the band skip.

    When she was diagnosed with the flesh eating disease the doctor
       gave her 13 years to live.

    She puts mayonnaise on aspirin

    Her cereal bowl came with a lifeguard.

    When she goes to the zoo the elephants throw her peanuts.

    Her high school graduation picture was an aerial photograph.

    Her driver's license says "Picture continued on other side."

    All the restaurants in town have signs that say "Maximum
       Occupancy: 240 Patrons OR Your Mama".

    When she ran away, they had to use all four sides of the milk
       carton.

    When she gets in an elevator, it HAS to go down.

    She was born with a silver shovel in her mouth.

    I had to take a train and two buses just to get on her good side.

    They had to grease a door frame and hold a Twinkie on the other
       side to get her through.

    She has to iron her pants on the driveway.

    She's on BOTH sides of the family.

    She could sell shade.

    When she crosses the street, cars look out for her.

    People jog around her for exercise.

    She gets runs in her jeans.

    Her blood type is Ragu.

    When she goes to a restaurant, she doesn't get a menu, she gets
       an estimate.

    She has to put her belt on with a boomerang.

    When she turns around, people throw her a welcome back party.

    She can't even jump to a conclusion.

    She went to the movies and sat next to everyone.

    Her belly button doesn't have lint, it has sweaters.

    She was walking down the street, I swerved to miss her, and ran
       out of gas.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ OT: Lesser-known British/American Usages (Re: Reuse of old data

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<809t49$kpo$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com...
   <snip>
>
> How about you Mr. Klein - pick up any 'funnies' on your recent visit ?
>
  <snip>
> Jimmy, Calgary AB

Serious and semi-serious:

 - For those who haven't heard yet, MERANT (Micro Focus) this week has told
some of their employees (one rumor that I have heard is almost 20% of the
company) that they are "redundant".  To an American most of these people may
have been laid off, but they most certainly were (are) NOT redundant.

 - I used to be interested in Queen Victoria.  It is a well known fact that
the day she died her daughter destroyed all of the diaries that she had kept.
What I still don't know is whether this is the current British use of "diary"
(American "appointment book") or American use of "diary"  (a journal of the
private thoughts).


Humo<u>rous

After moving to the UK to work for Micro Focus, I was being sent back to the
US for a business trip.  The travel person announced that they had bought me
a RETURN ticket.  This worried me (as an American, this is the 2nd half of a
round-trip ticket) while they only bought me a round-trip ticket.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Lesser-known British/American Usages (Re: Reuse of old data

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38288665.B12F34CD@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com> <809t49$kpo$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:

> Humo<u>rous
> 
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> round-trip ticket) while they only bought me a round-trip ticket.

I tend to ask for a one-way ticket.  When they ask "to where?", I say
"to here, of course".

As a programmer, I tend to want to put my quotes incorrectly for
English.  

The following sentence should abend.
Jack said "Where is the tree"?
I prefer
Jack said "Where is the tree?".
which is entirely incorrect - but works with my compiler!!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Lesser-known British/American Usages (Re: Reuse of old data

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sj1$eKAQmVK4EweM@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com> <809t49$kpo$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <38288665.B12F34CD@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
How come your prefered construct is incorrect? According to my
understanding of the English language the question is within the quoted
phrase. That is, to put the ? outside of the phrase turns the whole
sentence into a question and begs a reply. As the sentence is a report
the ? should be within the quotes. Just in case I'm entirely wrong, I
didn't get taught grammer at school (my excuse).

In article <38288665.B12F34CD@NOSPAMhome.com>, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> writes
>
>As a programmer, I tend to want to put my quotes incorrectly for
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>which is entirely incorrect - but works with my compiler!!!
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Lesser-known British/American Usages (Re: Reuse of old data

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38289416.1F446391@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com> <809t49$kpo$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <38288AE9.51DDFC9@home.com>`

```
James J. Gavan wrote:
> 
> "William M. Klein" wrote:
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> preference any day !

She was born in May 24, 1819, so she's much older than the gorgeous
Sophia.  But if you are going to be interested in long dead women, I
suppose you can pick which age to be interested in.  After all, she was
Britain's (or Englands?) longest reigning monarch.  The current queen
mother at age 99 is the oldest British royalty ever and her daughter the
queen is more than a decade older than 62.

After all, Cleopatra was 12 years old (right before her 13th birthday). 
And Bill Klein was named after Sarah Bernhart.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Lesser-known British/American Usages (Re: Reuse of old data

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3828A037.6D0F4C99@home.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
> 
> James J. Gavan wrote:
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> And Bill Klein was named after Sarah Bernhart.

What the hell has Sarah Bernhart got to do with one WKM ? I know she was
an actress - but Bill ? You're not seriously suggesting I give Lilibet
the edge over Sophia are you, even though the former is closer to me in
age. I'll leave that to Philip.

As regards Cleopatra, as the English saying goes, "I wouldn't crawl over
her to get at Mark Anthony in the bed !".

Seeing that Bill is a Queen Vickie fan - I highly recommend renting a
video, if you haven't done so already -  "Mrs. Brown" - terrific
performance by (Dame) Judi Dench.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
James J. Gavan <jjgavan@home.com> wrote in message
news:38288AE9.51DDFC9@home.com...
>
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> preference any day !

When I was stationed in Germany in 71-72, I heard a story about an American
GI taking a British girl on a date.  Both of them enjoyed the outing, but
the guy was surprised at her casual invitation to "knock me up next
weekend".  Apparently the British usage is "knock on my door next weekend",
while the GI's definition was, er, much more "up close and personal".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Lesser-known British/American Usages (Re: Reuse of old data

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382A36A3.3CBC06EA@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com> <809t49$kpo$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <38288AE9.51DDFC9@home.com> <iwhW3.63794$23.2515542@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <382A2FBE.AE60F819@att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
> Don't get me started! How about:
> 
> Lend us a fag.
> 
> Take a rubber to it.

Well you started it. Some 25+ years ago Dutch friends here were back
East and the 'she' of the pair worked in a drugstore. In walks a gent 
who asked her for a rubber. She couldn't understand the 'singular'
quantity but went looking and found them. 

Then she came back and said "How many ?", to which consumer replied
grumpily, "Three". Oh well she thought, unusual request, but went and
brought him back three elastic rubber bands.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT: Lesser-known British/American Usages (Re: Reuse of old data

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<wy8tOA6UKsDD+M9SFOTtZasQ2H5n@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com> <809t49$kpo$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <38288AE9.51DDFC9@home.com> <iwhW3.63794$23.2515542@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <382A2FBE.AE60F819@att.net>`

```
The venerable William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net> bestowed upon
comp.lang.cobol on Wed, 10 Nov 1999 21:53:50 -0500 these words:

>Don't get me started! How about:
>
>Lend us a fag.

The story is told that Elvis Presley drew some erroneous conclusions
about The Beatles when he heard John Lennon say, "Let's get some fags
sent up to the room."

>Take a rubber to it.

In 1978, I was at one job in Salt Lake City with several programmers
just brought in from the UK.  I had a stick eraser with a paper skin
that you peel off half an inch at a time, like grease pencils are
often packaged.  An English lady saw me using it, and thought I was
crossing something out with a gease pencil but no marks were appearing
on the paper.  Then she figured out what it was and burst out with,
"Oh, it's a rubber."

On the same job an English fellow took his car back where he had
bought it and said, "My boot leaks."  Another Brit was looking to get
the dents taken out of his wing.   

BTW, Brits, if a Yank says you're "quite a guy", that's a compliment.
```

```
Randall Bart wrote:
>

> 
In UK faggot has three meanings, slang for ...., bundle of sticks for
burning (olde English) and a highly-spiced meat pattie, (not unlike
haggis) and something I miss here. Used in your context, 'fags' means
'smokes' (cigarettes).

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:

> In the UK a rubber is also a sheath. More commonly refered to as a
> Durex. Not to be confused with the Aussie Durex which (I believe) is a
> role of sticky tape. 

And which caused great confusion to an Australian exchange student who once worked for 
me in London.  He was most upset that the young lady at the Woolworths stationery 
counter refused to sell him Durex and threatened to call her manager when he insisted.

Almost as confused as the Englishman who asked for Durex in a drug store in Melbourne. 
He was asked "what colour - we've got clear, red, blue, green and since it's Christmas 
we've also got it with holly and robins on it".
```

```
My favorite was the (true story) of the male night clerk who was asked by an
american woman
if she could have an early morning wake-up call.

Well you Brits who've been in the U.S. awhile are probably grinning already.

The reason she got upset is he said 'yes ma'am, I'll come round in the
morning and knock you up.'
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reuse of old data

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<VjsxOAA0dVK4Ew5d@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com>`

```
For the record, TOOL is widely used in the UK in the same context as the
P word used below. MUPPET is local slang to north Hertfordshire for
someone who is a harmless idiot. Local to Nottingham is the phrase
'flying three sheets in the wind' for someone who is deemed mad. We also
use the phrase 'a sandwich short of a picnic' to mean mad. 
 
In article <38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com>, "James J. Gavan"
<jjgavan@home.com> writes
>
>
…[28 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reuse of old data

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3829B43E.437A8290@home.com>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com> <VjsxOAA0dVK4Ew5d@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:
> 
> For the record, TOOL is widely used in the UK in the same context as the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> use the phrase 'a sandwich short of a picnic' to mean mad.

Alistair,

Thanks for clarification on 'Muppet'. And I roared at 'a sandwich short
of a picnic' - I'd never heard it before. (I wonder what Lord Sandwich
would think ?) Seems I remember your 'flying three sheets in the wind'
as something more like 'being pissed three sheets to the wind', or if
you prefer, 'pissed as a newt'.

And regarding 'tool' you forget to mention 'Hampton' or 'John Thomas' -
but slightly different context ?

Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reuse of old data

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Ut5RdFAgdbK4EwZm@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl> <f%eV3.34371$oa2.98391@iad-read.news.verio.net> <1qspMAA79sJ4Ew1c@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <809dqa$dhe$1@ssauraac-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <38286123.BA0D4F41@home.com> <VjsxOAA0dVK4Ew5d@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3829B43E.437A8290@home.com>`

```
In article <3829B43E.437A8290@home.com>, "James J. Gavan"
<jjgavan@home.com> writes
>
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>would think ?)

History lesson:

I was schooled at Hinchingbrooke Grammar School, Huntingdon, England.
When King Charles II came over from Holland (?) to claim his throne he
was escorted by Lord Montague, Earl of Sandwich (the man who invented
the sandwich), and gifted him the house in which I was schooled.
Incidentally, it was the same house that Oliver Cromwell (cheers!)
nearly met his death at the hands of Thomas (the man who was responsible
for the dissolution of the monasteries under Henry VIII) Cromwell's pet
monkey.  

> Seems I remember your 'flying three sheets in the wind'
>as something more like 'being pissed three sheets to the wind', or if
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>but slightly different context ?

I know about John Thomas. I believe Hampton refers to the Wick and not
the Court - cockney rhymeing slang.

>
>Jimmy
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Reuse of old data

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3829C6F2.F15BC18E@home.com>`

```


Alistair Maclean wrote:
> 
> In article <3829B43E.437A8290@home.com>, "James J. Gavan"
…[36 more quoted lines elided]…
> the Court - cockney rhymeing slang.

Remembered after writing, it should have been the Earl of Sandwich.

And for the info of our 'American' readers - Cockney slang :-

 "Hampton"....... Hampton Wick........ as in p....

 (Hampton Wick is roughly half-way between Richmond(Palace) and
Hampton   Court (Palace) on the Thames. Richmond Palace in Elizabeth I's
time 
  was first to have a 'water closet' in England and was where she
died    as her trusted advisors rushed from her deathbed to Scotland
to       acknowledge James VI (her nephew via her father's sister, Mary
Rose   and her daughter Mary Queen of Scots) as James I of England -
start 
  of the Stuarts, James being grandfather of Charles II).

 "let's have a butchers".... butcher's hook ... "let's have a look"
 "apples and pears"....... stairs
 "skin and blister"......... sister
  etc....

Not strictly one of the above, "In the Clink", there having been a Jail
in Clink Street, central (old) London.

Jimmy
```

```
James J. Gavan wrote:

> And regarding 'tool' you forget to mention 'Hampton' or 'John Thomas' -
> but slightly different context ?

Robert Heinlein often tried to get terms past censors.  In the novel THE
STAR BEAST, the main character was named the same as his father and
grandfather - I believe it was John Thomas, but certainly a name with
phallic connotations.  The eponymous character said his hobby was
raising John Thomases.
```

```
Bob Wolfe wrote:
> 
> "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…

Well ! Having made the effort to 'talk' but not 'sound' N.American I was
surprised to find spanner was kosher. But as I live only five minutes
from our local firehall, I went walking and rang their bell. Shoulda
seen the look of disappointment when I said I only wanted info, no there
was no fire.

Anyway the nice gentleman confirmed, yep they have spanners and then
took me to a fire wagon to show me one. It sure as hell didn't look like
any spanner or wrench I have seen; it is a specialized tool for
de-coupling hoses, (and I wont get into 'de-coupling' again <G>), very
different in shape.

You are lucky QuizMeister Randall Bart didn't latch on to this one.

What do you call that 'misinformation' or 'disinformation' - stick to 
SP2 - Naughty Bobby ! 

Jimmy
```

#### ↳ Re: Reuse of old data

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3825A5FF.12E2@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<3824FC60.EA1D51F4@greennet.gl>`

```
J-O Nyboe Nielsen wrote:
> 
> Greatings from Greenland.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> Is there any tools out there that can help us??

If you still have the COBOL source, you could write a "throw away" to export to a file.

Otherwise you could use a generic parser like ParseRat 
(http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm).  Tools like this will read pretty much 
anything and export as fixed, delimited dBase or MailMerge format.

You can either parse from the raw files, or if you have the old executables you can 
"print" reports to a file and then use the tool to parse the print file (e.g. a popular 
way to export data about books from a library system is to "print" the catalogue to a 
file and then parse the page images to extract the data).
```

```
So Norton Utillities was the tool to do the job. That tool needed a
competent person, which by the way also is a programmer.

Thanks

Keep up the good work and .. :)


Dino
```

## Thread: Determine Day-of-Week for Y2K — 15 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Date and calendar processing`](topics.md#dates)_

### Determine Day-of-Week for Y2K

- **From:** "Tom Choma" <sfpc@palmnet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<01bf285a$be67bae0$a463a6d0@sfpc.palmnet.net>`

```
I have to update an old program to determine the day of the week. Currently
it is hard-coded each year. I know there is a forumla to do this, but do
not know it. Does anyone know the day of the week formula! Thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: Determine Day-of-Week for Y2K

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<801beo$6qo$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<01bf285a$be67bae0$a463a6d0@sfpc.palmnet.net>`

```
If you are looking for TODAY's day-of-week, just use the ACCEPT from
DAY-OF-WEEK syntax.  If you are trying to figure out the day of the week of
an "arbitrary" date *AND* if you are using a compiler that has intrinsic
functions then:

1) Get the date into YYYYMMDD format (or YYYYDDD format)

2) Convert the date to an integer via FUNCTION INTEGER-of-DATE (or
INTEGER-of-DAY)

3) Divide by 7

4) The remainder will tell you the day of the week.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Determine Day-of-Week for Y2K

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TZWU3.11930$B15.133750@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<01bf285a$be67bae0$a463a6d0@sfpc.palmnet.net> <801beo$6qo$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Bill is correct.  But if you don't have intrinsic functions, here is
Zeller's Congruence, the method most commonly used to calculate day
of week from a date.  You can download DATES.ZIP from my website below.
It has a number of COBOL date routines, including this one.

                          Zeller's Congruence:

Zeller's Congruence is used to determine the day of the week of any
date in the Gregorian calendar, which was instituted October 15, 1582
(and various later times for various places).  The formula is:

w = (d + (m*2) + int((m+1)*3/5) + y + int(y/4)
        - int(y/100) + int(y/400) + 1) mod 7

Where w   = weekday (0=Sun,1=Mon,2=Tue,3=Wed,4=Thu,5=Fri,6=Sat)
      d   = day of the month
      m   = month of year  (Jan & Feb = 13 & 14 of prev year)
      y   = year (year - 1 if month is Jan or Feb)
      int = integer part (eg: int(3.5) = 3)
      mod = modulus or remainder part (eg: (10 mod 7) = 3)

Example Feb 29, 2000   d = 29, m = 14, y = 1999 (w = 2 Tue)
        Jul 4, 1776    d =  4, m =  7, y = 1776 (w = 4 Thu)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Determine Day-of-Week for Y2K

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** John Grznar <grznar@exchange.uta.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3839901C.43DE9488@exchange.uta.edu>`
- **References:** `<01bf285a$be67bae0$a463a6d0@sfpc.palmnet.net> <819bn2$gtk$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <38383719.6A751380@home.com> <81c0j9$m5a$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`

```
I'm not sure day-of-week is a standard intrinsic function.  If not, try
something like:

MOVE 19991122 TO TODAYS-DATE
EVALUATE FUNCTION REM (FUNCTION INTEGER-OF-DATE (TODAYS-DATE),7)
  WHEN 0
     DISPLAY "Sunday"
  WHEN 1
     DISPLAY "Monday"
  WHEN 2
     DISPLAY "Tuesday"
  WHEN 3
     DISPLAY "Wednesday"
  WHEN 4
     DISPLAY "Thursday"
  WHEN 5
     DISPLAY "Friday"
  WHEN 6
     DISPLAY "Saturday"
END-EVALUATE.

John Grznar
```

```
                            ,7) * 9 + 1):9).
```

```
work.  What I actually use is a table of the weekday names, and use
(INTEGER-OF-DATE + 1) as a subscript.  I dislike using EVALUATE when a

just as clear to read.  To be honest, I was just kidding John a bit. ;-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Determine Day-of-Week for Y2K

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** John Grznar <grznar@exchange.uta.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3839C896.EAB95B58@exchange.uta.edu>`
- **References:** `<01bf285a$be67bae0$a463a6d0@sfpc.palmnet.net> <819bn2$gtk$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <38383719.6A751380@home.com> <81c0j9$m5a$1@uranium.btinternet.com> <3839901C.43DE9488@exchange.uta.edu> <GLg_3.902$t57.7699@news2.mia>`

```
Well, if you want to get fancy, smancy, ok.  But yours displays a lot of
extra spaces at the end of the day, which can be hard on the eyes to see.
So, to conserve space, how about:

01  DAY-LENGTHS       VALUE "6679868".
      05  DAY-LENGTH OCCURS 7 TIMES PIC 9.
77  DAY-CODE  PIC 9.

 COMPUTE DAY-CODE = FUNCTION MOD(FUNCTION
                    INTEGER-OF-DATE(MY-DATE),7) + 1

 DISPLAY MY-DAYS(DAY-CODE : DAY-LENGTH(DAY-CODE)).


John Grznar



Judson McClendon wrote:

> John Grznar wrote:
> >I'm not sure day-of-week is a standard intrinsic function.  If not, try
…[34 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."
```

```
>Help!!!  I had a disk crash on the drive (D:) that COBOL was installed on.

…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You may have to manually edit the registry to delete all registry keys that 
contain references to Fujitsu.  Since the drive/directories have been wiped,
the Add/Remove programs option from the Control Panel may not be able
to successfully perform its tasks.
```

```
I don't know about reinstallation, but when I moved from v 3.0 to v 4.0, I
had to deinstall all of version 3 before beginning.  I had to do this
piece meal from "My Computer/Control Panel/Add-Remove Programs" by
choosing each Fujitsu COBOL item individually and delete it.  After
removing all the various parts of the Fujitsu package, I was able to
install v. 4.0.

Hope this helps.

Stan Barndt (stan@nextaxs.com) wrote:
: Help!!!  I had a disk crash on the drive (D:) that COBOL was installed on.
: I replaced the drive and went to
: reinstall COBOL.  I keep getting the message "COBOL97 is already installed
: in C:\WINDOWS".  Why is this
: happening?  How do I get past this message????

: Any help would be greatly appreciated.

: Stan Barndt
: HSB Consulting
```

## Thread: Fujitsu & Install Shield — 9 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Fujitsu & Install Shield

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Sw6V3.238$zd3.23467@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
We're have a bit of trouble using Install Shield
with a FJ application. There are a handful of
FJ modules (about 10) that refuse to register.

If you're using the commercial version of Install
Shield (not the installation wrapper provided by
Fujitsu), we'd be very interested in your comments.

Thanks.
```

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38250518.162727448@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<Sw6V3.238$zd3.23467@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
On Sat, 6 Nov 1999 21:40:32 -0600, "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
wrote:

>We're have a bit of trouble using Install Shield
>with a FJ application. There are a handful of
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>

can't get it to work.  Is this the error you are seeing?

---
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu & Install Shield

- **From:** Michael Smart <nightshade-nospam@ev1.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ES4nOPfonpBII13XXzQzjHAgzqd0@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<Sw6V3.238$zd3.23467@typhoon01.swbell.net> <38250518.162727448@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
On Sun, 07 Nov 1999 04:51:56 GMT, thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane
Hubbell) wrote:

>If I don't install using the Wrapper I get "COBOL97 Not properly
>Installed".  I'd love to use something OTHER than the wrapper, but I
>can't get it to work.  Is this the error you are seeing?

That is the same error message that we are getting. The problem,
evidently, stems from the fact that FJ wants some specific entries in
the registry. If you look on your development machine under the key:

HKEY_LOCAL_MACHINE\Software\Fujitsu

you will see a plethora of listings that the FJ wrapper will install
on client machines. The trick, so far, seems to be to figure out how
to get those keys with the proper values in them without using the
supplied wrapper!

If you have any luck, let us know!

Michael
```

#### ↳ Re: Fujitsu & Install Shield

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<803t3u$nbb$1@neptunium.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<Sw6V3.238$zd3.23467@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```

Jerry P wrote in message ...
>We're have a bit of trouble using Install Shield
>with a FJ application. There are a handful of
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Some modules will very rarely self register, firstly
make sure that no-other programs are running when you run
install shield.
If this still doesn't work you can do it manually.
I don't know if you have tried this but it should work, works every time for
me.

launch a DOS session inside windows.
change the directory to \windows\system

type: regsvr32 {moduleName} -c
without the brackets, it will reg all servers.


Simon R Hart
Eaton, Ottery St. Mary, UK.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu & Install Shield

- **From:** Michael Smart <nightshade-nospam@ev1.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uy8nOBpJJ0yKh5DDHUkVeaGw7GYG@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<Sw6V3.238$zd3.23467@typhoon01.swbell.net> <803t3u$nbb$1@neptunium.btinternet.com>`

```
On Sun, 7 Nov 1999 12:58:23 -0800, "Simon R Hart"
<hart1@technologist.com> wrote:

>Some modules will very rarely self register, firstly
>make sure that no-other programs are running when you run
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>Eaton, Ottery St. Mary, UK.

In this case these particular files do not self-register. However, it
has become apparent that they are dependent upon other files. If you
are really interested in looking at the message these files generate,
try running RegSvr32 on the following file:

f3bibtrv.dll

File should be located in: C:\FSC\COBOL97 directory.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Fujitsu & Install Shield

- **From:** "Tom" <someone@microsoft.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZENV3.53054$YB4.1390142@typ12.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<Sw6V3.238$zd3.23467@typhoon01.swbell.net> <803t3u$nbb$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <uy8nOBpJJ0yKh5DDHUkVeaGw7GYG@4ax.com>`

```
I've done install shield for a bit and if you know where you want to put the
values in the registry its a simple call in install shield.  Variables in
the installation are no real chore either.
However my personal opinion with install shield is to go through the
training first, it is not a tool, it is its own language (albeit very
similar to c) The idiosyncracies can drive you nuts. The training will save
more than its cost in wasted time.  OrangeBrain does some excellent training
from what I've been told.  I firmly believe that many screwed up PC's are
the result of improperly designed installations, and no installation
testing.

Tom

Michael Smart wrote in message ...
>On Sun, 7 Nov 1999 12:58:23 -0800, "Simon R Hart"

for
>>me.
>>

```

<a id="t-648e36bf73"></a>
## Thread: Cobol field types — 9 msgs

### Cobol field types

- **From:** "Tony Tooley" <tony@infosearch.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nBLU3.1942$OQ.242293@news.flash.net>`

```
I have a btrieve database populated by a Dos 3rd party program written in
Cobol.
I am told it is MS Cobol.

Q1  Is there an MS Cobol for Dos or could it be MF Cobol?  Where can I get
my hands on a copy?

My problem is there are no file layouts available.  I have successfully
dumped the btrieve files and determined where most of the ascii fields are
delimited.  It appears there are several binary fields.  Some are 4bytes,
some are 6 (but could be 4 + 2 byte integer!@#)

Q2  Where can I get a list of the standard Cobol types and their definitions
(e.g. IEEE 4 byte float, 8 byte float, signed 2 byte binary etc etc)

Any other suggestions would be more than welcome.  The author of the system
is unreachable and there is no documentation.  The database HAS to be
converted because the client has already changed their software.

tia
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol field types

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<800pmj$gnm$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<nBLU3.1942$OQ.242293@news.flash.net>`

```
Hi,
MS or MF? it could be both. For some time MS-Cobol was produced by MF. Never
mind, your problem is obviously connected to btrieve and good old EXTFH from
Merant Micro Focus can handle that. The filehandler can be called from a C
program if that is easier for you.

Look at www.merant.com for nearest office or distributor and discuss the
problem with them. Bear in mind, that many of the products comes out with a
30 days evaluation period.

Good hunting
Ib

>Cobol.

definitions
>(e.g. IEEE 4 byte float, 8 byte float, signed 2 byte binary etc etc)
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
tia
```

```
Q1: No
Q2: No
Go to the Merant office or Distributor and ask for an evaluation copy.
Go to http://pita.microfocus.com/prodsupp/adv/netx/nesamp.htm#mixedlanguage
and download cextfhcn.zip.

Try to access your btrieve files and if you have luck, make a deal with the
distributor about license fee for use of EXTFH.

 That's the way - I think.

Regards
Ib
```

#### ↳ Re: Cobol field types

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JXrV3.146$Av4.5862@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<nBLU3.1942$OQ.242293@news.flash.net>`

```
>Q2  Where can I get a list of the standard Cobol types and their
definitions
>(e.g. IEEE 4 byte float, 8 byte float, signed 2 byte binary etc etc)


I hate to say it. And my hands shake as I type it, but there is a really
good chance to get the info you are looking for by.........

disassembling the offending progams into symbolic assembler source code and
interpret the definitions from the disassembled code.

This is of course if all else fails.

Then again there may possibly be a 1 in a million chance that there is a
machine language to COBOL disassembler out there.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Cobol field types

- **From:** stephen_j_spiro@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<807324$a6l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<nBLU3.1942$OQ.242293@news.flash.net> <JXrV3.146$Av4.5862@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`

```
Mmmmm, try the manual. Most manuals identify which data types are
standard, and which are Extensions. Note that the internal
representation of COMPUTATIONAL and PACKED-DECIMAL are implementor-
defined.
There are a WHOLE BUNCH of new data-types in the new COBOL (NOT Cobol!)
standard, which will be out soon (we hope!).  Many of the new data-
types are already extensions in various complilers, of course.

Stepjen J Spiro
President, Wizard Systems
Member, J4 COBOL Committee

  "Gumbo" <a@a.com> wrote:

> definitions
> >(e.g. IEEE 4 byte float, 8 byte float, signed 2 byte binary etc etc)
>
> I hate to say it. And my hands shake as I type it, but there is a
really
> good chance to get the info you are looking for by.........
>
> disassembling the offending progams into symbolic assembler source
code and
> interpret the definitions from the disassembled code.
>
> This is of course if all else fails.
>
> Then again there may possibly be a 1 in a million chance that there
is a
> machine language to COBOL disassembler out there.
>
>

Before you buy.
```

```
This is a serious response, and I'm posting it so everyone can read.
My dad suffered from vertigo for several years, and the doctors could
not find a reason.  Finally, one doctor asked "Do you consume peanuts
or peanut oil?"  My dad stopped eating peanuts, and my mom stopped
using peanut oil for cooking, and the vertigo quickly went away.
```

<a id="t-1e22b6f031"></a>
## Thread: strings in cobol — 5 msgs

### strings in cobol

- **From:** "Maarten Van der Biest" <van.der.biest@pandora.be>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<gZRU3.669$l%1.22914@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
how can I make a string from a name that was given in the program. What I
want to do is ask someone's last name, and than sort it. But for a good
sort, i need to convert the name to a new name where all the capitals are
replaced in lower cases and where special signs like space or '... are
excluded. So I have to deal with every letter seperately. I there a way to
deal with the name as a string? Or does anyone already have code for this
conversion?

Kind regards,

Maarten Van der Biest
e-mail: van.der.biest@pandora.be
ICQ: 38851611
```

#### ↳ Re: strings in cobol

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tbZU3.1530$Ss1.12364@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<gZRU3.669$l%1.22914@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
In addition to the suggestions you already have, take a look at INSPECT
REPLACING and INSPECT CONVERTING.

They may not get the job done in this particular case, but it's always nice
to know what tools might be available next time.
```

#### ↳ Re: strings in cobol

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<801278$t13$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<gZRU3.669$l%1.22914@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
Hi,
It looks to me as a study in perform varying. Redefine the name as a table
and define an output table. Go through inputtable and move input element to
output element if the value suits you. Remember to have a separate index to
the output table (Start with 1 and increase only when you move to output).
You should do an inspect replace before checking the value - it simplifies
the logic.

Don't worry - be happy
Ib

Maarten Van der Biest skrev i meddelelsen ...
>how can I make a string from a name that was given in the program. What I
>want to do is ask someone's last name, and than sort it. But for a good
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: strings in cobol

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7AWU3.11884$B15.133004@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<gZRU3.669$l%1.22914@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
Maarten Van der Biest wrote:
>how can I make a string from a name that was given in the program. What I
>want to do is ask someone's last name, and than sort it. But for a good
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>conversion?

If you want to replace non alphabetic characters with spaces:

  01  TXT     PIC  X(30).
  01  X       PIC  9(02) COMP.

  PERFORM VARYING X FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X > FUNCTION LENGTH(TXT)
      IF TXT(X:1) ALPHABETIC
          MOVE FUNCTION LOWER-CASE(TXT(X:1)) TO TXT(X:1)
      ELSE
          MOVE SPACE TO TXT(X:1)
      END-IF
  END-PERFORM

If you want to shift following text to the left when non alphabetic
characters are found:

  01  TXT     PIC  X(30).
  01  X1      PIC  9(02) COMP.
  01  X2      PIC  9(02) COMP.

  MOVE 1 TO X2
  PERFORM VARYING X1 FROM 1 BY 1 UNTIL X1 > FUNCTION LENGTH(TXT)
      IF TXT(X1:1) ALPHABETIC
          MOVE FUNCTION LOWER-CASE(TXT(X1:1)) TO TXT(X2:1)
          ADD 1 TO X2
      END-IF
      IF X1 > X2
          MOVE SPACE TO TXT(X1:1)
      END-IF
  END-PERFORM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: strings in cobol

- **From:** gstueb@okay.net (Gregor Stuebner)
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-Wn7LT4OfJUCT@localhost>`
- **References:** `<gZRU3.669$l%1.22914@afrodite.telenet-ops.be> <7AWU3.11884$B15.133004@news3.mia>`

```
If you only need to convert a string from lower case to upper case 
this also should work:

       78  small        value "abcdefghijklmnopqrstuvwxyz".
       78  big          value "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ".

           inspect myfield replacing all small by big.

	or sometimes:

           transform myfield from small to big.

Gregor
```

## Thread: packed decimal question — 13 this month / 26 total

_Thread started [1999-10](1999-10.md#t-afa0ce2e97)._

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Unbeliever" <popsider@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<802sf6$kna$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com> <37F4F581.F6EA7070@NOSPAMhome.com> <967J3.2981$Pt.23590@news4.mia> <OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper>`

```
On the subject of signs - came across an interesting phenomena last week.
Not sure if it's just Cob-370 -
Moving record field by field my program converted assumed positive sign of F
to C in my comp-3 fields. Just a plain move. I didn't ask it to change the
field,
just move it. Cob-370 just wants to show some initiative I guess!

Gumbo <a@a.com> wrote in message news:OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper...
> >Howard, the IBM mainframe is not my forte', but is the sign not in
> >the rightmost nibble?  Like this: 99 99 99 9s where 's' = hex 'F'
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Correct, with a further clarification. The SIGN IS LEADING clause moves
the
> sign to the leading nibble:
> sign is leading s9 99 99 99
> sign is trailing 99 99 99 9s
>
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<d7gb2sgqn94f7m0lcshqobi7l6p2hnn50b@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com> <37F4F581.F6EA7070@NOSPAMhome.com> <967J3.2981$Pt.23590@news4.mia> <OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper> <802sf6$kna$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Sat, 6 Nov 1999 20:26:39 -0700 "Unbeliever" <popsider@ix.netcom.com> wrote:

:>On the subject of signs - came across an interesting phenomena last week.
:>Not sure if it's just Cob-370 -
:>Moving record field by field my program converted assumed positive sign of F
:>to C in my comp-3 fields. Just a plain move. I didn't ask it to change the
:>field,
:>just move it. Cob-370 just wants to show some initiative I guess!

If it was a move from a COMP-3 to a COMP-3, the odds are strong that a ZAP
instruction was generated.

ZAP, like most decimal instructions will force a "preferred" sign, either C(+)
or D(-).

If you wish to retain the sign, either redefine the field as X(n) or move the
group level.

:>Gumbo <a@a.com> wrote in message news:OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper...
:>> >Howard, the IBM mainframe is not my forte', but is the sign not in
:>> >the rightmost nibble?  Like this: 99 99 99 9s where 's' = hex 'F'
:>> >because the field is 'unsigned' (otherwise 'C' = + and 'D' = -).

:>> Correct, with a further clarification. The SIGN IS LEADING clause moves
:>the
:>> sign to the leading nibble:
:>> sign is leading s9 99 99 99
:>> sign is trailing 99 99 99 9s
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Unbeliever" <popsider@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<804ros$kmc$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com> <37F4F581.F6EA7070@NOSPAMhome.com> <967J3.2981$Pt.23590@news4.mia> <OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper> <802sf6$kna$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <d7gb2sgqn94f7m0lcshqobi7l6p2hnn50b@4ax.com>`

```
Good thought - could have been ZAPped.
I have a record layout that is INITIALIZEd in program 1, and then passes
though various programs.
The F->C sign change occurs in a field which was apparently INITIALIZEd in
program 1, then has
never been filled, so it is x'000000000F' and the move makes it
x'000000000C'.

Seems like I assumed that INITIALIZE would do a better job. But ultimately,
the only problem was that
my SUPERC compare failed on the sign change. As I was curious, I used a
Fileaid repl, to change
all zero-filled fields from a sign of F to C - then the before/after files
matched QED!

Binyamin Dissen <postingid@dissensoftware.com> wrote in message
news:d7gb2sgqn94f7m0lcshqobi7l6p2hnn50b@4ax.com...
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<805114$87j$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com> <37F4F581.F6EA7070@NOSPAMhome.com> <967J3.2981$Pt.23590@news4.mia> <OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper> <802sf6$kna$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <d7gb2sgqn94f7m0lcshqobi7l6p2hnn50b@4ax.com> <804ros$kmc$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
I have *not* tried this - but I would EXPECT that

INITIALIZE with a field defined as PIC 9   (Usage Display or Packed-Decimal)
   would move a X'F' to the receiving (initialized) field's sign-nibble

INITIALIZE with a field defined as PIC S9  (Usage Display or Packed-Decimal)
  would move a X'C' to the receiving (initialized) field's sign-nibble.

I would also THINK about what NUMPROC compiler option I was using - but I
*think* that for a receiving field, any setting SHOULD set the sign-nibble to
what you tell it to.

Moving from a PIC 9 to a PIC S9 field almost certainly will "zap" the
sign-nibble (I do not THINK that the reverse move would "remove" the sign,
but I won't swear to that).

If your INITIALIZE is really of a field defined with a sign and the
sign-nibble is X'F' and not X'C' - then I think you have found a "bug" in the
compiler.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382610B5.F0FF8FC8@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com> <37F4F581.F6EA7070@NOSPAMhome.com> <967J3.2981$Pt.23590@news4.mia> <OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper> <802sf6$kna$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <d7gb2sgqn94f7m0lcshqobi7l6p2hnn50b@4ax.com> <804ros$kmc$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net> <805114$87j$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
A move from S9(n) to 9(n) for either packed-decimal or display usages in
the receiving field will guarentee an 'F' zone in the receiving field. 
I have used this since COBOL D for DOS release 26 on a 360/30.  A move
from 9(n) to S9(n) does cause code to be generated that forces a C zone
(either Zero add packed or a combination of OR immediate followed by AND
immediate).

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca  

"William M. Klein" wrote:
> 
> 
…[74 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6CqV3.16191$aS6.186111@news4.mia>`
