```
SIGN is LEADING does NOT apply to comp-3 / packed-decimal.  That makes it
comp-5.

Unbeliever wrote in message <802sf6$kna$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>...
>On the subject of signs - came across an interesting phenomena last week.
>Not sure if it's just Cob-370 -
>Moving record field by field my program converted assumed positive sign of
F
>to C in my comp-3 fields. Just a plain move. I didn't ask it to change the
>field,
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<805g5q$8mh$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com> <37F4F581.F6EA7070@NOSPAMhome.com> <967J3.2981$Pt.23590@news4.mia> <OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper> <802sf6$kna$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <6CqV3.16191$aS6.186111@news4.mia>`

```
Check the dialect being talked about, in this case it was "COB-370" which was
an IBM mainframe dialect - which doesn't even KNOW about COMP-5.

FYI,
  I actually know of NO dialect in which "sign is leading" changes a COMP-3
to COMP-5.  Certainly EVERY PC compiler that I know of *does* support "sign
is leading" for COMP-3 items (which are treated as equivalent to
PACKED-DECIMAL).

Can you tell us what dialect DOES use "sign is leading" to change COMP-3 to
COMP-5? You aren't (by any chance) confusing SIGN IS LEADING with big-endian
versus little-endian?  If that is the case it changes how usage BINARY (or
sometimes COMP) is stored, but not where the sign-nibble is for DISPLAY or
PACKED-DECIMAL items.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<uGsV3.168$Av4.6861@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com> <37F4F581.F6EA7070@NOSPAMhome.com> <967J3.2981$Pt.23590@news4.mia> <OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper> <802sf6$kna$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <6CqV3.16191$aS6.186111@news4.mia>`

```
>SIGN is LEADING does NOT apply to comp-3 / packed-decimal.  That makes it
>comp-5.
>


Regardless of what name you call it. The syntax of COMP-3 allows for the use
of the SIGN IS LEADING. SIGN IS LEADING places the sign in the high order
nibble of the leftmost byte of a field.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3826926e_2@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com> <37F4F581.F6EA7070@NOSPAMhome.com> <967J3.2981$Pt.23590@news4.mia> <OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper> <802sf6$kna$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <6CqV3.16191$aS6.186111@news4.mia> <uGsV3.168$Av4.6861@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`

```
Gumbo <a@a.com> wrote in message
news:uGsV3.168$Av4.6861@typhoon.columbus.rr.com...
> >SIGN is LEADING does NOT apply to comp-3 / packed-decimal.  That makes it
> >comp-5.
>
> Regardless of what name you call it. The syntax of COMP-3 allows for the
use
> of the SIGN IS LEADING. SIGN IS LEADING places the sign in the high order
> nibble of the leftmost byte of a field.

I don't think so, and it would in any case be hardware dependent.
SIGN IS LEADING may be allowed syntactically with some compilers
but strictly speaking is only allowed for USAGE DISPLAY.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<806703$i9q$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com> <37F4F581.F6EA7070@NOSPAMhome.com> <967J3.2981$Pt.23590@news4.mia> <OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper> <802sf6$kna$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net> <6CqV3.16191$aS6.186111@news4.mia> <uGsV3.168$Av4.6861@typhoon.columbus.rr.com> <3826926e_2@news3.prserv.net>`

```
I stand corrected on my earlier post.  The Standard *does* require that the
item be explicitly (or implicitly) defined as USAGE DISPLAY.

The IBM LRM is a little fuzzy on whether they have a documented extension
allowing it for other USAGES and I would have sworn that Micro Focus did (but
can't find any documentation supporting this).

Sorry for the "mis-information" in my previous note(s).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<804579$436$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com> <37F4F581.F6EA7070@NOSPAMhome.com> <967J3.2981$Pt.23590@news4.mia> <OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper> <802sf6$kna$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Sign handling with ALL (old and new) IBM mainframe COBOLs is "an art without
an artist" <G>

If you have a field defined as signed (Packed or display) but that has a
sign-nibble of X"F" or an unsigned field with a sign-nibble of X"C".  What
happens is a  truly "remarkable" study in undocumented rules.  When you start
getting into things like X'40' in a field that is SUPPOSED to be numeric, the
variations grow dramatically.

Bottom-line,
   If you have data in a numeric (or numeric-edited) field that doesn't match
its picture clause, do NOT rely on it doing any of this - but don't be
surprised at anything that it does (and don't assume that what it does today
will happen tomorrow  - much less if you ever change compiler options or
releases).
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<nzsV3.164$Av4.6536@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<19990930234135.02152.00000391@ng-cm1.aol.com> <37F4F581.F6EA7070@NOSPAMhome.com> <967J3.2981$Pt.23590@news4.mia> <OhHQ3.1371$CS3.42989@viper> <802sf6$kna$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
>On the subject of signs - came across an interesting phenomena last week.
>Not sure if it's just Cob-370 -
>Moving record field by field my program converted assumed positive sign of
F
>to C in my comp-3 fields. Just a plain move. I didn't ask it to change the
>field,
>just move it. Cob-370 just wants to show some initiative I guess!


Something I ran into recently was right up the same alley.370 is not
converting an assumed sign of F to C. It is converting an unsigned numeric
field to a signed numeric field. "F" is being carried over from the old
versions of card punches. I am far too young to have an experienced
knowledge of punch cards. But it had something to do with how signed
numerics were coded on the odld punch cards. Currently COBOL allows for
these values to be input to a COBOL program, but internal processing will
convert the old, defunct, vestigal sign of "F" to the proper value of "C".

If you display signed numeric fields, then look at the hex values of such,
you will get C or D in their appropriate vaues. Unsigned numerics will
generate a proper "F" since they are meant to be compatible with the PIC X
version of the same value.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: sign is leading....

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Nilo Paim" <npaim@bigfoot.com>

```

of

the

I recall that some years ago, i had to ADD ZERO TO FIELD9 in order to get

remember details on it. But i'm sure it was a IBM4341 with VM and DOS/VSE. I
can't say what Cobol version was...

Nilo npaim@zaz.com.br

## Thread: 32-bit (NT) compiler for MF COBOL — 6 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### 32-bit (NT) compiler for MF COBOL

- **From:** "Ian Bate" <ian.bate@virgin.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<801hhu$hkt$1@nclient13-gui.server.virgin.net>`

```
Firstly, I don't program in COBOL at all.

We have a team who writes COBOL for an IBM MVS m/f using Micro Focus COBOL
(quite an old version).  They run COBOL Workbench on Windows 3.1.

There are certain pieces of code which we, (the PC Development team), get
from them and compile (using workbench) into a 16-bit DLL which we call from
Visual Basic (16-bit).

Trouble is, we are moving the Visual Basic code to 32-bit and so need to
(re)compile the DLL as 32-bit.  I don't know what we need.  The 32-bit
version of Workbench?  Net Express?  Mainframe Express?

I'd be grateful for any suggestions, Merant are being like really unhelpful
and not answering our support calls.
```

#### ↳ Re: 32-bit (NT) compiler for MF COBOL

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<801lc8$dc8$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<801hhu$hkt$1@nclient13-gui.server.virgin.net>`

```
Hi Ian,
I am a little frustrated to hear that Merant is unhelpful. They have some
expensive 1st quality products, and I beleive they should show some interest
to your problem.

Nevertheless, your choice of product should depend on the flavour of the
Cobol Code you have to maintain. Your staff is satisfied?? with WB 3.1 but
you haven't told if you have CICS Option, IMS Option or in what way you
interact with VB.

MVS Express is for maintaining mainframe programs, NetExpress is for PC
programs including Internet applications. If you can buy WB 4.0 (and update
to 4.0.38), you will have a new and better workbench, similar to the one you
have now.

Please feel free to mail me if you like my opinion. Tell me a little bit
more about your use of Cobol now and in the future, and I will help you to
find the right product from Merant.

I am not employed by Merant or any of the distributors. I have been a
support engineer at a distributor for almost 10 years, but today I have no
commercial interest in your choice.

I just like the products and hate to see customers ignored.

Cheers
Ib
Ian Bate skrev i meddelelsen
<801hhu$hkt$1@nclient13-gui.server.virgin.net>...

#### ↳ Re: 32-bit (NT) compiler for MF COBOL

- **From:** mark.warren@merant.com (Mark Warren)
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38278d3b.1812658@hyperion>`
- **References:** `<801hhu$hkt$1@nclient13-gui.server.virgin.net>`

```
On Sat, 6 Nov 1999 15:30:09 -0000, "Ian Bate" <ian.bate@virgin.net>
wrote:

>Firstly, I don't program in COBOL at all.
>
Don't worry about it, maybe one day ... ;-)

>We have a team who writes COBOL for an IBM MVS m/f using Micro Focus COBOL
>(quite an old version).  They run COBOL Workbench on Windows 3.1.
These people will want Mainframe Express to get the IMS support. It
really has moved on a long way since V3.1. In particular, there are
some very exciting IMS additional features out soon.

>Trouble is, we are moving the Visual Basic code to 32-bit and so need to
>(re)compile the DLL as 32-bit.  I don't know what we need.  The 32-bit
>version of Workbench?  Net Express?  Mainframe Express?
You'll need Net Express to do the linking of COBOL with another
language such as VB. Mainframe Express doesn't have the tools or run
time libraries to do the linking (but that's not really what it's
intended for). Once those DLLs are built, then there shouldn't be any
problem calling them from COBOL in Mainframe Express (watch out for
EBCDIC/ANSI issues though - use the "Non-mainframe debugging" option).

Don't worry too much about the need for two products. In reality not
everyone is going to need both - I guess the IMS people don't do a lot
of the VB work. But even if you did want everyone to have both, I'm
sure MERANT would be able to help out making a reasonable deal
(especially if upgrading from an older product). 

>
>I'd be grateful for any suggestions, Merant are being like really unhelpful
>and not answering our support calls.

Very disappointed to hear this. Let me know via email who you've been
talking to  and I'll see what I can do.
>

If you'd like any more information, please contact me via email and
I'll see what I can do.
Regards,
Mark Warren
MERANT International, Newbury, UK
Email: Mark.Warren@MERANT(removethis).COM
```

## Thread: Line Spacing — 7 msgs

### Line Spacing

- **From:** dc606@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<801p0e$o84$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Anybody know why I don't get line spacing with following code:


MOVE STAR-LINE TO PRINT-LINE.
WRITE PRINT-LINE.


MOVE HEADING-LINE-ONE TO PRINT-LINE.
WRITE PRINT-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 3 LINES.
MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
WRITE PRINT-LINE.

MOVE HEADING-LINE-TWO TO PRINT-LINE.
WRITE PRINT-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
WRITE PRINT-LINE.

MOVE HEADING-LINE-THREE TO PRINT-LINE.
WRITE PRINT-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
WRITE PRINT-LINE.




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Line Spacing

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<801t81$uak$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<801p0e$o84$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
How are you "viewing" your output to determine whether spacing is occurring
or not?  Often if you look at a report "online", it appears that spacing
isn't occurring - but when you actually print it out, it does.

You don't mention your compiler or environment - but some of us could help
you figure this out better if you tell us that (and show us your
Select/Assign statement) - if just printing it to hardcopy doesn't solve the
problem.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Line Spacing

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80261r$jl8$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<801p0e$o84$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <801t81$uak$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <8021pi$u1i$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Do you have LINE SEQUENTIAL specified (in your Select/Assign - or via
directive)?  Have you tried copying this file to a printer - to see if the
blank lines do appear there?

The "issue" may well be that when you "look at" the file, you aren't seeing
carriage-control/line-feed "characters" that are actually in the file.
Assuming "normal" processing of print-files in MF, you do NOT get a complete
line of "blanks" when you do a WRITE AFTER ADVANCING - rather you get one or
more CR/LF characters.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Line Spacing

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<802gm3$p6j$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<801p0e$o84$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <801t81$uak$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net> <8021pi$u1i$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <80261r$jl8$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
    It has been said before and bears repeating - do not mix write before &
write after.  Since a write with no before or after
can be either before or after, depending on where you run (before 1 on
pc/unix, who knows on mainframe), do not mix them in the same
program.  We used to be able to get odd effects by writing to the same
physical location more than once, but now that we have
to consider laser printers, that is no longer a usefull technique.

    Use whatever (before or after) fits in your head best.  Personally,
after makes my head hurt.


William M. Klein <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:80261r$jl8$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net...
> Do you have LINE SEQUENTIAL specified (in your Select/Assign - or via
> directive)?  Have you tried copying this file to a printer - to see if the
> blank lines do appear there?
>
> The "issue" may well be that when you "look at" the file, you aren't
seeing
> carriage-control/line-feed "characters" that are actually in the file.
> Assuming "normal" processing of print-files in MF, you do NOT get a
complete
> line of "blanks" when you do a WRITE AFTER ADVANCING - rather you get one
or
> more CR/LF characters.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill <dc606@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:8021pi$u1i$1@nnrp1.deja.com...

> >
…[59 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Line Spacing

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3826E08F.8091E4BE@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<801p0e$o84$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Have you printed out your output, or just looked at it on-line?

In the environments I have worked, the output uses carriage control in
column 1 to show line spacing.  Some environments this can be seen by
shifting the display left.

dc606@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Anybody know why I don't get line spacing with following code:
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Line Spacing

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<5hrV3.129$Av4.4841@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<801p0e$o84$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
When using after advancing in one WRITE statement and not in all others to
same file, ADVANCING LINES is sometimes defaulted to 0 lines, thus over
writing the displayed line. Which may appear to eliminate blank lines
because the carriage control and line data is being over written.

>MOVE STAR-LINE TO PRINT-LINE.
>WRITE PRINT-LINE.


change to :
WRITE PRINT-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.

>
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>WRITE PRINT-LINE.


change to :
WRITE PRINT-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.

>
>MOVE HEADING-LINE-TWO TO PRINT-LINE.
>WRITE PRINT-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
>MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
>WRITE PRINT-LINE.


change to :
WRITE PRINT-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.

>
>MOVE HEADING-LINE-THREE TO PRINT-LINE.
>WRITE PRINT-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 2 LINES.
>MOVE SPACES TO PRINT-LINE.
>WRITE PRINT-LINE.


change to :
WRITE PRINT-LINE AFTER ADVANCING 1 LINE.
```

---

<a id="t-0cbd79bb05"></a>
## Thread: Idea for computer program...Free cellular LOOK — 13 msgs

### Idea for computer program...Free cellular LOOK

- **From:** dolphinride@usa.net
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.2600,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.perl.misc
- **Message-ID:** `<801mbp$md6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Okay, several cellular providers offer what they call "first inbound
minute free".


   I am thinking of a computer program that would allow someone to
initiate a phone call to their home phone, connected to a computer
running this software. The software would recognize from caller I.D.
data that it is your cell phone calling. You terminate the call without
the computer answering.

The computer then calls your cell phone back (free first inbound
minute) and asks you to input the phone number you want to call. The
computer then utilizes your 3-way calling feature and places a call to
whatever number you specify. During your 3-way conversation with this
person the computer automatically terminates the call to your cell
phone, and immediately calls your cell phone back EVERY MINUTE.

    Any feedback appreciated.

     If anyone is interested, you write it, I will market the program,
split it 50-50.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Idea for computer program...Free cellular LOOK

- **From:** Patricia Shanahan <pats@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.2600,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.perl.misc
- **Message-ID:** `<38246311.50EB7E59@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<801mbp$md6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Free first minute seems a useful feature to avoid getting charged for
wrong numbers and advertising. Why would anyone go to a lot of
programming effort in order to kill it off, or at a minimum cause it to
be hedged around with messy conditions about the number of free calls in
any time period or from any one number?

dolphinride@usa.net wrote:
> 
> Okay, several cellular providers offer what they call "first inbound
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Idea for computer program...Free cellular LOOK

- **From:** dolphinride@usa.net
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.2600,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.cobol,comp.lang.perl.misc

```
In article <38246311.50EB7E59@acm.org>,
  Patricia Shanahan <pats@acm.org> wrote:
> Free first minute seems a useful feature to avoid getting charged for
> wrong numbers and advertising. Why would anyone go to a lot of
> programming effort in order to kill it off, or at a minimum cause it
to
> be hedged around with messy conditions about the number of free calls
in
> any time period or from any one number?
>

   The question is how many people would have to start using this
program on a regular basis for the cellular provider to change all
advertising as a result of it.

   My belief is that this program would not be used by a high
percentage of cellular customers that have the availability if "first
inbound minute free"

  If cellular provider "A" stopped offering first inbound minute free,
cellular provider "B" would have a competative edge on the
sales/advertising floor. These are the glaring reasons why cellular
providers will not stop offering "first inbound minute free" just
because of this program...

   When I got my PrimeCo phone, I bought it JUST because of the first
inbound minute free. Turned out they were billing me at earlier that 60
seconds because of "switch time" or whatever. When I learned about the
error, I politely, and presistantly brought it to their attention. They
waited a couple weeks, then called me to let me know that they had
credited my account for more than $800.

   I still insist that they keep a close eye on my account to ensure my
first inbound minute free. SInce they initally credited my account
about 1.5 years ago, they have credit my account at least $1000 to
ensure that I am in fact getting my first minute free. They have not
cut my phone off, nor have they threatened to stop offering me or
anyone else this feature. I rack up about 899-1000 minutes a month that
way.

   I am tired of saying "call me right back". This program is a better
way.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Idea for computer program...Free cellular LOOK

- **From:** kragen@dnaco.net (Kragen Sitaker)

- **References:** `<801mbp$md6$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <go%U3.48703$23.1840278@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <s29qs0iuhpc7@corp.supernews.com>`

In article <s29qs0iuhpc7@corp.supernews.com>,
Wai Wu <waikwu@redconnect.net> wrote:
>Kragen Sitaker wrote in message ...
>>In article <801mbp$md6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,  <dolphinride@usa.net> wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>So is International Callback

I think you're talking about a system where you call a machine in the
US (from e.g. Japan), hang up, and it calls you back and gives you a
dial-tone -- from which you can dial an international call from the US,
to, say, Beijing.  Then you pay the company that operates the machine
the cost for the two international calls, plus a bit, and it's still
cheaper than if you'd dialed the call directly from Japan.

Is that what you're talking about?  It is, I think, illegal in some
places (competing with the PTTs is usually illegal), but it's certainly
not something for nothing.  You're just buying your long-distance
service from someone other than who you'd normally buy it from, at a
lower rate.
```

An interesting ancedote. A few years ago, one of these call-back
services was located in Kansas and some South American country
got tired of losing it $6.00/minute charges so they BLOCKED all
attempts to call the area code in Kansas used by the company.

The company retaliated by moving their operation to Washington DC.

cHaoTiCa (nospam@this.net) wrote:
: Wai Wu wrote:
: > 
: > Not a good idea to me. You will have a conversation that is 55
: > seconds on, 15 seconds off, 55 seconds on, 15 seconds
: > off................................. What a wast of Time. In a 10

: 
: keep in mind that i don't know an awful lot about cellular....but
: there has to be an initiating packet of some sort, yes?  so if one
: were to send a disconnect and a connect in rapid succession, the
: "waste of time" could be minimized.  though this would require a
: specially made phone...
: 
: i really should buy that cellular telephony book i saw at borders.  :)

Well, I have this mental image of the radio connected to a land line at
some point, while probally a high speed data protocal theses days, there
still is heavy latency between dial and pickup, even if that latency is
just the person's arm or finger moving to go onhook.  Cell to cell on the
other hand might have less bogus stuff to deal with, but only within the
came carrier, and enough carriers are offering free cell to cell dialing
plans.  

When I think about hacking into a cellular network, I really think to my
self why bother.  Go radio!  Granted the inital investment is higher, but
the per usage is limited to the electricity, possibly batteries.  Geting
the amature licence is easier then it use to be, from what I understand
you don't even need to know morse.  But you can get some pretty sweet high
band hardware to do data, and don't even have to bother with phone
companies, or go ultra low band (ham) and with the right antena brodcast
round the globe.  Or if you want to geek to your heart's concent, play
with a CB.  It's not used as much as it use to be, and might be adquate
for 2400 baud, perhaps 9600.  You'll need a pair two channel to emulate
full duplex, but hey.  Granted it's a pretty low tech solution by today's
standards, but it's open and free to the public!!!  You can do quite a bit
with just 5watts, limited, but never the less you'll be a real geek.
```

## Thread: TestBefore/After Example — 4 msgs

### TestBefore/After Example

- **From:** Bill <dc606@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80204m$st6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I understand how Perform Varying test before and test after
works, but would like to see two coded programs that do the same
thing - with the exception of when it is tested

1.a "real coded program" without
using the words "test before" in the program itself

2.a "real coded program" without using
the words "test after" in the program itself

------------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Is this a test after program because Paragraph 1(MOVE) is done
before the Varying is done?

Perform Paragraph 1
  Varying ws-count
   from 1 by 2
      until ws-count=7

Paragraph 1
 MOVE 5 to Temp-Table(ws-count)

------------------------------------------------------------------------
Is this a test before because varying is done before MOVE statement?

2.  Perform Varying ws-count
     from l by 2
      until ws-count=7
    MOVE 5 to Temp-Table(ws-count)

------------------------------------------------------------------------
Ws-Count     Temp-Table
1              5
2
3              5
4
5              5
6                (test before "and" test after goes here)
7              5 (test after goes here - test before does "not" go here)


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: TestBefore/After Example

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-06T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yP0V3.2004$Ss1.22085@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<80204m$st6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Bill wrote in message <80204m$st6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I understand how Perform Varying test before and test after
>works, but would like to see two coded programs that do the same
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>


You sure you understand this stuff?

You cannot have a "real program" which uses "WITH TEST AFTER" without the
words "WITH TEST AFTER" appearing in the program, so you can forget about
that one.

If you read the syntax diagrams (all flavors of COBOL), you will see that if
you do not specify "WITH TEST BEFORE" or WITH TEST AFTER", then "WITH TEST
BEFORE" is assumed.

This loop...

PERFORM [WITH TEST BEFORE] UNTIL condition
....
END-PERFORM

... may not ever execute: if condition is true when PERFORM is encountered,
the loop will never execute.

OTOH...

PERFORM WITH TEST AFTER UNTIL condition
...
END-PERFORM

... will *always* execute at least once.


MCM
```

<a id="t-84dc7c0539"></a>
## Thread: Signed numeric fields in Personal MFCobol — 73 msgs

### Signed numeric fields in Personal MFCobol

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Hi,

We students are having a problem with Microfocus v2.0.02 Personal Cobol.
The data file we are using (which came with the compiler, so it should be
compatible) uses an embedded sign.  In this particular case it's O (letter
O), which should give us a 6 and a negative sign.  The input and output are
coded as signed.  But the program bombs out, giving an error message of
"invalid character in numeric field".  If we manually change the file to a
numeric, it's fine, so we know that's the character the compiler doesn't
like.  And, our instructor ran it on the RM compiler, and it works just
fine - give the proper negative answer in the output.  So the coding is
correct, but the compiler isn't recognizing it.

Can anyone help??  There must be some compiler-specific point that none of
us has found yet.  I looked everywhere I know where to look.

Thanks,
Pat
```

#### ↳ Re: Signed numeric fields in Personal MFCobol

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<807e24$1grt$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
The letter O is EBCDIC for 6 with a negative sign. The
program most likely needs the ASCII equivalent character.

After reading the record and before using the data, execute
the following instruction for each signed (numeric-display) field.

INSPECT field-name CONVERTING "}JKLMNOPQR"
TO "pqrstuvwxy".

This will convert ASCII character, EBCDIC signs, to ASCII
character, ASCII signs.

Micro Focus is sensitive to this.

HTH
------------------
Rick Smith

Pat wrote in message ...
>Hi,
>
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>us has found yet.  I looked everywhere I know where to look.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Signed numeric fields in Personal MFCobol

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bMNV3.414$e41.2995@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <807e24$1grt$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick wrote in response to my question:

>After reading the record and before using the data, execute
>the following instruction for each signed (numeric-display) field.

>INSPECT field-name CONVERTING "}JKLMNOPQR"
>TO "pqrstuvwxy".

I coded this, right after the read (at the beginning of the sub procedure -
it wouldn't let me do it between the READ INTO and the AT END):
 INSPECT IR-ACCOUNT-BALANCE
                CONVERTING ")JKLMNOPQR" TO "pqrstuvwxy"
 INSPECT IR-CURRENT-AMOUNT
                CONVERTING ")JKLMNOPQR" TO "pqrstuvwxy"
 INSPECT IR-OVER-30
                CONVERTING ")JKLMNOPQR" TO "pqrstuvwxy"
 INSPECT IR-OVER-60
                CONVERTING ")JKLMNOPQR" TO "pqrstuvwxy"

Still get the run-time error  **but** if I manually change the offending
data file character to "v" (which I'm assuming is equivilent to the EBCDIC
"O")it works beautifully.   What did I do wrong in the conversion
statement????

Thanks much!
Pat
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Signed numeric fields in Personal MFCobol

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <807e24$1grt$1@news.hitter.net> <bMNV3.414$e41.2995@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
With READ INTO, the data you are using is in working-storage.
Use the field-names from the record description in working-storage
in the INSPECT statements. Place the INSPECT statements
immediately after the READ INTO.

It is also possible to change the READ INTO to a combination
of READ and MOVE. In that case, the INSPECT statements
would be between the READ and the MOVE and would use
the field-names of the record description for the FD.

Also, as Michael Mattias mentioned, change the right parenthesis,
")", to a right brace, "}".

HTH
------------------
Rick Smith

Pat wrote in message ...
>Rick wrote in response to my question:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>                CONVERTING ")JKLMNOPQR" TO "pqrstuvwxy"
[...]
>Still get the run-time error  **but** if I manually change the offending
>data file character to "v" (which I'm assuming is equivilent to the EBCDIC
>"O")it works beautifully.   What did I do wrong in the conversion
>statement????
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Signed numeric fields in Personal MFCobol

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <807e24$1grt$1@news.hitter.net> <bMNV3.414$e41.2995@newsfeed.slurp.net> <808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
In article <808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net>,
Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
>With READ INTO, the data you are using is in working-storage.
>Use the field-names from the record description in working-storage
>in the INSPECT statements. Place the INSPECT statements
>immediately after the READ INTO.

... which is the way that all Good, Decent and Competent Coders would do
it.

>
>It is also possible to change the READ INTO to a combination
>of READ and MOVE. In that case, the INSPECT statements
>would be between the READ and the MOVE and would use
>the field-names of the record description for the FD.

... which is something done by fools, dolts, simpletons, churls, lackwits
and chuckleheaded Janitors-Who-Masquerads-As-Programmers who have not
learned the Commandment of:

Thou Shalt NOT Manipulate Data In File-Buffers.

(this, of course, is a Strict Application of the observation 'Arithmetic
manipulation of data in file-buffers may have unpredictable results'; a
Good Coder will eliminate the temptation to do this by *always* performing
READ INTO and WRITE FROM)

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <807e24$1grt$1@news.hitter.net> <bMNV3.414$e41.2995@newsfeed.slurp.net> <808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message ...
>In article <808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net>,
>Rick Smith <ricksmith@aiservices.com> wrote:
[...]
>>It is also possible to change the READ INTO to a combination
>>of READ and MOVE. In that case, the INSPECT statements
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>READ INTO and WRITE FROM)

To summarize previous threads related to this topic;

For IBM Mainframes,
    Thou Shalt NOT Manipulate Data In File-Buffers.
For Other ANS-85 COBOL,
    Thou Shalt NOT Manipulate Data In File-Buffers,
        While The File is Closed.
For Micro Focus COBOL,
    Do Whatever You Want With Data In File-Buffers;
        Just Don't Expect The Whatever To Be Portable.

Are there good and decent reasons for accessing the data
in file buffers directly? Yes!

Given that all computer programs consist of code that
implements concepts and that these concepts are
implemented in different ways depending upon the
language used; then the statement "READ file-name INTO
record-name" implements the concept "Get record into
record-name.", directly. Other concepts might include:
"Get corrected record into record-name.", "Get transformed
record into record-name.", "Get subset of record into
record-name.", etc.; such that the data in record-name is
precisely that which is required for the other concepts that
are implemented in code.

Each of the other concepts may be implemented by
accessing the data in the file buffer, directly. They may also
be implemented by incorporating the additional step of
reading the external record into working-storage and by
transforming or manipulating the data within the internal
records. The only precaution one should take before
accessing an input buffer is that the record was sucessfully
read. (I did not mention this in my response to Pat.)

Since data, successfully read, may be accessed from and
manipulated directly in the file buffer; whether to do so is
a matter of style or preference. I find that using the buffer
directly is clearer than moving the data to working-storage.

The above comments apply equally to WRITE and WRITE
FROM. If file status indicates the record buffer is available
use it directly if it pleases you to do so.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80%V3.5635$Hk.42923@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <807e24$1grt$1@news.hitter.net> <bMNV3.414$e41.2995@newsfeed.slurp.net> <808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
>
>Since data, successfully read, may be accessed from and
>manipulated directly in the file buffer; whether to do so is
>a matter of style or preference. I find that using the buffer
>directly is clearer than moving the data to working-storage.

I agree.  What kind of lame-brain 'programmer' can't keep up with
whether the buffer has been properly read or not, or if the file is
open or not?  If they can't do that, then READ INTO isn't going to
help one bit.  We should not set programming standards based on
people who should not be programming in the first place. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991109173217.19242.00000103@ngol03.aol.com>`

```
In article <80%V3.5635$Hk.42923@news1.mia>, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> writes:

>
>Rick Smith wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I have found that it takes only a slight amount of planning to design your
program to work from the file buffers.  It makes much more sense to work
from the file buffers when dealing with large records and performance.
The only time that I have found it useful to operate from W-S is when 
merging ultiple records, and even that can be tweaked into using only the
Input and output buffers.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3828BADF.7166@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<80%V3.5635$Hk.42923@news1.mia> <19991109173217.19242.00000103@ngol03.aol.com>`

```
Sff5ky wrote:
> 
> In article <80%V3.5635$Hk.42923@news1.mia>, "Judson McClendon"
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Input and output buffers.


My shop also enforces a standard that you do not work with the data
record in the buffer area.  Believe it or not, a lot of junior COBOL
programmers just don't understand why they get S0C4 abends when they
reference a field in the buffer area that is off the end of their VB
record!

Any standard that protects idiot programmers from beginner mistakes is a
good standard.  The standard has been in place for over 20 years, so
idiot-S0C4 abends just don't happen very often here.

Oh, and the "extra effort" of creating code to READ INTO and WRITE FROM
is totally automated by a home grown code generator.

Pete
```

<80%V3.5635$Hk.42923@news1.mia>...
> 
> I agree.  What kind of lame-brain 'programmer' can't keep up with
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> people who should not be programming in the first place. :-)

Right.
And to take this a step further: Refer to the original data (file buffer,
linkage section) whenever possible, don't copy it around to numerous
variables so it is impossible to trace where it came from.  Some Cobol
programs are so packed with unnecessary MOVEs that you must think the
programmer was paid per line.

Gunnar.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3828AA92.13EDE836@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <807e24$1grt$1@news.hitter.net> <bMNV3.414$e41.2995@newsfeed.slurp.net> <808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <80%V3.5635$Hk.42923@news1.mia> <01bf2afc$8258b500$0100007f@vaagshaugen>`

```
Gunnar Opheim wrote:
> 
> Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote in article
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gunnar.

If your standard is READ INTO, then have the file description 01 
MY-FILE  PIC X(180).  No fields defined.  Sort records are excepted,
only to provide the key data.

One advantage of READ INTO is when you use COBOL to extract and/or
change data so that you WRITE FROM the same working-storage field.

Either use the fields in the file section or don't have them available
there.

Just thought of an exception.  If you need to save off header records,
do a read then move, with just enough information in the file section to
know what type of record it is.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Dirk Munk <munk@bart.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <807e24$1grt$1@news.hitter.net> <bMNV3.414$e41.2995@newsfeed.slurp.net> <808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net>`

```


Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> docdwarf@clark.net wrote in message ...
…[52 more quoted lines elided]…
> read. (I did not mention this in my response to Pat.)

How can it not be ? I am used to compilers that will not allow further
program execution after a unsuccessful read, unless explicitely made
possible by a "invalid" option or appropriate coding in the
declaratives. If not, the program crashes as it should do. I can't
imagine one should check explicitely every time if a read (or write) was
successfull. 

> 
> Since data, successfully read, may be accessed from and
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Rick Smith

I agree, what is the use of a record description if you can't manipulate
the data fields in that description ? We might as well have record
descriptions that only exist from a 01 level description, something like
01 this-record pic x(500) or so.
 
Furthermore, a record area/buffer is not quite the same as a file
buffer. Files are read with a certain amount of disk blocks at the time,
and there can be many records in those chunks af data blocks. The file
system is responsible for extracting the records from that buffer and
presenting every single record to the program. Think of statements like
"reserve n areas" or "apply deferred write" in the IO control paragraph.
These statements can speed up programs considerably, but only influence
the way the file system works, not the data areas in the program itself.
The record areas in a program should be nothing else as data areas where
the filesystem can put and get the records.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl>`

```
In article <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl>, Dirk Munk  <munk@bart.nl> wrote:

[snippage]

>I agree, what is the use of a record description if you can't manipulate
>the data fields in that description ? We might as well have record
>descriptions that only exist from a 01 level description, something like
>01 this-record pic x(500) or so.

What one 'can' do and what one 'should' do are not necessarily the same;
one 'can' write a perfectly funtioning program full of ALTERs, GO TOs and
only enough data-names to allow for use of reference modification (MOVE
INREC(442:4) TO WS-COMP3-FLD8).

As for '01 this-record pic x(500)' under an FD... I've seen QA reviews
which would not allow anything else into Prod, for precisely this reason.

DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```


docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
> 
> In article <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl>, Dirk Munk  <munk@bart.nl> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> INREC(442:4) TO WS-COMP3-FLD8).

Sure, but I have never heard that using the data fields in the record
description would be bad programming practice, and I have never had any
trouble with it. But maybe this has something to do with the quality of
the compilers, instead of bad programming practice. For instance if a
field in the working storage is declared as pic 9(6), than I am sure
that I will find it filled with 000000 the first time I want to use it,
where as with certain compilers one has to initialize this field
explicitly before using it, because otherwise it is filled with garbage.
It all depends on how elegant the compiler was constructed.   

> 
> As for '01 this-record pic x(500)' under an FD... I've seen QA reviews
> which would not allow anything else into Prod, for precisely this reason.

If it is necessary because otherwise you can't trust your program,
that's fine. But in my view one should look for another compiler if
using the fields in the record description give unpredictable results.

regards,

Dirk

> 
> DD
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl>`

```
Dirk Munk wrote:
>
>docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>using the fields in the record description give unpredictable results.

This is an "IBM Thing".  Because (correct me if I am wrong, IBM'ers :)
IBM COBOL compilers don't actually assign a separate record area, they
use pointers into the actual file buffers.  On input, the record area
isn't 'available' until after the file is opened and a record read.
On output, the record area is not available until the file is opened,
and the contents of the record just written is not available in the
record area, because the pointer has been repositioned to the next
record position in the buffer.  I can see only two reasons for their
'only use READ INTO and WRITE FROM' obsession.  One is they don't seem
confident that IBM COBOL programmers can be taught the above rules.
Unless IBM COBOL programmers are morons ;-), this is pointless.  The
second reason is that it may be less efficient to access an indirectly
referenced record area than a directly referenced Working Storage area.
On modern processors, this should be negligible, but I am not familiar
enough with IBM architecture to know for sure.  At any rate, I believe
most of us would agree that such efficiency considerations would be of
less importance than program clarity.

I believe the practice was started a long time ago by IBM (after all,
*they* don't mind if programs use more memory :), rather than teach
simple record area discipline.  This has been brainwashed into the
generations of IBM programmers since.  They are so indoctrinated to it
that many of them can't view this issue in an unbiased manner. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00

```
Judson McClendon wrote:
> 
> Dirk Munk wrote:
…[62 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have everlasting life."


Hey, I resemble that remark!!  ;-)

It is still true today in IBM Mainframe COBOL, that debugging problems
can be simplified by the use of "READ INTO" and "WRITE FROM".  Anytime
debugging is simplified, it saves time and money.

(The old dog has spoken.)
Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3828CD2B.2CEBD769@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <3828C0AE.6EF4@saif.com>`

```
First of all, starting with the IBM COBOL 1985 compilers, the record
area associated with a file is available between OPEN and CLOSE. 
Further after some PTF level with Language Environment 1.5 (OS390 level
1.3), the CEEDUMP listing included the records for the files for all
COBOL 1985 compilers. This means that the results of a straight read are
easier to find in the file area than they are in working-storage. 
Currently they get messed up with the file names (DDNAME for the file,
not the FD name) in the dump if there is an internal sort and the SD is
not the last descriptor in the FILE SECTION.  Trust the BLF numbers
associated with the file in that case.  Since either the working storage
or the file area contains the record to be written for a WRITE statement
depending on whether the from option is used, write from buys nothing in
the new environment other than you don't have to initialize the record
completely before each write but you can use fields that are still
valid. 

The COBOL standard specifically states that the availability of a record
area associated with a file is undefined before OPEN and after CLOSE. 
With prior IBM compilers, addressability for input records was dropped
at end of file for at least the non-VSAM sequential files so that READ
file AT END MOVE HIGH-VALUE to record would probably cause a protection
exception.  

The main danger with using READ rather than READ INTO comes with
variable length records and probably is the same danger on most
platforms.  If you attempt to access fields that are not in the actual
record, results are unspecified and if you move to a field beyond the
last byte of the current record, you will corrupt a subsequent record in
the buffer.  With VSAM I suspect that you always have a separate record
area but I have not investigated the code.     

Note there are other subtle problems you get with READ INTO that you
don't get with READ when you are dealing with indexed files.  If the
only true description of the record is in working storage, if that
changes when you use READ INTO/WRITE FROM all sorts of debugging hell
can occur if you done change the record lengths correctly.  Despite my
dislike of the COBOL awkward form of qualification, in any new program I
copy the record description in wherever the record is used so that I am
guaranteed that I make ONLY ONE change to change all instances of the
record.  
With the advent of large working storage sections (over 64K) it may take
the loading of a register to provide addressability to the record either
in working storage or in the file buffer so the efficiency of addressing
is the same.  You save a MOVE when you use READ or WRITE instead of READ
INTO or WRITE FROM. 

Because of the changes in technology, they have fairly well stopped
enforcing the READ INTO/WRITE FROM requirement at the place where I am
currently contracting.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca 
Pete Wirfs wrote:
> 
> Judson McClendon wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >that I will find it filled with 000000 the first time I want to use it,

insert comment  vvvv
** > (You may be sure but the compiler isn't unless you specify a value
clause
      and the IBM OS390 compilers probably initialize display and packed
decimal     
      fields to non-numeric garbage or LOW-VALUE which is non-numeric)
                ^^^^
> > >where as with certain compilers one has to initialize this field
> > >explicitly before using it, because otherwise it is filled with garbage.
…[40 more quoted lines elided]…
> Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382CA048.78E8@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <3828C0AE.6EF4@saif.com> <3828CD2B.2CEBD769@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```
Thank you Clark for confirming that using the buffer area on variable
length records can be dangerous.  This is the reason for READ INTO/WRITE
FROM still being enforced here.

When it comes to VSAM records, you might define your file as
MAXLRECL=100 and AVGLRECL=100, and your dataset will still accept
shorter records.  VSAM does not have a MINLRECL parameter.  (This fact
is not an every day concern, it is just an interesting technical fact. 
I have only gotten bit by it once when a junior programmer did a
one-time REPRO into a production dataset with short records.)

I think this just proves that READ INTO/WRITE FROM is a grey area with
no "perfect" standard, unless your standard says just use the
appropriate method, and you trust all of your developers to make the
correct choice.

Personally, I still believe the standard of using READ INTO/WRITE FROM
on the MVS platform results in less risk.

Pete



Clark Morris wrote:
> 
> First of all, starting with the IBM COBOL 1985 compilers, the record
…[119 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2G3X3.10252$uk.79067@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <3828C0AE.6EF4@saif.com> <3828CD2B.2CEBD769@nbnet.nb.ca> <382CA048.78E8@saif.com>`

```
Pete Wirfs wrote:
>Thank you Clark for confirming that using the buffer area on variable
>length records can be dangerous.  This is the reason for READ INTO/WRITE
>FROM still being enforced here.

Why do you consider permitting a programming error to pass by masking
it in a WS buffer to be better than nailing it?  If a program references
outside a variable length record, it is an *error*.  Letting errors
pass undetected is *not* a good thing.  I cannot imagine how (or why!)
anyone could convince themselves that it is. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80inn2$5n6$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <3828C0AE.6EF4@saif.com> <3828CD2B.2CEBD769@nbnet.nb.ca> <382CA048.78E8@saif.com> <2G3X3.10252$uk.79067@news3.mia>`

```
Judson,
  There are two different answers - depending on whether you are using a '74
Standard compiler or an '85 Standard compiler.

It is true, that in an '85 Standard compiler, you can DEFINITELY figure out
the size of a record that is read (via the RECORD VARYING IN SIZE DEPENDING
ON clause).

HOWEVER, there was no such "facility" in the '74 Standard.  Therefore,
depending on who/how the file structure was defined (which may WELL not have
been by someone who knows - or cares about COBOL), it is entirely possible
that you might want to READ INTO a "maximum size" working storage area and
FROM THE DATA figure out which size "real" record you have retrieved.

Personally, whenever I was involved in the "file design" stage for a '74
Standard program, I put "something" into the "fixed" part of the record to
give me a clue as to which type/size record I was reading.  HOWEVER, as
stated above, there was no guarantee that I was in control of the record
layout.  As other software DID allow you to "process" various sized records
(with no clue in the fixed part of which record it was), this was (and I
think still IS) a valid "issue" - and not a "programming error".

As far as "newer" COBOL applications (with the added functionality), I can't
think of any reason (off the top of my head) why you couldn't process it in
the FD.  On the other hand, I am so used to systems where there is minimal
(if any) benefit in doing this - and processing the record in WS *can* avoid
certain types of problems (that are programming logic problems - but are
nevertheless "real") so I will admit that I am still "happier" processing all
records via READ INTO/WRITE FROM.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<FyhX3.16991$z26.157477@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <3828C0AE.6EF4@saif.com> <3828CD2B.2CEBD769@nbnet.nb.ca> <382CA048.78E8@saif.com> <2G3X3.10252$uk.79067@news3.mia> <80inn2$5n6$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
William M. Klein wrote:
>
>It is true, that in an '85 Standard compiler, you can DEFINITELY figure out
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>FROM THE DATA figure out which size "real" record you have retrieved.

That doesn't change a thing, Bill.  I already said that there are
specific situations where READ INTO or WRITE FROM are warranted.  By
all means, use them when needed.  My objection is to the 'blanket
mandate' to *always* use them. :-)

In my opinion, any system that writes variable length records with no
indication in the record of how long it is, is poorly designed.  Good
design is not just about addressing needs you *know* will be there,
but to address needs that *might* be there.  This is called robustness
and flexibility; qualities that make a system effective with little
maintenance for many years. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 15)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80jkq1$28cn$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <3828C0AE.6EF4@saif.com> <3828CD2B.2CEBD769@nbnet.nb.ca> <382CA048.78E8@saif.com> <2G3X3.10252$uk.79067@news3.mia> <80inn2$5n6$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Could someone please explain the difference between
the '74 and '85 standards for the syntax rules for READ
with INTO.

I checked a few LRMs (not standards) and it appears
that READ INTO is invalid in '74 when record sizes are
different; but valid in '85. However, I have been unable
to create an example where ANS74 flagging tells me
there is a syntax error.

Quote from Micro Focus COBOL 3.2 LRM, READ
statement, Syntax Rules, All Formats (All Files):

"The INTO phrase must not be used when the input file
contains records of various sizes as indicated by their
record descriptions."

It also has an indication for '85 that "This restriction has
been removed."

An IBM LRM, GC28-6396-5, for COBOL F (ANS68) and
OS/VS COBOL (ANS68/ISO72) makes no mention of
READ INTO being invalid with "records of various sizes."
[Note that the first release of OS/VS COBOL pre-dates
the ANS74 standard.]

-----------
The IBM COBOL F, Programmer's Guide, GC28-6399-3,
suggests using READ INTO and WRITE FROM for two
reasons:

1. Debugging is simpler.
2. Trying to access a record area after AT END can cause
    problems.

-----------
Although clearly not related to this thread, for those
interested in solving mysteries, the IBM Programmer's Guide
also has this suggestion for PERFORM.

"Always either PERFORM routine-name THRU
routine-name-exit, or PERFORM section-name. A PERFORM
paragraph-name can cause trouble for the programmer
trying to maintain the program. For example, if a paragraph
must be broken into two paragraphs, the programmer must
examine every statement to determine whether or not this
paragraph is within the range of the PERFORM statement.
Then all statements referencing the paragraph-name must
be changed to PERFORM THRU statements."
------------------
Rick Smith

William M. Klein wrote in message <80inn2$5n6$1@nntp5.atl.mindspring.net>...
>
>  There are two different answers - depending on whether you are using a
'74
>Standard compiler or an '85 Standard compiler.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-13T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382CAAE6.2769B087@home.nl>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <3828C0AE.6EF4@saif.com> <3828CD2B.2CEBD769@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```


Clark Morris wrote:
> 
> First of all, starting with the IBM COBOL 1985 compilers, the record
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> the buffer.  

That depends on the compiler and the filesystem. With the compiler I'm
using this is absolutely impossible. If a record is read that is shorter
than the maximum length, parts of a previous record will be found in the
remaining part of the record area. In fact the data is moved from the
file buffer to the record area, the record area is not a kind of address
mapping over the file buffer as seems to be the case with your compiler. 

But the read into can also have its problems if you don't take the
record length in consideration. The read into is a alphanumeric move, so
the remaining part of record area in the working storage is filled with
spaces. Suppose there are numeric fields specified in that area, the
program will crash with 'invalid decimal data' as soon as one tries to
read that data (at least my compiler does).

> With VSAM I suspect that you always have a separate record
> area but I have not investigated the code.
…[90 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Pete
```
