###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3828ffa3.228091374@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia>`

```
On Tue, 9 Nov 1999 18:32:36 -0600, "Judson McClendon"
<judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:

>This is an "IBM Thing".  Because (correct me if I am wrong, IBM'ers :)


Not really.  It's a COBOL standard thing.  The contets of the data
area in an FD are "Undefined" before an open and after a close.  The
data contents after a "Write" are similarly undefined.

Even with IBM COBOL once the file is open you can manipulate the data
buffer before a read.  (How else would one move a value into a key
field for a Start or Direct read).  Also, AFTER a write, the contents
of the data buffer may or may not be flushed by the OS - typically
under IBM they are NOT, so the contents are Undefined - AS THE
STANDARD SAYS.  (Not yelling, Emphasis).  Anyway, I DO manipulate data
in the buffers - but not before an open, after a close, or after a
write.

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tlcW3.279$uk.1909@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <3828ffa3.228091374@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>data contents after a "Write" are similarly undefined.

What I meant was that the programming philosophy "always use READ INTO
and WRITE FROM" is pretty much associated with the IBM community.  Yes,
the COBOL standard specifies when record areas are available.  But to
my knowledge over some 30+ years, I have never seen this philosophy so
prevalent in any other community, except where it was influenced by
IBM'ers, either directly or historicly. :-)

>Even with IBM COBOL once the file is open you can manipulate the data
>buffer before a read.  (How else would one move a value into a key
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>write.

Sounds like we're pretty much in agreement; there is no need for a
global "*always* use READ INTO and WRITE FROM" dictate, either in the
IBM community, or elsewhere. :-)

Note that there may be very good reason to use READ INTO or WRITE FROM
in specific situations.  For example, I almost always use WRITE FROM
when printing page headers.  Page headers usually have so much literal
data that it is more efficient to set them up in WS than use several
moves to construct them in the FD record at run time.  It is only the
broad global dictate that I object to.  Takes memory, takes time, does
not have a sound logical basis. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into the diffinitive answer...

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** clastname@nospam.com (Charles Haatvedt Jr.)
- **Date:** 1999-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.12982005248f47069896c3@news>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <3828ffa3.228091374@news1.attglobal.net> <tlcW3.279$uk.1909@news3.mia>`

```

In article <tlcW3.279$uk.1909@news3.mia>, judmc123@bellsouth.net says...
> Thane Hubbell wrote:
> >Judson McClendon wrote:
> >>
> >>This is an "IBM Thing".  Because (correct me if I am wrong, IBM'ers :)
> >
 ....  snip   ....

In the IBM cobol 74 days when defining a variable length file as follows

FD  INPUT-FILE
    BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS
    RECORD CONTAINS 100 TO 30100 CHARACTERS.

01  RCD-1.
 	05  FILLER            PIC X(98).
      05  RCD-CNTR-1  	    PIC S9(4)  COMP.
      05  RCD-BYTE-1    OCCURS 0 TO 10 TIMES
                        DEPENDING ON RCD-CNTR-1 
                            PIC X.

01  RCD-2.
 	05  FILLER            PIC X(29998).
      05  RCD-CNTR-2  	    PIC S9(4)  COMP.
      05  RCD-BYTE-2    OCCURS 0 TO 100 TIMES
                        DEPENDING ON RCD-CNTR-2 
                            PIC X.

When the "READ INPUT-FILE" statement was executed, it called a cobol 
library routine to calculate the variable length of EVERY "01" level 
associated with file....

hence ===>>> if the file were copied / reblocked by utility or program 
which used PUT LOCATE instead of PUT MOVE (I can't remember which is 
which)   but one of these two would check to see if there was room in the 
buffer for the longest possible record and then move it.... the other PUT 
LOCATE (???) would check the lenght of the ACTUAL record being written to 
see if it would fit into the buffer before moving it....

any way....  if the file were reblocked or written by a COBOL program 
using APPLY WRITE-ONLY, then it was POSSIBLE to have a "short" record at 
the end of the block...

Imagine a short record at the end of the block with the above file 
definition...   when attempting to calculate the record length for the 
RCD-2, it would access a location 29,998 bytes from the start of the 
current record (plus 4 bytes for the RDW), if that address was outside 
the address space of the program,  a SOC4 abend would result....

===>>>>  notice that NOWHERE in the COBOL source code was an attempt made 
         to reference data outside of the record, only in the COBOL 
         library routine...


As for WRITE XXX FROM.... I have encountered situations where programmers 
accessed the buffer after a write....  This leads to a situation where if 
the file is allocated to JES it allocates a single buffer and is NOT 
blocked...  hence the buffer remains the same after a write is issued...

but nasty things occur when the JCL is changed to write the data to a 
file instead....   now the record pointer is pointing to the next area in 
the buffer in the COBOL 74 days any way...  unpredictable results....

PS... It took me all night looking at a dump before I discovered the 
cause of my SOC4 abend on a "READ" statement...
  
====  oh the pain of being a ROOKIE....  situations which occur once 
every two or three years,  always bite a ROOKIE on call when help is not 
readily available....   

the silver lining to this story was that it ONLY burned me once...  I 
changed the code to the following

fd    input-file
       .....

01    filler              pic x(min-length).
01    filler              pic x(max-lenght). 

and used read input-file into working-storate-max-lenght....
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into the diffinitive answer...

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9Js0OIhf7nwJF=ai6MOTSnAobD5M@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <3828ffa3.228091374@news1.attglobal.net> <tlcW3.279$uk.1909@news3.mia> <MPG.12982005248f47069896c3@news>`

```
The venerable clastname@nospam.com (Charles Haatvedt Jr.) bestowed
upon comp.lang.cobol on Sun, 14 Nov 1999 07:25:51 GMT these words:

>In article <tlcW3.279$uk.1909@news3.mia>, judmc123@bellsouth.net says...
>> Thane Hubbell wrote:
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>In the IBM cobol 74 days when defining a variable length file as follows

In other words, it was a bug in the Cobol-74 compiler.  It didn't
happen if you used fixed length records, it didn't happen if you used
variable length records with a single 01, it didn't happen if you used
variable length records with multiple compatible 01s, and it doesn't
happen now if you use a comiler of recent vintage.  I can't recall the
last time I even saw a file multiple conflicting variable records,
though I probably designed one in my ignorant youth (and thought I was
damned clever, too).  I'm sure more than one file in a thousand is
laid out this way.

Due to this rare and ancient bug, otherwise respectable Cobol gurus
give the advice "always READ INTO and WRITE FROM".  Why didn't you
IBMers just insist that IBM fix the bug?

You're right, Jud.  It's just an "IBM thing".
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into the diffinitive answer...

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** Bill Thompson <wthompson@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81bkv4$s5$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <3828ffa3.228091374@news1.attglobal.net> <tlcW3.279$uk.1909@news3.mia> <MPG.12982005248f47069896c3@news>`

```
Dang!!! I am really getting to hate these Deja delays and omissions....

No!  The routine in the generated COBOL code did not get called upon a
READ!  That code only got called when the elementary field that was the
object of "occurs depending on" phrase got altered, that included a
change to any group level to which that elementary field was a part of.

That was why any intelligent programmer would identify which of those
01s in the FD was the proper record just read in and then before
referring to any variable field in that record would "MOVE RCD-CNTR-
(whichever) TO RCD-CNTR-(itself)".  This then caused the compiler to
set the length of THAT 01 to the correct length.

READ INTOs always used max record as the from length and the length of
the to area as the to length.  This could result in either truncation
or padding as will any move of dissimilar length areas.

As a note, to avoid the constant recalculation of variable length
areas, the ODO object was often redefined with a slightly different
name and then could be referenced without the compiler doing a
recalculation.

With all this clamor about READ versus READ INTO of variable length
records, I now am understanding why some interviewers in the past were
so concerned with variable length record I/O skills - while I never
thought much about them....

I learned in a shop that had many variable length KSDS files,
synchronized to each other (SQL was a long ways away).  Many of those
files had multiple ODO phrases and one had four.  To modify there
records did require a READ INTO but mere input only reads did not.

Learning a few simple "rules" that were required to avoid clobbering
storage was all that was needed, and these rules are still valid to
this day.

Bill
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into the diffinitive answer...

_(thread depth: 13)_

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3qb%3.2294$Di3.24889@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <3828ffa3.228091374@news1.attglobal.net> <tlcW3.279$uk.1909@news3.mia> <MPG.12982005248f47069896c3@news>`

```
Actually that is NOT an IBM - thing, All COBOLs allow Variable length
records
and almost all Vendors put those type of things into library routines.

The 'Apply Write Only' is an IBM COBOL addition for performance

And the problem below occurred because of an improper use of the syntax and
or a failure
to read the notes in the manual.

Sorry

Charles Haatvedt Jr. <clastname@nospam.com> wrote in message
news:MPG.12982005248f47069896c3@news...
>
> In article <tlcW3.279$uk.1909@news3.mia>, judmc123@bellsouth.net says...
…[86 more quoted lines elided]…
> remove space in email address....
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38290d3f.2376737@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia>`

```
On Tue, 9 Nov 1999 18:32:36 -0600, "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote:
>
>This is an "IBM Thing".  Because (correct me if I am wrong, IBM'ers :)
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>record position in the buffer. 
Correct.
> I can see only two reasons for their
>'only use READ INTO and WRITE FROM' obsession.  One is they don't seem
>confident that IBM COBOL programmers can be taught the above rules.
>Unless IBM COBOL programmers are morons ;-), this is pointless.  
Correct.
>The
>second reason is that it may be less efficient to access an indirectly
>referenced record area than a directly referenced Working Storage area.
Not correct.  Access to any memory area is reference to a base register plus an offset,
and the overhead would be in only specifying another base/offset combination, which will
be done at compile time.  
The actual overhead is in the MOVE mode.  The file buffer will be accessed anyways, and
then the contents moved into Working-Storage.  One more move and more storage usage (and a
larger reference set as well)

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Status codes

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38297F49.A5E03D30@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia>`

```
Wish list.

It seems out of the spirit of COBOL to read a record and then check to
see if a number in a working-storage field contains some number.

I would prefer English:

READ MY-RECORD
    AT END  SET MY-SWITCH TO TRUE
    ON ERROR PERFORM MY-ERROR-ROUTINE.

Then maybe have MY-ERROR-ROUTINE look at the status code.  Maybe have a
series of 88 messages the way IDMS does, allowing you to check to see
what the error is:

READ MY-KEYED-RECORD
    ON RECORD-NOT-FOUND CONTINUE
    ON ERROR PERFORM MY-KEYED-ERROR-ROUTINE.

In this case, RECORD-NOT-FOUND would have to move the logic beyond ON
ERROR.  If you hadn't asked that, it would be ON ERROR.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Status codes

_(thread depth: 11)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80cfad$7me$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <38297F49.A5E03D30@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@nospamhome.com> wrote:

: Then maybe have MY-ERROR-ROUTINE look at the status code.  Maybe have a
: series of 88 messages the way IDMS does, allowing you to check to see
: what the error is:

: READ MY-KEYED-RECORD
:     ON RECORD-NOT-FOUND CONTINUE
:     ON ERROR PERFORM MY-KEYED-ERROR-ROUTINE.

: In this case, RECORD-NOT-FOUND would have to move the logic beyond ON
: ERROR.  If you hadn't asked that, it would be ON ERROR.

      EVALUATE File-Status
      WHEN Status-OK         CONTINUE
      WHEN Record-Not-Found  ....
      WHEN OTHER              ....
      END-EVALUATE

No need for any new syntax.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Status codes

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3829D441.B0403E@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <38297F49.A5E03D30@NOSPAMhome.com> <80cfad$7me$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```
Richard Plinston wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee <brazee@nospamhome.com> wrote:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> No need for any new syntax.

Correct, but the 88 level has to be written by you, and more
importantly, the File-Status is not tied to the READ.  It is quite
possible to have a bug where you are checking the wrong file status
after a read.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Status codes

_(thread depth: 14)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382AE3BB.603DA8F2@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <38297F49.A5E03D30@NOSPAMhome.com> <80cfad$7me$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3829D441.B0403E@NOSPAMhome.com> <GVpW3.1515$AG3.28551@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> Howard Brazee wrote in message <3829D441.B0403E@NOSPAMhome.com>...
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> it right).

Anything made automatic will ALWAYS be in anybody's code, and ALWAYS be
what is expected.  It's not the effort of writing an 88 which is a
bother, it is the lack of assurance that it is the same in all programs.
 
> Status not "tied" to the read?
> 
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

I'm not saying that we can't code well with the language as it is.  But
if I were designing COBOL right from scratch there would be some
differences.  For instance, there would be no dropping through from
paragraph to paragraph.  I have seen people cut and paste GO TO
01000-EXIT, and forget to change that to GO TO 02000-EXIT.   Certainly
people will cut and paste EVALUATE THE-FILE01-STATUS and forget to
change it to EVALUATE-THE-FILE02-STATUS.

COBOL has an existing style.  You don't say
    READ FILE-1  AT END OF FILE-1 PERFORM....   The file referred to in
the AT END clause only fits the READ it is attached to.  I am saying
that other STATUS checks should work the same way.  There will be fewer
errors, less confusion, and more consistency.
```

- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <38297F49.A5E03D30@NOSPAMhome.com> <80cfad$7me$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3829D441.B0403E@NOSPAMhome.com> <GVpW3.1515$AG3.28551@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <382AE3BB.603DA8F2@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>
>if I were designing COBOL right from scratch there would be some
>differences.  For instance, there would be no dropping through from
>paragraph to paragraph.

Hear! Hear!

>I have seen people cut and paste GO TO
>01000-EXIT, and forget to change that to GO TO 02000-EXIT.

That's why you need to run a simple checker on every source before it
goes into production.  The checker can check for missing/extra periods
and GOTO wrong exit, and PERFORM THRU wrong paragraph.  From lots of
experience, I can tell you that many programs have these bugs, just
waiting to bite you at the worst time. :-)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Status codes

_(thread depth: 16)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-12T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382C2615.ED41906F@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <N23W3.6075$Hk.47423@news1.mia> <38297F49.A5E03D30@NOSPAMhome.com> <80cfad$7me$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <3829D441.B0403E@NOSPAMhome.com> <GVpW3.1515$AG3.28551@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <382AE3BB.603DA8F2@NOSPAMhome.com> <7BKW3.767$Vn2.30494@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> You want to change the standard to protect us from our own bad habits and/or
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

No, but the standard has been changed to make it easier to make
structured code, to make it easier to make code without dumb errors, and
to make the language more unified.

There is nothing in my suggestion which disallows old ways of checking
status codes - it just adds a way which fits in with the way COBOL works
already. 

The AT-END clause works fine.  We can use status checking to do the same
thing as AT-END, but AT-END fits the spirit of COBOL better, is more
clear, and less likely to be in error.  Expand on this good idea for
other status checking, you can ignore it if you like (or do you use
AT-END yourself?)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3828BBC1.6D0A@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl>`

```
Dirk Munk wrote:
> 
> docdwarf@clark.net wrote:
…[38 more quoted lines elided]…
> > DD

It is not a compiler issue.  I have often seen junior COBOL programmers
get hung up debugging S0C4 abends because they have attempted to access
a data field in the buffer that is off the end of their VB record.  

My shop has a 20-year old standard of "READ INTO" and "WRITE FROM", so
if the same logic error exists, it pops up as an S0C7 instead of a S0C4,
thus making it much easier to debug.

Any standard that makes debugging easier will save time and money.

Pete
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<29ui2sshtrnjmqjtodldtdb51g32cd876m@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <Pg0W3.35360$oa2.123155@iad-read.news.verio.net> <3828A0DD.9E563475@home.nl> <3828BBC1.6D0A@saif.com>`

```
On Tue, 09 Nov 1999 16:26:41 -0800 Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com> wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>It is not a compiler issue.  I have often seen junior COBOL programmers
:>get hung up debugging S0C4 abends because they have attempted to access
:>a data field in the buffer that is off the end of their VB record.  

I would see that as a reason TO use the buffers. If the program goes outside
the buffer it will fail immediately.

:>My shop has a 20-year old standard of "READ INTO" and "WRITE FROM", so
:>if the same logic error exists, it pops up as an S0C7 instead of a S0C4,
:>thus making it much easier to debug.

Or, perhaps, the program won't fail and use incorrect data.

Also, the program may fail a lot later due to the bad data.

:>Any standard that makes debugging easier will save time and money.

Agreed. I just don't understand why an 0C7 sometime after the failure is
easier to debug than an 0C4 at the time of failure.

After all, when you get the 0C7, you will still have to track it down.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<809nqu$2pf2$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <807e24$1grt$1@news.hitter.net> <bMNV3.414$e41.2995@newsfeed.slurp.net> <808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl>`

```

Dirk Munk wrote in message <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl>...
>
>Rick Smith wrote:
[...]
>>  The only precaution one should take before
>> accessing an input buffer is that the record was sucessfully
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>successfull.

Consider the following code:

read input-file next
    at end
        move "no" to more-records
end-read
inspect numeric-display-1 converting ...
...
move input-record to ws-record
.

This was my second suggestion to Pat. The one to which DD
objected.

After the 'end-read', the file status could be anything. The
expected cases are either "00" or "10"; that is, successful read
or end of file. The program will not be terminated on end of file;
but there are circumstances with IBM mainframe COBOL where
the 'inspect' statements might modify locations in memory
that are not within the file or record buffer. This can cause
unpredictable behavior. The code beginning with the first
'inspect' through the 'move' should be within an 'if' that
checks for a successful read, normally 'input-file-status = "00"'
would suffice (though other additional status values may be
appropriate). Also checking for 'more-records not = "no"'
and allowing the program to crash on other errors might be
acceptable.

Howver, not every program "should" be allowed to crash.
With PCs, for example, it is most important to provide an
understandable error message for the user and for support.
Also, there are times when knowing the file status can allow
the programmer to take action which prevents the program
from crashing and repairs or recovers from damaged or
corrupt files.

There are some who suggest using only the file status and
not using 'at end' or 'invalid'. One reason for doing this has
to do with the meaning of 'not at end' and 'not invalid'. The
instructions after these 'not' phrases will be executed whether
the access was successful or failed in a way that did not
cause the 'at end' or 'invalid' to be taken. Using the 'not'
phrase as an indication of success is ambiguous! In these
cases, specifying file status and not using declaratives,
'at end', and 'invalid' provides the tightest control over how
the program will respond to the unexpected.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3828AA5D.4FC34E54@home.nl>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <807e24$1grt$1@news.hitter.net> <bMNV3.414$e41.2995@newsfeed.slurp.net> <808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <809nqu$2pf2$1@news.hitter.net>`

```


Rick Smith wrote:
> 
> Dirk Munk wrote in message <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl>...
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> or end of file. 

I see your point, but it should be 10. The statement ( not the read !! )
was executed succesfull because the "at end" clause was there to resolve
a specific kind of unsuccesfull read. 

The program will not be terminated on end of file;
> but there are circumstances with IBM mainframe COBOL where
> the 'inspect' statements might modify locations in memory
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> acceptable.

I agree, but this all depends on how the compiler is constructed. IF the
specifications of the compiler clearly state that after executing a
unsuccesfull read the record area is no longer valid, then of course one
should not use it anymore and construct the program accordingly.
Otherwise the 'old' record data should still be valid. 

> 
> Howver, not every program "should" be allowed to crash.
> With PCs, for example, it is most important to provide an
> understandable error message for the user and for support.

True, but the program will crash with all kind of information about why
it crashed and at what line number in the source etc.  

> Also, there are times when knowing the file status can allow
> the programmer to take action which prevents the program
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> the program will respond to the unexpected.

But this is impossible with the compiler I use (Digital / Compaq -
OpenVMS). Any read or write that will not end with a status 00 will
activate the declaratives, or if these are not present the program will
crash. The exceptions on this rule are of course "invalid" and "at end"
clauses. But if I try to make a program without declaratives, and I
program a read without a "at end" or "invalid" clause, the compiler will
give an error and no object will be produced.

Every status beginning with 0 is succesfull in principle, but even for a
status 02 (duplicate key on a alternate index after a write/rewrite ) I
have to make provisions in the declaratives because those are called if
it is not a status invalid (and that clause is used !) or success (00).
AFAIK this behaviour will be changed in the next Cobol standard. 

Regards.

Dirk Munk


> ------------------
> Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

Dirk Munk wrote in message <3828AA5D.4FC34E54@home.nl>...
>
>Rick Smith wrote:
[...]
>> There are some who suggest using only the file status and
>> not using 'at end' or 'invalid'. [...]
>
>But this is impossible with the compiler I use (Digital / Compaq -
>OpenVMS). [...]

Life is seldom fair! ;-)

It has been possible as an extension with some compilers
including IBM and Micro Focus for a long time.

In any case, I am not suggesting that I think it a good idea
for everyone; only that some like it and I have some
understanding why.

Rick Smith

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 9)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3828D089.B63B8792@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <807e24$1grt$1@news.hitter.net> <bMNV3.414$e41.2995@newsfeed.slurp.net> <808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <809nqu$2pf2$1@news.hitter.net> <3828AA5D.4FC34E54@home.nl>`

```
If you have specified a STATUS CODE for the file, the declaratives
should not be executed as I recall the standard.  Unfortunately some one
was brain dead when specifying the standard so that technically you need
AT END and INVALID KEY clauses even if you specify STATUS CODE.  The
general intent of the status code is that all errors are trapped and
presented to the next instruction after the I-O instruction in the form
of a non-zero status code.  COMPAQ may be enforcing the AT END/INVALID
KEY requirement but you should not need DECLARATIVES for any file with
an associated STATUS CODE.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca

Dirk Munk wrote:
> 
> Rick Smith wrote:
…[99 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Rick Smith
```

wrote:

>If you have specified a STATUS CODE for the file, the declaratives
>should not be executed as I recall the standard. 

You need (Presently) either declaratives or invalid key.  Of the PC
compilers, Fujitsu enforces it, Realia and MicroFocus do not.  I don't
know about others.  This is lifted in the next standard.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From

_(thread depth: 10)_

- **From:** Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382926CD.60E27B32@home.nl>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <807e24$1grt$1@news.hitter.net> <bMNV3.414$e41.2995@newsfeed.slurp.net> <808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <809g3l$2kv7$1@news.hitter.net> <38288AB9.957B57FB@bart.nl> <809nqu$2pf2$1@news.hitter.net> <3828AA5D.4FC34E54@home.nl> <3828D089.B63B8792@nbnet.nb.ca>`

```


Clark Morris wrote:
> 
> If you have specified a STATUS CODE for the file, the declaratives
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> an associated STATUS CODE.

Maybe, but I'm glad the Compaq compiler does !! It means that it is not
left to programmer to check every read/write, but that the compiler
forces the programmer to make provisions for every single non-success
error he expects to find. However I also saw programs where the
programmer had only had one line in the declaratives for every single
file, and that consisted out of "Use after error procedure on
this-file.", effectively disabling the declaratives. 
Compaq uses the declaratives as a kind of automatic GOSUB (sorry,
PERFORM :)) ) routine for checking every single read or write that
doesn't end in success (00) or doesn't have "at end" or "invalid"
clauses. So with the example of status 02 I mentioned, the declaratives
could look like this:

This-file error section.
	use after error procedure on this-file.
p00.
	if not file-stat = "02"
		display "error on this-file"
		stop run.
p99.
Other-file error section .....

In case the status was "02", the program will execute these lines in the
declaratives, and subsequently return to where it was in the program.
Of course now you can still check for the file status in the normal body
of the program.

By the way, it seems that every "0x" status is considered success in the
present compiler, so that this example is not quite accurate anymore.

 
  

> 
> Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca
…[103 more quoted lines elided]…
> > > Rick Smith
```

>... which is something done by fools, dolts, simpletons, churls, lackwits
>and chuckleheaded Janitors-Who-Masquerads-As-Programmers who have not
>learned the Commandment of:


Thanks for attributing all these term to me...  because I have been using the locate mode
ever since I heard of it (Assembler, some 20 years ago).


What is the difference between a buffer set up by the system, or one set up by me?  Oh,
that buffer is gone after I closed the file?  So what, after closing the file the file
variables, wherever they are, are "taboo" <spelling?>

There was only one reason to use locate mode instead of move mode:  That was in CICS 1.5,
when locate mode acquired a STRING in CICS and might block out the file from usage, but
this was many summers and winters ago.  No such issue any more.

Pray, tell,  what is so BAD about locate mode?  What have I been doing (and teaching)
wrong all these years?

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Signed numeric fields in Personal MFCobol

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<H90W3.35357$oa2.123445@iad-read.news.verio.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <382884be.44730819@news2.ibm.net>`

```
In article <382884be.44730819@news2.ibm.net>,
Volker Bandke <vbandke@bsp-gmbh.com> wrote:
>On Tue, 09 Nov 1999 17:04:09 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>ever since I heard of it (Assembler, some 20 years ago).

And I worked *so* hard to describe *you*, exactly, so precisely... Great
Efforts can have rewards!

[die kleine snippigkeit]

>Pray, tell,  what is so BAD about locate mode?  What have I been doing (and teaching)
>wrong all these years?

You have been Tempting Fate and Leading Newbies Into Error, of course...
consider:

Arithmetic manipulations in file buffers can have unpredictable results.

That which causes unpredictable results is best avoided.

Arithmetic results are a subset of data manipulations.

Eliminate the set and all subsets are eliminated.

Thou Shalt Not Perform Data Manipulations In File-Buffers.

Simple, no?

DD
```

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38290917.1313779@news2.ibm.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <808vvp$2c18$1@news.hitter.net> <duYV3.35289$oa2.121362@iad-read.news.verio.net> <382884be.44730819@news2.ibm.net> <H90W3.35357$oa2.123445@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

On Tue, 09 Nov 1999 21:15:19 GMT, docdwarf@clark.net () wrote:

>You have been Tempting Fate and Leading Newbies Into Error, of course...
>consider:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
No, because this is a proof of the old GIGO principle:  GArbage in - Garbage out.

When the first assertion is wrong, the chain of reasoning is wrong as well.


If I read a record into a buffer, do some arithmetic operations on its field(s), why
should these operations fail? Or, if I build a record in the buffer, do some arithmetics
on it, then write.  What could go wrong here? Could you give an example?  An do NOT cite
an example where data is processed either
	before open 
	after reading another record
	(or after writing this record)
	after close
which is never valid

It is ASCII spaces, signed positive; X'20' (ASCII) = X'40 (EBCDIC)

>If this is not the expected number, I would not fault the usage
>of the file buffer, but would strongly recommend to open an error report, APAR, or
>whatever it is called in the WANG world, because that would be a COMPILER problem

You asked for an example of the problem and I supplied one, as best as my
porous memory would allow.  There are, it seems, at least two courses of
action when one encounters such a difficulty:

Yours:  Open an error-report on what appears to be a compiler problem.
(The results of such actions are unpredictable)

Mine: Remember the Commandment of 'Thou Shalt NOT Manipulate Data In The
File-Buffers', follow this Commandment, get the proper results and get the
job done.

DD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Signed numeric fields in Personal MFCobol

_(thread depth: 12)_

- **From:** docdwarf@clark.net ()
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00

The ONLY real problem is if you manipulate the record AFTER a write

recently in fact (well nearly a year ago now).  I still remember it.

#### ↳ Re: Signed numeric fields in Personal MFCobol

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4nsV3.159$Av4.6509@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
>We students are having a problem with Microfocus v2.0.02 Personal Cobol.
>The data file we are using (which came with the compiler, so it should be
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>us has found yet.  I looked everywhere I know where to look.


First I need some more specifications:
What do you mean by an embedded sign? Leading or trailing?
You state the offending character is an "O". What is the hexadecimal and
binary representation, on your system?
    - on mainframe compilers, several different nibbles can indicate a
negative value

Lacking any clue as to a problem with 2 diffent compilers, especially with
sign problems:
1 - code a program to write to a file the values 0 through 9 both negative
and positive values defined the same as in the original offending field
2 - compile the program to different executables under both compilers
3 - run both executables
4 - examine hexadecimal representations of both output
5 - using comparison, geuss at the answer
```

#### ↳ Re: Signed numeric fields in Personal MFCobol

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-09T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<%s5W3.255$Up1.2998@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Hi,

I sure appreciate all the advice I've gotten about MF Cobol and signed
numeric fields.  And it was really interesting to read the debate concerning
"READ - INTO"!!

I can manually put the ACSII code into my data file and get a negative
number.   But I can't get the INSPECT statement to work .  I suppose because
the program won't even let the record into the program, it won't let it into
a numeric field.  I guess maybe I could read it into an alpha-numeric, do
the INSPECT and convert and then move it into WS field??   Maybe something
to play with.......for now our teacher doesn't mind, matter of fact you guys
found the answer before he did.  He looked up the ASCII codes for imbedded
negatives after I showed him the answers you gave.  He's still waiting for
Micro Focus to answer his question.....

Thanks,
Pat
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Signed numeric fields in Personal MFCobol

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IhnW3.1914$Av4.38777@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <%s5W3.255$Up1.2998@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
>I can manually put the ACSII code into my data file and get a negative
>number.   But I can't get the INSPECT statement to work .  I suppose
because
>the program won't even let the record into the program, it won't let it
into
>a numeric field.  I guess maybe I could read it into an alpha-numeric, do


You should be able to redefine the PIC 9 field as a PIC X field of the same
length. Do your INSPECT REPLACING on the PIC X fields. As long as you don't
reference the invalid numeric field before the offending nibble is
corrected, you should be OK.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Signed numeric - THANKS!

- **From:** "Pat" <patp@cpinternet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-11T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<78AW3.2240$Up1.12305@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <%s5W3.255$Up1.2998@newsfeed.slurp.net> <IhnW3.1914$Av4.38777@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`

```
Gumbo (!?) replied to my ongoing problem:

>You should be able to redefine the PIC 9 field as a PIC X field of the same
>length. Do your INSPECT REPLACING on the PIC X fields. As long as you don't
>reference the invalid numeric field before the offending nibble is
>corrected, you should be OK.

Yes!  With the help of people on this newsgroup the problem is solved!!  My
teacher was very interested in the method I used (you all) to get an answer.
He came up with the step I missed (Gumbo's step, above) and all is well.
Luckily, all the fields that needed inspection were in a row in the record,
so a group field took care of them all in one step.  I learned alot.

Thanks again!!!

Pat
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Signed numeric fields in Personal MFCobol

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80cq07$1ej3$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<lvoV3.1621$Xc.11816@newsfeed.slurp.net> <%s5W3.255$Up1.2998@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
The problem might be related to the directive IOCONV.
This directive affects the behavior of READ INTO and
WRITE FROM.

You might try inserting the statement

$SET NOIOCONV

beginning in column 7 as the first line of the program to
see if the behavior changes. If I understand the directive
correctly, it will cause the READ INTO to bypass certain
tests; such as, the sign check on the numeric fields. The
fields with the EBCDIC signs still need conversion; but
the conversion can be done in working-storage. Otherwise,
you will need to replace the READ INTO with the
combination READ, the INSPECTs, and MOVE.
------------------
Rick Smith

Pat wrote in message
[...]
>I can manually put the ACSII code into my data file and get a negative
>number.   But I can't get the INSPECT statement to work .  I suppose
because
>the program won't even let the record into the program, it won't let it
into
>a numeric field.  I guess maybe I could read it into an alpha-numeric, do
>the INSPECT and convert and then move it into WS field??   Maybe something
>to play with.......for now our teacher doesn't mind, matter of fact you
guys
>found the answer before he did.  He looked up the ASCII codes for imbedded
>negatives after I showed him the answers you gave.  He's still waiting for
>Micro Focus to answer his question.....
```

#### ↳ Re: please help me

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991108102642.02249.00002311@ng-cs1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<3825f17a_1@nebula.superior.net>`

```
Our class did a sub-program project and almost everyone used CALLs to an ENTRY
point in the sub-module.  They drove themselves crazy.

I liked the model in our book that passed a function code.  It was a lot easier
to keep track of what I was doing.

Right before each place in your main program where you want to branch to the
sub-program, set FUNCTION-CODE (in your main working storage) to a letter
representing the routine you will want to do, i.e. 'I' for init-routine, 'P'
for print report, 'C' for close files, etc.  Pass that code in your USING
clause.

In the sub-module, you will have a mainline that looks something like.

IF FUNCTION-CODE = 'I'
   PERFORM 000-INIT
ELSE IF FUNCTION CODE = 'P'
   PERFORM 100-PRINT
ELSE IF FUNCTION CODE = 'C'
   PERFORM 200-CLOSE.
GOBACK.

If you are using COBOL II, you can use an EVALUATE statement to code the above
logic.

Those of us who used function code logic in our sub-module had much less
difficulty in debugging.

RoadRaat
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: please help me

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<l6nW3.1910$Av4.38458@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3825f17a_1@nebula.superior.net> <19991108102642.02249.00002311@ng-cs1.aol.com>`

```
>Our class did a sub-program project and almost everyone used CALLs to an
ENTRY
>point in the sub-module.  They drove themselves crazy.


To my knowledge, and belief, entry points are a violation of structured
programming. I have worked at 8 or so different shops/companies and only one
of them used multiple entry points.

Imagine having to figure out what source code to look at for a CALL
statement if multiple entry points are used? One shop I worked at did this
and I had to write a program to generate a cross reference file to determine
which source code module to look at to track a simple call statement. The
job to scan the members ran for hours. Imagine if the source code was spread
over multiple PDSs/Directories? You then would not only have to create a
cross reference, but a precedence list, yet still scanning every memeber in
every PDS/Directory. Even then you would have to know the relation between
the PDSs/Directories and the load module PDS/Directories. Then you would
have to know what search path was used at the time of the error.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: please help me

- **From:** "Unbeliever" <popsider@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-10T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80d4gk$nrk$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3825f17a_1@nebula.superior.net> <19991108102642.02249.00002311@ng-cs1.aol.com> <l6nW3.1910$Av4.38458@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`

```
With static linking, multiple entry points were "OK". They aren't as common
now though, as dynamic linking proves far easier to maintain.

BUT, the rationale was - and it applied only to data-orientated programming
methodologies like JSP - a sub module dealing with a single output file
would have 3 entry points.
Entry point 1 would be the init process, open file etc.
Entry point 2 would be the detail record processing
Entry point 3 would be end processing of trailer etc.

But as problem-orientated logic still dominates, this really shouldn't
apply.
people fail to design their logic correctly and have to use a single
sub-module
for disparate processes.

Just remember to use REUS when linking programs, and there is no discernible
performance problem - that I have seen.

- I once supported a system that was a highly complex reporting tool. It
dynamically
coded COBOL & then compiled & linked it. When making changes to the skeleton
code, you had to statically link 11 levels of module. If you got just 1
module out of
sequence you were hosed, and probably took a whole day to start again. This
may
have influenced the decision to axe the system!


Gumbo <a@a.com> wrote in message
news:l6nW3.1910$Av4.38458@typhoon.columbus.rr.com...
> >Our class did a sub-program project and almost everyone used CALLs to an
> ENTRY
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> programming. I have worked at 8 or so different shops/companies and only
one
> of them used multiple entry points.
>
> Imagine having to figure out what source code to look at for a CALL
> statement if multiple entry points are used? One shop I worked at did this
> and I had to write a program to generate a cross reference file to
determine
> which source code module to look at to track a simple call statement. The
> job to scan the members ran for hours. Imagine if the source code was
spread
> over multiple PDSs/Directories? You then would not only have to create a
> cross reference, but a precedence list, yet still scanning every memeber
in
> every PDS/Directory. Even then you would have to know the relation between
> the PDSs/Directories and the load module PDS/Directories. Then you would
> have to know what search path was used at the time of the error.
>
>
```

#### ↳ Re: please help me

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8050f9$rga$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<3825f17a_1@nebula.superior.net>`

```
I am such a friendly guy. I will help you, but you must tell me about the
project. I could easily send you thousand lines of code, but we haven't got
the time, do we?

You say your project involves use of sub programs. Do you know what the sub
programs was meant to do?

Start with the main-program, and whenever you like to call a sub program,
make a statement like: Call Sub-1 using Sub-1-linkage,  Call Sub-2 using
Sub-2-linkage and so on.

Define Sub-1-linkage, Sub-2-linkage.........Sub-n-linkage in
working-storage.

Make a comment in the main program about the functionality you expect from
the sub programs you call. It is nice to have later on.

Don't bother about the sub programs until you have a well structured main
program. Then you make the sub programs. Sub-1 will have a linkage section,
where Sub-1-linkage is defined
and you will put in 'using Sub-1-linkage' after 'Procedure Division'. In
fact, all the sub programs will have linkage section and using clause.

Therefore, instead of collecting code, you'll waste a lot of time to
understand, you should begin to make your programs. Let's see how fast you
are - we will be here for you, if your program fails, or if you need
comments on the programs.

Keep up the spirit

Ib Tanding


Mark Doesburg skrev i meddelelsen <3825f17a_1@nebula.superior.net>...
>I have a project due which involves the use of sub programs. the example in
>the book sucks and the Prof. isn't much more help. I am not looking for any
>answers but if anyone could please send me some sample source code
including
>sub programs I would greatly appreciate it.
>thanks in advance
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: please help me

- **From:** "Gumbo" <a@a.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ZBrV3.138$Av4.4319@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<3825f17a_1@nebula.superior.net>`

```
>I have a project due which involves the use of sub programs. the example in
>the book sucks and the Prof. isn't much more help. I am not looking for any
>answers but if anyone could please send me some sample source code
including
>sub programs I would greatly appreciate it.
>thanks in advance
>mark creative@superior.net

Since your request lacks any kind of specifics here is a very simple
example. This demonstrates simple operations performed on 3 different
variables. The linkage section indicates parameters passed to a subprogram.
Passed paramteres are indicated also in the USING CLAUSE which indicates all
01 level parameters defined in LINKAGE SECTION which are to be used by that
ENTRY POINT. ENTRY POINTS other than the default, which is used in this
example, should never be used by a beginner, amature or expert as they add
extreme complication to tracking errors in programs/subprograms. NOTICE the
field definition of PARM-2 is defined differently in the subprogram than the
calling program. This shows that a redefinition of the linked data can occur
as long as the redefined data is the same byte size of the pased data.
Certain compilers allow for inconsistent definitions, but that should never
be used. This is an excellent place to use a COPYBOOK with a single 01
containing all sub parameters to be used.

NOTE: the code which is actually here is correct. Missing DIVISIONS and
SECTIONS may be needed. I did not run this example through a compiler.


IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. MAINPRG.
DATA DIVISION.
WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
01 PARM-1 PIC X(08) VALUE '11111111'.
01 PARM-2 PIC X(08) VALUE '22222222'.
01 PARM-3 PIC 9(08) VALUE '33333333'.
PROCEDURE DIVISION.
0000-MAIN-PROCESSING.
    DISPLAY 'OUTGOING PARMS:'
    DISPLAY 'PARM-1 ' PARM-1
    DISPLAY 'PARM-2 ' PARM-2
    DISPLAY 'PARM-3 ' PARM-3
    CALL 'SUBPGM1' USING PARM-1 PARM-2 PARM-3.
    DISPLAY 'RETURNED PARMS:'
    DISPLAY 'PARM-1 ' PARM-1
    DISPLAY 'PARM-2 ' PARM-2
    DISPLAY 'PARM-3 ' PARM-3
    GOBACK.
    STOP-RUN.
END-PROGRAM.

IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
PROGRAM-ID. SUBPGM1.
DATA DIVISION.
LINKAGE SECTION.
01 PARM-1 PIC X(08).
01 PARM-2 PIC 9(08).
01 PARM-3 PIC 9(08).
PROCEDURE DIVISION USING PARM-1, PARM-2, PARM-3.
0000-MAIN-PROCESSING.
    DISPLAY 'INCOMING PARMS:'
    DISPLAY 'PARM-1 ' PARM-1
    DISPLAY 'PARM-2 ' PARM-2
    DISPLAY 'PARM-3 ' PARM-3
    MOVE '00000000' TO PARM-1
    ADD 11111111 TO PARM-2
    ADD 22222222 TO PARM-3
GOBACK.
STOP-RUN.
END-PROGRAM.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: please help me

- **From:** "William M. Klein" <wmklein@nospam.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<805jom$9l1$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<3825f17a_1@nebula.superior.net> <ZBrV3.138$Av4.4319@typhoon.columbus.rr.com>`

```
Just because it is "easy to miss" for new programmers, the match between the
calling and called programs is by "order" and not by name or anything else.
For example, if the CALLING program uses,

   Call "ABC" using Parm1 Parm2 Parm3

Then it is the ORDER of the parameters in the PROCEDURE DIVISION USING
statement (and not in Linkage Section or by name) that does the matching.
Therefore, if the called subprogram (ABC) had the following:

Linkage Section.
01  FieldC  Pic X.
01  FieldB  Pic X.
01  FieldA  Pic X.
Procedure Division Using FieldA FieldC FieldB.

Then Parm1 in the calling program would be matched to FieldA in the called
program.
          Parm2 in the calling program would be matched to FieldC in the
called program
          Parm3 in the calling program would be matched to FieldB in the
called program.

NOTE: it is a "nice" (recommended) coding habit to get use to, to put your
fields in the same order in Linkage Section as they appear in the USING
statement.  Using the same name, however, is not nearly as common. (For
example, some programmers regularly prefix fields in Working-Storage with
"WS-" while they use "LS-" for Linkage Section items.)

It is - the PROGRAMMERS responsibility to insure that the appropriate fields
are defined identically in the CALLING and CALLED programs.  (There are times
that you want to do "tricky" things where they don't match - but that
shouldn't be the case in a "beginning" assignment).

It is also important to understand that BY DEFAULT the parameters are passed
"BY REFERENCE" which means the subprogram actually addresses the storage in
the calling program.  You can also specify BY CONTENT which means that the
subprogram only gets to "play with" a COPY of the data in the original
program (and any change that occurs in the called program does NOT get
reflected back to the calling program).  Your compiler MAY support other ways
of "passing data" - but again, these shouldn't be needed in a "beginning
assignment".

Finally, you should know that any data in the CALLED (subprogram) is (by
default) found in "last used state" when you call the same program for a 2nd
thru Nth time.  If you do NOT want that, you can override it by using the
"INITIAL" attribute on the PROGRAM-ID paragraph of your called subprogram OR
by using the CANCEL verb in the calling program.  Probably you will NOT need
to do this in a "beginning assignment" - but you do need to know what happens
to Working-Storage data in that subprogram - if you call it more than once.

P.S.  If this assignment goes so well (is so easy) you may also want to look
in your reference material for the EXTERNAL attribute for "extra credit".
But this is not the "normal" way of passing data and should only be used in
cases where it is either required or is "commonly used" as a shop standard.
```

1. I have an inventory tracking system programmed in Cobol. Not suer if it's
y2k compliant or not. It''s a custom job, no documentation etc.

Are there any tools availble to test for Y2K compliance?

2. Are there any tools available to export this data into another format
that could be imported into some sort of off-the-shelf application?

(i.e. Quick Books Pro or Peachtree) I'm not sure what the best product would
be. Suggestions are welcome.

Excel. The product is not a pattern matching tool - it aims for usage.

facilities, meant for Y2k.

Bill Morrison wrote:
> 
> 1. I have an inventory tracking system programmed in Cobol. Not suer if it's
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> that could be imported into some sort of off-the-shelf application?

A generic parser like ParseRat (http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm) might be able 
to help you convert to something else.  If your data is 2-digit-years it can convert to 
4-digit-years based on a user defined "window".  e.g. you can tell it that any 2 
digit year below 20 is post 2000, so  09 gets converted to 2009 but 21 gets converted to 
1921.
```

## Thread: linking in version 4.0 mf cobol — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### linking in version 4.0 mf cobol

- **From:** MARK Lavine <gcsii@erols.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-07T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38264419.4A4B@erols.com>`

```
I get an error(11) missing c runtime library when i use the cbllink
utility in version 4.0

If anyone knows what is causing this error please let me know

Thanks
Mark Lavine
```

#### ↳ Re: linking in version 4.0 mf cobol

- **From:** "Gael Wilson" <gael.wilson@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-08T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8067r2$sum$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38264419.4A4B@erols.com>`

```
Mark,


MARK Lavine wrote in message <38264419.4A4B@erols.com>...
>I get an error(11) missing c runtime library when i use the cbllink
>utility in version 4.0

CBLLINK is looking for MSVCRT.LIB and cannot find it on the path pointed to
by the LIB environment variable. Make sure you have that set up correctly ie
to your COBOL\LIB directory and everything should then work okay.
```
