<a id="t-a2af4757c7"></a>
## Thread: Open Source COBOL — 31 msgs

### Open Source COBOL

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I want to open up a discussion of the possibilities and problems of
Open Source (OS) COBOL.  Although I have found some public domain COBOL
on the Internet, I have not yet found any OS COBOL and I would like to
know why.

1. Open Source

For the purpose of this discussion "open source" means the formal
definition (http://www.opensource.org/osd.html) given by the Open
Source Organization.

3. License and Copyright

OS is not the same as public domain.  OS is copyrighted and licensed.
The most popular OS license is the GNU General Public License or GPL
(http://www.opensource.org/licenses/gpl-license.html) although there
are other possibilities (http://www.opensource.org/licenses/).

4. Making Money from OS

This has been a much-discussed subject.  I would recommend a look at
http://www.opensource.org/for-hackers.html.

5. Modifications

One of the problems with the GPL is that section 2 (a) requires you to
make public any modifications that you make to the original code.  This
works for system software or general utilities where any confidential
information is contained in data files.  However, COBOL programming is
usually a process of encoding a company's policies and procedures so a
computer can carry them out.  These policies and procedures are
sensitive data and I don't know of a company that would want them made
public.

How would this condition of the GPL have to be changed to make OS
practical for COBOL?  I feel that the principle is important because it
provides a major incentive for programmers to create OS in the first
place.  You get back all the enhancements to your original work and a
lot of knowledge about the possibilities of your code.

One possibility would be to allow the private use of modified code
within a single organization but require publication if it moves
outside that organization.  However, defining "organization" becomes an
interesting problem when many of us work for huge international
companies with many different entities.

6. Fair Use

If you were the programmer who had invested hundreds or thousands of
your valuable hours in creating OS software, what would you consider to
be the fair use of your software?

Having a company make money by selling the results of your effort is
obviously unacceptable and is forbidden by the GPL.  But to what extent
would you accept an organization keeping their enhancements of your
software private?  In fact, what constitutes an enhancement to the
original product compared to a modification to make the product work
for a specific company?

For example, suppose you create a point-of-sale system as OS.  A
company modifies your software to make it web based.  Is this a private
modification for that company because it happens to do business over
the web or a general enhancement that should be made public?

7. Standards

I don't see anything about the use of standard forms of a programming
language.  However, I would assume that the spirit of OS would
encourage the production of the most portable code.  This would mean
sticking with the published standards and avoiding any extension
provided by a specific compiler.

In particular, this would mean, for the moment, giving up OO and GUI
based user interfaces.  However, should you also give up ODBC or CICS
because Microsoft and IBM control them?

The report writer and screen section are a standard part of COBOL but
they are not available on IBM mainframes that are still the most common
environment for COBOL.  Should these also be avoided for proper OS?
```

#### ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382ee7c5.45409691@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Sun, 14 Nov 1999 15:53:59 GMT, Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com> wrote:

>I want to open up a discussion of the possibilities and problems of
>Open Source (OS) COBOL.  Although I have found some public domain COBOL
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>1. Open Source

Ed, 

There are at least 3 different Open Source COBOL projects.

ETK comes to mind first.  This is not a compiler, but the whole
library of source is Open Source, complying with the open source
document you site.

Second - GNU COBOL to C translator.  Designed to create code in C from
COBOL source that can be compiled by the GNU C Compiler.

Third - a new effort by the Linux community to create a COBOL 74, then
a COBOL 85 compiler for Linux.

The first two are mentioned in the FAQ, and the second may soon be.

---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382f56eb_3@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <382ee7c5.45409691@news1.attglobal.net> <__BX3.2192$j%2.210@cabot.ops.attcanada.net>`

```

> The ETK website mentions Open Source but the downloads do not contain a
> license agreement or copyright statements.  I believe that makes them
public
> domain rather than Open Source.


Ed, why is the more restrictive agreement and statements important to
you? ETK is open source in the sense that we encourage people to
work on the sources. The changes won't become part of ETK unless
we have determined that they should be. I agree that that is strictly
speaking not OS, but we prefer the more lax interpretation.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80oopc$prb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <382ee7c5.45409691@news1.attglobal.net> <__BX3.2192$j%2.210@cabot.ops.attcanada.net> <382f56eb_3@news3.prserv.net>`

```
In article <382f56eb_3@news3.prserv.net>,
  "Leif Svalgaard" <leif@ibm.net> wrote:
>
> > The ETK website mentions Open Source but the downloads do not
contain a
> > license agreement or copyright statements.  I believe that makes
them
> public
> > domain rather than Open Source.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> speaking not OS, but we prefer the more lax interpretation.

I think that the restrictive agreements are critical because they
provide recognition for the programmers who did the work and prevent
abuse.  You can do anything you want with public domain code -
including stripping out any reference to the real authors and selling
it as a commercial product. I don't see how you can expect significant
contribution from the community of COBOL programmers without at least
the guarantee of recognition.

ETK is almost unique in providing a complete application as PD COBOL
code.  If PD was acceptable to the community at large, I would expect
there would be more PD COBOL code than OS C code because there are far
more us. As it is, the PD COBOL code is only a small fraction of OS C
code.

BTW, how frequently do you get contributions to ETK from other
programmers?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382FE6E3.7BC160D7@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <382ee7c5.45409691@news1.attglobal.net> <__BX3.2192$j%2.210@cabot.ops.attcanada.net> <382f56eb_3@news3.prserv.net>`

```
Leif Svalgaard wrote:
> 
> > The ETK website mentions Open Source but the downloads do not
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> speaking not OS, but we prefer the more lax interpretation.

This is NOT public domain is explicitly stated in the works
somewhere.  It is implicitly copyright to the author.  They must
release the work for it to be anything else.

By the way inclusion of changes into the body of the work is
implicitly part of the Open Source methodology.  If the author deems
it useful then it is in otherwise another strand may start using your
code as a base.  Evolution applies and the better solution wins. 
Linux had 5 disk methods and now you only see EXT2 used.

Can we have a formal statement of copyright (or copyleft) from ETK. 
Grow your own if you desire this is also very open source.

Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<382F1C8C.3C6FC4D7@home.com>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Ed Milne wrote:
> 
> I want to open up a discussion of the possibilities and problems of
…[78 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Ed,

A real nice dream and one I am very interested in. But so far as I am
concerned, what Thane has outlined isn't going anywhere fast. I've just
found out that Laura Tweedy(?) the Project Manager(?) for GNU COBOL has
'resigned'; so where does that leave that particular project.

It seems that the various GNUs/Open Source, or whatever you care to call
them, are for the most part Linux-based using C as the VM. Allowing for
the current court saga to go on for many years yet, as Time Magazine
pointed out, Windows is still the major OS (90+ %) while little 'ole
Linux represents about 3%. That isn't to suggest that Linux is a loser,
but an awful lot of Windows-designed software has to be converted for
Linux, (AND user-friendly front-end), before consumers are going to
switch to the new upstart.

If you don't think this a problem - a UK and US oil company merged - the
Calgary branches were using ProductX-97; some nut in London decided the
new group would not use ProductX-97, nor 95, nor 94 but that the whole
corporation would standardize on ProductX-90 !). So my question - what
is the point of concentrating on Linux when an Open Source for Windows
is the current need ?

If you check on the 'GNUs', GNU-COBOL, COBCY, Tiny COBOL etc. they are
for the most part, in a transitional state - "Not yet finished, but you
can give it a try", and haven't taken into account 'COBOL-97' standards. 
(Also in grand isolation are mainframe WATCOB from U. of Waterloo, not
supported, and the COBOL650). What is the point of just a compiler, even
if it provides 'all'; we now have many of the popular compilers wrapped
into an IDE with source editing, screen-designing, compiling, linking
etc. Open Source that offers less than we have come to expect from
Fujitsu, Merant, AcuCobol, Liant etc. is a non-sequitur.

For Open Source to work it has to be a real team-effort, and I would
suggest, with lots of input from people in this NewsGroup who are
'code-oriented'. The only name currently that springs to mind is Tim 
Josling (Australia) who is involved with Tiny COBOL I believe.
Unfortunately, Tim 'hates' all the new Reserved Words, which only add to
his problems of compiler designing. To be blunt, the current progress
appears to me to be in the category 'Amateur Hour'. (No, don't ask me to
contribute, I'm not a 'coder').

Just to throw a spanner/wrench in the works, I would define Open Source
slightly differently :-

(a) Open Source compiler - which fits your definition above
(b) Open Source examples - a library of source code showing how to do
    things in COBOL, like Bill has included for 'Dates' in the FAQ.

I don't think the concept of an Open Source compiler violates corporate
confidentially as you posed - the compiler needs to contain code to
handle all COBOL syntax, in general terms - and not related to the needs
of a particular user corporation. You cited a POS system as an example -
this is a specific application, and I don't see it as a part of Open
Source.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80nhh9$v07$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <382F1C8C.3C6FC4D7@home.com>`

```

> A real nice dream and one I am very interested in. But so far as I am
> concerned, what Thane has outlined isn't going anywhere fast. I've
just
> found out that Laura Tweedy(?) the Project Manager(?) for GNU COBOL
has
> 'resigned'; so where does that leave that particular project.

Part of my frustration is that everyone seems to relate Open Source
only to systems software.  I am interested in OS code written in COBOL
rather than compilers.

[snip]

> Just to throw a spanner/wrench in the works, I would define Open
Source
> slightly differently :-
>
> (a) Open Source compiler - which fits your definition above
> (b) Open Source examples - a library of source code showing how to do
>     things in COBOL, like Bill has included for 'Dates' in the FAQ.

The dates procedures are the type of thing that I am interested in.
However, this code is not copyrighted or licensed and is therefore
public domain. The key difference is that anyone making enhancements to
OS code would be required to make the enhancements public under the
same conditions as the original.  Thus the code can grow and improve as
a cooperative effort.

> I don't think the concept of an Open Source compiler violates
corporate
> confidentially as you posed - the compiler needs to contain code to
> handle all COBOL syntax, in general terms - and not related to the
needs
> of a particular user corporation. You cited a POS system as an
example -
> this is a specific application, and I don't see it as a part of Open
> Source.

As I stated, there is no problem with system programming because
confidential information is only in the files.  The problem is to find
a way to make specific applications OS so that COBOL developers also
can benefit from OS.
```

```
Well, there will be at least one significant difference, while the Mac had

hardware. In addition it is worthwhile to mention that the Mac OS never have
been widely known for its multitasking capacity.

On the other side, when recalling all the problems IBM had with Win support
in their OS/2, with Microsoft continously introducing new cripples to the
windows system files, my guess is that Linux will have a though run to make
this in the long run.

Cheesle
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<_cUX3.361$DT3.3088@news4.mia>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <382F1C8C.3C6FC4D7@home.com> <VpHX3.23294$Hk.199596@news1.mia> <382f9d73.91926348@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Thane Hubbell wrote:
>
>Judson McClendon wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>quantity of software, it has to run under Windows.

Ah, but the situation now is very different from then. :-)  Around
1980 Apple was at the top of the PC heap.  But they started playing
hardball with dealers and customers.  I had part ownership in a
dealership at the time, and was well aware of what was going on.
Apple had made a pile marketing open systems.  If an engineer called
Apple in the early (Apple II) days, asking for information to design
an interface card, Apple was eager to help.  They knew that the more
stuff that was available for the Apple II, the more popular it would
be.  They were so right that IBM, a company that had *never, ever*
sold any open computer, when they were laying plans for their PC
division, took Apple as their model, because of Apple's success.
This literally stunned the industry.  History revealed what happened,
IBM ate everybody's lunch, *until they went with the closed PS/2.
In the meantime, Apple had decided to change tactics.  I don't know
why, but have always thought it was because of the declining
influence from Steve Wozniak, and the increasing power of Steve Jobs.
In succession, Apple released three closed systems; Apple III, Lisa,
Mac.  Apple III and Lisa *bombed*, Big Time.  I have heard that Apple
was left with more unsold Lisa computers in stock than they sold.  I
know we didn't order one.  And if it weren't for the almost fanatical
loyalty of the Apple fans, the Mac would have bitten the dust, and it
nearly did.  This loyalty came mostly, I believe, from their feelings
for the 'old' Apple that really listened to them.  The poor Appleites
didn't realize that Apple, under Steve Jobs, had become a very
different company, that didn't give a rip for what anybody but Steve
Jobs thought.  As it was, the Mac had so many boneheaded, intentional
restrictions that many loyal Apple users simply gave up before Apple
would admit their stupidity and address the problems.  When you
bought a new Mac, you soon discovered that if you loaded BASIC, you
had only *5k* free memory for BASIC programs, and the Mac was
*intentionally designed* so that the memory could not be expanded!
You also soon learned that, while everybody else was adding hard
drives to their PCs, Apple had carefully designed the Mac to make
that impossible for you.  When you set up your Mac, you quickly
found that you could not simply remove the floppy disk, unless the
Mac 'permitted' you to do so.  And these guys have the *gall* to
call the IBM crowd "Big Brother"?!?  Wow, not only so arrogant as
to think they know better that you what you want, they have the
brazen temerity to try and put the shoe on the other guy's foot!
When I saw all this stuff happening, I knew then that Apple was
going to come to no good from that sort of attitude.  Last year,
Apple had to be bailed out, to the tune of $350 million, and by
Apple's arch enemy Bill Gates, no less.  What irony!  To summarize,
the reason the Mac did not beat out Windows was to a large extent
because Apple (Jobs) was *stupid*, and Gates was smart.  And because
there was no definitive reason for people to switch to the Mac.

Now, the situation is very different with Linux.  Linux has no
Heavy Handed baggage as did Apple.  And, more importantly, there is
a definitive reason to go to Linux.  In fact, there are four very
good reasons to want Linux, that the Mac did not have.

1. Reliability.  Every single user of Windows has had good reason
to complain about the frequent unexplained crashes.  And Linux has
a reputation for incredible stability.

2. Cost.  Have you seen the new prices for Windows 2000?  Consider
companies that have hundreds or thousands of PCs, and the users who
can't afford, or doesn't want, to spend that kind of money.  Both
are going to save a bundle on Linux.

3. Microsoft has now become very Heavy Handed, like Apple.  I see a
real growing resentment against Microsoft.  Not just because they are
so successful, but because they force you to relearn a new interface
with almost every Windows release.  Microsoft rarely just adds new
features, they change the way old ones work so you must relearn how
to use them.  And your old, perfectly good software often does not
run on the next release.  Microsoft is *chock full* of very young
people who don't have enough experience to have learned the value of
stability.

4. The Mac users who bought PC emulation did so mostly because they
liked the Mac better, but had some PC software they needed to run.
They didn't really want Windows in the first place, it was only an
unpleasant necessity to them.  Hardware emulation often costs about
as much as buying another PC to run Windows, and software emulation
suffers serious performance loss.  If Linux gains a truly good
Windows emulator, there will be little or no cost, and probably a
performance gain because of Linux' efficiency.  If Linux is easy to
load, use and maintain, most PC users will have little reason not
to try Linux.  After all, Gates makes them learn a new interface for
Windows all the time, anyway. :-)

Does that make sense?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80oqvo$27mr$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <382F1C8C.3C6FC4D7@home.com> <VpHX3.23294$Hk.199596@news1.mia> <382f9d73.91926348@news1.attglobal.net> <_cUX3.361$DT3.3088@news4.mia>`

```

Judson McClendon wrote in message <_cUX3.361$DT3.3088@news4.mia>...
[...]
>Now, the situation is very different with Linux.  Linux has no
>Heavy Handed baggage as did Apple.

I am not so sure about that statement.

Some published test results show that a single-processor
computer running Linux will outperform an equivalent
configuration running Windows NT. Those results also
show that a four-processor computer running Linux will
underperform an equivalent Windows NT. A portion of
the performance loss was attibuted to the Apache Web
Server. I believe that some of the loss is also attibutable
to the Linux kernel.

Also, I read a report concerning memory performance with
LinuxPPC. The report mentioned there is a requirement
that any port of the Linux kernel must meet certain
requirements that pertain to Intel processors. I understood
other statements to mean that performance could be
improved if more flexibility were permitted in porting the
kernel.

Elsewhere, I read that Linus Torvalds controls the Linux
kernel development effort.

Selected quotes from: Optimizing the Idle Task and Other
MMU Tricks, Cort Dougan, Paul Mackerras, and Victor
Yodaiken, Department of Computer Science, New Mexico
Institute of Technology, Socorro, NM 87801

"Our optimization effort was constrained by a requirement
that we retain compatibility with the main Linux kernel
development effort. Thus, we did not consider optimizations
that would have required major changes to the (in theory)
machine independent Linux core."

"The core of Linux memory management is based on the
x86 two-level page tables. We could change the organization
of the PTEs in these tables to match the requirements
of the PPC architecture (a hash table instead of a two-level
page table), but we were committed to using these page
tables as the initial source of PTE�s due to the design of
Linux."

Although the MMU report shows a subtantial performance
improvement over other kernels for the PPC. I cannot
help but to wonder if "Light-Handed" control of the kernel
might be preventing non-Intel and multi-processor computers
from achieving the level of performance that is required to
kick Microsoft's butt.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38314B94.7F9DC7DB@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <382F1C8C.3C6FC4D7@home.com> <VpHX3.23294$Hk.199596@news1.mia> <382f9d73.91926348@news1.attglobal.net> <_cUX3.361$DT3.3088@news4.mia> <80oqvo$27mr$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:
..snippage..

> Some published test results show that a single-processor
> computer running Linux will outperform an equivalent
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

..more snippage..     ...Linux bigotry alert...

The tests were run at Microsoft's request and the fundamental
structure of those tests were stacked against Linux.  They had NT
experts on hand but they picked up red hat and installed it.  The
hardware they used had known linux performance problems, the majority
of machines on desktops do not have these problems.  These tests were
biased!

You can get better performance from NT but you must be willing to pay
for it (a lot of consulting fees).  The up time of LInux over Wintel
is the best seller add downtime from the equation and LInux wins hands
down.  I know of a Linux boxes running on NT networks and they stay up
when NT crashes around them due to network problems.

If you talk about internet access there is a 25% performance increase
in accessing the internet using Linux on lower end machines.  This is
real press enter on a windows box and a linux box at the same time and
the screen should noticeably faster on Linux.

Regarding the code updates to the kernel (snipped).  If the
development team does not like the control aspects they can simply
develop a competing release and the best man will win.  There is
something to be said with staying mainstream though.  Optimize within
constraints and then go into major development with a solid grounding
on what it does.  The politics of Linux is becoming muddy now it is a
stable platform.
Try and get a major performance enhancement accepted by IBM or
Microsoft.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80riv7$r53$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <382F1C8C.3C6FC4D7@home.com> <VpHX3.23294$Hk.199596@news1.mia> <382f9d73.91926348@news1.attglobal.net> <_cUX3.361$DT3.3088@news4.mia> <383045A1.8F845CD6@home.com>`

```
[snip]
> As regards COBOL, even in a Linux environment, it makes sense we would
> want to see it wrapped in a COBOL-oriented IDE.

Typically, Unix/Linus doesn't work this way. One of its strengths is its
ability to mix and match different tools to produce the desired result.

Assuming someone develops a COBOL to C transalator, the rest of the
development tools - C compiler, linker, make, editors, etc. - are
already there in multiple versions. You can select which set of tools
you like best. If someone produces a new make or editor that you like
better then you just use it. The "integrated" part of IDE means that you
are retricted to someone else's idea of the best tools to use.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>

```
> I *WISH* what you said was true.  But Linux won't be the only OS

…[11 more quoted lines elided]…

What sort of client and applications are you talking about?  My idea of
```

#### ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

- **From:** Andru Luvisi <luvisi@andru.sonoma.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<m2n1sfi1qs.fsf@andru.sonoma.edu>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com> writes:

> 
> I want to open up a discussion of the possibilities and problems of
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://www.opensource.org/for-hackers.html.

So far, so good...

> 5. Modifications
> 
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> public.

This is incorrect.  The GPL says that anybody you distribute the
software to has to get the same, full set of rights you got.  In other
words, if I give you a binary of gcc, I have to give you the full
source to *that* binary, all under the GPL, even the parts that I
wrote or modified.  It places *absolutely* no requirements on me to
give you the source if I don't give you the binary.  I don't even have
to give it to Stallman himself.  This is one of the freedoms that come
with Free Software: the freedom *not* to share, also known as
privacy.  The point is that you are *not* allowed to take someone
*else's* freedom away by giving them a binary without any source.  You
give them both, just the source, or neither.  You may not give them
just the binary.

> How would this condition of the GPL have to be changed to make OS
> practical for COBOL?  I feel that the principle is important because it
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> companies with many different entities.

This is exactly the way the GPL already works.

> 6. Fair Use
> 
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> obviously unacceptable and is forbidden by the GPL.  

This is incorrect.  I can sell you gcc and emacs binaries all I want,
as long as I give you the full source with them.  The *only*
restriction the GPL places on price is if I give you only the
binaries, with the source code being a separate offering.  Under those
circumstances, the price of the source code must be no more than the
cost of distribution.  This is to prevent me from selling you the
binaries for $20 and the source for $2,000,000,000, effectively
keeping it from you.  I am, however, perfectly free to charge you
$2,000,000,000 for gcc binaries and source as a package.

> But to what extent
> would you accept an organization keeping their enhancements of your
> software private?  In fact, what constitutes an enhancement to the
> original product compared to a modification to make the product work
> for a specific company?

This is, of course, a personal question.  I don't care how much money
they make off my code, as long as they don't give anyone else binaries
without source, thereby using my code to *restrict* other peoples'
control over their own computers.

> For example, suppose you create a point-of-sale system as OS.  A
> company modifies your software to make it web based.  Is this a private
> modification for that company because it happens to do business over
> the web or a general enhancement that should be made public?

Under the GPL, they can keep it private all they want.  If they sell
it, they have to sell the whole thing, including their enhancements,
under the GPL, with full source code.

> 7. Standards
> 
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> provided by a specific compiler.

This would be a worthy goal, yes.

> In particular, this would mean, for the moment, giving up OO and GUI
> based user interfaces.  However, should you also give up ODBC or CICS
> because Microsoft and IBM control them?

This would be a project specific question.  My recommendation would be
to write code which *could* use them, but which also could compile on
a Free Software COBOL compiler.

> The report writer and screen section are a standard part of COBOL but
> they are not available on IBM mainframes that are still the most common
> environment for COBOL.  Should these also be avoided for proper OS?

See my last paragraph.

One of the things I think you are missing is that it is difficult to
write Free Software without a compiler that is also Free Software,
because otherwise you are still at the mercy of your vendor - in this
case the compiler vendor - and so is everybody who wishes to use your
software.  I suspect there will be far more Free Software written in
COBOL once a Free Software COBOL compiler makes it to prime time.

Andru
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38314D65.A5F7814F@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <m2n1sfi1qs.fsf@andru.sonoma.edu>`

```
Andru Luvisi wrote:
> 
> > 5. Modifications
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> just the binary.

The other GNU license is the library license.  This says that you can
use the library within your code and acknowledge this use. The library
and library changes are covered by GNU but the resulting application
is not.  This is probably a more appropriate model for most of what we
are creating.  If I use a generic screen routine I don't want my whole
application opening up as a result.

Ken
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80ri0b$qe9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <m2n1sfi1qs.fsf@andru.sonoma.edu>`

```
[snip]
> One of the things I think you are missing is that it is difficult to
> write Free Software without a compiler that is also Free Software,
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> COBOL once a Free Software COBOL compiler makes it to prime time.

I disagree with this one. If you stick to the published standards then
the code should be usable under any number of different compilers. The
extensions offered by the different compilers are not a barrier to
producing OS applications.

Also, Fujitsu Power COBOL 3 is already available as a free COBOL IDE.
Since OS development is typically done by individuals or small groups on
their personal computers, it is perfectly adequate for the job.

Which brings me back to my original question.  Is there something
inherent in the idea or practices of OS that inhibits the development of
COBOL OS applications?  If so, what is it and how do we fix it?
```

#### ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)
- **Date:** 1999-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38321126.71712123@news1.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Sun, 14 Nov 1999 15:53:59 GMT, Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com> wrote:

>I want to open up a discussion of the possibilities and problems of
>Open Source (OS) COBOL.  Although I have found some public domain COBOL
>on the Internet, I have not yet found any OS COBOL and I would like to
> know why.

Ed, 

I've been thinking about this, and I think I know at least part of the
answer.  Years ago I was involved in a "open source" project that
pre-dated Linux.  This was WWIV, the BBS software.  Registered users
were given the source code and they could "Mod" it, (Modify it -
reminds me of a humerous story, I'll tell it at the end), and publish
the "mods" for others to incorporate in the source.  The best of these
usually ended up in the next version of the BBS software.  I created
several mods for my own use, but I was not nearly as prolific as some
of the other "modders".  Why?  I programmed full time for a living,
these other individuals modifed their code for fun, and very few of
them were professional coders.  Many of them were gifted however.  

WWIV is written in C.  Any individual who desired can pick up a C
compiler fairly cheaply.  I used Borland Turbo C++.  However, until
Fujitsu came into the market, an individual could hardly afford a
COBOL compiler.

Until we get a hobbiest following for COBOL there's not much hope for
many open source projects.

-------------
Now the humerous story.  Shortly after coming to a new division at my
last job, I ran accross some interesting code comments.  The standard
was to place a description of any program changes in comments at the
top.  I found the following:

* Mod the program logic to handle control breaks differently
(Programmer initials)

Then a few lines down:

* Mod must be short for Murder (Different programmers initials).


---
Try a better search engine:  http://www.google.com
My personal web site: http://www.geocities.com/Eureka/2006/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38325408.A623E773@home.com>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38321126.71712123@news1.attglobal.net>`

```


Thane Hubbell wrote:
> 
> On Sun, 14 Nov 1999 15:53:59 GMT, Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com> wrote:
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> were given the source code  <snip>

> Until we get a hobbiest following for COBOL there's not much hope for
> many open source projects.

Thane, that really says it - it probably is a hobbyist thing - but I
suspect that not too many COBOL programmers are hobbyists; we live
day-by-day in the real business world. Having written 'I don't think
it's going anywhere fast' - to date nobody has refuted that comment.

The other thing that concerns me is that some appear to be hidebound by
the definition of Open Source, (i.e. can you, can't you ?). If the
existing 'Open' definition is a stumbling block, make a new set of rules
for COBOL and call it 'Open COBOL', (or whatever), instead of 'Open
Source'.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

- **From:** dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<819d97$gjm$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
"Ed Milne" <emilne@attcanada.net> writes:

>I should have been more specific.  By Open Source COBOL, I meant code
>written in COBOL rather than compilers.


Then what you're talking about are Open Source Applications.

I think things like the GPL apply to changes to the
item covered by the GPL, not products produced by the
GPL'd item.

For example, if you make improvements in Gnu C you have
an obligation to plow those changes back into the Gnu C
effort.  If you write a complete banking system for your
employer in Gnu C I doubt that there is any obligation
for it to be covered by the GPL.  The term "derivitive
works" comes to mind.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Open Source COBOL

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383A8B2F.9BE8BD6C@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<80mlun$cei$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <819d97$gjm$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
"David K. Bryant" wrote:
> 
> "Ed Milne" <emilne@attcanada.net> writes:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> works" comes to mind.


This is not strictly correct.  The difference is that the application
GNU C takes text and as a result creates object modules.  This may be
a derivative work but I do not think so.  If you took ANY code from
the C compiler and used it you would have to release a useful portion
of your work (say release a useful library with code).

When you link you link in the GNU runtime library.  This is covered by
the Library license which says that you can use the library in an
application so long as you attribute a portion of your application to
GNU (published use of GNU code).

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

<a id="t-f493c8afcb"></a>
## Thread: Cerco utenti MF Cobol e NetExpress — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Cerco utenti MF Cobol e NetExpress

- **From:** "Giancarlo" <gcan@libero.it>
- **Date:** 1999-11-14T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
hello, i am wondering if anyone can suggest a solution to my problem. here
it is:
i am using the fujitsu cobol 3.0 complier. i have enclosed below the input,
output and code. even though it doesn't look it here, the input is
orgainized correctly. the first line is read and calculated correctly but in
every line after that the data at the left moves into the CUST-NAME-IN
region by 2 spaces with each consecutive read. the data is not calculated
correctly either after the first read. its like the CUST-NAME-IN shrinks by
2 spaces each time.
does anyone know whats going on here?!
thanks for any help you might offer.
```

#### ↳ Re: output problem

- **From:** thaneH@softwaresimple.com (Thane Hubbell)

```
                            ORGANIZATION IS LINE-SEQUENTIAL.  

after each select.

>            SELECT ACCOUNT-MASTER ASSIGN TO 'E:\TEMP3\NEWMSTR.DAT'.
```

<a id="t-fc85c0df01"></a>
## Thread: Copy replacing — 10 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### Copy replacing

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38307ECF.B5AFE62@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Sometimes we need to convert a file from an old format to a new one. 
The standard procedure is to create a new copy member for the new file
format, changing as few field names as possible, as they will be used
in  the programs using it.

So we have
01   MY-FILE-NAME.
     05   TT-FIELD-1     PIC X(05).
     05   TT-FIELD-2     PIC X(20).
      etc

This is when we would like to use a COPY REPLACING TT- BY TTOLD- for the
conversion program, but we end up making a copy of the copy member and
editing that REPLACING by hand.

Are we going to have a more direct way of doing this simple editing
function ?
```

#### ↳ Re: Copy replacing

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<jQGMiKAHpWM4Ew9s@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38307ECF.B5AFE62@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
It already exists in modern versions of Cobol.

In article <38307ECF.B5AFE62@NOSPAMhome.com>, Howard Brazee
<brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> writes
>Sometimes we need to convert a file from an old format to a new one. 
>The standard procedure is to create a new copy member for the new file
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Copy replacing

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80rtug$csc$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`
- **References:** `<38307ECF.B5AFE62@NOSPAMhome.com> <jQGMiKAHpWM4Ew9s@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Yes but not very usefully for existing code.

You have to define the replaceable part as something like (TT) I think i.e..
01 MY-FILE-NAME.
     05  (TT)-FIELD-1   PIC X(05).
     05  (TT)-FIELD-2   PIC X(20).

You ALWAYS have to use COPY REPLACING.
E.g. either
    REPLACING ==(TT)== BY ==TT==
or
    REPLACING ==(TT)== BY ==TTOLD==

However I think it is worthwhile if you can change all places where the copy
book is used.   I wish we could have found the time.

Rick


Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> wrote in message
news:jQGMiKAHpWM4Ew9s@ld50macca.demon.co.uk...
> It already exists in modern versions of Cobol.
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
> People are like teabags - you have to put them in to hot water before you
find
> out how strong they are.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Copy replacing

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38319719.C07BC329@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
That has been my experience exactly.  You can DESIGN your copy member
from the beginning to use COPY REPLACING effectively.  But simple
editing of existing copy members in your program doesn't work well. 
Maybe Alistair has had a different experience (with a different
compiler) than we have.

Rick Price wrote:
> 
> Yes but not very usefully for existing code.
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
> > out how strong they are.
```

```
So do this:

 01   MY-FILE-NAME.
      05   :TT:-FIELD-1     PIC X(05).
      05   :TT:-FIELD-2     PIC X(20).

COPY whatever REPLACING ==:TT:== BY ==TTOLD==.
COPY whatever REPLACING ==:TT:== BY ==TT==.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Copy replacing

- **From:** wmklein@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<820nq2$g38$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<38307ECF.B5AFE62@NOSPAMhome.com> <bsb%3.2298$Di3.24915@news2.mia> <3842BFAB.681A08AF@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Check your compiler to see if your vendor ALREADY supports syntax that
will be STANDARD in the next COBOL Standard.  (I know that Micro Focus
does - but don't personally know of any other that does it already.)

Look for the "leading" and "trailing" options in the COPY/REPLACING (and
REPLACE) statements - that "officially" support partial word replacement
- without planning on it when you create your original COPY member.

Note:  If your vendor does not support this already, you might want to
find out their method of doing an "official" enhancement request - such
as IBM has via the SHARE user group or via the support channel "REQUEST"
feature.

This probably won't help you with your immediate requirement - but MIGHT
be faster than waiting for your vendor to provide a fully conforming
compiler - for the next Standard.

In article <3842BFAB.681A08AF@NOSPAMhome.com>,
  Please, do, not, e-mail, replies, post, them!! wrote:
> I won't change an existing copy member, breaking scores of existing
> programs, so that I can use it a bit differently in my new program.

file
```

## Thread: Restore data from corrupted IBM COBOL/2 File — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Restore data from corrupted IBM COBOL/2 File

- **From:** gstueb@okay.net (Gregor Stuebner)
- **Date:** 1999-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-Tew1PT8WmxqI@localhost>`

```
Hello,

i have a indexed file which is saved in two files called "file.dat" 
and "file.idx". The "file.idx" is corrupted.

How can i read the "file.dat" sequential, i.e. without reading the 
corrupted "file.idx"?

The used compiler (DOS is: 

IBM COBOL/2
Version 1.00   (C)Copyright IBM Corporation 1985, 1987, 1988
               (C)Copyright Micro Focus Ltd. 1985, 1988

thanks for help
Gregor
```

#### ↳ Re: Restore data from corrupted IBM COBOL/2 File

- **From:** "Unbeliever" <popsider@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80q6hl$g17$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-Tew1PT8WmxqI@localhost>`

```
If you don't have a development tool to rebuild the index, all I can
suggest, is treat the .DAT as a flat file & build a program of your
own, to populate an empty indexed file with the old .DAT data.


Gregor Stuebner <gstueb@okay.net> wrote in message
news:fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-Tew1PT8WmxqI@localhost...
> Hello,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gregor
```

#### ↳ Re: Restore data from corrupted IBM COBOL/2 File

- **From:** "Dennis Komeshak" <compusolve@highlandil.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-15T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<M72Y3.554$9F4.3274@newsfeed.slurp.net>`
- **References:** `<fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-Tew1PT8WmxqI@localhost>`

```
You should have a program named REBUILD.EXE which is a utility that corrects
the errors in your file.
Assuming you have it, at a DOS command line, type REBUILD for help.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Restore data from corrupted IBM COBOL/2 File

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hwkMKOANqWM4Ewe3@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```

>the errors in your file.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00

- **Message-ID:** `<fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-24pcdd38esBz@localhost>`
- **References:** `<fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-Tew1PT8WmxqI@localhost> <M72Y3.554$9F4.3274@newsfeed.slurp.net>`

```
Hi Dennis,

> You should have a program named REBUILD.EXE which is a utility that corrects
> the errors in your file. Assuming you have it, at a DOS command line, type REBUILD for help.

the program "rebuild" is useless. It only say "Input file not present 
or currupt". As shown in the program "rebuild" i have tried to read my
file in the same way:


select old-file
   organization sequential
   ...
fd old-file
  record is varying from 1 to 120 characters
  ...

If i try to open my indexed file with this select-clause, i get a 
runtime-error 13.

cu,
Gregor
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restore data from corrupted IBM COBOL/2 File

- **From:** "Russell Styles" <RWSTYLES@COMPUSERVE.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81196n$av4$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`
- **References:** `<fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-Tew1PT8WmxqI@localhost> <M72Y3.554$9F4.3274@newsfeed.slurp.net> <fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-24pcdd38esBz@localhost>`

```
    1.  Backup old file (both pieces).
    2.  Assuming rebuild fails, beg borrow or steal an .IDX of the same
exact type from somewhere else.
    3.  Place old .DAT and new .IDX in same dir, with IDX renamed to same
root name as original.  New IDX can be empty, so long
          as it is valid.
    4.  Run rebuild.  When done, run again, REBUILD OLD.DAT,NEW.DAT /V
         No space around the comma.  /V produces a running count.

    You now have a totally rebuilt file.


    By the way, when you run rebuild, it wants the FILE.DAT, not FILE.IDX
name.

news:fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-24pcdd38esBz@localhost...

corrects

…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> Gregor
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Restore data from corrupted IBM COBOL/2 File

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** gstueb@okay.net (Gregor Stuebner)
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-nThM9xvnCO1g@localhost>`
- **References:** `<fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-Tew1PT8WmxqI@localhost> <M72Y3.554$9F4.3274@newsfeed.slurp.net> <fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-24pcdd38esBz@localhost> <81196n$av4$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Maybe, we do not have the same rebuild-program:

>     1.  Backup old file (both pieces).
>     2.  Assuming rebuild fails, beg borrow or steal an .IDX of the same
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>          No space around the comma.  /V produces a running count.

... the shipped rebuild-program ....


       Select old-file
              assign to f-name-1
              organization sequential
              access sequential
              status is f-stat-1
              .
       Select new-file
              assign to f-name-2
              organization indexed
              access dynamic
              record key is key-f-1
              alternate record key key-f-2 with duplicates
              status is f-stat-2
              .
       Data Division.
       File Section.
       fd  old-file
           record is varying from 1 to 120 characters
           .
       01  old-rec.
           03  details    pic x(120).
       fd  new-file.
       01  new-rec.
           03  key-f-1    pic x(9).
           03  filler     pic x(11).
           03  key-f-2    pic x(10).
           03  filler     pic x(90).
       Working-storage section.
       01  f-stat-1.
           03  old-file-byte-1   pic 9.
           03  old-file-byte-2   pic x.
           03  old-file-byte-2-a pic 9(2) comp
                         redefines old-file-byte-2.
       01  f-stat-2.
           03  new-file-byte-1   pic 9.
           03  new-file-byte-2   pic x.
           03  new-file-byte-2-a pic 9(2) comp
                         redefines new-file-byte-2.
       77  end-flag  pic 9.
       77  f-name-1  pic x(64) value "c:corrupt.dat".
       77  f-name-2  pic x(64) value "c:fixed.dat".
       Procedure division.
           open input old-file
           if old-file-byte-1 = 0
           then
               Open output new-file
               perform until end-flag = 1
                   read old-file at end move 1 to end-flag
                             not at end write new-rec from old-rec
                                        end-write
                   end-read
               end-perform
               close old-file, new-file
           else
               display " Input file not present or corrupt"
           end-if
           stop run.
```

```
Anyone out there in COBOL-land have any tips on debugging a VSAM retcode 93
(the IBM COBOL manual refers to a resource shortage)?

The programmer has tried a variety of things, including increasing the region
size as well as ensuring that the data set in question is closed to CICS.

Alternate indices are also in use with this data set.  CA doesn't have any
reported hits like this one.

Thanks in advance!

Rick J. Valles
```

- **References:** `<2ccY3.301$UW4.10548@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
    Everybody lost track of the original guestion.  This varies from
compiler to compiler and platform to platform,
but basically, you need to check for the presence of a keystroke without
actually accepting it.  the call (CBL_READ_KBD_STATUS on MF) returns at once
even if there is no keystroke waiting, and does not disturb the keystroke.

    If a keystroke exists, read it.  You will need to locate the exact
syntax yourself.  See DD's response.

that
> you need to check all times if any key is pressed, and not at a specific
> moment...
```

<a id="t-71a4dd5761"></a>
## Thread: System calls for UNIX — 4 msgs

### System calls for UNIX

- **From:** suz@webpagedesign.com.au
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38313f84.7558247@news.minerva.com.au>`

```
Hi, I was wondering how to start a ftp client from cobol in a UNIX
environment. I'm writing a program that checks a directory for files,
and if there are any files then those files should be ftped.
Is there a way to send a command to a shell from cobol?

Could any replied be sent to
<a
href='mailto:asekeris@pacidicaccess.com.au'>asekeris@pacificaccess.com.au</a>
```

#### ↳ Re: System calls for UNIX

- **From:** "Steven Lalewicz" <strl@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80rocr$npg$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38313f84.7558247@news.minerva.com.au>`

```

suz@webpagedesign.com.au wrote in message
<38313f84.7558247@news.minerva.com.au>...
>Hi, I was wondering how to start a ftp client from cobol in a UNIX
>environment. I'm writing a program that checks a directory for files,
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>href='mailto:asekeris@pacidicaccess.com.au'>asekeris@pacificaccess.com.au</
a>

Hi there,

ftp versions I use allow a batch mode, ftp -n -s:client.ftp
(see man ftp for more info on these flags)

where client.ftp is a file listing the commands ftp needs to carry out, for
example:

open client
user guest
guestpasswd            # This line contains the password for the user on the
previous line
bin
cd somewhere
prompt
put myfiles.tar
quit

To execute the command from COBOL, depends on what COBOL system you are
using. For Microfocus COBOL you can use the X"91" routine.

For example (a fragment of a program)

  01 result pic x comp-x.
  01 func pic x comp-x value 35.
  01 cmd-param-len pic x comp-x value 1.
  01 cmd-line pic x(80).
  ...
  call-os.
      move "ftp -n -s:client.ftp" to cmd-line
      display cmd-line upon command-line
      move 0 to cmd-param-len
      call X"91" using result func cmd-param-len
      if result not = zero
          display "COMMAND FAILED " cmd-line
          stop run
      end-if
      .

or

CALL "CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT" USING ... (see docs for this one)

or

CALL "system" using null-terminated-command-line (UNIX only, uses standard
library system call)

If you are using another COBOL product, please tell us and someone familuar
with that product I'm sure will help.

Regards,
Steve.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: System calls for UNIX

- **From:** "Steven Lalewicz" <strl@mfltd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<80s9t6$ppj$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38313f84.7558247@news.minerva.com.au> <80rocr$npg$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
Steven Lalewicz wrote in message <80rocr$npg$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>...
<snip>
>ftp versions I use allow a batch mode, ftp -n -s:client.ftp
>(see man ftp for more info on these flags)
>

Sorry, I've been living in the PC world too long, a few corrections .....

There is no -s option on UNIX versions of ftp and for the -n option, from
the man page:

      -n  Do not attempt "auto-login"  upon  initial  connection.
          If  auto-login  is  not disabled, ftp checks the .netrc
          file in the user's home directory for an entry describ-
          ing  an  account  on  the  remote machine.  If no entry
          exists, ftp will prompt  for  the  login  name  of  the
          account on the remote machine (the default is the login
          name on the local machine), and, if necessary,  prompts
          for a password and an account with which to login.
<snip>
>  ...
>      call X"91" using result func cmd-param-len

X"91" is for the PC only.

>CALL "CBL_EXEC_RUN_UNIT" USING ... (see docs for this one)

>

Only for Server Express products on UNIX (not v4.0 or v4.1).

>CALL "system" using null-terminated-command-line (UNIX only, uses standard
>library system call)


I've been told that you should use the RTS version called "SYSTEM",
this will reset the screen back to default settings before executing
the command and return it back into a COBOL state afterwards.

After all this you are probably using a differnt compiler.....

I'm off to resurrect my UNIX machine.

Regards,
Steve.
```

#### ↳ Re: System calls for UNIX

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3832985D.B01D0C35@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<38313f84.7558247@news.minerva.com.au>`

```
suz@webpagedesign.com.au wrote:
> 
> Hi, I was wondering how to start a ftp client from cobol in a UNIX
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Create a script to hide the specific implementation.  Here is one that
relies on a netrc to provide signon to the other computer.  the set -x
is debugging.  This way the directory on the client machine is easy to
modify.  note 'ibmlaser' is a machine name and can be an IP address. 
The netrc is documented in man pages and really handy.

Note the bin transfers binary files (no translation) ascii translates
and is expands linefeeds on unix to carriage return linefeeds in PC
land.

------
#!/bin/ksh
#  ftp Australia Combined files to the IBM Laser box

# set -x

FILE=$1
ftp ibmlaser << !!
cd /var/psf/incoming
bin
put $FILE
quit
!!
---------

The other alternative to FTP is 'rcp' if the other side is a unix
box.  possibly easier.

Ken
```

---

<a id="t-7f319ded54"></a>
## Thread: centering a pic x field — 6 msgs

### centering a pic x field

- **From:** webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr>
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383173AD.53ECBB49@eii.fr>`

```
Hi

Hi does it exist a simple way to center
the data in a pic x field in order to
have a pretty centered display on the
screen instead of a left align ?

NB: I can not use any screen specific
section since I am actually working on
french minitel, so my only statement to
display is "display myField".

thanks
```

#### ↳ Re: centering a pic x field

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3gLJ+RAw7WM4Ew8u@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<383173AD.53ECBB49@eii.fr>`

```
Use inspect or a subscripted byte by byte examination of the field to
determine the length of it. Then either:

1. Move spaces to the myfield and use
      MOVE sourcefield (n) TO myfield (n/2+1, n)
   if your compiler allows subscripted moves.

or

2. Move spaces to myfield then use a subscripted byte by byte move n 
   times to move the source field into myfield appending after the
   spaces.

or

3. Try using String. I don't know if this would work. 

In article <383173AD.53ECBB49@eii.fr>, webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr>
writes
>Hi
>
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: centering a pic x field

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3831829A.99062169@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<383173AD.53ECBB49@eii.fr>`

```
webmaster wrote:
> 
> Hi
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks

There isn't a language feature to do this, but you can do some character
manipulation by hand.  Use INSPECT to determine starting and ending
character position and length of the field to be moved, calculate where
to move it, and then use reference modification to move it to the
desired spot.
```

#### ↳ Re: centering a pic x field

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-11-16T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3831ad73.5519616@news2.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<383173AD.53ECBB49@eii.fr>`

```
On Tue, 16 Nov 1999 15:09:33 +0000, webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr> wrote:

>Hi
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>screen instead of a left align ?
Try this

      * +------------------------------------------------------------+
      * !                                                            !
      * !   Name: CENTER                                             !
      * !                                                            !
      * !   Type: Cobol source file                                  !
      * !                                                            !
      * !   Desc: center a string                                    !
      * !                                                            !
      * !   Useage: CALL 'CENTER' USING BY REFERENCE In-str          !
      * !                               BY CONTENTS LENGTH OF In-str !
      * !                               BY REFERENCE Out-str         !
      * !                               BY CONTENTS LENGTH OF Out-str!
      * !                                                            !
      * !   Creation date: 5 Sep 1999, creation time: 20:35:46       !
      * !                                                            !
      * !   Author: (c) Volker Bandke                                !
      * !                                                            !
      * +------------------------------------------------------------+

       Identification Division.
        Program-Id. CENTER IS INITIAL.
       Environment Division.
       Data Division.
        Working-Storage Section.
         77 Left-spaces          PIC S9(9)  BINARY.
         77 Right-Spaces         PIC S9(9)  BINARY.
         77 Input-ptr            PIC S9(9)  BINARY.
         77 Output-ptr           PIC S9(9)  BINARY.
        Linkage Section.
      * String to be centered
         01 Text-in-string.
            05 Text-in-chars     PIC X OCCURS 1 to 32767 TIMES
                                       DEPENDING ON Text-in-length.
         01 Text-in-length       PIC 9(9)    BINARY.
      * Area to receive centered string
         01 Text-out-string.
            05 Text-out-chars    PIC X OCCURS 1 to 32767 TIMES
                                       DEPENDING ON Text-out-length.
         01 Text-out-length      PIC 9(9)    BINARY.
       Procedure Division USING Text-in-string
                                Text-in-length
                                Text-out-string
                                Text-out-length.
       CENTER-entry.
           MOVE SPACES TO Text-Out-String
           IF (Text-in-string NOT = SPACES)
               PERFORM VARYING input-ptr
                       FROM Text-in-length
                       BY -1
                       UNTIL Text-in-string(Input-ptr:1) NOT = SPACE
               END-PERFORM
               COMPUTE Right-spaces = Text-in-length - input-ptr
               PERFORM VARYING Input-ptr
                       FROM 1
                       BY 1
                       UNTIL Text-in-string(Input-ptr:1) NOT = SPACE
               END-PERFORM
               COMPUTE left-spaces = Input-ptr - 1
               COMPUTE Output-ptr = 1 + (  text-out-length
                                     - text-in-length
                                     + left-spaces
                                     + right-spaces
                                    ) / 2
               IF Output-ptr < 1 THEN
                  COMPUTE Input-ptr = Input-ptr
                                     - output-ptr
                                     + 1
                  MOVE 1 TO output-ptr
               END-IF
               MOVE Text-in-string(input-ptr:)
                 TO Text-out-string(output-ptr:)
           END-IF
           GOBACK
           .

     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         From an actual insurance claim: I had to have my armpits removed.
```

<a id="t-1776f3d249"></a>
## Thread: Data conversion on READ (why not?) — 7 msgs

### Data conversion on READ (why not?)

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991117011023.08513.00000190@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```
I'm reading a flat file containing display data.  I need to issue SQL queries
comparing some of these fields to database values which are packed.  Therefore,
before making the SQL statement, I must MOVE my display values to packed
fields.

Can't I do this?

Under my FD do this
01  REQ-REC.
      05  JOBCODE-REC  PIC S9(3)V.
      05  REQNO-REC      PIC X(6).
      05  MINSAL-REC      PIC PIC S9(6)V99.

In Working-Storage do this
01  REQ-REC-WS.
      05  JOBCODE-WS  PIC S9(3)V             USAGE IS COMP-3.
      05  REQNO-WS      PIC X(6).
      05  MINSAL-WS      PIC PIC S9(6)V99   USAGE IS COMP-E.

When I READ do this
READ REQ-FILE
    AT END
        SET EOF TO TRUE
    NOT AT END
        MOVE JOBCODE-REC TO JOBCODE-WS
        MOVE REQNO-RED TO REQNO-WS
        MOVE MINSAL-REC TO MINSAL-WS
END-READ

I usually read INTO my working storage, and I understand that it is dangerous
to reference a buffer, but doesn't a read INTO do almost exactly what I've done
here?  Doesn't it read to the buffer and then perform a move?  It seems to me
the only thing I've done is break up the group move of a READ INTO, making
several elementary moves instead.  This allows me to convert the usage right
within my READ STATEMENT.  It seems nicer than reading into working storage and
then performing another move to yet another group of variables in working
storage.

This method works fine in my DB2 project, but my teacher advises me not to get
in the habit of doing this.  So why not?  It's not as if I'm coming back later
to reference the buffer.  I'm doing it right within the READ statement, the way
COBOL does it anyway, no?

RoadRaat
```

#### ↳ Re: Data conversion on READ (why not?)

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38324AB2.2D3EB293@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<19991117011023.08513.00000190@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```
RoadRaat wrote:
> 
> I'm reading a flat file containing display data.  I need to issue SQL queries
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
> END-READ

Sure you can do that, but what will your program do if JOBCODE-REC or
MINSAL-REC contain non-numeric data?

And if your program terminates abnormally, will you be able to find the
input buffer in the storage dump?  It's generally good practice to keep
your records in working-storage, and it's not really that much more
work.

Regards,
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Data conversion on READ (why not?)

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383294F3.F4C5171E@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<19991117011023.08513.00000190@ng-cr1.aol.com> <38324AB2.2D3EB293@worldnet.att.net>`

```
If the COBOL environment you have is designed properly (and with IBM
mainframes that fluctuated until finally they got it right with the
version of Language Environment with OS390 2.5 and possibly with PTF's
with that available with 1.3), you can easily find your input record.
With that version of LE all you have to do is find the area associated
with the BLF number in your dump (note if there are SD's in the program
before the last FD the associated DDNAME's are wrong starting with the
BLF associated with the SD).  

Of course a READ file-name INTO record-area will give you bad data in
record-area if bad data is on the file.  Unless you use the same
copybook for both the record area and for the description of the record
on the file, you can get certain other subtle errors and not so subtle
errors when the record description changes in size or key locations.
<rant on> Now if we had annual updates to the COBOL standard we might
have gotten interest in getting COPY REPLACING LEADING/TRAILING before
we all retire.  Maybe someone would push for having decent specification
of qualification such as FILEA.GROUP1.CUSTOMER-NUMBER instead of the
awkward CUSTOMER-NUMBER OF GROUP1 OF FILEA because they might see it
before they die. In English we normally say John's book, not the book of
John. <rant off>.   

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services. 

Arnold Trembley wrote:
> 
> RoadRaat wrote:
…[42 more quoted lines elided]…
> http://home.att.net/~arnold.trembley/
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Data conversion on READ (why not?)

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2cbJxJAw8qM4Ewf6@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<19991117011023.08513.00000190@ng-cr1.aol.com> <38324AB2.2D3EB293@worldnet.att.net>`

```
In article <38324AB2.2D3EB293@worldnet.att.net>, Arnold Trembley
<arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net> writes
>RoadRaat wrote:
>> 
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
When you read a record with invalid data some (all?) cobols will abend
before the record is made available. I'm thinking particularly of Cobol
for MVS. So you are still stuck with squinting through a dump.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Data conversion on READ (why not?)

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3833FA4C.E3C48AD9@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<19991117011023.08513.00000190@ng-cr1.aol.com> <38324AB2.2D3EB293@worldnet.att.net> <2cbJxJAw8qM4Ewf6@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`

```
Alistair Maclean wrote:
> 
> In article <38324AB2.2D3EB293@worldnet.att.net>, Arnold Trembley
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
> Alistair Maclean

1. The program only abends with invalid data when you reference it.
You should code defensively for input programs from foreign sources
(external clients, external systems not in your control).  If you are
causing your own problems...

2. The standard CEEDUMP for language environment is easy to work
with.   It formats quite a readable dump if you include symbols in
your load library.

Ken
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Data conversion on READ (why not?)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3834B0F9.5424DC90@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<19991117011023.08513.00000190@ng-cr1.aol.com> <38324AB2.2D3EB293@worldnet.att.net> <2cbJxJAw8qM4Ewf6@ld50macca.demon.co.uk> <3833FA4C.E3C48AD9@zip.com.au>`

```
Be aware that the SYMDUMP option (please check the manual for syntax)
that formats the dump and shows things by field name will make your
module 10 times bigger and I found that the resulting listing was
relatively large even for a moderate size program.  I don't even want to
think about how big either the load module or the dump listing would be
from some of our larger vendor supplied programs.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services - morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca

Ken Foskey wrote:
> 
       <snip>
> 
> 2. The standard CEEDUMP for language environment is easy to work
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> Ken
```

#### ↳ Re: Data conversion on READ (why not?)

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-17T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<zMGIlGAF6qM4Eweg@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<19991117011023.08513.00000190@ng-cr1.aol.com>`

```
The idea of reading into a working storage area was to identify the last
record read/processed for the file. This meant that you did not need to
calculate displacements from the start of a buffer in order to identify
the last record read. However, good dump facilities can identify the
last record (and in the case of Abend-aid, the previous record), so it
should no longer be necessary to READ INTO. However, if you are happy
with your method carry on.

In article <19991117011023.08513.00000190@ng-cr1.aol.com>, RoadRaat
<roadraat@aol.com> writes
>I'm reading a flat file containing display data.  I need to issue SQL queries
>comparing some of these fields to database values which are packed.  Therefore,
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>RoadRaat
```

<a id="t-960ff8e53c"></a>
## Thread: URGENT! Please help me with data problem (export) — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: URGENT! Please help me with data problem (export)

- **From:** scomstock@aol.com (S Comstock)
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991118120907.27429.00000469@ng-fu1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<dsTY3.4040$%b.109366@amoeba1.soneraplaza.nl>`

```
Danny Bouman writes...

>I received a databse from a client of me which has been written in cobol.
>The program dates from 1988 and they are still using it.
>
Let's get precise here. A database is not written in a language. A database may
be created by a program, or maintained by a program written in almost any
language. But there is no such thing as a "COBOL database" (or a "c database",
etc.).

Since you are referring to a COBOL program that has been in use since 1988, I
would assume that the program was initially for some mainframe. [This is just a
guess, because I'm not aware of any COBOL compilers available on workstations
back then; I could be way off here.] Do you know what kind of machine the
program was orginally coded for (or even what kind of machine it is currently
running on)?

The database itself could be stored in a number of ways; for IBM mainframes,
for example, it could be an ISAM file (pretty old), a VSAM file, a DB2
database, an IMS database, or even an in-house developed manner, or by some
third party product. For IBM mainframes, character data is stored in EBCDIX,
not ASCII. This is true for some other platforms too.

Can you tell us what kind of database format the data is currently in?

>My problem:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>only get  a lot of strange signs and not the data I am looking for.


Is "example.dat" the actual name? How is your customer sending you the data?
Perhaps they don't know how to package it or transmit it.

>
>Please help me. In the program itself there is no export function and I need
>the data for their new databse very badly.

Please give us more information. Be calm, precise, and complete in gathering
and explaining to us the details of your platform and current database format.

Regards,


Steve Comstock
Telephone: 303-393-8716
www.trainersfriend.com
email: steve@trainersfriend.com
256-B S. Monaco Parkway
Denver, CO 80224
USA
```

- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00


#### ↳ Re: Calling Cobol from C

- **From:** "Wiggy" <wignomore@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81229q$ef3$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<01bf31d3$12c4a180$29c24ac1@pa.schaubroeck.be> <811kso$d34$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```

Steven Lalewicz <strl@mfltd.co.uk> wrote:
> Yes it can be done. I know the documentation exists for it, but alas I
can't
> find it anywhere. Some where in the docs there should be a section on
"Mixed
> Language Programming".
>
> So hopefully someone can answer your question in greater detail than me
(I'd
> only get it wrong if I tried to describe what to do from memory).

I assume you're working with Object COBOL V4.1 here?

The Programmers Guide to Writing Programs, Chapter 10, covers Mixed Language
Programming, with Section 10.9 specifically covering working on UNIX. There
is a demo program referred to in this section - account.cbl - which is part
of a C calling COBOL demo application. If you look at $COBDIR/demo/cmain.c,
you'll find instructions on how to compile and link this.

Hope this helps.

Wiggy.
```

- **From:** perrella@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<812ef9$r8m$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<01bf31d3$12c4a180$29c24ac1@pa.schaubroeck.be> <811kso$d34$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk> <81229q$ef3$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```
In article <81229q$ef3$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>,
  "Wiggy" <wignomore@yahoo.com> wrote:
>
> Steven Lalewicz <strl@mfltd.co.uk> wrote:
> > Yes it can be done. I know the documentation exists for it, but
alas I
> can't
> > find it anywhere. Some where in the docs there should be a section
on
> "Mixed
> > Language Programming".
> >
> > So hopefully someone can answer your question in greater detail
than me
> (I'd
> > only get it wrong if I tried to describe what to do from memory).
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> The Programmers Guide to Writing Programs, Chapter 10, covers Mixed
Language
> Programming, with Section 10.9 specifically covering working on UNIX.
There
> is a demo program referred to in this section - account.cbl - which
is part
> of a C calling COBOL demo application. If you look at
$COBDIR/demo/cmain.c,
> you'll find instructions on how to compile and link this.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>


You're in luck. I just did this.

Ok, first of all you need to call cobinit() in your C mainline:

/* mycprog.c */
extern  void cobinit();

int main()
{

   cobinit();

   cblprog1();

   return 0;
}

Then, compile with the following flags:

cc -c mycprog.c
cob -x -e "" mycprog.o cblprog1.cbl

(you can use the -l and -L flags like in C to add additional libraries
to compile)

If my memory serves me right, that should make it work for you.

If not, talk to MF customer support. They were able to send me sample
code that worked.

Now, if only I could get it to work in C++ with RogueWave V6.1
libraries... (But that's another thread)

Good luck,

Ron


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```
