## Thread: open nfs file — 3 msgs

### open nfs file

- **From:** webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr>
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38344745.5E384642@eii.fr>`

```
Hello

When I tried to open an NFS imported
file, I receive a RT 213 (too many locks
!!). I have tried with animator (MF).

The right of the file are ok.

What can be the problem ? (the prog did
work properly when the file was not nfs
imported but directly on the server at
the same path).

THanks

Christophe
```

#### ↳ Re: open nfs file

- **From:** "Ian Marshall" <Ian.Marshall@merant.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8126pr$eu2$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<38344745.5E384642@eii.fr>`

```
File / record locking is an optional feature on NFS and many implementations
do not have this enabled by default. The NFS process'es that control locking
are lockd and statd (these names may be prefixed by in. or rpc. depending on
your operating system e.g. in.lockd or rpc.statd, etc.)

If these processes are not running the will give undefined results when you
attempt to perform a locking operation.

Make sure these processes are running on *both* the NFS client and NFS
server. If they're not, you'll need to get your sysadmin to enable them.
(Usually just commenting out a couple of lines in the NFS startup script).

If you're on a PC based NFS client, I assume these will have configurable
locking as well, although I haven't used a PC NFS program for years. ( Long
live Samba!!! :-) ).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: open nfs file

- **From:** webmaster <webmaster@eii.fr>
- **Date:** 1999-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38351394.912ACC2F@eii.fr>`
- **References:** `<38344745.5E384642@eii.fr> <8126pr$eu2$1@hyperion.mfltd.co.uk>`

```


Ian Marshall wrote:

> File / record locking is an optional feature on NFS and many implementations
> do not have this enabled by default. The NFS process'es that control locking
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> server. If they're not, ...

I am sorry to say that they are runing on both systems... maybe they are some
parameters for these ?

>
> > Hello
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
> >
```

<a id="t-2faeb4e0d8"></a>
## Thread: Data conversion on READ (why not?) — 2 msgs

### Re: Data conversion on READ (why not?)

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <jeffreyfarkas@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.129e1d2232c56f3e9896ab@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<38324AB2.2D3EB293@worldnet.att.net> <19991117095522.04551.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com>`

```
In article <19991117095522.04551.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com>, 
roadraat@aol.com says...
>>Sure you can do that, but what will your program do if JOBCODE-REC or
>>MINSAL-REC contain non-numeric data?
…[20 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I'm sure this has been beat to death.. but since this post has not been 
replied to.. at least on my server... 

 First, testing the SQLCODE will only check the results of the call to 
DB2 and not the contents or validity of the data.

 Second, READ INTO works differently than the MOVE statements you use. 

 Let's look at an example. Assume you have a record with an invalid 
numeric value and you read it into your buffer. You then execute your 
move in the READ statement it will abend and the dump will not have 
display the data you tried to move to the working storage because it was 
invalid and the buffer was wiped clean. 

 
Hope that helps..
Jeff..

http://home.earthlink.net/~jeffreyfarkas/
```

#### ↳ Re: Data conversion on READ (why not?)

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3834AD7E.17C74531@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<38324AB2.2D3EB293@worldnet.att.net> <19991117095522.04551.00000239@ng-cd1.aol.com> <MPG.129e1d2232c56f3e9896ab@news.earthlink.net>`

```
Huh:

READ file-name INTO record-name is defined as a READ followed by a group
level MOVE. That means that the record read exists both in
WORKING-STORAGE and in the buffer.  It may contain complete garbage but
if a record was read as opposed to end-of-file, record-not-found or
i-o-error of some sort that record will be in the buffer.  If you
execute a COBOL instruction that does an explicit arithmetic operation
(or an implicit one such as a MOVE from a smaller numeric field to a
larger one in packed-decimal on an IBM mainframe which would use a Zero
Add Packed Instruction) you will probably get an arithmetic exception
and the receiving field contents may be the value prior to the
instruction or something else.  The sending field in the buffer or
WORKING-STORAGE will still be there as will the buffer.  On any IBM
mainframe COBOL 85 using LE at a high enough level the offending record
will be in the dump in an area associated with its BLF number.  I
believe that the LE is 1.5 with PTF's and I know that LE with OS390 2.5
works this way (1.8 I THINK).  I will try to check the manual in the
morning for the exact description of READ ... INTO for the exact
description when variable length records are involved. 

Jeff Farkas wrote:
> 
> > snip
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> -- Crow MST3K
```

---

<a id="t-8067e7eb8c"></a>
## Thread: evolution — 5 msgs

### Re: evolution

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<lyYY3.35640$Hk.317304@news1.mia>`

```
Howard Brazee wrote:
>Judson McClendon wrote:
>> Howard Brazee wrote:
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
>acceptable evidence, we can't talk evidence at all.

I have already gived two answers to that question. :-)  First off, when
nobody has a clue how autogenesis could occur, then a completely natural
evolution is impossible to accept as fact.  Second, experimental evidence
to support evolution actually happening.  Since neither of these things
exist, and since the fossil record has not one single indication of one
species evolving into another, and can be readily explained by the Noah
flood, what other evidence *could* be accepted.  What proponents put
forward are conclusions that a stronly biased segment of the scientific
community have put forward.  But when that same evidence is evaluated
from a creation/flood perspective position, it makes far more sense.
You rarely hear this articulated, because of the intense bias against
any Bible based interpretation.  In fact, the entire body of evolution
science has been created with the a priori assumption of a random
universe (which science is busily proving is not true) without any God.
People who believe in a God based evolution only do so because of the
intense propaganda war that has been going on for almost 200 years that
has strongly influenced their thinking.  They also must back away from
a literal interpretation of the Bible, and literally force a completely
unnatural meaning on Scriptures that are perfectly clear and reasonably
understood in a literal sense.  One of the prime tenets of Biblical
interpretation is "If the literal sense makes common sense, then seek
no other sense."  This rule must be immediately violated if one assumes
a God based evolution, because there are many places where Scripture
directly contradicts an evolution.

Understand, I am not presenting the Bible as evidence against evolution
for non believers; they discount the Bible already.  Non believers have
the evidence of the actual facts, if they bother to research.  Believers
have that and the evidence of the Bible.

>> The only 'evidence' given is simple adaptation
>> to environment, and does not in the slightest point to an ability to
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>those who don't don't.

Ah, but that process cannot create new, functional genes.  Since there
has not been one single case of a beneficial mutation happening in all
recorded history, that is simply an unsupported hypothesis with no basis
in established fact.  The fact is that observed mutations are always
detrimental to the organism.  Sometimes not so as to influence
survivability, but never, never beneficial.  When you actually try to
create a scenario through which bisexual reproduction, or the human eye,
could have evolved, you quicky see that the concept of evolution
producing everything we see is simply preposterous.  Go ahead, prove
me wrong.  Tell me how evolution could produce a human eye? :-)  How
about bisexual reproduction?  There would be absolutely no survivability
attributes to any of the structures needed until they were *complete*.
Also, explain to me why virtually all animals are bilaterally symetrical?
Where are all those animals that had non semetrical mutations that did
not affect survivability?  Where are all the near-men (or near anything
else) that resulted from mutations all along the million years or so it
took men to evolve?  Where all those protruding half-limbs, or those
half formed eyes that are in process of being evolved?  Just because a
mutation happens to increase survivability does not mean that those
individuals without it *must* have died, in *every single case*
throughout billions of years.  If evolution actually happened, there
would be *far more* such animals than 'normal' ones.  Why do we not
see them in the fossil record?  The number of such contradictions is
almost infinite.  The concept of evolution is literally preposterous.
It cannot bear up under even casual critical scrutiny.  Which is why
I dropped it. :-)

>I don't know why God does what He does.  But certainly I see creatures
>in environments which soon kill them.  I certainly see that even species
>have become extinct.

This is true.  But man has caused enormous climate changes by clearing
rain forests, etc.  However, the major reasons are that the earth is
under a curse from God

  Then to Adam He said, �Because you have heeded the voice
  of your wife, and have eaten from the tree of which I
  commanded you, saying, �You shall not eat of it�:
  �Cursed is the ground for your sake;
  In toil you shall eat of it
  All the days of your life.
  Both thorns and thistles it shall bring forth for you,
  And you shall eat the herb of the field.
  In the sweat of your face you shall eat bread
  Till you return to the ground,
  For out of it you were taken;
  For dust you are,
  And to dust you shall return.�
  (Genesis 3:17-19)

(aside: the above may be explaination for Murphy's Law :-)

and was once devastated by a worldwide flood.  My point was simply that
God would not create a hostile environment for creatures He was about
to create and put in that environment.  It just doesn't make sense.
```

```
Judson McClendon wrote:

> >> >What kind of evidence would be acceptable to you?  What would this
> >> >evidence look like?  It doesn't make any sense to look for evidence
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> evolution is impossible to accept as fact.  

That wasn't the topic of discussion.  It was proposed that natural
selection (e.g. evolution) is a process used by the Creator in
creation.  But even so - there are LOTS of phenomena which we accept
from observation without having a clue on how it got started.  You
observe God without having a clue on how He could come about.  

At any rate, you said evolution does not exist because there isn't
evidence to support it.  I asked what is the type of evidence which, if
it was there, would be convincing.

> Second, experimental evidence
> to support evolution actually happening.  Since neither of these things
> exist, and since the fossil record has not one single indication of one
> species evolving into another, and can be readily explained by the Noah
> flood, what other evidence *could* be accepted.  

What would such a fossil record look like?  What kind of fossil evidence
WOULD be indicative of such an evolution?  If there is no type of
evidence that you can accept, then argument is useless.  If I find
fossils of proto-horses and fossils of very similar horse like animals
through the ages until I got zebras, donkeys, and horses - I would call
that good evidence.  But apparently you demand something different as
evidence.  What is it that you demand to see?

I suspect you demand to see nothing.  If so, then I am signing off this
discussion.


> Ah, but that process cannot create new, functional genes.  Since there
> has not been one single case of a beneficial mutation happening in all
> recorded history, that is simply an unsupported hypothesis with no basis
> in established fact.  The fact is that observed mutations are always
> detrimental to the organism.  

The fact is that observed mutations are often detrimental to the
organism.  They are also often neutral and occasionally beneficial.  You
have made many false statements (God wouldn't allow a species to die,
you won't use the Bible to debunk evolution - but use Adam's curse and
Noah's flood in the same posts).

This stop at the water cooler was interesting - I made sure to put OT:
in my subject line to avoid bothering others, but you failed to do so,
even after I brought it to your attention.  Anyway, it's back to work
for me.  I don't have time to persuade others that all Christians aren't
irrational right now.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Re: evolution

- **From:** "Leif Svalgaard" <lsvalg@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3834c779_2@news3.prserv.net>`
- **References:** `<50cqOC8H1ZSjcMGLq8S1NJdTRNyZ@4ax.com> <rZHW3.458$rG3.3660@client> <HVMrOJ+4NF2b7wp65waTtQqLQobn@4ax.com> <80fv3p$6p1$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net> <80fvtu$hu7$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <80ie6q$md4$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <1OlX3.3280$B_1.141533@typhoon01.swbell.net> <Z5mX3.17964$z26.165755@news4.mia> <382df4f9_3@news3.prserv.net> <aTIX3.23620$Hk.201486@news1.mia> <OtMX3.4831$B_1.227329@typhoon01.swbell.net> <v5jY3.30066$Hk.260618@news1.mia> <Y_pY3.317$Uh1.26266@typhoon01.swbell.net> <WEzY3.8470$dW4.67759@news3.mia> <383302B8.9967A44E@NOSPAMhome.com> <7wHY3.9758$dW4.76734@news3.mia> <3834159F.A0E31A38@NOSPAMhome.com> <lyYY3.35640$Hk.317304@news1.mia> <38347BE6.694BF5DE@NOSPAMhome.com> <a70Z3.36016$Hk.321672@news1.mia>`

```
I can't believe I'm doing this, but I feel I have to.

Judson McClendon <judmc123@bellsouth.net> wrote in message
news:a70Z3.36016$Hk.321672@news1.mia...
> Howard Brazee wrote:
> >Judson McClendon wrote:
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> will eventually be, a million years later?

Judson, that's not the way evolution works. Mother Nature is not
teleological. Half a wing is no good, a tiny hole that serves no purpose
until millions of years later, are no good and are NOT how it works.
Evolution works with what is already there and what already serves
a purpose and what already is an advantage for the organism.
Then slowly, the precursor may assume a new function while
all the way still performing some of its old function albeit less and
less.
A classic case is that of the eye. Even Darwin himself said:
"To suppose that the eye, with all its inimitable contrivances
for adjusting the focus to different distances, for admitting different
amounts if light, and for the correction of spherical and chromatic
aberration, could have been formed by natural selection, seems,
I freely confess, absurd in the highest possible degree."

When we speak of 'the' eye we are not doing justice to the
problem. It has been estimated that eyes have evolved no
fewer than 40 times, and probably more that 60 times,
independently in various parts of the animal kingdom. In
many cases these eyes use radically different principles. Nine
distinct principles have been recognized among the about 50
independently evolved eyes.
These eyes, BTW, are all eyes of modern animals. No animal
ever made a living by being an intermediate stage on some
evolutionary pathway. The simplest eyes are so simple that
they scarcely deserve to be called eyes at all. It is better to
say that some area of the body is slightly sensitive to light.
This is true of some single-celled organisms, jellyfish, leeches
and the like. All they can sense (dimly) is the presence of
light. This light-sensitivity might mean the difference between
life and death, when a predator is looming over you and
blocking some of the light. From such simple beginnings
the rest follows, not in anticipation of the complicated eye
that will be there eventually, but because each step on the
way is useful to the animal at the time. There are no half-
eyes or half-legs needing another million years of change
to become useful.
Do yourself (and the rest of us) the favor of purchasing,
borrowing, stealing, whatever, a book by Richard Dawkins
"Climbing Mount Improbable". Dawkins explains how
evolution really works. It might convince you, but maybe not
as the difference between a scientist and a religious man
is that the scientist knows that he may be wrong, but the
believer knows that he can't be wrong. In order for something
to be scientific it must have the property of being potentially
falsifiable, while religion is concerned about absolute truth
and therefore does not admit falsifiability.
```

## Thread: Data conversion on READ (why not?) — 2 msgs

### Re: Data conversion on READ (why not?)

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3833FA40.85DD1089@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<19991117011023.08513.00000190@ng-cr1.aol.com> <38324AB2.2D3EB293@worldnet.att.net> <383294F3.F4C5171E@nbnet.nb.ca> <3832E798.FCE6823D@home.com> <38331324.23CB8AA8@NOSPAMhome.com> <38336F07.6D4904F7@home.com>`

```
"James J. Gavan" wrote:
> 
> At the moment each revision to the standard appears as a 'monumental
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I went to a standards meeting once (it was interesting ONCE too). 
Standards are not allowed to be updated annually.  The minimum
standard cycle is three years.  This allows the environment to
stabilize before the next onslaught.  There are exceptions for
maturing standards and you might consider OO cobol part of a maturing
substandard that requires constant maintenance but nothing else.

Ken
```

## Thread: Ordinal no 152 Not Located in DLL FAACICNT.DLL — 1 msgs

### Re: Ordinal no 152 Not Located in DLL FAACICNT.DLL

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383460ea.1733522@news2.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<8107pe$mvr$1@bunyip.cc.uq.edu.au>`

```
On Thu, 18 Nov 1999 16:56:25 +1000, "James" <jamsan@uq.net.au> wrote:

>I am writing a small program to talk to the CICS environment on my NT
>system. I am using VA Cobol 2.2 and VA CICS 3.3.
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>
I just tried in on my system, VA COBOL 2.2, with Fixpack 3, and VA COBOL AD 3.1, no
fixpack

I  used the following CMD file to compile the sample:

  SET GM=%1
  if %1.==. SET GM=ETISAMP
  CALL CICSENV
  ECHO %LIB%
  COB2 %GM%.cbl -qcallint(cdecl) faacicnt.lib   kernel32.lib
  pause

The sample was modified to contain my userid/password in lower case

No other changes were made.  And it works perfectly

This is the relevant part of the LIB directory:

17.09.98  19:12                298.904 EBCUTIL.LIB
26.02.98  08:47                    534 FAACBID.LIB

26.02.98  08:22                 52.122 FAACICNT.LIB
26.02.98  14:39                  4.076 FAACLIB.LIB

26.02.98  14:40                 22.540 FAAOTSCC.LIB
26.02.98  14:40                  2.420 FAAOTSLK.LIB
26.02.98  08:49                  3.882 FAAOTSP2.LIB
26.02.98  08:47                    534 FAAPLID.LIB
26.02.98  11:55                  1.890 FAAPSTRT.LIB
26.02.98  08:55                  3.412 FAASR32.LIB
26.02.98  14:41                  3.008 FAASYPCM.LIB
26.02.98  08:38                  2.005 FAAVLIB.LIB
```

## Thread: COBOL Freeware Utility — 1 msgs

### COBOL Freeware Utility

- **From:** "editor" <editor@aboutlegacycoding.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4rZY3.200$xK5.1857@client>`

```
Download this new COBOL freeware utility called SIZER.  With SIZER you copy
any COBOL record layout to the clipboard and this application will calculate
"the size in bytes" of the (largest) record found.
www.aboutlegacycoding.com.
```

```
code, debug and install in two weeks would cost about $8,000, without
overtime.
```

<a id="t-6ddd85bb76"></a>
## Thread: please help! — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: please help!

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc123@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g2_Y3.35796$Hk.317922@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<s35evu7thsq48@corp.supernews.com> <19991118112741.01096.00000659@ng-fy1.aol.com>`

```
RoadRaat wrote:
>
>And I won't have anything smart-assed to say about your question like that last
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>shouldn't have to sacrifice your dignity in order to "pay" for assistance.

I agree with RoadRaat.  But there are two different categories of poor
English.  People for whom English is not their first language often do
not know English well enough to articulate clearly and precisely.  For
those people, it is cruel to criticize them for their improper spelling
or use of grammar.  After all, they may speak English far better than
we speak their native language. :-)

The other category is people who simply don't bother to articulate
clearly, or use language properly.  They type everything in all upper
case or all lower case, no punctuation, and horrible spelling.  If
they expect people to help them, then they should be willing to provide
a readable request for help.  Criticism of their laziness and carelessness
is deserved.  It may even prompt them to improve, to their own benefit,
which is the only valid basis for that type of criticism. :-)
```

#### ↳ Re: please help!

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991118231028.07475.00000585@ng-cs1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<g2_Y3.35796$Hk.317922@news1.mia>`

```
>I agree with RoadRaat.  But...If
>they expect people to help them, then they should be willing to provide
>a readable request for help.  Criticism of their laziness and carelessness
>is deserved.
>

Are we bickering about how somebody asked a question?  I think the guy's
original question was perfectly legitimate and polite.  It just needed a little
more clarification.  I hope he doesn't see the thread this turned out to be.

RoadRaat
```

<a id="t-e3ac2010ce"></a>
## Thread: Restore from corrupted ibm cobol/2 .dat file — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Restore from corrupted ibm cobol/2 .dat file

- **From:** gstueb@okay.net (Gregor Stuebner)
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-jmr0i467keR7@localhost>`

```
Hello,
 
i have a indexed file which is saved in two files called "file.dat"  
and "file.idx". The "file.idx" is corrupted.
 
How can i read the "file.dat" sequential, i.e. without reading the 
corrupted "file.idx"? The shipped rebuild.cbl program doesn't work 
(Runtime Error 13). 
 
The used compiler (DOS) is: 
 
IBM COBOL/2
Version 1.00   (C)Copyright IBM Corporation 1985, 1987, 1988
               (C)Copyright Micro Focus Ltd. 1985, 1988
 
any ideas?
Gregor
```

#### ↳ Re: Restore from corrupted ibm cobol/2 .dat file

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3834E5A2.67416C4A@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-jmr0i467keR7@localhost>`

```
Gregor Stuebner wrote:
> 
> Hello,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>                (C)Copyright Micro Focus Ltd. 1985, 1988


Looks like you are using an old MF compiler that was licensed
to IBM for some reason (never heard about this before).

We have a datafile reader for MF files that uses only
*.dat file and not *.idx file (cannot do indices yet).

So you can use mfdata2flat from http://www.siber.com/datafile/
to convert your file to comma-separated text values
and then read them back in with Cobol program.

Or go straight to a big database.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

---

<a id="t-cd40311119"></a>
## Thread: URGENT! Please help me with data problem (export) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: URGENT! Please help me with data problem (export)

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383484D6.7612@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<dsTY3.4040$%b.109366@amoeba1.soneraplaza.nl>`

```
Kaekel wrote:
> 
> I received a databse from a client of me which has been written in cobol.
…[19 more quoted lines elided]…
> danny@tigris.nl

Try using a generic parsing program like ParseRat 
(http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm).

If you have no idea what the original format is, you will have to click several of 
the option buttons before you see data which you recognise. (The file could be EBCDIC or 
ASCII, the numbers could be packed, binary, zoned etc. If binary they could be 
big-endian or little-endian depending on the original machine).
There might be a bit of trial and error involved.  It will be easier if you have a small 
sample file and a report or other printout of what the data in the file SHOULD be.
```

---

<a id="t-8d97eb1bc1"></a>
## Thread: Cobol for cgi, new maillist starting — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Web, XML, modern integration`](topics.md#web)_

### Cobol for cgi, new maillist starting

- **From:** zentara@gnat.net (zentara)
- **Date:** 1999-11-18T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3833e032.109496@news.gnat.net>`

```
Hi,

Deskware (www.deskware.com) has released "cobolscript"
which allows cobol programmers to write cgi programs. It runs on
win32, linux, and Sun, for real cross-platform flexibility. 

Pat Newberry, who does cobol  programming on mainframes,
has started a maillist for cobol programmers who want to  use their
cobol skills in cgi development. He is currently developing cgi
programs using cobolscript on a linux-apache platform.

If anyone is interested in this maillist, here is the url:
http://www.onelist.com/community/cobolscript

Pat Newberry's email address is:
goshawk@gnat.net
```

```
Hi guys,
I need to take a program that wrote the output to a file, and give a user
the option to print the output, display it, or write it to a file.  I
understand the DISPLAY and ACCEPT.
So say I Display "would you like your output:
                              A) Printed
                              B) Displayed to screen
                              C) Written to file              "
I would ACCEPT it as  CHOICE
Okay, now here is my question:
What is the proper verb to use to take the file from my input output
section, and now make it do one of the above?  I EVALUATEd choice, but don't
know what to do if "A" or "B" so forth.   I have learned I can't OUTPUT,
ASSIGN, or WRITE *by trial and error...*

I'm using Microfocus...
Thanks,
Sunshyne
```

## Thread: Calling DD, was: COBOL COMPILED WITH DATA=31: PLUSES & MINUSES? — 3 msgs

### Re: Calling DD, was: COBOL COMPILED WITH DATA=31: PLUSES & MINUSES?

- **From:** fixxit@bright.net (steve)
- **Date:** 1999-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.software.year-2000,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38350274.267090608@news.bright.net>`
- **References:** `<008d01bf24bc$e0f271a0$8df32cc7@cylemon-cyber> <382b96e7.76989611@news.bright.net> <382BF289.AB189DC4@home.com> <382ef7c8.218206108@news.bright.net> <ESTY3.39138$oa2.201594@iad-read.news.verio.net>`

```

I said:
->>Bad taste?! You should try being the one on display! But, thanks for
->>not paying attention ;>

docdwarf@clark.net () was, like, :
->You're quite welcome... but please, I'll pass on that offer of being 'on
->display'; I did not realise that I was dealing with one who followed
->what are purported to be Inuit customs and would lend me his spouse.

I'll not be sharin' m' spouse wi' th' likes of you, dwarf, no matter
HOW cold it gets! Besides, she's the one plasterin' herself to my back
at night to keep warm.

I said:
->>Seriously, tho, I probably have the best wife on the planet. She *has*
->>to be, to be able to put up with me these 25 years!
->>
->>So, only say *nice* things about my wife; she needs the encouragement!

dwarf:
->Ahem... it might be concluded that putting up with you is a *highly*
->commendable act and that for this she is to be congratulated.

Yes, congratulations are indeed in order (we just had our
anniversary!), but encouragement is necessary; it's such a *difficult*
and *tedious* task bearing the weight of my dependence, that I fear
she may falter under the sheer hopelessness of it all!
```

#### ↳ Re: microfocus cobol linking with C++ and RogueWave libraries

- **From:** perrella@yahoo.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81gpul$oqv$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8105vc$5ep$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <nGSY3.75$CO.2865@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <812e4i$qub$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <812e4i$qub$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  perrella@yahoo.com wrote:
> In article <nGSY3.75$CO.2865@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>,
>   "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote:
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>

The plot thickens. We now know that C++ iostreams (cout/cerr, etc) work fine
as long as we don't link in RogueWave.	When RW is linked in, the cout/cerr
variables are not initialized.	Furthermore, any C++ static constructors are
not called either.

So, you get either C++ static constructors being called or you get RW
initialization being called but not both.

Does anyone know what this is symptomatic of?  Is there a flag for the linker
(ld) that I can use to tell it to run C++ initialization routines as well as
the ones in RW?

Thanks,

Ron


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-13a11466b6"></a>
## Thread: URGENT! Please help me with data problem (export) — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: URGENT! Please help me with data problem (export)

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-19T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3834E4BB.46FDF1B0@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<dsTY3.4040$%b.109366@amoeba1.soneraplaza.nl>`

```
Kaekel wrote:
> 
> I received a databse from a client of me which has been written in cobol.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> the data for their new databse very badly.

Siber Systems sells a number of tools that convert Cobol data files
to Ascii CVS, or dBase IV DBF, or even read it directly into
Crystal Reports.

More on this at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

Currently we do it for MF and FSC Cobol data files and for any
generic sequential data file which is just a sequence of records.

So feel free to try our tools on your file, or send it in to us,
so that we can examine it and maybe adjust our readers to handle it.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

---

<a id="t-5f0e7b8c90"></a>

- **From:** ba600@FreeNet.Carleton.CA (Mike Kenzie)
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8150mi$dko@freenet-news.carleton.ca>`

```

The last time I had to use IDMS I was able to make use of access routines
that kept me in the dark about the inner workings.  Now I need to know it.

I've asked at the local bookstores and they can't find anything in print,
I've searched online without any luck.

I need to know how to access records in an IDMS database in both local and
CV modes.

Thanks
```

- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00

```
OK if there is nothing online, does anyone know of any printed material?
```

#### ↳ Re: Online IDMS resources?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3839673A.279B4E04@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<8150mi$dko@freenet-news.carleton.ca>`

```
There is an IDMS newsgroup, but it gets very little traffic. 

The difference between accessing records in local or CV modes is in the
JCL:

I commented out the following card to run a job in local mode
//*SYSCTL     DD DSN=IDMS50..SYSCTL,DISP=SHR

Of course you lose journaling when you do this - but for retrieval only
that's no problem.  CV mode keeps track of all of your changes and does
a rollback after an abort or when you tell it to roll back.

Programmers where I have worked don't have the ability to run CV mode,
nor local mode update against production.  Usually we could run local
mode retrieval.

With fast machines of today, it is important to do commits often to
avoid filling up the journal files.  Trouble is is you can't then roll
back to the beginning of the job!!



Mike Kenzie wrote:
> 
> The last time I had to use IDMS I was able to make use of access routines
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Thanks
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Online IDMS resources?

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81l1a702oig@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<8150mi$dko@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <3839673A.279B4E04@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

Howard Brazee wrote...    

> 
> With fast machines of today, it is important to do commits often to
> avoid filling up the journal files.  Trouble is is you can't then roll
> back to the beginning of the job!!
>

It can be done with some other routines.  We use something called RSAM
Restartable Sequential Access Method.  I think it's homegrown.  At least
some of the COBOL modules are.

The logic says that I call RSAM to do all my I/O.  It also keeps track of
the database and file positions.  I do opens, closes, writes, reads,
commits, etc using calls to a module.

What happens is let's pretend that my program uses RSAM and bombs out in the
middle of execution (like CV goes down for backup or whatever, or the job
times out, or there is contention, or it's just plain killed by accident).
RSAM holds the files open and also keeps a record of my w-s image on the
database.  When I restart my program, it calls RSAM to see if it's being
restarted.  If so, RSAM reloads the program's w-s and processing continues
as if it had never stopped.

The beauty of this is that we can commit large jobs often keeping the
database running smooth.  We call RSAM to do a commit that's when it copys
the w-s area to the database and releases all the committed file records.
All JCL DD statements are set up to (MOD,CATLG,CATLG) any output files.

Pretty slick stuff I think.  Most of you will probably yawn.  I'm sure it
has limitations but it works well for a database sweep or update from/to
flat files and it's nice to have for programs that spawn many other
processes and are time critical.

Jeff
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Online IDMS resources?

- **From:** jamesj@chesapeake.net (James Johnson)
- **Date:** 1999-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3844316a.101759058@news.chesapeake.net>`
- **References:** `<8150mi$dko@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <3839673A.279B4E04@NOSPAMhome.com> <81l1a702oig@enews4.newsguy.com>`

```
RSAM is product of either  Anderson Consulting or Compuware (Can't remember for
sure and don't work at that shop anymore).  It essentially turns reads/writes
into database like selects/commits with rollbacks.  And programs have to be
specially written to integrate RSAM functionality into the code.

James Johnson
Ex nuke, now just a coder.

wrote:

…[33 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jeff
```

## Thread: converting 1 byte binary to display — 14 msgs

_Topics: [`Migration and conversion`](topics.md#migration)_

### converting 1 byte binary to display

- **From:** John Roberts <puck@bestweb.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383736BE.280F9174@bestweb.net>`

```
I'm reading in a simple sequential file that contains a 1 byte binary
field representing a 2 digit year portion of a date. I'm trying,
unsuccessfully  to convert it to a DISPLAY format.  I define the pic
clause like this

  03   im-m-yy-bin    pic 99 comp.

I then move that to

01  work-member-year    pic 99.


This results in erroneous years in the output. According to the manuals
the binary format uses 2 bytes of storage. Examination of the input
records and the erroneous output indicated the program was taking the
two bytes at that location as the binary number.

 This is on an IBM Main frame (ES9000). The compiler reported in the
listings is IBM  VS COBOL II Release 4.0. If anyone has any idea if
there is a way around this I'd appreciate the help.
Thanks

John Roberts
puck@bestweb.net
```

#### ↳ Re: converting 1 byte binary to display

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7%JZ3.1189$A86.38554@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<383736BE.280F9174@bestweb.net>`

```
This is, of course, almost the extreme of the Y2K fiasco,
representing the year in, not two, but ONE byte.

       02  im-m-yy-bin     PIC X.

01  NEW-YEAR            PIC S9(4) COMP VALUE 0.
01  FILLER REDEFINES NEW-YEAR.
     02  FILLER            PIC X.
     02  OLD-YEAR      PIC X.

MOVE im-m-yy-bin TO OLD-YEAR.
MOVE NEW-YEAR TO WHERE-I-WANT-IT.

Of course this doesn't work if the input year is negative.
```

#### ↳ Re: converting 1 byte binary to display

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00

```
John Roberts wrote in message <383736BE.280F9174@bestweb.net>...
>I'm reading in a simple sequential file that contains a 1 byte binary
>field representing a 2 digit year portion of a date. I'm trying,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>01  work-member-year    pic 99.

>
>This results in erroneous years in the output. According to the manuals
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>


Well, in the OS/390 cobol there is an easier way, but in VS COBOL II there
are no intrinsic functions, so I would try:
01  BINARY-YEAR-X
  10 HEX-00              PIC X(01).
  10 Binary-YEAR     PIC X(01).
01 Numeric-Year  REDEFINES BINARY-YEAR-X PIC S9(04) USAGE COMP.

MOVE X'00'   TO HEX-00                 <<< this may not be allowed in VS
COBOL II. If not, use a WS-CONSTANT.
MOVE MY-BYTE  TO BINARY-YEAR
DISPLAY|MOVE|ADD| whatever  NUMERIC-YEAR.

MCM
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: converting 1 byte binary to display

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3837ACAB.76B62EF4@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<383736BE.280F9174@bestweb.net> <2MHZ3.1356$VO5.31549@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Michael Mattias wrote:
> 
> Well, in the OS/390 cobol there is an easier way, but in VS COBOL II there
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> MCM

MOVE X'00' is perfectly legal in IBM COBOL II.  MOVE LOW-VALUE would
also accomplish the same thing.

Coercing a hex byte into a numeric field is a useful technique to have
in your toolbox.  But it may not be portable because it relies on a
knowledge of how numeric data is actually stored on your hardware.
```

#### ↳ Re: converting 1 byte binary to display

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3837CE8A.A6254BFC@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<383736BE.280F9174@bestweb.net>`

```
John Roberts wrote:
> 
> I'm reading in a simple sequential file that contains a 1 byte binary
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> puck@bestweb.net

Comp has two external definitions two byte or four byte.  The results
are consistent with the definition.

There are some interesting code relying on the fact that code is the
right endian (non portable).  If you have a choice of compilers (the
one you have does not support functions) you may consider FUNCTION ORD
as the portable method.  This returns the numeric value of the
character underneath it, define the field as character one.  This
gives a portable result and makes it clearer to the compiler what you
are attempting (possibly get better optimization).

compute num-val = function ord(char-val).
```

```
You may have to subtract 1 to get the binary value. Ordinal values
got from 1 to 256 rather than from 0 to 255.
```

#### ↳ Re: converting 1 byte binary to display

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<818p7f$381$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<383736BE.280F9174@bestweb.net>`

```
In article <383736BE.280F9174@bestweb.net>,
  John Roberts <puck@bestweb.net> wrote:
> I'm reading in a simple sequential file that contains a 1 byte binary
> field representing a 2 digit year portion of a date. I'm trying,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> This results in erroneous years in the output. According to the
manuals
> the binary format uses 2 bytes of storage. Examination of the input
> records and the erroneous output indicated the program was taking the
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

John,

I'm assuming that the 1-byte binary-year value can be in the range of
X'00' through X'FF'. If, for example, the binary-year value is X'3F'
(decimal 63), try the following:

03 WS-PACKED-X.
   05 WS-PACKED PIC 9(01)V9 PACKED-DECIMAL VALUE ZERO.
03 WS-DISPLAY-X.
   03 WS-DISPLAY PIC 9(02).
03 WS-BINARY-YEAR PIC X(01).

MOVE WS-BINARY-YEAR TO WS-PACKED-X (1:1).
MOVE WS-PACKED TO WS-DISPLAY.

INSPECT WS-DISPLAY-X CONVERTING X'FAFBFCFDFEFF' TO 'ABCDEF'.

The answer in WS-DISPLAY-X (WS-DISPLAY) will be X'F3C6' (C'3F').

I've only used this technique on an IBM Mainframe.

OR, maybe I've misunderstood. Maybe all you need to do is:

03 WS-HWORD-X.
   05  WS-HWORD PIC S9(04) BINARY VALUE ZERO.

MOVE WS-BINARY-YEAR TO WS-HWORD-X (2:1).
MOVE WS-HWORD TO WS-DISPLAY.

WS-DISPLAY WILL NOW CONTAIN 63.

========================================================================

HTH....

Cheers,

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: converting 1 byte binary to display

- **From:** Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3837415D.D838E973@home.nl>`
- **References:** `<383736BE.280F9174@bestweb.net>`

```
You could try this:

record-area

03	yy-record		pic x.

working storage:
	03 im-m-yy-bin		pic 99 comp.
	03 filler redefines im-m-yy-bin.
		05 filler	pic x.
		05 yy-alph	pic x.


01  work-member-year    pic 99.


Initialize im-m-yy-bin
move yy-record to yy-alph
move im-m-yy-bin to work-member-year

regards,

Dirk


John Roberts wrote:
> 
> I'm reading in a simple sequential file that contains a 1 byte binary
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> puck@bestweb.net
```

#### ↳ Re: converting 1 byte binary to display

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38395426.B5096421@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<383736BE.280F9174@bestweb.net>`

```
On IBM mainframes, a binary field's size depends on how many digits are
in the picture clause:
1-4 = 2 bytes
5-9 = 4 bytes
10-18 = 8 bytes

Try creating a  PIC XX field around your binary value, with your number
in the second character.

Then move that PIC XX value to:

05   BIGGER-ALPHA.
     10  BIGGER-FILLER   PIC X.
     10  FILLER          PIC X.
05   MY-NUMBER       REDEFINES BIGGER ALPHA   PIC 99  USAGE IS BINARY.

Then move LOW VALUES to BIGGER-FILLER.

Now MY-NUMBER will have your value on the right, with binary zeros to
the left.  It should work now, depending on how your sign is stored.

John Roberts wrote:
> 
> I'm reading in a simple sequential file that contains a 1 byte binary
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> puck@bestweb.net
```

#### ↳ Re: converting 1 byte binary to display

- **From:** WOB Consulting <wobconsult@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81caen$i14$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<383736BE.280F9174@bestweb.net>`

```
In article <383736BE.280F9174@bestweb.net>,
  John Roberts <puck@bestweb.net> wrote:
> I'm reading in a simple sequential file that contains a 1 byte binary
> field representing a 2 digit year portion of a date. I'm trying,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> This results in erroneous years in the output. According to the
manuals
> the binary format uses 2 bytes of storage. Examination of the input
> records and the erroneous output indicated the program was taking the
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

John,

In my previous post, I defined WS-DISPLAY as PIC 9(02), whereas it
needs to be PIC 9(03). You could encounter high-order truncation when
the DECIMAL-value of the 1-byte binary field is greater than 99 and WS-
HWORD is moved to WS-DISPLAY. Granted, we are talking about a year-
value. But, it is better to be safe than sorry.

Cheers,

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

- **From:** "Jim" <jpprice@surfsouth.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cSY_3.1121$zQ4.79211@news2.giganews.com>`

```
03  im-m-yy-bin   pic 9(04)  binary sync.

Move file value to im-m-yy-bin.  Move im-m-yy-bin to work-member-year.

Binary Sync will align the bytes correctly.
```

```
In article <cSY_3.1121$zQ4.79211@news2.giganews.com>,
  "Jim" <jpprice@surfsouth.com> wrote:
> 03  im-m-yy-bin   pic 9(04)  binary sync.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

<Original Snipped>

The original post indicates that this is an IBM Mainframe. You would
get a compiler error moving the 1-byte binary field to an aligned (or
unaligned) halfword. This would be PIC X(01) to PIC 9(04) BINARY SYNC,
which is illegal. Review several of the other replies, which redefines
the receiving halfword (IM-M-YY-BIN) workarea as two separate bytes.

Gobble Gobble,

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

## Thread: **Redefines — 4 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax)_

### **Redefines

- **From:** Bill <dc606@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8170u8$v72$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
This is the first time I am using the REDEFINES.
When I monitor program through Microfocus Animator it shows

20 20 20 20 20 20 for Salary-Numeric
and
blank for Det-Salary.


Of course there is going to be a blank in Det-Salary
because nothing is moved there yet.

Any suggestions?

Thank you.

DATA DIVISION
       FD  SALARY-FILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 46 CHARACTERS.
       01  SALARY-RECORD.
           05  SAL-SALARY              PIC X(6).
           05  SALARY-NUMERIC
               REDEFINES SAL-SALARY    PIC 9(6).

WORKING STORAGE
      01  DETAIL-LINE.
           05 DET-SALARY               PIC $ZZZ,ZZ9.

PROCEDURE DIVISION
       900-WRITE-DETAIL-LINE.
           MOVE SALARY-NUMERIC TO DET-SALARY



Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: **Redefines

- **From:** Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38374350.F61C066B@home.nl>`
- **References:** `<8170u8$v72$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Bill wrote:
> 
> This is the first time I am using the REDEFINES.
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Any suggestions?

I don't know MF Cobol, but 20 is most likely 20 hex, or space (in
ASCII). So your Salary-Numeric field is filled with spaces, not with
zeroes.
Try "Move zero to SALARY-NUMERIC", or "MOVE 123456 TO SALARY-NUMERIC"
and then "MOVE SALARY-NUMERIC TO DET-SALARY", and see what happens. 

> 
> Thank you.
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: **Redefines

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1MHZ3.1355$VO5.31549@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<8170u8$v72$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Bill wrote in message <8170u8$v72$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>This is the first time I am using the REDEFINES.
>When I monitor program through Microfocus Animator it shows
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>


With a redefining field, you really cannot effectively use the VALUE IS
clause, nor does INITIALIZE work reliably.

You might try  MOVE ZERO TO SALARY-NUMERIC  before attempting to access the
field.

MCM
```

#### ↳ Re: **Redefines

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991125001351.20766.00000281@ng-fn1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8170u8$v72$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
The REDEFINES clause doesn't automatically perform a data conversion.  Whatever
underlying hex values are contained in SAL-SALARY, those same values will be
there if you reference SALARY-NUMERIC.  The two names refer to exactly the same
location in memory.

If you move alphanumeric values to SAL-SALARY and then reference
SALARY-NUMERIC, you don't suddenly have numeric hex values in there; it's still
the same (presumably) ASCII values, even though you re-defined the field as
numeric.  The only thing that WILL happen new if you reference SALARY-NUMERIC
after putting alpha in there is this.  If you try to perform an arithmetic
operation on SALARY-NUMERIC while alpha is in there, you will get an abend.

RoadRaat
```

---

<a id="t-71bae57e3e"></a>
## Thread: READ INTO (was: Re: Data conversion on READ (why not?)) — 4 msgs

### Re: READ INTO (was: Re: Data conversion on READ (why not?))

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991120154104.08843.00000543@ng-xa1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<8144ee$n3u$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
>The larger question is whether I would intentionally hide
>a conversion of the data by using a READ INTO or show
>the conversion by using separate READ and MOVE. My
>preference would be to show the conversion.

That was the point of my original post.  I thought it would be cool to break up
my MOVEs to working storage so my W.S image of my input record would already be
converted to the data-type I needed, instead of reading into a W.S. image and
then MOVEing to ANOTHER W.S. (converted) image.

I guess you are indicating your preference for the latter approach?

RoadRaat
```

#### ↳ Re: READ INTO (was: Re: Data conversion on READ (why not?))

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<816ngp$2faq$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<8144ee$n3u$1@news.hitter.net> <19991120154104.08843.00000543@ng-xa1.aol.com>`

```

RoadRaat wrote in message <19991120154104.08843.00000543@ng-xa1.aol.com>...
>>The larger question is whether I would intentionally hide
>>a conversion of the data by using a READ INTO or show
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>That was the point of my original post.  I thought it would be cool to
break up
>my MOVEs to working storage so my W.S image of my input record would
already be
>converted to the data-type I needed, instead of reading into a W.S. image
and
>then MOVEing to ANOTHER W.S. (converted) image.
>
>I guess you are indicating your preference for the latter approach?

Strictly speaking, my comment, above, does not pertain
to records with multiple fields.

However, on Nov 9, 1999, I posted comments in the thread
"Read versus Read Into and Write versus Write From"
where I expressed my preference for separate READ and
MOVE statements under certain cases, including conversion
of data.

Though you might not have seen, read, or remembered the
post, I felt that posting the same comments 8 days later was
too soon.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

<a id="t-5da6aa66e8"></a>
## Thread: Sequential Update — 5 msgs

### Sequential Update

- **From:** Bill <dc606@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81515d$me9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I am trying to write a program doing a sequential update.  I only have
an example of a program to do a "non"sequential update and in this
program INVALID KEY and NOT VALID KEY statements are used.  Of course,
my program is not even compiling properly.  I have a feeling a lot of
the errors are due to these statements.  Are these statements used soley
for nonsequential updates?  If so, what statements do I use for
sequential updates?

Thanks


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sequential Update

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<816fb7$nkk$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<81515d$me9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
KEYS are for keyed files.  That is, files with an index.  For a sequential
file, the only input variation is "AT END".   The verbs are READ, WRITE, and
REWRITE.

Bill wrote in message <81515d$me9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>I am trying to write a program doing a sequential update.  I only have
>an example of a program to do a "non"sequential update and in this
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sequential Update

- **From:** paulr@delphi.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38378efa$1$cnhye$mr2ice@news.stonemedia.com>`
- **References:** `<81515d$me9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Essentially, for a sequential file, you read, then rewrite. 
It can get a bit tricky depending upon exactly what kind of file it  is
though. Is this on an IBM mainframe with VSAM, or something else? -Paul



In <81515d$me9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>, on 11/20/99 
   at 02:31 AM, Bill <dc606@my-deja.com> said:

>I am trying to write a program doing a sequential update.  I only have an
>example of a program to do a "non"sequential update and in this program
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>updates?

>Thanks


>Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
>Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Sequential Update

- **From:** jgwolfe@netcom.ca (Gerry Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38384617.8066923@nntp.netcom.ca>`
- **References:** `<81515d$me9$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38378efa$1$cnhye$mr2ice@news.stonemedia.com>`

```
The REWRITE implies an update in place.  I wouldn't want to do that
for a couple of reasons:

1. If you run into problems, the original (input) file would be
corrupted and you would have to restore it before re-processing.
Assuming there was a backup done before the update!!!
2. Sequential file maintenance also implies adding new records or
perhaps deleting existing ones.  You can't do this with an
input/output file.

Instead, you probably want logic to match the input master file to an
input transaction file, creating a new master file as output.  This
would allow you to add/delete records, and not have to worry about
recovery if the job abended (ie simply re-run the job).

rgds, G.

On Sun, 21 Nov 1999 00:18:26 -0600, paulr@delphi.com wrote:
>Essentially, for a sequential file, you read, then rewrite. 
>It can get a bit tricky depending upon exactly what kind of file it  is
>though. Is this on an IBM mainframe with VSAM, or something else? -Paul
>
[snips here...]
```

#### ↳ Re: Sequential Update

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<e9qInyIN$GA.281@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<81515d$me9$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
What platform?
What compiler?
What type of source file?
What type of target file?
What does your code look like?
What errors are you getting?
Are you just looking for an algorithm that does sequential updating?
In place updating, or creation of an entirely new file with deletion of the
old (or GDG processing for MVS and related environments)?

What?


Bill <dc606@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:81515d$me9$1@nnrp1.deja.com...

> an example of a program to do a "non"sequential update and in this
```

```
Hi,
Just write the data, you want to export to a line sequential file. Every
field must be separated with semi-colon (';'). No separator before first
field and no separator after last field, For lazy people: 'use .CSV as
extension for the file.

In excel you just choos file, open and choose CSV-format, and your data will
be loaded.

This solution is universal to every possible compiler you might have.

regards
Ib
```

## Thread: Restore from corrupted ibm cobol/2 .dat file — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Restore from corrupted ibm cobol/2 .dat file

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3835E773.CECE8378@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-jmr0i467keR7@localhost> <3834E5A2.67416C4A@siber.com> <fycvU7zT9Qru-pn2-vxZ12UDfXC5D@localhost>`

```
Gregor Stuebner wrote:
> 
> > So you can use mfdata2flat from http://www.siber.com/datafile/
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> incorrectly.

Soory, my mistake. The correct URL is:

http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

> 
> Anyway - that program could solve my problem.
> Where can i get information about the internal structure of a .DAT
> file?

You need to have a source of any Cobol program that can
read or write this file. I understand that you have it.

You let this source go thru cbl2fdd program that extracts
all file definitions (SELECT and FD statements) and
puts them into FDD and RDD files (our own proprietary text format)
that basically encode the file and record structure.

Then you use any of the DataFile readers, such as 
mfdata2flat, mfdata2dbf, or mfdata2cr to convert
the data from data file to your favorite format.

Hope this helps.

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8150rm$m4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
1.I wrote the following program using subscripts and have now revised it
using indexes.  I had no problem with this program when I used
subscripts.  Now that I am using indexes, I get 2 x illegal character
(163)  errors. I can't figure out why I am getting these errors.
I have tried changing ORGANIZTION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL TO ORGANIZATION IS
INDEXED FOR VALIDX.DAT but get other errors.  Any suggestions?
I have put ************** where errors occur.

          SELECT SALARY-FILE ASSIGN TO 'A:\VALIDX.DAT'
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
           SELECT SALARY-REPORT ASSIGN TO 'A:\BIGBUCKS.DAT'
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  SALARY-FILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 46 CHARACTERS.
       01  SALARY-RECORD.
           05  SAL-SOC-SEC-NO          PIC X(9).
           05  SAL-NAME-AND-INITIALS   PIC X(15).
           05  SAL-BIRTH-DATE.
               10  SAL-BIRTH-MONTH     PIC 9(2).
               10  SAL-BIRTH-YEAR      PIC 9(2).
           05  SAL-LOCATION-CODE       PIC X(3).
           05  SAL-EDUCATION-CODE      PIC 9.
           05  SAL-TITLE-DATA.
               10  SAL-TITLE-CODE      PIC X(3).
               10  SAL-TITLE-DATE.
                   15  SAL-TITLE-MONTH PIC 9(2).
                   15  SAL-TITLE-YEAR  PIC 9(2).
           05  SAL-RATING              PIC X.
           05  SAL-SALARY              PIC X(6).

       FD  SALARY-REPORT
           RECORD CONTAINS 132 CHARACTERS.
       01  PRINT-LINE     PIC X(132).

       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  TITLE-VALUE.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '010PRESIDENT'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '020VICE PRES'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '030MKT VP'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '040MKT MGR'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '050MKT REP'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '060DP VP'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '070DP MGR'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '080DP PROG'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '090CLERK'.
           05 PIC X(18) VALUE '100ADM ASST'.

       01  TITLE-TABLE REDEFINES TITLE-VALUE.
           05  TITLES OCCURS 10 TIMES
               INDEXED BY TITLE-INDEX.
               10  TITLE-CODE          PIC X(3).
               10  EXP-TITLE           PIC X(15).
           05  REPORT-TITLE            PIC X(10).

       01  LOCATION-VALUE.
           05   PIC X(14)   VALUE 'MIAMIAMI'.
           05   PIC X(14)   VALUE 'CHICHICAGO'.
           05   PIC X(14)   VALUE 'LA LOS ANGELES'.
           05   PIC X(14)   VALUE 'NY NEW YORK'.
           05   PIC X(14)   VALUE 'ATLATLANTA'.

       01  LOCATION-TABLE REDEFINES LOCATION-VALUE.
           05   LOCATIONS OCCURS 5 TIMES
                INDEXED BY LOCATION-INDEX.
                10  LOCATION-CODE      PIC X(3).
                10  EXP-LOCATION       PIC X(11).

           05  REPORT-LOCATION         PIC X(14).

       01  EDUCATION-VALUE.
           05  PIC X(15)   VALUE '1GRADE SCHOOL'.
           05  PIC X(15)   VALUE '2HIGH SCHOOL'.
           05  PIC X(15)   VALUE '3ASSOCIATES'.
           05  PIC X(15)   VALUE '4BACHELORS'.
           05  PIC X(15)   VALUE '5MASTERS'.
           05  PIC X(15)   VALUE '6DOCTORATE'.

       01  EDUCATION-TABLE REDEFINES EDUCATION-VALUE.
           05   EDUCATIONS OCCURS 6 TIMES
                INDEXED BY EDUCATION-INDEX.
                10  EDUCATION-CODE     PIC 9.
                10  EXP-EDUCATION      PIC X(14).
           05   REPORT-EDUCATION       PIC X(15).

       01  DETAIL-LINE.
           05 DET-SSN                  PIC X(9).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X      VALUE SPACES.

           05 DET-NAME                 PIC X(15).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(4)   VALUE SPACES.

           05 DET-TITLE                PIC X(10).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(4)   VALUE SPACES.

           05 DET-LOCATION             PIC X(11).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(4)   VALUE SPACES.

           05 DET-EDUCATION            PIC X(12).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(3)   VALUE SPACES.

           05 DET-RATING               PIC X.
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2)   VALUE SPACES.

           05 DET-SALARY               PIC $ZZZ,ZZ9.

       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       100-PROCESS-SALARY-RECORDS.
           OPEN INPUT  SALARY-FILE
                OUTPUT SALARY-REPORT
           PERFORM 100-GET-TODAYS-DATE.
           PERFORM UNTIL WS-END-OF-FILE-SWITCH='YES'
               READ SALARY-FILE
                   AT END
                       MOVE 'YES' TO WS-END-OF-FILE-SWITCH
                   NOT AT END
                       PERFORM 400-CREATE-SALARY-REPORT
                       PERFORM 900-WRITE-DETAIL-LINE
               END-READ
           END-PERFORM.
           CLOSE SALARY-FILE
                 SALARY-REPORT.
           STOP RUN.

       100-GET-TODAYS-DATE.
               ACCEPT TODAYS-DATE FROM DATE.
               MOVE TODAYS-MONTH TO HDG-MONTH.
               MOVE TODAYS-DAY TO HDG-DAY.
               MOVE TODAYS-YEAR TO HDG-YEAR.

       400-CREATE-SALARY-REPORT.
           SET TITLE-INDEX TO 1.
           SEARCH TITLES
               AT END
                   MOVE "UNKNOWN" TO REPORT-TITLE
               WHEN SAL-TITLE-CODE=TITLE-CODE (TITLE-INDEX)
                   MOVE EXP-TITLE (TITLE-INDEX) TO REPORT-TITLE
           END-SEARCH.

           SET LOCATION-INDEX TO 1.
           SEARCH LOCATIONS
               AT END
                   MOVE "UNKNOWN" TO REPORT-LOCATION
**********ERROR163 WHEN SAL-LOCATION-CODE=LOCATION-CODE(LOCATION-INDEX)
                   MOVE EXP-LOCATION(LOCATION-INDEX) TO REPORT-LOCATION
           END-SEARCH.

           SET EDUCATION-INDEX TO 1.
           SEARCH EDUCATIONS
               AT END
                   MOVE "UNKNOWN" TO REPORT-EDUCATION
               WHEN SAL-EDUCATION-CODE=EDUCATION-CODE(LOCATION-INDEX)
                   MOVE EXP-EDUCATION(EDUCATION-INDEX) TO
                   REPORT-EDUCATION

           END-SEARCH.


       900-WRITE-DETAIL-LINE.
           MOVE SAL-SOC-SEC-NO TO DET-SSN
           MOVE SAL-NAME-AND-INITIALS TO DET-NAME
           MOVE REPORT-TITLE TO DET-TITLE
           MOVE REPORT-LOCATION TO DET-LOCATION
           MOVE REPORT-EDUCATION TO DET-EDUCATION
           MOVE SAL-RATING TO DET-RATING
           MOVE SAL-SALARY TO DET-SALARY****************ERROR 163
           WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL-LINE
           ADD 1 TO LINE-COUNT.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Subscripts & Indexes

- **From:** Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38365D5C.9C4568F1@home.nl>`
- **References:** `<8150rm$m4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


Bill wrote:
> 
> 1.I wrote the following program using subscripts and have now revised it
…[47 more quoted lines elided]…
>            05 PIC X(18) VALUE '100ADM ASST'.

Title-value has a length of 10 * 18 = 180 byte. 

> 
>        01  TITLE-TABLE REDEFINES TITLE-VALUE.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>            05  REPORT-TITLE            PIC X(10).

This redefine has a length of 10 * (3 + 15) + 10 = 190 byte, so it is
incorrect.

> 
>        01  LOCATION-VALUE.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>            05   PIC X(14)   VALUE 'ATLATLANTA'.

Location value has a length of 5 * 14 = 70 byte

> 
>        01  LOCATION-TABLE REDEFINES LOCATION-VALUE.
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>            05  REPORT-LOCATION         PIC X(14).

The redefine has a length of 5 * ( 3 + 11 ) + 14 = 84 byte, again
incorrect.

> 
>        01  EDUCATION-VALUE.
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>            05   REPORT-EDUCATION       PIC X(15).

Same problem here

> 
>        01  DETAIL-LINE.
…[78 more quoted lines elided]…
>            MOVE SAL-SALARY TO DET-SALARY****************ERROR 163

Sal-Salery is a Alphanumeric X(6) field, Det-Salary is a Numeric-Edited
field  
Sal-Salery should be a Numeric Field 9(6) .

>            WRITE PRINT-LINE FROM DETAIL-LINE
>            ADD 1 TO LINE-COUNT.
> 
> Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
> Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Subscripts & Indexes

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<815ssj$1trt$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<8150rm$m4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <38365D5C.9C4568F1@home.nl>`

```

Dirk Munk wrote in message <38365D5C.9C4568F1@home.nl>...
>
>Bill wrote:
[...]
>>            MOVE SAL-SALARY TO DET-SALARY****************ERROR 163
>
>Sal-Salery is a Alphanumeric X(6) field, Det-Salary is a Numeric-Edited
>field
>Sal-Salery should be a Numeric Field 9(6) .

A move of alphanumeric to numeric-edited is permissible.

The move would be made, AS IF the alphanumeric field
were described as numeric; that is, AS IF the X(6) were
described as 9(6).

The error suggests that SAL-SALARY has characters
other than numbers. Since this is a student problem,
the field most likely has leading spaces.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

#### ↳ Re: Subscripts & Indexes

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8168ro$25uc$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<8150rm$m4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Bill wrote in message <8150rm$m4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>1.I wrote the following program using subscripts and have now revised it
>using indexes.  I had no problem with this program when I used
>subscripts.  Now that I am using indexes, I get 2 x illegal character
>(163)  errors. I can't figure out why I am getting these errors.
[...]
>I have put ************** where errors occur.
[...]
>**********ERROR163 WHEN SAL-LOCATION-CODE=LOCATION-CODE(LOCATION-INDEX)

I do not see any possible way to get a 163 on this line.

>               WHEN SAL-EDUCATION-CODE=EDUCATION-CODE(LOCATION-INDEX)

You could get a 163 on this line if SAL-EDUCATION-CODE
contains a SPACE.

The two 163 errors, when taken together, allow me to
conclude that you may not be reading a valid record.
Perhaps a record that contains all spaces.

Use the Animator to examine the contents of the record
after the READ.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

---

#### ↳ Re: View Readable Indexed file

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<JQyZ3.201$VO5.7026@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<815103$mc6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Depends on how you defined the index file - if YOU defined the indexing.

If you let the COBOL compiler handle it (i.e., ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
RECORD KEY IS whatever), then the structure of the index file is determined
by the compiler publisher and the answer is almost surely, "No."  Some
compilers create a separate physical file for the index, some include the
index info in the data file.

Regardless, in general the only way to read data from a COBOL indexed file
is to use a COBOL program created using the same compiler as was used to
create the software which created the data.

Michael Mattias
Tal Systems
Racine WI USA
The views and opinions expressed herein are my own.
As I am self-employed, they also express the views of my employer.
```

#### ↳ Re: View Readable Indexed file

- **From:** Vadim Maslov <vadik@siber.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3836D1EF.280DEC4D@siber.com>`
- **References:** `<815103$mc6$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Bill wrote:
> 
> I created an index file and tried to view it using NotePad. It is one
> big long line.  Is there any way of viewing this file in a readable
> format?

Siber Systems sells a number of products that convert
your Cobol data file to flat text file that contains
readable values separated by commas, one record per line.

So once your file is written to by Cobol program,
you can use our data2flat converter to quickly convert
it to text format and see what's really in there.

More on this at http://www.siber.com/sct/datafile/

Regards,
Vadim Maslov
```

<a id="t-2f835a8834"></a>
## Thread: Sequential Update — 3 msgs

### Sequential Update

- **From:** Bill <dc606@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<815179$meg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I am trying to write a program doing a sequential update.  I only have
an example of a program to do a "non"sequential update and in this
program INVALID KEY and NOT VALID KEY statements are used.  Of course,
my program is not even compiling properly.  I have a feeling a lot of
the errors are due to these statements.  Are these statements used soley
for nonsequential updates?  If so, what statements do I use for
sequential updates?

Thanks


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sequential Update

- **From:** Dirk Munk <munk@home.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38365695.B9DDDD6F@home.nl>`
- **References:** `<815179$meg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
It depends on the way you defined to access the file in the Environment
division.

If you use "Access is Sequential" than a read will get you the next
record, and a rewrite will rewrite the present record. If I'm not
mistaken a write can only be done if the primairy key is higher than the
highest primary record already in the file. The file will always be
accessed in sequence of the primary key!

If you use "Access is Random" you will have to fill one of the keys of
the record, an a subsequent "Read Key is key-just-filled" will give you
the record you want unless there are more records with the same key
value , or you get a "Invalid" situation. A Write and Rewrite can
allways be done in this mode, unless of course you duplicate errors etc.

With "Access is Dynamic" you get the best of both worlds. You can still
use the same method as with Random, but you can also use a "Read Next"
to read the next record in sequence of the key you specified the in last
Read. There is also a Start command you can use. Let's say you want to
read keys with a value higher than 100, then you can use the following
code:

Move 100 to key-I-need
Start file Key > Key-I-need
(invalid or not-invalid etc.)
Read file Next  

This code will give you the first record with a key > 100.

Hop this helps

Regards,

Dirk
   

Bill wrote:
> 
> I am trying to write a program doing a sequential update.  I only have
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Sequential Update

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<IQyZ3.200$VO5.7026@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<815179$meg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
[NOT ] INVALID KEY is used only on I-O for files with access random or
dynamic.  Sequential files use a relative key, indexed files use one or more
record keys.

For sequential files, you use READ and REWRITE with no intervening access of
the target file. (This may not be supported by some COBOLs on some operating
systems, I'm not sure of that).
```

## Thread: HELP! — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: HELP!

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<815u7r$1us7$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<8154r6$hv$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
What it is telling you that *a* file name is an "Illegal file
name" on the OPEN statement. Because two files are
OPENed in the same statement, one of the two has an
"Illegal file name". In this case, it is likely the file-name
for FINAL-REPORT, device-choice, which contains
spaces.

The file-name for FINAL-REPORT is filled in
700-DISPLAY-RTN.

Try PERFORMing 700-DISPLAY-RTN before you
OPEN the files.
------------------
Rick Smith

SArndt wrote in message <8154r6$hv$1@nntp1.atl.mindspring.net>...
>Okay, I am posting a program.... it compiles just fine on Microfocus, but
it
>won't run... it says "student-data" is an illegal file name. I don't know
>why....
[...]
>       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
>       100-MAIN-MODULE.
Insert    perform 700-display-rtn   here
>           OPEN INPUT  STUDENT-DATA
>                OUTPUT FINAL-REPORT
>           PERFORM 200-HEADING-RTN
```

<a id="t-8116fdde5d"></a>
## Thread: y2k- entire mainframe recompile — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Y2K and Year-2000 remediation`](topics.md#y2k) · [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: y2k- entire mainframe recompile

- **From:** "Ira D. Baxter" <idbaxter@semdesigns.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s3dfr2ph3ef18@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<r6dc3s8m6fslq1q7gpsuft9c4ata59jqmj@4ax.com>`

```
I claim such massive code changes can be carried out, in practice.

You probably can't avoid recompiling the entire million-line source code
base,
but that might merely be an overnight problem.    The database conversion
you can do incrementally: simply add a parallel relation which records for
every record whether it is expanded or not, initally marking all records as
"unexpanded".
(Many of the Y2K fixes hide this bit in the existing representation of the
year,
such that the current year values essentially indicate "unexpanded").

Then the code needs to read records and consult the "unexpanded" bit
to decide how to complete the read.   On a write, always write the expanded
form and set the bit.   At some point, run a utility to clean up the
unconverted files.

So this is sort of easy.

What's hard is getting the entire source base to change overnight.  You
cannot do
this by hand.    You need a tool that reads the entire source base (between
application programmer revisision, so probably overnight, or at best over a
day or two),
and make consistent code changes to accomplish the above.

See http://www.semdesigns.com/Products/DMSToolkit.htm to see a tool
that could be used for this purpose.

-- IDB

records....
>

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>10% of the problem takes 90% of the time, right?
```

#### ↳ Re: y2k- entire mainframe recompile

- **From:** "Unbeliever" <popsider@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81793p$127$1@nntp6.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<r6dc3s8m6fslq1q7gpsuft9c4ata59jqmj@4ax.com> <s3dfr2ph3ef18@corp.supernews.com>`

```
Not sure if this is what was being asked, but - online database
re-engineering can reasonably be accomplished using shadow
databases.

Create a second copy of the DB & make the OS point O/L Apps.
at the shadow, instead of the original. Then use the original (or latest
image copy), to unload/reload with a user exit in the reload to re-engineer,
with the new DBD.

I'm trying to convince my current clients to use switchable DB's
for an application that has 24 hour O/L window, but needs to take
the DB down for update & reorg. Of course this requires more
disk & CPU resources, so they are reticent. But eventually they
will see that this is the optimum solution - I hope!

> G Moore wrote in message ...

…[18 more quoted lines elided]…

```
