G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote:

: also, it has been rumored that ms puts bugs into some of it's products
: for the sole purpose of keeping out competitors, having larger hard
: drives and spiking technical pay-per-call support lines.

There is a known deliberate bug in Excel.  This was introduced
by MS as a means of identifying if a competitor had reverse
engineered and copied the core code.  Certain values give a
deliberately wrong result.

But MS are not the only ones to make code tracable for
copyright reasons, that is how MS were caught out when they
stole Stacker, it is also how DRI proved that MS-DOS 1.0
used reverse engineered code from CP/M 1.4 (DOS carried over
a bug from CP/M's BDOS on a file close) forcing IBM to
rewrite all the code eliminating all DRI code and making
MS-DOS 1.1 as a complete rewrite.

So MS are not even innovative in using this mechanism.

They stole that idea too.
```

<a id="t-5f989a89ed"></a>
## Thread: Data Recovery — 1 msgs

### Re: Data Recovery

- **From:** PAL3000 <pduboisREMOVETHIS@enteract.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-20T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EwM3OIuSBx=IMU0URBrorSutPFJu@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3836DF4F.37651D72@cobi.qc.ca>`

```
I work a lot with Real World accounting software.  The RM85 version
comes with file utilities.

Just go to your main Realword directory and type:

   run85 FIX??

where ?? is the module that contains the file you want to fix.  For
example, Customer file is in AR so you would type run85 FIXAR

Then you will be presented with a menu.  Select "Recover a file in
place" and then you will be presented with a list of files to choose
from.  Choose your file then this will run the RM utility to rebuild
the file.  

I would recomend making a backup of the file before doing this just in
case something happens.

Good luck,

Paul

F1.dat, F2.dat, F3.dat and F4.dat?  I've currently got them in the same 
directory as the program.  Is this right?  Or should I be specifying 
something like "C:\cobol\F1.dat"???

Thanks

Neil
neilmunn@ukonline.co.uk

### How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** ba600@FreeNet.Carleton.CA (Mike Kenzie)
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca>`

```

Some students were asking just how big a mainframe was.

In the text I found that a 3350 was 649,000,000 bytes thats 649 MEGS!

I now have 128 MEG of RAM and 15 GIG of HD on my PC.  What is it that
makes the mainframe so powerfull and expensive?

Are the newer drives much bigger I noticed that there are now 3390's
installed.  How much acutal RAM is in the machine?
```

wrote:

>Some students were asking just how big a mainframe was.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…

It's really not how big it is, it's how it works.  Where I used to
work they have a 4361 Mainframe that has about a gig of disk storage
and 2 meg of Ram.  But it's a mainframe, because of how it works.

>and 2 meg of Ram.  But it's a mainframe, because of how it works.  

You're absolutely right, Thane. But for the "speeds and feeds" crowd, I see the
latest boxes from IBM can come with 32GB ram, 10 or more processors, and can
support thousands of disk drives, each with over 10GB capacity. Then you can
tie many of these systems together. Of course, there's also tape cartridge
devices, robot tape libraries, laser printers, and network support. On and
on...

#### ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81987b$g7l$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca>`

```
Hi,

First of all - never compare a mainframe with a PC. The technology is very
different, though many of the diciplines from the mainframe world will be
made availlable to the pc-world.

Try to arrange an excursion to a large mainframe installation. I think many
students would benfit from that, and some of your teachers as well.

The mainframes are often mentioned similar to Jurasic Park, but even if that
area still use dumb terminals, there is a tremendous number of intelligent
clients (PC's, LAN-Servers (PC and Unix)) connected.

You mentioned 3350 as if only one was used. I do not think that 3350
actually is used any more, but that is not important. A mainframe could
easily have several hundred disk units connected. They are connected in
strings to avoid contention.

When did you last make a bacup of your HD. How long will it take you to
recover your data if your HD crashes. What's going on in the mainframe world
is extreemly different. Surface bacups of the disc units are running
continiously and image-copies and backup tasks are running in fixed
schedules. I have seen recovery after a reel fire in a disc unit made within
3 hours. Most of the time was spent to move data around to make room for the
restore process. The customers only lost data from the last 'ENTER' before
close-down.

Try real hard to make the excursion. Prepare all your questions and send
them in advance. That will enable the company, you are visiting, to get the
answers for you. Time is money, and if you respect that, I think, you would
get valuable information of the technology.

Good luck
Ib Tanding

Mike Kenzie skrev i meddelelsen <8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca>...
>
>Some students were asking just how big a mainframe was.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>installed.  How much acutal RAM is in the machine?
```

The latest G6-CMOS Server (A/K/A Mainframe) executes 1.6 billion
instructions per second.

As far as how big it is, I believe that someone has you thinking that
Mainframes are these behemoths that require floor space that rivals
that of a bakers-dozen of '59 Cadillacs. That may have been true two
decades (or more) ago, but nowadays they're a lean, mean processing
machine.

As Steve has suggested, a tour of a Mainframe shop will give you a
greater appreciation and understanding of dinosaurs and their natural
habitat :-)

Cheers,

WOB


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "Unbeliever" <popsider@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<819ae0$c55$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca>`

```
There have already been plenty of responses about the comparative
RAM & processors.

But for me, possibly the biggest difference is in the system software.
A mainframe will often run multiple operating systems such as VM or
MVS, nested inside each other, with applications running in different
logical places.

The most common would be where a mainframe has a "live" and
"test" environment. Also, there are multiple environments, controlled
by database operational software such as IMS or CICS. My current
client has perhaps as many as 50 different IMS environments. This
is a large company, with many different projects going on simultaneously.
But, This also highlights another important difference between PCs
and mainframe - software release/version control.

Having said all this, with the growth in client/server & distributed
platforms
the differences are diminishing. But hopefully the client/server world won't
be reduced the state of the mainframe, where software releases can be a
truly painful experience - once the applications programmer has completed
his testing, the pain has only just begun!


"Mike Kenzie" <ba600@FreeNet.Carleton.CA> wrote in message
news:8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca...
>
> Some students were asking just how big a mainframe was.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> installed.  How much acutal RAM is in the machine?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38396006.56253B2C@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <819ae0$c55$1@nntp8.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Unbeliever wrote:

> Having said all this, with the growth in client/server & distributed
> platforms
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> his testing, the pain has only just begun!

On the other hand, the needs for lots of people to handle lots of data
securely are increasing.  Think of a data warehouse on a large intranet
with various customers having different access to parts of your
warehouse - all needing reliable and quick simultaneous usage.  While
E-Bay can run on Unix (at least until it crashed for a couple of days),
sometimes your needs require a faster, more secure, and bigger data
environment.  

But I guess your point is, comparing PCs and Minis with main frames used
to be like comparing sports cars and vans with trains.  Now it is more
like comparing trucks with trains.  Sometimes the trucks win the battles
to deliver what is needed more cheaply and more flexibly.  But other
times trains win.
```

#### ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
On 21 Nov 1999 at 15:08, ba600@FreeNet.Carleton.CA (Mike Kenzie) said:

>In the text I found that a 3350 was 649,000,000 bytes thats 649 MEGS!

The 3350 is over 20 years old, your text-book needs to be updated.

>What is it that makes the mainframe so powerful and expensive?

For me, it's the number of users you can connect to it that makes it BIG. 
A S/390 sysplex can support tens of thousands of concurrent users with
99.999% availability.  You don't get this kind of numbers with Unix, let
alone NT.

 Gilbert Saint-flour
 http://members.home/net/gsf/

#### ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<BPWZ3.259$gz2.5084@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca>`

```

Mike Kenzie <ba600@FreeNet.Carleton.CA

> Some students were asking just how big a mainframe was.
>

While I've worked on 'mainframes,' I, too, had no idea until I
visited the Shell Datacenter in Houston. The 'computer' room
was bigger than an airplane hanger with maybe 50 'big irons'
from IBM, CDC, UNIVAC, and others, dynamic satellite tracking
maps on the wall monitoring data connections, pipelines, and
ships at sea, UPS stations as big as house trailers
(generators in the back yard), maybe 200 nameless souls
to hang tapes, replenish paper, etc. All this stuff, we were told,
connected to 50,000 data terminals around the globe.

I was visiting as a vendor - our shop had four big machines
and 250,000 reels of tape - and was impressed.

The most memorable thing, though, was our 'guide' who
said: "Remember the oil crunch under Carter? Just
imagine what this room was like...." and kind of trailed
off into a shudder. No one, not even I, had the courage
to ask him to continue.

A good definition of a "Mainframe" is: it's the most
expensive peripherial you can connect to your PC.
```

- **From:** theodp@aol.com (Theo DP)

That was then...this is now!

Now-obolete DASD (e.g., your 3350's) consumed most of the floorspace in earlier
large-scale installations.

For example, my current client recently replaced more than two thirds of their

#### ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe? (physical size)

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<OkIlAIJN$GA.249@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca>`

```
The actual size of a mainframe is not related to it's processing capacity
and storage.

Older mainframes were quite large.  We had an old 3384 4 processor mainframe
and it was the size of about 8 large refrigerators bolted together (in light
blue cabinets; thus comes "Big Blue").

Our 3390-300E had 6 large refrigerator size cabinets, only 3 processors, and
kicked the 3384 around the block on processing power (50% increase).  It had
128MB of central storage (effectively RAM), and 128MB Expanded Storage (RAM
used as a phantom hard drive for high speed data caching, although it had
(and still has, I think, though IBM is trying to bypass this) the problem
that data set to Expanded Storage must be first pulled back to central
storage in order to actually move it back to a real hard disk; in other
words, central storage stood between expanded storage and the hard disks).
We got this machine used in 1994, and it was probably built back in the
early 80s.

Both of these mainframes required several large auxilliary components,
amounting to about 8 further large cabinets, including two Power
Distribution Units, and a water cooler, all three were each the size of two
very large fridges side by side.

The last mainframe we got was an IBM 9672 RB4 (later upgraded to R24) with 1
gigabyte of storage divided up between three "partitions" each running a
copy of MVS/XA 2.2.3 (yes, we were still running that late 80's version in
the late 90's).

The 9672 RB4 is the size of a single fridge, was jet black, and had a red
stripe down the middle of the front cabinet door, quite a departure from the
old hardline blue cabinets.  It also kicked the the 3390 around the block on
processing power, although it was running the old MVS/XA system in
"emulation", being by hardware design meant to run the MVS/ESA and later
software, and would have run much faster on that operating system.

The 9672 RB4 saved us roughly $2000 a month in electricity alone, and
required no external support units of any kind (no water cooler for this air
cooled mainframe).  Overall, it was much cheaper to run than old mainframes,
and wasn't a whole lot bigger than our big NCR and SUN unix servers, and
unlike those two machines, the mainframe ran 80% of a $3 billion dollar a
year retail enterprise.  It took one 6 processor NCR server (at about 80%
the size of the 9672) just to run check-clearance.  The mainframe ran
accounts receivable and payable, general legder, warehouse supply chain and
purchasing (for three warehouse complexes and 150 stores), retail pricing,
payroll, EDI, a DB2 region, a 500 user online CICS system for access to all
these and many other subsystems, plus two more CICS and DB2 regions for
development and modeling.  In short, we got far more for our money on that
mainframe than we did on any other platform, all while it had one hand tied
behind it's back by an obsolete operating system.

Mike Kenzie <ba600@FreeNet.Carleton.CA> wrote in message
news:8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca...
>
> Some students were asking just how big a mainframe was.
>
> In the text I found that a 3350 was 649,000,000 bytes thats 649 MEGS!

Oh, I'm also fairly sure that the old 3350 mainframes did not have 649 megs
of central storage, that was probably an auxilliary storage figure, one
which is strictly dependent on how many strings of DASD (Direct Access
Storage Device; the old mainframe term for racks of hard drives in IBM style
blue cabinets) or other storage system was connected.

> I now have 128 MEG of RAM and 15 GIG of HD on my PC.  What is it that
> makes the mainframe so powerfull and expensive?

Costs on mainframes are way down since the old days of the 3350 (the 70s),
although they still aren't as far down as the really need to be (they can
still give people sticker shock once all the widgets and doodads of 3rd
party software utilities and system aids are added).  Many oftware vendors
especially still price the way they did in the 70s and 80s, not having
reduced costs the same way IBM, Ahmdahl, and the others have done.


> Are the newer drives much bigger I noticed that there are now 3390's
> installed.  How much acutal RAM is in the machine?
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe? (physical size)

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3838C6D4.EE9ED989@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <OkIlAIJN$GA.249@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```


ChrisOsborne wrote:
...
> Older mainframes were quite large.  We had an old 3384 4 processor mainframe
> and it was the size of about 8 large refrigerators bolted together (in light
> blue cabinets; thus comes "Big Blue").
...
Try 3084, the 4 processor model in the family that included 3083 (1
processor), and 3081 (2 processor)-- the numbering scheme appears
irrational, but I believe relates to order in which models became
available.
...
> 
> Mike Kenzie <ba600@FreeNet.Carleton.CA> wrote in message
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > In the text I found that a 3350 was 649,000,000 bytes thats 649 MEGS!

That 649 MB figure has got to be the result of misinterpretation of the
3350 specs. The 3350's were packaged as two drives in a single box, but
the maximum effective capacity of a single logical drive was only 19069
x 30 x 555, or about 317.5 MB (that's "real Greek" 10**6 MB, not funny
2**20 "MB", but in any event this was late 1970's technology, long
obsolete. You didn't run with just one, but with multiple strings of up
to 8 logical drives per string.  By 1984 when we started to phase out
3350's in place of 3380 drives, we had 8 strings of 8 drives, or around
20 GB on the floor.  PC's were certainly not generally available when
the 3350 was new, and if there were any in lab environments at that time
their hard drives would have been miniscule.  It wasn't until late 80's
that a 20 MB PC hard drive became "small".

Comparing present day PC's with mainframes of about 20 years ago is
about right.  In terms of gross throughput, hardware reliability, and
Operating System reliability PC's are at least 20 years behind
mainframes, and in 2020 people will be comparing their successors to the
mainframes of today, but mainframe technology will have moved beyond as
well.
...
> 
> > Are the newer drives much bigger I noticed that there are now 3390's
> > installed.  How much acutal RAM is in the machine?

Capacity of a logical 3390 drive is around 2.8 GB (10**9 Bytes) for a
3390-3 (triple density).  Real 3390 DASD was packaged with 4 logical
drives in a unit the size of a large refrigerator and could be combined
into "lonnnng" strings of up to 32 logical drives (imagine 8 large
refrigerators bolted side by side), but the only real 3390's available
today are in the used market.  They have been replaced by newer DASD
subsystems which still look like 3390 drives to the operating system,
but which emulate the 3390 architecture (track size, tracks per
cylinder, and cylinder count) using 3.5" drives and which take less than
a 30th of the floor space.  Today we have three refrigerator-sized boxes
with somewhere in the neighborhood of 900+ GB of emulated 3390's
attached to a single mainframe (values are approximate because hardware
data compression is involved and the hardware is physically capable of
supporting much more if we pay more for the priviledge of using it).  

The processing power (and RAM) in just the controller of one mainframe
disk subsystem today is almost 3 orders of magnitude more powerful than
our mainframe of 20 years ago.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe? (physical size)

- **From:** ba600@FreeNet.Carleton.CA (Mike Kenzie)
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81bso4$2hb@freenet-news.carleton.ca>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <OkIlAIJN$GA.249@cpmsnbbsa03> <3838C6D4.EE9ED989@acm.org>`

```

"Joel C. Ewing" (jcewing@acm.org) writes:

were DASD units.  Come to think of it, "3350" was probably a very old

Our 3390 DASD units were all used, there were either model "-2" or "-3", I
can't remember.  We had 4 full strings, and four 3990 controllers (not "-6"
models, but older) all side by side and set up for dual framing. They did

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe? (physical size)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81q8u811arh@enews3.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <OkIlAIJN$GA.249@cpmsnbbsa03> <3838C6D4.EE9ED989@acm.org> <u6#eaDRN$GA.281@cpmsnbbsa03>`

```
Our mainframe is a IBM 9672 CPU.  We have 2GB of RAM and 48 I/O channels.  I
don't know what that means exactly but I guess 48 files can be opened at
once.  I've seen the computer run 20-30 jobs simultaneously.  I was told we
have like 1900GB of storage but I dunno if that means tapes as well.  We
have alot of tapes (1,000s).  We run OS/390 but less than a year ago we were
running MVS/ESA.  The actualy CPU is probably a little bigger than a
standard household refrigerator and it is either brown or black.  The DASD
3390s flashes a bunch of lights but I don't know what that means.  In the
time I've worked there (about a year), they've 'cicled' the mainframe once
as far as I know.  Shit, our gateway server fails nearly every day.  The
network people call it a hiccup; I call it a smoke break.

Jeff

----------

```
On Sat, 27 Nov 1999 22:52:39 -0500 "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
wrote:

:>Our mainframe is a IBM 9672 CPU.  We have 2GB of RAM and 48 I/O channels.  I
:>don't know what that means exactly but I guess 48 files can be opened at
:>once.  

No, it means there can potentially be 48 concurrent I/O operations, i.e., 48
different disks could be reading/writing at the same time.

You can have ten of thousands of files open at one time.

I am not familiar with that model so I cannot be more specific.

:>       I've seen the computer run 20-30 jobs simultaneously.  

+ some large number of TSO users + some large number of CICS users + etc.

:>                                                              I was told we
:>have like 1900GB of storage but I dunno if that means tapes as well.  

Unlikely.

Most likely disk space.

   [ snipped ]
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe? (physical size)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#6SihTdO$GA.51@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <OkIlAIJN$GA.249@cpmsnbbsa03> <3838C6D4.EE9ED989@acm.org> <u6#eaDRN$GA.281@cpmsnbbsa03> <81q8u811arh@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```

Jeff Baynard <union27@macconnect.com> wrote in message
news:81q8u811arh@enews3.newsguy.com...
> Our mainframe is a IBM 9672 CPU.  We have 2GB of RAM and 48 I/O channels.
I
> don't know what that means exactly but I guess 48 files can be opened at

When you have 48 I/0 "Channels", you have 48 different actual full blown
computers (inside the mainframe and in addition to the CPUs), each one of
which can be passed a set of information retrieval commands (a "channel
program") so that the CPU can then go on it's merry way until the Channel
gets back to the CPU with a message that it has the information, and then
the CPU gets back to the Channel when it's ready.

As to how many files the CPU can have open at once?  Heck, with two
gigabytes of central storage (while it is physical RAM, that's not the
terminology of mainframes) is enough to have . . .  Hmm, I don't actually
know the max files open for MVS in any version, but lets say that even on
heavy operating nights, with max schedules running, never once did we ever,
ever get a message informing us we had too many files open.  I would hazard
a guess that we had as many (including CICS) as 1,000 - 2,000 files open at
any one time during the day, and maybe (not including CICS) fifty to two
hundred at once during nightly batch cycles.

Offhand, and in speculation, I would think the maximum number of files that
MVS could open at once would be heavily linked into the Global Resource
Serialization software; but as I said, that just a guess.  I only know a
little bit about how GRS controls locking, not it's full operating limits.

> once.  I've seen the computer run 20-30 jobs simultaneously.  I was told
we
> have like 1900GB of storage but I dunno if that means tapes as well.  We

It probably doesn't include tapes.  We only had about 600 or so gigs of
DASD, but we had about 6 terrabytes of old 3480 tapes, and we only had about
12,000 of them.  This is usually considered quite small for a tape library.
(Each 3480 tape holds a rough average of 500 megabytes, sometimes as low as
200, and sometimes as much as 2 gigs)

> have alot of tapes (1,000s).  We run OS/390 but less than a year ago we
were

What kind of tape do you have?  3490E tapes hold (on average) four times
more than a 3480.

> running MVS/ESA.  The actualy CPU is probably a little bigger than a
> standard household refrigerator and it is either brown or black.  The DASD

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe? (physical size)

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** SimonLElliott <"Simon.L.Elliott[Delete.Me]"@CapGemini.Co.UK>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3842BA5A.2E27B842@CapGemini.Co.UK>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <OkIlAIJN$GA.249@cpmsnbbsa03> <3838C6D4.EE9ED989@acm.org> <u6#eaDRN$GA.281@cpmsnbbsa03> <81q8u811arh@enews3.newsguy.com>`

```
Something I've not seen mentioned in this discussion which I think is significant
is storage protection.

It's my understanding that while PC architectures use a form of storage
protection, it isn't supported by the hardware in the way it is on a mainframe.
That's why it's easy for a poorly written application (and you can find them
everywhere) to crash a PC OS, requiring a re-boot. You really have to try very
hard to do the same thing on a mainframe.
```

```
In five years, I saw MVS crash on our mainframe once, on a "diabled spin
loop", I suppose something violated the hierarchy of locks.  The 80s era
3090-300E cache was unreliable and crashed the whole machine several times,
but MVS was very stable no matter what sort of user application crashed on
it (which happened frequently).

I remember working on the IBM 1130 when I started out.  It only had 1 hard
drive attached to it and it was half he size of the card reader.
After that I worked on VAX and AMDAHL's but never got a close look at the
machines.  When I backed up VAX clusters I noticed that the mini VAX
3000's were desktop size, but didn't notice the HD's just the tapes I
needed.  I knew the max blocksize of the 3390's I was using but never
thought about the actual capacity of the drive just that there was 4
TERA-bytes of DASD upstairs and tape silos for any overflow.  When I found
the able listing the 3380 as a 650 MEG drive I was suprised.  The students
I was teaching were familiar with a VAX 6380 that they have in the corner.

I have waited almost a full day for a compile of a small COBOL program on
the mainframe when I compile at over 30000 lines per second on my home PC.
I have seen SQL queries bring a small mini to it's knees and shops drop
the VAX cluster to go to a pentium network for the annual cost of the mini.
 I have also had 2 weeks off due to system failure during last years ice
storm, I suspect that the delay was partially due to people having trouble
actually getting to the machines and the network that connected us.

#### ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3838CD00.4243F32B@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca>`

```
Mike Kenzie wrote:
> 
> Some students were asking just how big a mainframe was.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> installed.  How much acutal RAM is in the machine?

Nowadays, physically not that big. Visit www.ibm.com, do a find on 'G6', and
take a look. As far as why it is so expensive, etc., below is an extract
from the latest Alan Freedman 'Desktop Computer Encyclopedia':

-------------------------------
Mainframe

A large computer.  In the "ancient" mid 1960s, all computers were called
mainframes, since the term referred to the main CPU cabinet.  Today, it
refers to a large computer system.
  There are small, medium and large-scale mainframes, handling from a
handful to tens of thousands of online terminals.  Large-scale mainframes
support multiple gigabytes of main memory and terabytes of disk storage. 
Large mainframes use smaller computers as front end processors that connect
to the communications networks.
  The original mainframe vendors were Burroughs, Control Data, GE,
Honeywell, IBM, NCR, RCA and Univac, otherwise known as "IBM and the Seven
Dwarfs."  After GE and RCA's computer divisions were absorbed by Honeywell
and Univac respectively, the mainframers were known as "IBM and the BUNCH."
  IBM has the lion's share of the mainframe business, and Hitachi Data
Systems and Amdahl are its major competitors, making System/390-compatible
computers (see IBM-compatible mainframe).  Unisys, Sun, Digital and others
make mainframes or mainframe-class machines, but run under proprietary or
UNIX-based operating systems, not IBM's OS/390.

There Is a Difference!

One might wonder why mainframes cost up to several millions of dollars when
their raw megahertz (MHz) or MIPS rates are no higher than a PC costing
1,000 times less.  There are reasons.  Firstly, in a small computer such as
a PC, the CPU does almost all the processing.  Unless direct memory transfer
(DMA) is used, the CPU is also involved with getting data to and from the
peripherals, the most time-consuming part of the whole operation.
  A mainframe provides enormous amounts of throughput by offloading its
input/output processing to a peripheral channel, which is a computer in
itself.  Mainframes can support hundreds of channels, up to 512 in some
models. Mainframes also have multiple ports into memory and especially into
high-speed caches, which can be 10 times faster than main memory. 
Additional computers may act as I/O traffic cops between the CPU and the
channels and handle the processing of exceptions (what happens if the
channel is busy, if it fails, etc.).  All these subsystems handle the
transaction overhead, freeing the CPU to do real "data processing" such as
computing balances in customer records and subtracting amounts from
inventories, the purpose of the computer in the first place.
  Secondly, the internal bus transfer rates of mainframes are also higher
than small computers.  A 200MHz Pentium has a data bus that runs at 66MHz,
but a 200MHz mainframe may have a data bus that also runs at 200MHz, three
times as fast.  The multipliers add up.  Three times the bus speed, 10 times
the cache speed, perhaps 32 or 64 overlapped data transfers.  Multiply one
times the other, and the combination of fast buses, fast caches, multiple
memory ports and independent channels and subsystems makes a processing
machine unlike anything else.
  Thirdly, much of the hardware circuitry in a mainframe is designed to
detect and correct errors. Every subsystem is continuously monitored for
potential failure, in some cases even triggering a list of parts to be
replaced at the next scheduled downtime.  As a result, mainframes are
incredibly reliable.  The mean time between failure (MTBF) is generally 20
years!
  In addition, mainframes are highly scalable. Based on symmetric
multiprocessing (SMP), mainframes can be expanded by adding CPUs to a system
or by adding systems in clusters.

They're Here to Stay

Once upon a time, mainframes meant "complicated" and required the most
programming and operations expertise.  That is no longer.  Networks of
client/server-based PCs make mainframes look easy. Nothing is more
complicated than the DOS/Windows environment.  Add NetWare, throw in a
little UNIX for good measure, and you have enterprise computing at its most
complex ever.  With more than two trillion dollars worth of mainframe
applications in place, mainframes are here to stay, and their centralized
architecture, which is easiest to manage, may just be the wave of the
future!
-----------------------------

The only thing I can add is that compared to the modern G5/G6 Enterprise
Servers, the figures above seem way too conservative.

Kind regards,
==================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, Fl 
==================
```

#### ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00

```
many things, not the least of which  is a much LESS BLOATED OPERATING SYSTEM
MORE EFFICIENT COMPILERS,
AND
about 1000 times more BANDWIDTH.

Harddrives that have access times 1/10 that of your PC, and transfer rates
several times that.
It SNEERS at a transfer rate of 1 or 2 MB per second.

here's a intherealworld example:  while one system is processing 600
reservationists work at the
same time,  it also had 50 programmers editing, and testing code (compilers,
debuggers,
Database system) and it was transferring in the background 10's of thousands
of 4K records a day
from a central location to remote CPU (Same bldg, just different timezone),

ALSO processing email from all over the world
and printing 10's of thousands of pages a day or reports / microfiche
and hundreds of managers in the field were reviewing reports and workloads
at that same time
and when a programmer had to wait 5 minutes for a COBOL / CICS program that
had 30,000 lines
of source code and created a listing of 180,000 lines, we complained 'man
the system is SLOW today'.

Here's another:
All your checks and credit cards are processed (at purchase time whether at
store, or mailorder,
or telephone) by about 15 major businesses.

A place I work bought out a smaller company (has a lot of the e-business in
US) and literally hundreds
of banks credit card / check processing (Chase / Bank of Hawaii / Sam's /
Well's Fargo / etc)
ADDED to what we already do, and we only had to increase disk space by about
20 disks,
just from this one project, we process over 1 MILLION credit card / check
transactions daily
plus send the info the the card-issuer, and money to the merchant, and bill
for services.

A 'Mid-range' Wang system with 9 CPUs and 500 terminals and 400 small
printers, 10 large printers
that processed WIC for 300,000 persons weekly, and did the State's Health
Dept. related licensing,
and PAYROLL, etc, used over 100 SCSI RAIDs that were 2GIG each, (mirroring)
with access times
less than 9 ms (in 93) and each had a 2 MB Cache, the machines each had 64 -
96 MB ram
and had terminals spread over a state that was 8 hours driving (w/only pit
stops on fairly straight
roads) by 6 hours driving.

PLUS, at the first place we got part of our Bonus based upon 'UP' time.  in
a BAD month, besides
the 1 hr weekly of downtime, we might have another 2.  In a good month, 0
extra.
Every time you reboot a manframe with 30 tape drives, 2000 disks on-line
(many more off-line)
and about 1000 terminals directly connected plus maybe 1000 to 500,000 (yes
that's right)
'other' devices attached, you are 'down' 15 to 30 minutes, and lose money.

How often do you have to 'reboot' that crappy WIndoze system, or Novell
server (much better)
or Unix/Linux (even better, but they don't stay up that long EXCEPT on
Mainframes)

Hope this answers your question.

#### ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>

- **Message-ID:** `<38395D8B.5DC3EAE8@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca>`

```
Mike Kenzie wrote:
> 
> Some students were asking just how big a mainframe was.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> installed.  How much acutal RAM is in the machine?


I have worked on mainframes with 64K partitions and hard drives much
smaller than my PCs drives.  But even today, it isn't so much how big
the mainframe is, but how wide it is!!!  What I mean is that bandwidth
is very important.  The ability to handle hundreds and thousands of
simultaneous users is what makes the mainframe cost effective.  Sure,
speed and size help this ability, but the architecture helps more.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** Garry Milks <milksg@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383B2357.56D1805F@home.com>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <38395D8B.5DC3EAE8@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
I work in a shop with many mainframes, some of them using CMOS technology
and older ones not. Whats the big dif? Lots! To get onto my company's
mainframe development environment, one signs onto our WAN and connects
through a gateway OR one gets onto a dumb terminal to connect directly
through a controller. Unfortunately, I have seen our WAN in a state of not
being able to connect, not because the mainframe crashed but because the
WAN or gateway crashed. Not to say mainframes are perfect and never have
downtime, that would be untrue but what is true is that our mainframe is
more dependable than our WAN. Thats unimportant though in view of the fact
that our mainframes are up servicing our 10's of thousands of users across
Canada on a daily basis with hardly a hitch. As in the attached, its not
the size thats important, its how you use it. BANDWIDTH is the be it and
end all. Servicing thousands daily simultaneously is where its at and that
all comes down to internal architecture.
My IT shop develops mainframe based solutions as well as distributed
solutions and the vast majority of our bread and butter applications are
mainframe based and in fact, some of our distributed solutions have been
dumped in favour of a mainframe solution. Thats real life stuff from a real
life shop (our IT employs some 2000 IT professionals and supports about
100,000 users...we are big). I am not saying that all solutions should be
mainframe, what I am saying is that many distributed solutions never
should've been distributed in the first place...they became too costly and
too complex.
As far as RAM, we work with a 16 meg core + extended. As far as storage, we
have terrabytes on hand in the form of 3390 DASD devices (we literally have
an entire floor we call the DASD farm that has many, many 3390's). So,
while my PC has 64 megs it really (under DOS architecture) only has 1 meg
core + 63 megs extended not to mention the fact I have no where near a
terrabyte of hard drive space. Furthermore, a good percentage of my CPU
gets sucked back by the GUI...our mainframe is not GUI, its a number
cruncher and we're not interested in the overhead which GUI requires. As a
result, I can run programs against large volumes of data in very little
time on my mainframe, on my PC I'd have to give it up for a few days to run
the same program against the same volume.
Later

Howard Brazee wrote:

> Mike Kenzie wrote:
> >
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> speed and size help this ability, but the architecture helps more.
```

#### ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** rtwolfe@flexus.com (Bob Wolfe)
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383ac6b4.624928@news.enter.net>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca>`

```
ba600@FreeNet.Carleton.CA (Mike Kenzie) wrote:

>
>Some students were asking just how big a mainframe was.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>installed.  How much acutal RAM is in the machine?

Mike:

Here's an analogy I like to use.....

If it is fair to compare a mainframe to a PC, then presumably, it
would also be fair to compare a Boeing 747 to a Boeing F/A-18 Super
Hornet.

Like the mainframe, Boeing 747's have been around a long time.  The
first Boeing 747 flew in 1969.  Wow!  That's 30 years ago....but they
are indeed still very much used in their industry.  So are mainframe
computers.  Just because the technology was introduced many years ago
doesn't automatically make it useless.  In fact, the avionics and
other systems on Boeing 747's have been enhanced and updated over the
years....just as the components on mainframe computers have been
enhanced and updated over the years.

Boeing 747's can carry between 416 to 524 passengers about 8,400
miles.  Mainframe computers were also designed to efficiently handle
large scale tasks as well for a large number of concurrent users.  The
Boeing 747-400 series offers the lowest operating cost per seat of any
commercial jetliner.  Hey!  Mainframe computers also offer the lowest
operating costs per transaction for large scale processing.

Now a Boeing 747-400 costs between $167 million US$ and $187 million
US$ and that is indeed a lot of money.  Mainframe computers also cost
a lot of money too!

Now let's focus on one of the newest fighter jets available, the
F/A-18 Super Hornet.  Like as PC's are fairly new, this is a
relatively new aircraft.  Just like your PC with 128 MB of RAM, these
babies are really fast.  These fighter jets can reach speeds of up to
mach 1.5 (almost a thousand miles per hour!) but the Boeing 747 is
rated to cruise at a mere mach .85, or approximately 550 miles per
hour.

The F/A-18 Super Hornet are designed to hold either 2 or 3 seats.
PC's are also designed for only a small number of concurrent users.

Now....

If you were a pilot in an aerial war, would you prefer to use the 747
or Super Hornet in a dogfight?  I would prefer the Super Hornet.

...BUT...if you are now the owner of a commercial airlines and want to
establish a New York to Los Angeles route and sold 400 tickets, would
you deploy a single 747 or would you hire 400 pilots to fly 400 Super
Hornets each holding a single customer?

The moral of the story is that just like aircraft, computers cannot be
classified as either being bad or good.  They can only be classified
as being appropriate or inappropriate **FOR THE SPECIFIC TASK AT
HAND.**

Hope this helps.

Special thanks to www.boeing.com and my friend, Stephen Burns from
Boeing!  Great web site...very cool indeed!




Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81g9us$gbj$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<8191tl$t7c@freenet-news.carleton.ca> <383ac6b4.624928@news.enter.net>`

```
What at nice way to explain the topic. The only thing I can add is this:

At the computer-center where I work as a consultant we have 8 mainframes for
production purposes and 1 is isolated for Y2K purposes. That one will
shortly be added to the 'fleet'. 70 banks are 'flying' with us - each one
with their own PC-based network for normal, individual business, but when
they need our common services, they simply change session.

Close to 16.000 batch-jobs are running every night, but we carry many
passengers and support many destinations so we manage to be on schedule.

To your conclusion! We use the different technologies to what they at meant
for. GUI front for users and customers, mainframes to make things happen,
when large scale I/O, sort and datamanipulations are needed.

The future is not either PC or mainframe - it is both PC and mainframe.

Regards
Ib
Bob Wolfe skrev i meddelelsen <383ac6b4.624928@news.enter.net>...

…[76 more quoted lines elided]…

<a id="t-d061a5aa76"></a>
## Thread: mainframe io rates — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: mainframe io rates

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<f2s4OMMYtcQGtcSIjhO0Q7ZXE2kE@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<o5fc3sonj4c6bbcqn2ep62cp05mifapeaa@4ax.com>`

```
The venerable G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> bestowed upon
comp.lang.cobol on Sat, 20 Nov 1999 01:17:44 -0500 these words:

>two questions:
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>b> what are the io rates of mainframe hard drives compared with pcs?

Another mainframe user wondering why mainframe hardrives cost 20 times
as much per megabyte as PC drives.

Some mainframes support SCSI.  If so, you can add a SCSI drive without
problem.  I don't know of any mainframe where you can use IDE/EIDE
drives, though it conceivable that somene could have built a
controller for that purpose.

SCSI drives will usually be slower than the high priced mainframe
drives.  If this is for a high performance database, that's a problem.
For most purposes, having more drives can reduce contention, and
that's the real performance issue.
```

#### ↳ Re: mainframe io rates

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3838A7B8.D6A6A2D8@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<o5fc3sonj4c6bbcqn2ep62cp05mifapeaa@4ax.com> <f2s4OMMYtcQGtcSIjhO0Q7ZXE2kE@4ax.com>`

```


Randall Bart wrote:
> 
> The venerable G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> bestowed upon
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

Two words: Reliability and Throughput.

The most current mainframe disk storage technology actually uses 3.5"
hard drive modules, and while these undoubtably have lower access times
and higher reliability than run-of-the mill PC hard drives, it is the
way they are organized into functional storage subsystems that is the
most significant difference.  You don't add a disk to a mainframe, you
add a disk subsystem, consisting of many logical drives, subsystem
controllers, cache memory, nonvolatile cache memory, multiple channel
interfaces, etc. The emphasis is on error-detection, error-correction,
no single points of failure, parallel operation, isolation of failing
components while maintaining concurrent operation, concurrent
maintenance, etc.

The first indication you have of a drive failure on your typical PC
platform is when your system is toast and won't reboot,followed by
extended down time. This can be caused either by a failure in the hard
drive or by a failure in your single SCSI or EIDE interface to the
drive.  The first indication you typically have on a modern mainframe of
disk subsystem problems is when the IBM CE (customer engineer) arrives
unexpectedly to service a unit that is still reliably running production
work, but which had automatically "phoned home" to report a component
that needed servicing.  The CE does the repair, and while the system
operator may be aware there was a problem, the the 10,000 users of the
system are unaware any problem existed!

Modern mainframe subsystems use either mirroring or RAID-5 storage
techniques to avoid data loss in the event of single hard-drive failure.
Cache storage in the multi-GB range is used to speed up read and write
access, and this is managed so as to avoid data loss even in the event
of power failure.  Redundant power supplies and power connections are
used. A single subsystem may consist of 64 - 1024 logical drives, with a
total data capacity of 300 GB to several TeraBytes.  There may be 8
independent data buses between the controller and the physical DASD
devices, and 8 independent 17 MB/sec channel paths between the
controller and the mainframe.  All logical drives may have I/O
operations in some state of progress at the same time, with up to 8
concurrently transferring data to/from the mainframe.  All devices are
accessible over any internal bus and from any of the mainframe channels
using whatever paths are free at the time. Bus or channel failures are
detectable, and a failing component can be isolated and the operation
redriven over functional paths.  There are extensive checks and balances
and use of error detection-correction codes to insure that undetected
errors do not occur within the disk subsystem or in transferring data
to/from the mainframe.  These subsystems can easily sustain 100%
reliable I/O rates well in excess of 1000 operations per second, 24 hrs
a day, 7 days a week, and these are not your 512 byte PC file transfers,
but more typically in the range of 8K to 28K blocks of data and
sometimes much larger.  And then of course one mainframe may have many,
not just one, such disk subsystems attached.

Disk storage on a mainframe is more expensive per MB than a PC hard
drive, because it takes much more than just the hard drive to achieve
reliable 24x7 sustained operation.  This is just one of the many reasons
why comparing PC resources with mainframe resources by numbers alone is
like comparing grapes and grapefruits.  You will find there are many
people today whose computing experience has been so limited to the PC or
workstation environment, where Operating System failure or occasional
hardware failures are the norm, that they have no concept of what
reliable operation really looks like, or any concept of the costs
associated with unscheduled downtime of critial online systems in the
real world.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: mainframe io rates

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dn164s86j60u7epfl2k2tgbfald3u6hlhc@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<o5fc3sonj4c6bbcqn2ep62cp05mifapeaa@4ax.com> <f2s4OMMYtcQGtcSIjhO0Q7ZXE2kE@4ax.com> <3838A7B8.D6A6A2D8@acm.org>`

```
The venerable "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org> bestowed upon
comp.lang.cobol on Sun, 21 Nov 1999 20:17:28 -0600 these words:

>The most current mainframe disk storage technology actually uses 3.5"
>hard drive modules, and while these undoubtably have lower access times
>and higher reliability than run-of-the mill PC hard drives, it is the
>way they are organized into functional storage subsystems that is the
<snip>

Yeah, yeah, I know all about the great things these modern high priced
RAID systems do.  That wasn't my point.  In the early 1980s I worked
for Valley Federal Savings (of Van Nuys, CA) and all the disk drives
we had held less data than the drive in my laptop comuter.  None of
those drives were as reliable as this drive.  None of those drives
supported as much throughput as this drive.  We did a hell of a lot
with those ancient machines.

I wouldn't want to return to those days.  I wouldn't run a bank's live
data on anything less than the most reliable.  But for testing, long
term history, and programmers miscellaneous archives, one of those
$10/gigabyte drives will do just fine.
```

<a id="t-19558dc7d5"></a>
## Thread: Index File Help (Attn: Thane and others) — 9 msgs

### Re: Index File Help (Attn: Thane and others)

- **From:** "Ed Milne" <emilne@attcanada.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<YIJZ3.3820$j%2.294@cabot.ops.attcanada.net>`
- **References:** `<3836E45E.5D9F3E55@worf.netins.net>`

```
> Second, if I change the "if...end-if" statement that
> processes the error to a "invalid key...end-write" clause I
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> WRITE STATEMENT ASSUMED VALID.

The compiler is having trouble with a WRITE statement within another WRITE,
What you have is

WRITE <record>
INVALID KEY
....
  WRITE <record>
END-WRITE

If you move the code in the INVALID KEY clause to a separate paragraph and
PERFORM it, the compiler can handle it.

Ed Milne
```

- **References:** `<3836E45E.5D9F3E55@worf.netins.net> <YIJZ3.3820$j%2.294@cabot.ops.attcanada.net>`

```

wrote:

>> processes the error to a "invalid key...end-write" clause I
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Index File Help (Attn: Thane and others)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<819elh$1446$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<3836E45E.5D9F3E55@worf.netins.net> <YIJZ3.3820$j%2.294@cabot.ops.attcanada.net> <38381b0a.232964098@news1.attglobal.net>`

```
Based upon what I remember from Bill's earlier posts
on the question, the standard does not specify to which
of two WRITE verbs a single END-WRITE should be
associated.

Most, if not all compilers, use the nearest association;
but the question deserves consideration because the
indentation shows the programmer's intent to associate
the END-WRITE with the first WRITE verb. It is because
the compiler used the nearest association, that the
syntax error occurred.

As to the nested WRITEs working with any other compiler,
one cannot say that with certainty unless ANS 85 flagging
is active. The statements work with Micro Focus also;
but with ANS 85 flagging turned on, the compiler reports
the syntax error.

Although it may sound confusing, the syntax error with
the Fujitsu compiler and messages from ANS 85
flagging can be solved by adding another END-WRITE
scope delimiter. This is because the compiler associates
each END-WRITE to the nearest WRITE statement causing
that nearest WRITE statement, which is imperative, to be
treated as a delimited-scope statement, which is implicity
imperative. This causes the outer WRITE statement to be
treated as conditional, eventhough the programmer
intended the outer WRITE statement to be treated as a
delimited-scope statement and therefore imperative.
Adding a second END-WRITE causes both WRITE
statements to be treated as delimited-scope and implicitly
imperative, which satisfies the requirements for the
enclosing in-line PERFORM which requires an imperative
statement.

In other words, if the compiler reports that an imperative
statement is needed, add an appropriate scope delimiter
or move code as Ed Milne suggested.

The exception is that, if there are no statements, add a
CONTINUE statement. For example, an inline PERFORM,
INVALID KEY, AT END, etc. with no statements still
requires an imperative statement under ANS 85 rules and
that imperative statement may be CONTINUE.
------------------
Rick Smith

Thane Hubbell wrote in message <38381b0a.232964098@news1.attglobal.net>...
>This brings up a question.  These nested writes work under VMS COBOL,
>but I am not sure about others.  What's the standard say on this
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>>The compiler is having trouble with a WRITE statement within another
WRITE,
>>What you have is
>>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>>Ed Milne
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Index File Help (Attn: Thane and others)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<819tka$1d53$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<3836E45E.5D9F3E55@worf.netins.net> <YIJZ3.3820$j%2.294@cabot.ops.attcanada.net> <38381b0a.232964098@news1.attglobal.net> <819elh$1446$1@news.hitter.net>`

```

Rick Smith wrote in message <819elh$1446$1@news.hitter.net>...
>Based upon what I remember from Bill's earlier posts
>on the question, the standard does not specify to which
>of two WRITE verbs a single END-WRITE should be
>associated.

The draft standard, CD 1.6, appears to have addressed
the issue.

From 14.6.4.1, Scope of Statements, page 388:

"When a delimited scope statement is nested within
another delimited scope statement with the same
statement name, each explicit scope terminator
terminates the statement begun by the most recently
preceding, and as yet unterminated, occurrence of
that statement name."

I don't like it; but there it is for the next standard.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Index File Help (Attn: Thane and others)

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<4anp3sohaf81947dlekb06nqpd8g57lv41@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3836E45E.5D9F3E55@worf.netins.net> <YIJZ3.3820$j%2.294@cabot.ops.attcanada.net> <38381b0a.232964098@news1.attglobal.net> <819elh$1446$1@news.hitter.net> <819tka$1d53$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
The venerable "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> bestowed upon
comp.lang.cobol on Sun, 21 Nov 1999 21:53:55 -0500 these words:

>From 14.6.4.1, Scope of Statements, page 388:
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>Rick Smith

You don't like it.  How would you like it to work?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Draft Standard Treatment of Scope Terminators

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81j8co$14pp$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<3836E45E.5D9F3E55@worf.netins.net> <YIJZ3.3820$j%2.294@cabot.ops.attcanada.net> <38381b0a.232964098@news1.attglobal.net> <819elh$1446$1@news.hitter.net> <819tka$1d53$1@news.hitter.net> <4anp3sohaf81947dlekb06nqpd8g57lv41@4ax.com>`

```

Randall Bart wrote in message
<4anp3sohaf81947dlekb06nqpd8g57lv41@4ax.com>...
>The venerable "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> bestowed upon
>comp.lang.cobol on Sun, 21 Nov 1999 21:53:55 -0500 these words:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>You don't like it.  How would you like it to work?

Such that scope terminators are guaranteed to terminate
any outstanding, unterminated conditional statements of the
same name. This most frequently reflects (and may always
reflect) the programmer's intent and prevents the introduction
of syntax errors when removing or replacing unrelated
imperative statements.

Consider the following (draft statndard) code snippet.

build-indexed-file.
    set no-more-records to false
    perform until no-more-records
        read input-file
          at end
            set no-more-records to true
          not at end
            perform build-indexed-record
            write indexed-record
              invalid key
                *> format error record
                write error-record
                initialize error-record
            end-write
            *> possible additional code
        end-read
    end-perform
    .
Moving 'initialize error-record' to the beginning of
the code for the 'invalid key' option is a reasonable
change (and likely should have been placed there
initially). Doing so introduces a syntax error at the
'end-read' because the 'end-write', under the current
draft, terminates the 'write error-record' instead of
terminating the 'write indexed-record' as originally
written. If the standard guaranteed that scope
terminators would terminate outstanding, unterminated
conditional statements of the same name, the change
in the code would not introduce a syntax error.

Furthermore, the presence of 'possible additional code'
introduces an element of surprise since the syntax
error cannot be reported until the 'end-read'. This
surprise is that, given 5 lines of additional code, the
syntax error occurred because of a code change some
7 lines before that point at which the syntax error is
reported and the 'initialize' was not part of the 'write'.

To make matters worse, many implementors, as an
extension, treat unterminated delimited scope
statements as if the statements were terminated at
the point where the syntax error would occur under the
standard. In the example, above, with the code change
and the presence of additional code after the 'end-write',
the logic of the program will change and the programmer
may not be aware of the change until testing. The logic
change occurs because the compiler, essentially, moves
the additional statements into the 'invalid key' option of
the 'write indexed-record' when the programmer and
original logic placed the statements within the 'not at end'
option for the 'read'.

I can think of no way that introducing this change into
the new COBOL standard will 'break" any existing code
that has been checked with ANS 85 flagging active.

It could "break" existing code that was not checked
with ANS 85 flagging.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Draft Standard Treatment of Scope Terminators

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```

: written. If the standard guaranteed that scope
: terminators would terminate outstanding, unterminated
: conditional statements of the same name, the change
: in the code would not introduce a syntax error.

And what happens when the first END-IF terminates all
outstanding, unterminated IFs ?
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Draft Standard Treatment of Scope Terminators

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<59364sg3ois4906grmajkgq2lkrgaj72q2@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<3836E45E.5D9F3E55@worf.netins.net> <YIJZ3.3820$j%2.294@cabot.ops.attcanada.net> <38381b0a.232964098@news1.attglobal.net> <819elh$1446$1@news.hitter.net> <819tka$1d53$1@news.hitter.net> <4anp3sohaf81947dlekb06nqpd8g57lv41@4ax.com> <81j8co$14pp$1@news.hitter.net> <81ju3r$gpt$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz> <81jsd0$1gr5$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
The venerable "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com> bestowed upon
comp.lang.cobol on Thu, 25 Nov 1999 16:34:30 -0500 these words:

>
>Richard Plinston wrote in message <81ju3r$gpt$1@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>You read more into my statement than I intended.

Maybe more than you intended, but exactly what you said.  You said
that END-WRITE should end all outsanding WRITEs and END-ADD should end
all outstanding ADDs.  Why should we infer that you want END-IF to
only one IF?  And how do you figure that having END-ADD end more than
one ADD won't break existing code:

ADD A1 TO A2
    NOT ON SIZE ERROR 
        ADD B1 TO B2 
            ON SIZE ERROR 
                PERFORM ERROR-CODE
        END-ADD
        PERFORM DO-STUFF

Currently, the END-ADD ends only ADD B1, thus DO-STUFF is only
performed when there wasn't an error on ADD A1.  Under your proposal,
DO-STUFF is PERFORMed unconditionally.
```

```
Kent Feiler <kfeiler@cpiusa.com> writes:

>      05  (TT)-CC   PIC9(2).
>      05  (TT)-YY   PIC 9(2).
>      05  (TT)-MM   PIC9(2).
>      05  (TT)-DD   PIC 9(2).

>...you can create a pre-Y2K date by using a REPLACING like:

>     REPLACING ==05  (TT)-CC   PIC9(2).== BY ====


As long as someone didn't change the number of spaces
before the PIC or some other variation.

with whatever symbol your compiler uses to denote
in-line comments.   What is it in Fujitsu, "*>" ?

#### ↳ Re: Copy replacing

- **From:** Randall Bart <Barticus@usa.spam.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<QWc4ODW3A=KwmAzw8rgFBgQIXWiA@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<383BC329@NOSPAMhome.com> <Qic0OAiBr4HujsoFXpIcc2PbUEdH@4ax.com> <819e29$em$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net>`

The venerable dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant) bestowed upon
comp.lang.cobol on 21 Nov 1999 18:35:53 GMT these words:

>Kent Feiler <kfeiler@cpiusa.com> writes:

>>     REPLACING ==05  (TT)-CC   PIC9(2).== BY ====
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>in-line comments.   What is it in Fujitsu, "*>" ? 

1)  I take the lake of space between "PIC" and "9(2)." as a simple
typo.

2)  REPLACE and COPY ... REPLACING work on text words.  Extra spaces
won't change that.

3)  *> is the inline comment indicator in the COBOL-20XX draft.

4)  According to the draft, you can't make a line into a comment via
text replacement.  Does Fujitsu (or anyone else) support this?
```

```
I would be curious if various people try this with different compilers -
to see if
   ==(a:b)==

does or does not match
  ==(a : b)==

in copy/replacing.  I haven't checked my '85 Standard - BUT the two do
match for TEXT WORDS - but just have different "text separators".
Without looking at the Standard, I *think* (but am not positive) that
these SHOULD match.

Obviously (back to the original comment) something like

  ==a-b==
would NOT match
  ==a - b==

but I really would be interested in how various compilers handle the
spaces around a colon.

You're saying that in Wang you can replace part of the text with *>
and the rest of the line becomes a comment.  This is not my
understanding of the COBOL-20XX draft.

several old (non-working) PCs, a couple of minis, and

somewhere that strips the gold off the contacts, recovers the

I hope you find a way, and thank you very much for trying.

Regards
Ib

<a id="t-499fd5ae24"></a>
## Thread: MOVE Alphanumeric to Numeric-Edited (was: Re: Subscripts & Indexes) — 11 msgs

### MOVE Alphanumeric to Numeric-Edited (was: Re: Subscripts & Indexes)

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<818kf4$jk3$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<8150rm$m4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <pt8f3s84nllv9lqqj3asc2ioknmm2uqqnc@4ax.com>`

```

G Moore wrote in message ...
[...]
>>           MOVE SAL-SALARY TO DET-SALARY****************ERROR 163
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>*by tyler welburn and wilson price, 4th edition

With text books there is difficulty separating the author's
preferences from that which is permitted by the language.
Stern & Stern, 8th Edition, 1997, has a similar proscription.
On page 195, they show a table which says the move is not
permissible. On page 194, they write,

"As a rule, do not move alphanumeric fields to numeric
fields. The move will be performed by the computer; but
the results could cause a program interrupt or termnation
of the job if the receiving field is used later on in an
arithmetic operation and it does not contain numeric data."

In other words, writing such a move into a program is
permitted by the language and will not cause a syntax error;
but because the programmer may make a mistake (in not
ensuring the data is numeric), they (the authors) would
prefer that the programmer not write such a statement.

There are similiar, stronger statements proscribing such
moves in Stern & Stern, 1st Edition, 1970. They clearly
don't like it and never have!

As nearly as I can tell, the move of an alphanumeric data
item to a numeric or numeric-edited data item has been
permissible since at least COBOL 68. One need only
consult the LRM for the compiler to see that it is permitted.

For such a move, the COBOL standard requires the
compiler treat the alphanumeric field AS IF it were defined
as an unsigned numeric integer. If the content of the
alphanumeric field is not an integer, the result of the
move is undefined.

Finally, whether to validate the field before using it,
whether to change its data description, or whether to
explicitly redefine the field are matters of programmer
preference. Personally, I see no problem with it, as
written.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

#### ↳ Re: MOVE Alphanumeric to Numeric-Edited (was: Re: Subscripts & Indexes)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3839649F.21005CA1@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<8150rm$m4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <pt8f3s84nllv9lqqj3asc2ioknmm2uqqnc@4ax.com> <818kf4$jk3$1@news.hitter.net>`

```
Rick Smith wrote:

> As nearly as I can tell, the move of an alphanumeric data
> item to a numeric or numeric-edited data item has been
> permissible since at least COBOL 68. One need only
> consult the LRM for the compiler to see that it is permitted.

I have programs which aborted when converted to COBOL for MVS when
spaces were moved to numeric fields.  The old compilations handled this
fine.  (I didn't know that as I never tried doing this with my own
code).
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MOVE Alphanumeric to Numeric-Edited (was: Re: Subscripts & Indexes)

- **From:** Daniel Jacot <daniel.jacot@winterthur.ch>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81dhal$d58$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<8150rm$m4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <pt8f3s84nllv9lqqj3asc2ioknmm2uqqnc@4ax.com> <818kf4$jk3$1@news.hitter.net> <3839649F.21005CA1@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
In article <3839649F.21005CA1@NOSPAMhome.com>,
  Please, do, not, e-mail, replies, post, them!! wrote:
>
> I have programs which aborted when converted to COBOL for MVS when
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

You might want to check the setting of the compiler option ZWB (Zero
When Blank). If set to yes in the older compiler and to no in the
current compiler, this could be the cause of these troubles.
```

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00

```

>
>I have programs which aborted when converted to COBOL for MVS when
>spaces were moved to numeric fields.  The old compilations handled this
>fine.  (I didn't know that as I never tried doing this with my own
>code).

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MOVE Alphanumeric to Numeric-Edited (was: Re: Subscripts & Indexes)

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383AE20D.E349AF57@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<8150rm$m4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <pt8f3s84nllv9lqqj3asc2ioknmm2uqqnc@4ax.com> <818kf4$jk3$1@news.hitter.net> <3839649F.21005CA1@NOSPAMhome.com> <383bcd9d.48934413@news2.attglobal.net>`

```
Volker Bandke wrote:
> 
> On Mon, 22 Nov 1999 08:43:27 -0700, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Could have to do with the Zero When Blank compiler option (ZWB)

Certainly.  All programs moving to production use the standard compiler
options (via Endeavor) for a particular compiler.  This makes sense so
that programs don't break when a maintenance programmer uses his
options.  But switching from compiler to compiler brings up problems due
to different compile options defaulting.

Some other shop may have different options set and have different aborts
while upgrading compilers.
```

Yes, the defaults... Compiler defaults, overridden by Installation default, overridden by
JCL settings........


That's why I (try to) put the more juicy options into CBL statements at the beginning of
the source code,  then I have control, and no Endeavor, vendor, other sysprog tells my
program how to behave,.  I want to be the one who sets the rules <g>

Now again, I am from the IBM world, and do not know if other compiler provide the way of
setting compile options (like ZWB) in the source.  If it is possible, and I I rely on the
functions it provides, it would be a good idea to specify it in the source (just like
NUMPROC, or TRUNC)

> Yes, the defaults... Compiler defaults, overridden by Installation default, overridden by

What options can be specified in the source?   I love the concept - we
get what we want and nobody is going to accidentally override by using
his own compiler JCL.

I get a compiler message telling me that I can use a more efficient
version of DFSORT (by changing my compiler options).  Lots of good that
does me if I need to have a standard compile!

My example of programs breaking because the new compile doesn't accept
spaces into numeric edited fields is one where a compatibility command
in the source would be nice.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MOVE Alphanumeric to Numeric-Edited (was: Re: Subscripts & Indexes)

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383c4086.1887363@news2.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<8150rm$m4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <pt8f3s84nllv9lqqj3asc2ioknmm2uqqnc@4ax.com> <818kf4$jk3$1@news.hitter.net> <3839649F.21005CA1@NOSPAMhome.com> <383bcd9d.48934413@news2.attglobal.net> <383AE20D.E349AF57@NOSPAMhome.com> <383b06f6.7729915@news2.attglobal.net> <383BF523.86AC9188@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Wed, 24 Nov 1999 07:24:35 -0700, Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote:

>
>What options can be specified in the source?   I love the concept - we
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>in the source would be nice.

Nearly all IBM compile time options can be specified in the source.  The rules are fast
and simple

Place one or mor CBL statements rigt at the beginning ofyour source, there may be no
preceedig empty line or comment.  Add as many compile time options as you need.  If one
line is not enough, add another CBL line.  Do not end the CBL line witha dot.

I prefere to put the CBL line into column 8  (guess why)

Therefore, the bginning of a COBOL program might look like this:


        CBL NOZWB, TRUNC(BIN), SSRANGE
        CBL FASTSRT, APOST, NOOPTIMIZE
        CBL NOSEQ, LIB
        IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
         PROGRAM-ID. Mypgm.
etc etc



Thus, 
     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         All computers wait at the same speed.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MOVE Alphanumeric to Numeric-Edited (was: Re: Subscripts & Indexes)

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81jff2$g1q$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<8150rm$m4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <pt8f3s84nllv9lqqj3asc2ioknmm2uqqnc@4ax.com> <818kf4$jk3$1@news.hitter.net> <3839649F.21005CA1@NOSPAMhome.com> <383bcd9d.48934413@news2.attglobal.net> <383AE20D.E349AF57@NOSPAMhome.com> <383b06f6.7729915@news2.attglobal.net> <383BF523.86AC9188@NOSPAMhome.com> <383c4086.1887363@news2.attglobal.net>`

```
You can do a similar thing with Fujitsu.  You use an ampersand in column
seven.  For example, say you want a program to link to C or C++, and so want
the symbol table to be case-sensitive. That requires the compiler directive
NOALPHA.

             @NOALPHA
000001 IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.
000002  PROGRAM-ID. "_BARIN".
000003 AUTHOR. D. L. Tees.
000004 SECURITY.
000005          Copyright (C), 1998, DONALD L TEES.
000006          Reads barcodes over a serial line.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MOVE Alphanumeric to Numeric-Edited (was: Re: Subscripts & Indexes)

_(thread depth: 8)_

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Tre%3.60$FL2.2739@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<8150rm$m4l$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <pt8f3s84nllv9lqqj3asc2ioknmm2uqqnc@4ax.com> <818kf4$jk3$1@news.hitter.net> <3839649F.21005CA1@NOSPAMhome.com> <383bcd9d.48934413@news2.attglobal.net> <383AE20D.E349AF57@NOSPAMhome.com> <383b06f6.7729915@news2.attglobal.net> <383BF523.86AC9188@NOSPAMhome.com> <383c4086.1887363@news2.attglobal.net> <81jff2$g1q$1@news.igs.net>`

```

donald tees <donald@willmack.com
> wrote in message news:81jff2$g1q$1@news.igs.net...

> You can do a similar thing with Fujitsu.  You use an ampersand ...

Actually an "at" symbol (@). A ligatured "ad" (Latin for 'each').

The 'Ampersand' has an interesting history. Written as the 27th
letter on English school children's chalk-boards, the recitation
of the alphabet usually ended with "... X, Y, Z, and, per se, And"
('per se' meaning 'standing alone'). Apparently the "And" took
the new name "Andperseand" or "Ampersand."

All of which is not as exciting as our new product "Kiddie
Kondoms," (for educational use only) which we intend to
market to the sex-education-crazed educrats. Kiddie Kondoms
have the advantage that they work better (and with less
resultant loss of esteem) than the adult-sized
"snuggies" models.

Sort   of a 'training bra' concept for boys.

To hell with programming - we're out to make money.
```

<a id="t-66e8875fe0"></a>
## Thread: help on accept verb — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Re: help on accept verb

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-21T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<818i0t$bdt$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<3835ac37.0@csd-32630.noc.unf.edu>`

```
Hi Anh Le and other new MF users.

Accept is described in language reference, and I think you know that accept
has 3 formats. Read carefully and enjoy.

Your installation could have online help, Then you can search for Accept,
and see the same description on screen. Older versions have HyHelp, and that
is online help in character mode.

If your installation is correct and one of the expenxive ones, try to write
the word 'Accept' in the editor. Position the cursor on the 'A' and press
Alt+1. Some of you might get a syntax diagram directly on the screen.

You could be the type who just like to see and example, and take what's
needed. In that case, go to www.merant.com and find the FTP-site. They have
many samples there.

Happy learning

Regards
Ib




Anh Le skrev i meddelelsen <3835ac37.0@csd-32630.noc.unf.edu>...
>Please, would someone tell me where i can find a brief description and
>examples on all available option of the accept verb in MF cobol for PC?
>
>
```
