       01  HEADING-ERROR-LINE-TWO.
       05  FILLER                      PIC X(13)
           VALUE 'SOC. SEC. NO.'.

       05  FILLER                      PIC X(4) VALUE SPACES.

       05  FILLER                      PIC X(10)
           VALUE 'TRANS CODE'.

       05  FILLER                      PIC X(2) VALUE SPACES.

       05  FILLER                      PIC X(13)
           VALUE 'ERROR MESSAGE'.


       01  ERROR-LINE.
           05 ERR-SOC-SEC              PIC X(9).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2).

           05 ERR-TRANS-CODE           PIC X(1).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2).

           05 ERROR-MESSAGE            PIC X(32).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2).


  PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       100-PROCESS-TRANSACTION-FILE.
           OPEN INPUT TRANSACTION-FILE
                I-O INDEXED-FILE
                ERROR-FILE.
           PERFORM 200-WRITE-ERROR-HEADINGS.

           PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE-SWITCH='YES'
               READ TRANSACTION-FILE INTO WS-TRANS-RECORD
                   AT END
                       MOVE 'YES' TO END-OF-FILE-SWITCH
                   NOT AT END
                       PERFORM 300-READ-INDEXED-FILE
                       PERFORM 400-APPLY-TRANS-TO-MASTER
               END-READ
           END-PERFORM.
           CLOSE TRANSACTION-FILE
                 INDEXED-FILE.
           STOP RUN.

       200-WRITE-ERROR-HEADINGS.
           MOVE HEADING-ERROR-LINE-ONE TO ERROR-RECORD.
           WRITE ERROR-RECORD.
           MOVE HEADING-ERROR-LINE-TWO TO ERROR-RECORD.
           WRITE ERROR-RECORD.

       300-READ-INDEXED-FILE.
           MOVE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO IND-SOC-SEC-NUM.
           READ INDEXED-FILE INTO WS-MASTER-RECORD
               INVALID KEY
                   MOVE 'NO' TO RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH
               NOT INVALID KEY
                   MOVE 'YES' TO RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH
           END-READ.

       400-APPLY-TRANS-T0-MASTER.
           EVALUATE TRUE
               WHEN ADDITION
                   PERFORM 500-ADD-NEW-RECORD
               WHEN CORRECTION
                   PERFORM 600-CORRECT-EXISTING-RECORD
               WHEN DELETION
                   PERFORM 700-DELETE-EXISTING-RECORD
               WHEN OTHER
                   DISPLAY 'INVALID TRANSACTION CODE'
           END-EVALUATE.






       500-ADD-NEW-RECORD.
           IF RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH='YES'
               PERFORM 800-WRITE-ADD-MSG-LINE
           ELSE
               MOVE SPACES TO WS-MASTER-RECORD
               MOVE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO MA-SOC-SEC-NUMBER
               MOVE TR-NAME-AND-INITIALS TO MA-NAME-AND-INITIALS
               MOVE TR-BIRTH-DATE TO MA-BIRTH-DATE.
               MOVE TR-LOCATION-CODE TO MA-LOCATION-CODE
               MOVE TR-EDUCATION-CODE TO MA-EDUCATION-CODE
               MOVE TR-TITLE-DATA TO MA-TITLE-DATA
               MOVE TR-RATING TO MA-RATING
               MOVE TR-SALARY TO MA-SALARY
               WRITE INDEXED-RECORD FROM WS-MASTER-RECORD
           END-IF.



       600-CORRECT-EXISTING-RECORD.
           IF RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH = 'YES'
               ADD WS-TRANS-RECORD TO WS-MASTER-RECORD
               REWRITE INDEXED-RECORD FROM WS-MASTER-RECORD
           ELSE
               PERFORM 900-WRITE-CORRECT-MSG-LINE
           END-IF.


       700-DELETE-EXISTING-RECORD.
           IF RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH= 'YES'
               DELETE INDEXED-FILE
           ELSE
               PERFORM 1000-WRITE-DELETE-MSG-LINE
           END-IF.


       800-WRITE-ADD-MSG-LINE.
               WRITE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO ERR-SOC-SEC.
               WRITE TR-TRANSACTION-CODE TO ERR-TRANS-CODE.
               WRITE ADD-MSG TO ERROR-MESSAGE.
               MOVE ERROR-LINE TO ERROR-RECORD.
               WRITE ERROR-RECORD.


       900-WRITE-CORRECT-MSG-LINE.
               WRITE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO ERR-SOC-SEC.
               WRITE TR-TRANSACTION-CODE TO ERR-TRANS-CODE.
               WRITE CORRECT-MSG TO ERROR-MESSAGE.
               MOVE ERROR-LINE TO ERROR-RECORD.
               WRITE ERROR-RECORD.

       1000-WRITE-DELETE-MSG-LINE.
               WRITE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO ERR-SOC-SEC.
               WRITE TR-TRANSACTION-CODE TO ERR-TRANS-CODE.
               WRITE DELETE-MSG TO ERROR-MESSAGE.
               MOVE ERROR-LINE TO ERROR-RECORD.
               WRITE ERROR-RECORD.


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: *Code for sequential updates

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<ejwrKMRN$GA.211@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<8192bp$910$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <8194or$bk_001@news.netdirect.net> <819cgv$fjr$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
There are sequential and non-sequential updates of indexed files, and
sequential updates of non-indexed files.

If you are doing a SEQUENTIAL update of an indexed file (say VSAM on an MVS
platform) I'd define it as . . .

ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED and
ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL

. . . and use ordinary READS and WRITES to do my updates, with no VALID KEY
or NOT VALID KEY.
You could use ACCESS IS RANDOM and VALID KEY and NOT VALID KEY, however, at
least on an MVS platform, even with 200 index and 200 data buffers
specified, randomly processing your way through each record of a VSAM
dataset takes a long, long time.  ACCESS IS SEQUENTIAL and normal READS
(with AT END and NOT AT END) and WRITES are much better.

And I'll ask again:

What platform?
What compiler?
What type of source file?
What type of target file?
What does your code look like?
What errors are you getting (reprint them here)?
Are you just looking for an algorithm that does sequential updating?  Of
indexed or non-indexed files?
In-place updating, or creation of an entirely new file with deletion of the
old (or GDG processing for MVS and related environments)?

What?



Bill <dc606@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:819cgv$fjr$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> My text book has an example for a NONsequential update.
>
…[312 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

>hearing stories that people are coming off of Y2K COBOL projects and
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>Sent via Deja.com

From my limited perspective I would say the demand
for COBOL programmers is just as strong now as it
was 3 or 4 years ago.  

I've seen a few COBOL systems retired lately, but no
where near the number of new COBOL systems being
put together.  And COBOL is being put to work in places
that were previously considered the domain of 4GLs or
DBMs.  

I think folks are remembering why COBOL was so
popular to begin with.  It works, it's stable, and it
can be maintained.  

JC Jones.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Job market

- **From:** mel5133928@aol.com (MEl5133928)
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991127073619.27046.00000784@ng-fz1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<383F055E.8E614B7C@Castle.Net>`

```
The situation seems to be very similar in the UK. I am a freelance
COBOL/CICS/DB2 analyst/programmer and have managed to keep working in these
difficult market conditions, but a number of my ex-colleagues have been out of
work for months now as the numerous Y2K and COBOL1 - COBOL2/370 conversions
finish. Pundits are saying the market will recover next year, but I am of the
opinion that the main thrust of new development will involve e:commerce or
client/server development. I still feel the mainframe has a lot to offer -
companies have invested billions of pounds worldwide in this technology - but
we will need to look at developing new skills to remain competitive in the
labour market. I do feel the freelance market will shrink significantly as this
shift occurs.
```

#### ↳ Re: Job market

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s45l395jmvm92@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<81crje$uoc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
I have read 8 replies.

In my state, Connecticut, COBOL is really saturated, even for
Y2K. I am a good programmer with 4 years degree, but I think
that I should find another job with lower pay the next year.

The last few years, many companies hired COBOL programmers
without degrees, and gave them few months trainings. They
are good COBOL programmers now. Even though they may not good
in other programming languages, they do not require high
salary as I do, and the companies do not alway have
challenging tasks that require degrees. They will stay to
replace retired COBOL programmers. COBOL programmers are
however, necessary to maintain the running systems.
```

## Thread: Case Structure? — 35 this month / 39 total

### Case Structure?

- **From:** "Elorian" <not@home.now>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Cik_3.18630$T4.5266960@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`

```
Hiya,

I'm new to COBOL (first semester college programming) and my school
assignment requires me to check a variable and output different descriptions
to the monitor.  Now, I could do this with multiple IF statements but I
would prefer to use some sort of CASE statement.  Unfortunately, my $150.00
C school manual (COBOL; From Micro to Mainframe; 3rd Edition) tells nothing
of a COBOL CASE statement.  If anybody knows of a simple CASE statement, can
you please post it with a brief description?  Thanks in advance!
```

#### ↳ Re: Case Structure?

- **From:** John Grznar <grznar@exchange.uta.edu>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3839EE89.4CE8B465@exchange.uta.edu>`
- **References:** `<Cik_3.18630$T4.5266960@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`

```
  In COBOL we don't call them CASE statements, we call them EVALUATE statements,
but they are the same thing.
 I'm sure you can find info on EVALUATE statements in your textbook.

John Grznar



Elorian wrote:

> Hiya,
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>   Elorian
```

#### ↳ Re: Case Structure?

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<z5n_3.1531$5q.15234@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<Cik_3.18630$T4.5266960@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`

```
It is called 'EVALUATE'

the syntax is
EVALUATE conditional-expression    (conditional-expression can be level 88,
or ws-var = 'X', or
anything that can be coded 'IF ' at same location)
WHEN [TRUE | FALSE | ANY]  (any = I don't care)  [|] means pick one
    imperative statement ( DISPLAY, PERFORM, CALL, GO, ADD, etc)
WHEN [TRUE | FALSE | ANY]  (any = I don't care)  [|] means pick one
    imperative statement ( DISPLAY, PERFORM, CALL, GO, ADD, etc
....
WHEN OTHER
    imperative statement ( DISPLAY, PERFORM, CALL, GO, ADD, etc <== Optional
END-EVALUATE.

Evals can be nested
So example --==
01 WS-SWITCH      PIC X.
     88  ALL-DONE  VALUE 'Y'.
     88  NOT-DONE  VALUE 'N'.

EVALUATE  WS-SWITCH
    WHEN 'Y'
        DISPLAY 'DONE'
    WHEN 'N'
        DISPLAY 'MORE'
    WHEN OTHER
        DISPLAY 'Oh Crud!'
END-EVALUATE
 can ALSO be Coded
EVALUATE  ALL-DONE
    WHEN TRUE
        DISPLAY 'DONE'
    WHEN FALSE
        DISPLAY 'MORE!'
END-EVALUATE
or this way
EVALUATE  TRUE
    WHEN ALL-DONE
        DISPLAY 'DONE'
    WHEN NOT-DONE
        DISPLAY 'MORE'
    WHEN OTHER
        DISPLAY 'Oh Crud!'
END-EVALUATE

#### ↳ Re: Case Structure?

- **From:** "Terry Landry" <terryl@laci.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81d54a$a71$1@ffx2nh3.news.uu.net>`
- **References:** `<Cik_3.18630$T4.5266960@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`

```
There is a Case structure in COBOL... I am taking the class myself right
now.. ok. let me show you what it is.

say you want to evaluate a field called field1

Evaluate Field1
   when 0
     perform......
    when 1
      perform........
    when 2
      perform..........
End-Evaluate.

thats pretty much it.   hope it helps, if not do another post and i will try
to explain again.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Case Structure?

- **From:** "Warren Porter" <warrenp123@netdoor123.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MLx_3.39983$YI2.1992646@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<Cik_3.18630$T4.5266960@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <81d54a$a71$1@ffx2nh3.news.uu.net> <%Fp_3.19317$T4.5446427@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`

```
In computer science in general or Pseudocode, the CASE statement is a
specialized form of an IF statement where one item is tested for a number of
different values.  In COBOL it is done with EVALUATE .. WHEN as other
posters have pointed out.

Your post is a model of how students can obtain useful help in this
usegroup.  Your subject line reflected your question (not "help me") and
showed the effort you put into solving it before asking for question.  The
"Thank you" at the end also deserves cudos.

Congratulations.


Elorian <not@home.now> wrote in message
news:%Fp_3.19317$T4.5446427@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com...
> Thanks a lot for all the help guys!  Sorry for referencing CASE instead of
> EVALUATE.  My teacher used a Pseudocode example when talking about this
> option.  Cheers!

> > There is a Case structure in COBOL... I am taking the class myself right
> > now.. ok. let me show you what it is.
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> try

> > >I'm new to COBOL (first semester college programming) and my school
> > >assignment requires me to check a variable and output different
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> > >of a COBOL CASE statement.  If anybody knows of a simple CASE
statement,
> > can
> > >you please post it with a brief description?  Thanks in advance!
```

#### ↳ Re: Case Structure?

- **From:** "Steven Kalemkiewicz" <senseisk@flash.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hcl_3.6900$16.812451@news.flash.net>`
- **References:** `<Cik_3.18630$T4.5266960@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`

```
Here are a few simple examples.  One book I have lists EVALUATE the other
doesn't list it at all.  This *should* work on most compilers.

EVALUATE  field
    WHEN value1
        things to do
    WHEN value 2
        things to do
    WHEN OTHER
        things to do
END-EVALUATE.

EVALUATE TRUE
    WHEN field-a = field-b
        things to do
    WHEN field-c = field-d
        things to do
    WHEN OTHER
        things to do
END-EVALUATE.

EVALUATE field
    WHEN 1000 THRU 9999
        things to do
    WHEN 10000 THRU 99999
        things to do
    WHEN OTHER
        things to do
END-EVALUATE.

You can obviously make them more complex, however this should be sufficient
to start you off.

-Steven Kalemkiewicz
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: likes/dislikes of COBOL

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383E20E2.3B26C636@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<Cik_3.18630$T4.5266960@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <383BE335.F47B2DD9@brigands.com> <Pine.GSO.4.10.9911250023140.16859-100000@garbo.nepean.uws.edu.au> <ri0%3.1487$lT.27953@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7ko%3.455$f5.10544@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com>`

```


Gazaloo wrote:
> 
> Michael Mattias <michael.mattias@gte.net> wrote in message
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> --
 
The language per se may not be that verbose, but the traditional usage
of COBOL is to use long self-explanatory variable names, which can make
the resulting program quite verbose compared with conventional usage in
many other languages.  This is a useful practice when it comes to
maintenance, but can be tedious to the original author.  
```

Well, I find that amazing.  I use long self-explanatory variable names

deters me from this a little by its line length limit.

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: likes/dislikes of COBOL

_(thread depth: 7)_

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81qaat01651@enews4.newsguy.com>`
- **References:** `<Cik_3.18630$T4.5266960@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com> <383BE335.F47B2DD9@brigands.com> <Pine.GSO.4.10.9911250023140.16859-100000@garbo.nepean.uws.edu.au> <ri0%3.1487$lT.27953@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <7ko%3.455$f5.10544@news1.frmt1.sfba.home.com> <383E20E2.3B26C636@acm.org> <383E8056.62B3109D@zwg.zicl.co.uk>`

```
Cobol likes: Longevity of cobol programs, great for reporting, usually 
easier to enhance existing programs rather than writing new ones saving time
and money.  General self-documenting nature (I don't need to use many
comments except for tricky code).  Gratification knowing that something I
write today may still be used 10 or 20 years from now.  The PIC clause.

Dislikes: A tad verbose as was posted earlier but it's worth it.  Programmer
typing time is such a small amount of the whole SDLC.  Also, nobody thinks
COBOL is cool anymore also, although yes you do get COOL if you take out the
B.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: likes/dislikes of COBOL

Good programming practices are good programming practices.  And
traditional usages are what create stereotypes.  If a language is
traditionally terse or verbose, that is the stereotype.  Or structured,
or spaghetti, etc.
```

#### ↳ Re: Case Structure?

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s45l3b3lmvm33@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<Cik_3.18630$T4.5266960@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`

```
CASE is Not COBOL standard. It means that CASE is error with VAX and
Mainframe as I know.

In some PC COBOL, CASE is valid as in C/C++/VC++/VB languages. The
syntax may differ from language to language. To my experience, just
enter a structure similar to C or VB, and the compiler will give you
the suggestions on how to code it with correct syntax. If your
compiler does not give any suggestion, it does not support CASE.
With this compiler, CASE is invalid reserved word.

Most of the time, I code COBOL from my memory and edit the code
according to suggestions from the compiler, rather than looking up
the syntax from books.
```

#### ↳ Re: Case Structure?

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s45l3ccsmvm50@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<Cik_3.18630$T4.5266960@news1.rdc1.on.wave.home.com>`

```
I hear people discussing about EVALUATE.

EVALUATE is not standard COBOL, which means that EVALUATE is invalid
syntax in mainframe and VAX COB0L compilers.

However EVALUATE is supported with some PC COBOL compilers.
That is the reason old books, or standard COBOL books, or mainframe
COBOL books do not have EVALUATE.

Please, stop discussing which books that have EVALUATE. We need
to find whether the working system supports EVALUATE. It is useless
you found dozen of books that have EVALUATE, and your compiler
turn all them down!! You should have a compiler that supports the
command rather than have books that do not support the compilers.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Case Structure?

- **From:** "alan russel" <russelah@apci.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

```
AnhMy Tran wrote in message ...
>I hear people discussing about EVALUATE.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Sorry to disagree, but EVALUATE *IS* standard COBOL (at least for the last
14 years since COBOL '85).  IBM mainframe COBOL has suppported this verb
since the release of COBOL-II.  Are you only using COBOL '74 and old VAX
COBOL?

Alan Russell, PhD, CCP
Work - Russelah@apci.com
Home - AHRussell@computer.org
Home page - http://members.aol.com/ahrussell
-------------------------------------------------------
The views expressed are mine alone and do not necessarily reflect those of
my employer
```

<a id="t-cae9e09cee"></a>
## Thread: Adding extra fields... — 13 msgs

### Adding extra fields...

- **From:** morella3253@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi to all,

I have to add two extra fields to my existing record layout and
I was wondering whether once I have changed the copy book for this
new record layout, do I have to change anything in programs that
are not using these new fields??
(i.e will programs that are using the old record layout still work)

The two extra fields will be added to the end of the existing record.


Thanks
Morella Hernandez


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Adding extra fields...

- **From:** "James King" <mangogwr@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<h7n_3.1535$5q.15247@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Let's see, data record is n bytes,
ADD 6 to n
write new records at n+6 with changed progra
read records at n+6 in unchanged programs
CAN YOU SAY FILE-STATUS = 95?

At the very least you will need to RECOMPILE all PROGRAMS USING that
COPYBOOK

morella3253@my-deja.com wrote in message <81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Hi to all,
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Adding extra fields...

- **From:** "Anon" <anon@anon.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Spf_3.36931$YI2.1853881@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
<morella3253@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> I have to add two extra fields to my existing record layout and
> I was wondering whether once I have changed the copy book for this
> new record layout, do I have to change anything in programs that
> are not using these new fields??
> (i.e will programs that are using the old record layout still work)

Once you have converted the file it will be necessary to recompile every
program which uses that copybook.  Programs using the old record layout will
NOT work once the record length (maybe blocksize on some systems) changes.
HTH
```

#### ↳ Re: Adding extra fields...

- **From:** "ChrisOsborne" <chris_n_osborne@msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#8o4hQRN$GA.275@cpmsnbbsa03>`
- **References:** `<81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
What platform?
What compiler?
What type of files?

If you're using anything on a mainframe, then it's quite likely you'll have
a bunch of JCL to change, at least.


<morella3253@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi to all,
>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Adding extra fields...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383988E9.87BFB3C0@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
morella3253@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Hi to all,
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> The two extra fields will be added to the end of the existing record.

With fixed length records, this should not be any problem.  Obviously
anywhere where you had the old record length hard coded, the number
should change.  (JCL, FD) as long as anywhere you copy the record to and
from (READ INTO). 

Also consider whether you will need to access previous versions of this
file with your changed programs.  If so, you may wish to convert them to
the new record size with spaces (or whatever) for the new fields.
```

#### ↳ Re: Adding extra fields...

- **From:** "James J. Gavan" <jjgavan@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3839ABC9.70E0202A@home.com>`
- **References:** `<81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```


morella3253@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Hi to all,
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

As indicated by Anon you will have to re-compile any programs which
contain the larger record format for the new record. There are several
factors which can help with changing file record sizes :-

(a) If possible, make access to a file a common sub-routine so that the
only program affected is the 'file-program'; however, if you are
returning the COMPLETE record to your calling program then the copybook
change or the returned data through Linkage will necessitate the calling
program being re-compiled. (If you only return selective fields, then
'receiving' programs don't need to be re-compiled).

(b) It is not a bad idea to add a FILLER area to the end of your file
record format, say, 10 or 20 characters - so that this area can hold
values for future fields.

(c) If you are NOT CHANGING existing record size :-

    01 existing-record.
       05 existing data    pic x(......
       05 filler	   pic x(10).

    in your copybook change the filler to, e.g. :-      

    01 existing-record.
       05 existing data    pic x(......
       05 new-number	   pic 9(02).
       05 new-char	   pic x.
       05 filler	   pic x(07).   *> 10 - 3

Then you need to run a program - read in existing records and write out
new records either initializing or move zeroes or spaces to new-number
and new-char. (If you already know the values to go into new-number and
new-char, you could enter them at this stage).

(d) If you ARE CHANGING the record size :-

oldcopybook :-

    01 old-existing-record.
       05 old-existing data    pic x(......
	
newcopybook :-

    01 new-existing-record.
       05 new-existing data    pic x(......
       05 new-number	       pic 9(02).
       05 new-char	       pic x.
       05 filler	       pic x(10). 
  
Read in oldfile, move old-existing data to new-existing data; initialize
or move values to new-number and new-char; write new-existing record.
 
It's messy - but helps if you can plan for record-size increases. I
don't know which compiler you are using, but mine will re-compile all
programs which have a copybook which has been changed.

Jimmy, Calgary AB
```

#### ↳ Re: Adding extra fields...

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81ect5$17v$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
In article <81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>,
  morella3253@my-deja.com wrote:
> Hi to all,
>
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Morella Hernandez

As other posters have said: There are situations that require changes
to other programs and other situations that will work without the
changes.

However, let me throw in a plug for future maintenance. Let's say you
change the copybook/records for your immediate needs in program ABC but
don't change program XYZ's view of that file's records.
Three years later someone gets a hot project that causes them to add
some fields to the same record layout, for use in program XYZ. So they
use 'a few unused bytes at the end of the record'. Unfortunately they
don't see the changes you made, so they step on top of the 'few unused
bytes at the end of the record' that your activities changed. Your
previously-modified records get clobbered with their new data.
IMHO It's better to bite the time bullet and recompile them all. Maybe
you can convince the Q/A guys that the recompiles don't need to be
tested.

John

>
> Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
> Before you buy.
>


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Adding extra fields...

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<th8n3sk0qvr6k8lmiocr4488jmah4bg1em@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <81ect5$17v$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
On Tue, 23 Nov 1999 15:46:46 GMT john_mindock@my-deja.com wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>However, let me throw in a plug for future maintenance. Let's say you
:>change the copybook/records for your immediate needs in program ABC but
:>don't change program XYZ's view of that file's records.
:>Three years later someone gets a hot project that causes them to add
:>some fields to the same record layout, for use in program XYZ. So they
:>use 'a few unused bytes at the end of the record'. 

Which will be in the copybook.

They will see that those few bytes they thought were available by looking at
the listing aren't available.

As a side point, I would expect that few programmers look at the COBOL listing
(rather than the source) other than to check the DMAP/PMAP after an abend.

I used to, for complicated COBOL statements, after I had discovered a bug in
the compiler. I wanted to make sure the machine code was correct.

:>                                                   Unfortunately they
:>don't see the changes you made, so they step on top of the 'few unused
:>bytes at the end of the record' that your activities changed. Your
:>previously-modified records get clobbered with their new data.
:>IMHO It's better to bite the time bullet and recompile them all. Maybe
:>you can convince the Q/A guys that the recompiles don't need to be
:>tested.

If the source program uses the copybook there is no issue and no need to
recompile.

If the source program does not use the copybook you already have a maintenance
nightmare and I would advise rectification of the problem.

All recompiles should be QA'd. There is always the possibility that the source
and load do not match as well as changes in compiler options.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Adding extra fields...

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383BF15E.485ED864@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <81ect5$17v$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <th8n3sk0qvr6k8lmiocr4488jmah4bg1em@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:

> As a side point, I would expect that few programmers look at the COBOL listing
> (rather than the source) other than to check the DMAP/PMAP after an abend.

But line numbers in the abend reference the listing, not the source.
 
> I used to, for complicated COBOL statements, after I had discovered a bug in
> the compiler. I wanted to make sure the machine code was correct.

I have seen some troubles with pre-compiler nested IFs before - looking
at the listing will help here.


> If the source program uses the copybook there is no issue and no need to
> recompile.

WHAT?!?!  If you change the size of the record there certainly IS a need
to recompile.  It also helps you read formatted dumps if the record
format is current.

And it is easier to recompile everything when a copymember changes - as
opposed to finding implementing the new record format next time
maintenance is done on that program.  Change it everywhere, test it, and
keep the change simple.


> All recompiles should be QA'd. There is always the possibility that the source
> and load do not match as well as changes in compiler options.

What does "QA'd" mean?  

We use Endeavor to (among other things) force standard compile options
in all production jobs.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Adding extra fields...

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** postingid@dissensoftware.com (Binyamin Dissen)
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<g26o3sororpdiuohu2ga1udj7mg70aubc9@4ax.com>`
- **References:** `<81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <81ect5$17v$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <th8n3sk0qvr6k8lmiocr4488jmah4bg1em@4ax.com> <383BF15E.485ED864@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
On Wed, 24 Nov 1999 07:08:30 -0700 Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
wrote:

:>Binyamin Dissen wrote:

   [ snipped ]

:>> If the source program uses the copybook there is no issue and no need to
:>> recompile.

:>WHAT?!?!  If you change the size of the record there certainly IS a need
:>to recompile.  It also helps you read formatted dumps if the record
:>format is current.

If the record size changes you will have a bit more work than just
recompiling. File conversions, archival copies of programs to be able to run
against the data backups, etc.

If "unused bytes" are used and thus the record size didn't change, why
recompile?

:>And it is easier to recompile everything when a copymember changes - as
:>opposed to finding implementing the new record format next time
:>maintenance is done on that program.  Change it everywhere, test it, and
:>keep the change simple.

If it is a copybook it will be done automatically.

If unused bytes of a record are needed for a particular subset of programs why
recompile all programs?

:>> All recompiles should be QA'd. There is always the possibility that the source
:>> and load do not match as well as changes in compiler options.

:>What does "QA'd" mean?  

Quality Assurance, i.e., tested.

:>We use Endeavor to (among other things) force standard compile options
:>in all production jobs.

And one can hope that everyone uses the standards.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Adding extra fields...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383D2065.6B8621D8@zip.com.au>`
- **References:** `<81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <81ect5$17v$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <th8n3sk0qvr6k8lmiocr4488jmah4bg1em@4ax.com> <383BF15E.485ED864@NOSPAMhome.com> <g26o3sororpdiuohu2ga1udj7mg70aubc9@4ax.com>`

```
Binyamin Dissen wrote:
> 
> On Wed, 24 Nov 1999 07:08:30 -0700 Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

QA does not necessarily mean tested.  It does mean verified.  IF your
procedures allow a visual verification by another experienced
programmer to ensure functionality is not compromised then that is
absolutely fine.

If you do wish to test what level do you require.
-  Smoke test - run a parallel production run and does it fall over.
- Thorough test - run a full testing schedule against it.

We work with compromises, where do you draw the line.

I would not necessarily require a recompile, however I would have to
be very familiar with the application to be sure.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Adding extra fields...

_(thread depth: 5)_

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol

Then you continue with other statements which require that the record

#### ↳ Re: Adding extra fields...

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<i1I_3.1692$bH2.27451@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
All the responses so far are correct; you will have to
recompile all the programs. But if you will permit a
re-phrasing of your question, it might be made clearer.

"I have to add fields to the end of an existing record.
I will change the appropriate copy books, but does
record definition take place at execution time or at
compile time?

The answer is "compile time." Copy books are not
a form of data dictionary the program interrogates at
run time to access the latest rendition of the data.

<morella3253@my-deja.com> wrote in message
news:81btp7$7sh$1@nnrp1.deja.com...
> Hi to all,
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> The two extra fields will be added to the end of the existing
record.
>
>
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-cfd89450e8"></a>
## Thread: OT Programmer's stress/burnout. — 22 this month / 24 total

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### OT Programmer's stress/burnout.

- **From:** "Anon" <anon@anon.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
How do you handle the stress that eventually comes in a programming job?

If you know of anyone who left the profession and is still working what they
are doing now?

I have been a COBOL programmer for about 25 years and lurker / occasional
poster to this newsgroup for the past 1.5 years, but am down to the last two
weeks (my choice).  Have worked on Y2K for over a year but have been a state
of panic more and more often.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT Programmer's stress/burnout.

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3839BB06.7D8E96A7@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
Anon wrote:
> 
> How do you handle the stress that eventually comes in a programming job?
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> of panic more and more often.

Stress is more about the person than the job.  But over the years I have
decided to work fewer hour.  I will no longer work 60 hour work weeks. 
And rarely 50 hours.  But I know of people who work fewer hours than I
do who are always stressed out and hating the job.  That's really not
due to the job, but to the programmer.

The people who I know who have quit programming and work full time are
almost all people who couldn't program (and moved on within a few
years), or who have been promoted into management.
```

- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<bKD_3.1959$7a.46527@nnrp3.clara.net>`
- **References:** `<PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <3839BB06.7D8E96A7@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```

>> I have been a COBOL programmer for about 25 years and lurker / occasional
>> poster to this newsgroup for the past 1.5 years, but am down to the last
two
>> weeks (my choice).  Have worked on Y2K for over a year but have been a
state
>> of panic more and more often.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>years), or who have been promoted into management.

I agree that people have different susceptibilities to stress but in my
experience stress is more related to how much control one has over one's
job.  As a rule of thumb, the less control, the more stress.

It's all too easy for organisations with an oppresive or abusive management
style, to blame the stress they cause on the 'weakness' of the employees.
In the UK their have been a few high profile court cases with over-stressed
employees suing their employers - and often being successful.

Regards,

Stephen Thomas,
talkingcats@gobble.clara.co.uk ('gobble' = spamtrap)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT Programmer's stress/burnout.

- **From:** "John Czarnuszewicz" <jczar@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<KEY_3.59$3O3.18421@bgtnsc05-news.ops.worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <3839BB06.7D8E96A7@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
I too work fewer hours, especially when it comes to fixing problems made by
some poor management decision or some really pathetic coding by another
programmer. I do not enjoy 'cleaning up after elephants' or 'polishing
turds'.

When things are bad at work, I take more frequent breaks or 'work' on
something I like better.
I also walk away from the desk and go for a walk. I am technically good
enough to get away with this and not get dumped on by management. When this
stops working, I get another job.

I have been in data processing since 1966 and working as a full time
programmer since 1975.
PC based positions.
```

#### ↳ Re: OT Programmer's stress/burnout.

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81cc3a$301p$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```

Anon wrote in message ...
>How do you handle the stress that eventually comes in a programming job?

It depends entirely upon the individual and the source
of the stress. Unfortunately, the individual may not be
the best person to objectively identify the source.

In my case, I realized that I was at my best when
creating new applications or products. During the
first 14 years with my current employer, I had plenty
of opportunity to be creative, I was creative, and I was
happy with my job. During the past 8 years, it has been
a steady progression of one unsatisfying task after
another with little opportunity to be creative.

By mutual agreement my status with the company will
change from employee to consultant as of the end
of this year. For all practical purposes, I am retiring
while I am still young enough to enjoy it.

Problem solved!

Next problem, what to do for the next 20 to 30 years.
I have a few ideas to develop involving the COBOL
language that specifically does not include writing a
production compiler. These ideas should keep me
busy and creative for a few years. If not, there is
always golf.
------------------
Rick Smith
```

#### ↳ Re: OT Programmer's stress/burnout.

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81clfa$ig4$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
Hi Anon,
We are in a demanding business, and I feel that you should find a friend to
talk to. If you do not like to involve your friends or family, feel free to
write to me.

Good stress keeps you awake and alert to 'make the job done' but when it is
out of control it will take over. You will feel extreemly tired and you will
begin to doubt your own qualifications. If that sounds familiar, your body
and mind tells you that it is time to slow down.

'Slow down'! 'I have a schedule'!!!! Yes, my friend, but if do not slow
down, you'll need a doctor. My 'anti-stress-activity' is to change my daily
habits.

Many people with stress symptoms are working and working. The quality of the
work is decreasing. Even if you are busy, you should try just one day to
leave early, take a long walk, eat a good dinner, and sleep all night. Next
day will be a better day, and don't you forget to reward yourself. Do not
work harder - work smarter, and reward yourself for every little success.

Many of my friends left the profession over the years. One of them became a
handy-man and he made his living by minor repairs, preferably for single
women in the county. He was 55 and happy for a while - now he is married
again, and his wife tells us that he is still happy.

Others became teachers/instructors, security consultants and project
leaders. When we meet, we always talk about good old days, you know, working
all night 7 days a week, having fun behind schedule and so on. Think about
it - would you miss it?

Y2K is probably the best project to be on. There is a very clear deadline
and most of the programs will be ok shortly after roll-over. Think about
it - we have changed lots of programs which could not subtract 1 from
january 1st. Very few of them will actually run that day - more likely
January 3rd., and that bloody section will never be performed.

Find some frinds to talk to, and remember - we are here.

Regards
Ib


Anon skrev i meddelelsen ...
>How do you handle the stress that eventually comes in a programming job?
>
>If you know of anyone who left the profession and is still working what
they
>are doing now?
>
>I have been a COBOL programmer for about 25 years and lurker / occasional
>poster to this newsgroup for the past 1.5 years, but am down to the last
two
>weeks (my choice).  Have worked on Y2K for over a year but have been a
state
>of panic more and more often.
>
>
>
>
```

> 
> How do you handle the stress that eventually comes in a programming
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…

Seek professional advise.  It seems stupid but they can give you a
surprising amount of support.  Often it is a matter of unloading to

#### ↳ Re: OT Programmer's stress/burnout.

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81e9ha$ubf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
In article <PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>,
  "Anon" <anon@anon.com> wrote:
> How do you handle the stress that eventually comes in a programming
job?

It's always been my opinion that stress comes from not having control
over your job rather than long hours or doing critical work.  Clerks
being held responsible for things beyond their control have more stress
than surgeons or airline captains.

In programming, if I am doing the parts that I love, like developing
new software or being given a free hand to cleanup legacy code, the
hours matter little. I often have a hard time tearing myself away from
the work.

I do contract work and my mantra to remove all stress is "Remember, you
are being paid by the hour."
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT Programmer's stress/burnout.

- **From:** "Anon" <anon@anon.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<cpB_3.41093$YI2.2020083@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`
- **References:** `<PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <81e9ha$ubf$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```

Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com> wrote in message
> > How do you handle the stress that eventually comes in a programming
> job?
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> than surgeons or airline captains.

That seems pretty close to the mark.  Poor documentation on system flow (yes
I've researched parts on many occasions, but there are still holes), and the
fact we only have old copies of the client's data so testing a "finished"
product doesn't result is a "I KNOW this will work!" answer.  On getting new
assignments I have to wait for the "panic" to go away.

> In programming, if I am doing the parts that I love, like developing
> new software or being given a free hand to cleanup legacy code, the
> hours matter little. I often have a hard time tearing myself away from
> the work.

There were times I felt the same way, I enjoy writing utilities or tackling
challenging algorythms, indeed, a remark like "It probably can't be done in
COBOL," would usually get me in the thick of it.

I appreciate your post and others in this thread.  If I ever do get back
into this profession (I'm not much past 50)  what would I say about the time
out or things to do in a new job to keep it (me) from unraveling?
```

#### ↳ Re: OT Programmer's stress/burnout.

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<TfIwBBA0bpO4Ewb9@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
In article <PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>, Anon
<anon@anon.com> writes
>How do you handle the stress that eventually comes in a programming job?
>
I worked for a company for seven years. I found that if I arrived at
7:30 and was the first person in the front door I was also the last
person out of the door (at 20:00). Eventually I realised that it did not
matter how much I did there was always more to be done. If you feel that
you are doing a good worthwhile job and if the management show their
appreciation regularly then you would be surprised just how much stress
you can tolerate. Unfortunately, all you need is a worthless
unworthwhile slog or an unappreciative management and the fun goes out
of the job. That happened to me. When I realised what was happening I
started delegating all the work that I didn't fancy doing and would not
find interesting onto my juniors shoulders. I then concentrated on the
more interesting work (pre-emptive problem management, work on systems
that I knew well and liked..) and slowed down. Rather than do work in a
hurry (and make a mistake) then having to apologise/grovel to the
customer before doing a rushed fix (recursive fixs here) I checked and
double checked my work before releasing it to the live environment.
Result: less mistakes, less stress, happier me. If you can not delegate
work then slow down and make sure that what you do is correct. I saw one
programmer work every possible hour over six months on a new project
developing a new type of reporting suite (previously untried by our
company), he wanted the overtime to buy a new car. After the delivery he
went off sick for the next six months. The decline in his health had
been apparent during the project but the managers only cared about
delivery of the project and not their worker. Don't let it happen to
you.

Finally, I suspect it is not wise to tell your management that you are
suffering from stress. They won't see it as being an indictment of the
workplace but rather that you are incapable of being the young dynamic
systems professional that they had previously thought you were. I
watched one Business Analyst suffer a nervous breakdown. When his
managers spotted that he was missing meetings and failing to meet
deadlines they asked him what was going on. When they found out he was
ill he was told to resign. 
```

#### ↳ Re: OT Programmer's stress/burnout.

#### ↳ Re: OT Programmer's stress/burnout.

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<fVA_3.48153$Hk.435863@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com>`

```
Anon wrote:
>How do you handle the stress that eventually comes in a programming job?

When I was a young programmer, my boss and I had to work all night at
a client's site.  The next day as we were walking to lunch, and the
temperature was close to 100 degrees F., we complained a bit about long
hours.  As we crossed a plank over a deep ditch in the sidewalk, we
could see several men down there swinging picks.  My boss looked at me
and said "Jud, there are worse jobs than programming."  He was right. ;-)
```

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>

I have evolved a few rules over the years for stress. For one, I do not work
weekends, or at least not more than one or two a year.  Friday night to
monday morning is sacred off time.  No shop talk during meals and coffee
breaks.  A twelve by six snooker table and a piano in the programming
office.  etc.

>How do you handle the stress that eventually comes in a programming job?

they

two
>weeks (my choice).  Have worked on Y2K for over a year but have been a
state

##### ↳ ↳ Re: OT Programmer's stress/burnout.

- **From:** john_mindock@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81h2ls$vmc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <81fgvv$qgu$1@news.igs.net>`

```

> Anon wrote in message ...
> >How do you handle the stress that eventually comes in a programming
job?
Just as a mental exercise:
I've classified the 'stress' that I most notice into 4 technical
categories. There surely are more on the 'personality conflict and
office culture' side...

1. There's a transaction/job/system problem and it needs to be fixed
immediately. I.e., correctly, with little testing, no resultant new
bugs, and damage repaired.

2. There's a nighttime job failure and other jobs/systems are waiting
for it to be fixed/rerun.

3. There's a project with a management-declared deadline, that must be
met or managers will not get their bonuses.

4. The program you are writing must absolutely work correctly the first
time, and forever.

While all have nuances in their stress-causation, one common thread is
TIME.

Typically, management wants you work on #1 and #2 until you get it
fixed. You aren't expected to sleep, eat, rest, etc. until you get it
done.
Similarly, on #3, when you find yourself 'behind schedule', you're
expected to allocate (often for no compensation) weekend and evening
time to the project.

On #4 you need time to completely test the functionality.

Based on the above one-faceted view, it would seem that one could
alleviate some stress by demanding/begging for/simply taking/etc. more
time for the chores at hand.

But easier said than done - often management has other goals than
employee mental health. Time pressure is very common.
No need to detail (to this forum) how target dates are picked, how
specs change, how other things steal the time from the grunts.

So maybe it's true in this case - TIME is of the essence. Acquire the
time to do the job and maybe it's smoother sailing.

John












Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Programmer's stress/burnout.

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81jetb$fog$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <81fgvv$qgu$1@news.igs.net> <81h2ls$vmc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
john_mindock@my-deja.com wrote in message <81h2ls$vmc$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...

>1. There's a transaction/job/system problem and it needs to be fixed
>immediately. I.e., correctly, with little testing, no resultant new
>bugs, and damage repaired.


Backup first.  Then start at the beginning, work your way through the
middle, and end at the end.  It will take as long as it takes.  Do not
guess, and do not attempt a fast patch unless you know exactly what is
wrong. Work it through and do it right regardless.

I have done live updates to the control system of a nuclear reactor.  I
decided early in the game that if anybody told me to hurry, I would have
them kicked out of the control room as a distraction.  Although I have
learnt to ignore my surroundings, I still have no compunction about pulling
rank when an amateur gets in the way.

>
>2. There's a nighttime job failure and other jobs/systems are waiting
>for it to be fixed/rerun.


same advice.

>3. There's a project with a management-declared deadline, that must be
>met or managers will not get their bonuses.
>


How much is *my* bonus, and is it worth it?  If it is, do it. If it is not,
then let the managers earn their bonuses by getting enough resources to do
the job.  BTW, that *is* what management is all about ... I am a manager.
If they cannot get the facilities to do the job, and accepted the deadline
anyway, then they do not deserve the bonus.

>4. The program you are writing must absolutely work correctly the first
>time, and forever.


True of all programs, at least all but "the first time".  The first time,
you are just compiling for syntax errors.  I allow my programmers to compile
it a second time for production, though I never need that myself.  ;<)

Assign sysin=console
Assign sysout=program.exe
Cobol
Type program here.
run

(forgive me, early morning stress)
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: OT Programmer's stress/burnout.

_(thread depth: 6)_

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00

- **References:** `<PVh_3.37270$YI2.1874042@typ11.nn.bcandid.com> <81fgvv$qgu$1@news.igs.net> <81h2ls$vmc$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <81jetb$fog$1@news.igs.net> <383DEE67.AAE3EAA6@att.net> <81kve9$b7c$1@news.igs.net>`

## Thread: MERANT Netexpress 3.0 cannot find... — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MERANT Netexpress 3.0 cannot find...

- **From:** Juergen@bop99.ping.de (Juergen Vetter)
- **Date:** 1999-11-22T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<7TNi-7aWOtB@bop99.ping.de>`

```

I'm having several problems with netexpress version 3.0.

Problem 01:
===========
several compiler directives won't work with the baseinstallation.
This is a set of directives:
----------------------------
echo TESTPGM.OBJ                            > TEMPFILE.RSP
echo TESTPGM.LST, TESTPGM.GRP              >> TEMPFILE.RSP
echo CONFIRM                               >> TEMPFILE.RSP
rem echo MAKESYN "COMP-5" = "COMP"         >> TEMPFILE.RSP
rem echo LITLINK(1)                        >> TEMPFILE.RSP
rem echo CASE                              >> TEMPFILE.RSP
rem echo ASSIGN "EXTERNAL"                 >> TEMPFILE.RSP
rem echo OUTDD "SYSOUT.TXT" AREACHECK      >> TEMPFILE.RSP
rem echo DEFAULTBYTE "0" NOQUERY IBMCOMP   >> TEMPFILE.RSP
COBOL.EXE H:\VETTER\DIENST\DATA2\UP4000\TESTPGM, @TEMPFILE.RSP

Problem 02:
===========
WebSync

There is a serive pack out. But this file (30 MB) is corrupted.
After 23 mb download ended normally. If you want to install the service pack
you got the error: file is corrupted :(

Problem 03:
===========
i use the dos-script at the top in windows98 and it works fine (it's a  
workaround to me to check the linker) but the linker does not work

It tells me:
error (11) c runtime library not found

Which library does the linker need?
I look at the path and add some directories like  
c:\;c:\windows;c:\windows\system.
It does not work

I copied all c++ DLLs in the local directory
It does not work

I copied link.exe from netexpress\base\bin to the local directory
It does not work

Sure, i installed netexpress very well without errors in c:\netexpress.
I used the Netexpress commandline shell
=========================================================================
Micro Focus NetExpress V3
Version 3.0.14 Copyright (C) 1984-1998 Micro Focus Ltd.
URN AXCGG/AA0/00000
* Accepted - CONFIRM
* Compiling H:\VETTER\DIENST\DATA2\UP4000\TESTPGM.cbl
* Checking complete with no errors - starting code generation
* Generating H:\VETTER\DIENST\DATA2\UP4000\TESTPGM
* Data:         384     Code:         440     Literals:         124
Micro Focus NetExpress - CBLLINK utility
Version 3.0.14 Copyright (C) 1984-1998 Micro Focus Ltd.

Executing:cblnames /N /FS /V /S /DTESTPGM.def TESTPGM.obj

Scanning object files:-
TESTPGM.obj
        found public -> TESTPGM
        Creating cbllds.obj
        Creating TESTPGM.def
        Creating cbllds.lnk
        CBLNAMES completed

ERROR: (11) Cannot find C runtime library



=========================================================================
Who can help me?
```

My name is Denis Dew and I work for Metro Information Services.

I have about 10 years experience porting from one COBOL/Platform to another.
I have written conversion and transformation tools.  I have been using SP2
(a graphical front-end for COBOL) for about 5 years.  If possible, re-use
your code.  Investigate SP2 (http://www.flexus.com)

office at 615-373-6902.

Denis

## Thread: Data Validation Student Project (0/1) — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### Data Validation Student Project (0/1)

- **From:** Larry <mesaboogy@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2EQ7OHy1Qw3wXspgcFkN0OJyacZ0@4ax.com>`

```
Hi,
I've attached the source and input data file for a data validation
project for an introductory COBOL course. If anyone wants to look at
it and make some suggestions for better code, please do. It works the
way it is supposed to now, I just wanted to improve upon it. Thanks.

BTW, we use Microfocus Personal COBOL 2.0 for DOS.
```

#### ↳ Re: Data Validation Student Project (0/1)

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81jrvo$1na$1@neptunium.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<2EQ7OHy1Qw3wXspgcFkN0OJyacZ0@4ax.com>`

```

Larry wrote in message <2EQ7OHy1Qw3wXspgcFkN0OJyacZ0@4ax.com>...
>Hi,
>I've attached the source and input data file for a data validation
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>BTW, we use Microfocus Personal COBOL 2.0 for DOS.


One tip, you could refrain from using periods (.) after every line of
code, this is unnecessary and very "old" this is why we have END-*
statements nowadays.

Another tip, code in lowercase - it looks better and easier to read IMHO.

As for logic I only browsed your code, keep up the good work.

Simon R Hart.
```

news:3842C04F.62F91A85@NOSPAMhome.com...
> Simon R Hart wrote:
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> > Another tip, code in lowercase - it looks better and easier to read
IMHO.
> >
>
> But if you start working at our shop, put those periods back in.  I bet
> you would have a problem getting lower case to be accepted here as well.

Amen.  A former supervisor in this shop had a small revolt over this issue,
especially when the code ended up in mixed upper/lower case.  While putting
together a script to perform automated changes to programs checked out for
Y2K, I was able to add a program to convert all non-commented, non-quoted
text to uppercase.  That program was named in his honor <g>.
```

>
The best place to get a free compiler or to learn how to write COBOL
code is to buy Thane Hubbell's book, "Teach Yourself COBOL In 24
Hours!"

It is not only a great book, but also incudes 16 and 32 bit versions
of the Fujitsu COBOL compiler.  The ISBN number of Thane's book is:

0-672-31453-3

You can buy the book on-line at:

http://www.hasting.com
http://www.bookpool.com
http://www.barnesandnoble.com



Bob Wolfe, flexus
Check out The Flexus COBOL Page at http://www.flexus.com
```

#### ↳ Re: looking for good cobol editor

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<mfe04.364$cq6.6289@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<81f1o8$79q$1@trex.antw.online.be>`

```
If your student compiler comes with an editor, you're probably
ahead using it. Many times compiler vendors bundle an
editor that interacts with their compiler (editor jumps right to
errors detected from last compile). Further, vendor-supplied
editors know you are dealing with COBOL and can take
language-specific features to heart (indentation, highlighting
reserved words, etc.) then automatically invoke the proper
compiler, linker, and so forth.


Thomas Zoller <Thomas.Zoller@advalvas.be> wrote in message
news:81f1o8$79q$1@trex.antw.online.be...

>

good and
> easy Cobol-editor?
>
> Thanks in advance
>
>
```

```

www.ultraedit.com

grtx,
Anton

Thomas Zoller <Thomas.Zoller@advalvas.be> wrote in message
news:81f1o8$79q$1@trex.antw.online.be...

and

#### ↳ Re: looking for good cobol editor

- **From:** AnhMy Tran <anhmytran@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s45hk91tmvm48@corp.supernews.com>`
- **References:** `<81f1o8$79q$1@trex.antw.online.be>`

```
I studied COBOL in 1992, with VAX, and in 1993, I got a PC COBOL editor.
I am a mainframe COBOL now. I have been helping students in college with
COBOL, and their instructors encourage them to use Text editor.

However, my PC COBOL editor is more convenient to students than any Text
editors, Notepad, WordPad, Words, WordPerfect, DOS, Boland C/C++ editors
or MS Visual C++ editor. It is totaly free.

The problem is that I do not have manual book to give you the instruction.
It is very easy and simple to students of 1980s or early 1990s when we use
DOS screen without mouse and menu bar.

It is RM-COBOL editor and compiler, in a single floppy disk. Using this
editor, you cannot copy, cut or paste a selection. You cannot move the
cursor to the beginning or to the end of line as in MS environment.
You can copy, cut or paste a block of lines. The Tab key helps you to
move to the position 7 and 12, which are the critical position for the
coding, and it keeps each position as one byte. It means that when you
apply the Tab key, the code does not have a real tab, but 7 spaces, or
1 space, or 4 spaces instead. None of other text editors helps you to
type and jump at COBOL critical points like that.

Compare with MS editors as Notepad, WordPad and Words, RM-COBOL editor
is equally fast. It takes more time to save the change, and the skills
you learn from it cannot be applied in the MS environment. It helps you
get familiar with VAX and mainframe. That is the reason you may want to
try it, but not necessary to learn it. It take me few hours to use RM-
COBOL editor. It may take you few days or longer to figure it out. Please,
do not think that I under-estimate you. I am a student of early 1990s,
and you are students of Mouse and Menu bar, who never plays DOS games.

If you have the interest of exploring the old technology, write me.
```

---

I followed the thread and am quite impressed.  You began with a
pre-conceived notion that COBOL is obsolete because your friends said
that it was so.  Perhaps your friends may not have a significant
amount of real-world data processing experience.

you are re-thinking your position on the subject.  This is a mark of
true intelligence.  You are maintaining an open mind and that is what
will make you successful.

environments you know, the more valuable you will be in the workplace.

<a id="t-7d0ebe1afb"></a>
## Thread: java as subroutines for legacy programs. — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`COBOL's future, legacy, and obsolescence`](topics.md#future)_

### java as subroutines for legacy programs.

- **From:** Set.KuoLern@mail.uob.com.sg (kuolern)
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.java.databases,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3839fe31.5727565@news.pacific.net.sg>`

```
hi,

our cobol programs in HPUX and VB on NT has common need for the same
subroutines. We are exploring the posibility of writing the subroutine
in JAVA. This routines are required to access orocle database.

The issue is 
1. How do we manage the database connection? that is, if the routine
is used 100 time in a calling program, we don't want to connect 100
times.
2. Is there existing interface layer between MicroFocus cobol ver5 to
java?

Pls advise. 

thanks.
```

#### ↳ Re: java as subroutines for legacy programs.

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.java.databases,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383ADCBD.567@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<3839fe31.5727565@news.pacific.net.sg>`

```
kuolern wrote:
> 
> hi,
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks.

We are just beginning to design an "application server" tier for this
type of need.  Java and Java-beans will run on this server to perform
the common tasks of reading/writing to our oracle databases.  

I think my company has choosen IBM's Websphere and VA-Java for running
this middle tier.

Our driving need, is that this will also support many concurrent users
on the internet!  Since the internet is our ultimate goal, we are also
designing multiple firewall's into the system.

Pete
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: java as subroutines for legacy programs.

- **From:** David Sanders <dsanders@legacyj.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.java.databases,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383C3318.77C1F019@legacyj.com>`
- **References:** `<3839fe31.5727565@news.pacific.net.sg> <383ADCBD.567@saif.com>`

```
Our Cobol compiler, called PERCobol, supports Java and JavaBeans.  It
enables Cobol to run in the Java environment and has support for EXEC-SQL
through JDBC.  It could be what you are looking for.

Visit our site at:  www.legacyj.com

Thanks,

David Sanders
LegacyJ Corporation
www.legacyj.com
```

#### ↳ Re: java as subroutines for legacy programs.

- **From:** "J. S. Jensen" <jsjensen@saba.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.java.databases,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383B15CB.6F4FD123@saba.com>`
- **References:** `<3839fe31.5727565@news.pacific.net.sg>`

```
kuolern wrote:

> We are exploring the posibility of writing the subroutine in JAVA. This
> routines are required to access orocle database.

Good idea.

> 1. How do we manage the database connection? that is, if the routine is
> used 100 time in a calling program, we don't want to connect 100 times.

Yes, you will need to create a native wrapper.  The best way (in my
opinion) to implement this is to have a Java database connection daemon
with a persistent database connection, and then make native calls into it
via a named pipe, a socket, or RMI.

> 2. Is there existing interface layer between MicroFocus cobol ver5 to
> java?

These are few and far in between, but I've never personally come across a
good one.  Why don't you just talk local domain sockets to a java process
for database access/subroutines?
```

#### ↳ Re: java as subroutines for legacy programs.

- **From:** Paul Butkiewicz <pbutkiewicz@quoininc.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.java.databases,comp.lang.java.programmer,comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383C34D1.71143932@quoininc.com>`
- **References:** `<3839fe31.5727565@news.pacific.net.sg>`

```
kuolern wrote:

> hi,
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> thanks.

What about simply using CORBA?  I know an IDL binding for cobol has been
defined, and I'm sure various ORB's support it.

Paul
```

<a id="t-7349a7bc74"></a>
## Thread: Cobol Program on NT — 1 msgs

### Re: Cobol Program on NT

- **From:** "Westley Lodge Pty Ltd" <wlodge@hotkey.net.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Kbt_3.114$rS1.2028@nsw.nnrp.telstra.net>`
- **References:** `<80vjv3$i16$1@bunyip.cc.uq.edu.au>`

```
James

I don't quite understand what you have done.

Is the program still a CICS program on the NT?. Or you've ported it to NT,
and converted it to a "batch, non-CICS" program.

In any case, if the program is still a CICS/DB2 program, you cannot compile
it to an exe. It must be a DLL. You use the CICSTCL command to transalte,
compile and link it.

ie... CICSTCL prgname.CCP (-q"SQL('DATABASE dbname')",TEST,DYNAM

if the program is now batch program then the correct command should be

COB2 -g -q"SQL('DATABASE dbname')" prgname.CBL

If you did any of the above, it should work fine


Good luck and keep on COBOLing

Rgds

Theo

James <jamsan@uq.net.au> wrote in message
news:80vjv3$i16$1@bunyip.cc.uq.edu.au...
> I have a CICS Cobol DB2 program which was written on an OS390 environment.
I
> have now ported that program to an NT system. The CICS & DB2 environments
> are local to the NT box.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…

but
> when I try to run it I get the message "Program XYZ.EXE is not a valid
> Windows NT application." (CICS was started before trying to run the
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-8c1fae8b58"></a>
## Thread: Read versus Read Into the diffinitive answer... — 1 msgs

### Read versus Read Into the diffinitive answer...

- **From:** Bill Thompson <wthompson@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81e4qe$qln$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Dang!!! I am really getting to hate these Deja delays and omissions!!!!

No!  The routine in the generated COBOL code did not get called upon a
READ!  That code only got called when the elementary field that was the
object of "occurs depending on" phrase got altered, that included a
change to any group level to which that elementary field was a part of.

That was why any intelligent programmer would identify which of those
01s in the FD was the proper record just read in and then before
referring to any variable field in that record would "MOVE RCD-CNTR-
(whichever) TO RCD-CNTR-(itself)".  This then caused the compiler to
set the length of THAT 01 to the correct length.

READ INTOs always used max record as the from length and the length of
the to area as the to length.  This could result in either truncation
or padding as will any move of dissimilar length areas.

As a note, to avoid the constant recalculation of variable length
areas, the ODO object was often redefined with a slightly different
name and then could be referenced without the compiler doing a
recalculation.

With all this clamor about READ versus READ INTO of variable length
records, I now am understanding why some interviewers in the past were
so concerned with variable length record I/O skills - while I never
thought much about them....

I learned in a shop that had many variable length KSDS files,
synchronized to each other (SQL was a long ways away).  Many of those
files had multiple ODO phrases and one had four.  To modify there
records did require a READ INTO but mere input only reads did not.

Learning a few simple "rules" that were required to avoid clobbering
storage was all that was needed, and these rules are still valid to
this day.

Bill
```

---
