<a id="t-dae47d2d7a"></a>
## Thread: Data records.... — 5 msgs

### Data records....

- **From:** morella3253@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81ejd0$6om$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Hi to all,

A few questions that have left me puzzled:

1) I have a record which has a filler PIC X(21) around the center
   of the record. I am now going to use 6 of those characters to
   hold a number (PIC 9(6)). All new records will have the 6 characters
   set to 000000 - 999999. What happens when I read an old record that
   has spaces (ie. part of PIC X(21)). Will i have to handle this in
   the program or can I do a CONVERT (OPENVMS) to pad or fill the
   6 characters with 000000?

2)  I have a field that is PIC 9(6) COMP-3, and its contents are
    NL but in one column (i.e. the N is above the L). What is this
    and what would happen if I read this field into PIC X(6)?

3) I have a record that is 256 characters and is made up of fields
   characters, comps, etc...
   Why is it that some records are longer than others, does 256 mean
   that if I counted 256 columns along the record, this should give me
   the end of the record? The FDL says that the record is 256
   characters and it is a fixed length record.

Thanks in advance for any help

Morella Hernandez


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: Data records....

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81erap$g0a$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<81ejd0$6om$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
1. Convert the data (If Blah-Blah not numeric move zeroes to Blah-Blah
End-if)
When you convert all records, you can be sure that the records match the
definition and you can avoid a data exception.

2. a comp-3 field is packed numeric data. Your 9(6) takes 4 bytes (2 digits
in every byte except the last byte. It contains 1 digit and 1 sign (C og
D)). +125 would look like this:
1st byte x'00'
2nd byte x'00'
3rd byte x'12'
4th byte x'5C'

If you count, you can see, that pic 9(7) would also take 4 byte. In fact, I
always define Comp-3 fields as an odd number of digits. You can easily move
a comp-3 field to a pic x field, but I cannot see why, except if you want to
display the contents. If so, move the com-3 field to a pic S9 (7) first.

3 A fixed length 256 record is 256 bytes long.

Regards
Ib
morella3253@my-deja.com skrev i meddelelsen <81ejd0$6om$1@nnrp1.deja.com>...
>Hi to all,
>
…[27 more quoted lines elided]…
>Before you buy.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Data records....

- **From:** johnw@stones.com (John Whistler)
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<B460DEE0966860A7C9@relay06-b.indigo.ie>`
- **References:** `<81ejd0$6om$1@nnrp1.deja.com> <81erap$g0a$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
In article <81erap$g0a$1@news.inet.tele.dk>,
"Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk> wrote:

>morella3253@my-deja.com wrote
>>Hi to all,
…[49 more quoted lines elided]…
>

1) The VMS Convert command will not alter the contents of a record, it can
only alter the type of file (sequential to indexed, variable to fixed etc.)
You will need to write a program to replace the spaces with zeros.

2) I assume you're looking at the data using the editor. This displays the
control characters corresponding to the binary value of a byte. The "NL"
means that the byte contains x'00' which, as Ib explains above, is
perfectly valid for a comp-3 field.

3) Again, I assume you're using the editor. If a comp or comp-3 field
contains x'09', the editor reads this as a tab character and moves the
cursor to the next tab stop. The only way to see exactly what is in your
data is to use the VMS 'dump' command. try 'dump/record' which will dump
records rather than disk blocks. 'help dump' will explain the format of the
output.

Regards,

John.
```

#### ↳ Re: Data records....

- **From:** Michiel Erens <I.dont.want.spam@my.address.in.NL>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383B14E8.45CC@my.address.in.NL>`
- **References:** `<81ejd0$6om$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
morella3253@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Hi to all,
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>    6 characters with 000000?

I don't believe Convert can do it. You don't want to extend the record,
but just fill it on a certain place. I would write a small program that
sequentially reads all the records and puts zeroes in the field. 

> 2)  I have a field that is PIC 9(6) COMP-3, and its contents are
>     NL but in one column (i.e. the N is above the L). What is this
>     and what would happen if I read this field into PIC X(6)?

Which utilily do you use to view the file ? 
 
> 3) I have a record that is 256 characters and is made up of fields
>    characters, comps, etc...
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>    characters and it is a fixed length record.

Again, which utilily do you use to view the file ? I guess the editor ?
Use the DUMP command to view the contents in decimal and hex notation. 
With DUMP /RECORD you can see the seperate records and their contents. 
With DUMP /RECORD=(START:5,COUNT:12) you'll see 12 records, starting
with 
number 5.
```

#### ↳ Re: Data records....

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383B8B39.2289FF12@att.net>`
- **References:** `<81ejd0$6om$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
morella3253@my-deja.com wrote:
> 
> Hi to all,
> 
(snip)
> 
> 3) I have a record that is 256 characters and is made up of fields
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>    characters and it is a fixed length record.

This one rang a bell - are you by any chance passing data from an IBM
mainframe to a UNIX box? UNIX (Solaris, anyway) doesn't do
packed-decimal (COMP-3) and the printouts make the mainframe data look
longer than it is. (I'm obviously a mainframed, sorry for any errors
concerning UNIX)

Bill Lynch
```

<a id="t-f1006680ab"></a>
## Thread: Cobol Program on NT — 1 msgs

### Re: Cobol Program on NT

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383accde.48744400@news2.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<80vjv3$i16$1@bunyip.cc.uq.edu.au>`

```
On Thu, 18 Nov 1999 11:18:03 +1000, "James" <jamsan@uq.net.au> wrote:

>I have a CICS Cobol DB2 program which was written on an OS390 environment. I
>have now ported that program to an NT system. The CICS & DB2 environments
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>The program is to run in batch mode
What does this mean?  If it is a CICS program, i.e. contains EXEC CICS Commands, it must
be compiled and linked to a DLL, a transaction code has to be set up in CICS NT, and then
the transaction has to be run under CICS

If you do NOT have a an EXEC CICS call, then you don't need to have CICS running anyways.


....

Unless, that is, you are trying to use CICS Client Server mechanisms, like the ECI.  If
this is the case, you need to link the ECI code into your application 


     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         WinErr: 01A Operating system overwritten - Please reinstall all your software. We are terribly sorry.
```

<a id="t-7c13e91d18"></a>
## Thread: Comp Formats — 11 msgs

### Comp Formats

- **From:** "Tony Tooley" <tony@infosearch.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<37A_3.7576$16.871133@news.flash.net>`

```
Can anyone tell me about the various computational fields in COBOL.
The MF Cobol manual I have has not details and I can find nothing in the
help files.
I nd to know

1)  How the data is stored (e.g IEEE, floating pt+mantissa, 2s complement
etc)
2) What the byte storage is for various numeric ranges
    e.g. S9(5)  COMP-0 takes 2 bytes,  how about 9(6), 9(8) COMP-0?
3) Is there anyway I can convert this data from outside COBOL?  Since I have
all the records already exported from Btrieve to a flat file, I wd like to
be able to read them and decode the computational fields from (say) Delphi.

tia
```

#### ↳ Re: Comp Formats

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<sVH_3.1641$bH2.27380@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<37A_3.7576$16.871133@news.flash.net>`

```
COMP-x is "implementer defined." There is no standard
definition.

We would need to know the hardware and (probably) the
compiler. To avoid confusion, you might try looking at the
data with a hex editor, comparing the bytes with known values,
and deducing (or verifying) the format by inspection.

Tony Tooley <tony@infosearch.com> wrote in message
news:37A_3.7576$16.871133@news.flash.net...
> Can anyone tell me about the various computational fields in COBOL.
> The MF Cobol manual I have has not details and I can find nothing in
the
> help files.
> I nd to know
>
> 1)  How the data is stored (e.g IEEE, floating pt+mantissa, 2s
complement
> etc)
> 2) What the byte storage is for various numeric ranges
>     e.g. S9(5)  COMP-0 takes 2 bytes,  how about 9(6), 9(8) COMP-0?
> 3) Is there anyway I can convert this data from outside COBOL?
Since I have
> all the records already exported from Btrieve to a flat file, I wd
like to
> be able to read them and decode the computational fields from (say)
Delphi.
>
> tia
>
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Comp Formats

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81gfi2$2gai$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<37A_3.7576$16.871133@news.flash.net> <sVH_3.1641$bH2.27380@typhoon01.swbell.net> <SAI_3.7940$16.912428@news.flash.net>`

```
For Micro Focus, the internal representation for all
data types is defined toward the beginning of the
Language Reference in a chapter titled, "Concepts
of the COBOL Language." If you have the LRM,
you have the answers.

One other note, when the MS directive is set, USAGE
COMP is equivalent to USAGE DISPLAY. This is
from the Microsoft V1.0 and V2.0 Syntax Support
I mentioned in my other post about COMP-0.
------------------
Rick Smith

Tony Tooley wrote in message ...
>I've tried the hex approach and was hoping for a shortcut!
>Forget comp-x for now - the other comps must be defined somewhere.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Comp Formats

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81gcf7$2egm$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<37A_3.7576$16.871133@news.flash.net> <sVH_3.1641$bH2.27380@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
Jerry, I had a good laugh from Tony's reply concerning
COMP-x; but I let you in on the joke.

In Micro Focus, COMP-X is defined as an unsigned
binary data item with the length determined by the
number of characters in the PICTURE clause. Hence,
COMP-X PIC X(4) is a four byte binary data item.

You might try COMP-? in the future.
------------------
Rick Smith

Jerry P wrote in message ...
>COMP-x is "implementer defined." There is no standard
>definition.
```

#### ↳ Re: Comp Formats

- **From:** "Terry Heinze" <terryheinze@prodigy.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81fnje$6ivo$1@newssvr03-int.news.prodigy.com>`

```

#### ↳ Re: Comp Formats

- **From:** "Rick Smith" <ricksmith@aiservices.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-23T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81fc89$1qdd$1@news.hitter.net>`
- **References:** `<37A_3.7576$16.871133@news.flash.net>`

```
Usage COMP-0, in Micro Focus, is covered under
Microsoft COBOL V1.0 and V2.0 Syntax Support.

I do not use COMP-0; but the Micro Focus LRM says:

"USAGE IS COMP-0 is allowed only with numeric
items. If an item is described with a PICTURE no
larger than S9(5), the effect is a data item whose
description is PIC S9(4) USAGE COMP; that is,
a 2-byte signed binary item. Otherwise, the effect
is a data item with the PICTURE clause actually
specified and with USAGE DISPLAY."

I located this statement by referring to the Master
Index.
------------------
Rick Smith

Tony Tooley wrote in message <37A_3.7576$16.871133@news.flash.net>...

>help files.
>I nd to know
[...]

>    e.g. S9(5)  COMP-0 takes 2 bytes,  how about 9(6), 9(8) COMP-0?
```

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<EUS_3.2877$bH2.56836@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
Yeah, but.

S9(4) COMP-0 implies a two-byte integer. We still
don't know whether the number "1" is (in hex)

A. 00 01    (0000 0000  0000 0001)
B. 00 10
C. 01 00
D. 10 00

Some hardware flips, spindles, and mutilates nibbles
(half-bytes) for its own purposes.

ASCII question, get and ANSI.

news:6wR_3.8135$16.939028@news.flash.net...

#### ↳ Re: Comp Formats

- **From:** Ed Guy <ed_guy@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383C2369.68EA@NOSPAMguysoftware.com>`
- **References:** `<37A_3.7576$16.871133@news.flash.net>`

```
Tony Tooley wrote:
> 
> Can anyone tell me about the various computational fields in COBOL.
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> be able to read them and decode the computational fields from (say) Delphi.

Various generic parsing/converting engines can read such files and export to fixed, 
delimited, dBase or mailmerge. Ours is ParseRat 
(http://www.guysoftware.com/parserat.htm), but is by no means the only one.
Unless you know the format (e.g. EBCDIC vs ASCII, big-endian vs little-endian, binary vs 
packed vs display etc.) you might have to click a few option buttons until you see data 
that makes sense.
```

<a id="t-8f91f26c66"></a>
## Thread: MVS data areas — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### MVS data areas

- **From:** "Jim" <jpprice@surfsouth.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<a5Z_3.1137$zQ4.81468@news2.giganews.com>`

```
Our group needs to access MVS Job Data Areas to ascertain the names (DSN) of
datasets assigned in JCL.  I've seen a sample of a program that accesses Job
name, programmer name, Step name, and MSGCLASS, but it doesn't reference the
DSN names.  Anyone know where I can find a map of the MVS Job Data Areas?

I've tried to search on "MVS Data Area" in the IBM BookManager, but to no
avail.
```

#### ↳ Re: MVS data areas

- **From:** Pete Wirfs <petwir@saif.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383C8AD8.F0@saif.com>`
- **References:** `<a5Z_3.1137$zQ4.81468@news2.giganews.com>`

```
Jim wrote:
> 
> Our group needs to access MVS Job Data Areas to ascertain the names (DSN) of
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jim Price


Here is an MVS/COBOL subroutine that if passed a DDname in the linkage
section (See: L01-COMM-AREA), it will return the DSN that is allocated
to that DDname.

       CBL NODYNAM,NOOPT,NOSSRANGE
       TITLE 'RETRIEVE JOB/STEP NAME PLUS DATASET INFO.'
       IDENTIFICATION DIVISION.

       PROGRAM-ID.  DPU020.
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *
      *  USING POINTERS, RETRIEVE OPERATING-SYSTEM INFORMATION AND
      *  RETURN IT TO THE CALLER.
      *
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
           EJECT
       ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       CONFIGURATION SECTION.

       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.

      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      * BEGINNING OF WORKING STORAGE
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.

      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      * W05-SUBSCRIPTS-POINTERS
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*

       01  FILLER PIC X(016) VALUE 'W05-SUBS-PNTRS**'.

       01  W05-SUBSCRIPTS-POINTERS.
           02  W05-TCB-ADDR-POINTER-X.
               03  W05-TCB-ADDR-POINTER       POINTER.

           02  W05-TIOT-SEG-POINTER           POINTER.
           02  W05-TIOT-BINARY  REDEFINES  W05-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
                                              PIC S9(09)  COMP.

           02  W05-JFCB-POINTER               POINTER.
           02  W05-JFCB-X       REDEFINES  W05-JFCB-POINTER.
               03  FILLER                     PIC X(01).
               03  W05-JFCB-LOWER-3           PIC X(03).
           EJECT
     
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      * W09-MISCELLANEOUS
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*

       01  FILLER PIC X(016) VALUE 'W09-MISC********'.

       01  W09-MISCELLANEOUS.
           02  W09-BINARY-2                PIC S9(04) COMP VALUE ZERO.
           02  FILLER  REDEFINES W09-BINARY-2.
               03  W09-BINARY-2L           PIC X(01).
               03  W09-BINARY-2R           PIC X(01).

           EJECT
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
      *  LINKAGE AREA
      *---------------------------------------------------------------*
       LINKAGE SECTION.

       01  L01-COMM-AREA.
           02  L01-JOB-NAME            PIC X(08).
           02  L01-STEP-NAME           PIC X(08).
           02  L01-DD-NAME             PIC X(08).
           02  L01-DS-NAME             PIC X(44).
           02  L01-GDG-OR-MEMBER       PIC X(08).
           02  L01-CATALOG-IND         PIC X(01).
               88  L01-FILE-IS-CATALOGED   VALUE 'Y'.
           02  L01-FILE-TYPE           PIC X(01).
               88  L01-FILE-IS-GDG         VALUE 'G'.
               88  L01-FILE-IS-JES         VALUE 'J'.
               88  L01-FILE-IS-PDS         VALUE 'P'.
               88  L01-FILE-IS-REGULAR     VALUE 'R'.
           02  L01-VOLUME-COUNT        PIC 9(01).
           02  L01-VOLUMES             PIC X(30).

       01  L02-TCB-POINTER                 POINTER.

       01  L03-TCB.
           02  FILLER                      PIC X(012).
           02  L03-TIOT-POINTER            POINTER.

       01  L04-TIOT.
           02  L04-JOB-NAME                PIC X(08).
           02  L04-STEP-NAME               PIC X(08).

       01  L04-TIOT-START                  PIC X(24).

       01  L04-TIOT-SEGMENT.
           02  L04-TIOT-LENGTH             PIC X(01).
           02  FILLER                      PIC X(03).
           02  L04-DD-NAME                 PIC X(08).
           02  L04-JFCB-ADDRESS            PIC X(03).

       01  L05-JFCB.
           02  FILLER                      PIC X(16).
           02  L05-DS-NAME                 PIC X(44).
           02  L05-GDG-OR-MEMBER           PIC X(08).
           02  L05-DMI-BYTE                PIC X(01).
           02  FILLER                      PIC X(33).
           02  L05-DSTYPE-BYTE             PIC X(01).
           02  FILLER                      PIC X(30).
           02  L05-VOLUME-COUNT-BYTE       PIC X(01).
           02  L05-VOLUME-LIST             PIC X(30).
           EJECT
       PROCEDURE DIVISION USING L01-COMM-AREA.

           MOVE X'0000021C' TO W05-TCB-ADDR-POINTER-X
           SET ADDRESS OF L02-TCB-POINTER  TO W05-TCB-ADDR-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF L03-TCB          TO L02-TCB-POINTER.
           SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT         TO L03-TIOT-POINTER.

           MOVE L04-JOB-NAME               TO L01-JOB-NAME.
           MOVE L04-STEP-NAME              TO L01-STEP-NAME.
           INITIALIZE L01-DS-NAME   L01-GDG-OR-MEMBER  L01-CATALOG-IND
                      L01-FILE-TYPE L01-VOLUME-COUNT   L01-VOLUMES.

      ****  IF THEY DID NOT SUPPLY A DD NAME, EXIT STAGE LEFT.

           IF L01-DD-NAME < 'A'
              GOBACK
           END-IF.

           MOVE LOW-VALUES TO W05-JFCB-X.
           SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT-START   TO L03-TIOT-POINTER.
           SET W05-TIOT-SEG-POINTER        TO L03-TIOT-POINTER.
           ADD 24                          TO W05-TIOT-BINARY.
           SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT-SEGMENT TO W05-TIOT-SEG-POINTER.

      ****  LOOP THROUGH ALL POINTERS TO THE DD NAMES.
      ****  WHEN WE GET A MATCH, MOVE IN ALL THE FILE DATA AND EXIT.

           PERFORM UNTIL L04-TIOT-LENGTH = LOW-VALUES
              MOVE L04-JFCB-ADDRESS           TO W05-JFCB-LOWER-3
              SET ADDRESS OF L05-JFCB         TO W05-JFCB-POINTER
              IF L04-DD-NAME = L01-DD-NAME
                 MOVE L04-DD-NAME             TO L01-DD-NAME
                 MOVE L05-DS-NAME             TO L01-DS-NAME
                 MOVE L05-GDG-OR-MEMBER       TO L01-GDG-OR-MEMBER
                 IF L05-DMI-BYTE > X'79' AND < X'90'
                    MOVE 'Y'                  TO L01-CATALOG-IND
                 ELSE
                    MOVE 'N'                  TO L01-CATALOG-IND
                 END-IF
                 IF L05-DMI-BYTE > X'19' AND < X'30'
                    MOVE 'J'                  TO L01-FILE-TYPE
                 ELSE
                    IF L05-DSTYPE-BYTE = X'00'
                       MOVE 'R'               TO L01-FILE-TYPE
                    ELSE
                       IF L05-DSTYPE-BYTE = X'01'
                          MOVE 'P'            TO L01-FILE-TYPE
                       ELSE
                          IF L05-DSTYPE-BYTE = X'02'
                             MOVE 'G'         TO L01-FILE-TYPE
                          ELSE
                             MOVE '?'         TO L01-FILE-TYPE
                          END-IF
                       END-IF
                    END-IF
                 END-IF
                 MOVE L05-VOLUME-COUNT-BYTE   TO W09-BINARY-2R
                 MOVE W09-BINARY-2            TO L01-VOLUME-COUNT
                 MOVE L05-VOLUME-LIST         TO L01-VOLUMES
                 GOBACK
              ELSE
                 ADD 20                    TO W05-TIOT-BINARY
                 SET ADDRESS OF L04-TIOT-SEGMENT
                                           TO W05-TIOT-SEG-POINTER
              END-IF
           END-PERFORM.

           GOBACK.
      *------------ END OF SUBROUTINE DPU020 -------------------------*

```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MVS data areas

- **From:** "Jim" <jpprice@surfsouth.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00

Thanks, Pete, for the code.  I want to use the code to use any of the SELECT

program to do what you want.  More importantly, it gives you the IBM
manual (in the top comments) where you can find out about the control
blocks.

If you actually want the DCB (or ACB) layouts, then you should be
checking the file-handling manuals.

## Thread: How BIG is a mainframe? — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383BF349.ED910724@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com>`

```
dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)

>First, PC programmers are terrible.  Everything is "good enough".
 
It really doesn't make sense to do much more.  PCs are working in a
dynamic environment which includes lots of programming type functions. 
You make calls to the operating system's methods and depend on the
operating system to be able to do complex GUI type actions for you.

It's sort of like significant digits in science.  If your precision is
higher than the precision of the rest of the numbers, it is being
wasted.

So you plug into the operating system and hope that you can rewrite
cheaply when the operating system changes to make your application
obsolete.
```

#### ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81jdb3$er8$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383BF349.ED910724@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
It is fairly simple to make a PC (even under windows) stable enough for
anything you like.  Firsts of all, you use a separate computer for code and
data, set up as a server.  Secondly, you never allow an amateur near the
console of that computer.  Then you allow the users to use their own
computers as relatively dumb terminals.

If people allowed amateurs to install software and play games on the console
of a mainframe, it would be as flakey as a micro.

The micro network that I am currently replacing has been up for nine years.
My last service call was five years ago. It runs 24/7.


Howard Brazee wrote in message <383BF349.ED910724@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>dbryant@netcom.com (David K. Bryant)
>
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
>obsolete.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81juf7$gpt$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383BF349.ED910724@NOSPAMhome.com> <81jdb3$er8$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees <donald@willmack.com> wrote:
: It is fairly simple to make a PC (even under windows) stable enough for
: anything you like.  Firsts of all, you use a separate computer for code and
: data, set up as a server.  Secondly, you never allow an amateur near the
: console of that computer.  Then you allow the users to use their own
: computers as relatively dumb terminals.

: If people allowed amateurs to install software and play games on the console
: of a mainframe, it would be as flakey as a micro.

We did have some simple games that ran on the console some
decades ago.  They never brought the machine down.

: The micro network that I am currently replacing has been up for nine years.
: My last service call was five years ago. It runs 24/7.

If it was 9 years ago then the server won't be Windows.  If you
are currently replacing it with NT/2000 then expect a great
increase in instability, and a requirement for regular reboots.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81luup$4et$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383BF349.ED910724@NOSPAMhome.com> <81jdb3$er8$1@news.igs.net> <383DECB1.11060F11@att.net>`

them, wrapping them, and shipping them somewhere.  I move 15 million tonnes
of rock a year.  I cannot go on site without full safety equipment.

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383DEDF6.7522DD8D@att.net>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383BF349.ED910724@NOSPAMhome.com> <81jdb3$er8$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:
> 
> It is fairly simple to make a PC (even under windows) stable enough for
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> computers as relatively dumb terminals.

With all due respect, I don't think so. It would probably be a lot more
stable, but not up to mainframe standards. MVS has nothing I'm aware of
like Windows' Blue Screen of Death. Windows is nowhere as robust as MVS
(not yet, anyway), and I don't think Billy the G is motivated in that
direction. Windows is still trying to support too many legacy devices.

Bill Lynch
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81m2f3$687$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383BF349.ED910724@NOSPAMhome.com> <81jdb3$er8$1@news.igs.net> <383DEDF6.7522DD8D@att.net>`

```
Bill, I am going to take issue with this one.  Before starting to scrap,
though, I will say that I agree the mainframe is a totally different beast,
and used for totally different things.  A PC CANNOT replace it, and I do not
agree that you can do the same thing as a mainframe with a bunch of micros.
The two are a completely different thing.  "Distributed processing" is a
buzzword that you and I  both take with the grain of salt it deserves.

However.  Stability (not size, not number of transactions) is another
matter.

I have a server beside me, used exclusively for software development and
testing.  It has not so much as hiccuped in five months. It was reset five
months ago because we had a power outage. It is running Win98 on a plain 233
PC with 64 megs.  It cost $1200 bucks, and if I wanted to put a $800 power
backup on it, I could let it sit for years.

What you say about legacy devices is true.  However, there is nothing that
requires you to install such devices.  The stability of a PC goes way down
as you load up various crap.  Just do not do it. This peer-to-peer stuff is
not suitable for anything beyond casual network use. It is designed for
email and small device sharing.  That is not the job of a file server.

If, on the other hand, you take a dedicated PC, load the OS, equip it with a
quality disk drive and a quality Ethernet card, it is not going to
spontaneously change.  It is going to sit there and do disk IO as long as it
has decent power.  If you want to do something else, put another PC on the
Ethernet bus, and do it.  We even do backup on a station. And it is still
*way* cheaper than getting a mainframe.  The load cannot justify a
mainframe. (I am only talking a hundred transactions per minute).

A large reason for the stability of the mainframe is that it is designed to
be used exactly this way, and nobody even tries to do otherwise. Take your
MVS system, and put a GUI on the operator's console. Remove all software,
and power it down.  Then give a twenty page manual to someone that just
failed out of an accounting course at the local college, and tell them to
install/setup/manage the mainframe. Give them no programmers, and a budget
of $1000 do get all the software they need.  Allow them to load up as many
games as they can collect on the internet into the OS area.  Do you
seriously think you will get a solid system?  I would bet money that they
could not even get the system to say "hello world".

That is how 99% of all PC's are set up.  It is a small miracle that they run
at all.  Expecting stability ... well ...

William Lynch wrote in message <383DEDF6.7522DD8D@att.net>...
>donald tees wrote:
>>
>> It is fairly simple to make a PC (even under windows) stable enough for
>> anything you like.  Firsts of all, you use a separate computer for code
and
>> data, set up as a server.  Secondly, you never allow an amateur near the
>> console of that computer.  Then you allow the users to use their own
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>Bill Lynch
```

### DDE & MicroFocus

- **From:** "PDMars" <pdmars@yahoo.com>

```
I need to use a VB program to communicate with a MicroFocus program using
DDE.
Any sample code would be greatly appreciated.

Thanks
Paul
```

#### ↳ Re: DDE & MicroFocus

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81jrpf$1kc$1@neptunium.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<81i4sg$t81$1@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

>I need to use a VB program to communicate with a MicroFocus program using
>DDE.
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I dont think that Micro Focus products support DDE,
as I have never used it.

You could use OLE instead though this I have used.

Simon R Hart.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: DDE & MicroFocus

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81juob$gpt$3@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<81i4sg$t81$1@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <81jrpf$1kc$1@neptunium.btinternet.com>`

```
Simon R Hart <hart1@technologist.com> wrote:

: I dont think that Micro Focus products support DDE,
: as I have never used it.

DDE is in the Windows API.  MF can call the API.  Thus MF can
use DDE when in a Windows environment.

In fact I have used DDE in MF Cobol, but the code is not
available for posting, and not suitable either being in a
production system rather than a simple demo.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: DDE & MicroFocus

- **From:** "Simon R Hart" <hart1@technologist.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81m5mv$4as$1@uranium.btinternet.com>`
- **References:** `<81i4sg$t81$1@fir.prod.itd.earthlink.net> <81jrpf$1kc$1@neptunium.btinternet.com> <81juob$gpt$3@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`

```

Richard Plinston wrote in message <81juob$gpt$3@aklobs.kc.net.nz>...
>Simon R Hart <hart1@technologist.com> wrote:
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>

I would hate to think of the code to use DDE under the Win32s
API module.
Much like using Win32s API for ODBC.
It is a total nightmare, more like ASM than a 4gl.
MicroFocus do *not* support DDE in any form. Thats *not* to say
you cant access DDE from the Win32s API's within Cobol.  But I would love
to see a workable commercial Win32s API DDE application up and running.
The fact is I dont think any exists, it would be ten times easier to use
OLE.


Simon R Hart.
```

<a id="t-7b1b13c1d1"></a>
## Thread: Index corruption in Microfocus — 9 this month / 12 total

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** George Roldan <71075.2340@CompuServe.COM>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
Hi,

   We're using microfocus indexed sequential files and one of 
them has become corrupted. The error we get is that the index is 
corrupted. We've found that parts of the file can be read with 
the index but not others. I had hoped to drop the index (by 
erasing the idx file) and rebuild it, on the suspicion that the 
data in the dat file is okay. However the utilities seems not to 
be able to read the file at all once the index is erased. 
   We'd like to avoid going to backups that are days old. Any 
suggestions?
Thanks,
Neil
```

#### ↳ Re: Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GX__3.384$Di3.6645@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
George Roldan wrote:
>   We're using microfocus indexed sequential files and one of
>them has become corrupted. The error we get is that the index is
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>suggestions?

Sure, don't remove the IDX file.  Rename both files, but use the same
suffixes.  Then run Rebuild to rebuild the renamed file to another
file with the old name.  You don't say which version of MF COBOL you
have, but with the 3.2 DOS version the command would be (assuming a
.DAT extension):

    REBUILD oldname.DAT,newname.DAT [ options ]
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81ick7$qha$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <GX__3.384$Di3.6645@news2.mia>`

```
Well, the index *is* erased. Rename the .DAT file.
Make a tiny program. Take the .DAT file as sequential input and your normal
indexed file structure as output. You might have to add 'recording mode is
V' in the FD statement for the input file.

After this little operation you should follow Judson's advice carefully.
Regards
Ib

Judson McClendon skrev i meddelelsen ...
>George Roldan wrote:
>>   We're using microfocus indexed sequential files and one of
…[23 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** "PDMars" <pdmars@yahoo.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81jrls$6h8$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
George:

I've been able to rebuild files with corrupted indices by using the
MicroFocus "REBUILD" program.  It can rebuild the index, verify it, etc.  It
can even (if you choose the option of /d) rewrite the physical data file
build a new index.  That's helpful if there is a bad record in the real data
file (bad RDW, etc).

Rebuild should be part of your MF installation.

Paul DeMarsh

George Roldan <71075.2340@CompuServe.COM> wrote in message
<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>...
>Hi,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Neil
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81kj93$9fr$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <81jrls$6h8$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>`

```
That's it. In fact, you can create an index from any  sequential file. The
use of all the options in the rebuild program can be overwelming, but you
proved it: When everything fails - read the manual - congratulations.

Regards
Ib

PDMars skrev i meddelelsen <81jrls$6h8$1@holly.prod.itd.earthlink.net>...
>George:
>
>I've been able to rebuild files with corrupted indices by using the
>MicroFocus "REBUILD" program.  It can rebuild the index, verify it, etc.
It
>can even (if you choose the option of /d) rewrite the physical data file
>build a new index.  That's helpful if there is a bad record in the real
data
>file (bad RDW, etc).
>
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** SBrueggemann@gmx.net (Sven Brueggemann)
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383d7baf.852742854@news.btx.dtag.de>`
- **References:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>`

```
George Roldan <71075.2340@CompuServe.COM> schrieb:

>   We're using microfocus indexed sequential files and one of 
>them has become corrupted. The error we get is that the index is 
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>suggestions?

By deleting the idx file you also deleted information about the file's
key structure. You have to either rebuild the file providing the key
information on the command line or - if you don't know the structure -
do a little trick:

Copy the dat-file to a temporary directory. Restore the old idx file
from your old backup. Do a rebuild [filename] on the file. If rebuild
gives no further error messages, you can copy dat and idx-file to your
original data directory.

Best regards


Sven
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81kj18$94t$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <383d7baf.852742854@news.btx.dtag.de>`

```
That is correct. the index doesn't have to fit the .Dat file. The .IDX
file's file header contains all the information needed for a Rebuild.

Regards
Ib

Sven Brueggemann skrev i meddelelsen
<383d7baf.852742854@news.btx.dtag.de>...

There are two ways to rebuild ... one just rebuilding the index by the
command "rebuild name" and the other a complete rebuild using "rebuild
infile outfile".  Usually, if the first will not work, the second will.

George Roldan <71075.2340@CompuServe.COM> wrote in message
<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com>...
>Hi,
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>Neil
```

```
>   We're using microfocus indexed sequential files and one of
>them has become corrupted. The error we get is that the index is
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>be able to read the file at all once the index is erased.


If you are on HPUX, AIX, SCO or NT/WINDOWS go to www.csisoft.com
and download ISAMATION for MicroFocus COBOL.  It is faster than rebuild,
easyier to use and will recover any corruption.


Glen Johnson
```

<a id="t-f9facb9101"></a>
## Thread: MF Cobol - Deleted Records — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### MF Cobol - Deleted Records

- **From:** Joan Thompson <Joant@csionline.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383B4680.784498F2@csionline.net>`

```
I'm using DataJunction to export MF Cobol data files to Fixed ASCII.
How do I determine which records are deleted in the source (MF Cobol)
files so I can skip exporting them?
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Cobol - Deleted Records

- **From:** "Glen Johnson" <glen@csisoft.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<hcT%3.2233$SO.115257@typhoon2.austin.rr.com>`
- **References:** `<383B4680.784498F2@csionline.net>`

```

Joan Thompson wrote in message <383B4680.784498F2@csionline.net>...
>I'm using DataJunction to export MF Cobol data files to Fixed ASCII.
>How do I determine which records are deleted in the source (MF Cobol)
>files so I can skip exporting them?
>


In I0 formated files, the record will be zeroed out.  In compressed formats,
the 0x20 bit of the first byte indicates the record is free.  For
uncompressed
I3 and I4 formats the first byte being 0x40 means used and 0x20 free.

                                                                        If I
remember right
                                                                        Glen
Johnson
```

---

<a id="t-0f03f74512"></a>
## Thread: centering a pic x field — 6 msgs

### Re: centering a pic x field

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s83bceab.099@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
It is possible that shine98 doesn't quite understand the 'jargon' :)  So to clarify if that is the case:

When refering to an alphanumeric field regardless of the length programmers usually say the field is PIC X.  When refering to a numeric field regardless of the length programmers usually say PIC 9.  Of corse some will say the field is DISPLAY which (at least to me - a mainframer) means the field is PIC X also, or a group name which by definition defaults to PIC X even if the data stored in the sub fields below it are PIC 9 .  A packed field is always PIC 9 Comp-3, and a binary field is always PIC 9 COMP (again at least to me and my collegues).

An example:
Employee Name could be say 25 positions long - in your program PIC X(25).  When talking about the field one would just say is is a PIC X or DISPLAY field.

Social Security Number (assuming it is not stored with hyphens) is in your program PIC 9(9) but one would say it is a PIC 9 field.  One could also say it was a packed field if it was defined as PIC 9(9) COMP-3.

Webmaster is trying to center an Employee Name type field (more than 1 position)  that is usually stored left justified by reason of its PIC.  I've seen some interesting answers to that question.

>>> webmaster 11/17 4:30 am >>>
 
I do not know what is this ? How does it work ?

thanks

shine98@my-deja.com wrote:

> It seems to me that with no bytes left and no bytes right, a "pic x"
> field would always be centered :)
…[22 more quoted lines elided]…
> Before you buy.




 _____________________________________________________________
 Deja.com: Before you buy.
 http://www.deja.com/
 * To modify or remove your subscription, go to
 http://www.deja.com/edit_sub.xp?group=comp.lang.cobol
 * Read this thread at
 http://www.deja.com/thread/%3C383275B6.66368B4%40eii.fr%3E



 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: centering a pic x field

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383C3355.F3623CC5@NOSPAMhome.com>`

Eileen Preston wrote:

> Social Security Number (assuming it is not stored with hyphens) is in your program PIC > 9(9) but one would say it is a PIC 9 field.  One could also say it was a packed field > if it was defined as PIC 9(9) COMP-3.

I don't define social security numbers PIC 9(9), I don't plan on doing
any arithmetic with it, so I define it PIC X(9).  I can check to see if
it is numeric easily enough, it will always be justified left, I can do
alpha compares just as easily as numeric compares.  The only thing I
can't do is abort with a data exception.
```

so it's easy to differentiate a true SSN from a TIN.

Bill Lynch

<a id="t-01aa3f0e3d"></a>
## Thread: redefining — 6 msgs

### redefining

- **From:** "STERN FAN" <p.limitone@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<GqT_3.267$lT.6467@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
hello group,

i have a question about redefine statements.  i know you can redefine
something once, but can you call the same thing two different names for use
in a program?

ie: 02    hours-in    pic 99.
     02   hours redefines hours-in    pic 99.
     02   more-hours redefines hours-in   pic 99.

sorry if you think this is a dumb post, but i am a student and want to have
this question answered before i get back to school on monday.  i need to
know for a program i am writing.

thank you

phil
```

#### ↳ Re: redefining

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81hcsn$9b7$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<GqT_3.267$lT.6467@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
Yes you can.  You can redefine it as many times as you wish ... in fact that
is the exact mechanism that I always use for setting up various print line
formats.  For example:

01 print-line                    picture x(132).
01 dumy-line-one    redefines print-line.
    02 column1-5            picture x(5).
    02 filler                       picture x.
    02 column7-8            picture 99.
            etc.
 01 dumy-line-two redefines print-line.
    02 filler                       picture xx.
    02 column3-10          picture x(8).
            etc.
 01 dumy-line-three redefines print-line.
    02 etc.

The advantage is that you can then use a standardized print routine as
follows.

        move data to column1-5.
        perform print-routine.


print-routine.
        if line-count is greater than page-limit
            perform new-page.
        move print-line to print-record.
        write print-record.
        move space to line-for-print.

One thing that you should note: some compilers require that each redefines
reference the original name, while others require that you reference the
previous.  I do not know what the standard says, perhaps Bill can tell us.
However, I have used compilers that would crap out on the above, demanding
that dumy-line-two should redefine dumy-line-one rather than the original
print-line.

STERN FAN wrote in message ...
>hello group,
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: redefining

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383C3B1D.B948B00@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<GqT_3.267$lT.6467@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net> <81hcsn$9b7$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:

> One thing that you should note: some compilers require that each redefines
> reference the original name, while others require that you reference the
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> print-line.

Also, some compilers don't want you to redefine 01 levels, and/or only
allow 01 levels redefined in WORKING-STORAGE.  But these limits are easy
to adjust around.
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: redefining

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<9w__3.1027$Wo6.15844@typhoon01.swbell.net>`

```
Plus some (all?) compilers complain when the redefining object
is longer than the redefined one,

02  ITEM-1                    PIC X(100).
02  ITEM-2 REDEFINES ITEM-1 PIC X(200).

or has an occurs depending on,
yak-yak-yak.

We're really telling you more than you want to know.


Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com> wrote in message
news:383C3B1D.B948B00@NOSPAMhome.com...
> donald tees wrote:
>
> > One thing that you should note: some compilers require that each
redefines
> > reference the original name, while others require that you
reference the
> > previous.  I do not know what the standard says, perhaps Bill can
tell us.
> > However, I have used compilers that would crap out on the above,
demanding
> > that dumy-line-two should redefine dumy-line-one rather than the
original
> > print-line.
>
> Also, some compilers don't want you to redefine 01 levels, and/or
only
> allow 01 levels redefined in WORKING-STORAGE.  But these limits are
easy
> to adjust around.
```

#### ↳ Re: redefining

- **From:** Fred Zinner <technik@aktiv.co.at>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383C09FA.E6E864ED@aktiv.co.at>`
- **References:** `<GqT_3.267$lT.6467@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
STERN FAN wrote:
> 
> hello group,
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
> phil
this makes no sence...
but maybe this

02 act-time pic 999999.

02 act-time-special redefines act-time.
  05 act-hours pic 99.
  05 act-minutes pic 99.
  05 act-secundes pic99.

hope it helps
cu
fred
```

#### ↳ Re: redefining

- **From:** "The Programmer" <someone@somewhere.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-24T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol,alt.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<RHU_3.50281$Hk.457169@news1.mia>`
- **References:** `<GqT_3.267$lT.6467@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`

```
As far as COBOL is concerned, you can redefine any data item (elemental or group) as many
times as you want. There may be some practical considerations, of course... like
attempting to redefine something ten thousand times. Mind you, in some MSA programs I saw
items redefined up to 300 times. Talk about difficult to follow logic... :o

Though not practical, or useful, the example you quoted with the three redefinitions is
therefore, perfectly valid. In fact, depending on the compiler, you can have HOURS
redefine HOURS-IN, and then have MORE-HOURS redefine HOURS. Assuming that "HOURS" isn't a
reserved word, of course... I don't think it is.

The COBOL REDEFINES clause is just the ORG instruction with an address.
```

## Thread: FILESEARCHING ?? — 16 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### FILESEARCHING ??

- **From:** "Christer" <christer.wikstroem@swipnet.se>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<yH9%3.4384$kb.9680@nntpserver.swip.net>`

```
If I have a file named Telephonenumbers.dat with the following content:


James    Doe         666-666666
Joe        Smith       555-555555

And so on...

How do I search for a persons last name (or first) and if it does excist in
the file displays the whole line to the operator ?? I hope my english make
sense and that someone may help me.

Thanx,

christer.wikstroem@swipnet.se
```

#### ↳ Re: FILESEARCHING ??

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81jgrh$glm$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<yH9%3.4384$kb.9680@nntpserver.swip.net>`

```
The way that I handle names/name lookup is to always have a separate index
for the purpose, and then include that in the data entry. There are really
no algorithms to handle it in a completely standard way, due to differences
in human language conventions.

You can easily devise a method to create that index that will handle 95% of
all names, but stuff like "ABC, a Division of 1234 Ontario" is always going
to require hand tweaking to get it to fit in where you want it.

Christer wrote in message ...
>If I have a file named Telephonenumbers.dat with the following content:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FILESEARCHING ??

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3842CCD7.D7BF4B22@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<yH9%3.4384$kb.9680@nntpserver.swip.net> <81jgrh$glm$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Oh, a name searching routine almost always needs some phonetic look up. 
The most ubiquitous of these is Soundex, but it has its limitations. 
But it is easy and quick.
```

```

…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Where can I find a reference on Soundex?  I remember having worked on

I wish that I had that code available for reference.
Any assistance in locating a good example or discussion with the

```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: FILESEARCHING ??

_(thread depth: 4)_

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>
- **Date:** 1999-11-30T00:00:00

#### ↳ Re: FILESEARCHING ??

- **From:** "michael vasmatics" <vasmati@snet.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81lb8d$t7r@news1.snet.net>`
- **References:** `<yH9%3.4384$kb.9680@nntpserver.swip.net>`

```
Why can't you describe the record and just do a compare for what your
looking for, please note I'm a student myself.
lets say you describe a record as such;

FD CUSTOMER.
01 CUSTOMER-RECORD.
        05 FIRST-NAME                PIC X( 20).
        05 LAST-NAME                 PIC X (20).
        05 PHONE-NUMBER        PIC X(10).

WORKIN-STORAGE SECTION.
01 EOF-FLAG                            PIC X(3) VALUE 'NO-'.

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
100-MAIN.
        200-OPEN-FILE
        READ TELEPHONENUMBERS-FILE
                AT END MOVE 'YES' TO EOF-FLAG
        PERFORM 300-FIND-NAME UNTIL EOF-FLAG = 'YES'
        400-CLOSE-FILES
        STOP RUN.
200-OPEN-FILE.
        OPEN TELEPHONENUMBER-FILE.
300-FIND-NAME.
        IF
            FIRST-NAME = "JAMES                     "         (NOTE THE
SPACES TO PICK UP THE FIELD LENGHT)
            OR LAST-NAME = "DOE                   "
             DISPLAY COSTUMER-RECORD
        END-IF
        READ TELEPHONENUMBERS-FILE
                AT END MOVE 'YES' TO EOF-FLAG.
400-CLOSE-FILE.
        CLOSE TELEPHONENUMBER-FILE.


Just wondering if something along these lines wouldnt work.
Keep in mind that if the assignment is to do a table search then this thing
is al wrong.
For tables, you need to create it and then use the SEARCH word to find the
data.







> If I have a file named Telephonenumbers.dat with the following content:
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> How do I search for a persons last name (or first) and if it does excist
in
> the file displays the whole line to the operator ?? I hope my english make
> sense and that someone may help me.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FILESEARCHING ??

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81miq1$g51$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<yH9%3.4384$kb.9680@nntpserver.swip.net> <81lb8d$t7r@news1.snet.net>`

```
You are making an assumption on the file layout that first name and second
name are fixed length fields.  That is not usually true.  A description
like:

01 CUSTOMER-RECORD.
        05 name                              PIC X( 40).
        05 PHONE-NUMBER        PIC X(10).

is much more common.  There is also the problem of time.  Very few telephone
books have only two names.  Estimate how long it would take to look up a
number in the average telephone book if you use the method "start at a, and
read every name until you come to it".  I do not think I would give you much
of a mark if that is the best you can come up with.



michael vasmatics wrote in message <81lb8d$t7r@news1.snet.net>...
>Why can't you describe the record and just do a compare for what your
>looking for, please note I'm a student myself.
…[56 more quoted lines elided]…

make
>> sense and that someone may help me.
>>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FILESEARCHING ??

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<309EAD8F6E8A61F6.707AB2E7EDDA03E5.FB37288A1D84527A@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<yH9%3.4384$kb.9680@nntpserver.swip.net> <81lb8d$t7r@news1.snet.net>`

```
On Fri, 26 Nov 1999 02:08:54 -0800, "michael vasmatics"
<vasmati@snet.net> enlightened us:

>Why can't you describe the record and just do a compare for what your
>looking for, please note I'm a student myself.
…[66 more quoted lines elided]…
>

Not a bad solution if you only have say a 100 records.  But what if
your file has 100,000 records and the user requests one lookup then
another, then another....  How long will it take to read through the
file sequentially every time until you get a match?

A much better solution is to use an indexed file.  Your text book
should have information on it.

Regards,


          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Living on Earth is expensive, but it does include a free trip around the sun.



Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

One usually, or preferably, has to also take into account multi-user
access of files.  Loading up into memory or creating a sorted
version of a file is less than useful when several users may be
accessing the file.

```
Hi Christer,
You will need to convert your data into an index sequential file. In the
select statement you will choose a record key and 1 or 2 Alternate keys.
Reading is done with at start statement followed by a read next.

The start statement can refer to your record key or the alternate key (your
choice).

Good luck
Ib

Christer skrev i meddelelsen ...
>If I have a file named Telephonenumbers.dat with the following content:
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: FILESEARCHING ??

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3842C8C3.7D6A97C9@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<yH9%3.4384$kb.9680@nntpserver.swip.net>`

```
It looks as though you don't know which column the first letter of the
last name starts in.  If you knew for sure that it was the second word
on the line, an unstring will work, but the odds are you need to do some
data cleansing.

Names and addresses can have very weird formats.  Unless you tightly
control the format you get them in, cleaning them up is a tough task. 
While there are some popular commercial programs out there, a company I
worked for spent lots of money developing a trade secret method of doing
a better job of this.

Can you force your data into a fixed format, or will you need to figure
out name formats?

Howard E. Howard

Christer wrote:
> 
> If I have a file named Telephonenumbers.dat with the following content:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> christer.wikstroem@swipnet.se
```

#### ↳ Re: FILESEARCHING ??

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3842C973.1BC02B6@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<yH9%3.4384$kb.9680@nntpserver.swip.net>`

```
It looks as though you don't know which column the first letter of the
last name starts in.  If you knew for sure that it was the second word
on the line, an unstring will work, but the odds are you need to do some
data cleansing.

Names and addresses can have very weird formats.  Unless you tightly
control the format you get them in, cleaning them up is a tough task. 
While there are some popular commercial programs out there, a company I
worked for spent lots of money developing a trade secret method of doing
a better job of this.

Can you force your data into a fixed format, or will you need to figure
out name formats?

How do you read the following?
Howard E. Howard   
Frank O'Haris        (you know that Irish football player)
Peters, John       
John Jacob Jinglheimer Smith 
JJ Junior
M. James Smith III
Margarita Maria Lopez y Gonzales


Christer wrote:
> 
> If I have a file named Telephonenumbers.dat with the following content:
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…

##### ↳ ↳ Re: FILESEARCHING ??

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81utq8$l78$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<yH9%3.4384$kb.9680@nntpserver.swip.net> <3842C973.1BC02B6@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Reding all the different views upon this matter I remember a system I made
many years ago. We had discussions with elements similar to many of the
elements in this thread. The solution was a standard for entering names and
a standard for displaying of names.

We didn't split the name into different fields but whenever we stored a
name, it was in the format: <Last-name', 'other-names>.  When we displayed a
name, we turned the format around (the comma and space told us where to find
the name(s) to put in front)

In that standard, your name-examples would be our 'display format' and the
names we stored, would look like this:

Howard, Howard E.
O'Haris, Frank
Peters, John            **** Contains comma - stored asis (displayed correct
ever after)
Smith,  John Jacob Jinglheimer
Junior, JJ
III. M. James Smith
Gonzales, Margarita Maria Lopez y

Except for 'M. James Smith III'  the store format is fine for searching and
handling that exception is up to the designer. If the users know the
difference between the second and third Smith you can leave it, but if it is
normal to search for Smith, you must add logic to handle roman numeral, JR
and all that stuff that primarily is a part of US naming conventions.

Another thing we did was to convert the stored name to upper case and put
the result to another name-field, and the name index was built upon that
field. Every search field entered was converted to upper case before the
search.

Perhaps the ideas can be useful.

Regards
Ib
Howard Brazee skrev i meddelelsen <3842C973.1BC02B6@NOSPAMhome.com>...
>It looks as though you don't know which column the first letter of the
>last name starts in.  If you knew for sure that it was the second word
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…

in
>> the file displays the whole line to the operator ?? I hope my english
make
>> sense and that someone may help me.
>>
>> Thanx,
>>
>> christer.wikstroem@swipnet.se
```

## Thread: cobol and informix — 5 msgs

### cobol and informix

- **From:** "Lars Niemann" <Lars.Niemann@Buerodorf.de>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81iorq$kg7$1@news04.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Hello group,

sorry, my english is verry bad, but i have a problem and i hope you can help
me.
I'm a "little" cobol-programmer on unix (rm600) and i want to connect a
cobol program with a informix-table. I haven't with informix in a cobol
program. How can i included my sql-statement and the table-infos in my cobol
program. How can i learn to work with cobol and my informix database.

sorry for my english, but i hope you can help me....;-)))
thanks :-) Lars
```

#### ↳ Re: cobol and informix

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81kgkm$5nm$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<81iorq$kg7$1@news04.btx.dtag.de>`

```
Hi Lars,
We need to know what compiler you are using?
Did you try to get an Informix pre-compiler?

You see, when you put an Exec Sql statement into your cobol program it is
normal that the DB vendor gives you a 'translator'. That means that your
pretty Exec Sql.....End Exec is expanded to some pure cobol statements that
actually reads the row.

Some compilers has 'built in' preprocessors. In that case, you will never
see the precompiled code. When you debug you will only se the code you put
into the program.

If you tell us what compiler you are using, you can be sure that someone has
tried this before, and he/she will help you immediately.

Let's try to solve your problem,

Regards
Ib

cobol

You are right, Sample programs are a fine way to get started.
If you want to access Informix, you need the Informix ESQL precompiler.

I've looked around for samples, and you can find some at
http://www.merant.com/ads/support/indexf.asp

Have fun while you are testing the samples, and change them to your own
flavour, but save the programs step by step. The learning process is to
create small successes for yourself. When you save the steps, it is easy to
fall back to the last reliable version.

Have fun
Ib

<a id="t-794622cb61"></a>
## Thread: Index corruption in Microfocus — 5 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81jdmn$f4a$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <vh7p3sob1piae3kdd79vid47ib6k58n8g0@4ax.com>`

```
No.  That will not work.  A sequential read requires that records use <CR
LF> as record terminators.  A relative file description may allow access,
but you will get a great deal of garbage along with the data.

You can always read a data file by defining it as a one byte record, and you
can do the same with the data portion of and Isam file, but you are then
going to have to filter out the deleted records, pointers, delimiters, etc.
that are used internally.  To do that, you need a fair idea of the way the
Isam data is physically stored.

The rebuild facility is by far the easier solution.

G Moore wrote in message ...
>On 24 Nov 1999 23:07:18 GMT, George Roldan <71075.2340@CompuServe.COM>
>wrote:
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

#### ↳ Re: Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81kion$8oj$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <vh7p3sob1piae3kdd79vid47ib6k58n8g0@4ax.com> <81jdmn$f4a$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Please............
A *LINE* sequential record is the kind of sequential record that needs
CR/LF. The .DAT part of an indexed file *CAN* be considered as a sequential
file. It's just that it has a length field in front of the record. That is
the reason for 'Recording mode is V' - try it, I've done so from time to
time.

Regards
Ib

donald tees skrev i meddelelsen <81jdmn$f4a$1@news.igs.net>...
>No.  That will not work.  A sequential read requires that records use <CR
>LF> as record terminators.  A relative file description may allow access,
>but you will get a great deal of garbage along with the data.
>
>You can always read a data file by defining it as a one byte record, and
you
>can do the same with the data portion of and Isam file, but you are then
>going to have to filter out the deleted records, pointers, delimiters, etc.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81m3tg$7gj$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <vh7p3sob1piae3kdd79vid47ib6k58n8g0@4ax.com> <81jdmn$f4a$1@news.igs.net> <81kion$8oj$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```
That depends entirely on the compiler.  Yes, a line sequential file is the
standard <CR LF> format.  However, assuming that an ordinary sequential file
and an Isam data file are compatible is a huge assumption, and will be wrong
99% of the time.

Many Isams are linked lists.  As well as possible internal pointer data,
there is also deleted data to account for.  There may be index blocks
intermingled with the data blocks.  There may, or may not be record
delimiters. The actual data blocks may be variable lengths.  About the only
way that you can be assured of reading it sequentially and accurately is one
byte at a time, analysing the data as you go.

If you happen to be on a specific platform, and you happen to have a
specific Isam from a specific vendor, and you specifically know the
internals of that Isam method, and you know the sequential definition for
the Cobol you are trying to do the recovery with matches, then sure.  Write
a program and read it sequentially.  In a situation where I knew no
specifics, and I did know there was an Isam recovery program, I still think
the using the recovery a better solution.

BTW, as another person has noted, the index need not match the file for a
rebuild.  You can take an old index for the file, from a previous backup,
and the MF rebuild will replace it with a new one.  The MF
index is only needed  to get the structural details for the file.

MF does format the data portion of a file sequentially, and I have done a
recovery using that method.  However, it is not a trivial exercise. There
are places where the data is realigned to physical blocks that are
exceptions.  You also have to check for deleted records.  If the file is
old, there could be hundreds of garbage records in the middle of it. It only
works easily immediately after a full rebuild, which sort of defeats the
purpose.
Ib Tanding wrote in message <81kion$8oj$1@news.inet.tele.dk>...
>Please............
>A *LINE* sequential record is the kind of sequential record that needs
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>>going to have to filter out the deleted records, pointers, delimiters,
etc.
>>that are used internally.  To do that, you need a fair idea of the way the
>>Isam data is physically stored.
…[25 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81nudm$bbn$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <vh7p3sob1piae3kdd79vid47ib6k58n8g0@4ax.com> <81jdmn$f4a$1@news.igs.net> <81kion$8oj$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <81m3tg$7gj$1@news.igs.net>`

```
Speaking in general you are right. My reply was limited to the facts George
Roldan gave us. The corrupted file was created by MF FH and even if it was
from before 1991 (IXSIO format) you could read the .DAT part as a sequential
file or take an old index and do the rebuild.

You are right about deleted records, but we cannot get everything in life.
The IDX file *was* deleted, so George has to decide the method. Because I am
lazy, I would try the rebuild method first, and hope for an undamaged .DAT
file. If the .DAT file is damaged, the old backups can be handy.

Regards
Ib

donald tees skrev i meddelelsen <81m3tg$7gj$1@news.igs.net>...
>That depends entirely on the compiler.  Yes, a line sequential file is the
>standard <CR LF> format.  However, assuming that an ordinary sequential
file
>and an Isam data file are compatible is a huge assumption, and will be
wrong
>99% of the time.
>
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>way that you can be assured of reading it sequentially and accurately is
one
>byte at a time, analysing the data as you go.
>
…[17 more quoted lines elided]…
>old, there could be hundreds of garbage records in the middle of it. It
only
>works easily immediately after a full rebuild, which sort of defeats the
>purpose.
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>>CR/LF. The .DAT part of an indexed file *CAN* be considered as a
sequential
>>file. It's just that it has a length field in front of the record. That is
>>the reason for 'Recording mode is V' - try it, I've done so from time to
…[15 more quoted lines elided]…
>>>that are used internally.  To do that, you need a fair idea of the way
the
>>>Isam data is physically stored.
>>>
…[26 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

```
I'm taking a cobol course and in order to practice writing programs and
complete projects I need to go to the colleges'  lab.  Not always practical. It
would be much more practical to be able to edit, compile and debug the projects
on my home pc and then go to the lab with the working code.

I currently have fujitsu V4, which came with a book, and unless Im doing
something wrong it is difficult to use and does not seem to simulate the Cobol
85 Compiler my college uses.

All suggestions would be welcomed and appreciated.
```

#### ↳ Re: Compiler/Program to practice 74/85 on home PC

- **From:** roadraat@aol.com (RoadRaat)
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991127141517.27756.00000871@ng-fb1.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<19991124234137.04410.00000237@ng-fv1.aol.com>`

```
>I'm taking a cobol course and in order to practice writing programs and
>complete projects I need to go to the colleges'  lab...It would be much more
practical to be able to edit, compile and debug the projects
on my home pc and then go to the lab with the working code.

That could be a problem, depending on what kind of environment you are working
in.  I wanted to do the same thing you are describing, but it didn't really
work.  At my school, we work in the MVS operating system on an IBM mainframe. 
The compiler on my PC is different in several respects.

1. Assign clause: on the mainframe I must assign file-names to a ddname used in
my JCL submission.  On the PC, I must assign to specific files.
2. Status codes: the PC environment likes to have status codes (and an
evaluation of them) for all file operations.  At school, we never did this for
sequential files.  We didn't learn about that until we worked with VSAM.
3. File organization.  I don't use the PC much, and I still need to focus on
Thane Hubbell's chapter about this.  I'm not very hip to LINE SEQUENTIAL, etc. 
That's another consideration when you want to code school projects at home. 
Org will likely be different at school.
4. COBOL version. If you are learning from Thane's book, you are looking at
COBOL II.  My course didn't get into that until the very end.  You can code
COBOL I on the Fujitsu compiler, but if you are trying to use ideas from the
book on your school examples, they might not work if you're using a COBOL I
compiler at school.  COBOL I doesn't have features like scope-terminators and
the EVALUATE verb, etc.

I found the Fujitsu useful for my school projects mostly as a syntax check.  I
looked up just as much as I needed to make my school project compile on my PC,
even though everything before the WORKING-STORAGE SECTION was always very
different and the reports, DISPLAYs and input test data didn't work at all the
way they would on the mainframe.  But I did eliminate a bunch of syntax and
logic errors, and then I took my laptop to school and just re-typed what I had
written.  Kludgey, but it reduced the time I had to listen to the goofballs
horsing around in our school access room.

RoadRaat
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Compiler/Program to practice 74/85 on home PC

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81q5lc$gvs$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<19991124234137.04410.00000237@ng-fv1.aol.com> <19991127141517.27756.00000871@ng-fb1.aol.com>`

```
I would think you should approach the prof for permission to do your work on
alternative platforms.  I see no reason why a teacher would disapprove.
