<a id="t-1b5e2f0d6c"></a>
## Thread: How do you keep a VSAM file healthy? — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`VSAM, files, sorting`](topics.md#files)_

### How do you keep a VSAM file healthy?

- **From:** "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81kv7n01i13@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
I support/enhance an old MVS/COBOL II system that runs almost completely 
using VSAM datasets (there's a little bit of IDMS).  The system creates,
maintains, and reports on the host side of a POS system for a large grocery
store chain.  Many of the files we use are cleared out after the schedules
run but are not deleted and redefined.  Programs just open the file as
output and this clears them.  Looking at these datasets, I can see many of
them have been around since 1992 and several of them are extended out a
little too far for my tastes.  I'm wondering what kind of effect this has on
the speed of a VSAM dataset.  Is there something I should do, maybe do an
IDCAMS model to create a new dataset and repro to it?  Is there some other
IDCAMS utility to 'reorg' the dataset?  As an aside, these VSAM files would
kick IDMSs butt in any speed contest, any day of the week.  But I'm like Tim
Allen and if I can make it better with little effort, I want to know how.

Jeff
```

#### ↳ Re: How do you keep a VSAM file healthy?

- **From:** Gilbert Saint-flour <gsf@ibm.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383e9d1a$1$tfs$mr2ice@news>`
- **References:** `<81kv7n01i13@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
In <81kv7n01i13@enews2.newsguy.com>, on 25 Nov 1999 at 22:36, "Jeff
Baynard" 

>........  Many of the files we use are cleared out after the 
>schedules run but are not deleted and redefined.  Programs just open the
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
>it?  Is there some other IDCAMS utility to 'reorg' the dataset?

From what I understand, these files are reusable (defined with REUSE) and
you OPEN them for OUTPUT with the RESET option to clear them; this would
make the files permanent but the data in them temporary.  If that's the
case, you shouldn't be concerned with CI/CA splits, as I believe this only
occurs with data sets with permanent data in them, so forget about REORGs
and IDCAMS.

If these files have too many extents, you can consolidate them using the
COPY command of DFDSS (ADRDSSU) or a similar utility, although, depending
on the type of DASD you're using, this may not buy you much.

Another thing I'd recommend to optimize VSAM clusters: if the files have
been defined with IBMED and/or REPLICATE (which only wastes space on
cached DASD), re-DEFINE them with NOIMBED and NOREPLICATE.  You may want
to revisit the CI sizes also; bumping the CISZ of the Index from 512 bytes
to 4096 (or more) sometimes does a lot of good (you'd have to test it).

There is a simple trick you can use to speed up VSAM access without having
to even touch the file: it's called BLSR (Batch Local Shared Resources)
and works like a cache to reduce the amount of I/O performed against a
KSDS or AIX.  I've seen jobs recently go from 4 hours down to 15 minutes
just by modifying the JCL to use BLSR.  The catch is that, although BLSR
is a standard component of any MVS/ESA or OS/390 system, your sysprog has
to set it up in SYS1.PARMLIB(IEFSSNxx) before you can use it.  To check if
you can use BLSR, just modify any job that processes a KSDS, as shown in
the example below:

Without BLSR:

 //VSAMJOB EXEC PGM=VSAMPROG
 //KSDSDD   DD DSN=IBMUSER.AUDECHS.CLUSTER,DISP=SHR 

With BLSR:

 //VSAMJOB EXEC PGM=VSAMPROG
 //KSDSDD   DD SUBSYS=(BLSR,'DDNAME=KSDSDD2')
 //KSDSDD2  DD DSN=IBMUSER.AUDECHS.CLUSTER,DISP=SHR 

If your job fails with the following message:

 IEFC746I SUBSYSTEM BLSR DOES NOT EXIST 

then you have to ask tech support to modify SYS1.PARMLIB(IEFSSNxx) to add:

 SUBSYS SUBNAME(BLSR) INITRTN(CSRBISUB)  

BLSR can make you the king of the hill - try it today.

 Gilbert StFlour
 http://members.home/net/gsf/ 

PS: BLSR works 90% of the time.  If your program abends because it
receives unexpected VSAM status codes, try another one.
```

#### ↳ Re: How do you keep a VSAM file healthy?

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383E251A.28612E8B@att.net>`
- **References:** `<81kv7n01i13@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> I support/enhance an old MVS/COBOL II system that runs almost completely
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Jeff

In my experience, VSAM reorgs consist of:

1. REPRO the file to a flat file (or run a purge program will delete (&
archive) old data)

2. DELETE/DEFINE the cluster

3. REPRO the data from the first step into the new cluster

You're done! As far as performance is concerned, do a LISTCAT and look
for CI & CA splits - especially CA splits.

Bill Lynch
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How do you keep a VSAM file healthy?

- **From:** Arnold Trembley <arnold.trembley@worldnet.att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383CDC60.4A0ED50D@worldnet.att.net>`
- **References:** `<81kv7n01i13@enews2.newsguy.com> <383E251A.28612E8B@att.net>`

```
William Lynch wrote:
> 
> Jeff Baynard wrote:
…[29 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch

Many years ago I worked in a VM and VSE shop, and this was the standard
practice.  Most VSAM files in those systems were delete/defined every
day.  This has the advantage of potentially removing secondary
allocations automatically, if you have files that are subject to large
variations in the number of records day to day.

Now I work with large CICS systems that run 24x7, and we have mirrored
files.  During most of the day activity accumulates on the "A" files and
batch refresh jobs read the ESDS transaction log and apply updates to
the inactive "B" files.  During nightly maintenance the files are
switched.  We purge many of our "A" files while "B" files are active. 
Ten years ago we could not delete/define a VSAM file if it was allocated
to a running CICS region.  Now we can, but we still don't.  Our VSAM
files are defined with the REUSE option.  When we're ready to reload
them, we just issue and IDCAMS "REPRO" command with the REUSE option.  I
understand this completely rebuilds indexes as if the file had never
been previously loaded, but it won't remove secondary allocations.

I'm not sure, but I believe that if you use the REUSE option, or
possibly SHAREOPTIONS(3 3), that the maximum number of secondary
allocations you can get goes down from 123 to 16.  If this is true, or
is still a VSAM limitation, it would become more important to choose
good sizes for your primary and secondary allocations.

About 15 years ago I had a class in VSAM tuning.  There's a lot of
tricks, but basically it's a compromise between access speed, storage
utilization, and disk space utilization.  VSAM will generally keep
working even if your files aren't tuned very well, but you'll either be
slowing down your access speed or wasting memory or disk.

We used to worry a lot about CA splits in VSAM files in CICS.  It could
really slow down your access.  Generally, you want both your primary and
secondary allocation to be at least one cylinder.  At least that's a
general rule that seems to work fairly well.

There were some really odd problems that could occur.  A large data CA
size with a very small index CI size and looooong keys could create
files with record slots that you could never fill.

Normally you tune VSAM files differently depending upon whether they are
primarily used by batch applications or CICS, although memory is not as
big a limitation as it once was.  Tuning your freespace percentages can
have a big impact, depending upon the typical pattern of insert activity
you need to support.  But if your freespace percentages are high, you
will be preallocating lots of disk space you may not need.

It's always a tradeoff.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How do you keep a VSAM file healthy?

- **From:** "Mike Fleischer" <softwaresolver@home.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<1mZ%3.146$59.52801@news1.rdc2.tx.home.com>`
- **References:** `<81kv7n01i13@enews2.newsguy.com> <383E251A.28612E8B@att.net>`

```

William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net> wrote in message
news:383E251A.28612E8B@att.net...
> Jeff Baynard wrote:
> >
> > I support/enhance an old MVS/COBOL II system that runs almost completely
> > using VSAM datasets (there's a little bit of IDMS).  The system creates,
> > maintains, and reports on the host side of a POS system for a large
grocery
> > store chain.  Many of the files we use are cleared out after the
schedules
> > run but are not deleted and redefined.  Programs just open the file as
> > output and this clears them.  Looking at these datasets, I can see many
of
> > them have been around since 1992 and several of them are extended out a
> > little too far for my tastes.  I'm wondering what kind of effect this
has on
> > the speed of a VSAM dataset.  Is there something I should do, maybe do
an
> > IDCAMS model to create a new dataset and repro to it?  Is there some
other
> > IDCAMS utility to 'reorg' the dataset?  As an aside, these VSAM files
would
> > kick IDMSs butt in any speed contest, any day of the week.  But I'm like
Tim
> > Allen and if I can make it better with little effort, I want to know
how.
> >
> > Jeff
…[13 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch

One more trick you may want to consider..
1) Do a VSAM ALTER to another dataset vs repro to a flat file (no I\O to
read through the file)
2) Then do the define of the orig. cluster
3) repro from altered dsn to original dsn
4) delete altered files.

One extra step but less reads through the file.

Mike
```

#### ↳ Re: How do you keep a VSAM file healthy?

- **From:** vbandke@ibm.net (Volker Bandke)
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383e2515.2389175@news2.attglobal.net>`
- **References:** `<81kv7n01i13@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```
On Thu, 25 Nov 1999 22:36:31 -0500, "Jeff Baynard" <union27@macconnect.com> wrote:

>I support/enhance an old MVS/COBOL II system that runs almost completely 
>using VSAM datasets (there's a little bit of IDMS).  The system creates,
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>Jeff

The usual way to "reorg" a VSAM dataset is with a four-step IDCAMS approach

	REPRO INDATASET(my.vsam.cluster) OUTFILE(SEQDD)
	DELETE my.vsam.cluster PURGE CLUSTER	
	DEFINE CLUSTER NAME(my.vsam.cluster) ......
	REPRO INFILE(SEQDD) OUTDATASET(my.vsam.cluster)
and, in a separte Step (or Job, just to play it safe)
	DELETE sequential.copy PURGE NONVSAM


     With kind Regards            |\      _,,,---,,_
                            ZZZzz /,`.-'`'    -.  ;-;;,
     Volker Bandke               |,4-  ) )-,_. ,\ (  `'-'
      (BSP GmbH)                '---''(_/--'  `-'\_)

         As a computer, I find your faith in technology amusing.
```

#### ↳ Re: How do you keep a VSAM file healthy?

- **From:** "Joel C. Ewing" <jcewing@acm.org>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383E265F.2A925A4D@acm.org>`
- **References:** `<81kv7n01i13@enews2.newsguy.com>`

```


Jeff Baynard wrote:
> 
> I support/enhance an old MVS/COBOL II system that runs almost completely
…[11 more quoted lines elided]…
> Allen and if I can make it better with little effort, I want to know how.

Assuming you are talking about VSAM KSDS files, having a number of
extents is not as bad as having a large number of CI and CA splits;
although widely separated extents will degrade performance some,
especially if the file is not behind a cached controller or gets a poor
percentage of cache hits.  To reset a VSAM KSDS back to ground zero
without a delete and redefine, the file has to be defined with REUSE
attribute and it has to be opened with RESET (? not sure about the COBOL
equivalent) option, not just for output.  If this is being done on a
regular basis, then the file is in effect being reorganized at that
point.  The main issue may be a need to redefine the file with a large
enough primary allocation to eliminate extra extents.  

There is no special utility just for reorganizing a KSDS.  REPROing a
VSAM KSDS dataset to new KSDS dataset will give a reorganized version of
the original dataset (with a new name).  Another common technique is to
REPRO the KSDS dataset to a sequential file, verify generation of the
flat file was good, delete and redefine the KSDS, REPRO the sequential
file back to the KSDS, and redefine any AIX or PATHs and rebuild any
AIX's.
```

<a id="t-43acb8b96b"></a>
## Thread: Index corruption in Microfocus — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Compilers and vendors`](topics.md#compilers)_

### Re: Index corruption in Microfocus

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81jv7c$gpt$4@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<81hr36$dl7$1@ssauraab-i-1.production.compuserve.com> <vh7p3sob1piae3kdd79vid47ib6k58n8g0@4ax.com>`

```

:>We've found that parts of the file can be read with 
:>the index but not others. I had hoped to drop the index (by 
:>erasing the idx file) and rebuild it, on the suspicion that the 
:>data in the dat file is okay. However the utilities seems not to 
:>be able to read the file at all once the index is erased. 

The .IDX file contains information on the key sizes and positions.
The REBUILD utility should work with a corrupted index (which
is what it is for actually), but may fail if the key information
is corrupt.  There are command line options for specifying the
record size, key size and position, and that the data file
should be copied to create a new file.

I have used this to recreate a file and index some time ago.

It may depend on the file format thugh as MF can create and use
several different indexed formats: Level II, BTrieve, C2.
Some combinations of format and other parameters seem not
to work.
```

### Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-25T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81j64r$q3f$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383B8876.9E337E19@att.net> <bedp3sohv7qf2vsho1ibgdammfshdiammq@4ax.com>`

```
I wonder - how many PC's could you buy for what a maniframe costs. If it is
true that everything could run on a PC, why are you the first to think about
that.

I can see lots of IF's in the statement. that could be the reason. Likewise
the word 'theoretical' is used.

PC stands for Personal Computer, and that is what they are meant for. I/O in
that environment is like a snail compared to a Porsche. The programs cannot
handle multi-session satisfactory and the people in the PC-world will never
realize that some jobs simply are handled better in another environment.

You mentioned that PC's were only 20 years old. I am surprised, that all the
clever engineers started from scratch. Why didn't they learn from the
business. We still suffer from errors in the operating systems, National
characters were solved 20 years ago, but in the PC business we struggle with
the problem every time a new program is introduced.

The PC's are here to stay, and you can make many, many good applications in
that environment. You can do simple accesses on large databases and the
technology is becoming respectable, useful and I love it. But I know that
when I need to start the 'Turbo', I must connect to a technology, designed
for that kind og work.

Evolution, Not Revolution.

Regards
Ib
G Moore skrev i meddelelsen ...

>wrote:
…[50 more quoted lines elided]…
>reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam

## Thread: Help merging 2 flat files with dfsort — 7 msgs

_Topics: [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### Help merging 2 flat files with dfsort

- **From:** mdaugl235@aol.com (Mdaugl235)
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991126024335.19479.00000340@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```

I want to merge 2 flat files with dfsort, but I don't want the matches, I want
the mismatches (in other words, if the first 15 bytes (key) in the files match,
then they will NOT be written to the output file).  I can do this with icetool
I believe (which is basically dfsort with other options), and also with SAS,
but these are massive files (5 million rows in each file) so SAS is out.  I'm
curious if anyone has any input on the best solution for this (and also the
best performance option also). (Icetool would be fastest as far I as what I can
think of)  This is MVS mainframe.
  
             Thank you
```

#### ↳ Re: Help merging 2 flat files with dfsort

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383F6EBF.F43154F1@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<19991126024335.19479.00000340@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
Mdaugl235 wrote:
> 
> I want to merge 2 flat files with dfsort, but I don't want the matches, I want
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>              Thank you

You _cannot_ match files with DFSORT or SyncSort. The MERGE statement is
intended to _interleave_ the files, not match them. Same in COBOL. If you
need a lot of speed and do have Easytrieve, use it. It does what you need
rapidly. As far as ruling SAS out is concerned, it does not match flat files
as fast as EZ, yet I use it on a regular basis (same MVS) to process
multiple files and DB2 tables in excess of 200 million records without
experiencing performance problems I can't deal with. On our OS/390 machine
(far from being as contemporary, fast, and uncongested as I wish), it takes
SAS about 60 CPU seconds to read, process, and output a 34 million record
flat file with LRECL about 100 bytes. Therefore, it takes a SAS programmer
to write your program (including JCL and comments) run it, and produce the
output faster than writing a post about how to do it. Assuming that 1) your
files are presorted the first 15 bytes, 2) both are 100 bytes long and
associated with DDs A and B, 3) the output file, whose DD is called C,
should contain the key and the non-key parts of the input files (185 bytes
total), here it goes:

// EXEC SAS,OPTIONS='ERRORABEND'
//SYSIN DD *,DLM='$$'

*** Refer to the input - can do same in JCL;

FILENAME A 'name of file A' DISP=SHR;
FILENAME B 'name of file B' DISP=SHR;

/*----------------------------------------------------*/
/* Note: Using views against presorted files as       */
/* opposed to physical SAS data sets is critical here */ 
/* since it avoids IO otherwise required to write and */
/* read intermediate results. Same with DB2 tables.   */   
/*----------------------------------------------------*/

DATA A / VIEW=A;
   INFILE A;
   INPUT @1 KEY $CHAR15. @16 REST_A $CHAR85.;
RUN;
DATA B / VIEW=B;
   INFILE B;
   INPUT @1 KEY $CHAR15. @16 REST_B $CHAR85.;
RUN;

*** Allocate output: don''t forget NONCAT2;

FILENAME C 'name of file C' DISP=(MOD,DELETE);
FILENAME C CLEAR;
FILENAME C 'name of file C'
   DISP=NEW LRECL=185 RECFM=FB UNIT=3390 
   BLKSIZE=0 SPACE=(CYL,(100,10),RLSE); 
    
*** Unmatch the files ;  

DATA _NULL_;
   MERGE A(IN=A) B(IN=B);
   BY KEY;
   IF A AND B THEN DELETE;
   FILE C;
   PUT KEY REST_A REST_B;
RUN; 

*** Done! ;
$$

Kind regards,
++++++++++++++++++
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL 
++++++++++++++++++
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: Help merging 2 flat files with dfsort

- **From:** JohnTrifon <jtrifon@ix.netcom.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383FE2F6.3FF76757@ix.netcom.com>`
- **References:** `<19991126024335.19479.00000340@ng-cm1.aol.com> <383F6EBF.F43154F1@earthlink.net>`

```
DFSORT/ICETOOL  can certainly do exactly what you want (and more). It will also take
care of LRECL's, and RECFM's, copying it from the input. When the file's LRECL
expands,  you don't need to revisit your SAS control cards and modify all the places
where you've specified LRECL's.

//S1 EXEC PGM=ICETOOL
//TOOLMSG  DD SYSOUT=*
//INPUT   DD DSN=FILE1...
//               DD DSN=FILE2...
//OUTPUT DD DSN=FILE3,DISP=(,CATLG),SPACE=(...),UNIT=...
//TOOLIN  DD  *
SELECT FROM(INPUT) TO(OUTPUT) ON(1,15,CH)  NODUPS

"Paul M. Dorfman" wrote:

> Mdaugl235 wrote:
> >
…[77 more quoted lines elided]…
> ++++++++++++++++++
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: Help merging 2 flat files with dfsort

- **From:** "Paul M. Dorfman" <sashole@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38400A07.5A1D1E7@earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<19991126024335.19479.00000340@ng-cm1.aol.com> <383F6EBF.F43154F1@earthlink.net> <383FE2F6.3FF76757@ix.netcom.com>`

```
John,

Either you misunderstand the task, or it is formulated incorrectly. The
original poster asked how to _match_ the files and output mismatches. In
other words, one has 2 files:

File A:        File B:
------         ------
KEY REST_A     KEY REST_B 
--- ------     ----------
1   AAA        1   SSS
1   BBB        2   TTT
3   CCC        3   UUU
4   DDD        4   VVV
5   EEE        5   WWW
6   FFF        5   XXX
6   GGG        7   YYY 
7   HHH        8   ZZZ
----------------------
and is required to produce the following:

File C:
------
KEY REST_A REST_B
-----------------
2          TTT
6   FFF
6   GGG
8          ZZZ  	
-----------------

That's what _matching_ means. Note that the satellites are not required to
be of equal length or even the same type. To accomplish this, the input
files must be read concurrently, i.e. treated as different streams, which by
no means can be achieved by concatenating them in the input. ICETOOL treats
input as a single stream, no matter how many concatenated files FROM is
supposed to swallow, and has no way of knowing which key comes from which
file. As a result, your ICETOOL job (assuming that the satellites have
identical attributes) will produce

File C:
------
KEY REST_C
----------
1   AAA
2   TTT
3   CCC
4   DDD
5   EEE
6   FFF
7   HHH
8   ZZZ      
----------

and will certainly contain keys _common_ to both files, that is, fail the
task. So, ICETOOL, no pun intended, is not the tool for the job. As opposed
to that, EZ and SAS provide automated file-matching capabilities. As far as
performance is concerned, IBM have a very long way to go if they want their
DFSORT to catch up with SyncSort. Personally, I don't believe it ever will.

Kind regards,
=================
Paul M. Dorfman
Jacksonville, FL 
=================

JohnTrifon wrote:
> 
> DFSORT/ICETOOL  can certainly do exactly what you want (and more). It will also take
…[93 more quoted lines elided]…

#### ↳ Re: Help merging 2 flat files with dfsort

- **From:** fyaeger@vnet.ibm.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81opvk$43t$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<19991126024335.19479.00000340@ng-cm1.aol.com>`

```
In article <19991126024335.19479.00000340@ng-cm1.aol.com>,
  mdaugl235@aol.com (Mdaugl235) wrote:
>
> I want to merge 2 flat files with dfsort, but I don't want the
matches, I want
> the mismatches (in other words, if the first 15 bytes (key) in the
files match,
> then they will NOT be written to the output file).  I can do this with
icetool
> I believe (which is basically dfsort with other options), and also
with SAS,
> but these are massive files (5 million rows in each file) so SAS is
out.  I'm
> curious if anyone has any input on the best solution for this (and
also the
> best performance option also). (Icetool would be fastest as far I as
what I can
> think of)  This is MVS mainframe.
>
>              Thank you
>

Yes, you can use DFSORT's ICETOOL to do this and since it will use
DFSORT to do the sort, the performance should be fine.  Here's an
example of the kind of ICETOOL job you'd use:

//DOIT JOB ...
//NODUPS EXEC PGM=ICETOOL
//TOOLMSG DD SYSOUT=*
//DFSMSG DD SYSOUT=*
//IN DD DSN=... input file 1
//   DD DSN=... input file 2
//OUT DD DSN=... output file
//TOOLIN DD *
  SELECT FROM(IN) TO(OUT) ON(p,m,f) NODUPS
/*

Besides NODUPS, SELECT also these other operands for working with
duplicates:  ALLDUPS, FIRST, LAST, HIGHER(n), LOWER(n) and EQUAL(n), and
it also has DISCARD(savedd) if you want to save the records that are NOT
selected as well as the records that are selected.

For more information on ICETOOL, visit the DFSORT home page at URL:

http://www.ibm.com/storage/dfsort/

Frank Yaeger - DFSORT Team


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-7ff8c660b2"></a>
## Thread: How to read hex values in comp-6 field? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Language features and syntax`](topics.md#syntax) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to read hex values in comp-6 field?

- **From:** "zb" <zomersbuiten@tip.nl>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81lrfo$4cr$1@news.worldonline.nl>`

```
cobol version :
rm,/cobol-85 version 6.0 on a NCR unix 4.3

Problem:
We are working with cobolprograms from a software house. I make very simple
programs to read the cobol files (generated by cobolprograms of the
softwarehouse) to present the data in an onther way. But I have a problem in
the new millennium. The software house changed the date field and I can not
read the date field for dates in the new millennium. What's happen:
This is a part of the "old" file info. The file name is DATES

004500 BLOCK CONTAINS 4 RECORDS
004600 RECORD CONTAINS 128 CHARACTERS
004700 LABEL RECORDS ARE STANDARD.
004800
004900 01 OPENSTAANDEKLACHT.
005000 03 OPSTKL-KEY.
005100 05 OP-KEY PIC S9(7) COMP-3.
005200
005300 03 NEW-DATE PIC 9(6) COMP-6.
l

When you dump the file DATES, you will see (for example) a date of 990101 in
the field New-Date. That represents a date of 1 january 1999. In this
situation my program works fine.
But when I use the program of the softwarehouse to fill a date in the new
millennium say may 24 2000, the dump of the file is :
9A0524

When I use my program, the field New-Date will be respresented as 99 05 24.
The makers of the program use for dates in the new era a hexidecimal 9A. So
9A is 2000, 9B is 2001. My program cannot read the hexidecimal value. Al
hexidecimal values will be represented as a 9. So when I read 9A, my program
think that it is 99.

Does somebody know how I should change the field New-Date so I can read a
hexidecimal value (with Comp-6)

Thanks,

Steve Bakker
```

#### ↳ Re: How to read hex values in comp-6 field?

- **From:** "WOB" <wobconsult@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81m59i$qc1$1@nntp4.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<81lrfo$4cr$1@news.worldonline.nl>`

```
Steve,

        When you add X'1F660000' to your example value X'9A0524', the result
is X'20000524'. If you always add this value to your date-value, you'll wind
up with a date-format of X'CCYYMMDD' as a 4-byte Hex-Value. Seems to work
fine when the CCYY is X'9B', X'9C', etc. I'm not NCR literate, but I'm sure
there's a way to do this.

HTH....

Cheers,

WOB

zb <zomersbuiten@tip.nl> wrote in message
news:81lrfo$4cr$1@news.worldonline.nl...
> cobol version :
> rm,/cobol-85 version 6.0 on a NCR unix 4.3
>
> Problem:

simple
> programs to read the cobol files (generated by cobolprograms of the
> softwarehouse) to present the data in an onther way. But I have a problem
in
> the new millennium. The software house changed the date field and I can
not
> read the date field for dates in the new millennium. What's happen:
> This is a part of the "old" file info. The file name is DATES
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
> When you dump the file DATES, you will see (for example) a date of 990101
in
> the field New-Date. That represents a date of 1 january 1999. In this
> situation my program works fine.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> When I use my program, the field New-Date will be respresented as 99 05
24.
> The makers of the program use for dates in the new era a hexidecimal 9A.
So
> 9A is 2000, 9B is 2001. My program cannot read the hexidecimal value. Al
> hexidecimal values will be represented as a 9. So when I read 9A, my
program
> think that it is 99.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>

<a id="t-1295aff151"></a>
## Thread: Runtime Enviornment Setup, what is this? — 3 msgs

### Runtime Enviornment Setup, what is this?

- **From:** Mike Frederickson <mayfred@shinbiro.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383E7CB6.DCD0CB72@shinbiro.com>`

```
    OK, I am taking a class in COBOL and have written my first program,
complied and linked and came up with an executable version. (It was a
little more invovled than I made it sound, does any language have
understandable error codes?)  But every time I run the program, there is
a "Runtime Enviornment Setup" dialog box that pops up.  If I click OK,
my program continues to load and then executes.  What I am trying to
figure out is there any way to get rid of Runtime Enviornment Setup(RES)
dialog? Do I need to link an additional file?  Is this thing important?
What does the RES do? Oh, by the way,  I am using the Fujitsu V3 COBOL
85 to do this. I guess that might nake a little difference.
```

#### ↳ Re: Runtime Enviornment Setup, what is this?

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81m53q$92a$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<383E7CB6.DCD0CB72@shinbiro.com>`

```
When the box comes up, click on the "environment setup" menu item.
Click "keyword".  Scroll to @EnvSetWindow, and click on "selection". The
parameter will jump to the upper window of the background screen, and the
front window will close. Cursor over to after the "=" sign, and type "UNUSE"
(caps on).  Click "set" then "save". You will have to answer "Ok" to all the
stupid "are you sure, I am doing it, I am the compiler and smarter than you"
messages, but it should solve your problem.

Mike Frederickson wrote in message <383E7CB6.DCD0CB72@shinbiro.com>...
>    OK, I am taking a class in COBOL and have written my first program,
>complied and linked and came up with an executable version. (It was a
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Runtime Enviornment Setup, what is this?

- **From:** sff5ky@aol.comxxx123 (Sff5ky)
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<19991126113223.09285.00000204@ngol01.aol.com>`
- **References:** `<383E7CB6.DCD0CB72@shinbiro.com>`

```
In article <383E7CB6.DCD0CB72@shinbiro.com>, Mike Frederickson
<mayfred@shinbiro.com> writes:

>my program continues to load and then executes.  What I am trying to
>figure out is there any way to get rid of Runtime Enviornment Setup(RES)
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>

If your program does not need any pointers to files or limited use environment
variables, you canset @ENVSETWINDOW=UNUSE.
In the case of internal sorts or external file connections, you use this window
to set the environment variables that will be used by the program.

BSORT-TMPDIR=<some big drive space>
INFILE=<drive:path\filename or \\domain\path\filename>[,BSAM]
OUTFILE=<drive:path\filename>

This method is used when the Select/Assign is done to implementor names
versus hardcoded internal file names or working-storage names.

Select input-file assign to infile.
Select output-file assign to outfile.

To help reduce the amount of re-entry, you can click on the SAVE button
to store the information into COBOL85.CBR.  This will help on the next
execution
of the same program.
```

<a id="t-9fd3d680bd"></a>
## Thread: MF Fileshare V2 - Unix. — 5 msgs

### MF Fileshare V2 - Unix.

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81mgks$gqc$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`

```
As far as I can see from reading the manuals the answer to the following
questions is no, but just in case.  BTW this is all on hp unix and aix using
MF Cobol 4.1.

Is there any way for an application set up for transaction processing to
tell that it isn't  running as a FileShare Client?   The only way I can
think of is to check for the environment variable FHREDIR but its absence
doesn't necessarily mean we are not running as a FS Client.

A corollary question is, is there any programmable way to see if the
FileShare Server is running?

Finally is there any way to tell the I/O to wait 'until available' or for a
certain time, when trying to read a record locked by another user?   At the
moment, on a file status 9-068 I keep retrying the read for a period of
time.  This seems likely to be a resource user.

TIA
Rick
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Fileshare V2 - Unix.

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81mou6$i6f$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<81mgks$gqc$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`

```
I'd suggest just putting a "sleep" call into the read loop.  That should
make sure that it does not become a resource hog with minimal code changes.

Rick Price wrote in message <81mgks$gqc$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>...
>As far as I can see from reading the manuals the answer to the following
>questions is no, but just in case.  BTW this is all on hp unix and aix
using
>MF Cobol 4.1.
>
…[16 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

#### ↳ Re: MF Fileshare V2 - Unix.

- **From:** "Ken Mullins" <KenMullins@mindspring.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81n07u$qhi$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<81mgks$gqc$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`

```
Does your version of the MF compiler have the RETRYLOCK compile directive?
If, it specifies that when a READ statement finds that a record is locked
the read operation is to be retried repeatedly until the record is
available. If the record is locked, control is not returned to you until it
is available...

Don't know of a way to see if fileshare is running or not...Maybe try
opening and closing a dummy file you know can only be found via
fileshare...Problem there is there is a 2 minute timeout...

hope this helps some...

kenmullins


Rick Price <rick@hpd.co.uk> wrote in message
news:81mgks$gqc$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net...
> As far as I can see from reading the manuals the answer to the following
> questions is no, but just in case.  BTW this is all on hp unix and aix
using
> MF Cobol 4.1.
>
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
> Finally is there any way to tell the I/O to wait 'until available' or for
a
> certain time, when trying to read a record locked by another user?   At
the
> moment, on a file status 9-068 I keep retrying the read for a period of
> time.  This seems likely to be a resource user.
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: MF Fileshare V2 - Unix.

- **From:** "Rick Price" <rick@hpd.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81tish$rni$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`
- **References:** `<81mgks$gqc$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <81n07u$qhi$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
Thanks.   I'm not sure about RETRYLOCK.  It waits forever but that might be
OK.  However according to my manual it doesn't take effect if there is a
specific declarative for a file which could be a problem.  I think I'll put
a short sleep in the loop as suggested by Donald Tees and hope it doesn't
affect interactive responses too badly.  This is pretty easy for us to do as
our 'non standard' file status checks for all files are dealt with in a sub
routine.
I'm not sure how big a problem this loop is.   I only really thought about
it when testing FileShare with trace and holding a record on purpose.   The
trace went haywire on the retry loop (obviously) and made me think about
what might happen in real life situations.

Ken Mullins <KenMullins@mindspring.com> wrote in message
news:81n07u$qhi$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net...
> Does your version of the MF compiler have the RETRYLOCK compile directive?
> If, it specifies that when a READ statement finds that a record is locked
> the read operation is to be retried repeatedly until the record is
> available. If the record is locked, control is not returned to you until
it
> is available...
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> news:81mgks$gqc$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net...
<snip>
> > Finally is there any way to tell the I/O to wait 'until available' or
for
> a
> > certain time, when trying to read a record locked by another user?   At
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: MF Fileshare V2 - Unix.

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81tume$m34$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<81mgks$gqc$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net> <81n07u$qhi$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net> <81tish$rni$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>`

```
I have been coding my file waits that way for several years with minimal
problems.  I would suggest one other thing, though, and that is some sort of
screen indication when the lock is in place and the delay takes place.  In
most of my code, in the past, I updated the screen clock once on each retry,
and put the status byte beside it.  That allowed me to see what was
happening if there ended up being a "permanent" file lock for some reason.

The only real problem that causes is that it makes the screen I-O and the
file I-O co-dependent.  That has caused some problems on the latest
conversion of some code to GUI.  Now that I can effectively code to any
size, I am removing that and placing the file screen indicators into their
own window.  IE-on file lock, open a window, show the lock, delay, close the
window, repeat.  That is very CPU intensive for the local machine
experiencing the lock, but what the heck, it cannot do anything until the
lock is done anyway.  Eventually, I will trap keystrokes in that loop so the
user can break out of it gracefully.



Rick Price wrote in message <81tish$rni$1@starburst.uk.insnet.net>...
>Thanks.   I'm not sure about RETRYLOCK.  It waits forever but that might be
>OK.  However according to my manual it doesn't take effect if there is a
>specific declarative for a file which could be a problem.  I think I'll put
>a short sleep in the loop as suggested by Donald Tees and hope it doesn't
>affect interactive responses too badly.  This is pretty easy for us to do
as
>our 'non standard' file status checks for all files are dealt with in a sub
>routine.
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>> Does your version of the MF compiler have the RETRYLOCK compile
directive?
>> If, it specifies that when a READ statement finds that a record is locked
>> the read operation is to be retried repeatedly until the record is
…[31 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

<a id="t-0a7031edd5"></a>
## Thread: OT: Lesser-known British/American Usages (Re: Reuse of old data — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Off-topic and spam`](topics.md#spam)_

### Re: OT: Lesser-known British/American Usages (Re: Reuse of old data

- **From:** "Robert Annandale" <snarfle99@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383ed926$0$1111@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
There I was 14 years old sitting in Math class.
Brand new in Edmonton, Canada, just arrived from Zimbabwe.

I made a pencilling error, turned to the cute girl behind me.
(Wendy baby, where are you?)
And I asked her in my clipped polite accent...

"Excuse me, do you have a rubber...".

You could hear a pin drop.

One of my life most embarassing moments.

Regards,

Robert Annandale
```

#### ↳ Re: OT: Lesser-known British/American Usages (Re: Reuse of old data

- **From:** "George E. Zielinski" <georgezielinski@retired.airforce.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-26T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<6FD964D608259D82.D40C0867AA552FAD.0A28C1B7C4ECBCDC@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<383ed926$0$1111@fountain.mindlink.net>`

```
Being in the military (US Air Force) we were exposed to a multitude of
"foreigners" .  After an evening meal with the family next door, my
neighbors wife calmly turned to me and said, "On your way to work, Knock me
up in the morning."  Needless to say there was some immediate instantaneous
explaining to my wife on exactly what she meant.
```

---

<a id="t-51049a1c03"></a>
## Thread: Unisys 2200/500 data formats: Excess-Three — 2 msgs

### Unisys 2200/500 data formats: Excess-Three

- **From:** ~NS~Rob@Pro-Central.Com (Rob W. Smetana & Pro~Formance)
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38411ab3.1711749@netnews.att.net>`

```


Could some kind soul explain what the "excess-three"
data format is on Unisys 2200/500 systems?  

Also are "packed-binary" fields on these Unisys systems
comparable to those on IBM platforms, or do the formats
differ?

Thanks for your help.

=======================================

Visit www.Pro-Central.Com for details
on all our products, such as PDT-Windows,
our 18-quintillion byte file editor!

E-mail us at:  Support@Pro-Central.Com
```

#### ↳ Re: Unisys 2200/500 data formats: Excess-Three

- **From:** Michael McKernan <mck@pobox.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<udaenwptuz.fsf@tivoli.mv.com>`
- **References:** `<38411ab3.1711749@netnews.att.net>`

```
~NS~Rob@Pro-Central.Com (Rob W. Smetana & Pro~Formance) writes:

> Could some kind soul explain what the "excess-three"
> data format is on Unisys 2200/500 systems?  
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> 

I am unfamiliar with the Unisys 2200 and I thought that excess-three
was last seen, in hardware at least, in the Univac Three.  In any
case it's a system of encoding decimal digits in four-bit "nybbles"
in a way that's more convenient for computation.  The XS3 number is
formed simply by adding three to each digit.

	12345 (dec) + 33333 = 45678 (XS3)

This makes the number symmetrical within the range of the four-bit
field with three unused values at the bottom of the range and three
unused values at the top.  When two XS3 numbers are added, the digit
sums which produced carries are again natural decimal digits and
those that did not are six greater than natural decimal digits.
There are well known methods for correcting such sums.

Signed arithmetic, in either nines complement or tens complement is
also possible in the system.

I have no idea what packed-binary is.  Is it related to XS3?

Best regards,

Mike
```

<a id="t-80e228f301"></a>
## Thread: USA Today and COBOL — 3 msgs

### Re: USA Today and COBOL

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81oc0t$cc6$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<19991126193254.14781.00000647@ng-bg1.aol.com> <095v3s4ues16o12o0622ajmm2qff4o6ggj@4ax.com>`

```
*YES*
For the last couple of months the investments in new projects have been
close to zero. Many new projects awaits the roll over. I hear from all my
sources, that many Y2K projects found several areas in every installation
ready for reengineering or redesign.

Planned projects plus all the reengineering and redesign of existing
applications will probably generate increased demands for Cobol skills. We
can expect that many applications in that process will be 'right-sized'.
They will partly or totally go from mainframe to PC, and some of the
buisiness logic (the huge undocumented part) will be reengineered by Cobol
programmers.

Regards
Ib
G Moore skrev i meddelelsen <095v3s4ues16o12o0622ajmm2qff4o6ggj@4ax.com>...
>On 27 Nov 1999 00:32:54 GMT, bob7536@aol.com (Bob7536) wrote:

the

looking
>>for work next year.
>
…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
>reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

#### ↳ Re: USA Today and COBOL

- **From:** "Michael Mattias" <michael.mattias@gte.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<0qU%3.460$at4.8689@dfiatx1-snr1.gtei.net>`
- **References:** `<19991126193254.14781.00000647@ng-bg1.aol.com> <095v3s4ues16o12o0622ajmm2qff4o6ggj@4ax.com> <81oc0t$cc6$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`

```

Ib Tanding wrote in message <81oc0t$cc6$1@news.inet.tele.dk>...
>*YES*
>For the last couple of months the investments in new projects have been
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>...

About eight months ago I started asking that same question: "What happens
when all the Y2K remediation is done?"

At that time, a heard a lot of "all the deferred development will need to be
done, so COBOL skills will remain in high demand."

That was music to my ears, but I thought I'd wait before counting my
chickens. Now I'm counting. The whole Y2k-remediation thing either sucked up
all the development resources or, as mentioned above, really aided in
identifying some major economies avvailable through re-engineering.

From a purely selfish point of view, the best news of all is that a lot of
this "delayed development"  work is not suitable for entry-level
programmers, and experienced programmers like me are simply going to have to
raise our rates to maintain any sort of supply-demand balance. Bummer, huh?.

MCM
```

<a id="t-43e5f50b63"></a>
## Thread: Delete — 4 msgs

### Delete

- **From:** "Gert Beeckmans" <gbeeckmans@hotmail.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<oJP%3.4152$UW4.100277@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
Hi,
I'm using the following delete statement in a file with a relative
organisation: "Delete Klantrecord". But my compiler always gives the
following error message:  JMN2516I-S  'KLANTRECORD' IN DELETE STATEMENT MUST
BE FILE OTHER THAN SORT-MERGE FILE
I 've already tried everything but I can't find a solution to the problem.
Does anyone one how to fix it? Thanks a lot
```

#### ↳ Re: Delete

- **From:** "Ib Tanding" <ib@tanding.dk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81ot0v$dm6$1@news.inet.tele.dk>`
- **References:** `<oJP%3.4152$UW4.100277@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
There's one thing you didn't try.

Example:
FD  Klantfile.
01   Klantrecord.
       05 ...........

If your FD is like this, your delete should be 'Delete Klantfile Record'.

Regards
Ib

Gert Beeckmans skrev i meddelelsen ...
>Hi,
>I'm using the following delete statement in a file with a relative
>organisation: "Delete Klantrecord". But my compiler always gives the
>following error message:  JMN2516I-S  'KLANTRECORD' IN DELETE STATEMENT
MUST
>BE FILE OTHER THAN SORT-MERGE FILE
>I 've already tried everything but I can't find a solution to the problem.
>Does anyone one how to fix it? Thanks a lot
>
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Delete

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<aGT%3.13$3L2.1356@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<oJP%3.4152$UW4.100277@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
Fujitsu's error message parser has some REALLY big holes. You can try:

1. Rearranging the order of the FDs / SDs.
2. Rearrange the order of the SELECT statements.
3. Add/Remove optional clauses (RECORD CONTAINS, BLOCK CONTAINS,
LABEL RECORDS STANDARD, etc.)
4. Add extra records to the FD/SD that have no funcitonal use:
   FD  MYFILE.
   01  MYRECORD                          PIC X(80).
   01  DUMMYRECORD                 PIC X(80).

Whatever technique you find will not necessarily work on the next
occurrence of the same message.

Good Luck.

Gert Beeckmans <gbeeckmans@hotmail.com> wrote in message
news:oJP%3.4152$UW4.100277@afrodite.telenet-ops.be...
> Hi,
> I'm using the following delete statement in a file with a relative
> organisation: "Delete Klantrecord". But my compiler always gives the
> following error message:  JMN2516I-S  'KLANTRECORD' IN DELETE
STATEMENT MUST
> BE FILE OTHER THAN SORT-MERGE FILE
> I 've already tried everything but I can't find a solution to the
problem.
> Does anyone one how to fix it? Thanks a lot
>
>
```

#### ↳ Re: Delete

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81pfsq$5ji$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<oJP%3.4152$UW4.100277@afrodite.telenet-ops.be>`

```
go to the blackboard and write out 100 times.

Read a file.  Write a record.  Delete a file [record].
Translated from Japanese:
You are trying to delete a record.
This is an error.
The object of a delete statement must be a (normal) file name,
(not the name of a sort merge file)

Gert Beeckmans wrote in message ...
>Hi,
>I'm using the following delete statement in a file with a relative
>organisation: "Delete Klantrecord". But my compiler always gives the
>following error message:  JMN2516I-S  'KLANTRECORD' IN DELETE STATEMENT
MUST
>BE FILE OTHER THAN SORT-MERGE FILE
>I 've already tried everything but I can't find a solution to the problem.
>Does anyone one how to fix it? Thanks a lot
>
>

<a id="t-ed8507f4ce"></a>
## Thread: How to trap B34 abends in COBOL OS390 — 2 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe) · [`Help requests and how-to`](topics.md#help)_

### How to trap B34 abends in COBOL OS390

- **From:** "Jordaens Robert" <icare@wanadoo.be>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81om34$1kp8$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```
Anyone that knows how to trap a B34 or any file exception in OS390 so that
the program can give its own message and finish normally ?

Thanks,
```

#### ↳ Re: How to trap B34 abends in COBOL OS390

- **From:** Clark Morris <morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<383FF170.FA010F88@nbnet.nb.ca>`
- **References:** `<81om34$1kp8$1@buty.wanadoo.nl>`

```
Add a status-code on the select statement (status-code is pic xx and is
aprogrammer-defined field in working-storage). Then check the
status-code after ALL i-o statements for the file(s).  Note that the IBM
abend is trapped and mapped to the COBOL defined status-code.  See the
manual for details.

Clark Morris, CFM Technical Programming Services, morrisc@nbnet.nb.ca or
cmorris@fox.nstn.ca 

Jordaens Robert wrote:
> 
> Anyone that knows how to trap a B34 or any file exception in OS390 so that
> the program can give its own message and finish normally ?
> 
> Thanks,
```

---

<a id="t-ef4bfb585c"></a>
## Thread: **Update-Index-File — 1 msgs

### **Update-Index-File

- **From:** Bill <dc606@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81p3k8$a9j$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Purpose of Program:  To update index file
I am getting errors that I expect to get in my error report, therefore,
I assume other transactions were added, deleted or corrected.

Is there another way to confirm that transactions were added, deleted or
corrected?

Is there any way that I can view Validx.Dat in a readable format?

Can I confirm somehow through Indexed-Status-Bytes that update was done
correctly?



Miscellaneous Question
Should Indexed-Status-Bytes be used for every program that includes an
index file?
Of course, Index-Status-Bytes should be used only for programs that
include an indexed file right?

Thank you.


************************************************************************

     ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

           SELECT TRANSACTION-FILE ASSIGN TO 'A:\TRANS.DAT'
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
           SELECT INDEXED-FILE ASSIGN TO 'A:\VALIDX.DAT'
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS RANDOM
               RECORD KEY IS IND-SOC-SEC-NUM
               FILE STATUS IS INDEXED-STATUS-BYTES.
           SELECT ERROR-FILE ASSIGN TO 'A:\ERROR.DAT'
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  TRANSACTION-FILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 47 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS TRANSACTION-RECORD.
           01  TRANSACTION-RECORD      PIC X(47).


       FD  INDEXED-FILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 46 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS INDEXED-RECORD.
       01  INDEXED-RECORD.
           05 IND-SOC-SEC-NUM           PIC X(9).
           05 IND-SSN-NUMERIC
              REDEFINES IND-SOC-SEC-NUM PIC 9(9).
           05 IND-REST-OF-RECORD       PIC X(37).

       FD  ERROR-FILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 132 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS ERROR-RECORD.
       01  ERROR-RECORD                PIC X(132).


       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  FILLER                      PIC X(14)
               VALUE 'WS BEGINS HERE'.
       01  WS-TRANS-RECORD.
           05  TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER       PIC X(9).
           05  TR-TRANSACTION-CODE     PIC X.
               88  ADDITION    VALUE 'A'.
               88  CORRECTION  VALUE 'C'.
               88  DELETION    VALUE 'D'.
           05  TR-NAME-AND-INITIALS    PIC X(15).
           05  TR-BIRTH-DATE.
               10  TR-BIRTH-MONTH     PIC 9(2).
               10  TR-BIRTH-YEAR      PIC 9(2).
           05  TR-LOCATION-CODE        PIC X(3).
           05  TR-EDUCATION-CODE       PIC 9.
           05  TR-TITLE-DATA.
               10  TR-TITLE-CODE       PIC X(3).
               10  TR-TITLE-DATE.
                   15  TR-TITLE-MONTH  PIC 9(2).
                   15  TR-TITLE-YEAR   PIC 9(2).
           05  TR-RATING               PIC X.
           05  TR-SALARY               PIC X(6).
       01  WS-MASTER-RECORD.
           05  MA-SOC-SEC-NUMBER       PIC X(9).
           05  MA-NAME-AND-INITIALS    PIC X(15).
           05  MA-BIRTH-DATE.
               10  MA-BIRTH-MONTH      PIC 9(2).
               10  MA-BIRTH-YEAR       PIC 9(2).
           05  MA-LOCATION-CODE        PIC X(3).
           05  MA-EDUCATION-CODE       PIC 9.
           05  MA-TITLE-DATA.
               10  MA-TITLE-CODE       PIC X(3).
               10  MA-TITLE-DATE.
                   15  MA-TITLE-MONTH  PIC 9(2).
                   15  MA-TITLE-YEAR   PIC 9(2).
           05  MA-RATING               PIC X.
           05  MA-SALARY               PIC X(6).
       01  ERROR-REASONS.
           05  ADD-MSG                 PIC X(40)
               VALUE 'DUPLICATE RECORD'.
           05  CORRECT-MSG             PIC X(50)
               VALUE 'KEY DOES NOT EXIST ON MASTER FILE-CORRECT-MSG'.
           05  DELETE-MSG              PIC X(50)
               VALUE 'KEY DOES NOT EXIST ON MASTER-FILE-DELETE-MSG'.

       01  PROGRAM-SWITCHES.
           05 END-OF-FILE-SWITCH       PIC X(3)     VALUE 'NO'.
           05 RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH  PIC X(3) VALUE 'NO'.
           05 INDEXED-STATUS-BYTES     PIC 99.

       01  ERROR-LINE.
           05 ERR-SOC-SEC              PIC X(9).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(13).

           05 ERR-TRANS-CODE           PIC X(1).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(6).

           05 ERROR-MESSAGE            PIC X(47).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       100-PROCESS-TRANSACTION-FILE.
           OPEN INPUT TRANSACTION-FILE
                I-O INDEXED-FILE
                OUTPUT  ERROR-FILE.
           DISPLAY 'OPEN STATEMENT EXECUTED'.
           DISPLAY ' FILE STATUS BYTES = ', INDEXED-STATUS-BYTES.
           DISPLAY ' '.

           PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE-SWITCH='YES'
               READ TRANSACTION-FILE INTO WS-TRANS-RECORD
                   AT END
                       MOVE 'YES' TO END-OF-FILE-SWITCH
                   NOT AT END
                       PERFORM 300-READ-INDEXED-FILE
                       PERFORM 400-APPLY-TRANS-TO-MASTER
               END-READ
           END-PERFORM.
           CLOSE TRANSACTION-FILE
                 INDEXED-FILE
                 ERROR-FILE.
           DISPLAY 'CLOSE STATEMENT EXECUTED '.
           DISPLAY ' FILE STATUS BYTES = ', INDEXED-STATUS-BYTES.
           DISPLAY ' ' .
           STOP RUN.

       300-READ-INDEXED-FILE.
           MOVE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO IND-SSN-NUMERIC.
           READ INDEXED-FILE INTO WS-MASTER-RECORD
               INVALID KEY
                   MOVE 'NO' TO RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH
               NOT INVALID KEY
                   MOVE 'YES' TO RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH
           END-READ.

       400-APPLY-TRANS-TO-MASTER.
           EVALUATE TRUE
               WHEN ADDITION
                   PERFORM 500-ADD-NEW-RECORD
               WHEN CORRECTION
                   PERFORM 600-CORRECT-EXISTING-RECORD
               WHEN DELETION
                   PERFORM 700-DELETE-EXISTING-RECORD
               WHEN OTHER
                   DISPLAY 'INVALID TRANSACTION CODE'
           END-EVALUATE.

       500-ADD-NEW-RECORD.
           IF RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH='YES'
               PERFORM 800-MOVE-ADD-MSG-LINE
           ELSE
               MOVE SPACES TO WS-MASTER-RECORD
               MOVE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO MA-SOC-SEC-NUMBER
               MOVE TR-NAME-AND-INITIALS TO MA-NAME-AND-INITIALS
               MOVE TR-BIRTH-DATE TO MA-BIRTH-DATE
               MOVE TR-LOCATION-CODE TO MA-LOCATION-CODE
               MOVE TR-EDUCATION-CODE TO MA-EDUCATION-CODE
               MOVE TR-TITLE-DATA TO MA-TITLE-DATA
               MOVE TR-RATING TO MA-RATING
               MOVE TR-SALARY TO MA-SALARY
               WRITE INDEXED-RECORD FROM WS-MASTER-RECORD
           END-IF.

       600-CORRECT-EXISTING-RECORD.
           IF RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH = 'YES'
               MOVE SPACES TO WS-MASTER-RECORD
               MOVE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO MA-SOC-SEC-NUMBER
               MOVE TR-NAME-AND-INITIALS TO MA-NAME-AND-INITIALS
               MOVE TR-BIRTH-DATE TO MA-BIRTH-DATE
               MOVE TR-LOCATION-CODE TO MA-LOCATION-CODE
               MOVE TR-EDUCATION-CODE TO MA-EDUCATION-CODE
               MOVE TR-TITLE-DATA TO MA-TITLE-DATA
               MOVE TR-RATING TO MA-RATING
               MOVE TR-SALARY TO MA-SALARY
               REWRITE INDEXED-RECORD FROM WS-MASTER-RECORD
           ELSE
               PERFORM 900-MOVE-CORRECT-MSG-LINE
           END-IF.

       700-DELETE-EXISTING-RECORD.
           IF RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH= 'YES'
               DELETE INDEXED-FILE
           ELSE
               PERFORM 1000-MOVE-DELETE-MSG-LINE
           END-IF.


       800-MOVE-ADD-MSG-LINE.
               MOVE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO ERR-SOC-SEC.
               MOVE TR-TRANSACTION-CODE TO ERR-TRANS-CODE.
               MOVE ADD-MSG TO ERROR-MESSAGE.
               MOVE ERROR-LINE TO ERROR-RECORD.
               WRITE ERROR-RECORD.


       900-MOVE-CORRECT-MSG-LINE.
               MOVE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO ERR-SOC-SEC.
               MOVE TR-TRANSACTION-CODE TO ERR-TRANS-CODE.
               MOVE CORRECT-MSG TO ERROR-MESSAGE.
               MOVE ERROR-LINE TO ERROR-RECORD.
               WRITE ERROR-RECORD.

       1000-MOVE-DELETE-MSG-LINE.
               MOVE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO ERR-SOC-SEC.
               MOVE TR-TRANSACTION-CODE TO ERR-TRANS-CODE.
               MOVE DELETE-MSG TO ERROR-MESSAGE.
               MOVE ERROR-LINE TO ERROR-RECORD.
               WRITE ERROR-RECORD.




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

<a id="t-a0afd3cc38"></a>
## Thread: How BIG is a mainframe? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<dAT%3.8$3L2.998@typhoon01.swbell.net>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383BF349.ED910724@NOSPAMhome.com> <81jdb3$er8$1@news.igs.net> <383DEDF6.7522DD8D@att.net> <1t6s3sspfd4bri2gmhbpd78utqpgu06qk5@4ax.com>`

```
G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:1t6s3sspfd4bri2gmhbpd78utqpgu06qk5@4ax.com...
>
> there are a couple of things about the mainframe standards. first,
> until raid becomes available for pcs, the standards are all
> theoretical, in regards to 24/7 operation. windows, as an os, does
not
> have the concept of disk mirroring.

RAID and Mirroring (and Disk Duplexing) have been available in
WinNT for quite some time. Software utilities are available to add
RAID and Mirroring to Win95.

> so if the code crashes on a single
> cpu, it's over. since the *data* is usually run from a third party
…[4 more quoted lines elided]…
> maintenance (always the case). then we have the problems of
mainframes
> having *channels* which, the pc's equiviliant, is smartdrive.

Smartdrive was a disk-cache program - not even close in concept
to a 'channel.' Channels are programmable, access memory
directly, operate independently of the CPU, etc. Smartdrive is a
buffer.

>I  believe smartdrive is removed from 95+ (gee, it couldn't be gates
is
> sleeping with the mainframers?).

It was not so much 'removed' as 'replaced' by dynamic disk cacheing
as an automatic (and less error-prone) feature. In Win95 no resources
are dedicated to a disk cache, the resources are shared amongst
running tasks. A disk-intensive application on Win9x could easily have
a much larger cache than Smartdrive in Win3.xx, and conversely a
compute-
intensive application could use the RAM otherwise dedicated to
Smartdrive to improve efficiency.

In other words, Win9x says that RAM is a resource that should be
dynamically shared among active programs; sometimes as a disk
cache, sometimes as an application partition, sometimes as
something else, and these priorities will be adjusted every couple
of milliseconds.

>it also could be the case that
> database vendors wrote their own caching software, and it interferes
> with smartdrive, and they took it out for that reason.

Yep. Database vendors probably decided a) The operating system can
maintain a cache much more efficiently than we, and b) If the OS is
cacheing, having an additional cache in our software means that TWO
giant caches have to be searched for every access.

> using alternate news server source.
> reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam
```

<a id="t-5abe44301c"></a>
## Thread: **Update-Index-File (Dynamic Access) — 1 msgs

### **Update-Index-File (Dynamic Access)

- **From:** Bill <dc606@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81p4di$atb$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`

```
Purpose of Program:  To update an index file

I just coded this program for RANDOM ACCESS and am now trying to figure
out how to code it for DYNAMIC ACCESS and SEQUENTIAL ACCESS

I have put asterisks as to where I believe changes should take place.

**I am not quite sure what to do with statements for Record-Key
allocated switches though since I don't use INVALID KEY OR NOT INVALID
KEY?

Miscellaneous
I this program I have coded in Working Storage Section a filler
VALUE "WS BEGINS HERE".  I believe this statement is supposed to be used
for debugging.  Am I supposed to see this statement while compiling or
executing program?

Thank you.


ENVIRONMENT DIVISION.
       INPUT-OUTPUT SECTION.
       FILE-CONTROL.

           SELECT TRANSACTION-FILE ASSIGN TO 'A:\TRANS.DAT'
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.
           SELECT INDEXED-FILE ASSIGN TO 'A:\VALIDX.DAT'
               ORGANIZATION IS INDEXED
               ACCESS IS DYNAMIC/SEQUENTIAL*****************************
               RECORD KEY IS IND-SOC-SEC-NUM
               FILE STATUS IS INDEXED-STATUS-BYTES.
           SELECT ERROR-FILE ASSIGN TO 'A:\ERROR.DAT'
               ORGANIZATION IS LINE SEQUENTIAL.

       DATA DIVISION.
       FILE SECTION.
       FD  TRANSACTION-FILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 47 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS TRANSACTION-RECORD.
           01  TRANSACTION-RECORD      PIC X(47).


       FD  INDEXED-FILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 46 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS INDEXED-RECORD.
       01  INDEXED-RECORD.
           05 IND-SOC-SEC-NUM           PIC X(9).
           05 IND-SSN-NUMERIC
              REDEFINES IND-SOC-SEC-NUM PIC 9(9).
           05 IND-REST-OF-RECORD       PIC X(37).

       FD  ERROR-FILE
           RECORD CONTAINS 132 CHARACTERS
           DATA RECORD IS ERROR-RECORD.
       01  ERROR-RECORD                PIC X(132).


       WORKING-STORAGE SECTION.
       01  FILLER                      PIC X(14)
               VALUE 'WS BEGINS HERE'.
       01  WS-TRANS-RECORD.
           05  TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER       PIC X(9).
           05  TR-TRANSACTION-CODE     PIC X.
               88  ADDITION    VALUE 'A'.
               88  CORRECTION  VALUE 'C'.
               88  DELETION    VALUE 'D'.
           05  TR-NAME-AND-INITIALS    PIC X(15).
           05  TR-BIRTH-DATE.
               10  TR-BIRTH-MONTH     PIC 9(2).
               10  TR-BIRTH-YEAR      PIC 9(2).
           05  TR-LOCATION-CODE        PIC X(3).
           05  TR-EDUCATION-CODE       PIC 9.
           05  TR-TITLE-DATA.
               10  TR-TITLE-CODE       PIC X(3).
               10  TR-TITLE-DATE.
                   15  TR-TITLE-MONTH  PIC 9(2).
                   15  TR-TITLE-YEAR   PIC 9(2).
           05  TR-RATING               PIC X.
           05  TR-SALARY               PIC X(6).
       01  WS-MASTER-RECORD.
           05  MA-SOC-SEC-NUMBER       PIC X(9).
           05  MA-NAME-AND-INITIALS    PIC X(15).
           05  MA-BIRTH-DATE.
               10  MA-BIRTH-MONTH      PIC 9(2).
               10  MA-BIRTH-YEAR       PIC 9(2).
           05  MA-LOCATION-CODE        PIC X(3).
           05  MA-EDUCATION-CODE       PIC 9.
           05  MA-TITLE-DATA.
               10  MA-TITLE-CODE       PIC X(3).
               10  MA-TITLE-DATE.
                   15  MA-TITLE-MONTH  PIC 9(2).
                   15  MA-TITLE-YEAR   PIC 9(2).
           05  MA-RATING               PIC X.
           05  MA-SALARY               PIC X(6).
       01  ERROR-REASONS.
           05  ADD-MSG                 PIC X(40)
               VALUE 'DUPLICATE RECORD'.
           05  CORRECT-MSG             PIC X(50)
               VALUE 'KEY DOES NOT EXIST ON MASTER FILE-CORRECT-MSG'.
           05  DELETE-MSG              PIC X(50)
               VALUE 'KEY DOES NOT EXIST ON MASTER-FILE-DELETE-MSG'.

       01  PROGRAM-SWITCHES.
           05 END-OF-FILE-SWITCH       PIC X(3)     VALUE 'NO'.
           05 RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH  PIC X(3) VALUE 'NO'.
           05 INDEXED-STATUS-BYTES     PIC 99.

       01  ERROR-LINE.
           05 ERR-SOC-SEC              PIC X(9).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(13).

           05 ERR-TRANS-CODE           PIC X(1).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(6).

           05 ERROR-MESSAGE            PIC X(47).
           05 FILLER                   PIC X(2).
       PROCEDURE DIVISION.
       100-PROCESS-TRANSACTION-FILE.
           OPEN INPUT TRANSACTION-FILE
                I-O INDEXED-FILE
                OUTPUT  ERROR-FILE.
           DISPLAY 'OPEN STATEMENT EXECUTED'.
           DISPLAY ' FILE STATUS BYTES = ', INDEXED-STATUS-BYTES.
           DISPLAY ' '.

           PERFORM UNTIL END-OF-FILE-SWITCH='YES'
               READ TRANSACTION-FILE INTO WS-TRANS-RECORD
                   AT END
                       MOVE 'YES' TO END-OF-FILE-SWITCH
                   NOT AT END
                       PERFORM 300-READ-INDEXED-FILE
                       PERFORM 400-APPLY-TRANS-TO-MASTER
               END-READ
           END-PERFORM.
           CLOSE TRANSACTION-FILE
                 INDEXED-FILE
                 ERROR-FILE.
           DISPLAY 'CLOSE STATEMENT EXECUTED '.
           DISPLAY ' FILE STATUS BYTES = ', INDEXED-STATUS-BYTES.
           DISPLAY ' ' .
           STOP RUN.

********300-READ-INDEXED-FILE. ***************************************
             READ NEXT INDEXED-FILE INTO WS-MASTER-RECORD
              AT END
               MOVE 'YES' TO END-OF-FILE-SWITCH
              NOT AT END
               PERFORM 400-APPLY-TRANS-TO-MASTER
           END-READ.

************************************************************************
READ statement above is for Dynamic Access. I believe this READ
statement would be the same for Sequential Access except that I don't
include the word NEXT in READ statement for Sequential Access?
************************************************************************
       400-APPLY-TRANS-TO-MASTER.
           EVALUATE TRUE
               WHEN ADDITION
                   PERFORM 500-ADD-NEW-RECORD
               WHEN CORRECTION
                   PERFORM 600-CORRECT-EXISTING-RECORD
               WHEN DELETION
                   PERFORM 700-DELETE-EXISTING-RECORD
               WHEN OTHER
                   DISPLAY 'INVALID TRANSACTION CODE'
           END-EVALUATE.

       500-ADD-NEW-RECORD.
           IF RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH='YES'
               PERFORM 800-MOVE-ADD-MSG-LINE
           ELSE
               MOVE SPACES TO WS-MASTER-RECORD
               MOVE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO MA-SOC-SEC-NUMBER
               MOVE TR-NAME-AND-INITIALS TO MA-NAME-AND-INITIALS
               MOVE TR-BIRTH-DATE TO MA-BIRTH-DATE
               MOVE TR-LOCATION-CODE TO MA-LOCATION-CODE
               MOVE TR-EDUCATION-CODE TO MA-EDUCATION-CODE
               MOVE TR-TITLE-DATA TO MA-TITLE-DATA
               MOVE TR-RATING TO MA-RATING
               MOVE TR-SALARY TO MA-SALARY
               WRITE INDEXED-RECORD FROM WS-MASTER-RECORD
           END-IF.

       600-CORRECT-EXISTING-RECORD.
           IF RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH = 'YES'
               MOVE SPACES TO WS-MASTER-RECORD
               MOVE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO MA-SOC-SEC-NUMBER
               MOVE TR-NAME-AND-INITIALS TO MA-NAME-AND-INITIALS
               MOVE TR-BIRTH-DATE TO MA-BIRTH-DATE
               MOVE TR-LOCATION-CODE TO MA-LOCATION-CODE
               MOVE TR-EDUCATION-CODE TO MA-EDUCATION-CODE
               MOVE TR-TITLE-DATA TO MA-TITLE-DATA
               MOVE TR-RATING TO MA-RATING
               MOVE TR-SALARY TO MA-SALARY
               REWRITE INDEXED-RECORD FROM WS-MASTER-RECORD
           ELSE
               PERFORM 900-MOVE-CORRECT-MSG-LINE
           END-IF.

       700-DELETE-EXISTING-RECORD.
           IF RECORD-KEY-ALLOCATED-SWITCH= 'YES'
               DELETE INDEXED-FILE
           ELSE
               PERFORM 1000-MOVE-DELETE-MSG-LINE
           END-IF.


       800-MOVE-ADD-MSG-LINE.
               MOVE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO ERR-SOC-SEC.
               MOVE TR-TRANSACTION-CODE TO ERR-TRANS-CODE.
               MOVE ADD-MSG TO ERROR-MESSAGE.
               MOVE ERROR-LINE TO ERROR-RECORD.
               WRITE ERROR-RECORD.


       900-MOVE-CORRECT-MSG-LINE.
               MOVE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO ERR-SOC-SEC.
               MOVE TR-TRANSACTION-CODE TO ERR-TRANS-CODE.
               MOVE CORRECT-MSG TO ERROR-MESSAGE.
               MOVE ERROR-LINE TO ERROR-RECORD.
               WRITE ERROR-RECORD.

       1000-MOVE-DELETE-MSG-LINE.
               MOVE TR-SOC-SEC-NUMBER TO ERR-SOC-SEC.
               MOVE TR-TRANSACTION-CODE TO ERR-TRANS-CODE.
               MOVE DELETE-MSG TO ERROR-MESSAGE.
               MOVE ERROR-LINE TO ERROR-RECORD.
               WRITE ERROR-RECORD.




Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```
