<a id="t-f5d1711b54"></a>
## Thread: <<------------------ quotation marks in literals ---------------------->> — 8 msgs

### <<------------------ quotation marks in literals ---------------------->>

- **From:** "Luc Mert���s" <luc.mertes@village.uunet.be>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81phgl$d7s$1@nickel.uunet.be>`

```
I presently work with Fujistsu Cobol 85 and I would like to put quotation
marks in the VALUE of some literals, such as :

03 HTML-MSG     PIC X(18)  VALUE "<p ALIGN="center">".

As you see, there is a conflict with the quotations marks used as value
delimiters and the quotations marks used as text characters.

Is there a solution to this problem ?

Many thanks to all of you !

Luc Mertes (luc.mertes@village.uunet.be)
```

#### ↳ Re: <<------------------ quotation marks in literals ---------------------->>

- **From:** "Steven Kalemkiewicz" <senseisk@flash.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<K6Y%3.1095$0p4.157840@news.flash.net>`
- **References:** `<81phgl$d7s$1@nickel.uunet.be>`

```
There is a figurative constant QUOTES or QUOTE you should be able to use.

Example Usage:

03 HTML-MSG     PIC X(18)
               VALUE "<p ALIGN=" & QUOTE & "center" & QUOTE & ">".

-Steve Kalemkiewicz
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: <<------------------ quotation marks in literals ---------------------->>

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<WmY%3.13301$5q.108868@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<81phgl$d7s$1@nickel.uunet.be> <K6Y%3.1095$0p4.157840@news.flash.net>`

```
Steven Kalemkiewicz wrote:
>There is a figurative constant QUOTES or QUOTE you should be able to use.
>
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
>               VALUE "<p ALIGN=" & QUOTE & "center" & QUOTE & ">".

Using the '&' symbol as a concatenation operator is not part of the
COBOL 85 standard.  However, Merant/Micro Focus, Fujitsu and probably
other compilers support it as an extension, as does X/Open.
```

#### ↳ Re: <<------------------ quotation marks in literals ---------------------->>

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<E8Y%3.13272$5q.108145@news3.mia>`
- **References:** `<81phgl$d7s$1@nickel.uunet.be>`

```
Luc Mert�s wrote:
>I presently work with Fujistsu Cobol 85 and I would like to put quotation
>marks in the VALUE of some literals, such as :
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
>Is there a solution to this problem ?

Sure, use double quotes for an embedded quotation mark.  For example,
if you want a literal with the value: >He said "Boo!"<, you can use:

    "He said ""Boo!"""

Remember to count only one character for the "".  An empty nonnumeric
literal is "" and a single quotation mark is """".  Why would you use
an empty nonnumeric literal?  For the continuation of a nonnumeric
literal that ends in column 72, leaving no room for the terminating
quotation mark.  For example:
                                         column 72 v
    01  PIC X(??)  VALUE "ABCDEFGHIJKLMNOPQRSTUVWXYZ
   -                     "".

:-)
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: <<------------------ quotation marks in literals ---------------------->>

- **From:** wmklein@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81t32b$sjt$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<81phgl$d7s$1@nickel.uunet.be> <E8Y%3.13272$5q.108145@news3.mia>`

```
Judson,
  Although your example is "valid" - your terminology is NOT.  An "empty
nonumeric literal" is explicitly DISallowed in Standard COBOL - and
still will be in the next Standard.  It is imprtant to consider the
ENTIRE nonnumeric literal (both continued line and continuation line) as
a SINGLE nonumeric literal.  Therefore, although one line does have
nothing between the 1st and last quotation mark - this does not make the
entire literal "empty".

This is important to understand because "concatenation expressions" (as
shown in the original post) are considered (in the next Standard - where
they are well defined) as TWO separate literals.  Therefore, a
COPY/REPLACING =="ABC"== by something will match "AB" on one line with a
continuation of "C" on the next line - but will NOT match "AB" & "C".
(which is 3 separate text words).

Also speaking of the next Standard (and a feature that SOME but not all
vendors already support)...

The next Standard (and some vendors as a current extension) allow you to
use either the single or double quote as the nonumeric literal delimeter
- in fact you can use them BOTH in the same program.  (Older IBM
compilers didn't allow this - but new ones do.  Micro Focus has allowed
this for years).  Therefore,  if you want to move "' (double quote
followed by single quote) to a field in the next Standard, you will be
able to code:

     Move '"' & "'" to aField

Of course you could also code this as

   Move """" & '''' to afield

In article <E8Y%3.13272$5q.108145@news3.mia>,
  "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net> wrote:
> Luc Mert�s wrote:
> >I presently work with Fujistsu Cobol 85 and I would like to put
quotation
> >marks in the VALUE of some literals, such as :
> >
> >03 HTML-MSG     PIC X(18)  VALUE "<p ALIGN="center">".
> >
> >As you see, there is a conflict with the quotations marks used as
value
> >delimiters and the quotations marks used as text characters.
> >
…[21 more quoted lines elided]…
> whoever believes in Him should not perish but have ever


Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: <<------------------ quotation marks in literals ---------------------->>

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3840797F.9312CA5@att.net>`
- **References:** `<81phgl$d7s$1@nickel.uunet.be>`

```
"Luc Mertï¿½s" wrote:
> 
> I presently work with Fujistsu Cobol 85 and I would like to put quotation
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> delimiters and the quotations marks used as text characters.

The rule for any mainframe software I've ever used (as best as I can
remember, anyway) is that you code two quotes to represent one in the
defined text string, e.g.,:

	5	field	pic x(12) value 'bill''s quote'.

There are 13 characters between the outer quotes, but the two quotes
between "bill" and "s" count as one.

HTH,
Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: <<------------------ quotation marks in literals ---------------------->>

- **From:** "donald tees" <donald@willmack.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81q3ah$fon$1@news.igs.net>`
- **References:** `<81phgl$d7s$1@nickel.uunet.be>`

```
You can quote can quote single quotes with doubles, and vice-versa.
For example:  Picture x(10) value 'quote "this" unquote'.
There is also the word "quote" that can be used to quote both.
Display QUOTE this: "'" is an example of a double quotation mark containing
a single quotation mark QUOTE.

Student exorcise:  Display both types and the word "quote" on the same line.


Luc Mert�s wrote in message <81phgl$d7s$1@nickel.uunet.be>...
>I presently work with Fujistsu Cobol 85 and I would like to put quotation
>marks in the VALUE of some literals, such as :
…[12 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: <<------------------ quotation marks in literals ---------------------->>

- **From:** "Judson McClendon" <judmc@bellsouth.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** alt.cobol,comp.lang.cobol,fj.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<tKa04.12162$Di3.139111@news2.mia>`
- **References:** `<81phgl$d7s$1@nickel.uunet.be> <81q3ah$fon$1@news.igs.net>`

```
donald tees wrote:
>You can quote can quote single quotes with doubles, and vice-versa.
>For example:  Picture x(10) value 'quote "this" unquote'.
>There is also the word "quote" that can be used to quote both.
>Display QUOTE this: "'" is an example of a double quotation mark
>containing a single quotation mark QUOTE.

It is true that some compilers permit those forms.  But using ' instead
of " for a string separator is not in the COBOL 85 standard, and using
QUOTE as a string delimiter is specifically prohibited (see 4.2.2.2.3
paragraph 5).  QUOTE may only be used as a figurative constant. :-)
```

## Thread: How BIG is a mainframe? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38406FCA.5DFD002F@att.net>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383B8876.9E337E19@att.net> <bedp3sohv7qf2vsho1ibgdammfshdiammq@4ax.com> <81j64r$q3f$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <1dqs3ssj6rdfna9ua8vv9mad4n04t2htpj@4ax.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> here's the way i see it. i have seen very few benefits of mainframes
> in the last decade. 

I don't expect you've looked very hard, either:-)

> i've seen more sur-charges, and more ways to do
> the old transactions, but they cost the exact same price. banks still
> have lines,

This is a staffing, i.e., cost, issue. Become a high-balance customer
and sign up for private banking, you'll never see another line & your
personal banker will offer you coffee and an easy chair when you pop in.
If you look at the charters of the commercial banks in your area, I'll
bet they have least one thing in common, none of them is non-profit.

> even though transactions happen electronically. even
> though machines go 30 times faster, i still have to wait a day for the
> check to go into an account. sure, ...

You don't think the delay is because the bank is using a mainframe, do
you? The overnight delay makes sense, because your bank (e.g., Chase)
doesn't know until tomorrow if the (uncertified) check drawn on Citibank
is good or not. The banks don't clear checks in real time. They go
through Automated Clearing House overnight (longer if you're depositing
a check drawn on a California bank in a bank in Hartford, Conn). Why
they hold out of state checks so long is a different matter (ever heard
of "float"?).

Bill Lynch
```

---

<a id="t-86beebb12a"></a>
## Thread: How BIG is a mainframe? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-27T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<384075E7.3CAC7FAF@att.net>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383B8876.9E337E19@att.net> <bedp3sohv7qf2vsho1ibgdammfshdiammq@4ax.com> <383DEC50.40E1A992@att.net> <bh5s3ssjkig2l21vj3lvjetnerhqs1g70t@4ax.com>`

```
G Moore wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> >somewhere. You really don't have a grasp of what we (mainframers) run
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> factory, or whatever, has it's doors open.

You weren't paying attention, were you. Any really global business
probably has an office open somewhere across the globe at any time Mon.
thru Fri.

> let me take a guess at what mainframes do daily, eh?

Sounds to me as though you don't have a very good handle on what
commercial banks (especially what are called "money center" banks) do. 

> a>cache database transactions
> b>update a global debit/credit variable, and borrow/loan based on
…[3 more quoted lines elided]…
> adds a fee (which the sending and recieving bank both get a chunk of)

You're really pissed off that banks make money, aren't you?

> e>keep a system log of hard drive errors (which are replaced in the
> morning)

Mainframes (IBM, anyway) do this on their own and notify IBM service.
Sometimes the first notice we have of a malfunction is when the CE shows
up and says there's a problem in a certain string of DASD.

> f>shadow drives, log transactions and then update to the defragmented
> drive

Defragmented drive? LOL

> g>put out reports of which customers aren't keeping payments
> h>send reports to the irs of transactions which match above $10k

Not in my application (securities movement & control). We have *few*
transactions as small as $10K, and nothing I'm aware of which must be
reported to the IRS. Maybe to the SEC or the Fed if we suspect fraud?

> am i close?

Not at all. You didn't mention determining if the transaction (i.e.,
securities trade) settles in same day funds or not, checking if it's US
or global and, if global, the market where the trade is executed vs. the
market where where it settles, how to determine the custodian and
subcustodian. how to match trades entered by our customers with the
confirms we get from DTC (automated & manual, of course), what to do if
the trade has tax lot information, is the security book entry or
physical, etc. Keep in mind that customers in the US buy non-US
securities and vice versa and that Singapore & Tokyo are working in
tomorrow, not today (they're across the international date line). But,
what it's an FX trade? Does it include any USD? Do they settle in the US
or globally? Don't think of this as an exhaustive list, BTW.

I'm not going to debate this notion any more, G Moore (not that I'm
pissed at you, or anything, but I think it's a waste of bandwidth at
this point),

Bill Lynch
```

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<2438FE360EC5D42A.E474BEC6C7641831.6B4C43DD63E3CFB5@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383B8876.9E337E19@att.net> <bedp3sohv7qf2vsho1ibgdammfshdiammq@4ax.com> <81j64r$q3f$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <1dqs3ssj6rdfna9ua8vv9mad4n04t2htpj@4ax.com> <38406FCA.5DFD002F@att.net> <v3714so8nud7ks944177oc4n1mnq7rgfu8@4ax.com>`

```
On Sat, 27 Nov 1999 22:44:37 -0500, G Moore
<gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> enlightened us:

>On Sat, 27 Nov 1999 18:56:58 -0500, William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
>wrote:
…[14 more quoted lines elided]…
>reply to email gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com remove the spam

Having worked in the banking industry for 19 years I can unequivocally
say that is untrue.  They do not clear the check right away.  While
your current balance may reflect the check you've just deposited, your
available balance does not.  Your available balance is what you can
withdraw funds against, not your current balance.  Depending on your
account type and bank policy, the general rule is:  Available Balance
= Current Balance - Uncollected Funds.  Some banks use total float
instead of uncollected funds, but the result is the same except for
the number of days the funds are unavailable.  Float can be up to 7
days, uncollected funds can be up to 10 days.  However, the Fed
regulates how long a bank can "float" check deposits.  For interstate
checks I believe it is no longer than 7 days.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Living on Earth is expensive, but it does include a free trip around the sun.



Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

#### ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-28T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81sogh$80u$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383B8876.9E337E19@att.net> <bedp3sohv7qf2vsho1ibgdammfshdiammq@4ax.com> <81j64r$q3f$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <1dqs3ssj6rdfna9ua8vv9mad4n04t2htpj@4ax.com> <38406FCA.5DFD002F@att.net> <v3714so8nud7ks944177oc4n1mnq7rgfu8@4ax.com> <2438FE360EC5D42A.E474BEC6C7641831.6B4C43DD63E3CFB5@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote in message
news:2438FE360EC5D42A.E474BEC6C7641831.6B4C43DD63E3CFB5@lp.airnews.net...
> On Sat, 27 Nov 1999 22:44:37 -0500, G Moore
> <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> enlightened us:
…[30 more quoted lines elided]…
> checks I believe it is no longer than 7 days.

I work for a bank.  While we do not give *immediate* credit on checks (for
one big reason being we don't have an online teller system!), we give next
day credit on almost all deposits.  The only time we hold funds is if, the
morning after the deposit we call the drawing bank and they say the funds
are not available.  And, of course, we don't call on many checks.

We do delay paying interest on deposits for one or two days, depending on
the ABA number of the deposited item.
```

##### ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<F2E3119169D0DD38.9B830DAE33B298BE.729E83A2CEFBB0F9@lp.airnews.net>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383B8876.9E337E19@att.net> <bedp3sohv7qf2vsho1ibgdammfshdiammq@4ax.com> <81j64r$q3f$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <1dqs3ssj6rdfna9ua8vv9mad4n04t2htpj@4ax.com> <38406FCA.5DFD002F@att.net> <v3714so8nud7ks944177oc4n1mnq7rgfu8@4ax.com> <2438FE360EC5D42A.E474BEC6C7641831.6B4C43DD63E3CFB5@lp.airnews.net> <81sogh$80u$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net>`

```
On Sun, 28 Nov 1999 19:19:24 -0700, "Frank Swarbrick"
<infocat@sprynet.com> enlightened us:

>SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote in message
>news:2438FE360EC5D42A.E474BEC6C7641831.6B4C43DD63E3CFB5@lp.airnews.net...
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>the ABA number of the deposited item.

I forgot to mention that banks that have an online teller system will
give immediate credit for checks deposited that are drawn on their own
bank.

Also, for deposits that are really in question, the bank may elect to
put a "hold" on the amount of the deposit.  This hold can be for just
about any length of time.

And finally, most of the banks that I have done work for use the
following formula for calculating available balance:

Current Balance - Total Float - Total Uncollected Funds - Total Holds.

Regards,

          ////
         (o o)
-oOO--(_)--OOo-

Living on Earth is expensive, but it does include a free trip around the sun.



Remove nospam to email me.

 Steve
```

###### ↳ ↳ ↳ Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81vmq7$ugg$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383B8876.9E337E19@att.net> <bedp3sohv7qf2vsho1ibgdammfshdiammq@4ax.com> <81j64r$q3f$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <1dqs3ssj6rdfna9ua8vv9mad4n04t2htpj@4ax.com> <38406FCA.5DFD002F@att.net> <v3714so8nud7ks944177oc4n1mnq7rgfu8@4ax.com> <2438FE360EC5D42A.E474BEC6C7641831.6B4C43DD63E3CFB5@lp.airnews.net> <81sogh$80u$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <F2E3119169D0DD38.9B830DAE33B298BE.729E83A2CEFBB0F9@lp.airnews.net>`

```
SkippyPB <swiegand@neo.rr.com.nospam> wrote in message
news:F2E3119169D0DD38.9B830DAE33B298BE.729E83A2CEFBB0F9@lp.airnews.net...
>
> I forgot to mention that banks that have an online teller system will
…[5 more quoted lines elided]…
> about any length of time.

Definately not true.  There are two types of holds.  There is an "exception
hold," a hold that is put on for a specific reason (usually because we tried
to verify funds on the item and was told the funds were not available, or
the check was already returned once for NSF and has been redeposited).
These holds can be up to a certain amount of days, the number which I forget
(I haven't worked at a banking location for over three years).  I believe
it's 4 business days for a local check (within the say Federal Reserve
region) and 7 business days for a non-local.  The other is a "case-by-case"
hold, which can be put on for most any reason.  These holds can only last, I
believe, 2 / 3 (local/non-local) days.  There is certainly no such thing as
a permenant hold.

The length of these holds, by the way, is governed by Federal regulations.

> And finally, most of the banks that I have done work for use the
> following formula for calculating available balance:
>
> Current Balance - Total Float - Total Uncollected Funds - Total Holds.

Perhaps so, but not where I work.
```

```
PCs which would work with DOS, UNIX, Windows, whatever.

should have had mirroring for DOS, but, at the same time,
you don't think mirroring with software (DOS?) is a good
idea. Maybe they agreed with you?
```

<a id="t-3930898106"></a>
## Thread: COBOL/OS390 - WRITE — 8 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### COBOL/OS390 - WRITE

- **From:** "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<s842b72d.058@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
I'm converting a program from VS/COBOL to OS390/COBOL and I'm running into the following situation.

The program reads in a 133 character file that contains print records.  The first position is the carriage control and the rest is the data.

The output file is defined:
FD  PRINT-FILE
      LABEL RECORDS OMITTED
      BLOCK CONTAINS 0 RECORDS.
01  PRINT-REC.
      05  PRINT-CC           PIC X.
      05  PRINT-DATA      PIC X(132).
    
WORKING-STORAGE.
01  WS-PRINT-REC       PIC X(133).

PROCEDURE DIVISION.
<snipage>
      READ INPUT-FILE INTO WS-PRINT-REC..... snippage

      MOVE WS-PRINT-REC TO PRINT-REC

The program now does a WRITE PRINT-REC AFTER POSITIONING PRINT-CC.

POSITIONING is no longer valid with OS390/COBOL so I have to convert to:

WRITE PRINT-REC AFTER ?????

I can't say AFTER PRINT-CC as that is a PIC X.  Can't define as PIC 9 as I know it doesn't (won't?) contain numeric data.

Of course I can check the value of PRINT-CC and if '0' go to page, if SPACE do an AFTER 1 etc, however the JCL is defined:

PRINT1   DD  SYSOUT=S,DCB=(RECFM=FBA,LRECL=133,BLKSIZE=1330)

If the DD didn't contain any DCB information this would be a no brainer, but with the DCB information I'm not sure that I'd end up printing the carriage control in the first position of the output if I checked PRINT-CC to determine the WRITE AFTER ??? if I leave it at 133.

This particular program is used in many places and all the JCL has the DCB information on it, changing all of it would not be fun :)

So the question is:  If I leave the output definition and jcl as is, put in an EVALUATE statement so I have:

EVALUATE PRINT-CC
   WHEN '0'
       WRITE PRINT-REC AFTER PAGE
   WHEN ' '
       WRITE PRINT-REC AFTER 1
   WHEN...... etc
END-EVALUATE

Will I end up with the carriage control printing as the first position of the output?

All the dang files in the JCL are ampersand files so I can't even pull one and give it a try, and to create a file at this time would take a while (these jobs run about 30 minutes each - great number crunchers), and I'm not sure I have security clearence to some of the input files! (Doing this for another team - no good deed go unpunished).

Any thoughts out there?










































 Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
 Before you buy.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL/OS390 - WRITE

- **From:** Mike Kippels <mkippels@at-winternet-dot.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38430908.219E@at-winternet-dot.com>`
- **References:** `<s842b72d.058@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Eileen Preston wrote:
> 
> I'm converting a program from VS/COBOL to OS390/COBOL and I'm running into the following situation.
…[53 more quoted lines elided]…
>  Before you buy.
On input of a record, the system doesn't know that the file is a print
format file.  It's just data.  The carriage control will print as part
of the record the way you have your statement.

Change to 'WRITE PRINT-DATA AFTER ???'.

It looks like your dealing with ASA carriage controls based on your
sample code, but hete is the complete list (all controls are print
after carriage move):
	space = space 1
	zero  = space 2
	dash  = space 3
	'1'   = skip to channel 1
	'2'   = skip to channel 2
        .
	.
	.
	'A'   = skip to channel 10
	'B'   = skip to channel 11
	'C'   = skip to channel 12
There is a possibility that the CC characters might be machine-code
which is an entirely different set of controls.  Let me know if you
need any information on them.
	'1'   = skip to channel 1 and
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL/OS390 - WRITE

- **From:** Jeff Farkas <jeffreyfarkas@earthlink.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<MPG.12acd19058717e389896df@news.earthlink.net>`
- **References:** `<s842b72d.058@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
[This followup was posted to comp.lang.cobol and a copy was sent to the 
cited author.]

In article <s842b72d.058@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>, epreston@lear.com says...
>I'm converting a program from VS/COBOL to OS390/COBOL and I'm running into the following 
>situation.
…[41 more quoted lines elided]…
>DCB information on it, changing all of it would not be fun :)

 You shouldn't need to include he AFTER if you have the proper carriage 
control in the first position of the record. I have done it and seen it 
done a few times and it works just fine. Assuming you have the correct 
carriage control in the PRINT-CC you will have no difficulties..

So, WRITE PRINT-REC should work just fine and you will not have to make 
any JCL changes!

>
>So the question is:  If I leave the output definition and jcl as is, 
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
>Any thoughts out there?

I would just remove the AFTER clause and just try the WRITE PRINT-REC and 
see what happens. I have done it and it does work.
>
> Sent via Deja.com http://www.deja.com/
> Before you buy.
>

Jeff..
```

- **From:** Ken Foskey <waratah@zip.com.au>

```
William Lynch wrote:

…[10 more quoted lines elided]…
> Bill Lynch

Use the NOADV compile option.  This would have saved a lot of time on
my Fujitsu conversion, a lot of time getting rid of that extra byte.

Adding to the above.  If the ASI characters are correct the totally
remove the advancing clause.  The NOADV becomes irrelevant (ANY
advancing clause anywhere brings it into play).

Thanks
Ken Foskey
http://www.zipworld.com.au/~waratah/
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL/OS390 - WRITE

- **From:** Ed Milne <emilne@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81vk1m$nbg$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<s842b72d.058@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
In article <s842b72d.058@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>,
  "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com> wrote:
> I'm converting a program from VS/COBOL to OS390/COBOL and I'm running
into the following situation.
>
> The program reads in a 133 character file that contains print
records.  The first position is the carriage control and the rest is
the data.
[snip]

One thing that I found out (the hard way) was that VS/COBOL accepts
anything in the carriage control position with the POSITIONING clause
while COBOL II and above rigorously checks for valid carriage control
characters if the DCB specifies FA or FBA.

In my case, I was working with a legacy system that stacked several
reports on the same file and used a hex FF in the carriage control
position to indicate the beginning of a new report.  VS/COBOL with
WRITE POSITIONING had no trouble with this but in upgrading to COBOL II
I had to redefine the files as FB and use a straight WRITE statement
without the ADVANCING clause.

This mechanism was a leftover from ancient times when a spooler was a
separate computer with a tape drive and printer.  Stacking several
reports on the same reel of tape cut down on the number of manual tape
mounts required.  The report separator told the spooling computer to
pause so the operator could change paper.
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL/OS390 - WRITE

- **From:** William Lynch <WBLynch@att.net>
- **Date:** 1999-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<38436201.8DB8A026@att.net>`
- **References:** `<s842b72d.058@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
Eileen Preston wrote:
> 
(snip)
> 
> All the dang files in the JCL are ampersand files so I can't even pull one and give it a try,

I really hate the word "can't", especially when it impacts my testing:-)
Something I've to get a peek at this sort of thing is to code
DISP=(OLD,PASS) on the step where temporary dataset is last referenced
(override it, if necessary), then add an IEBGENER step at the end (after
the PROC, presumably) with the && file as SYSUT1. Then, copy it, print,
etc., whatever gives you that warm fuzzy feeling.

HTH,
Bill Lynch
```

#### ↳ Re: COBOL/OS390 - WRITE

- **From:** wmklein@my-deja.com
- **Date:** 1999-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<820p36$h3a$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<s842b72d.058@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>`

```
I have read a few of the replies.  I *think* that if you just want to
"copy" the formatting of the input file to the output file - then the
way to do this is

Use the NOADV compiler option
Specify RECFM=FBA (or FBM) for both input and output file
Just copy the input carriage control byte to the output (also defined
with 133 - not 132 bytes)

****

If what you are REALLY trying to do is not copy a print input file to a
print output file - but instead CREATE a print output file with the same
formatting logic as the old program, then look in the COBOL Migration
Guide (IBM publication) which tells you explicitly how to convert WRITE
AFTER POSITIONG to ADVANCING logic.  (Or use the CCCA product - which
will do it for you automatically)


In article <s842b72d.058@UTAEMAIL.UTA.COM>,
  "Eileen Preston" <epreston@lear.com> wrote:
> I'm converting a program from VS/COBOL to OS390/COBOL and I'm running
into the following situation.
>
> The program reads in a 133 character file that contains print records.
The first position is the carriage control and the rest is the data.
>
> The output file is defined:
…[18 more quoted lines elided]…
> POSITIONING is no longer valid with OS390/COBOL so I have to convert
to:
>
> WRITE PRINT-REC AFTER ?????
>
> I can't say AFTER PRINT-CC as that is a PIC X.  Can't define as PIC 9
as I know it doesn't (won't?) contain numeric data.
>
> Of course I can check the value of PRINT-CC and if '0' go to page, if
SPACE do an AFTER 1 etc, however the JCL is defined:
>
> PRINT1   DD  SYSOUT=S,DCB=(RECFM=FBA,LRECL=133,BLKSIZE=1330)
>
> If the DD didn't contain any DCB information this would be a no
brainer, but with the DCB information I'm not sure that I'd end up
printing the carriage control in the first position of the output if I
checked PRINT-CC to determine the WRITE AFTER ??? if I leave it at 133.
>
> This particular program is used in many places and all the JCL has the
DCB information on it, changing all of it would not be fun :)
>
> So the question is:  If I leave the output definition and jcl as is,
put in an EVALUATE statement so I have:
>
> EVALUATE PRINT-CC
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
> Will I end up with the carriage control printing as the first position
of the output?
>
> All the dang files in the JCL are ampersand files so I can't even pull
one and give it a try, and to create a file at this time would take a
while (these jobs run about 30 minutes each - great number crunchers),
and I'm not sure I have security clearence to some of the input files!
(Doing this for another team - no good deed go unpunished).
```

<a id="t-5271621b66"></a>
## Thread: Any Suggestions? — 2 msgs

### Re: Any Suggestions?

- **From:** Howard Brazee <brazee@NOSPAMhome.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<3842BDE8.942020E7@NOSPAMhome.com>`
- **References:** `<81t7ol$sos$1@husk.cso.niu.edu>`

```
Since you got your program working, I won't look very closely (in fact,
I am being very cursory, but address style questions:

1.  Lots of shops like to have field names with a prefix pointing to the
01 name, making  it obvious which records are being referenced.

2.  Depending on your compiler, you may wish to leave BLKSIZE out of the
code, and let your system or JCL determine it.

3.  If you do not have any input card, HEADER-ERROR will still be 'N'
and you will try to read an input card again.  What I would do is
(pseudocode):

Read first record at end set record-eof to true.
display error as you did,
perform main-routine until record-eof.

Then in main-routine, I check the valid header and continue.

4.  The standard of starting paragraph names with numbers is one I
like.  But leave room for new paragraphs inserted logically between
existing paragraphs.  And move the numbers of I/O down to 800- (or
8000-), plus error routines to 900-, etc, giving them a logical
separation from the normal flow.

5.  I like putting each open and close as a separate statement - when it
errors, I know which file errored.

6.  Some people prefer GOBACK to STOP RUN, but I haven't found it makes
any real difference.

End of my very quick scan and reply.

Jeremy Brook wrote:
> 
> I have just finished my COBOL program in my class.  It works now as designed
…[9 more quoted lines elided]…
>                   Encoding: x-uuencode
```

#### ↳ Re: Any Suggestions?

- **From:** Richard Plinston <riplin@kcbbs.gen.nz>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81v3tb$g35$2@aklobs.kc.net.nz>`
- **References:** `<81t7ol$sos$1@husk.cso.niu.edu> <3842BDE8.942020E7@NOSPAMhome.com>`

```
Howard Brazee <brazee@nospamhome.com> wrote:

: 4.  The standard of starting paragraph names with numbers is one I
: like.  

I used to do that.  It made it so much easier to find the 
places in the card box, and of cource on the lineflow
listing.

But 20 years ago or so I got some decent text editing and
other tools that made such considerations redundant.

      ;-)

: 6.  Some people prefer GOBACK to STOP RUN, but I haven't found it makes
: any real difference.

It probably doesn't on big iron, but it isn't Cobol in the
current standard (IBMers tend to think that 'Cobol' is what
is accepted by their compiler).
```

---

## Thread: How BIG is a mainframe? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "Jerry P" <bismail@bisusa.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00

## Thread: How BIG is a mainframe? — 1 msgs

_Topics: [`Mainframe, z/OS, JCL, CICS`](topics.md#mainframe)_

### Re: How BIG is a mainframe?

- **From:** "Frank Swarbrick" <infocat@sprynet.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<81veaq$kor$1@nntp2.atl.mindspring.net>`
- **References:** `<81126@news2.mia> <gnqh3s81jmoe1fgc236e5aqe506muk1du8@4ax.com> <81ddu6$o3v$1@nntp9.atl.mindspring.net> <e6fm3sk3d315osgfvua3n5t33tte62f4o0@4ax.com> <383B8876.9E337E19@att.net> <bedp3sohv7qf2vsho1ibgdammfshdiammq@4ax.com> <81j64r$q3f$1@news.inet.tele.dk> <1dqs3ssj6rdfna9ua8vv9mad4n04t2htpj@4ax.com> <38406FCA.5DFD002F@att.net> <v3714so8nud7ks944177oc4n1mnq7rgfu8@4ax.com> <2438FE360EC5D42A.E474BEC6C7641831.6B4C43DD63E3CFB5@lp.airnews.net> <81sogh$80u$1@nntp3.atl.mindspring.net> <9o844s8lvorlrflm65hm3drmb5gc0tl7ri@4ax.com>`

```
G Moore <gvwmoore@spam.ix.netcom.com> wrote in message
news:9o844s8lvorlrflm65hm3drmb5gc0tl7ri@4ax.com...
> On Sun, 28 Nov 1999 19:19:24 -0700, "Frank Swarbrick"
> <infocat@sprynet.com> wrote:
>
> >While we do not give *immediate* credit on checks (for
> >one big reason being we don't have an online teller system!), we give
next
> >day credit on almost all deposits.
>
…[6 more quoted lines elided]…
> soon as possible, and debits only when in absolute necessity.

Well, no.  At least not where I work.  The customer gets credit from the
bank before the check even clears the issuing bank.  Anyway, all checks are
basically assumed to clear.  That is, all the non "on-us" checks that are
deposited are given credit to our customers.  These checks are sent on a
cash letter to the Fed or a local clearing bank, and at that time we get
credit for all of them.  The Fed then sends cash letters to each of the
issuing banks (or another Fed branch, etc) and gets credit from that bank.
If, finally, the check ends up not clearing it goes back through the chain
on a returned items cash letter.  When it gets back to the depositing bank
it is then charged back to the account of deposit.

This is somewhat of a simplification, and may not be true for all banks, but
I bet it's true for a lot.  Even in the case of an on us check (one drawn on
our bank) there is a one to two day delay between the day a bad check is
deposited and the day it gets charged back.  As an example.

11/22/99:  You deposit a $100 check to your account.  You get credit on this
same business day.
11/23/99:  The debiting account on 11/22 did not have funds to cover it, so
it "bounces."  The earliest we can charge it back is this day.  Often times
the chargebacks don't even run until the *next* day (11/24/99).

No, I am not trying to teach people how to kite checks!
```

<a id="t-7f045c7286"></a>
## Thread: newbie and two level tables? — 3 msgs

_Topics: [`Tutorials, books, learning`](topics.md#learning)_

### newbie and two level tables?

- **From:** "Brad Hood" <Splitrail@email.msn.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<#78nustO$GA.293@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
Hi,
    I am in the middle of taking a COBOL 2 class. Totally lost on two level
tables I haven't been to school in about twenty years but it seems most of
the class is lost as well :(
    Any of you COBOL gurus have an analogy or a formula for remembering
these? Between the indexes and perform varying after statements or do I just
have to try and beat this into my head? Thanks for your time.
    P.S. Test on Thursday :o
```

#### ↳ Re: newbie and two level tables?

- **From:** Bill Thompson <wthompson@my-deja.com>
- **Date:** 1999-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<820k1r$dch$1@nnrp1.deja.com>`
- **References:** `<#78nustO$GA.293@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
What is it that you don't understand about "two level tables"?

Do you understand about "one level tables" (arrays)?

As far as the performs go, have you looked at the "flowchart" of the
performs?  remembering that a perform varying is a set of simple
commands and tests, i.e., set initial value, test condition, do work,
and increment counters (not ness in that order).....

I have always found that if you can break apart the "macro" problem
that the "micro" pieces can be easier to understand....

In article <#78nustO$GA.293@cpmsnbbsa02>,
  "Brad Hood" <Splitrail@email.msn.com> wrote:
> Hi,
>     I am in the middle of taking a COBOL 2 class. Totally lost on two
level
> tables I haven't been to school in about twenty years but it seems
most of
> the class is lost as well :(
>     Any of you COBOL gurus have an analogy or a formula for
remembering
> these? Between the indexes and perform varying after statements or do
I just
> have to try and beat this into my head? Thanks for your time.
>     P.S. Test on Thursday :o
>
>
```

#### ↳ Re: newbie and two level tables?

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-30T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<Y69iYEAbR8Q4Ew5N@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<#78nustO$GA.293@cpmsnbbsa02>`

```
In article <#78nustO$GA.293@cpmsnbbsa02>, Brad Hood
<Splitrail@email.msn.com> writes
>Hi,
>    I am in the middle of taking a COBOL 2 class. Totally lost on two level
…[7 more quoted lines elided]…
>
Treat the tables as a grid of graph paper. The first dimension is the
first index in the perform varying and the second dimension is the
second index, etc. There is no easy way and it can get really confusing
when you add extra levels.

Most tables have a structure and if you consider a table composed of
motor vehicle details (date of manufacture, model, trim level..) the
first dimension is the record of motor details. The second dimension is
the field type (date of manu....). Therefore name the index according to
it's use - first dimension index could be WS-VEHICLE-INDEX and the
second dimension could be WS-FIELD-INDEX.

Wait till you have multiple multi-dimensional tables on the go, and then
somebody re-uses an index/subscript. 
```

<a id="t-5015bf4926"></a>
## Thread: Any Suggestions? — 1 msgs

### Re: Any Suggestions?

- **From:** Alistair Maclean <LD50Macca@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>
- **Date:** 1999-11-29T00:00:00
- **Newsgroups:** comp.lang.cobol
- **Message-ID:** `<su9vdEAbloQ4Ew+q@ld50macca.demon.co.uk>`
- **References:** `<81t7ol$sos$1@husk.cso.niu.edu>`

```
Looks reasonably well laid out so any comments are minor:

1.  In the working storage section put explanations of field contents
with the relevant dataitem. Otherwise, 15 years down the line the
comments will have been lost or neglected and therefore be out of date.

2.  Put each FD on a new page.

3.  Use sections and not paragraphs. Prevents the risk of dropping
through. (Don't spam me I've already seen the lengthy debates :-).

4.  I don't like the MAIN section. I usually structure the MAIN section
with performs of INITIALIZE, MAIN and CLOSE sections and stick the read
ahead in the Initialize section. You are doing a read ahead (good) but I
don't like the multiplicity of checks that you do for errors after
completing the main process. 

If you read Michael Jackson's book on structured programming and follow
the advice to the extent of deducing the annotated program structure
diagram you should then be able to code direct from the diagram without
producing spaghetti code. Junk anything he says about pseudo code.

In article <81t7ol$sos$1@husk.cso.niu.edu>, Jeremy Brook
<jbrook@niu.edu> writes
>I have just finished my COBOL program in my class.  It works now as designed
>but I was wondering if there are any obvious improvements or real world
…[8 more quoted lines elided]…
>
```
